text: The Applicant seeks a Certificate of Citizenship and states that she acquired U.S. citizenship through a U.S. citizen parent. USCIS denied her Form N-600 after concluding that the record did not establish that she had been adopted by a U.S. citizen parent before age 16, and she appealed. The Applicant maintains that she was finally adopted in India on January 21, 1984 by N-A- and P-A-. The record includes a January 21, 1984 judgment from an Indian court stating that N-A- and P-A- were a married couple residing in the United States but present in India as the Applicant's caretakers, authorizing the Applicant to be given to N-A- in adoption with permission to take her to the United States, and directing that the adoption be communicated to the court when it took place. The Applicant's Indian birth certificate, issued January 23, 1984, records that she was born on January 1, 1984 to unknown parents and states that on January 21, 1984 a district judge appointed N-A- and P-A- as guardians and gave them permission to remove her to the United States preparatory to completion of her legal adoption. The record also contains January 23, 1984 affidavits from the secretary of the relevant Indian authority and from N-A- referring to N-A- and P-A- as the Applicant's adoptive parents, and a February 2019 legal opinion from counsel in India stating that, after reviewing the 1984 judgment and guardianship application, he concluded that the Applicant had been legally adopted in India in 1984. Government records show that the Applicant was issued an immigrant visa in IR-4 classification and was admitted to the United States as a lawful permanent resident in 1984 on that basis. The record further includes an amended adoption judgment from an Illinois court stating that N-A- and P-A- adopted the Applicant in Illinois in 2019, when she was 35 years old. The Applicant contends that this Illinois order should operate nunc pro tunc to the 1984 Indian judgment and that the Illinois court recognized the 1984 Indian judgment as a full and final adoption; the Illinois order states that proof of a full and complete 1984 Indian adoption had been submitted and also states that the order was entered nunc pro tunc to 2017. As described in the AAO decision's discussion of the record, the 1984 Indian judgment appears on its face to be a guardianship-related order rather than a final adoption decree because it granted permission for the Applicant to be given in adoption and removed to the United States and required that any completed adoption later be reported to the court, and the file does not contain a later Indian deed of adoption or other evidence showing that a further adoption was completed in India after that order.
statutes:
The record reflects that the Applicant was born abroad in India on I 11 , 1984. According to information on her birth certificate, her biological parents are unknown and abandoned her on 5, 1984. Onl 21, 1984, the District Court inl IIndia I I issued a Judgment order granting N-A- and P-A- permission to give the Applicant to N-A- in order for him to adopt her and further granting permission to remove the Applicant from the jurisdiction of thel I I I to the United States. 1 In I I 1984, the U.S. Department of State (State Department) issued an immigrant IR-4 visa to the Applicant classifying her as an orphan whose adoption by a U.S. citizen must be completed in the United States. 5 USCIS Policy Manual C .5(A), https://www.uscis.gov/policy-rnanual. Inl I1984, the Applicant was inspected and admitted to the United States as a lawful permanent resident based on her IR-4 classification. The record includes a copy of the Applicant's most recent Form 1-551, Alien Registration Card, which was issued in 2016 and continued to reflect that she was a lawful permanent resident based on her IR-4 classification. In 1 Names withheld to protect the individuals' identities. N-A- is the Applicant's adoptive father and P-A- is her adoptive mother.

2019, the Applicant's parents obtained an Amended Judgment Order for Adoption from a Circuit Court inl IIllinois. The record shows that the Applicant's adoptive father, N-A-, became a naturalized U.S. citizen in 1983. The Applicant does not claim, and the record does not show, that her adoptive mother, P-A-, is a U.S. citizen. Therefore, the Applicant seeks a Certificate of Citizenship under section 320 of the Act solely through N-A-. Section 320 of the Act, as amended by the Child Citizenship Act of 2000, Pub. L. No. 106-395, 114 Stat. 1631 (CCA), applies to the Applicant's derivative citizenship claim. 2 Section 320 of the Act provides that: (a) A child born outside ofthe United States automatically becomes a citizen ofthe United States when all of the following conditions have been fulfilled: (1) At least one parent of the child is a citizen of the United States, whether by birth or naturalization. (2) The child is under the age of eighteen years. (3) The child is residing in the United States in the legal and physical custody of the citizen parent pursuant to a lawful admission for permanent residence. (b) Subsection (a) shall apply to a child adopted by a United States citizen parent if the child satisfies the requirements applicable to adopted children under section [l0l(b)(l) of the Act]. See also 8 C.F.R. § 320.2. As the Applicant seeks to derive citizenship through an adoptive U.S. citizen parent, she must satisfy the provisions applicable to adopted children under section 320(b) of the Act by showing that she meets the definition of "child" under section l0l(b)(l) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § ll0l(b)(l). See also 8 C.F.R. § 320.1 (defining "adopted child" as one who has a full, final, and complete adoption, and who meets the requirements of section l0l(b)(l)(E) or (F) of the Act). Section l0l(b)(l) of the Act, provides, in pertinent part: The term "child" means an unmarried person under twenty-one years of age who is(E)(i) a child adopted while under the age of sixteen years if the child has been in the legal custody of, and has resided with, the adopting parent or parents for at least two years ... ; or 2 The CCA, which took effect on Febrnary 27, 2001, amended former sections 320 and 322 of the Act, and repealed former section 321 of the Act. The provisions of the CCA are not retroactive, and the amended provisions apply only to individuals, such as the Applicant, who were not yet 18 years old as ofFebrnary 27, 2001. 2

(F)(i) a child, under the age of sixteen at the time a petition is filed ... to accord a classification as an immediate relative under section 201 (b) of this title, who is an orphan because of the death or disappearance of, abandonment or desertion by, or separation or loss from, both parents, or for whom the sole or surviving parent is incapable of providing the proper care and has in writing irrevocably released the child for emigration and adoption; who has been adopted abroad by a United States citizen and spouse jointly, .. at least 1 of whom personally saw and observed the child before or during the adoption proceedings; or who is coming to the United States for adoption by a United States citizen and spouse jointly ... who have or has complied with the preadoption requirements, if any, of the child's proposed residence: For purposes of satisfying the section 320 of the Act regulatory requirements for issuance of a Certificate of Citizenship, "adopted" is defined at 8 C.F.R. § 320.1 to mean a full, final, and complete adoption. If a foreign adoption of an orphan was not full and final, was defective, or the unmarried U.S. citizen parent or U.S. citizen parent and spouse jointly did not see and observe the child in person prior to or during the foreign adoption proceedings, the child is not considered to have been fully, finally, and completely adopted and must be readopted in the United States. Id. Readoption requirements may be waived if the state ofresidence of the United States citizen parent(s) recognizes the foreign adoption as full and final under that state's adoption laws. Id. Thus, if a child was adopted outside of the United States and the child immigrated based on IR--4 classification, an applicant must provide evidence that the foreign adoption is recognized by the state where the child is permanently residing. 8 C.F.R. § 320.3(b )(viii). In addition, because the Applicant was born abroad, she is presumed to be a foreign national and bears the burden of establishing her claim to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. See Matter ofBaires, 24 I&N Dec. 467,468 (BIA 2008). The "preponderance of the evidence" standard requires that the record demonstrate the Applicant's claim is "probably true," based on the specific facts of her case. See Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. at 376 (citing Matter ofE-M-, 20 I&N Dec. 77, 79-80 (Comm'r 1989)).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the record (boolean predicates are arity 0)
born_in_india.
born_jan_1984.
birth_certificate_parents_unknown.
abandoned_at_foster_home.
indian_court_order_jan21_1984_authorized_adoption_and_permission_to_export.
contemporaneous_affidavit_secretary_describing_abandonment.
contemporaneous_affidavit_na_stating_present.
ds230_ir4_issued_1984.  % State Department classified applicant IR-4 (adoption to be completed in US)
admitted_lpr_1984.
form_i551_2016_reflects_ir4.
illinois_amended_adoption_2019_nunc_pro_tunc_2017.
na_naturalized_1983.
pa_not_citizen.
applicant_filed_N600_claim_320.
cca_applies.  % Child Citizenship Act applies to this applicant

% Legal rule predicates (arity 0 for yes/no)
% A U.S. citizen parent exists (N-A- naturalized in 1983)
us_parent_citizen :- na_naturalized_1983.

% The child was admitted as LPR and resided in the U.S. in the legal and physical custody
% of the citizen parent pursuant to lawful admission for permanent residence.
% The record shows admission as LPR in 1984 based on IR-4 classification.
lpr_admitted_and_resided_with_citizen_parent :-
    admitted_lpr_1984,
    us_parent_citizen.
% (We treat admitted_lpr_1984 + us_parent_citizen as evidence of residing in custody for purposes of the custody element.)

% Definition: a foreign adoption must be "full, final, and complete" to count under 8 C.F.R. § 320.1.
% Presence of an IR-4 immigrant visa classification indicates the State Department viewed the adoption
% as requiring finalization in the United States (i.e., not full/final abroad).
foreign_adoption_not_full_final :- ds230_ir4_issued_1984.

% There is no affirmative record evidence of a contemporaneous, full and final foreign adoption
% in India that would satisfy the "full, final, and complete" standard.
evidence_foreign_adoption_full_final :-
    % We would require specific positive evidence (explicit final adoption under Indian law),
    % which the record does not contain. Therefore this predicate fails (no clauses).
    fail.

% For adopted-child definitions under INA 101(b)(1)(F), one requirement is that
% "at least one of whom personally saw and observed the child before or during the adoption proceedings."
% The record contains a later affidavit from N-A- asserting presence, but there is no contemporaneous,
% independent, persuasive evidence satisfying the regulatory standard.
evidence_parent_personally_saw_child_contemporaneously :-
    % No contemporaneous independent proof exists in the record sufficient to satisfy the statutory/regulatory test.
    fail.

% Readoption in the U.S. could satisfy adoption requirements in some cases, but the U.S. readoption here
% occurred in 2019 when the applicant was an adult (35) and therefore does not satisfy the statutory age-based adoption
% criteria (e.g., adoption while under age 16 for §101(b)(1)(E)), nor does it create a qualifying foreign adoption at the relevant time.
readoption_while_under_16 :-
    % The 2019 Illinois adoption was not while the applicant was under 16; no qualifying readoption exists.
    fail.

% Determine whether the applicant meets the "adopted child" requirement for §320(b):
% must be either a qualifying adoption under 101(b)(1)(E) or 101(b)(1)(F) (for orphans) or qualify via state recognition/readoption waiver.
adopted_child_definition_met :-
    % Option A: adopted while under 16 with two years in custody/residence (101(b)(1)(E))
    % There is no evidence applicant was adopted while under 16 and met the two-year custody/residence in the adopting parent's home.
    % Option B: orphan adopted abroad with at least one adopting parent who personally saw the child (101(b)(1)(F))
    evidence_foreign_adoption_full_final,
    evidence_parent_personally_saw_child_contemporaneously.
    % Note: because evidence_foreign_adoption_full_final and evidence_parent_personally_saw_child_contemporaneously are not satisfied,
    % this conjunctive clause will fail, and no alternative clause establishes adopted_child_definition_met.

% For completeness, a separate clause would allow state recognition to waive readoption; the record does not contain
% adequate contemporaneous state recognition of a full, final foreign adoption at the relevant time.
state_recognizes_foreign_adoption :-
    % No contemporaneous state recognition of a full and final 1984 Indian adoption is present in the record.
    fail.

% Eligibility requires all elements of §320: a U.S. citizen parent, child under 18 during relevant period (CCA applies),
% lawful admission for permanent residence in the legal and physical custody of the citizen parent, and satisfaction of adopted-child rules.
eligibility_met :-
    cca_applies,
    us_parent_citizen,
    lpr_admitted_and_resided_with_citizen_parent,
    adopted_child_definition_met.

% Decision output and runtime harness (must be last per instructions)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a citizen of Bangladesh who was admitted to the United States in 2017 as a G-1 nonimmigrant to work as a computer operator and administrative officer with Bangladesh’s mission to the United Nations. He filed Form I-485 seeking adjustment of status. At his interview and in a written statement and affidavit, he said he could not return to Bangladesh because of increasing violence there and a risk that he and his children could be kidnapped by gangs or terrorists. He also stated he feared the Bangladeshi government might harm him because he did not return and “didn’t honor their words,” and he expressed a general concern that, having spent many years in the United States, he and his family “will be a general target to hunt down by the Islamic extremists” in Bangladesh. 

On appeal, he submitted news articles about protests and clashes between the ruling Awami League and the Bangladesh Nationalist Party, a 2021 report on terrorism in Bangladesh and a summary of an article on extremism, an affidavit restating the danger to his family and himself because of his past government and U.N. service, and an affidavit from his son stating the son has been accepted to college, cannot read or write Bangla, and would lose friends and educational/career opportunities if returned to Bangladesh. He appealed the denial of his adjustment application, asserting that these facts and evidence show he and his family cannot safely return to Bangladesh.
statutes: Section 13 is an adjustment of status category for foreign nationals who can demonstrate, in part: (1) failure to maintain A-1, A-2, G-1, or G-2 nonimmigrant status as of the application's filing date; (2) performance of diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties by the principal on behalf of the accrediting country; and (3) inability, because of compelling reasons, to return to the country that accredited the foreign national. 8 U.S.C. § 1255b(b); 8 C.F.R. § 245.3. 2 1 Pub. L. No. 85-316, 71 Stat. 642, amended by Pub. L. No. 97-116, 95 Stat. 161 (1981). The A nonimmigrant classification is for diplomats and foreign government officials (principal) as well as their immediate family members. The G nonimmigrant classification is for employees of certain international organizations (principal) and their immediate family members. See https:/ /travel.state.gov. 2 If the first three eligibility requirements are met, applicants must also establish that compelling reasons demonstrate that their adjustment would be in the national interest and would not be contrary to the national welfare, safety, or security of the United States and that they are of good moral character and admissible to the United States. Discussion of these remaining criteria is generally unnecessary in cases where the first three eligibility criteria have not been met.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (parsed from the record)
petitioner_citizen(bangladesh).
admitted_g1_2017.
employed_with_bangladesh_mission_un.
role_computer_operator.
role_administrative_officer.
filed_i485.
did_not_return_to_bangladesh.

% Statements and evidence submitted
stated_fear_general_political_violence.
stated_fear_kidnapping_by_gangs_or_terrorists.
stated_fear_govt_might_harm_for_not_returning.
submitted_affidavit_on_appeal.
submitted_news_articles_protests_and_clashes.
submitted_terrorism_report_2021.
submitted_article_summary_extremism.
submitted_son_affidavit_college_cannot_read_bangla.

% Auxiliary factual inference: because he did not return, he did not maintain G-1 status as of filing
did_not_maintain_g1_status :-
    admitted_g1_2017,
    filed_i485,
    did_not_return_to_bangladesh.

% For the class-of-eligibility rule, we model the three required elements:
% 1) failure to maintain A-1/A-2/G-1/G-2 status as of filing date
failed_to_maintain_status :-
    did_not_maintain_g1_status.

% 2) performance of diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties by the principal on behalf of the accrediting country
% The record shows employment with the Bangladesh mission to the UN in administrative/computer roles.
% We treat employment with the mission and work on behalf of the accrediting country as satisfying "performance of diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties".
worked_on_behalf_of_accrediting_country :-
    employed_with_bangladesh_mission_un.

performed_diplomatic_duties :-
    worked_on_behalf_of_accrediting_country,
    ( role_computer_operator ; role_administrative_officer ).

% 3) inability, because of compelling reasons, to return to the accrediting country
% We model "compelling_reasons" as requiring (a) a specific, targeted threat to the applicant or family,
% and (b) reliable, corroborating documentation of that targeted threat.
% The record contains assertions of fear and supporting countrywide evidence, but lacks evidence of targeted, personal threats corroborated by independent records.
specific_targeted_threat :-
    stated_fear_govt_might_harm_for_not_returning,
    evidence_of_personal_targeting.

% The record does not include independent corroboration of personal targeting (e.g., police reports, medical evidence, official findings).
evidence_of_personal_targeting :-
    fail.

reliable_corroborating_documentation :-
    corroborating_official_reports,
    corroborating_medical_or_police_records.

% No corroborating official or medical/police records were submitted in the record provided.
corroborating_official_reports :-
    fail.
corroborating_medical_or_police_records :-
    fail.

compelling_reasons :-
    specific_targeted_threat,
    reliable_corroborating_documentation.

% Eligibility requires all three primary elements
eligibility_met :-
    failed_to_maintain_status,
    performed_diplomatic_duties,
    compelling_reasons.

% Decision/reporting entrypoint (required structure)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: This matter is before the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal from the New York, New York Field Office's denial of the Applicant's Form I-212. The Applicant seeks advance permission to reapply for admission before departing the United States because he intends to pursue an immigrant visa abroad and, according to the record, his prior removal order would make him inadmissible upon departure. The record indicates that he entered the United States without inspection in 1995. Government records state that he was placed in deportation proceedings and that an Immigration Judge issued an in absentia deportation order in 1996. In 2015 he married a U.S. citizen spouse, and a Form I-130 filed on his behalf was approved in 2016. Government records further state that an Immigration Judge reopened his case in 2019, that he did not submit an application for relief, and that he was ordered removed that year. He later filed a motion to reopen requesting cancellation of removal. The Field Office denied the Form I-212 after concluding that, upon departure, he would also face inadmissibility based on failing to appear at his 2019 removal proceedings without reasonable cause. On appeal, the Applicant argues that this finding was erroneous and that the Director did not adequately consider favorable factors he presented, including his lack of criminal history, continuous employment, maintenance of Temporary Protected Status since 2001, tax filing history, hardship to his U.S. citizen spouse and minor child, and hardship to him in light of conditions in El Salvador. The record also states that any approval of the Form I-212 in these circumstances would take effect only if he departs the United States.
statutes:
Section 212(a)(9)(A)(ii) of the Act provides, in part, that a noncitizen, other than an "arriving alien," who has been ordered removed under section 240 of the Act, 8 U.S. C . § 1229a, or any other provision of law, or who departed the United States while an order of removal was outstanding, and who seeks admission within 10 years ofthe date of such departure or removal, is inadmissible. Noncitizens found inadmissible under section 212(a)(9)(A) of the Act may seek permission to reapply for admission under section 212(a)(9)(A)(iii) of the Act if, prior to the date of the reembarkation at a place outside the United States or attempt to be admitted from foreign contiguous territory, the Secretary of Homeland Security has consented to the noncitizen's reapplying for admission.

Section 212(a)(6)(B) of the Act provides that any noncitizen who, without reasonable cause, fails to attend or remain in attendance at a proceeding to determine the noncitizen's inadmissibility or deportability, and who seeks admission to the United States within five years of the noncitizen's subsequent departure or removal, is inadmissible. There is no waiver for this inadmissibility. Approval of an application for permission to reapply is discretionary, and any unfavorable factors will be weighed against the favorable factors to determine if approval of the application is warranted as a matter of discretion. Matter ofLee, 17 I&N Dec. 275, 278-79 (Reg'l Comm'r 1978). Factors to be considered in determining whether to grant permission to reapply include the basis for the prior deportation; the recency of deportation; length of residence in the United States; the applicant's moral character; the applicant's respect for law and order; evidence of the applicant's reformation and rehabilitation; family responsibilities; any inadmissibility under other sections of law; hardship involved to the applicant or others; and the need for the applicant's services in the United States. Matter ofTin, 14 I&N Dec. 371, 373-74 (Reg'l Comm'r 1973).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the petitioner and timeline (arity-0 predicates used for yes/no facts)
entered_without_inspection.
entry_year(1995).

% Removal events
in_absentia_deportation_1996.
last_removal_year(2019).    % most recent removal order entered in 2019
i212_filed_year(2021).      % Form I-212 filed in 2021

% Family / immigration petition facts
married_uscitizen_2015.
immigrant_petition_approved_2016.
minor_child_present.

% Favorable factors asserted by petitioner (arity-0 true facts)
no_criminal_history.
continuous_employment_since_2001.
tps_since_2001.
filed_income_tax_returns.
family_hardship_to_spouse_and_child.  % petitioner's claim of hardship to US citizen spouse & minor child
country_conditions_el_salvador.        % hardship to petitioner if returned

% Unfavorable facts
prior_deportation_1996.
recent_removal_2019.
motion_reopen_denied_prior_to_1997.   % IJ denied cancellation because placed in proceedings prior to 1997

% Disputed fact: whether petitioner failed to attend the 2019 proceeding.
% The field office concluded he failed to appear; the petitioner asserts that was error.
% In this model we represent that the petitioner disputes the field office finding and no
% affirmative fact of failure to attend is established. Therefore failed_to_attend_proceeding is false.
failed_to_attend_proceeding :- false.

% Helper to check whether the I-212 was filed within N years of the last removal
filed_within_years_of_last_removal(Years) :-
    last_removal_year(RemY),
    i212_filed_year(FileY),
    Diff is FileY - RemY,
    Diff =< Years.

% Inadmissibility under INA 212(a)(6)(B): failure to attend without reasonable cause,
% and seeking admission within 5 years of subsequent departure/removal. This is non-waivable.
inadmissible_6B :-
    failed_to_attend_proceeding,
    filed_within_years_of_last_removal(5).

% Inadmissibility under INA 212(a)(9)(A)(ii): ordered removed and seeks admission within 10 years
inadmissible_9A :-
    last_removal_year(_),
    filed_within_years_of_last_removal(10).

% Discretionary factors (Matter of Lee; Matter of Tin).
% Favorable factors are represented by the true facts above.
favorable_factor(no_criminal_history) :-
    no_criminal_history.
favorable_factor(continuous_employment_since_2001) :-
    continuous_employment_since_2001.
favorable_factor(tps_since_2001) :-
    tps_since_2001.
favorable_factor(filed_income_tax_returns) :-
    filed_income_tax_returns.
favorable_factor(family_hardship_to_spouse_and_child) :-
    family_hardship_to_spouse_and_child.
favorable_factor(country_conditions_el_salvador) :-
    country_conditions_el_salvador.

% Unfavorable factors derived from the record
unfavorable_factor(prior_deportation_1996) :-
    prior_deportation_1996.
unfavorable_factor(recent_removal_2019) :-
    recent_removal_2019.
unfavorable_factor(motion_reopen_denied_prior_to_1997) :-
    motion_reopen_denied_prior_to_1997.
% The existence of a removal order itself is an unfavorable factor
unfavorable_factor(prior_removal_order) :-
    last_removal_year(_).

% Count favorable and unfavorable factors
count_favorable(N) :-
    findall(F, favorable_factor(F), L),
    sort(L, SL),            % avoid double-counting any duplicates
    length(SL, N).

count_unfavorable(N) :-
    findall(F, unfavorable_factor(F), L),
    sort(L, SL),
    length(SL, N).

% Discretionary approval: if the applicant is not barred by a non-waivable ground (6B),
% then weigh favorable vs unfavorable factors. Approve if favorable factors outweigh unfavorable.
discretionary_approval_granted :-
    \+ inadmissible_6B,     % cannot grant if non-waivable bar applies
    inadmissible_9A,        % application seeks permission because 9A applies
    count_favorable(Fav),
    count_unfavorable(Unfav),
    Fav > Unfav.

% Eligibility: all conditions necessary to Accept the case
eligibility_met :-
    discretionary_approval_granted.

% Final decision structure and entrypoint (must appear exactly as specified)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a U.S. citizen who filed Form I-600 in August 2019 seeking classification of a Nigerian child (the Beneficiary) as an orphan. He asserted that the Beneficiary was abandoned by his birth mother in April 2013 and submitted, with the initial filing, a memorandum and an Affidavit of Facts from the Ministry and an intake report from the orphanage as documentation of the child’s origins and abandonment. The petition was initially approved in September 2019.

Following a consular I-604 investigation, the petitioner received a Notice of Intent to Revoke in April 2020 and, through counsel, filed a timely response. In that response he provided the Beneficiary’s court file containing various documents concerning the child’s abandonment and adoption. After the approval was later revoked, the petitioner filed a combined motion to reopen and reconsider and submitted additional evidence, including an updated personal statement, the Beneficiary’s baptismal record and vaccination card, a report from the National Human Rights Commission, a police extract from the Nigeria Police Force, several affidavits from family members, a letter from his Nigerian attorney, his spouse’s passport with entry/exit stamps, and assorted photographs.

On appeal the petitioner reasserts that the Beneficiary was abandoned and is eligible for classification as an orphan. He argues that he submitted sufficient and credible evidence of abandonment, emphasizes that unwanted children are frequently abandoned in Nigeria and that police resources are limited, and maintains that a consular investigator should have checked the Magistrate Court records after being told the Ministry had forwarded documents there. The petitioner appealed the revocation to challenge that decision and to insist that the submitted evidence establishes the Beneficiary’s origins and abandonment.
statutes:
A child who meets the definition of an orphan under section 101 (b)(1 )(F)(i) of the Act is eligible for classification as the immediate relative of a U.S. citizen. 8 C.F.R. § 204.3. An orphan is defined as a child, under the age of 16 at the time a petition is filed on his or her behalf, who is an orphan because of the death or disappearance of, abandonment or desertion by, or separation or loss from, both parents, or for whom the sole or surviving parent is incapable of providing the proper care and has in writing irrevocably released the child for emigration and adoption; who has been adopted abroad by a United States citizen, or who is coming to the United States for adoption by a United States citizen; provided, that the Secretary of Homeland Security is satisfied that proper care will be furnished if the child is admitted to the United States. Section lOl(b)(l)(F)(i) of the Act. Regarding the revocation of approved visa petitions, section 205 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1155, states the following: "The Secretary of Homeland Security may , at any time, for what he deems to be good and sufficient cause, revoke the approval of any petition approved by him under section 204. Such revocation shall be effective as of the date of approval of any such petition."

The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.3(k)(l), regarding a consular officer's Form 1-604, Determination on Child for Adoption (Form 1-604), provides, in pertinent part: An 1-604 investigation must be completed in every orphan case. The investigation must be completed by a consular officer except when the petition is properly filed at a Service office overseas, in which case it must be completed by a Service officer. ... In any instance where an 1-604 investigation reveals negative information sufficient to sustain a denial or revocation, the investigation report, supporting documentation, and petition shall be forwarded to the appropriate Service office for action. Depending on the circumstances surrounding the case, the 1-604 investigation shall include, but shall not necessarily be limited to, document checks, telephonic checks, interview(s) with the natural parent(s), and/or a field investigation. The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.3(b) states, in pertinent part, the following: Abandonment by both parents means that the parents have willfully forsaken all parental rights, obligations, and claims to the child, as well as all control over and possession of the child, without intending to transfer or without transferring these rights to any specific persons.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
petitioner_is_us_citizen.
petition_filed_aug_2019.
petition_filed_within_age_limit.    % filing occurred while child would be under 16 (assumed by record timing)
abandoned_claim_asserted.           % petitioner asserts abandonment in April 2013
consular_investigation_completed.
consular_investigation_raised_concerns.  % consular officer questioned provenance/accuracy
documents_have_inconsistent_dates.  % some submitted documents were dated 2015, inconsistent with claimed abandonment
ministry_cannot_produce_original_case_records. % ministry did not retain original case files for verification
director_revoked_initial_approval.
petitioner_responded_noir.
petitioner_submitted_additional_evidence. % baptismal record, vaccination card, affidavits, photos, etc.
additional_evidence_present.
additional_evidence_failed_to_corroborate. % additional evidence did not resolve authenticity / provenance concerns

% Documents are considered verified only if no consular concerns about provenance, dates, or missing originals
documents_verified :-
    \+ consular_investigation_raised_concerns,
    \+ documents_have_inconsistent_dates,
    \+ ministry_cannot_produce_original_case_records.

% Abandonment is proved only if claim is asserted and documents are verified (consular investigation corroborates)
abandonment_proved :-
    abandoned_claim_asserted,
    documents_verified.

% Orphan definition requires that the child was under 16 at filing and abandonment is proved
orphan_definition_met :-
    petition_filed_within_age_limit,
    abandonment_proved.

% Secretary's satisfaction mirrors the requirement that evidence be verified and reliable
secretary_satisfied :-
    documents_verified.

% Eligibility requires petitioner is US citizen, orphan definition met, and Secretary satisfied
eligibility_met :-
    petitioner_is_us_citizen,
    orphan_definition_met,
    secretary_satisfied.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-

               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a citizen of Nigeria who entered the United States in 2017 on a visitor visa. He married A‑J‑, a U.S. citizen, in 2018 and filed a VAWA self‑petition in 2018 based on that marriage. His filing included a Nigerian High Court divorce decree that purported to terminate his earlier marriage to I‑E‑. The Vermont Service Center issued a request for evidence concerning the termination of that prior marriage and later denied the petition. 

On appeal, the petitioner contends the Service Center violated his due‑process rights by not giving him an opportunity to respond to derogatory information about the divorce decree. He asserts that his former spouse initiated and completed the divorce in Nigeria while he was already in the United States, and that, if given another RFE or a notice of intent to deny, he could have investigated and addressed perceived inconsistencies in the record. He did not submit additional evidence with his appeal to remedy the issues raised about the divorce document.
statutes: A petitioner who is the spouse of a U.S. citizen may self-petition for immigrant classification if the petitioner demonstrates, in part, that they entered the marriage with the U.S. citizen spouse in good faith and the petitioner was battered or subjected to extreme cruelty perpetrated by the qualifying relative. Section 204(a)(l)(A)(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(l). A petitioner must also show that they are eligible to be classified as an immediate relative under section 20l(b)(2)(A)(i) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. 1151 (b)(2)(A)(i). Id. Among other things, a petitioner must submit evidence ofthe qualifying marital relationship in the form of a marriage certificate and proof of the termination of all prior marriages for the petitioner and the abuser. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(b )(2), ( c )(2)(ii). Petitioners are "encouraged to submit primary evidence whenever possible," but may submit any relevant, credible evidence to establish eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(i). U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) determines, in our sole discretion, what evidence is credible and the weight to give to such evidence. Section 204(a)(l)(J) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(i).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

text:
The petitioner is a goalkeeper director who filed Form I-140 seeking classification based on extraordinary ability and intends to continue working as a goalkeeper director and coach in the United States. The Nebraska Service Center director denied the petition, finding that the petitioner did not meet the evidentiary criteria the director examined. The petitioner claimed to meet four specific criteria related to: published material about his work; serving as a judge of others’ work; serving in a leading or critical role for organizations or establishments; and commanding a high salary or other significantly high remuneration.

On appeal, the petitioner maintains his eligibility and explains why he believes the director’s decision was erroneous. He asserts that the director did not review and consider evidence he submitted, improperly disregarded relevant documents, and failed to acknowledge and analyze all the submitted materials. Regarding published materials, the petitioner says he submitted articles and circulation statistics for the publications in which the articles appeared; he acknowledges some articles lacked an author but contends that other submitted articles did identify an author and that circulation information was provided. Concerning the judging criterion, the petitioner states he provided a certificate from the city showing he served for four years as a judge on a seven-member panel, a letter from the town hall attesting to that role, a copy of the jury selection rules, and other supporting evidence, and he contends the director did not address that evidence with specificity. For the leading-or-critical-role claim, the petitioner reports he submitted eleven recommendation letters and nine supporting exhibits describing his participation in creating and executing training methodology, improving goalkeeping methodology and scouting, and other contributions, and he argues those materials were not given proper consideration. For the high-salary claim, the petitioner submitted a job offer and contends the director improperly discounted the offer by treating it as insufficient evidence of his ability to command a high salary. Finally, the petitioner notes that he previously obtained O-1 nonimmigrant status and asserts that the director failed to afford consideration to that prior approval. These contentions form the basis of his appeal.
statutes:
Section
203(b )(1 )(A) of the Act makes visas available to immigrants with extraordinary ability if: (i) the [ noncitizen] has extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics which has been demonstrated by sustained national or international acclaim and whose achievements have been recognized in the field through extensive documentation, (ii) the [noncitizen] seeks to enter the United States to continue work in the area of extraordinary ability, and (iii) the [noncitizen's] entry into the United States will substantially benefit prospectively the United States.

The term "extraordinary ability" refers only to those individuals in "that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2). The implementing regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) sets forth a multi-part analysis. First, a petitioner can demonstrate recognition of their achievements in the field through a one-time achievement (that is, a major, internationally recognized award). If that petitioner does not submit this evidence, then they must provide sufficient qualifying documentation that meets at least three of the ten criteria listed at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i) - (x) (including items such as awards, published material in certain media, and scholarly articles). Where a petitioner meets these initial evidence requirements, we then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination and assess whether the record shows sustained national or international acclaim and demonstrates that the individual is among the small percentage at the very top of the field of endeavor. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F .3d 1115 (9th Cir. 2010). ( discussing a two-part review where the documentation is fust counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination); see also Visinscaia v. Beers, 4 F. Supp. 3d 126, 131-32 (D.D.C. 2013); Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
petitioner_goalkeeper_director.
intends_to_continue_work.
filed_i140.
% Evidence submitted
article_with_circulation.             % Newspaper and other articles with circulation statistics
some_articles_missing_author_or_url.  % Some articles lacked author or legible URL (not dispositive)
author_attributed_articles.           % Some articles had author attribution
certificate_judge.                    % Certificate indicating service as judge for 4 years on a 7-member panel
town_hall_letter_with_rules.          % Town-hall letter attesting judging role and providing panel rules
served_on_panel_four_years.
letters_support_leading_role.         % Eleven letters addressing leading/critical role
supporting_exhibits_for_leading_role. % Nine supporting exhibits for leading/critical role
compensation_documentation_including_offer. % Compensation documentation including an employment offer
prior_o1_approval.                    % Previously obtained O-1 classification based on extraordinary ability

% USCIS regulatory framework encoded
% Criterion (iii) — published materials about the person in major media (satisfied here by circulation + authored articles)
criterion(iii) :-
    article_with_circulation,
    author_attributed_articles.

% Criterion (iv) — judging the work of others (satisfied here by certificate + town hall attestation)
criterion(iv) :-
    certificate_judge,
    town_hall_letter_with_rules,
    served_on_panel_four_years.

% Criterion (viii) — leading or critical role for organizations with distinguished reputations
criterion(viii) :-
    letters_support_leading_role,
    supporting_exhibits_for_leading_role.

% Criterion (ix) — commanding a high salary or other significantly high remuneration
% (here taken as satisfied by compensation documentation; an offer plus compensation records are present)
criterion(ix) :-
    compensation_documentation_including_offer.

% Count qualifying criteria (must meet at least three of the ten)
initial_requirements_met :-
    findall(C, criterion(C), L),
    length(L, N),
    N >= 3.

% Final merits determination (Kazarian two-step): consider totality of the record
% We model sustained national/international acclaim as supported where (a) prior O-1 approval exists,
% (b) initial evidentiary criteria are met, and (c) there is documentary publicity (articles with circulation).
sustained_acclaim :-
    prior_o1_approval,
    initial_requirements_met,
    article_with_circulation.

% The petitioner states intent to continue working in the U.S. in the area of extraordinary ability
% and the record includes evidence of prospective benefit (coaching/directing).
prospective_benefit.

% Eligibility requires the initial evidentiary showing, sustained acclaim on the merits,
% intent to continue work, and prospective benefit to the United States.
eligibility_met :-
    initial_requirements_met,
    sustained_acclaim,
    intends_to_continue_work,
    prospective_benefit.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is an attorney who filed Form I-140 seeking classification as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or of exceptional ability and requesting a national interest waiver of the job offer requirement. In the initial petition the petitioner described an intention to build a legal career in the United States as a legal and tax expert in sustainable investment in the financial services sector and to establish a private equity firm to attract U.S. private investments in renewable energy infrastructure in emerging and developing countries.

With the initial filing the petitioner submitted supporting materials including: an article from PwC about environmental, social, and governance (ESG) issues; a 2021 Edelman Trust Barometer report on ESG investing attitudes; the U.S. Department of Commerce Strategic Plan 2022–2026; an International Energy Agency report on financing clean energy transitions in emerging and developing economies; the petitioner’s résumé; documentation of educational degrees, diplomas, and certificates; documentation of service as a student marshal and an invitation to speak at a law school event; recommendation letters; employment-related letters; and articles and op-eds authored by the petitioner.

The Texas Service Center issued a request for additional evidence (RFE) asking for further documentation about the proposed endeavor, its importance, and how the petitioner was positioned to advance it. In response to the RFE the petitioner submitted a business proposal for “I Saving Bank,” a list of community development financial institutions (CDFIs) in Massachusetts, schedules of qualifications and job descriptions for roles at I Saving Bank, a Massachusetts Department of Environmental Protection report on economically distressed communities, extracts of U.S. strategic plans, a UN climate panel report, an International Energy Agency report, the 2020 Sustainable Development Report, documentation of volunteer work, evidence of an application for a community leadership fund, correspondence with biogas digester manufacturers, correspondence with bank representatives, a draft memorandum of understanding with suppliers for renewable energy product training, a fundraising flyer and correspondence with potential investors, a record of a videoconference with a professional mentor, a publication by the petitioner on proposed ESG disclosure requirements, an article by the petitioner on small business cashflow management post–COVID‑19, and a copy of an appointment to a Banking and Finance Committee.

After the RFE response, the petitioner’s described plans shifted from the initial legal/private-equity focus to activities that included ownership and operation of a distributor of biogas digesters sourced from Chinese manufacturers for East Africa and operating a community savings bank. Over the course of the proceedings the petitioner advanced multiple endeavors related to banking or finance rather than a single, consistently described project. The petitioner also submitted correspondence noting confusion about a USCIS Case Status Online portal update that appeared to show issuance of a second RFE.

The petitioner filed an appeal of the Texas Service Center’s adverse decision. The appeal challenges the denial of the petitioner’s request for a national interest waiver.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. Whilst neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," we set forth a framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions in the precedent decision Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec . 884 (AAO 2016). Dhanasar states that USCIS may as a matter of discretion grant a national interest waiver of the job offer, and thus of the labor certification, to a petitioner

classified in the EB-2 category if they demonstrate that (1) the noncitizen' s proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance, (2) the noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor, and (3) that on balance it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification. The first prong, substantial merit and national importance, focuses on the specific endeavor that the noncitizen proposes to undertake. The endeavor's merit may be demonstrated in a range of areas such as business, entrepreneurialism, science, technology, culture, health, or education. In determining whether the proposed endeavor has national importance, we consider its potential prospective impact. The second prong shifts the focus from the proposed endeavor to the noncitizen. To determine whether the noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor, we consider factors including but not limited to the individual's education, skills, knowledge, and record of success in related or similar efforts. A model or plan for future activities, progress towards achieving the proposed endeavor, and the interest of potential customers, users, investors, or other relevant entities or individuals are also key considerations. The third prong requires the petitioner to demonstrate that, on balance of applicable factors, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification. USCIS may evaluate factors such as whether, in light of the nature of the noncitizen' s qualification or the proposed endeavor, it would be impractical either for the noncitizen to secure a job offer or for the petition to obtain a labor certification; whether, even assuming that other qualified U.S. workers are available, the United States would still benefit from the noncitizen's contributions; and whether the national interest in the noncitizen's contributions is sufficiently urgent to warrant forgoing the labor certification process. Each of the factors considered must, taken together, indicate that on balance it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the petitioner and the filings
petitioner_is_attorney.
filed_i140.
seeking_eb2.
requested_national_interest_waiver.

% Proposed endeavor as initially described in the petition
proposed_endeavor_initial.  % building legal/tax career in sustainable investment and forming private equity firm for renewable infrastructure

% Evidence submitted with the initial filing
resume_submitted.
educational_degrees_documented.
student_marshal_and_speaking_invitation.
recommendation_letters_submitted.
employment_letters_submitted.
authored_articles_and_opeds_submitted.
published_materials_on_esg_and_policy_submitted.  % PwC, Edelman, DOC plan, IEA report, etc.

% RFE and response facts
rfe_issued.
rfe_response_submitted.
rfe_response_deviated.  % RFE response changed/expanded the described endeavor (I Saving Bank, CDFIs, local bank model, etc.)

% Materials submitted with the RFE response
business_proposal_isaving_bank.
cdfi_list_submitted.
job_descriptions_isaving_bank.
ma_dep_report_on_distressed_communities.
strategic_and_international_reports_submitted.
volunteer_work_documented.
correspondence_with_manufacturers_and_banks.
draft_mou_for_training_suppliers.
fundraising_materials_and_investor_correspondence.
teleconference_with_mentor_recorded.
additional_publications_submitted.
appointment_to_banking_body_reported.

% EB-2 qualification rules
% Petitioner qualifies for EB-2 if they have an advanced degree (or exceptional ability).
% Here the record shows educational degrees and related documentation.
has_advanced_degree.

eb2_qualification :-
    has_advanced_degree.

% Dhanasar prong 1: proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance
% The initial endeavor (sustainable investment, renewable infrastructure) draws on submitted policy and sector materials which indicate national importance.
prong1_substantial_merit :-
    proposed_endeavor_initial,
    (   authored_articles_and_opeds_submitted
    ;   published_materials_on_esg_and_policy_submitted
    ).

% If the RFE response significantly changes the endeavor, the coherence of the specific endeavor may be undermined.
endeavor_significantly_deviated :-
    rfe_issued,
    rfe_response_submitted,
    rfe_response_deviated.

% We model national importance as present when the initial endeavor and sector materials are in the record and there is no significant deviation undermining the specific proposed endeavor.
prong1_national_importance :-
    proposed_endeavor_initial,
    published_materials_on_esg_and_policy_submitted,
    \+ endeavor_significantly_deviated.

prong1 :-
    prong1_substantial_merit,
    prong1_national_importance.

% Dhanasar prong 2: the noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor
% Factors include education, skills, record of success, a model/plan, progress, and interest of potential investors/users.
evidence_of_education_and_skill :-
    has_advanced_degree,
    resume_submitted,
    recommendation_letters_submitted.

evidence_of_record_and_publications :-
    authored_articles_and_opeds_submitted;
    additional_publications_submitted.

evidence_of_model_or_plan :-
    business_proposal_isaving_bank;
    draft_mou_for_training_suppliers.  % presence of a model or plan, possibly in RFE response

evidence_of_investor_interest :-
    fundraising_materials_and_investor_correspondence;
    correspondence_with_manufacturers_and_banks.

% For well-positioned we require the above factors AND that the endeavor described is coherent/consistent across filings.
consistent_endeavor :-
    proposed_endeavor_initial,
    \+ rfe_response_deviated.

well_positioned :-
    evidence_of_education_and_skill,
    evidence_of_record_and_publications,
    evidence_of_model_or_plan,
    evidence_of_investor_interest,
    consistent_endeavor.

% Dhanasar prong 3: on balance it would be beneficial to waive the job offer and labor certification
% This can be true when the petitioner shows that labor certification is impractical or that the petitioner provides unique/urgent contributions.
impractical_to_obtain_labor_certification :-
    % In this record we do not have evidence that labor certification is impractical.
    fail.

unique_or_urgent_contribution :-
    % The initial materials indicate potential national relevance, but if the endeavor is inconsistent across filings, urgency/uniqueness is weakened.
    proposed_endeavor_initial,
    published_materials_on_esg_and_policy_submitted,
    \+ endeavor_significantly_deviated.

prong3 :-
    (   unique_or_urgent_contribution
    ;   impractical_to_obtain_labor_certification
    ).

% Eligibility: all conditions necessary to Accept the case
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_qualification,
    prong1,
    well_positioned,
    prong3.

% Decision structure required by the specification
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a minor who, in 2023 at age twelve, was the subject of a juvenile court Order Appointing Sole Legal Custodian in Tennessee. That order placed the petitioner and his siblings in their mother’s custody and stated it would not be in the petitioner’s best interests to be returned to Guatemala, the family’s country of nationality. The juvenile court’s order noted that reunification with the children’s respective biological fathers was not viable: the petitioner’s father was deceased, and the father of the petitioner’s siblings had totally and completely abandoned the children.

The petitioner filed Form I-360 seeking classification as a special immigrant juvenile. The National Benefits Center director denied the petition, concluding the record did not establish that the juvenile court had made a qualifying determination regarding parental reunification; specifically, the director determined the original court order did not sufficiently explain how the father’s death constituted abandonment or a similar basis under state law.

In response, the petitioner appealed and submitted an Amended Order Appointing Sole Legal Custodian that the juvenile court issued in 2024, nunc pro tunc to the 2023 date of the original order. The amended order expressly states that reunification with the fathers was not viable because the petitioner’s father “abandoned his children by virtue of his death.” The petitioner argued on appeal that this amended order addresses the director’s concerns and contains the findings needed to support the petition.
statutes: To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b). Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.11 ( c )(1 ). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(2).

text:
The petitioner filed an I-526 immigrant investor petition in 2016, stating that on October 19, 2016 she invested $500,000 in a new commercial enterprise (NCE) sponsored by a USCIS-designated regional center. According to the petition and the Confidential Private Placement Memorandum, the NCE planned to raise $8,500,000 from 17 immigrant investors and invest in a job‑creating entity (JCE) that would develop and operate a learning facility in Florida providing educational and therapeutic services to children with autism and other developmental delays.

The petitioner indicated the investment was made in a targeted employment area (TEA). Her business plan originally proposed a project at an initial site in Florida, but she states that the original site was abandoned because of insufficient development funds and resulting delays. She and the JCE subsequently purchased an existing alternate facility in Florida in 2017 and completed renovation and construction at that site.

In response to a request for evidence (RFE) and in support of her petition and appeal, the petitioner submitted bank statements and a letter from the JCE manager documenting transfers from her personal bank account to the NCE and then to the JCE, which she contends shows she placed the required capital at risk. She also submitted a 2017 letter from the Florida Department of Economic Opportunity verifying that the alternate project site qualified as a TEA based on the area’s 2016 annual average unemployment rate.

To document the JCE’s operations, job creation, and project progress, the petitioner provided various records including: the NCE’s 2018 federal tax return; employer quarterly reports for the JCE from 2017–2019; wage and tax statements issued to JCE employees for 2017–2018; JCE financial statements for 2016–2019; project photographs; an invoice; real estate tax bills; a local business tax receipt; a 2019 letter from the project renovation manager; an interior renovation permit; and a list of JCE employees. On appeal she submitted additional materials, including website printouts, a schedule of the JCE’s capital expenditures, a 2020 letter from the JCE manager, and an Addendum to the EB-5 Economic Impact Analysis dated December 2019 that uses actual capital expenditures and updated revenue projections.

The petitioner is appealing the denial of her I-526 petition and seeks review of the record that includes the evidence summarized above, asserting that the submitted documents establish her investment and the project’s TEA status, progress, and job-creating activities.
statutes:
Generally, an immigrant investor must invest at least $1,000,000 in capital in an NCE that creates not fewer than 10 jobs. An exception exists if the immigrant investor invests their capital in an NCE that 1 On March 15, 2022, the EB-5 Reform and Integrity Act of 2022 was signed into law , revising general eligibility requirements, substantially reforming and codifying the Regional Center Program in section 203(b)(5) of the Act, and adding significant new integrity provisions. See section 203(b )(5) of the Act, 8 U .S.C. § 1153(b )(5) (2022). As the Petitioner had filed her petition in 2016, the relevant law then in existence governs this appellate adjudication.

is principally doing business in and creates jobs in a targeted employment area (TEA). In such a case, the immigrant investor must invest a minimum of $500,000 in capital. 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(5)(C)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(f)(2) (2016). To establish eligibility for the reduced minimum investment threshold of $500,000, the immigrant investor must invest their capital in an NCE that is principally doing business in and creates jobs in a rural area or an area of high unemployment. 8 U.S.C. § l 153(b)(5)(B)(i)-(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(i)(6) (2016). Applicable statute and regulations define a TEA as, at the time ofinvestment, a rural area or an area that has experienced unemployment of at least 150 percent of the national average rate. 8 U.S.C. § l 153(b)(5)(B)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e). To show that the petitioner has invested or is actively in the process of investing the required amount of capital, the petition must be accompanied by evidence that the petitioner has placed the required amount of capital at risk for the purpose of generating a return on the capital placed at risk. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(i)(2). Evidence of mere intent to invest, or of prospective investment arrangements entailing no present commitment, will not suffice to show that the petitioner is actively in the process of investing. Id. The petitioner must show actual commitment of the required amount of capital. Id. For the capital to be "at risk" there must be a risk of loss and a chance for gain. See Matter ofIzwnmi, 22 I&N Dec. 169, 186-87 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998). To demonstrate that the petitioner has placed such capital at risk for the purpose ofgenerating a return, the petitioner must first present evidence that they have made a qualifying investment of the minimum required amount ofcapital. The regulations define "invest" to mean a contribution ofcapital. 8 C .F.R. § 204.6( e). However, a contribution of capital in exchange for a note, bond, convertible debt, obligation, or any other debt arrangement between the alien entrepreneur and the NCE does not constitute a contribution of capital and, thus, does not constitute a qualifying investment. Id. As required by 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(i)(4)(i), the petition must establish that the investment of the required amount of capital in an NCE will create full-time positions for at least 10 qualifying employees within two years. See also 8 U.S.C. § l 153(b)(5)(A)(ii). For purposes of the Form I-526 adjudication and the job creation requirements, the two-year period described in 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(i)(4)(i)(B) is deemed to commence six months after the adjudication of the Form I-526. According to 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(i)(4)(i), to show that an NCE will create full-time positions for at least 10 qualifying employees within two years, the petition must be accompanied by: (A) Documentation consisting of photocopies of relevant tax records, Forms I-9, or other similar documents for ten (10) qualifying employees, if such employees have already been hired following the establishment of the new commercial enterprise; or (B) A copy of a comprehensive business plan showing that, due to the nature and projected size of the new commercial enterprise, the need for fewer than ten (10) qualifying employees will result, including approximate dates, within the next two years, and when such employees will be hired. A petition is not required to demonstrate that 10 full-time positions for qualifying employees have already been created by the NCE. However, where the jobs have not already been created, the petition must include a comprehensive business plan demonstrating the need for at least 10 employees within 2

the next two years. Matter ofHo explained that a comprehensive business plan must be sufficiently detailed to permit USCIS to draw reasonable inferences about job-creation potential. Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. 206, 213 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998). Mere conclusory assertions, however, do not enable USCIS to determine whether the job-creation projections are any more reliable than hopeful speculation. Id. Most importantly, the business plan must be credible. Id. Petitioners investing in an NCE within a regional center may rely on economic methodologies to demonstrate that the investment will create indirect jobs as a result of the investment in the NCE, but such methodologies must be reasonable. See Pub. L. No. 102-395; 8 C.F.R. §§ 204.6(e), (i)(4)(iii), (m)(7)(ii). The petitioners must provide sufficient evidence for USCIS to determine whether methodologies used are reasonable. Indirect jobs are those that are held outside of the NCE but are created as a result of the NCE. See generally 6 USCIS Policy Manual G.2(D)(4), https://www.uscis.gov/policy-manual/volume-6-part-g­ chapter-2. For example, indirect jobs include, but are not limited to, those held by employees of the job-creating entity (JCE) (when the JCE is not the NCE) as well as employees of producers of materials, equipment, or services used by the NCE or the JCE. Id. A petitioner must establish eligibility at the time of filing; a petition cannot be approved at a future date after the petitioner becomes eligible under a new set of facts. See Matter ofKatigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45, 49 (Comm. 1971). Therefore, a petitioner may not make material changes to a petition that has already been filed in an effort to make an apparently deficient petition conform to USCIS requirements. Matter ofIzwnmi, 22 I&N Dec. at 175; see also 8 C.F .R. § 103 .2(b)(1 ). Changes that occur in accordance with a business plan and other supporting documents as filed will generally not be considered material. In general, if, at the time of adjudication, the petitioner is asserting eligibility under a materially different set of facts that did not exist when they filed the immigrant petition, the petitioner must file a new immigrant petition. 2 See generally 6 USCIS Policy Manual, supra at G.3(C)(l).

text:
The petitioner is a woman who applied to adjust status to that of a lawful permanent resident and filed Form I-601 seeking a waiver of inadmissibility. She conceded and the record shows that she presented a fraudulent passport to enter the United States, and the New York City Field Office denied her waiver application. The petitioner does not contest her inadmissibility; instead, she appealed the denial, asserting that the adjudicator erred by not considering all relevant factors when evaluating the hardship her U.S. citizen mother would suffer if the petitioner were refused admission.

In her statements and supporting documents, the petitioner describes a long-standing, close caregiving relationship with her mother. She says they have lived together for most of the mother's time in the United States (the mother entered in 1998 and they have lived together except for a four-year period in the 1990s). The petitioner reports that she left an abusive relationship to come to the United States and that she provides daily and ongoing support to her mother: assisting with health-care decisions, taking her to medical appointments, ensuring she takes prescribed medications (including helping with multiple daily insulin injections), occasionally bathing her, and performing household duties such as cooking, cleaning, grocery shopping, and laundry. The petitioner states she earns about $300 per week and provides financial assistance to her mother; she also says her brother is married with a family and does not earn much, and that the petitioner’s other sibling is estranged from their mother.

The petitioner’s appeal supplements the record with additional evidence and details about her mother’s circumstances. She and her mother submitted updated affidavits describing the mother’s advanced age (69), recent retirement, and reliance on Social Security and Medicare rather than a pension. They describe the mother’s multiple physical and mental health conditions and limitations—conditions the petitioner says require continuous supervision and hands-on care. The petitioner asserts that the mother requires help for conditions including heart disease, Type II diabetes (requiring insulin two to three times daily), skin cancer, migraines, anxiety and depression, anemia, hypertension, a gallbladder mass, GERD, endometriosis, vertigo, insomnia, sleep apnea, back and knee pain, advanced osteoarthritis, and a walking imbalance that leads to frequent falls. The petitioner states she has provided the mother’s medications, assisted with long-term health care, and that the mother has been under care for more than ten years.

To support her appeal, the petitioner submitted evidence that includes: her and her mother’s updated affidavits; medical documentation listing the mother’s diagnoses, medications, and the treating provider’s statement that the mother has received care for over a decade and requires ongoing supervision; a psychological evaluation describing the mother’s history of abusive relationships, long-term antidepressant use, and a diagnosis of Major Depressive Disorder; updated financial documentation showing reduced retirement income and accrued debt; country-conditions information for Jamaica addressing the availability and quality of healthcare; and earlier-submitted materials such as a letter of support, the petitioner’s Form I-94, identity documents, and proof of the petitioner’s employment and income. The petitioner argues that, taken together, this new and prior evidence demonstrates additional hardship to her mother if the petitioner is denied admission, and she appealed on the ground that the adjudicator did not sufficiently consider these factors.
statutes:
Section 212(a)(6)(C)(i) of the Act renders inadmissible any noncitizen who, by fraud or willfully misrepresenting a material fact, seeks to procure ( or has sought to procure or has procured) a visa, other documentation, admission into the United States, or other benefit provided under the Act. Section 212(i) of the Act provides a waiver of the above ground of inadmissibility if refusal of admission would result in extreme hardship to a U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident spouse or parent of the noncitizen. If a noncitizen demonstrates the existence ofthe required hardship, then they must also show they merit a favorable exercise of discretion. Id. A determination of whether denial of admission will result in extreme hardship depends on the facts and circumstances of each case. Matter of Cervantes-Gonzalez, 22 l&N Dec. 560, 565 (BIA 1999). We recognize that some degree of hardship to qualifying relatives is present in most cases; however, to be considered "extreme," the hardship must exceed that which is usual or expected. See Matter of Pilch, 21 l&N Dec. 627, 630-31 (BIA 1996) (finding that factors such as economic detriment, severing family and community ties, loss of current employment, and cultural readjustment were the "common result of deportation" and did not alone constitute extreme hardship). In determining whether extreme

hardship exists, individual hardship factors that may not rise to the level of extreme must also be considered in the aggregate. Matter ofIge, 20 I&N Dec. 880, 882 (BIA 1994).

label: Accepted

```prolog
% Facts derived from the record (all yes/no predicates use arity 0)
inadmissible_presented_fraudulent_passport.

qualifying_relative_is_parent.
qualifying_relative_is_mother.
mother_us_citizen.

mother_age_69.
mother_lives_with_petitioner_longterm.
period_apart_in_1990s. % historical, not current

petitioner_left_abusive_relationship.

petitioner_provides_daily_care.
petitioner_assists_with_medications.
petitioner_assists_with_insulin.
petitioner_assists_with_bathing.
petitioner_cooks_cleans_shops_laundry.
petitioner_helps_with_healthcare_decisions.
petitioner_helps_with_financial_obligations.

petitioner_income_approx_300_per_week.
mother_relies_on_social_security.
mother_relies_on_medicare.
mother_has_accrued_significant_debt.
mother_recently_retired.
son_has_family_and_low_income.
mother_estranged_from_one_daughter.
evidence_submitted_initially.
evidence_submitted_on_appeal.
there_is_medical_evidence.
there_is_psychological_evaluation.

% Mother's medical and psychological conditions (treated as true/false predicates)
mother_has_type2_diabetes.
mother_requires_multiple_daily_insulin_injections.
mother_has_heart_disease.
mother_has_skin_cancer.

mother_has_GERD.
mother_has_endometriosis.
mother_has_vertigo.
mother_has_insomnia.
mother_has_sleep_apnea.
```
