```prolog
mother_has_walking_imbalance_and_falls.
mother_has_long_term_clinical_depression.
mother_has_major_depressive_disorder.
psych_eval_states_separation_would_high_risk_worsen_depression.

% No affirmative record in the file of criminal convictions or national-security issues
% (We represent absence of aggravating evidence as negative facts using explicit negation predicates)
no_evidence_of_criminal_conviction.
no_evidence_of_national_security_risk.

% Major hardship factor rules: a factor is "major" if grounded in the record facts.
major_factor(caregiving_dependence) :-
    petitioner_provides_daily_care.

major_factor(severe_medical_needs) :-
    (   mother_requires_multiple_daily_insulin_injections
    ;   mother_has_heart_disease
    ;   mother_has_advanced_osteoarthritis
    ;   mother_has_walking_imbalance_and_falls
    ).

major_factor(mental_health_risk) :-
    (   mother_has_long_term_clinical_depression
    ;   mother_has_major_depressive_disorder
    ;   psych_eval_states_separation_would_high_risk_worsen_depression
    ).

major_factor(lack_of_alternative_caregiver) :-
    son_has_family_and_low_income,
    mother_estranged_from_one_daughter.

major_factor(financial_vulnerability) :-
    petitioner_income_approx_300_per_week,
    mother_has_accrued_significant_debt,
    mother_relies_on_social_security.

% Minor hardship factors (support aggregate analysis)
minor_factor(advanced_age) :-
    mother_age_69.

minor_factor(recent_retirement) :-
    mother_recently_retired.

minor_factor(reliance_on_medicare) :-
    mother_relies_on_medicare.

minor_factor(long_term_cohabitation) :-
    mother_lives_with_petitioner_longterm.

% Count helpers
count_major_factors(Count) :-
    findall(F, major_factor(F), L),
    sort(L, Luniq),
    length(Luniq, Count).

count_minor_factors(Count) :-
    findall(F, minor_factor(F), L),
    sort(L, Luniq),
    length(Luniq, Count).

% Legal standard: extreme hardship considered in aggregate; here we model thresholds consistent with the record.
% We determine extreme hardship if:
% - two or more major factors are present; OR
% - one major factor plus two or more minor factors are present.
extreme_hardship :-
    count_major_factors(MajorCount),
    MajorCount >= 2.
extreme_hardship :-
    count_major_factors(1),
    count_minor_factors(MinorCount),
    MinorCount >= 2.

% Positive equities / favorable discretionary considerations
% We consider there to be positive equities where the petitioner provides essential care, has strong family ties,
% and has supplemented the record with evidence on appeal.
positive_equities :-
    petitioner_provides_daily_care,
    mother_lives_with_petitioner_longterm,
    evidence_submitted_on_appeal.

% No aggravating factors (based on record)
no_aggravating_factors :-
    no_evidence_of_criminal_conviction,
    no_evidence_of_national_security_risk.

% Favorable exercise of discretion: applicant merits it where extreme hardship is shown and there are positive equities
% and no aggravating factors.
favorable_exercise_of_discretion :-
    extreme_hardship,
    positive_equities,
    no_aggravating_factors.

% Eligibility: waived inadmissibility under INA 212(i) requires inadmissibility, extreme hardship to a qualifying
% relative (parent or spouse) and a favorable exercise of discretion.
eligibility_met :-
    inadmissible_presented_fraudulent_passport,
    qualifying_relative_is_parent,
    extreme_hardship,
    favorable_exercise_of_discretion.

% Decision/run structure required by the prompt
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Petitioner is a cybersecurity consultant seeking EB-2 immigrant classification as an individual of exceptional ability and a waiver of the job-offer requirement. The Nebraska Service Center Director denied the petition, and the matter was appealed to the Administrative Appeals Office. On appeal, the Petitioner argues that the Director did not properly consider all of the evidence he submitted in response to a notice of intent to deny. He also argues that his documentation shows that ISC2 membership requires more than payment of annual fees, and that the record demonstrates the significance of his ISC2 membership, his Certified Ethical Hacker recognition, and the reputation of the CISSP credential in the cybersecurity field. The record on appeal does not include additional evidence. Previously submitted materials describe ISC2 as a nonprofit organization focused on training and certifications for cybersecurity professionals, including CISSP certification. The record includes a 2021 CISSP certificate stating that the (ISC)2 Board of Directors awarded the Petitioner the credential after he met certification requirements that included a professional-experience prerequisite, adoption of the organization's code of ethics, and successful completion of the required examination, along with a letter from the president of a local ISC2 chapter describing CISSP prerequisites as at least five years of full-time security work in two or more ISC2 information domains and a four-hour exam. On appeal, the Petitioner also referenced previously submitted resumes of other cybersecurity professionals to argue that CISSP is not commonly held in the field. Other materials in the record state that about 160,000 individuals hold the CISSP credential, show that the Petitioner obtained a GIAC Enterprise Vulnerability Assessor certification in November 2021, and describe that certification as validating technical vulnerability-assessment skills and practical approaches to enterprise security. The record also includes two EC-Council letters, one congratulating him on being a Certified Ethical Hacker and another stating that he advanced to the final round of the CEH II 2021 competition.
statutes:
To qualify for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, a petitioner must establish they are an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(A) of the Act. Exceptional ability means a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business. 8 C.F.R . § 204.5(k)(2). A petitioner must initially submit documentation that satisfies at least three of six categories of evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F). 1 Meeting at least three criteria, however, does not, in and of itself, establish eligibility for this classification. 2 If a petitioner does so, we will then conduct a final merits determination to decide whether the evidence 1 If these types of evidence do not readily apply to the individual's occupation, a petitioner may submit comparable evidence to establish their eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(iii). 2 USCIS has previously confinned the applicability of this two-part adjudicative approach in the context of individuals of exceptional ability. 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.5(B)(2), https://www.uscis.gov/policy-manual.

in its totality shows that they are recognized as having a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the field. Ifa petitioner establishes eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then demonstrate that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion,3 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the case record (all true/false predicates use arity 0)
% Evidence submitted by petitioner
has_experience.            % resumes showing about ten years of full‑time experience
has_membership.            % membership in (ISC)2 and identified professional associations
has_certifications.        % CISSP, GEVA, CEH and CEH II achievement
has_letters.               % letters describing CISSP and accomplishments (incl. local chapter president)
has_comparable_resumes.    % copies of other cybersecurity professionals' resumes submitted for comparison
license_asserted.          % petitioner asserted he holds a license to practice

% Map evidence to the regulatory six categories (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F)).
% We label the six categories as: experience, academic_record, license, membership, recognition, comparable.
% Only those categories for which the record contains evidence are considered satisfied.

category_satisfied(experience) :-
    has_experience.

% No formal academic degree record was supplied in the facts; do not assert category academic_record.
% category_satisfied(academic_record) :- has_academic_record.  % not present in facts

category_satisfied(license) :-
    license_asserted,
    % The case indicates the license was asserted; because the record does not show independent
    % documentary proof beyond assertion, treat the license category as borderline. We include
    % it as a satisfied category only when explicitly asserted in the facts.
    license_asserted.

category_satisfied(membership) :-
    has_membership.

category_satisfied(recognition) :-
    (   has_certifications
    ;   has_letters
    ).

category_satisfied(comparable) :-
    has_comparable_resumes.

% Count how many distinct categories are satisfied
number_of_satisfied_categories(N) :-
    findall(C, category_satisfied(C), Cs),
    sort(Cs, UniqueCs),
    length(UniqueCs, N).

three_of_six_met :-
    number_of_satisfied_categories(N),
    N >= 3.

% Final merits determination (whether, in totality, petitioner is recognized as having a
% degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered).
% For final merits we require evidence of sustained acclaim or original/major contributions,
% or other indicia showing significance above the ordinary. The record shows certifications,
% membership, letters from local chapter leadership, and resumes for comparison. It does NOT
% show sustained national/international acclaim, major published contributions, influential
% citations, significant awards, or evidence of commanding a leading role commensurate with
% "significantly above" the ordinary.
evidence_of_sustained_acclaim :- fail.
evidence_of_major_original_contribution :- fail.

final_merits_met :-
    (   evidence_of_sustained_acclaim
    ;   evidence_of_major_original_contribution
    ).

% Dhanasar national interest waiver prongs
% 1) Proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance.
% Cybersecurity work is recognized as having substantial merit and national importance.
prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance.

% 2) Individual well-positioned to advance the proposed endeavor.
% The record shows relevant experience and certifications but lacks evidence of
% national/international impact, major leadership roles, or sustained record of advancing
% the field at a high level. Treat as not established.
prong2_well_positioned :- fail.

% 3) On balance, waiver would benefit the United States.
% Absent satisfaction of prong 2 and absent strong final merits evidence, the record does not
% establish that waiving the job offer requirement would on balance benefit the U.S.
prong3_benefit_on_balance :- fail.

prongs_met :-
    prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    prong2_well_positioned,
    prong3_benefit_on_balance.

% Eligibility requires both underlying EB-2 exceptional ability determination and Dhanasar prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    three_of_six_met,
    final_merits_met,
    prongs_met.

% Decision and runtime entry point (as required)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: This case concerns an appeal from the Vermont Service Center's denial of a Form I-360 VAWA self-petition seeking classification as an abused spouse of a U.S. citizen. The Director denied the petition after concluding that the record did not establish a qualifying spousal relationship with the claimed U.S. citizen spouse, and the matter then came to the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal. According to the record, the Petitioner is a citizen of Peru and based the self-petition on her 2003 marriage to G-A-. On appeal, she argues that although her prior marriage to A-M-S- had not been dissolved when she married G-A-, that prior marriage was later dissolved in Texas in 2009 and she and G-A- then continued living together as husband and wife and presenting themselves as married. The record includes a Texas county court final decree dissolving her marriage to A-M-S- in 2009. On appeal, she also submitted copies of federal income tax returns filed jointly with G-A- for 2015, 2016, and 2018. In an affidavit, she states that she believed she was already divorced from A-M-S- when she married G-A- because she had signed divorce documents in Peru in 2000, and that after learning those documents had never been filed, she sought and obtained the 2009 Texas divorce. She further states that she and G-A- continued living together as husband and wife until about October 1, 2019, and that they divorced in 2021 while these proceedings were pending. The AAO described the record as showing that, after the 2009 divorce, the couple continued living together and represented themselves to others as married.
statutes: A petitioner who is the spouse of a U.S. citizen may self-petition for immigrant classification as an immediate relative if the petitioner married the U.S. citizen spouse in good faith, resided with the spouse, was subjected to battery or extreme cruelty by the spouse, and is a person of good moral character. Section 204(a)(l)(A)(iii) of the Act. Immediate relatives are defined as, in pertinent part, the "spouses ... of a citizen of the United States .... " Section 201(b)(2)(A)(i) of the Act. Primary evidence of a qualifying spousal relationship is a marriage certificate issued by civil authorities, and proof of the termination of all prior marriages of the petitioner. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2( c )(2)(ii). Petitioners are "encouraged to submit primary evidence whenever possible," but may submit any relevant, credible evidence in order to establish eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(i). U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) determines, in our sole discretion, what evidence is credible and the weight to give to such evidence. Section 204(a)(l)(J) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.2( C)(2)(i).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
petitioner_from_peru.

prior_marriage_to_a_m_s.

filed_texas_divorce.
final_decree_of_divorce_2009.
cohabited_with_g_a_until_2019.
divorced_g_a_in_2021.
filed_vawa_self_petition_2020.
director_denied_prior_marriage_not_terminated.
on_appeal_submitted_final_decree_2009.

% Assume good moral character is not contested in the record presented.
good_moral_character.

    signed_peru_divorce_papers_around_2000_not_filed.

% The petitioner and G‑A‑ represented themselves to others as husband and wife,

represented_as_married :-

% Good faith marriage is established where the petitioner believed the prior marriage

good_faith_marriage :-
    believed_prior_marriage_ended_at_time_of_marriage,
    represented_as_married.

% Primary evidence requirement: proof of termination of prior marriages is preferred.
% The record contains a Final Decree of Divorce entered in 2009 (post‑dating the 2003 marriage).

% Although the prior divorce was entered after the 2003 marriage, the regulations

credible_evidence_of_spousal_relationship :-

% USCIS discretion to weigh evidence is modeled by allowing a good‑faith showing
% plus credible supporting evidence to establish a valid spousal relationship
% for purposes of the VAWA self‑petition, even where a prior divorce was finally
% entered after the second marriage.
spousal_relationship_valid :-
    good_faith_marriage,

% Residence with the spouse is established by the long cohabitation period.
resided_with_spouse :-

% Eligibility combines the statutory elements required by section 204(a)(1)(A)(iii).
% For this program we treat these elements as the complete eligibility test.
eligibility_met :-

    spousal_relationship_valid,
    good_faith_marriage,
    resided_with_spouse,
    subjected_to_battery_or_extreme_cruelty,

decision(Result) :-
```

text: The petitioner is a U.S. property development company that sought to continue employing the beneficiary as the company’s regional head in the United States by filing for an extension of her L-1A intracompany transferee visa, in a claimed executive capacity. The beneficiary, an Indian national, began working for the petitioner’s affiliate in India in 2012 as a co-managing director and has worked for the petitioner in the United States in L-1A status in the regional head position since 2019. A prior petition for her had been approved, extending her L-1A status through March 2023. The California Service Center denied the petitioner’s requested extension. The petitioner appealed that denial, arguing that the decision overlooked evidence and that the adjudicator should have followed or deferred to the prior approval.
statutes: An intracompany transferee is a noncitizen who, for at least one continuous year in the three years before their initial U.S. admission in nonimmigrant status, worked abroad and seeks to enter the country to temporarily work for a branch, parent, affiliate, or subsidiary of their foreign employer in a capacity that is managerial, executive, or involves specialized knowledge. Section 101(a)(15)(L) of the Act; 8 C.F.R . § 214.2(1)(1)(ii)(A). If a U.S. petitioner seeks to employ them in a managerial or executive capacity, they seek L-lA classification. U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS), "Instructions for Petition for Nonimmigrant Worker," 16, www.uscis.gov/sites/default/files/ document/forms/i-129instr.pdf; see also 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(a)(l) (incorporating form instructions into the regulations). If the proposed U.S. work involves specialized knowledge, they seek L-lB classification. Id.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text:
The petitioner filed Form I-140 on May 11, 2023, seeking EB-2 classification as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree and requesting a national interest waiver of the job offer/labor certification requirement. She stated that her proposed endeavor is to continue working as an Environmental Manager and to assist U.S. organizations in designing, developing, and implementing systems and procedures to protect workers from illness and injury, protect the environment, and avoid property damage. She described areas of expertise she would offer to U.S.-based companies, including environmental management, energy consumption, natural resources, and waste disposal. 

In response to a request for evidence, the petitioner submitted company formation documents for a business formed in 2023 (name redacted) and a business plan. The business plan contained industry and market analyses, a description of the company and its services (described as providing Environmental Health and Safety (EHS) due diligence and a comprehensive suite of EHS consultancy services), marketing strategies, a discussion of her education and work experience, a description of company personnel, and financial forecasts. The plan projected hiring 9 employees in year one, 11 in year two, 10 in year three, 11 in year four, and 10 in year five (51 total) and included revenue projections of $828,397 (year 1), $1,585,920 (year 2), $2,482,672 (year 3), $3,316,530 (year 4), and $4,294,237 (year 5). 

The record also contains materials the petitioner submitted about compliance and environmental managers, the role of environmental management in sustainability, worker shortages in environmental management, and U.S. environmental policies; articles on environmental challenges and on immigrants as entrepreneurs and job creators; multiple letters of support from professionals who commented on her environmental management capabilities (submitted under initials); and an expert opinion letter from Dr. A-A-, an adjunct professor of mathematics at [institution], submitted in support of her national interest waiver request.

The petitioner appealed the Texas Service Center’s denial of her I-140. On appeal she argued, among other points, that there is an impending shortage of environmental professionals that her work would help address, that her proposed endeavor aligns with federal initiatives on environmental protection and sustainability, that it would advance U.S. leadership in environmental sustainability and compliance, and that her work would support sustainable business practices and contribute to economic growth, public health, and environmental sustainability.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. If a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec . 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: 1 See also Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85 , 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D .C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS ' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver to be discretionary in nature).

• The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Case facts (informational)
filed_i140_date('2023-05-11').
petition_classification('EB-2').
petition_request('national_interest_waiver').
proposed_position('Environmental Manager').
proposed_endeavor('EHS consultancy through petitioner\'s company').
company_formed_after_filing.

% Evidentiary items submitted (treated as true/false arity-0 predicates where used
% in eligibility logic)
evidence_business_plan.
letters_of_support.
expert_opinion_letter.
projected_job_creation.        % business plan projects hiring 51 employees over 5 years
projected_revenue.             % business plan contains revenue forecasts
alignment_with_federal_initiatives. % petitioner argues alignment with federal initiatives
articles_and_policy_materials. % submitted articles on environmental challenges and policy

% Underlying EB-2 eligibility
% To qualify, petitioner must show either an advanced degree or exceptional ability.
% In the record provided, education and work experience were described, but there is
% no affirmative predicate establishing proof of an advanced degree or exceptional ability.
% We therefore define those predicates to fail (explicitly false).
has_advanced_degree :- fail.
has_exceptional_ability :- fail.

underlying_eb2_met :-
    (   has_advanced_degree
    ;   has_exceptional_ability
    ).

% Dhanasar prong 1: substantial merit and national importance
% The record contains a business plan, expert opinion, letters of support, and
% materials about environmental policy and challenges. Treat these as satisfying
% evidence of substantial merit and national importance.
prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    evidence_business_plan,
    expert_opinion_letter,
    (   letters_of_support
    ;   articles_and_policy_materials
    ).

% Dhanasar prong 2: petitioner is well-positioned to advance the endeavor
% Being well-positioned generally requires objective evidence such as an advanced degree,
% a record of success in the field, existing contracts or business operations prior to filing,
% or other clear indicators of ability to advance the proposed endeavor.
% Here: company was formed after filing (company_formed_after_filing), and the record
% does not establish an advanced degree or documented prior record of commercially
% successful operations. Model those absent elements as failing.
business_established_before_filing :- fail.
record_of_prior_success :- fail.

prong2_well_positioned :-
    % Require both: demonstrable plan materials AND one of the strong indicia of being well-positioned
    evidence_business_plan,
    expert_opinion_letter,
    (   has_advanced_degree
    ;   record_of_prior_success
    ;   business_established_before_filing
    ).

% Dhanasar prong 3: on balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the U.S.
% The record includes projections of job creation and revenue, alignment with federal initiatives,
% and letters supporting the national benefit. Treat those record items as evidence in favor.
prong3_benefit_to_us :-
    projected_job_creation,
    projected_revenue,
    alignment_with_federal_initiatives.

% Final eligibility: must meet underlying EB-2 and all three prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    underlying_eb2_met,
    prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    prong2_well_positioned,
    prong3_benefit_to_us.

% Required decision/run structure (entrypoint). Output is binary: 'Accepted' or 'Dismissed'.
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner seeks classification as a special immigrant juvenile. According to 2022 New York Family Court orders, the court appointed his biological mother, N-T-N-, as his guardian, placed him in her custody or guardianship, found reunification with his father not viable because of neglect and abandonment, and found that returning him to Vietnam would not be in his best interest because he would have no one there to care for or support him. The Family Court described the father as having failed for most of the petitioner's life to provide love, care, support, and attention, and as having failed since 2017 to provide for him or maintain a relationship with him; the court stated that the petitioner had not seen or spoken to his father in nearly five years. The court also noted that in the United States his mother cared for and provided for him, he had a place to stay and needed supervision, and he had prospects for college and employment. The petitioner filed his SIJ petition in August 2022. The Director later issued a notice of intent to deny stating that USCIS consent was not warranted because the record contained evidence the Director viewed as materially conflicting with SIJ eligibility requirements. Department of State records from the petitioner's September 2019 visa application indicated that N-T-N- presented herself as married, that the father was described as in good financial standing and paying the petitioner's travel costs, and that the petitioner presented himself as a first-year student; the Director treated those records as inconsistent with the Family Court findings. In May 2023, the petitioner responded with an affidavit from N-T-N-, a copy of a 2021 divorce decision involving N-T-N- and the father, and a letter from counsel. In her affidavit, N-T-N- stated that she had paid for the petitioner's education in Vietnam and had fully supported him since 2017, that she was still married at the time of the visa application and was not asked whether she was separated, that she did not disclose that she alone was supporting him in order to improve the visa application, and that although the father was "doing good financially," he had not supported her or the petitioner since 2017 and she paid the petitioner's tuition. The Director later stated that the record still contained an unresolved inconsistency and that the petitioner had not provided additional evidence showing the Family Court was aware of the visa-process statements when it made its reunification finding. On appeal, the petitioner argues that the NOID response and supporting documents already explained the discrepancies, that the Family Court had provided a detailed account of the father's neglect and abandonment and that the Director failed to address that response, and that the Director applied USCIS consent review too broadly.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101 ( a)(27)(J)(i) ofthe Act; 8 C.F .R . § 204.11 (b). Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.11 ( c )(1 ). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 101 ( a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F .R . § 204.11 ( c )(2). U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) has sole authority to implement the SIJ provisions of the Act and regulation. Homeland Security Act of 2002, Pub . L. No. 107-296, §§ 47l(a), 451(b), 462(c), 116 Stat. 2135 (2002). SIJ classification may only be granted upon the consent ofthe Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) , through USCIS , when the petitioner meets all other eligibility criteria and establishes that the request for SIJ classification is bona fide, which requires the petitioner to establish that a primary reason the required juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under State law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i)-(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R . § 204.l l(b)(5). USCIS may also withhold consent if evidence materially conflicts with the eligibility requirements such that the record reflects that the request for SIJ classification was not bona fide. 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b)(5).

I

```prolog
    guardian_mother_appointed.

juvenile_court_determined_non_reunification :-
    court_finding_reunification_not_viable_father_due_to_neglect_abandonment.

best_interest_determination_satisfied :-
    court_finding_not_in_best_interest_return_vietnam.

% Bona fide requirement: primary reason for seeking court order was relief from parental maltreatment
bona_fide_primary_reason_sought_relief :-
    juvenile_court_determined_non_reunification,
    (   father_no_financial_support_since_2017
    ;   father_belittled_petitioner
    ;   father_refused_pay_for_education_insisted_get_job
    ;   father_no_contact_nearly_five_years
    ).

% DOS records created a perceived inconsistency
dos_conflict :-
    dos_records_2019_exist,
    (   dos_records_reflect_mother_presented_as_married
    ;   dos_records_reflect_father_in_good_financial_standing_and_covered_travel
    ;   petitioner_presented_as_first_year_student_in_2019
    ).

% Petitioner submitted documentary evidence in response to the NOID that addresses the inconsistency
conflict_resolved :-
    petitioner_responded_may_2023,
    evidence_submitted_2021_divorce_decision,
    evidence_submitted_mother_affidavit.

% No material inconsistency remains (either there was none, or it was addressed)
no_material_conflict :-
    \+ dos_conflict.
no_material_conflict :-
    dos_conflict,
    conflict_resolved.

% USCIS may withhold consent only if a material conflict remains
uscis_consent_may_be_withheld :-
    dos_conflict,
    \+ conflict_resolved.

uscis_consent_not_withheld :-
    \+ uscis_consent_may_be_withheld.

% Eligibility is met only if all statutory elements and USCIS consent conditions are satisfied
eligibility_met :-
    unmarried,
    under_twenty_one_at_filing,
    juvenile_court_order_exists,
    custody_placement_satisfied,
    juvenile_court_determined_non_reunification,
    best_interest_determination_satisfied,
    bona_fide_primary_reason_sought_relief,
    no_material_conflict,
    uscis_consent_not_withheld.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner seeks classification as a special immigrant juvenile. In May 2018, a California Family Court placed him under the guardianship of his cousin, and in a later 2018 SIJ order the court found that reunification with both parents was not viable because of abandonment and that returning him to India was not in his best interest. The record describes the SIJ order as stating that the court had reviewed the evidence and as providing factual bases for the reunification and best-interest findings. Based on that order, the petitioner filed his SIJ petition in June 2018. In the guardianship petition, the cousin stated that the petitioner had lived with him since coming to the United States, that he could provide for all of the petitioner's needs, and that the petitioner had no one else to care for him. The Petition for Special Immigrant Juvenile Findings asserted that the petitioner had been imprisoned in India as a minor with his parents because of political beliefs and that, after his release, he denounced his parents' political party or views, fled India, and expected his family to denounce and abandon him. During adjudication, the Director issued a second request for evidence stating that government records indicated the petitioner had traveled to the United States using different names and birthdates and requesting evidence to establish his age. The Director also advised that government records reflected multiple prior uses of a 1998 birthdate before the petitioner entered the United States. The record further describes government encounter records from the petitioner's travel to the United States as listing a 1998 birthdate, but not showing that he presented identity documents with that date or explaining the source of it. By contrast, the petitioner's passport and birth certificate, both issued before his 2017 travel, show a 2000 birthdate, which is also the birthdate reflected in his arrival information, Family Court filings, and SIJ petition. The record describes him as unmarried when he filed in June 2018 and as under 21 at that time. The Director denied the petition, and the matter is before the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal. On appeal, the petitioner argues that the Family Court made qualifying findings based on the submitted evidence and that the 1998 birthdate entries do not undercut the 2000 birthdate shown in his passport and birth certificate.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(b). 1 Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F .R. § 204.11 ( c )( 1 ). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioner's best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 10l(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.l l(c)(2). U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) has sole authority to implement the SIJ provisions of the Act and regulation. Homeland Security Act of 2002, Pub. L. No. 107-296, §§ 47l(a), 451(b), 462( c ), 116 Stat. 2135 (2002). SIJ classification may only be granted upon the consent ofthe Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), through USCIS, when the petitioner meets all other 1 The Department of Homeland Security issued a final rule, effective April 7, 2022, amending its regulations governing the requirements and procedures for petitioners who seek SIJ classification. See Special Immigrant Juvenile Petitions, 87 Fed. Reg . 13066 (Mar. 8, 2022) (revising 8 C.F.R. §§ 204, 205, 245).

eligibility criteria and establishes that the request for SU classification is bona fide, which requires the petitioner to establish that a primary reason the required juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under State law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i)-(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b)(5). USCIS may also withhold consent if evidence materially conflicts with the eligibility requirements such that the record reflects that the request for SIJ classification was not bona fide. 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(b)(5).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text:
The petitioner is a foreign national who filed an I-140 petition seeking employment‑based second preference (EB-2) classification as an advanced-degree professional and a national interest waiver of the job‑offer requirement. She is co‑owner and chief executive officer of a Florida company (doing business as a named supermarket/event business) that, according to her business plan, began operations in February 2021. She describes the company as operating two related lines of business: a supermarket selling traditional Brazilian and other ethnic products, and an event‑planning arm. She states her business activities include importing traditional Brazilian products, working with Florida‑based vendors and suppliers, retail sales of ethnic foods and products in her supermarket, providing door‑to‑door and in‑store counter service as well as self‑service, digital, and delivery options, organizing events, promotions, and presentations, attending traditional Brazilian food fairs and shows, and supporting Central Florida communities. Her filings assert the business currently averages over 200 sales per day, has created six jobs, has generated over $2 million in sales, and reports net income and assets each in excess of $400,000. Her business plan forecasts growth via franchise expansion into other Florida towns with large and growing Brazilian populations and projects the creation of approximately 30 direct jobs (and additional indirect jobs) by the fifth year of operations.

In support of the petition and appeal, the petitioner submitted a business plan, financial statements and sales figures, invoices and documentation of events she has held, and an expert opinion letter from an associate adjunct professor addressing the event‑planning industry. In response to a request for evidence, she also argued that national importance should not be limited to impacts on economically depressed areas.

The petitioner appealed the denial of the national interest waiver. On appeal she contends the director imposed novel substantive and evidentiary requirements beyond the regulations, applied an improperly strict standard of proof, and did not give due regard to her business plan and supporting business documents. She maintains her endeavor will have broad positive effects—creating jobs, contributing revenue to the U.S. business ecosystem, and supporting societal and economic welfare—and that it aligns with initiatives to encourage women‑ and minority‑owned small business growth.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. An advanced degree is any U .S. academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. 1 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A U.S. bachelor's degree or a foreign equivalent degree followed by five years of progressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. Id. Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying classification, the petitioner must then establish eligibility for a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter ofDhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884 (AAO 2016), provides the framework 1 Profession shall include, but not be limited to, architects, engineers, lawyers, physicians, surgeons, and teachers in elementary or secondary schools, colleges, academics, or seminaries. Section 101 ( a)(32) of the Act.

for adjudicating national interest waiver pet1t10ns. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as a matter of discretion,2 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id. at 889.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
```

text:
The petitioner is an entrepreneur working in information technology (IT) education who filed an I-140 immigrant petition seeking EB-2 classification—either as a professional with an advanced degree or as an individual of exceptional ability—and asking for a national interest waiver of the job-offer requirement. He proposes to operate a U.S.-based business that provides IT-related training and online courses, including instruction via YouTube, and says his work will improve workforce skills, attract learners from diverse and disadvantaged areas, and generate economic benefits.

In support of the petition and on appeal, the petitioner submitted a business plan, a resume, industry reports and articles, an expert opinion letter describing his entrepreneurship and IT-education experience, and recommendation letters attesting to his training of senior developers and ability to deliver online courses. He also provided projections that by year five his company would create 90 direct and 108 indirect jobs and generate total revenue of $58,100,000, and he asserted substantial viewership for his YouTube training content. 

After the Texas Service Center denied the petition, the petitioner appealed. On appeal he argued that the denial was erroneous, contending that the evidence he submitted demonstrates he meets the eligibility criteria for EB-2 classification (including meeting at least three of the regulatory criteria and having five years of progressive post-baccalaureate experience) and that his proposed endeavor is of national importance. He also contended that the director applied an overly strict standard of proof in denying the petition.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," we set forth a framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions in the precedent decision Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec . 884 (AAO 2016). Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration

Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver of the job offer, and thus the labor certification, to a petitioner classified in the EB-2 category ifthe petitioner demonstrates that (1) the noncitizen's proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; (2) the noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor; and (3) that on balance it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements ofa job offer and thus ofa labor certification. The first prong, substantial merit and national importance, focuses on the specific endeavor the noncitizen proposes to undertake. The endeavor's merit may be demonstrated in a range ofareas such as business, entrepreneurialism, science, technology, culture, health, or education. In determining whether the proposed endeavor has national importance, we consider its potential prospective impact. The second prong shifts the focus from the proposed endeavor to the noncitizen. To determine whether the noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor, we consider factors including but not limited to the individual's education, skills, knowledge, and record of success in related or similar efforts. A model or plan for future activities, progress towards achieving the proposed endeavor, and the interest of potential customers, users, investors, or other relevant entities or individuals are also key considerations. The third prong requires the petitioner to demonstrate that, on balance of applicable factors, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification. USCIS may evaluate factors such as whether, in light of the nature of the noncitizen' s qualification or the proposed endeavor, it would be impractical either for the noncitizen to secure a job offer or for the petitioner to obtain a labor certification; whether, in light of the nature of the noncitizen's qualification or the proposed endeavor, it would be impractical either for the noncitizen to secure a job offer or for the petitioner to obtain a labor certification; whether, even assuming that other qualified U.S. workers are available, the United States would still benefit from the noncitizen's contributions; and whether the national interest in the noncitizen's contributions is sufficiently urgent to warrant forgoing the labor certification process. Each ofthe factors considered must, taken together, indicate that on balance it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
% certification, urgency, or unique benefit outweighing protection of labor certification). Represented here
% as not established.
benefit_to_us :- fail.

% Eligibility requires underlying EB-2 qualification plus satisfaction of all three Dhanasar prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_qualified,
    substantial_merit_and_national_importance,

               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed a Form I-918 (U petition) in March 2016 accompanied by a Supplement B signed and certified by a detective from the local California police department. That Supplement B indicated the petitioner was the victim of a crime described as “Other: Robbery” and cited the police investigation of the incident. The underlying contemporaneous police investigative report, dated the day of the incident in 2010, describes two suspects approaching the petitioner, one “bear-hugging” him from behind while the other punched him repeatedly. The report states the petitioner lost consciousness, that the suspects removed the petitioner’s wallet/ID and fled on foot, and repeatedly uses the term “assault” in recounting the event. The police narrative describes the petitioner being punched seven to ten times, falling to the ground, being struck further while on the ground, and later flagging down a passerby who drove him home; his family then took him to the hospital. The record includes contemporaneous photographs of the petitioner’s facial injuries and medical records documenting a pain score of 8, a nasal fracture, head injury, lacerations to the chin and inner cheek, swelling around the right eye, and CT findings noting extensive right frontal and periorbital soft tissue swelling, a right nasal bone fracture, a right medial orbital wall fracture, and a large right frontal scalp hematoma. The police report also notes the petitioner’s wallet was missing after the incident.

In March 2021, in response to a request for evidence, the petitioner submitted a second Supplement B for the same 2010 incident, certified by a different detective from the same police department; that Supplement B checked boxes indicating the petitioner was the victim of felonious assault and cited robbery in the agency’s description of the offenses investigated. The Nebraska Service Center director denied the U petition on the ground that the petitioner had not established he was the victim of qualifying criminal activity. The petitioner appealed that denial, asserting on appeal that he was the victim of robbery and of felonious assault and that the submitted police reports, medical records, photographs, and the second Supplement B demonstrate his victimization and his eligibility for U nonimmigrant classification.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for U-1 nonimmigrant classification, petitioners must show that they: have suffered substantial physical or mental abuse as a result of having been the victim of qualifying criminal activity; possess information concerning the qualifying criminal activity; and have been helpful, are being helpful, or are likely to be helpful to law enforcement authorities investigating or prosecuting the qualifying criminal activity. Section 101(a)(15)(U)(i) of the Act. The burden of proof is on a petitioner to demonstrate eligibility by a preponderance of the evidence. Section 291 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1361; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(4); Matter of Chawathe, 25 l&N Dec. 369, 376 (AAO 2010). A "victim of qualifying criminal activity" is defined as an individual who has "suffered direct and proximate harm as a result of the commission of qualifying criminal activity." 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(14). "Qualifying criminal activity" is "that involving one or more of' the 28 types of crimes listed at section 101(a)(15)(U)(iii) of the Act or "any similar activity in violation of Federal, State, or local criminal law." Section 101(a)(15)(U)(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(9). The term"'any similar activity' refers to criminal offenses in which the nature and elements of the offenses are substantially similar to the statutorily enumerated list of criminal activities" at section 101(a)(15)(U)(iii) of the Act. 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(9).

As required initial evidence, petitioners must submit a Form 1-918 Supplement B, U Nonimmigrant Status Certification (Supplement B), from a law enforcement official certifying the petitioners' helpfulness in the investigation or prosecution of the qualifying criminal activity perpetrated against them. 1 Section 214(p)(1) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(2)(i). U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) has sole jurisdiction over U petitions. 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(1). Although petitioners may submit any relevant, credible evidence for the agency to consider, USCIS determines, in its sole discretion, the credibility of and weight given to all the evidence, including the Supplement B. Section 214(p)(4) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(4).

11.

```prolog
supplement_b_second_checks_felonious_assault.
supplement_b_second_cites_robbery.

police_report_describes_assault.          % Police investigative report describes physical attack
attack_involved_bear_hug.                 % One suspect bear-hugged the petitioner from behind
attack_involved_repeated_punches.         % Other suspect punched him repeatedly in the face
victim_lost_consciousness.                % Report states petitioner lost consciousness

police_photos_present.                    % Police photographs of injuries are in the record

medical_records_document_high_pain.       % Pain score of 8 documented

right_medial_orbital_wall_fracture.       % Radiology: right medial orbital wall fracture
right_frontal_scalp_hematoma.             % Large right frontal scalp hematoma
transported_to_hospital.                  % Petitioner was transported to a hospital
medical_records_and_photos_contemporaneous. % Medical records and photos are contemporaneous with incident

% Legal and evidentiary rules (expressed as Prolog rules)

% Possession of information: petitioner possesses information about the criminal activity
possesses_information :-
    police_report_describes_assault.      % the police report documents the incident
possesses_information :-
    supplement_b_initial.                 % the petitioner submitted initial Supplement B
possesses_information :-
    supplement_b_second.                  % the petitioner submitted a second Supplement B

% Helpfulness: Supplement B from law enforcement certifying helpfulness is required initial evidence.
helpfulness_certified :-
    supplement_b_initial.                 % initial Supplement B present
helpfulness_certified :-
    supplement_b_second.                  % or the second Supplement B is present

% Substantial physical abuse: show direct physical harm documented by police, medical records, photos
suffered_substantial_physical_abuse :-
    (   victim_lost_consciousness
    ;   nasal_bone_fracture
    ;   right_medial_orbital_wall_fracture
    ;   right_frontal_scalp_hematoma
    ;   medical_records_document_high_pain
    ),
    police_photos_present.                 % corroborating photographic evidence

% Determine whether the facts establish a qualifying criminal activity.
% The petitioner's record can support felonious (aggravated) assault under CA Penal Code §245(a)(4)
% if the attack involved force likely to produce great bodily injury (represented here by loss of consciousness and fractures).
felonious_assault_under_ca_245a4 :-
    police_report_describes_assault,
    attack_involved_repeated_punches,
    victim_lost_consciousness;
    nasal_bone_fracture;
    right_medial_orbital_wall_fracture.

% Robbery with use of force (taking property by force or threat) can be a qualifying crime if the taking involved force causing injury.
robbery_with_force :-
    supplement_b_second_cites_robbery,
    wallet_removed,
    ( attack_involved_bear_hug
    ; attack_involved_repeated_punches
    ),
    ( victim_lost_consciousness
    ; nasal_bone_fracture
    ; right_medial_orbital_wall_fracture
    ).

% Qualifying criminal activity exists if either felonious assault (as pled) or robbery with force is established.
qualifying_crime :-
    felonious_assault_under_ca_245a4.
qualifying_crime :-
    robbery_with_force.

% Burden and credibility: USCIS evaluates credibility; evidence from multiple contemporaneous sources supports preponderance.
% We model that the combination of contemporaneous police report, contemporaneous medical records, and photos meets the preponderance standard.
evidence_meets_preponderance :-
    police_report_describes_assault,
    medical_records_and_photos_contemporaneous,
    ( nasal_bone_fracture
    ; right_medial_orbital_wall_fracture
    ; right_frontal_scalp_hematoma
    ).

% Eligibility: must meet all statutory elements for U-1 classification as provided in the problem statement.
eligibility_met :-
    qualifying_crime,
    suffered_substantial_physical_abuse,
    possesses_information,
    helpfulness_certified,
    evidence_meets_preponderance.

% Decision entrypoint and runtime harness (must be included exactly as requested)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'

    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is an entrepreneur and vocational counselor who filed an I-140 petition seeking EB-2 immigrant classification (advanced-degree professional) and a national interest waiver. He holds a 2014 "Licentiate in Psychology" from an institution in Bolivia, accompanied by an academic evaluation stating the degree is equivalent to a U.S. bachelor's degree in psychology. He submitted letters documenting over five years of progressive professional experience.

In his filing the petitioner described his proposed endeavor as bringing his understanding of the relationship between mental health and occupation to address and prevent mental health issues and to provide vocational coaching and counseling to American youth and young adults. He submitted a Proposed Endeavor Statement and a business plan that states the enterprise would be 80% nonprofit and 20% dedicated to repaying initial investment; funding sources would include his own investment, outside grants, and possible loans. The business plan projected hiring 10 employees in the first year, expanding to three new locations and increasing personnel by 50% in the second year, and reaching 15,000 people in the first year (figures the plan states were calculated using examples and industry figures). The petitioner described delivering services through one-on-one and family vocational counseling sessions and asserted his work would help individuals enter and remain in the workforce, reduce turnover for employers, and lower recruiting and staffing costs for businesses.

He also submitted supporting materials including industry articles and reports on youth unemployment and mental health, evidence regarding government priorities on mental health and equity, letters of recommendation, proofs of awards, documentation of continuing education and events attended, and letters of interest.

The Texas Service Center director denied the petition, concluding the record did not establish eligibility for a national interest waiver as a matter of discretion. On appeal to the Administrative Appeals Office, the petitioner contends the director’s decision contains instances of misunderstanding and misapplication of law that, in the petitioner’s view, go beyond harmless error and amount to an abuse of discretion; he challenges the director’s findings and seeks review of that denial.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. An advanced degree is any U.S. academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. A U.S. bachelor's degree or foreign equivalent degree followed by five years of progressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). If a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term"national interest," Matter ofDhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework

for adjudicating national interest waiver pet1t10ns. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts and inferences parsed from the Administrative Appeals Office case
% All yes/no predicates are arity 0 (true if present, false if not provable).
% The program is binary: decision(Result) yields 'Accepted' only if eligibility_met holds.

% -----------------------
% Underlying EB-2 qualification
% -----------------------

% Petitioner holds a foreign Licentiate in Psychology evaluated as a U.S. bachelor's degree.
has_foreign_degree.
degree_evaluated_equal_bachelors.

% Petitioner submitted letters documenting over five years of progressive experience.
five_years_progressive_experience.

% A U.S. bachelor's or foreign equivalent plus five years progressive experience
% is the equivalent of a master's degree for EB-2 purposes (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2)).
advanced_degree_equivalent :-
    degree_evaluated_equal_bachelors,
    five_years_progressive_experience.

% The petitioner qualifies for the underlying EB-2 classification if they have an advanced degree equivalent.
qualification_met :-
    advanced_degree_equivalent.

% -----------------------
% Dhanasar framework (national interest waiver)
% Three prongs:
%   1) Proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance.
%   2) The individual is well-positioned to advance the endeavor.
%   3) On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
% -----------------------

% Evidence in the record supporting the proposed endeavor and its merit:
% - Proposed Endeavor Statement, business plan, projections (15,000 people first year),
%   nonprofit component, staffing goals, letters of interest, letters of recommendation, awards, continuing education.
proposed_endeavor_statement.
business_plan_submitted.
projected_reach_15000.
nonprofit_component.
staffing_goals_specified.
letters_of_interest.
letters_of_recommendation.
awards_and_continuing_education.

% The record supports that the proposed endeavor has substantial merit (service to youth and workforce participation).
d_prong1_substantial_merit.

% However, the record does not sufficiently establish national importance at the level required:
% the business plan projections are speculative, funding sources are not committed, and the evidence
% links primarily to local/regional outcomes rather than demonstrated national impact.
% Represent absence by defining the predicate but forcing it to fail.
d_prong1_national_importance :- fail.

% Prong 2: Well-positioned to advance the endeavor.
% Although petitioner has relevant experience, letters, and a plan, the plan's key elements (funding commitments,
% concrete contracts, executed pilot programs, or realized impact metrics) are not present in the record.
% Therefore, we treat the well-positioned prong as not established.
d_prong2_well_positioned :- fail.

% Prong 3: Balance of factors (would waiving the job offer requirement benefit the United States?).
% Given the insufficient showing on national importance and that the petitioner is not shown well-positioned,
% the balance does not favor granting a national interest waiver.
d_prong3_balance_benefit :- fail.

% -----------------------
% Eligibility: all conditions necessary to Accept the case
% This must be the last substantive part: eligibility_met requires both EB-2 qualification and
% satisfaction of all three Dhanasar prongs.
% -----------------------

eligibility_met :-
    qualification_met,
    d_prong1_substantial_merit,
    d_prong1_national_importance,
    d_prong2_well_positioned,
    d_prong3_balance_benefit.

% -----------------------
% Decision and entrypoint (as required)
% -----------------------

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a publicly traded company that provides software development, testing, and support services. It filed an I-140 immigrant petition (EB-3, skilled worker) to permanently employ the beneficiary as a lead software engineer with a proffered wage of $124,883 per year. The petition’s priority date is December 20, 2022, and the I-140 was filed in January 2024.

In support of the petition, the company submitted its 2022 annual report showing annual net income of more than $419 million. After the director issued a request for additional evidence (RFE) asking for proof of the company’s ability to pay in 2023, the petitioner explained that its 2023 annual report was not yet available and instead submitted: IRS Forms W-3 showing total wages paid by the company in excess of $600 million in both 2022 and 2023; a report filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission indicating 2023 revenues exceeding $4.6 billion; and the beneficiary’s payroll records from December 2022 through January 2024, which show the beneficiary was paid more than the proffered wage in 2022 and 2023. U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services records also show the company filed immigrant visa petitions for other beneficiaries. On appeal the petitioner additionally submitted its 2023 annual report.

The petitioner appealed the director’s denial, arguing that the director misinterpreted the law and misapplied the facts to the law.
statutes:
Immigration as a skilled worker generally follows a three-step process. First, a prospective employer must obtain certification from the U.S. Department of Labor (DOL) that: there are insufficient U.S. workers able, willing, qualified, and available for an offered job; and a noncitizen's permanent employment in the job would not harm wages and working conditions ofU.S. workers with similar jobs. Section 212(a)(5)(A)(i) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1182(a)(5)(A)(i). Second, an employer must submit a DOL-approved labor certification with an immigrant visa petition to U .S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS). Section 204(a)(l)(F) ofthe Act. Among other things, USCIS determines whether a noncitizen beneficiary meets the requirements of a DOL-certified position and a requested immigrant visa category. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(1)(3)(ii)(D), ( 4).

Finally, if users approves a petition, a beneficiary may apply for an immigrant visa abroad or, if eligible, adjustment of status in the United States. See section 245(a) of the Act, 8 U.S.e. § 1255(a).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text:
The petitioner is a foreign-born minor who filed a Form I-360 for Special Immigrant Juvenile (SIJ) classification in June 2022, when she was 17. In 2022 a Maryland circuit court issued an order granting custody of the petitioner to her mother and making findings related to parental reunification and the petitioner’s best interests. The court’s factual findings included that the petitioner had not seen her father since 2014, that her father provided only limited financial support until 2021 and then stopped, and that he failed to prevent the petitioner’s grandfather from physically disciplining her. The court also found that the father’s family follows traditions that include female genital mutilation (FGM) and that the petitioner faced a risk of undergoing that procedure against her will. The court noted that the petitioner’s mother provided most of the petitioner’s living expenses and made decisions about her education, health, and well‑being.

The petitioner’s record contains multiple identity and residence documents. She submitted a birth certificate and the biographical pages of expired Sierra Leonean passports showing the same date of birth and indicating Sierra Leonean nationality, and a U.S. visitor visa page that also identifies her nationality as Sierra Leonean. The birth certificate also indicates she was born in England. A document from the Sierra Leone Ministry of Internal Affairs (captioned “Immigration Department”) states the petitioner may hold dual citizenship, is a citizen of Sierra Leone, was born to Sierra Leonean parents, and is a bona fide holder of a Sierra Leone passport. The petitioner also submitted school records from Sierra Leone for months in 2013–2014, and evidence that she entered the United States on a visitor’s visa in 2014.

The petitioner’s mother submitted a statement saying the family lived at the family residence in Maryland, that she had a verbal agreement with her husband to have the house and pay household and children’s expenses, and that when the husband traveled to the United States in 2014–2016 he used the family residence address on some documentation but stayed at hotels. The mother’s statement also described cultural constraints in Sierra Leone that limited the husband’s ability to oppose the patriarchal family elder’s discipline of the children.

The National Benefits Center director issued a notice of intent to deny and later denied the petitioner’s SIJ petition, citing perceived inconsistencies in the record concerning the petitioner’s age and identity, place of birth/citizenship, and the factual bases for the court’s parental‑reunification and best‑interest findings. The petitioner responded to the notice with documentary evidence (including the Sierra Leone immigration document and the mother’s statement) and, after the denial, appealed the director’s decision to the Administrative Appeals Office. The reason for the appeal is the director’s determination that USCIS consent to SIJ classification was not warranted due to those alleged inconsistencies.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(b).1 The juvenile court must have made a judicial determination related to the petitioner's custodial placement or dependency by declaring the petitioner dependent upon the juvenile court; or legally committing or placing the petitioner under the custody of an agency or department of a state, or an individual or entity appointed by astate or juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(l)(i). In addition, the record must contain a judicial or administrative determination that 1 The Department of Homeland Security issued a final rule, effective April 7, 2022, amending its regulations governing the requirements and procedures for petitioners who seek SIJ classification. See Special Immigrant Juvenile Petitions, 87 Fed. Reg. 13066 (Mar. 8, 2022) (revising 8 C.F.R. §§ 204, 205, 245).

it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country ofnationality or last habitual residence. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(2). The juvenile court must have made the requisite judicial determinations under applicable state law to establish eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(3). SIJ classification may only be granted upon the consent of USCIS when the petitioner meets all other eligibility criteria and establishes that the request for SIJ classification is bona fide, which requires the petitioner to establish that a primary reason the required juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under State law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i)-(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(b)(5). For USCIS to consent, petitioners must establish the juvenile court order or supplemental evidence include the factual bases for the parental reunification and best interest determinations and the relief from parental maltreatment that the court ordered or recognized. 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(d)(5)(i). USCIS may also withhold consent if evidence materially conflicts with the eligibility requirements such that the record reflects that the request for SIJ classification was not bona fide. 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(b)(5).

label: Accepted

```prolog
% Eligibility rule: all required predicates must hold for acceptance

    court_found_best_interest_not_return_to_sierra_leone,
    court_order_contains_factual_bases_for_reunification_and_best_interest,
    primary_reason_sought_court_determinations_was_relief_from_parental_maltreatment,

    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
```
