text:
The petitioner is a former professional soccer player from Brazil who, according to his filings, played professionally for more than 15 years. More recently he worked in Brazil as a soccer administrative manager and as a soccer coach. He filed an immigrant petition seeking EB-2 classification as an individual of exceptional ability and a discretionary waiver of the job-offer requirement so he could establish and operate a soccer development business in the United States.

In his petition and supporting materials the petitioner describes plans to found the soccer development business in the state of Georgia, serve as its head coach, and expand to four additional Georgia cities by the fifth year. The business plan submitted by the petitioner states the enterprise would offer soccer development classes for children, adolescents, professional and college teams, and individual athletes; create and implement age-appropriate training plans; identify and develop talent; and form and coach a soccer team. The business plan projects hiring eight direct employees and creating 19 indirect jobs within five years and estimates nearly $100,000 in tax revenue over that period. The plan also claims potential economic, health, and social-welfare benefits from the business.

To document his background and the proposed endeavor, the petitioner submitted a variety of materials: a high school academic transcript; a statement from the Union of Professional Football Athletes in Brazil acknowledging he played professionally for more than 15 years; a “certificate of football monitor” and an identity card from the Brazilian Association of Soccer Coaches indicating membership since 2022; an accountant’s statement listing his annual revenue for 2018–2022 and two articles about soccer salaries in Brazil; a membership certificate from the International Coaches Association; recommendation letters and recognition certificates and news articles about his playing career (including articles about a team he played with being promoted); a business plan with market analysis and financial projections; industry reports and articles about soccer’s growing popularity, economic effects of sports, and related topics; and an expert opinion from a lecturer in New Jersey addressing the importance of soccer coaching and the petitioner’s qualifications.

The petitioner appealed the denial of his petition, asserting that the director’s decision did not properly consider or analyze the evidence he submitted. On appeal he argued the director’s decision was vague and nonspecific, failed to identify the evidentiary deficiencies, and did not address the content of the documents he provided. He emphasized his documented achievements as a professional soccer player and submitted additional news articles and recommendation letters to support his contention that his past impact in Brazil and his proposed work as a coach in the United States demonstrate the significance of his proposed endeavor.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. An advanced degree is any U.S. academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree.1 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A U.S. bachelor's degree or a foreign equivalent degree followed by five years ofprogressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a ma ster's degree. Id. 1 Profession shall include, but not be limited to, architects, engineers, lawyers, physicians, surgeons, and teachers in elementary or secondary schools, colleges, academics, or seminaries. Section 101(a)(32) of the Act.

Exceptional ability means a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A petitioner must initially submit documentation that satisfies at least three of six categories of evidence: (A) An official academic record showing that the alien has a degree, diploma, certificate, or similar award from a college, university, school, or other institution of learning relating to the area of exceptional ability; (B) Evidence in the form of letter(s) from current or former employer(s) showing that the alien has at least ten years of full-time experience in the occupation for which he or she is being sought; (C) A license to practice the profession or certification for a particular profession or occupation; (D) Evidence that the alien has commanded a salary, or other renumeration for services, which demonstrates exceptional ability; (E) Evidence of membership in professional associations; or (F) Evidence of recognition for achievements and significant contributions to the industry or field by peers, governmental entities, or professional or business organizations. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii).2 Where a petitioner meets these initial evidence requirements, we then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination and assess whether the record shows that the petitioner is recognized as having a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the field.3 See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 2010) (discussing a two-part review where the documentation is first counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination); see also Visinscaia v. Beers, 4 F. Supp. 3d 126, 131-32 (D.D.C. 2013); Rijal v. USCIS , 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011). This two-step analysis is consistent with our holding that the "truth is to be determined not by the quantity of evidence alone but by its quality," as well as the principle that we examine "each piece of evidence for relevance, probative value, and credibility, both individually and within the context of the totality of the evidence, to determine whether the fact to be proven is probably true." Matter of Chawathe, 25 l&N Dec. at 376. Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying classification, the petitioner must then establish eligibility for a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the 2 If these types of evidence do not readily apply to the individual's occupation , a petitioner may submit comparable evidence to establish eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(iii). 3 USCIS has previously confirmed the applicability of this two-part adjudicative approach in the context of individuals of exceptional ability. See generally 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.5(8)(2), https: //www.uscis.gov/policy-manual. 2

term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that USCIS may , as matter of discretion,4 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
% Facts: submitted evidence (true/false predicates as arity 0)
has_high_school_transcript.
union_statement_professional_15yrs.
certificate_football_monitor.
identity_card_brazil_coaches.
accountant_statement_revenue_2018_2022.
articles_player_salaries.
membership_international_coaches.
recognition_certificates.
news_articles.
recommendation_letters.
team_promoted_evidence.
business_plan_with_projections.
industry_reports.
expert_opinion_lecturer_nj.

% Mapping evidence to regulatory initial-criteria for "exceptional ability"
% Criterion A: official academic record from a college/university/etc. relating to the field
% (Only a high school transcript was submitted; no college/foreign-equivalent degree present.)
has_college_degree :- false.
criterion_a :- has_college_degree.

% Criterion B: letters from current/former employer(s) showing at least 10 years full-time experience
% (Submitted: union statement acknowledging >15 years as a professional athlete, but not employer letters.)
employer_letters_experience :- false.
criterion_b :- employer_letters_experience.

% Criterion C: license or certification for the profession/occupation
% (Submitted: "certificate of football monitor" and identity card from Brazilian Association of Soccer Coaches.)
criterion_c :- certificate_football_monitor, identity_card_brazil_coaches.

% Criterion D: evidence of commanding a salary or other remuneration demonstrating exceptional ability
% (Submitted: accountant statement of revenue; documentary evidence does not establish salary demonstrating exceptional ability.)
accountant_statement_shows_salary :- false.
criterion_d :- accountant_statement_shows_salary.

% Criterion E: membership in professional associations
% (Submitted: membership certificate from International Coaches Association.)
criterion_e :- membership_international_coaches.

% Criterion F: recognition for achievements and significant contributions (by peers, gov't, orgs)
% (Submitted: recognition certificates, news articles, recommendation letters, evidence of team promotion.)
criterion_f :-
    recognition_certificates
    ;
    news_articles
    ;
    recommendation_letters
    ;
    team_promoted_evidence.

% Determine whether initial evidentiary requirement (at least 3 of 6) is satisfied.
initial_evidence_satisfied :-
    % Collect which criterion predicates succeed
    findall(Criterion,
            (
                member(Criterion, [criterion_a,criterion_b,criterion_c,criterion_d,criterion_e,criterion_f]),
                call(Criterion)
            ),
            Satisfied),
    length(Satisfied, N),
    N >= 3.

% Final merits assessment: quality, relevance, and credibility of the submitted evidence
% The record contains some supporting documents but also contains significant probative-quality issues:
% - The academic evidence is limited to a high-school transcript (no college/advanced degree).
% - Key experience evidence (employer letters) is absent; the union statement is supportive but not employer verification.
% - The business plan's projected economic impact is modest (small tax revenue over 5 years, limited job creation)
%   and is speculative (expansion projections, athlete outcomes, and revenue estimates not strongly substantiated).
% - The expert opinion is present but limited in scope and does not independently validate the projected national impact.
business_plan_speculative :- business_plan_with_projections.
tax_projection_small :- business_plan_with_projections.
expert_opinion_limited :- expert_opinion_lecturer_nj.

% The final merits determination requires that the totality of the evidence show the petitioner is recognized as having
% expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered. Here, quality concerns exist, so final merits are not met.
final_merits_positive :-
    \+ business_plan_speculative,
    \+ tax_projection_small,
    \+ expert_opinion_limited.

% Exceptional ability requires both the initial documentary threshold and a favorable final merits determination.
exceptional_ability :-
    initial_evidence_satisfied,
    final_merits_positive.

% Dhanasar framework for national interest waiver (three prongs)

% Prong 1: Proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance.
% The proposed endeavor (a soccer development business in Georgia with local/regional expansion) has local/regional merit,
% potential economic and health benefits, but the record does not demonstrate national importance given the modest
% projected economic impact and localized scope.
dhanasar_prong1_substantial_national_importance :- false.

% Prong 2: The individual is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor.
% The petitioner has long professional experience (>15 years), coaching/administrative experience, certification, memberships,
% and recognition—factors that support being well-positioned.
dhanasar_prong2_well_positioned :-
    union_statement_professional_15yrs,
    (certificate_football_monitor ; identity_card_brazil_coaches),
    membership_international_coaches,
    (recognition_certificates ; recommendation_letters).

% Prong 3: On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
% Given the speculative nature of the business plan and modest projected national economic impact, the balance does not favor a waiver.
dhanasar_prong3_balance_benefit :- false.

% Eligibility: must demonstrate underlying classification (exceptional ability) and all three Dhanasar prongs
eligibility_met :-
    exceptional_ability,
    dhanasar_prong1_substantial_national_importance,
    dhanasar_prong2_well_positioned,
    dhanasar_prong3_balance_benefit.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a 46-year-old native and citizen of Honduras who entered the United States without inspection in April 2003. He filed a Form I-918 Supplement (U petition), which USCIS approved, granting him U nonimmigrant status from December 2017 to December 2021. He timely filed an I-485 adjustment application in July 2021 seeking lawful permanent resident status based on his U classification.

The Vermont Service Center director issued a request for evidence (RFE) asking for proof of the petitioner’s continuous physical presence in the United States, a newly executed Form I-918, Supplement B (U Nonimmigrant Status Certification) or a statement describing his efforts to obtain one, evidence of cooperation with law enforcement in the criminal investigation or prosecution of his felonious assault, and an updated Form I-693 (medical exam). The director also questioned the acceptability of the petitioner’s initially submitted Honduran birth document under Department of State guidance. In response to the RFE, the petitioner submitted evidence of continuous physical presence, a letter from his attorney describing his cooperation with law enforcement, and a Certificacion de Acta de Nacimiento issued by the Registro Nacional de las Personas (RNP) with an English translation.

The director denied the I-485, finding that the RFE response did not include acceptable evidence of birth in Honduras, a newly executed Supplement B, or, alternatively, a statement from the petitioner describing his efforts to obtain a new Supplement B or other evidence of cooperation with law enforcement. On appeal, the petitioner submitted a brief and additional evidence reasserting his eligibility. He contested that his previously submitted Certificacion de Acta de Nacimiento and his attorney’s statement were sufficient, and he additionally provided a Copia de Folio de Libro de Nacimientos, a newly executed Supplement B, and a statement describing his efforts to obtain the newly executed Supplement B from the district attorney’s office in Georgia. He asked that the denial be reconsidered, stating he “worked diligently to provide everything that was required of him.”
statutes:
Section 245(m) of the Act contains the eligibility requirements for individuals seeking to adjust status to that of an LPR based on having been granted U nonimmigrant status. An applicant for adjustment of status is required to submit a certified copy of their birth certificate or secondary evidence that meets the requirements of 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(2). See also 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(a)(l) (explaining that every benefit request submitted to U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) must be executed and filed in accordance with the form's instructions, which are incorporated into the regulations requiring its submission); Form 1-485, Instructions for Application to Register Permanent Residence or Adjust Status (Feb. 21, 2023 ed.), at 10 (Form 1-485 Instructions) (requiring all applicants for adjustment of status to submit a copy of their birth certificate issued by the appropriate civil authority, or proof of its unavailability or nonexistence and acceptable alternative evidence ofbirth). In addition, an applicant must demonstrate that since being granted U nonimmigrant status, they have not unreasonably refused to provide assistance in the investigation or prosecution of the qualifying criminal activity that formed the basis of the underlying U nonimmigrant status. 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(a)(5), (b)(5). The applicant may submit, as supporting evidence, a newly executed Form 1-918 Supplement B, U Nonimmigrant Status Certification (Supplement B), or a signed statement from a

law enforcement agency or official affirming that they complied with, or did not unreasonably refuse to comply with, reasonable requests for assistance in the investigation or prosecution during the requisite period. 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(e)(l). If the applicant does not submit a newly executed Supplement B or signed statement as described above, they should submit an affidavit describing efforts to obtain such evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(e)(2).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text: The Petitioner seeks employment-based second-preference classification as a professional with an advanced degree and requests a waiver of the job-offer requirement. The Texas Service Center Director denied the petition after determining that, although the Petitioner qualified for EB-2 classification, the record did not show that waiving the job offer and labor certification requirements would be in the national interest. On appeal, the Petitioner submitted additional documentation and continued to assert eligibility for the requested benefit. The record includes support letters describing the Petitioner's proposed breast-cancer research and its potential impact in the United States. A letter from Dr. I states that the Petitioner's approach departs from age-based treatment guidelines by targeting therapies to tumor characteristics, and it describes that work as improving patient outcomes, helping allocate healthcare resources more efficiently, supporting development of drugs for high-risk patients, and improving the accuracy and timeliness of diagnosis through biomarker research. The same letter states that the Petitioner's findings have been disseminated through medical journals, publications, and conferences. The record also includes reference letters describing the Petitioner's expertise in biomedicine and prior research success, evidence that his published work has been frequently cited by independent researchers, and evidence that he holds a medical degree from the named institution and is pursuing a Ph.D. there. The decision also describes the record as showing broader benefits from progress in the early detection and treatment of breast cancer.
statutes:
To qualify for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first show eligibility for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. If a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar , 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance. • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and 1 See Flores v. Ga rland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS ' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver is discretionary in nature).

• On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.

text:
The petitioner filed a Form I-360 seeking immigrant classification as an abused spouse and later filed a Form I-485 to adjust status. USCIS records indicate the petitioner was arrested in 2018 by U.S. Border Patrol in connection with an alien-smuggling investigation and that his vehicle was administratively seized. The Vermont Service Center denied the petition, and the petitioner appealed that denial on May 30, 2023, filing a Form I-290B and indicating he would submit a brief or additional evidence within 30 days (no brief or additional evidence was later received).

Initially, the petitioner submitted a police certificate from a local department stating he had no criminal record in that jurisdiction and an affidavit in which he said, "I am a person of good moral character and have never been arrested." In response to a request for evidence about the 2018 incident, he submitted a second affidavit explaining that he answered “no” to the arrest questions on his Forms I-360 and I-485 because, in his view, he had not been arrested, cited, charged, or detained. He described being pulled over by officers who asked him to go to the station for questioning; after about five hours of questioning and waiting he said he voluntarily left, received no documentation, citation, summons, or court notice, and that his vehicle was returned to the bank as the title holder and not permanently forfeited.

On appeal the petitioner argued that he never knowingly failed to disclose the incident because he was not told he was arrested, never had a hearing, and was free to leave. He also asserted that the decisionmaker applied an overly high standard of proof (without specifying what that standard was), acknowledged he may have “made the wrong judgment” in not disclosing the incident, and emphasized that he cooperated with Border Patrol, answered their questions, and had no prior issues with law enforcement. He additionally submitted evidence of federal income tax payments, a college degree, and a contingent job offer.
statutes:
A petitioner who is the spouse of a U.S. citizen may self-petition for immigrant classification if the petitioner married the U.S . citizen spouse in good faith, is eligible for immigrant classification based on that relationship, resided with the spouse, was subjected to battery or extreme cruelty by the spouse, and is a person of good moral character. Section 204(a)(l)(A)(iii) of the Act. Section lOl(f) of the Act designates certain actions that bar a finding of good moral character, although the "fact that any person is not within any of the [specified] classes shall not preclude a finding that for other reasons such person is or was not of good moral character." A petitioner's good moral character is evaluated on a case-by-case basis taking into account the provisions of section 101 (f) of the Act and the standards of the average citizen in the community . 8 C .F.R. § 204.2(c)(l)(vii). Absent extenuating circumstances, petitioners will be found to lack good moral character if they committed unlawful acts that adversely reflect upon their moral character, although the acts do not require an automatic finding of lack of good moral character. Id.

R E V 3/2024 www.uscis.gov

The Act does not designate a time period during which petitioners must establish good moral character. However, primary evidence of a petitioner's good moral character is a local police clearance or state­ issued criminal background check from each of the petitioner's residences of six months or more during the three-year period preceding the filing of the petition. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(v). Where there is reason to believe a petitioner may lack good moral character beyond this three-year period, we consider all relevant, credible evidence, including affidavits from responsible persons who knowledgeably attest to the petitioner's character. Id. See Matter ofSanchez-Linn, 20 I&N Dec. 362 (BIA 1991) (finding the greater the gravity of a noncitizen's past misconduct, the longer the period of intervening good conduct must be to establish good moral character). While we consider any credible evidence relevant to the petition, the determination of what evidence is credible, and the weight given such evidence lies within our sole discretion. Section 204(a)(l)(J) of the Act.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the case
spouse_of_us_citizen.
married_in_good_faith.
eligible_for_classification_based_on_relationship.
resided_with_spouse.
subjected_to_battery_or_extreme_cruelty.

% Evidence items
border_patrol_arrest_2018.               % USCIS records show an arrest in 2018
vehicle_seized_2018.                     % vehicle administratively seized
answered_no_to_arrest_questions.         % petitioner answered "no" to arrest questions on Forms I-360/I-485
police_clearance_no_record.              % local police department certificate stating no criminal record
affidavits_attesting_good_character_and_no_arrest.  % two affidavits asserting good moral character and no arrest
explained_no_intent_to_misrepresent.     % petitioner explained he did not believe he had been arrested
beyond_three_year_period.                % 2018 arrest is outside the 3-year primary-evidence window

% Rules interpreting the evidence under the cited standards

% There is a contradictory official record when an authoritative record (Border Patrol) documents an arrest
% while the petitioner submitted a police clearance saying no record and answered "no" on immigration forms.
contradictory_official_record :-
    border_patrol_arrest_2018,
    police_clearance_no_record,
    answered_no_to_arrest_questions.

% A misrepresentation or knowingly false answer about arrests adversely reflects on moral character.
% We treat an affirmative record of an arrest plus a negative declaration on official immigration forms
% as evidence that the petitioner failed to disclose the arrest and that this adversely reflects on character.
adverse_reflection_due_to_false_answers :-
    border_patrol_arrest_2018,
    answered_no_to_arrest_questions.

% The regulations require consideration of evidence beyond the 3-year window when there is reason to believe
% the petitioner may lack good moral character. An admitted/recorded arrest outside that window is such reason.
reason_to_consider_past_conduct :-
    border_patrol_arrest_2018,
    beyond_three_year_period.

% Weighing of evidence (capturing the Director/AAO reasoning in this case):
% Even though the petitioner submitted a local police clearance and affidavits, the existence of an official
% Border Patrol arrest record that contradicts his sworn answers and the failure to disclose that arrest
% supports a finding that he lacks good moral character.
lacks_good_moral_character :-
    contradictory_official_record.
lacks_good_moral_character :-
    adverse_reflection_due_to_false_answers,
    reason_to_consider_past_conduct.

% Define person_of_good_moral_character by negation as failure to find lacks_good_moral_character.
person_of_good_moral_character :-
    \+ lacks_good_moral_character.

% Eligibility requires all listed elements, including good moral character.
eligibility_met :-
    spouse_of_us_citizen,
    married_in_good_faith,
    eligible_for_classification_based_on_relationship,
    resided_with_spouse,
    subjected_to_battery_or_extreme_cruelty,
    person_of_good_moral_character.

% Entrypoint and output structure as required
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner is a citizen of Honduras and the daughter of the principal applicant. She filed Form I-485 seeking adjustment to lawful permanent resident status under Section 13 of the 1957 Immigration Act. The National Benefits Center denied her application, stating she was ineligible to adjust status because the principal applicant was found ineligible after failing to establish compelling reasons that would prevent return to Honduras. The petitioner appealed that denial, submitting a copy of the brief filed with the principal applicant’s appeal and asserting that she is eligible to adjust status due to the compelling reasons that prevent her mother’s return to Honduras.
statutes: Section 13 is an adjustment of status category for foreign nationals who can demonstrate, in part: (1) failure to maintain A-1, A-2, G-1, or G-2 nonimmigrant status as of the application's filing date; (2) performance of diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties by the principal on behalf of the accrediting country; and (3) inability, because of compelling reasons, to return to the country that accredited the foreign national. 8 U.S.C. § 1255b(b); 8 C.F.R. § 245.3. 2 Section 13 applicants bear the burden of proving their eligibility to adjust status. Section 291 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1361. 1 Pub. L. No. 85-316, 71 Stat. 642, amended by Pub. L. No. 97-116, 95 Stat. 161 (1981). The A nonimmigrant classification is for diplomats and foreign government officials (principal) as well as their immediate family members. The G nonimmigrant classification is for employees of certain international organizations (principal) and their immediate family members. See https:/ /travel.state.gov. 2 If the first three eligibility requirements are met, applicants must also establish that compelling reasons demonstrate that their adjustment would be in the national interest and would not be contrary to the national welfare, safety, or security of the United States and that they are of good moral character and admissible to the United States. Discussion of these remaining criteria is generally unnecessary in cases where the first three eligibility criteria have not been met.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
% Facts of the case
petitioner_nationality(honduras).
petitioner_relationship(daughter, principal_applicant).
filed_form(i485, section_13).
principal_is_mother.
nbc_denied_principal_ineligible.
petitioner_submitted_same_brief_as_principal.
petitioner_asserted_compelling_reasons.

% Legal rule: Section 13 requires three threshold elements:
%  1) failure to maintain a qualifying nonimmigrant status as of filing date
%  2) principal performed diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties for accrediting country
%  3) compelling reasons preventing return to accrediting country
% All three must be proven by the applicant to meet section 13 eligibility.

% Existence of positive proof for each required element.
% Because the record contains no proof of the first two elements, and the
% petitioner only asserted (but did not establish) compelling reasons,
% these "proven_" predicates are defined to fail (exist but are always false).
proven_failed_qualifying_status :- fail.
proven_principal_performed_duties :- fail.
proven_compelling_reasons :- fail.

% Eligibility is met only if all three threshold elements are proven.
eligibility_met :-
    proven_failed_qualifying_status,
    proven_principal_performed_duties,
    proven_compelling_reasons.

% Decision/runner as required
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-

        ),

    ).

    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed an EB-5 immigrant investor petition asserting that he invested at least $500,000 in a new commercial enterprise (NCE) associated with a project described in a May 2016 business plan that sought to raise funds to build two upscale mixed‑use buildings in New Jersey. He says his EB‑5 investment funds were a gift from his mother. According to the petitioner’s submissions, his mother purchased a first Hong Kong property (Property A) with her spouse in 1991 and sold it in 1997. Using proceeds from that 1997 sale, she purchased a second Hong Kong property (Property B) in October 2003 for HKD 2,130,000 and obtained a 20‑year mortgage for HKD 1,491,000. The petitioner’s counsel stated that the mother made a down payment of HKD 637,000, allegedly financed by the 1997 sale of Property A. Records in the petition indicate the mother’s monthly mortgage payment for Property B was HKD 11,105.12 and that the mortgage was paid off when she sold Property B in December 2017.

The petitioner asserts that his mother sold Property B in December 2017 and later gifted him funds for the EB‑5 investment. The record includes a September 2018 statement from the petitioner’s mother saying she gifted him $550,000 with no expectation of repayment, and bank records showing a $550,000 remittance from the mother’s account to the NCE’s account on the petitioner’s behalf on September 10, 2018. Other bank documents in the record show that after the December 2017 sale she placed HKD 7,413,792.63 in an investment account and, when closing that account in March 2018, received HKD 7,437,557.39. Two “Account Withdrawal / Transfer Slip” forms indicate currency exchanges in April and August 2018: HKD 3,422,348.29 exchanged for $435,579.52 in April 2018, and HKD 4,960,000 exchanged for $631,284.21 in August 2018. The petitioner submitted copies of some of his mother’s bank statements and other documents, as well as statements from her former co‑workers describing her employment and earnings between 1990 and 1995 and statements about her spouse’s employment between 1997 and 2003.

The petition was denied by the Immigrant Investor Program Office on the ground that the petitioner had not documented the lawful source of the EB‑5 investment funds. The petitioner appealed that denial, submitting a brief and additional evidence and maintaining on appeal that he has established his eligibility and the lawful source of the funds.
statutes:
A noncitizen may be classified as an immigrant investor if they invest the requisite amount of qualifying capital in an NCE. A noncitizen may invest the required funds directly in an NCE or through a regional center, as the Petitioner has done in this case. Regional centers can pool immigrant 1 On March 15, 2022, President Joseph Biden signed the EB-5 Refonn and Integrity Act, which made significant amendments to the EB-5 program, including the designation of targeted employment areas and the minimum investment amounts. See Section 203(b )(5) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b )(5) (2022). As the Petitioner had filed his petition in September 2018, the relevant law then in existence governs this appellate adjudication. 2 The Petitioner indicates that the NCE is located in a targeted employment area, and that the required amount of qualifying capital is downwardly adjusted from $1 ,000,000 to $500,000. See 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(f)(2) (2018). 3 is an entity that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) has designated to participate in the EB -5 program. A regional center is an economic unit involved with the promotion of economic growth, "including ... improved regional productivity, job creation, and increased domestic capital investment." See 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e) (2018).

(and other) investor funds for qualifying projects that create jobs directly or indirectly. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(4)(iii) (2018). A noncitizen must demonstrate that they have placed their own capital at risk in the NCE. See Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. 206, 213 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998); Matter of Soffici, 22 I&N Dec. 158, 165 n.3 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998) (stating that "[a] petitioner must ... establish, pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e), that funds invested are [their] own"). In addition, the noncitizen must show that their invested capital did not derive, directly or indirectly, from unlawful means. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e). To show the lawful source of the funds, an investor must submit evidence such as foreign business and tax records or documentation identifying sources of the capital. See 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(3). Bank letters or statements corroborating the deposit of funds by themselves are insufficient to demonstrate their lawful source. Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at 210-11; Matter ofIzummi, 22 I&N Dec. 169, 195 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998). The record must trace the path of the funds back to a lawful source. 4 Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at 210-11; Matter ofIzummi, 22 I&N Dec. at 195.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
mother_sold_property_a_1997.
mother_bought_property_b_oct2003.
property_b_purchase_price_hkd_2130000.

some_mother_statements_provided.

% Governing legal requirements (encoded as predicates)

% The petitioner meets the investment amount requirement when he invested at least $500,000 in an NCE.
required_investment_met :-
    invested_amount_at_least_500000,
    invested_in_NCE.

% The petitioner claims the invested funds are a gift from his mother, and there is documentary evidence of the gift and a remittance.
donor_gift_documented :-
    claims_funds_are_gift_from_mother,
    mother_statement_sept2018_gift_550000,
    remittance_sep10_2018_550000_to_NCE_account.

% To be lawful, the source of the donor's funds must be traced back to a lawful origin without unexplained gaps.
% We model the available transactional chain from the mother's December 2017 property sale through account placements and currency exchanges,
% but we require no evidentiary gaps to accept the trace as complete.
trace_path_to_lawful_source :-
    mother_sold_property_b_dec2017,
    mother_placed_hkd_7413792_63_into_investment_account_for_three_months,
    investment_account_closed_march2018_received_hkd_7437557_39,
    currency_exchange_april2018_hkd_3422348_29_usd_435579_52,
    currency_exchange_aug2018_hkd_4960000_usd_631284_21,
    \+ evidence_gap_present.

% There are gaps in the documentary record (December 2017 through September 2018) and counsel statements alone do not cure the gap.
evidence_gap_present :-
    partial_bank_statements_with_gaps.

% Bank letters or statements alone are insufficient to establish lawful source per precedent; we model that as a constraint:
bank_statements_alone_insufficient.

% Funds are considered lawfully sourced only if the path to a lawful source is traced.
funds_lawful :-
    trace_path_to_lawful_source.

% The petitioner is treated as having invested his own capital when the gift is documented and the donor's source is lawful.
invested_own_capital :-
    donor_gift_documented,
    funds_lawful.

% Eligibility requires the required investment and that the petitioner invested his own capital lawfully.
eligibility_met :-
    required_investment_met,
    invested_own_capital.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
```

text:
The petitioner is an electrical engineer who filed Form I-140 seeking employment-based (EB-2) classification and a national interest waiver. He holds a bachelor's degree in engineering earned in 2011 and provided documentation from current and past employers showing approximately 12 years of experience as an electrical engineer. His petition describes continued work in electrical engineering focused on development of nuclear and fusion energy, upgrading transmission voltages, and retrofitting aged power plants. He is currently employed on an international collaborative project intended to demonstrate the feasibility of fusion as a large-scale, carbon-free energy source, and submitted a letter from a colleague describing his role in the design, construction, and operation of the fusion machine that contains the reaction and distributes the energy. The record includes letters from work associates and other professionals attesting to his skills and contributions, employment records, professional licenses, a performance award from a prior employer, copies of papers he authored, and letters of interest from potential employers.

The petitioner appealed after the Texas Service Center director denied the I-140 petition’s national interest waiver request, citing that the record did not establish he was well positioned to advance his proposed endeavor or that waiving the job offer requirement would be beneficial to the United States.
statutes:
To qualify for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, a petitioner must establish they are an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b)(2)(A) of the Act. An advanced degree is any U.S. academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. A U.S. bachelor's degree or foreign equivalent degree followed by five years of progressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. If a petitioner establishes eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then demonstrate that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship

and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion,1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id.

11.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the petitioner (boolean predicates use arity 0)
has_bachelors_engineering.    % petitioner earned a bachelor's degree in engineering in 2011
years_experience(12).         % approximately 12 years of experience as an electrical engineer

% Current project and role
working_on_international_fusion_project.   % currently working on an international collaborative fusion project
role_on_fusion_machine.                    % role involves work on the machine that contains the fusion reaction and distributes the energy

% Documentary evidence submitted
letters_from_associates.       % letters from work associates and other professionals attesting to skills/contributions
employment_records.            % employment verification from current and past employers
professional_licenses.         % professional licenses submitted
performance_award.             % performance award from a prior employer submitted
past_papers_authored.          % past papers authored by the petitioner submitted
letters_of_interest.           % letters of interest from potential employers

% Proposed endeavor focus and asserted benefits
proposed_work_nuclear_fusion_grid_upgrade_retrofit. % proposed work: nuclear/fusion energy, upgrading transmission voltages, retrofitting aged plants
benefits_energy_reliability_and_clean_transition.   % asserted benefits to energy supply reliability and transition to cleaner energy

% Administrative finding on first Dhanasar prong (record shows this prong was found satisfied by the director)
prong1_substantial_national_importance.  % the proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance

% Underlying EB-2 eligibility:
% An advanced degree OR a U.S. bachelor's (or foreign equivalent) plus 5 years progressive experience qualifies.
underlying_eb2_eligibility :-
    bachelors_equivalent_master.

bachelors_equivalent_master :-
    has_bachelors_engineering,
    years_experience(N),
    N >= 5.

% Dhanasar prong 2: well-positioned to advance the proposed endeavor
% We model available evidence that supports being well-positioned:
has_relevant_experience :-
    years_experience(N),
    N >= 10.   % petitioner has substantial (12) years of progressive experience

has_documentary_support :-
    letters_from_associates,
    employment_records,
    ( past_papers_authored ; performance_award ).  % at least papers or an award in addition to letters/employment records

has_active_project_role :-
    working_on_international_fusion_project,
    role_on_fusion_machine.

well_positioned_to_advance :-
    has_relevant_experience,
    has_documentary_support,
    has_active_project_role.

% Dhanasar prong 3: on balance, waiver would benefit the U.S.
% Model factors showing waiver would be beneficial:
unique_skillset :-
    has_relevant_experience,
    past_papers_authored,
    professional_licenses.

proposed_work_benefits_us :-
    proposed_work_nuclear_fusion_grid_upgrade_retrofit,
    benefits_energy_reliability_and_clean_transition.

waiver_beneficial :-
    proposed_work_benefits_us,
    letters_of_interest,   % evidence of ability to secure work or collaborate in the U.S.
    unique_skillset.

% Final eligibility: must satisfy underlying EB-2 and all three Dhanasar prongs
eligibility_met :-
    underlying_eb2_eligibility,
    prong1_substantial_national_importance,
    well_positioned_to_advance,
    waiver_beneficial.

% Entrypoint and decision output (must be last part per instructions)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The Petitioner is a U.S. company that designs and manufactures automotive components. An affiliated company in Ireland employed the Beneficiary as a data analyst beginning in July 2021. In June 2023 the Petitioner filed a petition to temporarily transfer the Beneficiary to its U.S. operations to perform the same data analyst position for the Petitioner.

The record describes the Beneficiary’s duties as providing systems expertise to the Petitioner’s global legal and compliance team: defining the team’s requirements for legal documentation and management, governance, and invoice processing; incorporating those requirements into the company’s customized databases and software by creating and defining database tool functions; training team members; and leading systems and tools practices to ensure proper use and implementation. The systems that support the legal team include a customized legal-matters platform, a system for outside attorneys to process invoices, databases for conflicts-of-interest and internal investigations, and software to monitor privacy-compliance and third-party privacy risks. The Petitioner states that it had acquired the privacy-compliance system before hiring the Beneficiary but had not assigned anyone to manage or configure it until she was hired; other systems were added after her joining. The company notes that these systems are commercially available and that some vendors have developed applications for them.

The Petitioner reports that the Beneficiary completed six months of training at the Irish affiliate after hiring, including certification in the privacy information system and a three-week course on European data protection. The company says her training also covered the legal team’s processes and reporting requirements, defining key performance indicators, security access levels, data-quality issues, and data transfer and cross-functionality between internal and external systems. The Petitioner submitted a chart comparing the Beneficiary’s skills to other legal-team workers (five in Ireland and four in the United States), identifying her as the only worker with the skills to manage the relevant systems and with experience in database management and design for legal systems. The company stated it has other IT employees but that none have experience with the systems supporting the global legal team. A company official described the Beneficiary as “the only one who can manage and configure the legal management systems, troubleshoot and handle unforeseen issues or problems, and transform data into meaningful reports.”

The Petitioner documented the Beneficiary’s education and training: a Bachelor of Science degree in Management Information Systems from an Irish university in 2020 (with an independent professional evaluation equating it to a U.S. BS in MIS), two 2021 certificates in business data analytics, and additional training received at the affiliate after hiring.

The California Service Center director denied the petition, finding the Petitioner had not demonstrated the specialized nature of the Beneficiary’s foreign and proposed U.S. work and had provided insufficient evidence that the Beneficiary possesses the education, training, and employment experience for the offered job. On appeal, the Petitioner contends that the director misapplied the facts and the law in reaching that decision.
statutes: A petitioner seeking to employ an L-lB intracompany transferee must demonstrate that - for at least one continuous year in the three years before a beneficiary's initial U.S. admission in nonimmigrant status -the petitioner or its parent, branch, subsidiary, or affiliate employed the noncitizen abroad in a capacity that was managerial, executive, or involved specialized knowledge. Section 101(a)(15)(L) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.2(1)(3)(i), (iii), (iv). An L-lB petitioner must also establish that the beneficiary would be employed in a specialized knowledge capacity in the United States and that their education, training, and experience qualify them for the offered job. 8 C.F.R. § 214.2(1)(3)(ii), (iv).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text:
The petitioner is a Honduran-born youth who sought classification as a special immigrant juvenile and filed an I-360 after a North Dakota juvenile court appointed a guardian for him in 2023. The juvenile court found that his parents had not cared for him, that he was residing with foster parents, and that he was in need of a guardian. The court record and related documents reflect inconsistent years of birth: one juvenile court filing listed his birth year as 2009 and noted that a certified birth certificate was unavailable because he entered the United States as an unaccompanied minor, while other materials in the record include a document listing 2006 as his date of birth.

When the petitioner filed the I-360, the record included two Honduran birth records showing different birth years (2006 and 2009) and affidavits from individuals in his community. The petitioner explained that the discrepancy arose because his aunt originally registered him with Honduran authorities in 2006 and was listed as his mother on that record; his biological mother later tried to correct the registration and obtained a record listing her as the mother but with the later year (2009), allegedly because she could not afford fees associated with a late registration. He provided statements from the people listed on the birth records and others in the community explaining this history.

The petitioner also submitted school records that he says are consistent with a 2006 birth year—showing typical ages for completing preschool and ninth grade in Honduras—and medical age-assessment evidence: a hand growth-plate radiological scan conducted in 2024 interpreted as consistent with about age 15, and a dental radiological scan from 2023 reviewed by an oral surgeon as consistent with about age 18. He voluntarily provided the disputed documents and explanations both to the juvenile court and to immigration authorities and was forthcoming about the differing dates of birth when seeking the guardianship order.

The petitioner appealed the denial of his I-360 on the ground that the available evidence, taken together, establishes that he was under 21 years of age when he filed the petition.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) ofthe Act; 8 C.F .R. § 204.1 l(b). Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court; alternatively, the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(l). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the juvenile's best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(c)(2). U.S . Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) determines whether age has been sufficiently established in SIJ petitions. See 8 C.F.R. 204.11 ( d)(2). USCIS will consider primary evidence of age

"in the form of a valid birth certificate, official government-issued identification, or other document that in USCIS' discretion establishes the petitioner's age." Id. If primary documentation is not available, USCIS may consider additional sources such as "baptismal certificates, school records, hospital records, or immunization records, in order to evaluate whether the petitioner has met the petitioner's burden of proof by the preponderance of the evidence. See generally 6 USCIS Policy Manual J.3, https://www.uscis.gov/policy-manual.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
    juvenile_court_appointed_guardian,
    juvenile_court_found_parents_neglect,
    juvenile_court_aware_of_birthyear_conflict.

% Eligibility: all requirements for SIJ-classification as framed in the rules provided
eligibility_met :-
    petitioner_unmarried,
    under_21,
    juvenile_court_non_reunify,
    dependency_or_custody,
    best_interest_determination,
    age_established_by_preponderance.

% Entrypoint and decision logic (provided structure)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

        OtherArity \= Arity

               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
```

text:
The applicant is a native and citizen of Mexico who currently resides in the United States. He married B‑M‑, a U.S. citizen, in 2021. In August 2021 B‑M‑ filed Form I-130 on the applicant’s behalf and the applicant concurrently submitted Form I-485 to adjust status. The I-130 was approved in August 2022; the applicant’s adjustment application was later denied in October 2023. The applicant previously entered the United States in H-2B status as the beneficiary of an approved H-2B visa in 2006.

At his adjustment interview, the applicant testified that he did not know the landscaping organization that petitioned for his H-2B visa, that he did not work for that organization and had no landscaping experience, and that he began working at a restaurant within a week of entering the United States. The applicant did not contest a finding of inadmissibility and filed Form I-601, an application for a waiver of inadmissibility, in support of his adjustment of status.

In support of the waiver application the applicant submitted a variety of evidence concerning hardship to his qualifying relative (his U.S. citizen spouse), including a personal hardship letter from his spouse, a psychological evaluation of his spouse, a hardship memorandum, country‑conditions and articles regarding sexual minorities in Mexico, affidavits from family members, employment letters, financial documents, and a lease. The record includes the spouse’s statement that he would not move to Mexico if the applicant were denied admission, that he currently sees a therapist every other week for depression and anxiety and relies emotionally on the applicant, and descriptions of their life together (meeting in 2019, marriage in early 2021, travel, caring for dogs, hosting gatherings, and retirement plans). One affiant stated the spouse relies on the applicant emotionally and as a life partner. The psychological evaluation reports the spouse’s symptoms described as an adjustment disorder with mixed anxiety and depressed mood tied to recent stressful events and work stress, notes the spouse’s worry about managing stress without the applicant, and reflects the spouse’s fear of traveling to parts of Mexico because of safety concerns and worry about not being able to openly present as a same‑sex couple in Mexico.

The applicant appealed the denial by filing Form I-290B and indicated on that form he would submit a brief or additional evidence within 30 days, though no brief or additional evidence was subsequently received. On appeal the applicant asserted that the field office director erred in determining he was inadmissible, erred in finding his qualifying relative would not suffer extreme hardship, failed to properly consider material evidence, incorrectly identified the qualifying relative in parts of the decision, and failed to consider the same‑sex/country‑conditions evidence the applicant submitted.
statutes:
Any noncitizen who, by fraud or willfully misrepresenting a material fact, seeks to procure (or has sought to procure or has procured) avisa, other documentation, or admission into the United States or other benefit provided under the Act, is inadmissible. Section 212(a)(6)(C)(i) of the Act. There is a discretionary waiver of this inadmissibility if refusal of admission would result in extreme hardship to the United States citizen or lawful permanent resident spouse or parent of the noncitizen. Section 212(i) of the Act. A determination of whether denial of admission will result in extreme hardship depends on the facts and circumstances of each case. Matter of Cervantes-Gonzalez, 22 l&N Dec. 560, 565 (BIA 1999) (citations omitted). We recognize that some degree of hardship to qualifying relatives is present in

most cases; however, to be considered "extreme," the hardship must exceed that which is usual or expected. See Matter of Pilch, 21 l&N Dec. 627, 630-31 (BIA 1996) (finding that factors such as economic detriment, severing family and community ties, loss of current employment, and cultural readjustment were the "common result ofdeportation" and did not alone constitute extreme hardship). In determining whether extreme hardship exists, individual hardship factors that may not rise to the level of extreme must also be considered in the aggregate. Matter of lge, 20 l&N Dec. 880, 882 (BIA 1994) (citations omitted).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
