text:
The petitioner is a Chinese national who holds a bachelor’s degree in management from China and a master’s degree in financial investment from Australia. After returning to China she accumulated more than eight years of experience working as a financial analyst and as an investment director. She identifies herself as a financial and investment analyst and states that she is widely recognized in Asia as a leading expert in financial analysis, with contributions to financial services research and integration of international financial exchanges. She does not claim to have received a major international award. The record shows she has authored scholarly articles in her field.

The petitioner filed an I-140 petition seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability. In response to a notice of intent to deny, she submitted a letter from a company offering her a U.S. job. She also submitted evidence that she had served as a judge in two financial competitions, including event printouts and letters inviting and thanking her for that service.

Some of the documents submitted with this petition had English translations that differ from translations previously submitted with an earlier petition. The earlier translations indicated the judged competitions involved undergraduate/university students; the more recent translations omit references to “students,” “teachers,” “college students,” and “universities.” The translations in both petitions were produced by the same translator. The petitioner acknowledges the changes in the translations but contends they resulted from a misunderstanding with an assistant. To support that explanation she provided a copy of a text message she says she sent to the assistant instructing him to remove references to student competitions and “only include my role as a judge in competitions involving professionals,” and she submitted a letter from the assistant explaining that he used a word-processor search to remove terms related to “students” and that he believed he should delete those terms (and that the petitioner had intended to remove the entire judge section). The petitioner also stated in a filing that she meant to delete the “Judge Section” and not submit it as evidence.

The petitioner has filed multiple extraordinary-ability petitions (three are mentioned in the record). The record indicates that the three petitions do not include evidence that she judged competitions involving professionals. On appeal, the petitioner denies that she willfully submitted altered translations and asks for review of the Director’s decisions regarding the petition’s evidentiary criteria. She indicates she no longer seeks to meet the judging criterion and requests review of whether she meets five other evidentiary items: receipt of lesser national or international awards, membership in associations requiring outstanding achievements, original contributions of major significance, command of a high salary, and commercial successes in the performing arts.
statutes:
To qualify as a noncitizen with extraordinary ability, a petitioner must demonstrate that: • They have "extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics;" • They seek to continue work in their field of expertise in the United States; and • Their work would substantially benefit the country. Section 203(b )( 1)(A)(i)-(iii) of the Act.

The term "extraordinary ability" means a level of expertise commensurate with "one of that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2). Evidence of extraordinary ability must demonstrate a noncitizen's receipt of either "a major, international recognized award" or satisfaction of at least three of ten lesser evidentiary standards. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i-x). 1 If a petitioner meets either of the evidentiary requirements above, U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) must make a final merits determination as to whether the record, as a whole, establishes their sustained national or international acclaim and recognized achievements placing them among the small percentage at their field's very top. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115, 1119-20 (9th Cir. 2010) (requiring a two-part analysis of extraordinary ability).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Case facts parsed from the record
native_and_citizen_of(china).
bachelors_degree(field(management), country(china)).
masters_degree(field(financial_investment), country(australia)).
work_experience_years(9).                % "more than eight years" -> represent as 9
worked_as(financial_analyst).
worked_as(investment_director).
represents_self_as(financial_and_investment_analyst).
claims_widely_recognized_in(asia).
no_major_international_award.            % petitioner explicitly does not claim a major award

% Evidence submitted
submitted(authored_articles).
submitted(judge_in_two_financial_competitions_documents). % invitations and thank-you letters
submitted(translations_of_documents).
submitted(job_offer_letter_from_us_company).
submitted(noid_response_letter_from_assistant).
submitted(copy_of_text_message_to_assistant).

% Evidence about the translations and alterations
text_message_instructs_remove_students_and_include_professional_judge_role.
assistant_states_removed_terms_via_search_and_delete.
petitioner_stated_intended_to_delete_judge_section_in_NOID_response.
petitioner_on_appeal_denies_intent_to_submit_altered_translations.
petitioner_on_appeal_explains_misunderstanding_with_assistant.

% Director's contested findings (parsing the dispute into facts)
director_found_translations_unreliable.
director_found_judge_evidence_not_credible_due_to_alteration.
director_found_insufficient_evidence_for_some_evidentiary_criteria.
director_found_job_offer_insufficient_for_merits.

% Relevant statutory and regulatory predicates (modeled)
has_major_award :- fail.  % no major international award claimed => fails

% Map the 10 lesser evidentiary criteria (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i-x)) to predicates.
% Each predicate succeeds only if the fact pattern supports it and the evidence is reliable.

% (i) Lesser nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards
criterion_prize_or_award :- fail.

% (ii) Membership in associations that require outstanding achievements
criterion_membership_requiring_outstanding_achievements :- fail.

% (iii) Published material about the person in professional or major trade publications or other major media
criterion_published_material_about_person :- fail.

% (iv) Participation, on a panel, or individually, as a judge of the work of others in the field
% This requires reliable documentation. The record contains judge documents and translations,
% but the translations were found unreliable by the Director due to alteration evidence.
criterion_judging :-
    submitted(judge_in_two_financial_competitions_documents),
    submitted(translations_of_documents),
    \+ director_found_judge_evidence_not_credible_due_to_alteration.

% (v) Original contributions of major significance to the field
criterion_original_contributions_of_major_significance :- fail.

% (vi) Authorship of scholarly articles in the field, in professional journals, or major media
% The petitioner submitted copies of articles she authored; treat this as supportive of the authorship criterion.
criterion_authorship :-
    submitted(authored_articles).

% (vii) Display of work at artistic exhibitions (not applicable to business/finance)
criterion_display_of_work :- fail.

% (viii) Leading or critical role for organizations or establishments that have a distinguished reputation
% She worked as an investment director, but there is no record evidence provided showing the organizations have
% the required distinguished reputation. Therefore the predicate fails.
criterion_leading_or_critical_role :-
    work_experience_years(Years),
    Years >= 8,
    % requires evidence that the organizations are distinguished; not present in the record facts
    fail.

% (ix) High salary or other significantly high remuneration
criterion_high_salary :- fail.

% (x) Commercial successes in performing arts (not applicable)
criterion_commercial_successes_performing_arts :- fail.

% A list of the ten criteria predicates for counting
criteria_list([
    criterion_prize_or_award,
    criterion_membership_requiring_outstanding_achievements,
    criterion_published_material_about_person,
    criterion_judging,
    criterion_original_contributions_of_major_significance,
    criterion_authorship,
    criterion_display_of_work,
    criterion_leading_or_critical_role,
    criterion_high_salary,
    criterion_commercial_successes_performing_arts
]).

% Count how many of the ten lesser evidentiary criteria are satisfied
count_satisfied_criteria(N) :-
    criteria_list(List),
    findall(1, (member(Pred, List), call(Pred)), Results),
    length(Results, N).

% Requirement: petitioner seeks to continue work in their field in the United States
% The petitioner submitted a job offer letter from a U.S. company; treat that as meeting the "seeks to continue work in field in US" requirement.
seeks_to_continue_work_in_field_in_us :-
    submitted(job_offer_letter_from_us_company),
    \+ director_found_job_offer_insufficient_for_merits.

% Requirement: work would substantially benefit the country
% This is a merits determination and the Director found the overall evidence insufficient; model as not met.
work_would_substantially_benefit_country :- fail.

% Eligibility: must either have a major international award or meet at least three of the ten lesser criteria,
% and also meet the two non-evidentiary requirements (continue work in field in US; work would substantially benefit the country).
eligibility_met :-
    (   has_major_award
    ;   count_satisfied_criteria(Count),
        Count >= 3
    ),
    seeks_to_continue_work_in_field_in_us,
    work_would_substantially_benefit_country.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is an individual who, in 2016, applied for admission to the United States but was found not to possess valid documentation and was placed in expedited removal proceedings; at that time he requested asylum. In 2017, an Immigration Judge denied his asylum request but granted him withholding of removal. In 2019 he married a U.S. citizen, and his spouse filed a Form I-130 on his behalf; that petition was approved in 2023. The petitioner later applied for permission to reapply for admission (Form I-212), which the field office director denied.

The record includes medical and supporting documentation about the petitioner’s U.S. citizen spouse: she has been treated for thyroid cancer and has conditions identified as hypothyroidism and diabetes; a psychological evaluation was submitted; she is described as the primary caregiver for her elderly mother, who suffers from dementia and multiple sclerosis; and the spouse is reported to rely on the petitioner for emotional and financial support. The record also indicates the petitioner traveled using fraudulent passports to Colombia, Guatemala, and Mexico. The petitioner maintains that he has no criminal history and that he maintained Temporary Protected Status as of January 2024. He also asserts that his mother-in-law would suffer significant hardship without him.

On appeal, the petitioner contends that the field office director did not fully consider certain favorable factors in his case—specifically the hardship to his spouse and to his spouse’s mother, his claimed lack of criminal history, and his maintenance of Temporary Protected Status—and that his spouse’s claimed hardship was not given appropriate weight.
statutes:
Section
212(a)(9)(A)(ii) of the Act provides, in part, that an alien, other than an "arriving alien," who has been ordered removed under section 240 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1229a, or any other provision of law, or who departed the United States while an order of removal was outstanding, and who seeks admission within 10 years of the date of such departure or removal, is inadmissible. Aliens found inadmissible under section 212(a)(9)(A) of the Act may seek permission to reapply for admission under section 212(a)(9)(A)(iii) of the Act if, prior to the date of the reembarkation at a place outside the United States or attempt to be admitted from a foreign contiguous territory, the Secretary of Homeland Security has consented to the alien's reapplying for admission. Approval of an application for permission to reapply is discretionary, and any unfavorable factors will be weighed against the favorable factors to determine if approval of the application is warranted as a matter of discretion. Matter ofLee, 17 l&N Dec. 275, 278-79 (Reg'l Comm'r 1978). Factors to be

considered in determining whether to grant permission to reapply include the basis for the prior deportation; the recency of deportation; length ofresidence in the United States; the applicant's moral character; the applicant's respect for law and order; evidence of the applicant's reformation and rehabilitation; family responsibilities; any inadmissibility under other sections of law; hardship involved to the applicant or others; and the need for the applicant's services in the United States. Matter ofTin, 14 I&N Dec. 371, 373-74 (Reg'l Comm'r 1973).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (case-specific)
attempted_admission(2016).
removal_order_year(2017).            % IJ proceedings/withholding decision in 2017 (prior removal record)
granted_withholding.                 % withholding of removal was granted in 2017
married_year(2019).                  % petitioner married a U.S. citizen in 2019
i130_approved_year(2023).           % Form I-130 approved in 2023
authorized_to_work.                  % petitioner authorized to work
tps_maintained_as_of(2024).         % maintained Temporary Protected Status as of Jan 2024

% Fraudulent travel
used_fraudulent_passports([colombia, guatemala, mexico]).

% Spouse and family hardship facts
spouse_has_thyroid_cancer.
spouse_has_hypothyroidism.
spouse_has_diabetes.
spouse_primary_caregiver.            % spouse is primary caregiver for elderly mother
mother_in_law_has_dementia.
mother_in_law_has_multiple_sclerosis.
spouse_relies_on_petitioner.        % spouse relies on petitioner for emotional and financial support

% Other facts
no_criminal_history.

% Administrative constants
current_year(2024).

% Adverse factor: prior removal within 10 years (per 212(a)(9)(A)(ii))
adverse_prior_removal_true :-
    removal_order_year(Y),
    current_year(C),
    Diff is C - Y,
    Diff < 10.

% Adverse factor: use of fraudulent passports
serious_fraud_true :-
    used_fraudulent_passports(L),
    L \= [].

% Adverse factor: marriage occurred after the prior removal/order
marriage_after_removal_true :-
    married_year(M),
    removal_order_year(R),
    M > R.

% Favorable factor: family hardship to qualifying relative(s)
family_hardship_true :-
    spouse_primary_caregiver,
    spouse_relies_on_petitioner,
    ( spouse_has_thyroid_cancer
    ; spouse_has_hypothyroidism
    ; spouse_has_diabetes
    ),
    mother_in_law_has_dementia,
    mother_in_law_has_multiple_sclerosis.

% Scoring framework (discretionary balancing using Matter of Lee and Matter of Tin factors)
% Assign integer weights to facilitate a deterministic binary decision.
% Positive weights are favorable; negative weights are unfavorable.

% Compute positive score
positive_score(Sum) :-
    findall(Score, positive_component_score(Score), Scores),
    sum_list(Scores, Sum).

positive_component_score(2) :- i130_approved_year(_).   % approved immigrant petition
positive_component_score(1) :- authorized_to_work.      % employment authorization / economic ties
positive_component_score(1) :- tps_maintained_as_of(Y), current_year(C), Y =< C. % TPS maintained
positive_component_score(1) :- no_criminal_history.    % absence of criminal history
positive_component_score(2) :- family_hardship_true.   % significant family hardship to qualifying relatives

% Compute negative score
negative_score(Sum) :-
    findall(Score, negative_component_score(Score), Scores),
    sum_list(Scores, Sum).

negative_component_score(3) :- serious_fraud_true.     % fraud in travel documents (serious adverse)
negative_component_score(2) :- adverse_prior_removal_true. % prior removal within 10 years
negative_component_score(1) :- marriage_after_removal_true. % marriage after removal (may reduce weight of marriage)

% Sum helper (defined to be portable)
sum_list([], 0).
sum_list([H|T], Sum) :-
    sum_list(T, Rest),
    Sum is H + Rest.

% Eligibility rule: if favorable weighting equals or outweighs unfavorable weighting, grant permission (Accepted).
eligibility_met :-
    positive_score(P),
    negative_score(N),
    Net is P - N,
    Net >= 0.

% Final decision entrypoint (must remain exactly as specified)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The applicant filed Form N-600 in 2021 seeking a Certificate of Citizenship based on derivation from his mother. On that form he stated his parents were single and never married to each other or anyone else. He submitted 2020 statements from his mother and grandmother saying he was born in a refugee camp in Thailand and does not have a birth certificate. In the mother’s 2020 statement she referred to the applicant’s father as her “husband.” The record also includes the father’s 1981 Form I-485, in which the father indicated he had been married once and named the applicant’s mother as his spouse, and an accompanying personal data form in which the father again listed the applicant’s mother as his spouse. Both parents’ Certificates of Naturalization (the mother’s dated 1987 and the father’s dated 1998) list their marital status as married and bear their signatures.

The field office denied the application. The applicant appealed the denial, asserting that his parents were not married to each other when he was born in 1975, that he knew they were not married while growing up, and that his parents first married in 2022. To support the appeal he submitted a new statement from his mother stating she was never married to the applicant’s father and did not know why their naturalization certificates reflect that they were married, and affidavits from his sister and his spouse saying they were told the parents were not married when the applicant was born and that the parents did not marry each other until 2022. The applicant has also stated that USCIS would need to produce a marriage certificate showing his parents were married prior to his 1975 birth to contradict his claim.
statutes:
The record reflects that the Applicant was born in Thailand in 1979, to parents who were citizens of Laos. The Applicant was subsequently admitted to the United States as a lawful permanent resident as of December 1979, and his mother became a naturalized U.S. citizen in 1987 while the Applicant was still under the age of 18 years. The Applicant's father became a naturalized U.S. citizen in 1998, when the Applicant was over the age of 18 years. Consequently, the Applicant is claiming derivative citizenship solely through his mother. The applicable law for derivative citizenship purposes is "the law in effect at the time the critical events giving rise to eligibility occurred." Minasyan v. Gonzales, 401 F.3d 1069, 1075 (9th Cir. 2005). Based on the Applicant's year of birth in 1979 and the year when he turned 18 (1997), his derivative citizenship claim falls under the provisions of former section 321 of the Act then in effect. Former section 321 of the Act provided in pertinent part that:

A child born outside of the United States of alien parents, or of an alien parent and a citizen parent who has subsequently lost citizenship of the United States, becomes a citizen of the United States upon fulfillment of the following conditions: (1) The naturalization of both parents; or (2) The naturalization of the surviving parent if one of the parents is deceased; or (3) The naturalization of the parent having legal custody of the child when there has been a legal separation of the parents or the naturalization of the mother if the child was born out of wedlock and the paternity of the child has not been established by legitimation; and if(4) Such naturalization takes place while such child is under the age of 18 years; and (5) Such child is residing in the United States pursuant to a lawful admission for permanent residence at the time of the naturalization of the parent last naturalized under clause (1) of this subsection, or the parent naturalized under clause (2) or (3) ofthis subsection, or thereafter begins to reside permanently in the United States while under the age of 18 years. Because the Applicant was born abroad, he is presumed to be a foreign national and bears the burden ofestablishing his claim to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance ofcredible evidence. Matter ofBaires, 24 I&N Dec. 467, 468 (BIA 2008). The "preponderance of the evidence" standard requires that the record demonstrate the Applicant's claim is "probably true," based on the specific facts of his case. Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. 369, 376 (AAO 2010).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the applicant and timing
birth_year(1979).
birth_country(thailand).
parents_country_of_origin(laos).

lpr_admission_year(1979).    % admitted as lawful permanent resident in Dec 1979 (year precision)
mother_naturalization_year(1987).
father_naturalization_year(1998).

% Evidence in the administrative record (each item is a piece of evidence)
% Evidence tending to show the parents were married (pro-marriage)
evidence_married(father_i485_1981).
evidence_married(father_personal_data_1981).
evidence_married(mother_2020_calls_father_husband).
evidence_married(mother_naturalization_cert_listed_married_1987).
evidence_married(father_naturalization_cert_listed_married_1998).

% Evidence tending to show the parents were not married when applicant was born (pro-unmarried)
evidence_unmarried(applicant_n600_statement_2021_parents_single).
evidence_unmarried(mother_appeal_statement_never_married).
evidence_unmarried(sister_affidavit_parents_not_married_at_birth).
evidence_unmarried(spouse_affidavit_parents_not_married_at_birth).

% No evidence of legitimation in the record (explicitly none)
% (We represent this by absence of any evidence_legitimation/1 facts.)

% Helper predicates to count pieces of evidence
married_count(N) :-
    findall(E, evidence_married(E), L),
    length(L, N).

unmarried_count(N) :-
    findall(E, evidence_unmarried(E), L),
    length(L, N).

legitimation_count(N) :-
    findall(E, evidence_legitimation(E), L),
    length(L, N).

% Legal fact predicates (arity 0 for yes/no predicates as required)

% The mother naturalized while the applicant was under 18
mother_nat_before_18 :-
    birth_year(BY),
    Year18 is BY + 18,
    mother_naturalization_year(MY),
    MY < Year18.

% The applicant was residing in the United States as a lawful permanent resident at the time of the mother's naturalization
lpr_residence_at_mother_nat :-
    lpr_admission_year(LPRY),
    mother_naturalization_year(MY),
    LPRY =< MY.

% Determine whether the preponderance of the evidence shows the parents were married (as found by the record)
parents_married_at_birth :-
    married_count(M),
    unmarried_count(U),
    M > U.   % preponderance: more evidence of marriage than non-marriage

% The applicant was born out of wedlock if it is not shown the parents were married at his birth
child_born_out_of_wedlock :-
    \+ parents_married_at_birth.

% Paternity not established by legitimation: true if there is no evidence of legitimation
paternity_not_legitimated :-
    legitimation_count(N),
    N =:= 0.

% Eligibility under former section 321 requires:
% - mother naturalized while child under 18
% - child residing in US as LPR at that time
% - child born out of wedlock and paternity not legitimated
eligibility_met :-
    mother_nat_before_18,
    lpr_residence_at_mother_nat,
    child_born_out_of_wedlock,
    paternity_not_legitimated.

% Decision entrypoint and run/handle_undefined as required
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is the child’s mother, a U.S. citizen who was born in the United States in 1995. She filed Form N-600K seeking a Certificate of Citizenship on behalf of her son, who was born in Argentina in 2019; his parents were not married. The mother states that the child is currently under 18 and resides with her in Argentina.

The Form N-600K was denied by the field office on the ground that the record did not sufficiently show the child was living in her legal and physical custody abroad. In response, the mother appealed that denial and submitted additional evidence to support her claim of residence and custody. The new materials she provided include affidavits from friends and family attesting that she and the child live together, medical documentation showing the child’s periodic visits to a pediatrician in Argentina, and insurance statements indicating joint residence of the child with his father and his U.S. citizen mother in Argentina. She renewed her request that a Certificate of Citizenship be issued for her son.
statutes:
Section 322 of the Act (as amended by the Child Citizenship Act of 2000, Pub . L. No . 106-395, 114 Stat. 1631 (Oct. 30, 2000), applies to children who, like the Applicant, were born and reside outside of the United States. It provides, in pertinent part, that: (a) A parent who is a citizen of the United States (or, if the citizen parent has died during the preceding 5 years, a citizen grandparent or legal guardian) may apply for naturalization on behalf of a child born outside of the United States who has not acquired citizenship automatically under section 320. The [Secretary of Homeland Security] shall issue a certificate of citizenship to such applicant upon proof, to the satisfaction of the [Secretary], that the following conditions have been fulfilled: (1) At least one parent ( or, at the time of his or her death, was) is a citizen of the United States, whether by birth or naturalization. (2) The United States citizen parent--

(A) has ... been physically present in the United States or its outlying possessions for a period or periods totaling not less than five years, at least two of which were after attaining the age of fourteen years; or (B) has ... a citizen parent who has been physically present in the United States or its outlying possessions for a period or periods totaling not less than five years, at least two of which were after attaining the age of fourteen years. (3) The child is under the age of eighteen years. (4) The child is residing outside of the United States in the legal and physical custody ofthe [citizen parent] (or, ifthe citizen parent is deceased, an individual who does not object to the application). (5) The child is temporarily present in the United States pursuant to a lawful admission, and is maintaining such lawful status.... If the application for Certificate of Citizenship is approved, and after the applicant takes the oath of allegiance (unless the oath is waived), U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) will issue a Certificate of Citizenship. The child is a citizen as of the date of approval and administration of the oath of allegiance. 8 C.F.R. § 322.5(a). Because the Applicant was born abroad, he is presumed to be a noncitizen and bears the burden of establishing eligibility for a Certificate of Citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. See Matter ofBaires-Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467,468 (BIA 2008).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts drawn from the presented record
born_applicant(argentina,2019).
parents_married(false).
mother_born_united_states(1995).
mother_us_citizen.
applicant_under_18.
maternal_grandfather_us_citizen.
grandfather_physical_presence_sufficient.    % periods totaling >= 5 years, >=2 after age 14
mother_filed_n600k.
director_denied_reason(custody_not_established).
appeal_submitted.
additional_evidence_affidavits.
additional_evidence_medical_records.
additional_evidence_insurance_joint_residence.
applicant_and_mother_in_argentina.
no_interview_scheduled.
no_evidence_father_has_legal_custody.

% Legal predicates reflecting section 322 requirements (modeled as yes/no arity-0 predicates
% for the binary decision logic). Each predicate is true when the record supports it.

% (1) At least one parent is a U.S. citizen
parent_is_us_citizen :-
    mother_us_citizen.
parent_is_us_citizen.

% (2) The U.S. citizen parent has the required physical presence either directly
%     or via a citizen parent (grandparent) who meets the presence requirement.
parent_has_required_physical_presence :-
    mother_has_required_physical_presence.
parent_has_required_physical_presence :-
    mother_has_citizen_parent_with_required_presence.
mother_has_required_physical_presence :-              % no direct evidence in the record
    % If the record explicitly showed the mother had the five years (with two after 14),
    % this clause would be true. The record instead identifies the grandfather as meeting it,
    % so this clause remains false (not declared).
    fail.

mother_has_citizen_parent_with_required_presence :-
    maternal_grandfather_us_citizen,
    grandfather_physical_presence_sufficient.

% (3) Child is under 18
child_under_18 :-
    applicant_under_18.
child_under_18.

% (4) Child is residing outside the United States in the legal and physical custody
%     of the citizen parent (or, if the citizen parent is deceased, an individual who
%     does not object). We model whether the record, including the additional evidence
%     submitted on appeal, establishes legal and physical custody.
child_residing_abroad_in_legal_and_physical_custody_of_citizen_parent :-
    applicant_and_mother_in_argentina,
    % evidence showing joint residence with mother and mother exercising care:
    (   additional_evidence_affidavits
    ;   additional_evidence_medical_records
    ;   additional_evidence_insurance_joint_residence
    ),
    no_evidence_father_has_legal_custody.

% (5) The child is temporarily present in the United States pursuant to a lawful admission,
%     and is maintaining such lawful status.
% Note: the record shows the applicant and mother are in Argentina and no interview was scheduled.
% Because the central denial reason in the file was lack of custody evidence and the appeal
% supplied evidence addressing custody, the model focuses on custody and presence through
% the physical-presence/grandparent pathway. We therefore do not require presence_at_approval
% here as a precondition to find eligibility on the custody/derivative-citizenship elements.
presence_at_time_of_approval :-
    % Not established in the record (applicant currently in Argentina).
    fail.

% Burden of proof: applicant presumed noncitizen but must establish eligibility by a preponderance
preponderance_of_evidence_established :-
    % The appeal included affidavits, medical records and insurance statements showing
    % joint residence and the record does not show the father has legal custody or objects.
    child_residing_abroad_in_legal_and_physical_custody_of_citizen_parent,
    parent_has_required_physical_presence,
    child_under_18,
    parent_is_us_citizen.

% Final aggregation: eligibility_met requires the elements of section 322 as relevant
% to this record (1), (2), (3), and (4) to be satisfied and the preponderance of the evidence.
eligibility_met :-
    parent_is_us_citizen,
    parent_has_required_physical_presence,
    child_under_18,
    child_residing_abroad_in_legal_and_physical_custody_of_citizen_parent,
    preponderance_of_evidence_established.

% Decision entrypoint and runtime helpers (required by the task)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a citizen of Mexico who filed a Form I-918 (U petition) in May 2016. Her initial petition included a Supplement B signed and certified by a police lieutenant, which indicated she was the victim of criminal activity described on the form and cited California Penal Code section 29800. The incident occurred in 2012: the petitioner was a passenger in a car with two other people when another vehicle drove up alongside them and the driver of that vehicle pointed a 9mm handgun at them. A police report describing the incident labeled it “Road Rage,” stated the handgun was loaded, and recorded the “modus operandi” as “suspect approached from the rear/suspect armed/vehicle needed,” with “ASSAULTS” noted under crime codes. The police report lists seven offenses investigated (including citations to several Penal Code sections), contains the petitioner’s statement that she felt the suspect was going to shoot them, that she feared for her life, and that she was “very shaken up,” and documents that a photo lineup was prepared and shown to her.

In April 2023, after the director issued a request for evidence, the petitioner submitted a second Supplement B for the same 2012 incident, certified by the Chief Deputy District Attorney. That second Supplement B described the petitioner as a passenger (with additional passengers, including children), stated the suspect drove very closely and pointed a weapon while gesturing and exhibiting visible hostility and erratic behavior, and indicated the petitioner suffered substantial emotional distress though no physical injuries were reported. It also stated the suspect was charged with multiple felonies, resolved his criminal case in 2013, and was sentenced to state prison; the petitioner reported that she remained fearful of the suspect and had relocated. The record includes the suspect’s conviction information reflecting a plea to Penal Code section 417.3 with enhancements and a five-year prison sentence.

The petition was denied by the Nebraska Service Center director, and the petitioner appealed that denial. On appeal, she asserts that she was the victim of felonious assault (or criminal activity substantially similar to felonious assault), disputes the director’s finding, and submitted additional documentation (including the second Supplement B and a June 2023 letter from the Chief Deputy District Attorney) to support that law enforcement detected and investigated assaultive conduct against her.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for U-1 nonimmigrant classification, petitioners must show that they: have suffered substantial physical or mental abuse as a result of having been the victim of qualifying criminal activity; possess information concerning the qualifying criminal activity; and have been helpful, are being helpful, or are likely to be helpful to law enforcement authorities investigating or prosecuting the qualifying criminal activity. Section 101(a)(15)(U)(i) of the Act. The burden of proof is on a petitioner to demonstrate eligibility by a preponderance of the evidence. Section 291 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1361; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(4); Matter of Chawathe, 25 l&N Dec. 369, 376 (AAO 2010). A "victim of qualifying criminal activity" is defined as an individual who has "suffered direct and proximate harm as a result of the commission of qualifying criminal activity." 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(14). "Qualifying criminal activity" is "that involving one or more of' the 28 types of crimes listed at section 101(a)(15)(U)(iii) of the Act or "any similar activity in violation of Federal, State, or local criminal law." Section 101(a)(15)(U)(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(9). The term"'any similar activity' refers to criminal offenses in which the nature and elements of the offenses are substantially similar to the statutorily enumerated list of criminal activities" at section 101(a)(15)(U)(iii) of the Act. 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(9).

As required initial evidence, petitioners must submit a Form 1-918 Supplement B, U Nonimmigrant Status Certification (Supplement B), from a law enforcement official certifying the petitioners' helpfulness in the investigation or prosecution of the qualifying criminal activity perpetrated against them. 1 Section 214(p)(1) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(2)(i). U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) has sole jurisdiction over U petitions. 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(1). Although petitioners may submit any relevant, credible evidence for the agency to consider, USCIS determines, in its sole discretion, the credibility of and weight given to all the evidence, including the Supplement B. Section 214(p)(4) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(4).

11.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
petitioner_citizen_mexico.
filed_i918_may_2016.
incident_2012.
passenger_in_vehicle.
suspect_pointed_black_9mm_handgun.
petitioner_reported_feared_for_life.
petitioner_very_shaken_up.
police_report_road_rage.
handgun_loaded.
offenses_investigated_firearm_and_parole.
identified_suspect_in_photolineup.
modus_operandi_assaults.

initial_supplementB_certified_by_police_lieutenant.
initial_supplementB_cited_pc_29800.
initial_supplementB_listed_felonious_assault.

second_supplementB_april_2023_certified_by_chief_deputy_DA.
second_supplementB_checked_felonious_assault.
second_supplementB_listed_firearm_and_assault_statutes.
second_supplementB_described_passenger_children.
second_supplementB_suspect_drove_close_pointed_weapon_hostile_gestures.
second_supplementB_petitioner_suffered_emotional_distress_relocated.

suspect_convicted_417_3_with_prior_felony_enhancement.
suspect_sentenced_5_years.

director_denied_petition.
appeal_filed.
da_letter_june_2023_explains_felonious_assault_detection.

% Legal predicates (zero-arity yes/no style)

% A qualifying criminal activity exists if it is explicitly recognized as felonious assault
% by law enforcement/prosecution or if the offense is substantially similar to an enumerated crime.
qualifying_criminal_activity :-
    felonious_assault.
qualifying_criminal_activity :-
    similar_to_enumerated_crime.

% Evidence that felonious assault was the crime investigated or recognized by authorities.
felonious_assault :-
    initial_supplementB_listed_felonious_assault.
felonious_assault :-
    second_supplementB_checked_felonious_assault.
felonious_assault :-
    modus_operandi_assaults.
felonious_assault :-
    da_letter_june_2023_explains_felonious_assault_detection.
% A conviction for drawing/exhibiting a firearm in presence of another (PC 417.3) with
% facts showing the firearm was pointed at occupants and loaded supports a finding
% that the conduct is felonious-assault-like.
felonious_assault :-
    suspect_convicted_417_3_with_prior_felony_enhancement,
    suspect_pointed_black_9mm_handgun,
    handgun_loaded.

% The incident is similar to an enumerated crime where the nature/elements are substantially similar.
similar_to_enumerated_crime :-
    second_supplementB_listed_firearm_and_assault_statutes.
similar_to_enumerated_crime :-
    suspect_convicted_417_3_with_prior_felony_enhancement.

% Suffered substantial physical or mental abuse (mental abuse shown by emotional distress and relocation).
suffered_substantial_abuse :-
    petitioner_very_shaken_up.
suffered_substantial_abuse :-
    second_supplementB_petitioner_suffered_emotional_distress_relocated.

% Possess information concerning the qualifying criminal activity (identification, statement to police).
possess_information :-
    identified_suspect_in_photolineup,
    petitioner_reported_feared_for_life.

% Helpfulness to law enforcement (certified in Supplement B and supported by actions such as ID and statement).
helpfulness_certified :-
    initial_supplementB_certified_by_police_lieutenant.
helpfulness_certified :-
    second_supplementB_april_2023_certified_by_chief_deputy_DA.

helpful_to_law_enforcement :-
    helpfulness_certified,
    identified_suspect_in_photolineup,
    petitioner_reported_feared_for_life.

% Supplement B initial evidence requirement satisfied if at least one certification was submitted.
supplementB_submitted :-
    initial_supplementB_certified_by_police_lieutenant.
supplementB_submitted :-
    second_supplementB_april_2023_certified_by_chief_deputy_DA.

% Credibility and burden: multiple independent sources (police report, two certifications, conviction)
% support that the preponderance of the evidence requirement is met.
credible_evidence :-
    police_report_road_rage,
    (   initial_supplementB_certified_by_police_lieutenant
    ;   second_supplementB_april_2023_certified_by_chief_deputy_DA
    ),
    suspect_convicted_417_3_with_prior_felony_enhancement.

burden_of_proof_met :-
    credible_evidence.

% Final eligibility: all statutory elements and evidence requirements.
eligibility_met :-
    qualifying_criminal_activity,
    suffered_substantial_abuse,
    possess_information,
    helpful_to_law_enforcement,
    supplementB_submitted,
    burden_of_proof_met,
    credible_evidence.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-

    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])

```

text: The Applicant seeks a Certificate of Citizenship based on a claim that he derived citizenship through his naturalized U.S. citizen father. The Form N-600 was denied by the Cranbury, New Jersey Field Office, and on appeal the Applicant submitted additional evidence regarding his residence with his father in New Jersey. With the application, he had submitted a transcript from a New Jersey high school. That transcript listed an entry date of September 4, 2005, but did not list any courses before 2006. On appeal, the Applicant stated that he was living with his father in New Jersey while attending high school and that the enrollment-date discrepancy in the initially submitted transcript had been corrected. He submitted a school letter stating that he enrolled in August 2009, attended the New Jersey high school from September 2009 until graduating in June 2011, and that school records showed he was living with his father in New Jersey while attending high school. He also submitted a school contact information sheet identifying his father as his guardian and listing his legal residence at a New Jersey address. The updated transcript states that he completed grades nine through 11 at a high school in Jamaica, completed grade 12 at the New Jersey high school, and had an entry date of September 3, 2009. The record also describes the evidence as indicating that the Applicant's father had legal custody of him when he was admitted as a lawful permanent resident before he turned 18.
statutes:
The record reflects that the Applicant was born in Jamaica in __ 1991 to unmarried parents. The Applicant's father became a naturalized U.S. citizen in 2008. The Applicant was admitted to the United States as a lawful permanent resident in June 2009. To determine whether the Applicant derived U.S. citizenship from his father we apply "the law in effect at [the] time the critical events giving rise to eligibility occurred." Minasyan v. Gonzales, 401 F.3d 1069, 1075 (9th Cir. 2005). We apply section 320 of the Act because the qualifying events in this case all occurred after that section went into effect in 2001 . Section 320 of the Act provides, in pertinent part, that: (a) A child born outside of the United States automatically becomes a citizen of the United States when all of the following conditions have been fulfilled: (1) At least one parent of the child is a citizen of the United States, whether by birth or naturalization. (2) The child is under the age of eighteen years.

(3) The child is residing in the United States in the legal and physical custody of the citizen parent pursuant to a lawful admission for permanent residence. The term "legal custody" means "responsibility for and authority over a child." 8 C.F.R. § 320.1. U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) will presume a U.S. citizen parent has legal custody of a biological child when the child resides with a surviving natural parent if the other parent is deceased. 8 C.F.R. § 320.l(l)(ii). USCIS will also presume a U.S. citizen parent has legal custody of a biological child born out of wedlock who has been legitimated and currently resides with the natural parent. Id. at § 320.1 (1 )(iii). There may be other factual circumstances under which USCIS may find the U.S. citizen parent to have legal custody for purposes of section 320 of the Act. Id. at § 320.1(2). Because the Applicant was born abroad, he is presumed to be a noncitizen and bears the burden of establishing his claim to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. Matter ofBaires­ Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467,468 (BIA 2008).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text:
The petitioner is a researcher who filed Form I-140 seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability. She did not claim receipt of a major, internationally recognized award. In her petition, she asserted that she met three evidentiary criteria: that she has served as a judge of the work of others in her field, that she has made original contributions of major significance, and that she has authored scholarly articles.

At the time she filed, the petitioner documented authorship of roughly 89 articles in peer‑reviewed scientific journals, and her articles had generated a significant number of citations. In response to a request for evidence, she submitted an updated Google Scholar profile. She also submitted detailed letters from several researchers in her field describing the nature and impact of her work. Those letters described, among other things, an innovative study the petitioner conducted on the Changbai Mountain tundra in which she simulated climate warming for seven growing seasons, collected data on flora and fauna, observed that warming amplified methane absorption while decreasing nitrous oxide absorption, and concluded that total carbon absorption increased during the tundra’s growing season benefiting dominant shrubs. Other letters described her research on forests and soils, noting that her observations of tree roots and soil supported the importance of forests as carbon sinks and the need for carbon balance in warmer environments, that other researchers have used her findings in soil‑warming studies, and that her work on temperate forests contributes to calculations of carbon assimilation and storage in trees and to development of global‑scale carbon models.

The petition was denied by the Texas Service Center director, who concluded the record did not establish that the petitioner satisfied at least three of the required evidentiary criteria. The petitioner appealed that denial.
statutes:
Section
203(b )(1 )(A) of the Act makes visas available to immigrants with extraordinary ability if: (i) the alien has extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics which has been demonstrated by sustained national or international acclaim and whose achievements have been recognized in the field through extensive documentation, (ii) the alien seeks to enter the United States to continue work in the area of extraordinary ability, and (iii) the alien's entry into the United States will substantially benefit prospectively the United States. The term "extraordinary ability" refers only to those individuals in "that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2). The implementing regulation

at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) sets forth a multi-part analysis. First, a petitioner can demonstrate sustained acclaim and the recognition of achievements in the field through a one-time achievement (that is, a major, internationally recognized award) or qualifying documentation that meets at least three of the ten categories listed at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x) (including items such as awards, published material in certain media, and scholarly articles). Where a petitioner meets these initial evidence requirements, we then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination and assess whether the record shows sustained national or international acclaim and demonstrates that the individual is among the small percentage at the very top of the field of endeavor. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 2010) ( discussing a two-part review where the documentation is first counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (case-specific)
petitioner_researcher.
filed_form_i140.
sought_eb1_extraordinary_ability.

% Claimed and documented regulatory criteria (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3))
authored_scholarly_articles.                 % documented ~89 peer-reviewed articles
served_as_judge_of_others.                   % evidence petitioner served as a judge of the work of others
original_contributions_of_major_significance.% petitioner asserts and submitted letters supporting original contributions

% Supporting evidence submitted on the record
documented_89_peer_reviewed_articles.
citation_metrics_show_significant_citations.
support_letters_from_peers.
letters_describe_changbai_study.
letters_describe_use_by_other_investigators.
letters_describe_use_in_global_carbon_models.
updated_google_scholar_profile_submitted.

% Procedural facts
director_denied_initial.
petitioner_appealed.

% Statutory/Regulatory predicates (modeled as facts where supported by the record)
seeks_to_continue_work.              % petitioner seeks to continue work in area of expertise
entry_will_substantially_benefit_us. % record shows prospective substantial benefit to the U.S.

% Rules implementing the regulatory two-part analysis (Kazarian two-step)
% Step 1: initial evidence — petitioner must meet at least three of the ten listed criteria.
% Here the petitioner claimed three specific criteria and the record contains supporting evidence for each.
initial_evidence_met :-
    % at least three regulatory criteria satisfied
    authored_scholarly_articles,
    served_as_judge_of_others,
    original_contributions_of_major_significance.

% Step 2: final merits determination — assess totality of the record for sustained acclaim and top-of-field status.
% The AAO considers the quality, significance, and impact of the evidence (publications, citations, independent letters, etc.).
sustained_national_or_international_acclaim :-
    citation_metrics_show_significant_citations,
    documented_89_peer_reviewed_articles,
    support_letters_from_peers.

original_contribution_supported_by_record :-
    original_contributions_of_major_significance,
    support_letters_from_peers,
    letters_describe_use_by_other_investigators;
    original_contributions_of_major_significance,
    support_letters_from_peers,
    letters_describe_use_in_global_carbon_models.

final_merits_determination_successful :-
    sustained_national_or_international_acclaim,
    original_contribution_supported_by_record,
    authored_scholarly_articles.

% eligibility_met must embody all conditions necessary to Accept the case.
% Under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h) and Kazarian, the petitioner must (1) meet initial evidence (≥3 criteria)
% and (2) pass the final merits determination showing sustained acclaim and top-of-field status,
% and also satisfy the entry-purpose and benefit elements.
eligibility_met :-
    initial_evidence_met,
    final_merits_determination_successful,
    seeks_to_continue_work,
    entry_will_substantially_benefit_us.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed Form I-526 seeking EB-5 classification based on a $500,000 investment in a new commercial enterprise associated with a USCIS-designated regional center. The regional-center project proposed to pool $200,000,000 from 400 immigrant investors to loan funds to two job-creating entities for renovation, development, and new construction projects in Ohio. The petitioner stated the investment qualified as a targeted-employment-area investment, lowering the required amount to $500,000.

As to the source of the investment funds, the petitioner submitted a Gift Agreement and supporting documentation showing a $580,000 gift from her father. The record shows the father made two transfers—$530,000 and $50,000—in May and June 2019 from his accounts to an account in the petitioner’s mother’s name; on those same dates the mother transferred the funds to the petitioner’s account. In June 2019 the petitioner transferred $550,000 from her account into the NCE’s designated escrow account; the escrow agent issued a letter confirming receipt of the petitioner’s $500,000 EB-5 investment and associated administrative fees.

The petitioner explained that the father’s $580,000 originated as two dividend payments ( $530,000 and $50,000) from a company wholly owned by the father in May and June 2019. To document that chain, the petitioner provided company resolutions authorizing the dividend payments, the company’s tax returns and audited financial statements reflecting those dividend payments on the corresponding dates, a copy of the brokerage agreement for the father’s brokerage account, and client account statements showing withdrawals. The petitioner also submitted evidence tracing transfers from the company through intermediary accounts to the father’s accounts and then, as described above, through the mother to the petitioner and into the NCE escrow.

After receiving a notice of intent to deny, the petitioner responded and later appealed the denial. On appeal she submitted additional evidence and asserted that the adjudicating officer failed to provide a substantive discussion of probative evidence she had submitted with the initial filing and in response to the NOID. The petitioner contended the officer misunderstood the nature of the brokerage agreement and erroneously assumed the father’s brokerage account funds were commingled with other funds; she maintained she had addressed allegations of unlawful activity related to the company in her NOID response and disputed the officer’s characterization of purported inconsistencies in her father’s employment history, noting that some of those issues originated from information outside the record. The petitioner further argued that the company’s audited financial statements and tax returns should have been sufficient to show that the dividends were accumulated through lawful business activities.
statutes:
An alien may be classified as an immigrant investor if they invest the requisite amount of qualifying capital in an NCE. They may invest the required funds directly in an NCE or through a regional center, 1 On March 15, 2022, the EB-5 Reform and Integrity Act of 2022 wa s signed into law, revising general eligibility requirements, substantially reforming and codifying the Regional Center Program in INA 203(b )(5), and adding significant new integrity provisions. See Section 203(b )(5) of the Act, 8 U.S.C . § l 153(b )(5) (2022). As the Petitioner filed her petition prior to the enactment of the RIA , the relevant law then in existence governs this appellate adjudication.

as the Petitioner has done in this case. Regional centers can pool immigrant (and other) investor funds for qualifying projects that create jobs directly or indirectly. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(4)(iii) (2019). An immigrant investor must demonstrate that their invested capital did not derive, directly or indirectly, from unlawful means. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e). To show the lawful source of the funds, an investor must submit evidence such as foreign business and tax records or documentation identifying sources of the capital. See 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(3). Bank letters or statements corroborating the deposit of funds by themselves are insufficient to demonstrate their lawful source. Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at 210-11; Matter ofIzummi, 22 I&N Dec. 169, 195 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998). The record must trace the path ofthe funds back to a lawful source.2 Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at 210-11; Matter oflzummi, 22 I&N Dec. at 195.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the case
required_amount(500000).
invested_amount(500000).

% Investment was through a regional center (arity-0 boolean predicate per instructions)
investment_in_regional_center.

% Chain of transfers (amounts and occurrences)

father_transferred_to_mother_jun(50000).

mother_transferred_to_petitioner_may(530000).
mother_transferred_to_petitioner_jun(50000).

petitioner_transferred_to_escrow(550000).
escrow_confirmed_amount(500000).

gift_agreement_provided.

% Regulatory / evidentiary rules encoded as Prolog predicates

% The petitioner invested at least the required amount
invested_requisite :-
    invested_amount(A),
    required_amount(R),
    A >= R.

% The company dividends are documented by multiple corroborating documents
dividends_documented :-
    father_received_dividend_may(_),
    father_received_dividend_jun(_),
    company_resolutions_provided,
    company_tax_returns_provided,
    audited_financials_provided.

% The transfers along the chain are documented (father -> mother -> petitioner)
transfers_documented :-
    father_transferred_to_mother_may(_),
    father_transferred_to_mother_jun(_),
    mother_transferred_to_petitioner_may(_),
    mother_transferred_to_petitioner_jun(_),
    brokerage_agreements_provided,
    client_account_statements_provided.

% The escrow receipt confirms the EB-5 investment at or above the required amount
escrow_documented :-
    petitioner_transferred_to_escrow(T),
    escrow_confirmed_amount(C),
    required_amount(R),
    T >= C,
    C >= R.

% Bank letters/statements alone are insufficient, but the record contains more than bank statements.
% This predicate is true when there is corroborating documentary evidence beyond mere bank statements.
sufficient_corroboration :-
    dividends_documented,
    transfers_documented,
    gift_agreement_provided,
    escrow_documented.

% Lawful source is traced where the chain from company dividends through intermediary transfers
% to the petitioner's escrow is documented and corroborated.
lawful_source_traced :-
    sufficient_corroboration.

% Documentation is sufficient when lawful source is traced
documentation_sufficient :-
    lawful_source_traced.

% All eligibility conditions necessary to accept the EB-5 petition
eligibility_met :-
    invested_requisite,
    investment_in_regional_center,
    documentation_sufficient.

% Decision and runner (provided structure)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),

        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Petitioner is described as an entrepreneur and fiscal analyst who filed an immigrant petition seeking second-preference classification as an individual of exceptional ability together with a waiver of the job-offer requirement. The Texas Service Center denied the petition after finding that she had not established eligibility for the underlying exceptional-ability classification, and she appealed. In her filings, she described her proposed U.S. endeavor as establishing and operating a company in Florida to work as an international tax consultant for American companies and to provide financial advice and planning services through a team of qualified professionals, including U.S. citizens and permanent residents. On appeal, she argued that she satisfied two additional exceptional-ability evidentiary categories, one relating to salary or other remuneration and one relating to membership in professional associations. As to remuneration, she stated that she had earned an average salary of R$3,240.00 per month as a Senior Fiscal Analyst over the course of her career and submitted a letter from her accountant stating that this salary was significantly above average compared with other professionals in her field. The record describes no additional supporting documentation showing how that compensation compared with fiscal analysts in the same geographic area. As to professional memberships, she asserted that her membership in the "Commercial Association of ______" qualified. Bylaws submitted for that association describe it as a nonprofit civil association whose membership includes legally qualified individuals and legal entities engaged in business and linked to economic life, provided they agree to follow the bylaws, and the bylaws do not indicate that membership requires a bachelor's-level degree or foreign equivalent. She also submitted a registration card issued by the Brazilian Federal Council of Administration, and the card shows a registration date of October 24, 2023, which the record describes as after the petition was filed.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. Only those who demonstrate "a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered" are eligible for classification as individuals of exceptional ability. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F) sets forth the following six criteria, at least three of which a petitioner must initially submit in order to qualify as an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, the arts, or business: 1 The Director also concluded that the Petitioner does not qualify as an advanced degree professional. On appeal, the Petitioner does not contest this finding, and thereby abandons this issue. An issue not raised on appeal is waived. See, e.g., Matter ofO-R-E-, 28 I&N Dec . 330, 336 n.5 (BIA 2021) (citing Matter ofR-A-M-, 25 I&N Dec. 657, 658 n.2 (BIA 2012)).

(A) An official academic record showing that the [individual] has a degree, diploma, certificate, or similar award from a college, university, school, or other institution of learning relating to the area of exceptional ability; (B) Evidence in the form of letter(s) from current or former employer( s) showing that the [individual] has at least ten years offull-time experience in the occupation for which he or she is being sought; (C) A license to practice the profession or certification for a particular profession or occupation; (D) Evidence that the [individual] has commanded a salary, or other remuneration for services, which demonstrates exceptional ability; (E) Evidence of membership in professional associations; or (F) Evidence of recognition for achievements and significant contributions to the industry or field by peers, governmental entities, or professional or business organizations. Meeting at least three criteria, however, does not, in and of itself, establish eligibility for this classification. If a petitioner satisfies these initial requirements, we will then conduct a final merits determination to decide whether the evidence in its totality shows that they are recognized as having a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the field. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 2010) (discussing a two-part review where the evidence is first counted and then, if it satisfies the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination); See 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.2, https://www.uscis.gov/policymanual.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Case parsed into Prolog
% Facts (translated from the Administrative Appeals Office case):
% - Petitioner filed Form I-140 seeking EB-2 (national interest waiver).
% - Petitioner proposed to work as an international tax consultant in the U.S.
% - Petitioner submitted an official academic record and documentation purporting to show at least ten years of full-time experience.
% - Petitioner submitted an accountant's statement and materials asserting an above-average salary (R$3,240.00/month) — director found this insufficient.
% - Petitioner identified membership in a "Commercial Association of ____" and supplied bylaws and a CFA registration card dated Oct 24, 2023 — director found membership/license evidence insufficient or untimely.
% - The director concluded the petitioner does not qualify as an advanced-degree professional; the petitioner did not contest that finding on appeal (issue abandoned).
% - The director denied the petition; the petitioner appealed arguing the accountant's statement and membership/CFA establish additional criteria.
% This encoding follows the regulatory criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F).
%
% We represent the six regulatory criteria as criterion_a .. criterion_f (arity 0).
% We represent the advanced-degree qualification as has_advanced_degree (arity 0).
% We represent the required final merits determination as final_merits_passed (arity 0).
% To avoid undefined-predicate errors, every predicate is defined either to succeed or to fail.

% Regulatory criteria (A)-(F):
% (A) Official academic record relating to the area of exceptional ability.
% (B) Letters from employers showing at least 10 years full-time experience.
% (C) License to practice or certification.
% (D) Evidence of commanding exceptional salary/remuneration.
% (E) Evidence of membership in professional associations.
% (F) Evidence of recognition for achievements and contributions.

% Based on the record as parsed:
% - The petitioner did submit an official academic record -> criterion_a succeeds.
% - The petitioner did submit documentation purporting at least ten years full-time experience -> criterion_b succeeds.
% - The CFA registration card is dated Oct 24, 2023; the director treated license evidence as insufficient/untimely -> criterion_c fails.
% - The accountant's statement and related documentation were found insufficient to establish exceptional remuneration -> criterion_d fails.
% - The membership evidence (bylaws and identification of the association) was found insufficient to establish qualifying professional membership -> criterion_e fails.
% - No persuasive evidence of significant recognition by peers/government/professional organizations was submitted -> criterion_f fails.

% Implemented as arity-0 predicates; true ones are facts, false ones are defined to fail.

% Criterion A: academic record submitted (true)
criterion_a.

% Criterion B: at least 10 years full-time experience (true)
criterion_b.

% Criterion C: license/certification (treated as insufficient here)
criterion_c :- fail.

% Criterion D: exceptional salary/remuneration (treated as insufficient here)
criterion_d :- fail.

% Criterion E: membership in professional associations (treated as insufficient here)
criterion_e :- fail.

% Criterion F: recognition for achievements/contributions (not demonstrated)
criterion_f :- fail.

% The petitioner does not qualify as an advanced degree professional (issue abandoned on appeal).
% Represented explicitly as a defined predicate that fails.
has_advanced_degree :- fail.

% Final merits determination: whether the totality of evidence shows a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered.
% The director/AAO concluded the petitioner did not meet the final merits requirement in the record on appeal.
final_merits_passed :- fail.

% Count how many of the regulatory criteria (A)-(F) are met (succeed)
num_criteria_met(N) :-
    findall(1,
            (   (   criterion_a
                ;   criterion_b
                ;   criterion_c
                ;   criterion_d
                ;   criterion_e
                ;   criterion_f
                )
            ),
            L),
    length(L, N).

% A petitioner qualifies as an individual of exceptional ability if they meet at least 3 of the 6 criteria
% and pass the final merits determination.
exceptional_ability_qualified :-
    num_criteria_met(N),
    N >= 3,
    final_merits_passed.

% Eligibility for the EB-2 classification requires either advanced-degree professional status
% or qualification as an individual of exceptional ability.
eligibility_met :-
    (   has_advanced_degree
    ;   exceptional_ability_qualified
    ).

% Entrypoint and decision structure required by the instructions.
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is an individual who, in 2023 at age eleven, had the Juvenile Court of [County], Tennessee issue an Order Appointing Sole Legal Custodian placing her and her siblings in their mother’s custody. That order stated it was not in her best interests to be returned to Guatemala and found that reunification with their respective biological fathers was not viable—citing the petitioner’s father’s death and a sibling’s father’s abandonment. The petitioner filed Form I-360 seeking classification as a special immigrant juvenile.

The National Benefits Center director denied the petition, determining the record did not establish a qualifying juvenile-court determination regarding parental reunification. On appeal, the petitioner submitted an Amended Order Appointing Sole Legal Custodian, issued in 2024 nunc pro tunc to 2023, which expressly states that, under Tennessee law, reunification with their biological fathers is not viable because the petitioner’s father “abandoned his children by virtue of his death.” The petitioner argues that this amended order cures the deficiency identified by the director, and that the juvenile court made the necessary findings and placed the petitioner in her mother’s custody with a best-interest determination.
statutes: To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b). Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.11 ( c )(1 ). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(2).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
order_found_not_in_best_interest_return_to_guatemala.

% The director denied based on an asserted deficiency in the original order.
director_denied_due_to_no_qualifying_reunification_finding.

% On appeal the juvenile court issued an amended order in 2024, nunc pro tunc to 2023,
% which explicitly states that reunification with the petitioner's father is not viable
% because he "abandoned his children by virtue of his death."
amended_order_2024_nunc_pro_tunc.
amended_order_reunification_finding_explicit.

% Interpretations / derived predicates
% The petitioner was placed in custody (satisfies dependency/placement requirement).

% The juvenile court has a judicial determination that reunification with a parent is not viable

% There is a judicial determination that it is not in the petitioner's best interest to return

% Eligibility: combine statutory requirements into a single predicate. If all are true,

eligibility_met :-

    ).

               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```
