The petitioner is a data scientist who holds a Master’s degree in Data Science obtained in 2021 and a Bachelor’s degree in social communication with a journalism concentration obtained in 2006. He currently works in a data science role at his employer (redacted in the record), where he is part of the Employee Learning Experiences and Insights team. His supervisor states that his work supports partners across the customer and partner solutions organization, helps ensure a data-informed learning experience for users, and impacts internal learning experiences for over 100,000 full‑time employees. The petitioner’s record includes a curriculum vitae, academic transcripts, recommendation letters from current and former colleagues, and a letter from a long‑time industry mentor in the pharmaceutical sector attesting to his skills and market knowledge.

During a selective internship sponsored by a third party, the petitioner co‑authored a paper published in the Journal of Medical Internet Research (2021); the record includes that publication and a citation record for it. His supervisor describes him as technically proficient in data science tools, able to build data products from conception through implementation, and able to advise partners on strategy and business goals. The petitioner also has past experience as an entrepreneur and as a journalist, which the record identifies as part of his background.

The petitioner proposed to use his data science training to provide expert data analytics services in the United States, with the goal of advancing the U.S. data science market and expanding the use of data across multiple industries. He intends to continue working as a data scientist with his current employer and in the information technology sector to advance that endeavor.

The petitioner filed a Form I-140 seeking EB-2 immigrant classification as an advanced‑degree professional and a national interest waiver of the job offer requirement. The Texas Service Center director denied the petition, finding that although the petitioner qualified for EB-2 classification, he had not established eligibility for a national interest waiver. The petitioner appealed that denial to the Administrative Appeals Office.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. An advanced degree is any U .S. academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. 1 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A U.S. bachelor's degree or a foreign equivalent degree followed by five years of progressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. Id. Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying classification, the petitioner must then establish eligibility for a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter ofDhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884 (AAO 2016), provides the framework 1 Profession shall include, but not be limited to, architects, engineers, lawyers, physicians, surgeons, and teachers in elementary or secondary schools, colleges, academics, or seminaries. Section 101 ( a)(32) of the Act.

for adjudicating national interest waiver pet1t10ns. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as a matter of discretion,2 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id. at 889.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

has_bachelors_degree.                     % Bachelor's degree (2006)

% Final eligibility: all conditions necessary to Accept the case

text:
The petitioner is a 27‑year‑old native and citizen of Mexico who entered the United States without inspection or admission in October 1998. In January 2014 she was granted derivative U nonimmigrant status. She filed Form I-485, Application to Register Permanent Residence or Adjust Status, in October 2017. The Vermont Service Center director denied that application and denied subsequent motions to reopen and to reconsider; the petitioner’s appeal to the Administrative Appeals Office was dismissed, and she then filed a motion to reopen before the AAO.

Relevant personal and case facts the petitioner has presented include: she has two U.S. citizen children, has lived in the United States for many years, has maintained steady employment, and has paid taxes. In 2016, while holding derivative U status, she was arrested and charged with “Assault Domestic Abuse by Use or Display of a Dangerous Weapon” and “Assault Domestic Abuse Causing Bodily Injury.” The record shows a deferred judgment on the weapon-related count that included two years of supervised probation and completion of the Iowa Domestic Abuse Program; the petitioner completed that program in 2016 and a 2017 letter indicates she was “mostly compliant” with probation terms. The record also contains the petitioner’s statements and an arrest report describing an altercation in which she admits she cut her former partner with a knife.

On motion, the petitioner submitted an updated affidavit reiterating that the 2016 incident was an altercation with her former partner and asserting that her actions were solely in self‑defense. She also submitted May and October 2021 orders of protection and screenshots and photographs she says show continued harassment and intimidation by her former partner. In her motion she argues that this new and updated evidence of ongoing abuse supports her account of the 2016 incident and that, on that basis, her I-485 should be granted in the exercise of discretion.
statutes: A motion to reopen must state the new facts to be provided in the reopened proceeding and be supported by affidavits or other documentary evidence. 8 C.F.R . § 103.5(a)(2). We may grant a motion that satisfies these requirements and demonstrates eligibility for the benefit sought. The burden ofproof is on the applicant to demonstrate eligibility by a preponderance of the evidence. Section 291 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1361; Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. 369, 375 (AAO 2010).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:

% Facts about the petitioner and procedural history
petitioner_age_27.
petitioner_nationality_mexico.
entered_without_inspection_oct_1998.
granted_derivative_u_jan_2014.
filed_i485_oct_2017.

% Criminal history and disposition facts
arrest_2016.
charged_weapon_assault.
charged_assault_bodily_injury.
deferred_judgment_weapon_charge.
deferred_judgment_requires_two_years_probation.
deferred_judgment_requires_completion_domestic_abuse_program.
completed_domestic_abuse_program_2016.
probation_status_2017_mostly_compliant.
no_documentation_probation_completed_through_apr_2018.

% Applicant's account and new evidence submitted on motion
petitioner_claims_self_defense_2016.
motion_to_reopen_submitted.
motion_included_updated_affidavit.
motion_included_protective_orders_may_oct_2021.
motion_included_screenshots_photos.
motion_purpose_challenge_denial_and_support_self_defense.

% Governing evidentiary rule (as facts/rules reflecting the regulation)
motion_must_state_new_facts.
motion_must_be_supported_by_affidavits_or_documents.

% Define when the motion states new facts (arity 0 predicate)
motion_states_new_facts :-
    motion_to_reopen_submitted,
    ( motion_included_updated_affidavit
    ; motion_included_protective_orders_may_oct_2021
    ; motion_included_screenshots_photos
    ).

% Define when the motion is supported by documentary evidence/affidavits
motion_supported_by_documentation :-

    ( motion_included_updated_affidavit
    ; motion_included_protective_orders_may_oct_2021
    ; motion_included_screenshots_photos
    ).

% The motion satisfies regulatory requirements if it both states new facts and is supported
motion_satisfies_requirements :-
    motion_states_new_facts,
    motion_supported_by_documentation.

% Burden of proof principle (fact)
burden_on_applicant.

% Define a disqualifying criminal issue for eligibility here:
% the petitioner was charged with weapon-related assault and bodily injury,
% received a deferred judgment requiring probation, and there is no documentation
% showing completion of probation through the required period.
disqualifying_crime :-
    charged_weapon_assault,
    charged_assault_bodily_injury,
    deferred_judgment_requires_two_years_probation,
    no_documentation_probation_completed_through_apr_2018.

% Preponderance of the evidence is required to establish eligibility.
% For acceptance, the applicant must show by a preponderance that the 2016 incident was self-defense
% and that favorable discretion is warranted. In this record, documentary gaps (no proof probation completed)
% and unresolved criminal disposition evidence prevent a finding that the preponderance standard is met.
preponderance_shown :-

    % require explicit documentary proof of probation completion and convincing evidence of self-defense
    completed_domestic_abuse_program_2016,
    % explicit predicate for proof of probation completion is NOT present in the record,
    % so preponderance_shown will fail (no clause providing that proof).
    fail.

% Eligibility requires that the motion satisfies requirements, there is no disqualifying crime,
% and the applicant has met the preponderance standard.
eligibility_met :-
    motion_satisfies_requirements,
    \+ disqualifying_crime,
    preponderance_shown.

% Entrypoint and decision structure (must be included as specified)

    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'

    ).

    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).

text: The petitioner is a computer systems analyst who filed an immigrant petition seeking second-preference classification as an individual of exceptional ability and a waiver of the job-offer requirement. The Nebraska Service Center director denied the petition after concluding that the petitioner had not established exceptional ability. On appeal, the petitioner submitted no additional evidence and argued that the director erred because he satisfied at least three of the required criteria and should receive a final merits review. He specifically asserted that he met the criteria labeled A, C, E, and F in the decision, while agreeing that he did not meet the salary criterion. The record on appeal describes several additional computer-related certificates submitted in response to a request for evidence as relating to courses completed after the petition filing date of April 28, 2021. The petitioner argued that, even if those certifications were not required to practice the profession, they were essential to the occupation. The record also states that he did not submit evidence that he held a license or certification to work as a computer systems analyst and did not show that such a credential was required for employment in that occupation. On appeal, he also relied on excerpts from two reference letters. He further argued that because the director did not question the national-interest-waiver factors, the director had implicitly acknowledged his eligibility for a national interest waiver.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. Because 1 The Petitioner did not claim to be nor did the record show he held an advanced degree. 2 We also acknowledge the Petitioner's concerns regarding the incorrect receipt date for his request for evidence response, as reflected in the Director's decision. However , there is no indication that this response was not properly received or considered.

this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. Exceptional ability means a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A petitioner must initially submit documentation that satisfies at least three of six categories of evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F). Meeting at least three criteria, however, does not, in and of itself, establish eligibility for this classification. See 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.5(B)(2), https://www.uscis.gov/policymanual. If a petitioner does so, we will then conduct a final merits determination to decide whether the evidence in its totality shows that they are recognized as having a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the field. Id.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the record (informational)
petitioner_occupation('computer systems analyst').
filing_date('2021-04-28').
submitted_certificates([cert(2008,'4-day','Brazil'),
                       cert(2020,'8-hour','MicrosoftCertifiedTrainer'),
                       cert(2020,'8-hour','MicrosoftCertifiedTrainer-2'),
                       cert(2020,'8-hour','other')]).
reference_letters(submitted).
rfe_response_certificates_after_filing(submitted).

% The petitioner did not claim an advanced degree and the record did not show one.
has_advanced_degree :- fail.

% Regulatory evidence categories (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F))
% For true/false predicates we use arity 0 and define them explicitly.
% All of these are defined but, based on the record and USCIS analysis, none
% meet the regulatory standard required to count toward the three-criteria test.

% (A) Official academic record showing a degree, diploma, certificate, or similar award
criterion_academic_records :- fail.

% (B) Letters documenting at least 10 years of full-time experience in the occupation
criterion_experience_letters :- fail.

% (C) A license to practice the profession or certification for the profession or occupation
% The petitioner submitted short course certificates; those were either minimal training
% and/or submitted after the filing date and thus do not establish licensure/certification.
criterion_license_certification :- fail.

% (D) Evidence that the individual has commanded a high salary or other significantly high remuneration
criterion_salary :- fail.

% (E) Membership in professional associations requiring outstanding achievements of their members
criterion_membership :- fail.

% (F) Recognition for achievements and significant contributions to the industry or field by peers, government entities, or professional/business organizations
% Reference letters described the petitioner as a valued employee but did not establish the required recognition for significant contributions to the field.
criterion_recognition :- fail.

% Map named criteria to the criterion predicates so we can count which are met.
met_criterion(acad) :- criterion_academic_records.
met_criterion(exp)  :- criterion_experience_letters.
met_criterion(cert) :- criterion_license_certification.
met_criterion(sal)  :- criterion_salary.
met_criterion(mem)  :- criterion_membership.
met_criterion(rec)  :- criterion_recognition.

% Count how many regulatory criteria are met
count_met_criteria(N) :-
    findall(C, met_criterion(C), L),
    length(L, N).

% To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, the petitioner must first
% demonstrate eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification (advanced degree or
% exceptional ability). Extraordinary/exceptional ability classification requires
% satisfaction of at least three of the six regulatory criteria.
eb2_classification_met :-
    count_met_criteria(N),
    N >= 3.

% The record indicates the director did not contest national interest waiver criteria,
% but a petitioner must still qualify for EB-2 first. For completeness we define the
% NIW requirement predicate; the determination here turns on EB-2 eligibility.
% The director did not explicitly deny NIW criteria in the decision, but EB-2 remains unmet.
niw_requirements_met :- true.

% Final eligibility: both EB-2 classification and NIW requirements must be met.
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_classification_met,
    niw_requirements_met.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),

        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a senior researcher and molecular bioscientist who filed an I-140 petition seeking EB-2 classification with a national interest waiver. She holds the equivalent of a U.S. doctoral degree (awarded in 2005) and works as a senior researcher at her institution. She described her work as involving CRISPR and genome editing, and stated her proposed endeavor is to continue research developing novel genome-editing techniques aimed at curing genetic disease and improving crop production. With her initial filing she submitted evidence of her education and experience, a personal statement outlining her proposed endeavor, recommendation and support letters, expert opinion letters, and documentation of peer-reviewed publications and citations. The Texas Service Center issued a request for evidence (RFE); in response she submitted an updated personal statement, additional expert opinion letters, and further evidence of citations to her research. The record also includes four independent advisory opinions describing her role in ongoing research and documentation that she is the primary researcher on a project funded by a U.S. National Institutes of Health grant of over $1.8 million.

After the petition was denied by the Acting Director, the petitioner appealed. In her appeal she contends the denial failed to analyze or explain perceived deficiencies in the record, misapplied the adjudicative framework, and imposed a higher standard of proof. She asserts the Acting Director did not discuss or apply the evidence in the record (including her personal statement, the independent advisory opinions, and the NIH grant documentation) when finding documentary support lacking, and that the denial’s lack of detail deprived her of a sufficient opportunity to prepare a substantive appeal. These assertions form the basis for her appeal.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. An advanced degree is any U.S. academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. 1 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A U.S. bachelor's degree or a foreign equivalent degree followed by five years ofprogressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. Id. 1 Profession shall include, but not be limited to, architects, engineers, lawyers, physicians, surgeons, and teachers in elementary or secondary schools, colleges, academics, or seminaries. Section 10l(a)(32) of the Act.

Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or an individual of exceptional ability, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion,2 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
advanced_degree_equivalent.
% PhD-equivalent awarded in 2005

employed_as_senior_researcher.
proposed_endeavor_genome_editing.
publications_peer_reviewed.
citation_counts_documented.
expert_opinion_letters.
advisory_opinions_independent.
nih_grant_primary_researcher.
recommendation_support_letters.
updated_personal_statement.
supplemental_evidence_after_rfe.

% EB-2 qualification: advanced degree professional (here satisfied by PhD equivalent).
eb2_qualification :-
    advanced_degree_equivalent.

% Dhanasar prong 1: proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance.
% The petitioner described research to cure genetic diseases and improve crop production,
% which carries both substantial merit and national importance.
prong1_substantial_merit :-
    proposed_endeavor_genome_editing.

prong1_national_importance :-
    proposed_endeavor_genome_editing.

% Dhanasar prong 2: the individual is well-positioned to advance the proposed endeavor.
% Consider education, position, publications, citations, expert/advisory opinions, and grant leadership.
well_positioned :-
    advanced_degree_equivalent,
    employed_as_senior_researcher,
    publications_peer_reviewed,
    citation_counts_documented,
    expert_opinion_letters,
    advisory_opinions_independent,
    nih_grant_primary_researcher.

% Dhanasar prong 3: on balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
% Factors supporting this finding include leadership of a substantial NIH-funded project and
% the demonstrated national importance of the research.
prong3_benefit_waiving :-
    nih_grant_primary_researcher,
    proposed_endeavor_genome_editing,
    employed_as_senior_researcher.

% National interest waiver is supported when all three prongs are met.
national_interest_waiver_conditions :-
    prong1_substantial_merit,
    prong1_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    prong3_benefit_waiving.

% Overall eligibility requires EB-2 qualification and meeting Dhanasar's national interest framework.
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_qualification,
    national_interest_waiver_conditions.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The Applicant is a native and citizen of Mexico who entered the United States without admission or parole in April 2009 at age 16. USCIS granted him U nonimmigrant status in 2017 as the victim of a felonious assault, and he filed this application to adjust status to lawful permanent residence in October 2020 while still in that status. The Director of the Vermont Service Center denied the application after concluding that the Applicant's positive and mitigating equities did not outweigh the adverse factors in his case, and the Applicant appealed, submitting an additional brief and supporting materials.

In response to requests for evidence and on appeal, the record received affidavits, court dispositions, a citation and criminal complaint relating to a 2017 arrest, a certificate of completion for a Victim Impact Panel, arrest and incident reports, witness statements regarding 2021 arrests, a supervised probation order, an order of protection, and material concerning New York disorderly-conduct law. The record also includes evidence and arguments concerning the Applicant's lengthy residence in the United States, family ties, victimization, employment as a drywall taper since 2015, homeownership, payment of federal income taxes, mental health treatment, and claimed hardship to relatives if he returned to Mexico.

The record reflects a 2017 New York arrest for driving while intoxicated or impaired and related traffic offenses. It also reflects a 2021 New York arrest for falsely reporting an incident and insurance fraud. In a sworn police deposition from that matter, an officer stated that the Applicant reported his vehicle stolen, but a later investigation found that the vehicle had not been stolen and that he was still operating it on the date of the reported theft, and the officer further stated that the Applicant filed a fraudulent theft claim with his automobile insurer. The record further reflects a 2021 New York arrest for endangering the welfare of a child, and related materials indicate that the incident involved the Applicant's sister, who was a minor at the time, a point the record states the Applicant does not dispute. The Applicant addressed his arrests in affidavits submitted before the Director and on appeal. On appeal, he argues that the Director wrongly treated his history as showing a pattern of concerning behavior, that several resulting convictions were for disorderly conduct rather than crimes under New York law, and that his positive equities outweigh negative inferences from his arrest history.
statutes:
U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may adjust the status of a U nonimmigrant to that of an LPR if they meet all other eligibility requirements and, "in the opinion" of USCIS, their"continued presence in the United States is justified on humanitarian grounds, to ensure family unity, or is otherwise in the public interest." Section 245(m) of the Act. The applicant bears the burden of establishing their eligibility, section 291 of the Act, 8 U .S.C. § 1361, and must do so by a preponderance ofthe evidence. Matter ofChawathe, 25 l&N Dec. 369,375 (AAO 2010). This burden includes establishing that discretion should be exercised in their favor, and USCIS may take into account all relevant factors in making its discretionary determination. 8 C.F.R . §§ 245.24(b)(6), (d)(l 1). 1 Additionally, the Director noted that the Applicant did not submit a complete copy of his Mexican passport, a new Form I-693, Report oflmmigration Medical Examination and Vaccination Record, a complete list of his employment history, or an answer to question #68 on his Form 1-485. 2 The Applicant submits updated affidavits from himself, his spouse, his mother, his three siblings, court dispositions for hisl 2021 arrests, a Form I-485 addendum , a complete copy of his Mexican passport, a summary of his psychotherapy visits, an employment letter, and a travel advisory for Mexico .

A favorable exercise of discretion to grant an applicant adjustment of status to that ofLPR is generally warranted in the absence of adverse factors and presence of favorable factors. Matter ofArai, 13 I&N Dec. 494, 496 (BIA 1970). Favorable factors include, but are not limited to, family unity, length of residence in the United States, employment, community involvement, and good moral character. Id.; see also 7 USCIS Policy Manual A.10(B)(2), https://www.uscis.gov/policy-manual (providing guidance regarding adjudicative factors to consider in discretionary determinations). However, where adverse factors are present, the applicant may submit evidence establishing mitigating equities. See 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(d)(ll) (stating that, "[w]here adverse factors are present, an applicant may offset these by submitting supporting documentation establishing mitigating equities that the applicant wants USCIS to consider when determining whether or not a favorable exercise ofdiscretion is appropriate").
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the petitioner (boolean predicates use arity 0)
native_and_citizen_of_mexico.
entered_without_admission_in_april_2009_at_age_16.
has_u_status.                             % granted U nonimmigrant status in 2017
filed_i485_in_october_2020_while_in_u.    % filed I-485 while in U status
approx_age_31.
years_in_us_approx_15.
married_to_lpr.
two_us_citizen_children.
works_as_drywall_taper_since_2015.
owns_home.
paid_federal_income_taxes_several_years.

% Evidence and post-decision submissions
submitted_initial_affidavits_and_documents.
submitted_additional_evidence_on_appeal.
submitted_complete_mexican_passport.
submitted_form_i693_updated.
submitted_employment_letter.
submitted_psychotherapy_summary.
submitted_form_i485_addendum.

% Criminal history (boolean predicates)
% 2017 driving-related conviction(s)
convicted_dwai_and_unsafe_lane_change_2017.
attended_victim_impact_panel_april_2018.
driver_license_suspended_90_days_2017.
received_one_year_conditional_discharge_2017.

% 2021 insurance/theft report -> reduced to disorderly conduct
arrest_for_false_report_and_insurance_fraud_2021.
conviction_reduced_to_disorderly_conduct_2021.
paid_225_fine_february_2023.

% 2021 endangering welfare of a child (domestic incident)
arrest_for_endangering_welfare_of_child_2021.
convicted_endangering_welfare_of_child_2021.
sentenced_to_12_months_supervised_probation_2021.
protective_order_in_effect_through_2022.

% Behavioral/contextual facts and mitigation evidence
explains_alcohol_use_in_2017_and_2021.
states_panicked_after_damaging_car_2021.
accepted_responsibility_and_expressed_remorse.
sought_therapy_after_last_encounter.
states_rarely_drinks_since.
committed_to_obey_us_laws_and_support_family.

% Legal and policy facts (booleans for conditions relevant to discretionary analysis)
applicant_bears_burden_of_proof.
uscis_may_exercise_discretion_under_section_245m.
favorable_exercise_generally_warranted_in_absence_of_adverse_factors.
adverse_factors_may_be_offset_by_mitigating_equities.

% Weight assignments for adverse factors (numeric constants via predicates)
weight_endangering(10).
weight_dwai(2).
weight_disorderly(1).
weight_protective_order(2).

% Weight assignments for favorable / mitigating factors
weight_family_unity(3).           % spouse is LPR
weight_children(3).               % two US citizen children
weight_years_in_us(2).            % ~15 years residence
weight_employment(1).             % steady employment since 2015
weight_homeownership(0).
weight_taxes(0).
weight_remorse_and_therapy(1).
weight_victim_panel(1).
weight_submitted_required_docs(1).

% Define predicates that map facts to components used in scoring
family_unity :- married_to_lpr.
children_us :- two_us_citizen_children.
years_residence_long :- years_in_us_approx_15.
stable_employment :- works_as_drywall_taper_since_2015.
has_homeownership :- owns_home.
has_tax_payment_history :- paid_federal_income_taxes_several_years.
showed_remorse_and_sought_treatment :- accepted_responsibility_and_expressed_remorse, sought_therapy_after_last_encounter.
completed_victim_program :- attended_victim_impact_panel_april_2018.
submitted_required_docs :- submitted_complete_mexican_passport, submitted_form_i693_updated, submitted_employment_letter, submitted_form_i485_addendum, submitted_psychotherapy_summary.

% Adverse component predicates
adverse_endangering :- convicted_endangering_welfare_of_child_2021.
adverse_dwai :- convicted_dwai_and_unsafe_lane_change_2017.
adverse_disorderly :- conviction_reduced_to_disorderly_conduct_2021.
adverse_protective_order :- protective_order_in_effect_through_2022.

% Compute adverse_score by summing relevant adverse weights
adverse_score(Total) :-
    (adverse_endangering -> weight_endangering(W1) ; W1 = 0),
    (adverse_dwai -> weight_dwai(W2) ; W2 = 0),
    (adverse_disorderly -> weight_disorderly(W3) ; W3 = 0),
    (adverse_protective_order -> weight_protective_order(W4) ; W4 = 0),
    Temp is W1 + W2 + W3 + W4,
    Total is Temp.

% Compute favorable_score by summing favorable weights
favorable_score(Total) :-
    (family_unity -> weight_family_unity(F1) ; F1 = 0),
    (children_us -> weight_children(F2) ; F2 = 0),
    (years_residence_long -> weight_years_in_us(F3) ; F3 = 0),
    (stable_employment -> weight_employment(F4) ; F4 = 0),
    (has_homeownership -> weight_homeownership(F5) ; F5 = 0),
    (has_tax_payment_history -> weight_taxes(F6) ; F6 = 0),
    (showed_remorse_and_sought_treatment -> weight_remorse_and_therapy(F7) ; F7 = 0),
    (completed_victim_program -> weight_victim_panel(F8) ; F8 = 0),
    (submitted_required_docs -> weight_submitted_required_docs(F9) ; F9 = 0),
    Temp is F1+F2+F3+F4+F5+F6+F7+F8+F9,
    Total is Temp.

% Mitigation_score: additional offset for acceptance of responsibility and other equities beyond base favorable factors
% (kept modest to reflect that mitigation exists but may be limited against serious adverse factors)
mitigation_score(Score) :-
    (accepted_responsibility_and_expressed_remorse -> Score = 0 ; Score = 0).

% Core eligibility rule:
% applicant must have been in U status and filed while in U, must have submitted required docs,
% and favorable + mitigation must outweigh adverse score for a favorable exercise of discretion
eligibility_met :-
    has_u_status,
    filed_i485_in_october_2020_while_in_u,
    submitted_required_docs,           % applicant eventually provided the missing submissions on appeal
    favorable_score(F),
    adverse_score(A),
    mitigation_score(M),
    TotalF is F + M,
    TotalF > A.

% Decision entrypoint: binary output 'Accepted' or 'Dismissed'
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a technology company that filed Form I-140 seeking employment‑based second preference (EB‑2) classification for the beneficiary and a national interest waiver of the job‑offer requirement. The beneficiary is a software engineer employed by the petitioner on its WebXT engineering systems team. In the initial petition the petitioner described the beneficiary’s work as in advanced computing, data processing, and analysis techniques, and said he is an expert in Cloud technology. The petitioner stated the beneficiary’s responsibilities include migrating large amounts of data from a legacy source‑control system to Git, developing platforms for code changes, gathering engineering‑satisfaction data, creating gating solutions to enforce software engineering practices, improving team security by standardizing authentication, and developing a Git filter called “Git‑hfs” to assist with data migration.

In response to a request for evidence (RFE), the petitioner additionally reported that the beneficiary built a cutting‑edge AI‑powered application that supports the software development lifecycle by automatically reviewing code and providing suggestions. The petitioner submitted supporting materials with the initial filing and with the RFE response, including news articles and reports about advanced computing, government memoranda referencing advanced computing, a letter from an associate professor discussing the beneficiary’s skills and the importance of advanced computing, a letter from a skip‑level manager with specifics about the beneficiary’s work on Git‑hfs, and a letter from a co‑worker expressing the view that Git‑hfs could be used by other organizations.

After the Nebraska Service Center denied the petition, the petitioner appealed that decision to the Administrative Appeals Office. In the appeal the petitioner argued that the record and the submitted letters and evidence (including the professor’s letter and the skip‑level manager’s letter about Git‑hfs) demonstrate the beneficiary’s work and its significance, and contended that the decision failed to properly consider that evidence.
statutes:
An advanced degree is any United States academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. A United States bachelor's degree or foreign equivalent degree followed by five years ofprogressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. If a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec . 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship

and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Case facts and evidentiary record (parsed from the provided case description).
% Use arity-0 predicates for yes/no facts where appropriate.

% Parties and filing
petitioner_is_technology_company.
beneficiary_is_software_engineer.
beneficiary_employed_by_petitioner.
beneficiary_team_webxt_engineering.

% Proposed endeavor as described in initial petition (task-level facts).
proposed_endeavor_advanced_computing.
proposed_task_migrate_legacy_source_control_to_git.
proposed_task_develop_platforms_for_code_changes.
proposed_task_collect_engineering_satisfaction_data.
proposed_task_create_gating_solutions_for_best_practices.
proposed_task_standardize_authentication_practices.
proposed_task_develop_git_hfs.

% Evidence submitted with the record
evidence_articles_about_advanced_computing.
letter_associate_professor_attesting_skills_and_discussing_general_significance.
letter_coworker_describing_git_hfs_and_expressing_belief_in_potential_use_by_others.

% RFE and new material in response
rfe_responded.
rfe_added_ai_application_not_in_original_filing.  % AI-powered app (automatic code review) was described only in RFE response

% Director's denial basis (as statements in the record)
director_concluded_record_did_not_establish_national_importance.
director_concluded_record_did_not_establish_well_positioned.
director_concluded_record_did_not_establish_benefit_to_united_states.

% Petitioner's arguments on appeal (assertions)
petitioner_asserts_evidence_was_not_adequately_considered.
petitioner_asserts_beneficiary_work_has_impact_and_significance.

% EB-2 degree/equivalence facts
% The record as provided here does NOT include proof of an advanced degree or of a bachelor's + 5 years progressive experience.
% Define these predicates but make them fail so they are explicitly present and determinable.
there_is_advanced_degree :- fail.
there_is_bachelors_plus_five_years :- fail.

% Underlying EB-2 eligibility follows statutory rule:
underlying_eb2_eligible :-
    (   there_is_advanced_degree
    ;   there_is_bachelors_plus_five_years
    ).

% Dhanasar national interest waiver framework encoded:
% Three prongs: (1) substantial merit and national importance; (2) individual well-positioned;
% (3) on balance, benefit to the United States.

% Assess whether the proposed endeavor has substantial merit.
% The record contains general evidence that advanced computing has importance.
proposed_endeavor_has_substantial_merit :-
    evidence_articles_about_advanced_computing.

% Assess whether the proposed endeavor has national importance.
% National importance requires the endeavor to have impact beyond the petitioner and
% to be reasonably likely to have wider significance or application.
% The record contains:
% - internal tools and migration work (git_hfs, migration) described in initial filing,
% - coworker expresses belief Git-hfs could be used by other organizations (opinion),
% - associate professor letter discusses general significance of the field, not specific adoption,
% - AI application (automated code review) was described for the first time in the RFE response (creates a specificity problem).
% There is no independent evidence of external adoption, scalability, or market/field recognition of Git-hfs or the beneficiary's tools.
evidence_of_external_adoption_or_scalability :- fail.
opinion_of_coworker_supports_potential_external_use :-
    letter_coworker_describing_git_hfs_and_expressing_belief_in_potential_use_by_others.
specificity_problem_due_to_new_material :-
    rfe_added_ai_application_not_in_original_filing.

proposed_endeavor_has_national_importance :-
    proposed_endeavor_has_substantial_merit,
    % require either independent evidence of external adoption/scalability OR specific, credible evidence tying the beneficiary's work to broader impact
    (   evidence_of_external_adoption_or_scalability
    ;   (   opinion_of_coworker_supports_potential_external_use,
            \+ specificity_problem_due_to_new_material,
            letter_associate_professor_attesting_skills_and_discussing_general_significance
        )
    ).

% Combine prong 1
prong_substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    proposed_endeavor_has_substantial_merit,
    proposed_endeavor_has_national_importance.

% Assess whether the individual is well-positioned to advance the endeavor.
% Factors typically include a record of success, relevant education/experience, evidence of influence, and a plan.
% The record here contains letters (associate professor and coworker) but lacks documented independent demonstrations
% of national-scale impact, adoption, awards, publications, or commercialization of beneficiary's tools.
evidence_of_record_of_success_like_national_recognition_or_adoption :- fail.
associate_professor_letter_present :-
    letter_associate_professor_attesting_skills_and_discussing_general_significance.
coworker_letter_present :-
    letter_coworker_describing_git_hfs_and_expressing_belief_in_potential_use_by_others.

individual_well_positioned :-
    % require both demonstrable record of success or adoption AND credible letters/plan
    (   evidence_of_record_of_success_like_national_recognition_or_adoption
    ),
    (   associate_professor_letter_present
    ;   coworker_letter_present
    ).

% Assess whether, on balance, waiving the job offer and labor certification requirements would benefit the United States.
% This is a discretionary balancing test that looks to benefit magnitude and necessity of waiver.
% Absent demonstrated national importance and a well-positioned individual, balance disfavors waiver.
benefit_to_united_states_on_balance :-
    prong_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    individual_well_positioned,
    % also require that benefits outweigh the interest served by labor certification
    (   evidence_of_external_adoption_or_scalability
    ;   evidence_of_record_of_success_like_national_recognition_or_adoption
    ).

% All three Dhanasar prongs must be met for an NIW recommendation.
niw_prongs_met :-
    prong_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    individual_well_positioned,
    benefit_to_united_states_on_balance.

% Full eligibility requires EB-2 underlying eligibility AND meeting the NIW prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    underlying_eb2_eligible,
    niw_prongs_met.

% Entrypoint and decision output as required by the prompt.
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a producer in the entertainment and advertising industries. Her body of work includes production credits on commercial and social media campaigns as well as television and film projects. She currently serves as head of production at an internationally known advertising agency and has received an offer to continue similar work with a branch of a U.S.-based company. 

She has submitted evidence showing awards and honors earned as part of production teams on numerous advertising campaigns, including campaigns for well-known brands and platforms. Examples of awards associated with projects she produced include recognitions such as Best Social Media Campaign, Most Creative Content Strategy, Best Digital Series Video, Best Digital Art Direction, Best Illustrative Representation, Best Digital Public Relations Campaign, Best Branded Film Content, and Best Online Film Content. She produced social- and new-media campaigns in 2017 that used traditional art (Ebru) to promote a streaming platform, work that quickly gained national attention.

The petitioner has documented specific campaign performance metrics: one social-responsibility campaign she produced was reported to have been quoted 19,000 times, created over 1.2 million impressions, and reached an audience of about 3 million people in Turkey (described as roughly 3% of the country’s population). That campaign later was pulled from circulation after individuals connected to it were arrested or detained, and some journalists who shared the campaign remain imprisoned.

She has also described leadership and advocacy activities outside individual productions. She co-founded and served as Vice-Chairman of an industry organization that was incorporated into a larger entity following her efforts to address the exclusion of advertising professionals from certain labor protections in Turkey. A letter from the president of Turkey’s Cinema and Broadcasting Union recounts her role in advocating for advertisers’ rights, producing reports documenting industry conditions (including a cited finding that 70% of advertisement professionals had been forced to leave positions because of substandard working conditions), and highlighting the economic contribution of advertisers (a reported $120 billion annually, per government statistical sources cited in the record). Her supporters describe her leadership as having empowered many industry professionals.

In support of the petition she submitted letters of recommendation and other documentation about projects, companies, awards, and media coverage detailing her roles and contributions to successful campaigns.

Reason for appealing:
The petitioner appealed the denial of her immigrant petition on the ground that the evidence she submitted was not properly evaluated. She contends that the supporting documentation—including awards and campaign metrics, letters describing her leadership and advocacy, and letters of support about her roles on high-profile projects—establishes the accomplishments she claims, and she argues that the initial decision incorrectly assessed that evidence.
statutes:
An individual is eligible for the extraordinary ability classification if they have extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics which has been demonstrated by sustained national or international acclaim and their achievements have been recognized in the field through extensive documentation; they seek to enter the United States to continue work in the area of extraordinary ability; and their entry into the United States will substantially benefit prospectively the United States. Section 203(b)(l)(A) of the Act. The term "extraordinary ability" refers only to those individuals in "that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2). The implementing regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) sets forth a multi-part analysis. First, a petitioner may demonstrate international recognition of a beneficiary's achievements in the field through a one-time achievement (that is, a major, internationally recognized award). Absent such an achievement, a petitioner must provide sufficient qualifying documentation demonstrating that a beneficiary meets at least three of the ten criteria listed at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x).

Where a petitioner meets these initial evidence requirements, we then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination and assess whether the record shows sustained national or international acclaim and demonstrates that the individual is among the small percentage at the very top of the field of endeavor. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 2010) ( discussing a two-part review where the documentation is first counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination); see also Visinscaia v. Beers, 4 F. Supp. 3d 126, 131-32 (D.D.C. 2013); Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the record (all boolean predicates are arity 0)
% Evidence items the petitioner submitted or circumstances shown in the record.
has_team_awards.           % awards/prizes received as part of production teams
has_media_coverage.        % objective media coverage corroborating activities
leadership_role.           % head of production; co-founder/vice chair of advocacy org
original_contribution.     % initiated reports/advocacy that impacted industry practices
commercial_success.        % campaigns with nationwide reach / millions of impressions
has_offered_position.      % offered position with a branch of a U.S.-based company
intends_to_continue_work.  % petitioner intends to continue similar work in US
beneficial_to_us.          % entry will prospectively substantially benefit US

% Map facts to the regulatory criteria (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x))
% We model the criteria the record supports based on the supplied facts.
criterion_i :- has_team_awards.         % evidence of lesser nationally/internationally recognized prizes/awards
criterion_iii :- has_media_coverage.    % published material about the person in major media
criterion_v :- original_contribution.  % original contributions of major significance
criterion_viii :- leadership_role.      % leading or critical role for distinguished organizations
criterion_x :- commercial_success.     % commercial successes in the performing arts (analogous: mass audience/ad campaigns)

% Count how many qualifying criteria are met
criteria_met_count(N) :-
    (  criterion_i -> A = 1 ; A = 0 ),
    (  criterion_iii -> B = 1 ; B = 0 ),
    (  criterion_v -> C = 1 ; C = 0 ),
    (  criterion_viii -> D = 1 ; D = 0 ),
    (  criterion_x -> E = 1 ; E = 0 ),
    N is A + B + C + D + E.

criteria_met_at_least(K) :-
    criteria_met_count(N),
    N >= K.

% Kazarian two-step: initial evidentiary showing and final merits determination.
% Initial showing: at least 3 of the regulatory criteria.
initial_evidence_ok :- criteria_met_at_least(3).

% Final merits assessment: we model sustained national/international acclaim and being among "that small percentage"
% For this case record, treat sustained acclaim as meeting at least 4 criteria.
sustained_acclaim :- criteria_met_count(N), N >= 4.

% Being among the very top of the field (small percentage) — modeled here as having leadership, commercial impact, and media recognition
top_of_field :- leadership_role, commercial_success, has_media_coverage.

% Eligibility requires initial evidentiary showing, sustained acclaim & top status, plus intent/offer and benefit to US
eligibility_met :-
    has_offered_position,
    intends_to_continue_work,
    beneficial_to_us,
    initial_evidence_ok,
    sustained_acclaim,
    top_of_field.

% Decision and run entrypoint (as requested)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a U.S. healthcare staffing company that filed an employment‑based immigrant petition on December 30, 2022 to permanently employ the beneficiary as a registered nurse, with a proffered annual wage of $49,192 and a Schedule A designation as a professional nurse. The petitioner stated it intended to assign the beneficiary to work at a client hospital in Kansas. In support of the petition, the company submitted a Department of Labor prevailing wage determination, a copy of the posted notice of the labor certification application, the beneficiary’s U.S. Bachelor of Science in Nursing (earned December 2021), and employment letters documenting the beneficiary’s work history: approximately two months as an “advanced nurse extern” at a hospital in Alabama (June 14, 2021 – August 6, 2021) and employment by the petitioner in the offered position from February 14, 2022 through the petition filing date (about ten months). The petitioner also submitted an amended labor certification form deleting a one‑year experience requirement and referenced the employer’s prior prevailing wage request form, which listed one year of experience.

The petitioner appealed the Nebraska Service Center’s denial. In its appeal the company argued that USCIS should not require that Schedule A beneficiaries meet the precise experience language listed on the labor certification form and cited prior agency guidance to that effect. The petitioner maintained that the beneficiary’s qualifications — including her U.S. nursing degree and the work and externship documented in its submissions — demonstrate that she meets the requirements for Schedule A classification. The company also argued that the externship was part of the beneficiary’s formal nursing education, that the beneficiary’s additional post‑secondary education should be treated as training, that experience the beneficiary gained while employed by the petitioner should be considered, and that the one‑year experience entry on the labor certification form could be treated as a scrivener’s error.
statutes:
Immigration as a skilled worker typically follows a three-step process. First, to permanently fill a position in the United States with a foreign worker, a prospective employer must obtain certification from the U.S. Department of Labor (DOL) . See section 212(a)(5)(A)(i) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1182(a)(5)(A)(i). Second, if DOL approves a position for a foreign worker, an employer must submit the certified labor application with an immigrant visa petition to U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS). Section 204(a)(l)(F) of the Act, 8 U.S.C . § 1154(a)(l)(F). Finally, if USCIS grants a petition, the noncitizen beneficiary may apply abroad for an immigrant visa or, if eligible, adjustment of status in the United States. See section 245(a) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1255(a).

DOL, however, has predetermined that the United States lacks sufficient "professional nurses" and that noncitizens' employment in these Schedule A jobs would not harm the wages or working conditions of U.S. employees in similar positions. 20 C.F.R. § 656.5. Thus, organizations seeking to employ noncitizen registered nurses need not file individual labor certification applications with DOL to test the U.S. labor market for qualified workers. Rather, DOL authorizes USCIS to adjudicate Schedule A labor certification applications in petition proceedings. 20 C.F.R. § 656.15(a). Thus, in this matter, USCIS mies not only on the immigrant visa petition, but also on its accompanying labor certification application. See 20 C.F.R. § 656.15( e) ( describing USCIS' determinations on Schedule A labor certification applications as "conclusive and final").
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the case (use arity-0 for yes/no predicates)
% Schedule A and labor certification requirements
schedule_a_requires_experience_in_job_offered.    % the application required experience "in the job offered"
schedule_a_listed_one_year_experience.            % the application listed 1 year of experience
schedule_a_disallows_alternate_experience.        % the application did not permit experience in an alternate occupation

% Beneficiary education and credentials (as provided at filing)
beneficiary_has_us_bsn.                           % U.S. Bachelor of Science in Nursing (Dec 2021)

% Experience evidence submitted with the petition
extern_days(54).                                  % externship: 54 days (June 14, 2021 - Aug 6, 2021)
extern_part_of_education.                         % the externship was part of the beneficiary's formal nursing education
extern_under_supervision.                         % extern duties performed under direction of a Registered Nurse
extern_as_needed_status.                          % extern was "in an as needed status"

employment_days_at_filing(320).                   % petitioner-employed in offered position: ~320 days at time of filing (Feb 14, 2022 - Dec 30, 2022)

% Regulatory context (schedule A adjudication by USCIS is conclusive on the listed requirements)
uscis_adjudicates_schedule_a_and_is_conclusive.    % 20 C.F.R. § 656.15(e)

% Rule-based determinations encoded as predicates

% The Schedule A application listed a clear, position-specific requirement:
% experience must be "in the job offered" and alternate-occupation experience is not accepted.
% Therefore only experience that meets that exact requirement and existed at filing counts.

% Whether the externship counts as qualifying "experience in the job offered"
% Under the recorded facts the externship was part of education, performed under supervision, and brief.
% It therefore does not meet the Schedule A's "experience in the job offered" requirement.
extern_counts_as_experience :- 
    % defined but deliberately fails under the present facts (extern was part of education, supervised, and not full employment)
    extern_part_of_education,
    !,
    fail.
extern_counts_as_experience :- 
    % fallthrough: no other evidence was submitted to show extern equaled qualifying employment, so fail
    fail.

% Post-filing experience does not count toward the required minimum unless the labor certification allowed it.
% Here we only count experience that existed at the time of filing.
post_filing_experience_counts :- fail.

% Training/education does not substitute for the explicit one-year experience requirement listed on the labor certification.
education_counts_as_experience :- fail.

% Compute total qualifying experience in days as of the filing date.
total_experience_days_at_filing(Days) :-
    employment_days_at_filing(EmpDays),
    (   extern_counts_as_experience
    ->  extern_days(Ext), Days is EmpDays + Ext
    ;   Days = EmpDays
    ).

% Determine whether the beneficiary meets the Schedule A experience requirement at filing.
required_experience_days(365).

meets_experience :-
    total_experience_days_at_filing(Days),
    required_experience_days(Req),
    Days >= Req.

% Eligibility: all necessary conditions must be met to accept the petition.
% For this simplified model we require at minimum that the Schedule A experience requirement be met.
% (Other requirements such as licensing, NCLEX, background checks, etc., were not established in the facts
% and therefore are not treated as satisfied here; the experience requirement alone is dispositive.)
eligibility_met :-
    meets_experience.

% Entrypoint and decision logic (must be included exactly as required)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a U-2 nonimmigrant who applied for adjustment to lawful permanent resident status by filing Form I-485 on October 18, 2021. Her spouse received U-1 nonimmigrant status (February 1, 2017). The petitioner received a U-2 visa in Kathmandu, Nepal on April 26, 2018 and was admitted to the United States in U-2 status on September 18, 2018. Her initial authorized period of stay was until January 31, 2021. She filed Form I-539 to extend/change nonimmigrant status on January 26, 2021; that filing resulted in an extension of her U-2 status until March 10, 2023. The petitioner has not departed the United States since her September 18, 2018 admission.

The Vermont Service Center director denied the petitioner’s Form I-485, concluding she had not met the required three-year continuous physical presence in the United States. On appeal, the petitioner contends she was admitted on September 18, 2018 and therefore was physically present in the United States for at least three continuous years prior to filing her Form I-485 on October 18, 2021.
statutes: U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may adjust the status of a U nonimmigrant to a lawful permanent resident (LPR) if that individual demonstrates that he or she has been physically present in the United States for a continuous period of at least three years since admission as a U nonimmigrant, has not unreasonably refused to provide assistance in a criminal investigation or prosecution, and the individual's continued presence in the United States is justified on humanitarian grounds, to ensure family unity, or is otherwise in the public interest. Section 245(m) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(f).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the case
% Dates as admitted_as_u_nonimmigrant(Year,Month,Day).
admitted_as_u_nonimmigrant(2018,9,18).
filed_i485(2021,10,18).

% Other factual predicates (yes/no predicates are arity 0 as requested)
beneficiary_of_derivative_u.
no_departure_since_admission.
not_refused_to_assist.
continued_presence_justified.

% Administrative facts about extensions and approvals (for completeness)
i918a_filed(2017,10,1).          % month/day not critical here; represent filing month
spouse_i918a_approved(2019,3,11).
u_visa_issued(2018,4,26).
i539_filed(2021,1,26).
u_status_extended_until(2023,3,10).

% Rule: "admission as a U nonimmigrant" is the actual admission date, not the spouse's I-918A approval date.
admission_start_date(Y,M,D) :-
    admitted_as_u_nonimmigrant(Y,M,D).

% Check whether the petitioner was physically present continuously for at least three years
% since admission as a U nonimmigrant up to the I-485 filing date.
physically_present_three_years :-
    admission_start_date(AY,AM,AD),
    filed_i485(FY,FM,FD),
    YearsDiff is FY - AY,
    (   YearsDiff > 3
    ->  true
    ;   YearsDiff =:= 3,
        (   FM > AM
        ->  true
        ;   FM =:= AM,
            FD >= AD
        )
    ).

% Continuous presence also requires no departures since admission (fact)
continuous_presence :-
    no_departure_since_admission,
    physically_present_three_years.

% Assistance in a criminal investigation/prosecution: must not have unreasonably refused.
assistance_requirement_met :-
    not_refused_to_assist.

% Continued presence requirement: petitioner must show humanitarian/family unity/public interest;
% derivative U status and spouse approval support that showing.
continued_presence_requirement_met :-
    beneficiary_of_derivative_u,
    spouse_i918a_approved(_,_,_),
    continued_presence_justified.

% eligibility_met is all necessary conditions for acceptance
eligibility_met :-
    continuous_presence,
    assistance_requirement_met,
    continued_presence_requirement_met.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner is a research scientist in organic chemistry who seeks EB-2 immigrant classification with a waiver of the job-offer requirement. The Director of the Texas Service Center denied the petition on the ground that the petitioner had not shown that waiving the job-offer and labor-certification requirements would be in the national interest, and the matter was then taken to the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal. The petitioner states that she proposes to continue research on green and environmentally friendly synthetic methods for preparing biologically and medically relevant nanomaterials, organic compounds, and carbon dots for environmental remediation, including wastewater treatment, pollutant absorption, and green chemistry. She states that her research focuses on food safety, purification of water pollutants, and synthesis of new compounds and catalysts with biological and medicinal properties. The record includes evidence of her research on reducing chemical waste in wastewater remediation, support letters from experts in her field, including a letter from her doctoral advisor, and journal articles authored by her that the record says were cited by other researchers. The record also describes two of her papers as ranking in the top 10 percent and top 20 percent of most-cited papers in her field. An expert opinion letter in the record states that she developed a method of safely and effectively removing methylene blue from wastewater and describes methylene blue as a carcinogenic, toxic, non-biodegradable, long-term hazardous textile dye. The petitioner has also presented a plan to continue research in food safety, waste and wastewater pollution, biopharmaceutics, and green chemistry with sustainable development. On appeal, she argues that the Director did not sufficiently explain the analysis of the evidence or identify specific reasons for concluding that she was not well-positioned to advance the proposed endeavor, and she again asserts an urgent national interest in environmental stewardship that she says her proposed endeavor addresses.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. An advanced degree is any United States academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. A United States bachelor's degree or foreign equivalent degree followed by five years of progressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). If a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship

and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
holds_phd.
employment_fda.
research_on_green_synthesis.
aims_environmental_remediation.
focus_food_safety.
authored_cited_journal_articles.
top_cited_papers.
expert_letters_submitted.
developed_method_remove_methylene_blue.
submitted_research_plan.
government_guidance_documents.

% Underlying EB-2 qualification: advanced degree (doctoral) qualifies
underlying_eb2_qualified :-
    holds_phd.

% Dhanasar framework - substantial merit
% Research in green synthesis, environmental remediation, or food safety is treated as having substantial merit.
proposed_endeavor_substantial_merit :-
    research_on_green_synthesis.
proposed_endeavor_substantial_merit :-
    aims_environmental_remediation.
proposed_endeavor_substantial_merit :-
    focus_food_safety.

% Dhanasar framework - national importance
% Environmental remediation or food safety research coupled with government guidance showing national concern indicates national importance.
proposed_endeavor_national_importance :-
    aims_environmental_remediation,
    government_guidance_documents.
proposed_endeavor_national_importance :-
    focus_food_safety,
    government_guidance_documents.

% Dhanasar framework - well-positioned to advance the endeavor
% Evidence of an advanced degree, relevant employment, publications with citations, expert letters, and a research plan show the petitioner is well-positioned.
well_positioned :-
    holds_phd,
    employment_fda,
    authored_cited_journal_articles,
    expert_letters_submitted,
    submitted_research_plan.

% Dhanasar framework - on balance, waiver benefits the United States
% If the endeavor has merit and national importance, the petitioner is well-positioned, and government guidance supports the national interest, waiver benefits the U.S.
waiver_benefit_us :-
    proposed_endeavor_substantial_merit,
    proposed_endeavor_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    government_guidance_documents.

% Eligibility is met if the petitioner qualifies for EB-2 and meets the Dhanasar criteria.
eligibility_met :-
    underlying_eb2_qualified,
    proposed_endeavor_substantial_merit,
    proposed_endeavor_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    waiver_benefit_us.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner is a native and citizen of Mexico who held U-1 nonimmigrant status from May 2019 through May 2023. He filed Form I-485, an application to adjust status based on his U classification, in October 2022. The Vermont Service Center director denied the application on the ground that the petitioner had not provided copies of all pages of his passport covering the period from August 2014 to August 2020. In response, the petitioner appealed that denial and, with the appeal, submitted a complete copy of all pages of the passport valid from August 2014 to August 2020 and also supplied a previously submitted passport valid from March 2007 to March 2012. The appeal challenges the denial tied to the alleged missing passport documentation and presents those passport pages for consideration.
statutes: U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, in its discretion, adjust the status of individuals lawfully admitted to the United States as a U nonimmigrant to that of lawful permanent resident (LPR) if they establish, among other requirements, that they have been physically present in the United States for a continuous period of at least three years since the date of admission as a U nonimmigrant. Section 245(m)(l) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(b)(3). To meet the continuous physical presence requirement, applicants must submit, in pertinent part, a photocopy of all pages of all passports that were valid during the required period, or an equivalent travel document, or explanation of why they do not have a passport. 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(d)(5).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:

petitioner_national_of_mexico.
u1_status_may_2019_to_may_2023.
admitted_as_u_may_2019.

appeal_submitted_passport_all_pages_aug_2014_to_aug_2020.
appeal_included_passport_mar_2007_to_mar_2012.
appeal_challenges_denial_as_docs_now_supplied.

% Regulatory rule fact
regulation_requires_photocopy_all_passport_pages_for_continuous_presence.

% Documentary requirement is satisfied when the required passport page copies have been submitted on appeal
documentary_requirement_met :-
    regulation_requires_photocopy_all_passport_pages_for_continuous_presence,
    appeal_submitted_passport_all_pages_aug_2014_to_aug_2020,
    appeal_included_passport_mar_2007_to_mar_2012.

% The petitioner filed after three years elapsed from U admission (May 2019 -> Oct 2022)
three_years_since_admission_before_filing :-
    admitted_as_u_may_2019,
    filed_i485_oct_2022.

% eligibility_met must represent all conditions necessary to Accept the case
eligibility_met :-
    three_years_since_admission_before_filing,
    documentary_requirement_met.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

    ).

               [Name, Arity])
    ).
