text: The Petitioner seeks U-1 nonimmigrant classification as a victim of qualifying criminal activity. In June 2016, he filed the U petition with a Supplement B certified by a designated officer of the Sheriff's Office, and the petition was based on a 1997 assault. The Director of the Nebraska Service Center denied the petition after concluding that the record did not establish that the Petitioner was a victim of qualifying criminal activity, and the matter was appealed. On the Supplement B, the certifying official identified felonious assault as the qualifying criminal activity and described that the Petitioner's neighbor threw a full beer bottle at his face, kicked him to the ground, pulled his hair, and punched him in the head; the form states that the Petitioner suffered bruises, contusions, and facial injuries after being hit with the beer bottle and later obtained an order of protection against the perpetrator. The Petitioner also submitted several continuation reports detailing additional interviews by responding officers. According to the record description, the underlying incident report and continuation reports included references to misdemeanor assault, while the incident report also states that the perpetrator threw a beer bottle that struck the Petitioner in the face. On appeal, the Petitioner argues that the certified aggravated assault is supported by the underlying record, that the certifying official had a basis to determine that it had been detected, that the beer bottle used in the assault should be treated as a dangerous instrument, and that the petition should be approved.
statutes:
U petitioners must establish that qualifying criminal activity was perpetrated against them, and that the certifying agency detected, investigated, or prosecuted this qualifying criminal activity. The record as a whole must support the certification of that victimization in order to establish a petitioner's eligibility for U nonimmigrant status. Section 214(p)(l), (4) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14( c )(2)(ii), ( 4). The term "investigation or prosecution" of qualifying criminal activity includes"the detection or investigation of a qualifying crime or criminal activity, as well as to the prosecution, conviction, or sentencing of the perpetrator of the qualifying crime or criminal activity." 8 C .F.R. § 214.14(a)(5). A "victim of qualifying criminal activity" is defined as an individual who has "suffered direct and proximate harm as a result of the comm1ss10n of qualifying criminal activity." 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(l4). "Qualifying criminal activity" is activity "involving one or more of' the 28 types of crimes listed at section 101 (a)(l 5)(U)(iii) of the Act or "any similar activity in violation of Federal, State, or local criminal law." Section 10l(a)(15)(U)(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(9).

The term "any similar activity" refers to criminal offenses in which the nature and the elements of the offenses are substantially similar to the statutory list of criminal activities at section 101 (a)( l 5)(U)(iii) of the Act. 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(9). As required initial evidence, U petitioners must submit Form I-918 Supplement B, U Nonimmigrant Status Certification (Supplement B) from a law enforcement official certifying a petitioner's helpfulness in the investigation or prosecution of the qualifying criminal activity. Section 2 l 4(p )(1) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(2)(i). The Supplement Bis required evidence which informs, but does not solely determine, whether a U petitioner is a victim of qualifying criminal activity. Section 214(p)(l) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(2)(i)-(ii), (c)(4). Although a petitioner may submit any relevant, credible evidence for consideration, USCIS determines, in its sole discretion, the credibility of and weight given to all the evidence, including the Supplement B. 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(4).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
petition_filed(june_2016).
crime_occurred(1997).

% Supplement B and certification facts
supplement_b(certified_by(county_sheriff_office)).
certifying_official_identified(felonious_assault).
supplement_b_certification_identified(felonious_assault).
cited_statutes(arizona_assault_related).

% Law enforcement reports and incident documentation
law_enforcement_report(describes([beer_bottle_thrown_at_face, struck, fell_to_ground, kicked, punched, swelling, cut_lower_lip, bruises, contusions])).
incident_report_notes(order_of_protection_obtained).
incident_charging_citation(misdemeanor_assault_citation).

% Simple member predicate to avoid dependency on built-ins
member_in(X,[X|_]).
member_in(X,[_|T]) :-
    member_in(X,T).

% Define what counts as a dangerous instrument in this record
dangerous_instrument(beer_bottle).

% Infer that a dangerous instrument was used if the report describes a beer bottle being thrown
dangerous_instrument_used :-
    law_enforcement_report(describes(L)),
    member_in(beer_bottle_thrown_at_face, L),
    dangerous_instrument(beer_bottle).

% A qualifying criminal activity can be aggravated assault (felonious assault).
% It is supported either directly by the certifying official identification
% or by showing the underlying elements (e.g., use of a dangerous instrument)
qualifying_criminal_activity(aggravated_assault) :-
    certifying_official_identified(felonious_assault).
qualifying_criminal_activity(aggravated_assault) :-
    dangerous_instrument_used,
    similar_to_listed(assault).

% "Similar activity" to a listed assault is inferred here from use of a dangerous instrument
similar_to_listed(assault) :-
    dangerous_instrument_used.

% The certifying agency detected/investigated/prosecuted if a Supplement B from a designated official exists
certifying_detected_investigated_prosecuted :-
    supplement_b(certified_by(_)),
    certifying_official_identified(_).

% The record supports the certification if the Supplement B identifies felonious assault
% and the law enforcement reports corroborate injury and/or the use of a dangerous instrument
record_supports_certification :-
    supplement_b_certification_identified(felonious_assault),
    law_enforcement_report(describes(L)),
    (   member_in(beer_bottle_thrown_at_face, L)
    ;   member_in(cut_lower_lip, L)
    ;   member_in(bruises, L)
    ;   member_in(contusions, L)
    ).

% Eligibility is met only if all three required elements for a U petition are satisfied:
% (1) qualifying criminal activity occurred, (2) certifying agency detected/investigated/prosecuted,
% and (3) the record as a whole supports the certification.
eligibility_met :-
    qualifying_criminal_activity(aggravated_assault),
    certifying_detected_investigated_prosecuted,
    record_supports_certification.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The applicant is a national of Venezuela who filed Form I-821 seeking Temporary Protected Status (TPS) under the Venezuelan designation. The Texas Service Center director denied the Form I-821, concluding that the applicant had not established the continuous residence and physical presence requirements for TPS. In a Request for Evidence (RFE), the director asked the applicant to submit additional documentation to demonstrate continuous residence in the United States. In response to the RFE the applicant provided a bank statement for June 2023 and a car insurance policy for another individual. After the denial, the applicant appealed and supplemented the record by submitting additional documents intended to demonstrate continuous residence and presence, including medical records covering January 26, 2023, through September 11, 2024 (showing regular follow-up appointments and prescription refills), a Form 1095-A Health Insurance Marketplace statement for the first half of 2023, a 2023 Form W-2, and a bank statement for June 2023. The stated reason for the appeal is to contest the denial based on the director’s finding that the applicant had not shown continuous residence and physical presence and to present the additional evidence to establish those facts.
statutes:
To be eligible for TPS , a national of a foreign state designated by the Secretary of Homeland Security must establish, in part that they have been continuously physically present in the United States since the effective date of the most recent designation of that foreign state for TPS and have continuously resided in the United States since a date designated by the Secretary of Homeland Security. 8 C.F.R . § 244.2. Individuals applying for TPS offered to Venezuelans (and persons without nationality who last habitually resided in Venezuela) must demonstrate that they have been continuously residing in the United States since July 31, 2023, and have been continuously physically present in the United States since October 3, 2023. 1 1 See Extension and R edesignation of Venezuela for Tem porary P rotected Status, 88 Fed. R eg. 68130 (Oct. 3, 2023).

Continuously resided means "residing in the United States for the entire period specified in the regulations," but an applicant shall not be considered to have failed to maintain continuous residence"because of a brief, casual and innocent absence ... or due merely to a brief temporary trip abroad required by emergency or extenuating circumstances outside the control of the [applicant]." 8 C.F.R. § 244.1. Continuously physically present means "actual physical presence in the United States for the entire period specified in the regulations," but an applicant shall not be considered to have failed to maintain continuous physical presence because of "brief, casual, and innocent absences." Id. The burden ofproof is on the Applicant to demonstrate eligibility for the requested immigration benefit by a preponderance of the evidence. Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. 369,376 (AAO 2010). The sufficiency of all evidence will be judged according to its relevancy, consistency, credibility, and probative value. 8 C.F.R. § 244.9(b). To meet his burden of proof, the Applicant must provide supporting documentary evidence of eligibility apart from his own statements. Id.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (use arity 0 for yes/no predicates as required)
national_of_venezuela.
filed_form_i821.
director_denied.
initial_june_2023_bank_statement_submitted.
initial_car_insurance_belonged_to_another.
appealed.
supplemented_with_medical_records.
submitted_1095a_first_half_2023.
submitted_w2_2023.
submitted_june_2023_bank_statement.

% Medical records date range submitted on appeal:
medical_records_start(date(2023,1,26)).
medical_records_end(date(2024,9,11)).

% TPS regulatory requirement dates for Venezuelans (per rule text)
residence_requirement_date(date(2023,7,31)).
presence_requirement_date(date(2023,10,3)).

% Utility: convert date(Y,M,D) to an integer for comparisons (lexicographic)
date_value(date(Y,M,D), V) :-
    V is Y*10000 + M*100 + D.

date_leq(A,B) :-
    date_value(A,VA),
    date_value(B,VB),
    VA =< VB.

date_between(Date, Start, End) :-
    date_leq(Start, Date),
    date_leq(Date, End).

% Document coverage predicates: whether a given submitted document covers (i.e., shows presence on) a given date.
% Medical records explicitly span a date range.
document_covers_date(medical_records, Date) :-
    medical_records_start(Start),
    medical_records_end(End),
    date_between(Date, Start, End).

% Form 1095-A submitted covers first half of 2023 (Jan 1 - Jun 30, 2023)
document_covers_date(form_1095a_first_half_2023, Date) :-
    date_between(Date, date(2023,1,1), date(2023,6,30)).

% June 2023 bank statement -> treat as evidence covering a date in June 2023 (use mid-month)
document_covers_date(june_2023_bank_statement, Date) :-
    date_between(Date, date(2023,6,1), date(2023,6,30)).

% Form W-2 for 2023 indicates presence sometime in calendar year 2023 (treated as covering any date in 2023)
document_covers_date(w2_2023, Date) :-
    date_between(Date, date(2023,1,1), date(2023,12,31)).

% Credibility: initial car insurance was not the petitioner's => that specific document is not credible.
document_is_credible(car_insurance_belonging_to_other) :- fail.  % explicitly not credible

% The other submitted documents on appeal are treated as credible for purposes of this analysis
document_is_credible(medical_records).
document_is_credible(form_1095a_first_half_2023).
document_is_credible(w2_2023).
document_is_credible(june_2023_bank_statement).

% There is supporting documentary evidence apart from the petitioner's own statements
supporting_documents_provided :-
    (   supplemented_with_medical_records,
        submitted_1095a_first_half_2023,
        submitted_w2_2023
    ).

% Evaluate whether the record proves continuous residence since July 31, 2023.
% For these rules we require at least one credible document that covers the residence requirement date.
proves_continuous_residence_since_requirement :-
    residence_requirement_date(ReqDate),
    % find a credible document that covers the requirement date
    document_covers_date(Doc, ReqDate),
    document_is_credible(Doc).

% Evaluate whether the record proves continuous physical presence since October 3, 2023.
% Similarly require at least one credible document that covers the presence requirement date.
proves_continuous_physical_presence_since_requirement :-
    presence_requirement_date(ReqDate),
    document_covers_date(Doc, ReqDate),
    document_is_credible(Doc).

% The burden of proof rule: applicant must submit supporting documentary evidence.
burden_of_proof_met :-
    supporting_documents_provided,
    % and at least one document is credible (not all are discredited)
    (   document_is_credible(medical_records)
    ;   document_is_credible(w2_2023)
    ;   document_is_credible(form_1095a_first_half_2023)
    ;   document_is_credible(june_2023_bank_statement)
    ).

% Eligibility determination: all necessary conditions to Accept the case
eligibility_met :-
    national_of_venezuela,
    filed_form_i821,
    burden_of_proof_met,
    proves_continuous_residence_since_requirement,
    proves_continuous_physical_presence_since_requirement.

% Decision and runner (must be last as requested)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The Applicant is a minor who seeks a Certificate of Citizenship, asserting that she derived U.S. citizenship from her father. She stated that she is under 18, that her father is a U.S. citizen, and that she was admitted to the United States as a lawful permanent resident in 2017. Her parents were married in Guyana in 2014, and she represented that they remain married and live together.

When she filed Form N-600, the Applicant submitted documentary evidence including her birth certificate and her parents’ marriage and divorce records, utility bills listing her parents’ residential address, correspondence mailed to her at that address, and a copy of a lease agreement her parents signed in 2021. The Brooklyn Field Office denied the Form N-600, stating that she had not provided sufficient documentation to show she was residing in the United States in her father’s legal and physical custody. The Director’s written denial did not explain the specific reasons in detail.

The Applicant appealed that denial and, in support of the appeal, submitted additional evidence aimed at establishing her residence with her parents. The new submissions included 2023 utility bills addressed to her parents, a 2023 lease renewal for the same residence, a 2021 financial contract for medical treatment listing the Applicant as a patient and showing her parents’ address, a school record confirming her registration at a New York public school in 2021, and family photographs.
statutes:
The record reflects that the Applicant was born abroad in02009 to unmarried noncitizen parents. Her father naturalized as a U.S . citizen in November 2013, when the Applicant was four years old. In 2014, he married the Applicant's mother and successfully petitioned for her and the Applicant to immigrate to the United States. The Applicant was admitted to the United States with her mother as a lawful permanent child of a U.S. citizen (IR-2) in late April 2017, at the age of seven years. In 2022, she filed the instant Form N-600, indicating that as of the date of filing she was residing in New York with her parents. In adjudicating the Applicant's derivative citizenship claim, we apply "the law in effect at the time the critical events giving rise to eligibility occurred." Minasyan v. Gonzales, 401 F.3d 1069, 1075 (9th Cir. 2005). The Applicant is currently under the age of 18 years, and the last critical event was her lawful admission to the Unites States for permanent residence in 2017. We therefore consider the Applicant's derivative citizenship under section 320 of the Act, as in effect since 2001. 1 The Applicant is a minor; her mother filed the instant Form I-290B, Notice of Appeal or Motion.

Section 320 of the Act provides, in pertinent part, that: (a) A child born outside ofthe United States automatically becomes a citizen ofthe United States when all of the following conditions have been fulfilled: (1) At least one parent of the child is a citizen of the United States, whether by birth or naturalization. (2) The child is under the age of eighteen years. (3) The child is residing in the United States in the legal and physical custody of the citizen parent pursuant to a lawful admission for permanent residence. The term "child," as used in section 320 of the Act includes children who were born to unmarried parents and legitimated under the law of the child's residence or domicile, or under the law of the father's residence or domicile, whether in the United States or elsewhere, if such legitimation takes place before the child reaches the age of 16 years2 and the child is in the legal custody of the legitimating parent. Section lOl(c)(l) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1 l0l(c)(l). Because the Applicant was born abroad, she is presumed to be a noncitizen and bears the burden of establishing her claim to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. Matter ofBaires­ Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467, 468 (BIA 2008).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the case
born_abroad.
parents_unmarried_at_birth.
father_naturalized_2013.
parents_married_2014.
admitted_LPR_2017.
child_under_18.

% Documents and evidence submitted (initial and on appeal)
lease_utility_2021_2023.
lease_renewal_2023.
school_record_2021.
medical_contract_2021.
insurance_correspondence.
photos_2021_2023.
school_record_identifies_father_as_parent_and_legal_guardian.

% Derived conditions based on statutory requirements (section 320)
parent_citizen :-
    father_naturalized_2013.

in_legal_custody_of_father :-
    school_record_identifies_father_as_parent_and_legal_guardian.

residing_with_parents_in_US :-
    lease_utility_2021_2023,
    school_record_2021.

residing_in_US_and_in_father_custody :-
    residing_with_parents_in_US,
    in_legal_custody_of_father.

% Legitimation (child born to unmarried parents but parents later married,
% which effectuates legitimation before age 16 for purposes of section 320)
legitimated_and_in_legal_custody :-
    parents_married_2014,
    in_legal_custody_of_father.

% Burden and sufficiency of evidence: applicant must prove derivative citizenship
% by a preponderance of credible evidence; here the submitted documents are
% modeled as sufficient.
evidence_sufficient :-
    lease_utility_2021_2023,
    school_record_2021,
    medical_contract_2021.

burden_established :-
    evidence_sufficient.

% Eligibility: all statutory elements of section 320 as in effect at the time
% of the relevant events must be met. If born to unmarried parents, legitimation
% and legal custody of the legitimating parent is required.
eligibility_met :-
    parent_citizen,
    child_under_18,
    admitted_LPR_2017,
    residing_in_US_and_in_father_custody,
    burden_established,
    (   parents_unmarried_at_birth
    ->  legitimated_and_in_legal_custody
    ;   true
    ).

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Petitioner is a technology company involved in electrical product and integrated software and hardware development and production. It seeks EB-2 classification for the Beneficiary, an embedded systems architect who is described as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree, and it also seeks a national interest waiver of the job offer requirement. The Director of the Texas Service Center initially denied the petition and later affirmed that decision on motion, concluding that the record did not establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement would be in the national interest. The Petitioner initially requested withdrawal of the appeal, and USCIS later reopened the appeal sua sponte to consider the merits of the Petitioner's appeal brief. The matter is before the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal. In support of the request, the record includes letters of support discussing how the Beneficiary's undertaking contributes to advancements in improved renewable energy technologies. The Petitioner submitted documentation indicating that circuit breaker and EV charging technology the Beneficiary has been essential in developing has been implemented with commercial and industrial clients throughout the United States and disseminated to others in the field through publication and presentation of his work. The Petitioner also represented that it intends to have the Beneficiary continue research and development in motor controls for large industrial and commercial facilities and EV charging, later stating that his work would focus on commercial fleet and multifamily EV charging, residential demand response, and solar energy storage product lines, and that this work would be shared through scientific conferences and meetings. Additional submitted information included an article about U.S. government initiatives to build out a national EV charging network to expand charging accessibility and support the competitiveness of the U.S. EV manufacturing industry. The record also contains material indicating widespread benefits associated with research progress in next-generation circuit breaker and EV charging technology.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate the beneficiary's qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. An advanced degree is any United States academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. A United States bachelor's degree or foreign equivalent degree followed by five years of progressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). 1 The Petitioner was permitted a period of 30 days in which to provide a supplemental brief. We did not receive a supplemental submission within that time frame and therefore consider the record to be complete as presently constituted.

If a petitioner demonstrates the beneficiary's eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, it must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter ofDhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 2 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. 3
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the record
petitioner_is_technology_company.
petition_filed_for_eb2_national_interest_waiver.
beneficiary_is_embedded_systems_architect.
beneficiary_has_masters.
beneficiary_employed_by_petitioner.
beneficiary_developed_firmware_for_flagship_product.
beneficiary_developed_web_ev_system.
commercial_implementation_across_US.
proposed_to_continue_rnd_in_motor_controls_and_ev_charging.
proposed_focus_on_commercial_fleet_multifamily_residential_solar.
proposed_work_will_be_shared_at_conferences.
record_includes_market_and_research_info_about_circuit_breaker_and_ev_charging.
letters_of_support_about_contributions_to_renewable_energy.
documentation_of_work_implementation_and_dissemination.

% EB-2 eligibility: advanced degree (master's) meets the classification
eligibility_for_eb2 :-
    beneficiary_has_masters.

% Dhanasar prong 1: proposed endeavor has substantial merit
% Substantial merit is supported by proposed R&D in EV/motor controls and market/research evidence.
proposed_endeavor_has_substantial_merit :-
    (   proposed_to_continue_rnd_in_motor_controls_and_ev_charging
    ;   proposed_focus_on_commercial_fleet_multifamily_residential_solar
    ),
    record_includes_market_and_research_info_about_circuit_breaker_and_ev_charging.

% Dhanasar prong 1 (continued): proposed endeavor has national importance
% National importance supported by commercial implementation across the U.S. and dissemination.
proposed_endeavor_has_national_importance :-
    commercial_implementation_across_US.
proposed_endeavor_has_national_importance :-
    proposed_work_will_be_shared_at_conferences,
    record_includes_market_and_research_info_about_circuit_breaker_and_ev_charging.

% Dhanasar prong 2: individual is well-positioned to advance the endeavor
% Evidence: advanced degree, relevant employment, track record of developing commercially implemented products,
% documentation of dissemination, and letters of support.
individual_well_positioned :-
    beneficiary_has_masters,
    beneficiary_employed_by_petitioner,
    (   beneficiary_developed_firmware_for_flagship_product
    ;   beneficiary_developed_web_ev_system
    ),
    documentation_of_work_implementation_and_dissemination,
    letters_of_support_about_contributions_to_renewable_energy.

% Dhanasar prong 3: on balance, a waiver would benefit the United States
% Benefit is supported when the endeavor is nationally important, the individual is well-positioned,
% and the work has been implemented and disseminated commercially in the U.S.
waiver_on_balance_beneficial :-
    proposed_endeavor_has_national_importance,
    individual_well_positioned,
    documentation_of_work_implementation_and_dissemination.

% Final eligibility: all conditions necessary to Accept the case
eligibility_met :-
    eligibility_for_eb2,
    proposed_endeavor_has_substantial_merit,
    proposed_endeavor_has_national_importance,
    individual_well_positioned,
    waiver_on_balance_beneficial.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner submitted a Form I-918 (U petition) in September 2016 seeking U nonimmigrant classification as a victim of criminal activity. Her initial Form I-918 Supplement B, signed in August 2016 by an investigator at the local sheriff’s office, indicated that the petitioner’s daughter was the victim of criminal activity involving rape and incest and that the petitioner possessed information about the crime and had been, was being, or was likely to be helpful in its investigation or prosecution. The investigator, however, did not attach the requested explanation of the assistance the petitioner provided and left several follow-up questions on the Supplement B unanswered.

The petitioner reported that she witnessed the criminal incident: she returned to her residence to retrieve something for work, entered her 17-year-old daughter’s bedroom, and saw her son sexually assaulting the daughter. She contacted the sheriff’s office; police responded and she provided a statement describing what she witnessed. She submitted the incident report from that contact. She also arranged therapy for her daughter, communicated with the sheriff’s office and the district attorney, and expressed interest in having the defendant arrested and prosecuted.

Following a request for additional evidence from the service center, the petitioner attempted to obtain a new Supplement B but states she was unable to do so promptly because of a backlog at the certifying agency. She later obtained a new Supplement B signed and certified in June 2024 by a lieutenant in the criminal investigation division and submitted it on appeal. That new Supplement B again identified the petitioner’s daughter as the victim of rape and incest and indicated that the petitioner had information about the crime and had been and was likely to be helpful; it also indicated the petitioner had not refused to provide assistance. The new Supplement B did not include detailed written explanations for those responses.

The petitioner also submitted a personal statement and a psychological evaluation describing changes in her life and emotional and physical symptoms after witnessing the assault, including fainting at the scene, persistent distressing images, sleep problems, loss of appetite, feelings of guilt and isolation, and other trauma-related symptoms. On appeal, the petitioner asserts she qualifies as an indirect victim because her daughter was incompetent or incapacitated from assisting law enforcement due to her age, and she asks that adjudicators consider the gravity of the crimes committed against her daughter.
statutes:
U-1 classification is provided to victims of qualifying crimes who suffer substantial physical or mental abuse as a result of the offense and who possess information regarding the qualifying crime and are helpful to law enforcement officials in their investigation or prosecution of the crime. Section 101(a)(15)(U)(i) of the Act

A U petition must be filed with a Supplement B from a law enforcement official certifying that a petitioner "has been helpful, is being helpful, or is likely to be helpful" in the investigation or prosecution of qualifying criminal activity. 1 Section 214(p)(l) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(2)(i). A "victim of qualifying criminal activity" is defined as one "who has suffered direct and proximate harm as a result of the commission of qualifying criminal activity." 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(l4). Although a petitioner may submit any evidence for us to consider, we determine, in our sole discretion, the credibility of and weight given to it, including the Supplement B. Section 214(p)(4) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(4).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the case description
filed_petition.  % Petitioner filed Form I-918 U in Sept 2016

% Supplement B facts
original_supplement_b_certified_aug2016. % Supplement B was signed/certified Aug 2016 by an investigator
original_supplement_b_certified_helpful_aug2016. % On that Supplement B the investigator checked "Yes" that petitioner had information and was helpful
original_supplement_b_missing_explanation. % Investigator did not attach requested explanation and left some Part 4 unanswered

later_supplement_b_certified_jun2024. % Petitioner later obtained a new Supplement B certified in June 2024 by a lieutenant
delay_in_obtaining_new_supplement_b_due_to_backlog. % Petitioner reported backlog at certifying agency prevented timely replacement

% Underlying criminal activity and petitioner actions
crime_involving_or_similar_to_rape_or_incest. % Criminal activity involving or similar to rape/incest against petitioner's daughter
petitioner_witnessed_assault. % Petitioner witnessed her son sexually assaulting her daughter
petitioner_reported_to_responding_officers. % She contacted the sheriff's office and described the incident to responding officers
petitioner_notified_district_attorney_request_prosecution. % She later notified the district attorney she wanted the defendant arrested/prosecuted
petitioner_arranged_therapy_for_victim_through_office. % She arranged therapy for her daughter through the sheriff's office/DA
petitioner_fainted_and_suffered_reactions_at_scene. % She fainted after witnessing the assault

% Psychological and medical evidence of harm
psychological_evaluation_submitted. % She later submitted a psychological evaluation
psychological_symptoms_listed. % Evaluation described shock, loss of consciousness, guilt, depression, disturbing images, breathing problems, insomnia, loss of appetite, suicidal thoughts
clinical_diagnoses_listed_in_report. % The psychological report lists clinical diagnoses attributed to symptoms

% Legal rule-based predicates (modeled from the statutes/regulations provided)
qualifying_crime :- crime_involving_or_similar_to_rape_or_incest.

% Direct victim definition per 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(14): "suffered direct and proximate harm as a result of the commission"
suffered_direct_proximate_harm :-
    % The petitioner experienced substantial mental abuse directly resulting from the criminal activity
    psychological_evaluation_submitted,
    psychological_symptoms_listed,
    clinical_diagnoses_listed_in_report.

% Possesses information about the qualifying criminal activity
possesses_information :-
    % She witnessed the assault and described it to responding officers, demonstrating possession of information
    petitioner_witnessed_assault,
    petitioner_reported_to_responding_officers.

% Helpful to law enforcement: can be proven either by certification or by concrete acts of assistance
helpful_to_law_enforcement :-
    % Either the certifying official indicated helpfulness on the Supplement B...
    original_supplement_b_certified_helpful_aug2016
    ;
    later_supplement_b_certified_jun2024
    ;
    % ...or the petitioner performed demonstrable helpful acts (reported, notified DA, arranged therapy)
    ( petitioner_reported_to_responding_officers,
      petitioner_notified_district_attorney_request_prosecution
    ).

% Certification requirement modeled: a U petition must be filed with a Supplement B certifying helpfulness.
% The program treats the August 2016 Supplement B as a certification (it was signed and the investigator checked "Yes"),
% and recognizes a later certification (June 2024) where a backlog prevented timely replacement as relevant.
certification_valid :-
    original_supplement_b_certified_aug2016,
    original_supplement_b_certified_helpful_aug2016
    ;
    ( later_supplement_b_certified_jun2024,
      delay_in_obtaining_new_supplement_b_due_to_backlog
    ).

% Eligibility: all statutory/regulatory elements must be satisfied for acceptance
eligibility_met :-
    filed_petition,
    qualifying_crime,
    suffered_direct_proximate_harm,
    possesses_information,
    helpful_to_law_enforcement,
    certification_valid.

% Entrypoint and decision code (do not modify)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Obligor signed an Immigration Bond (Form I-352) on September 17, 2018, on behalf of the bonded foreign national. On August 4, 2023, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement mailed a Form I-340, Notice to Obligor to Deliver Alien, to the Obligor via certified mail with return receipt; the record shows the Obligor received and accepted that notice. The Form I-340 set a delivery date of September 6, 2023. On September 8, 2023, ICE declared the bond breached, concluding the Obligor had not delivered the bonded foreign national. The Obligor appealed ICE’s breach determination, seeking reinstatement and cancellation of the bond. In support of his appeal, the Obligor stated that he had been advised by the bonded foreign national’s counsel that he did not need to deliver her because her immigration case would be reopened before the delivery date and thus there would be no need to present her for removal.
statutes: A delivery bond creates a contract between the U.S. Government and an obligor. United States v. Minn. Tr. Co., 59 F.3d 87, 90 (8th Cir. 1995); Matter ofAllied Fid. Ins. Co., 19 I&N Dec. at 125. An obligor secures its promise to deliver a foreign national by paying a designated amount in cash or its equivalent. 8 C.F.R. § 103.6(d). A breach occurs upon substantial violation of a bond's conditions. 8 C.F.R. § 103.6(e). Conversely, substantial performance of a bond's conditions releases an obligor from liability. 8 C.F.R. § 103.6(c)(3). Several factors inform whether a bond violation is substantial: the extent of the violation; whether it was intentional or accidental; whether it was in good faith; and whether the obligor took steps to comply with the terms of the bond. Matter ofKubacki, 18 I&N Dec. 43, 44 (Reg'l Comm'r 1981) (citing Int'l Fidelity Ins. Co. v. Crosland, 490 F. Supp. 446 (S.D .N .Y . 1980)); see also Aguilar v. United States, 124 Fed. Cl. 9, 16 (2015).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the case
signed_bond.
notice_mailed_certified.
notice_received.
delivery_required_date('2023-09-06').
bond_breached_date('2023-09-08').
obligor_appealed.
obligor_claimed_counsel_advice.
no_motion_to_reopen_on_delivery_date.    % explicitly: no motion was pending on the delivery date
motion_to_reopen_filed_after('2023-09-26').
motion_granted_and_admin_closed.

% There is no evidence the alien was delivered by the required date.
delivered_by_deadline :- fail.

% Define obligation to deliver: exists when a bond was signed and notice received.
obligation_to_deliver :-
    signed_bond,
    notice_received.

% Pending motion on the delivery date is negated by the explicit fact that no motion
% was pending on that date.
pending_motion_on_delivery_date :-
    \+ no_motion_to_reopen_on_delivery_date.

% Good faith reliance on counsel could be relevant, but here it only counts if
% a motion was pending by the delivery date (which it was not).
good_faith :-
    obligor_claimed_counsel_advice,
    pending_motion_on_delivery_date.

% No record that the obligor took steps to comply before the delivery date.
took_steps_to_comply :- fail.

% Substantial performance is either actual delivery by the deadline or the existence
% of a pending binding procedural circumstance (e.g., a pending motion) that
% effectively relieved the obligor. Neither exists here.
substantial_performance :-
    delivered_by_deadline
    ;
    pending_motion_on_delivery_date.

% A breach occurs when there is an obligation to deliver and there was not substantial performance.
breach_violation :-
    obligation_to_deliver,
    \+ substantial_performance.

% Eligibility to Accept (reinstatement/cancellation) requires that the obligor
% substantially performed (i.e., no breach). In this record that is not met.
eligibility_met :-
    obligation_to_deliver,
    substantial_performance.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Petitioner, a U.S. citizen, filed a Form I-600 seeking to classify a child as an immediate relative through an orphan petition. The petition was initially approved, but after a Form I-604 determination in Lagos, Nigeria, a consular officer reviewed the supporting evidence and noted that an Affidavit of Facts from the Ministry of Women Affairs and Social Development was dated 2013 even though the Petitioner had claimed the Beneficiary was abandoned in 2010. The officer then conducted site visits to the Ministry and the orphanage. According to the record, the Ministry's Deputy Director of Child Development stated that the Ministry did not keep records after an adoption case had been filed and had sent records to the Magistrate Court, and also said the Ministry could create letters at adoptive parents' requests without checking records. The record further states that the consular officer did not accept the Ministry's Affidavit of Facts as evidence of the Beneficiary's origins and that the orphanage could not verify the documentation submitted with the petition. After that investigation, the petition was returned for revocation, the Director issued a Notice of Intent to Revoke in April 2020, the Petitioner disputed the findings through counsel, and the approval was later revoked. The Petitioner then filed a combined motion to reopen and reconsider, which the Director denied, and he appealed with a brief again asserting the Beneficiary's eligibility. In support of the petition and later filings, the record includes the Beneficiary's court file with Ministry and orphanage documents, a personal statement, a National Human Rights Commission report, a Nigeria Police Force extract, family affidavits, a letter from the Petitioner's attorney in Nigeria, the spouse's passport with entry and exit stamps, medical reports, and photographs. The record also includes a March 2011 report referring to the Beneficiary and stating that a child was found abandoned in 2010, and a 2013 report that also refers to the Beneficiary but states that she was found abandoned in 2013; the record describes those reports as appearing to concern two different children while using nearly identical language about abandonment at the same orphanage. The record further indicates that the Beneficiary's name was not formally changed until the Magistrate Court granted the adoption order in 2013. On appeal, the Petitioner maintains that he submitted sufficient and credible evidence of abandonment, argues that the Director misunderstood the Nigerian adoption process, contends that the consular officer should have verified records at the Magistrate Court, and asserts that frequent child abandonment and limited police resources in Nigeria affect the available documentation.
statutes:
A child who meets the definition of an orphan under section 101 (b)(1 )(F)(i) of the Act is eligible for classification as the immediate relative of a U.S. citizen. 8 C.F.R. § 204.3. An orphan is defined as a child, under the age of 16 at the time a petition is filed on his or her behalf, who is an orphan because of the death or disappearance of, abandonment or desertion by, or separation or loss from, both parents, or for whom the sole or surviving parent is incapable of providing the proper care and has in writing irrevocably released the child for emigration and adoption; who has been adopted abroad by a United States citizen, or who is coming to the United States for adoption by a United States citizen; provided, that the Secretary of Homeland Security is satisfied that proper care will be furnished if the child is admitted to the United States. Section lOl(b)(l)(F)(i) of the Act. Regarding the revocation of approved visa petitions, section 205 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1155, states the following: "The Secretary of Homeland Security may , at any time, for what he deems to be good and sufficient cause, revoke the approval of any petition approved by him under section 204. Such revocation shall be effective as of the date of approval of any such petition."

The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.3(k)(l), regarding a consular officer's Form 1-604, Determination on Child for Adoption (Form 1-604), provides, in pertinent part: An 1-604 investigation must be completed in every orphan case. The investigation must be completed by a consular officer except when the petition is properly filed at a Service office overseas, in which case it must be completed by a Service officer. ... In any instance where an 1-604 investigation reveals negative information sufficient to sustain a denial or revocation, the investigation report, supporting documentation, and petition shall be forwarded to the appropriate Service office for action. Depending on the circumstances surrounding the case, the 1-604 investigation shall include, but shall not necessarily be limited to, document checks, telephonic checks, interview(s) with the natural parent(s), and/or a field investigation. The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.3(b) states, in pertinent part, the following: Abandonment by both parents means that the parents have willfully forsaken all parental rights, obligations, and claims to the child, as well as all control over and possession of the child, without intending to transfer or without transferring these rights to any specific persons.

text:
The petitioner is an executive and business owner with more than 20 years of experience in financial and business management. He describes a career path that began as an interpreter and later expanded into roles encompassing sales, marketing, manufacturing, procurement, and financial management.

He filed an I-140 seeking employment-based second-preference classification as a professional with an advanced degree and a waiver of the job-offer requirement (national interest waiver). His proposed endeavor is to establish and direct a New Jersey–based company that will manufacture and distribute functional, non‑alcoholic beverages throughout the United States. He describes the products as containing ingredients such as B vitamins, caffeine, an amino acid claimed to reduce anxiety and insomnia, a weight‑loss supplement, and other natural components intended to boost energy, stamina, strength, and mental capacity and to support stress management and improved health. His business plan states the company will both develop its own beverage and distribute products made by other manufacturers.

In support of the petition, the initial filing included the petitioner’s diploma and academic transcript (titled “Diplom”), a credential-evaluation report from Scholaro, Inc. stating a foreign equivalent of a U.S. bachelor’s degree in English awarded in July 2000, a personal statement describing his proposed endeavor, a business plan with five‑year financial and staffing projections (including a projection to hire 23 employees and projected net profit and tax figures), recommendation and support letters from former employers, colleagues, and business partners, and industry articles and reports about entrepreneurship, sustainability, and the U.S. beverage industry. The record also contains an employment book listing the petitioner’s positions from 1995 to 2000.

The Texas Service Center issued a request for evidence (RFE). In response, the petitioner submitted an evaluation of his educational credentials and additional recommendation/support letters. The director denied the petition. The petitioner appealed that denial, submitting a brief in which he contends the director erred in concluding his position of executive and business owner is not a professional position, that the director failed to give due weight to the evidence submitted with the petition and the RFE response, and that the director erred in concluding he was not eligible for a national interest waiver. On appeal the petitioner referenced the evidence already in the record and stated that it demonstrates, by a preponderance of the evidence, that he meets the EB‑2 classification criteria and merits a national interest waiver.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. An advanced degree is any United States academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. A United States bachelor's degree or foreign equivalent degree

followed by five years ofprogressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent ofa master's degree. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). Profession is defined as of the occupations listed in section 101 ( a )(32) of the Act, as well as any occupation for which a United States baccalaureate degree or its foreign equivalent is the minimum requirement for entry into the occupation. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3). Once eligibility for the EB-2 visa classification is established, a petitioner must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
% Facts derived from the record
there_is_foreign_academic_credential.
credential_evaluation_states_bachelors_equivalent.
diploma_and_transcript_provided.
employment_book_entries_1995_2000.
employment_entries_predate_diploma.
support_letters_without_dates.
submitted_business_plan_for_nj_beverage_company.
business_plan_lacks_timeline_sourcing_manufacturing_details.
business_plan_no_independent_objective_support.
director_found_inconsistencies_and_deficiencies.
appellant_submits_brief_only_referring_existing_record.

% Credibility/consistency findings noted by director
there_is_inconsistency_between_academic_and_eval.

% EB-2 classification rules
% Advanced degree pathway: either an academic degree above a bachelor's, or a U.S. bachelor's (or foreign equivalent)
% followed by five years progressive experience in the specialty (equivalent to a master's).
has_degree_above_bachelor :-
    % No evidence of a degree above a U.S. bachelor's was established in the record.
    false.

% A bachelor-equivalent assessment is not treated as established when there is an inconsistency
% between academic documentation and the credential evaluation.
bachelor_equivalent :-
    credential_evaluation_states_bachelors_equivalent,
    \+ there_is_inconsistency_between_academic_and_eval.

five_years_progressive_post_baccalaureate :-
    % Requires either dated employment or other documentary proof of five years progressive experience after the bachelor's.
    % The record lacks dated support and shows employment entries predating the diploma, so this cannot be established.
    support_letters_with_dates,
    employment_records_after_degree.

% Placeholders for predicates that would be required to prove five years; not satisfied in the record.
support_letters_with_dates :- false.
employment_records_after_degree :- false.

meets_bachelor_plus_5 :-
    bachelor_equivalent,
    five_years_progressive_post_baccalaureate.

meets_eb2_advanced_degree :-
    has_degree_above_bachelor
    ;
    meets_bachelor_plus_5.

% Dhanasar framework: three prongs
% 1) Substantial merit and national importance
has_substantial_merit_national_importance :-
    % Requires a business plan with credible specifics and independent objective evidence supporting projected impacts.
    submitted_business_plan_for_nj_beverage_company,
    \+ business_plan_lacks_timeline_sourcing_manufacturing_details,
    \+ business_plan_no_independent_objective_support.

% 2) Individual is well-positioned to advance the proposed endeavor
well_positioned :-
    % Requires consistent academic documentation, evidence of requisite experience (e.g., five years progressive post-baccalaureate if relying on bachelor),
    % credible supporting letters and objective corroboration.
    \+ there_is_inconsistency_between_academic_and_eval,
    five_years_progressive_post_baccalaureate,
    \+ support_letters_without_dates,
    \+ business_plan_lacks_timeline_sourcing_manufacturing_details.

% 3) On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States
waiver_on_balance_benefit :-
    % Requires that the record demonstrate projected national/industry benefit and that harms of waiver do not outweigh benefits.
    has_substantial_merit_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    \+ director_found_inconsistencies_and_deficiencies.

% Aggregate eligibility: must meet EB-2 qualification and all three Dhanasar prongs
eligibility_met :-
    meets_eb2_advanced_degree,
    has_substantial_merit_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    waiver_on_balance_benefit.

% Final decision entrypoint (provided as required)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is an architect who seeks classification in the EB-2 category and a national interest waiver. She holds the foreign equivalent of a bachelor’s degree in architecture and reports at least five years of progressive experience as an architect. In her petition she described a plan to continue her architectural career working on large-scale industrial, commercial, infrastructure, and residential projects, and to incorporate sustainable architecture techniques and “green” building methods into those projects. She also stated that she will offer trainings in sustainable architecture and green building methods.

As part of the record, the petitioner submitted a professional plan, an updated professional plan in response to a request for evidence, and letters of recommendation (one recommender described her as having “revolutionized the industry with her methods”). On appeal, the petitioner disagreed with the agency’s assessment of the importance of her proposed endeavor and argued that: sustainable design and her use of sustainable techniques have broader implications for the field and play a crucial role in mitigating and adapting to climate change; her proposed work will broadly enhance societal welfare; and her activities have significant potential to employ U.S. workers. She stated she intends to establish a company offering architectural design and training services that will hire at least 13 full‑time staff within five years and generate additional indirect jobs through outsourcing (for example, accounting and marketing). These statements and submissions form the factual basis for her appeal.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 immigrant classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or an individual of exceptional ability, the petitioner must then establish eligibility for a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter ofDhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national 1 The Form 1-290B, Notice of Appeal or Motion, was initially assigned receipt numberl Th e appeal was subsequently assigned a new receipt number, and the Petitioner notified of this change by correspondence dated February 21 , 2024.

I

interest waiver pet1t10ns. Dhanasar states that USCIS may, as a matter of discretion,2 grant a national interest waiver ifthe petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the petitioner and her petition (arity-0 predicates for true/false)
has_foreign_bachelors_in_architecture.
has_5_years_progressive_experience.
filed_immigrant_petition_eb2.
proposed_endeavor_sustainable_architecture_and_trainings.
intend_to_establish_company_hire_13.
claims_broader_impact_on_climate.

% Evidence weaknesses noted in the record (these negate certain Dhanasar considerations)
national_importance_not_established.
lack_of_evidence_of_past_success.
speculative_job_creation.

% EB-2 qualification rules
% A foreign bachelor's plus five years of progressive experience equates to an advanced-degree equivalent.
eb2_advanced_degree_equivalent :-
    has_foreign_bachelors_in_architecture,
    has_5_years_progressive_experience.

% The petitioner is eligible for the underlying EB-2 classification if she meets the advanced-degree-equivalent rule.
eb2_qualified :-
    eb2_advanced_degree_equivalent.

% Dhanasar framework (three prongs)

% Prong 1: Substantial merit and national importance.
% Substantial merit is present if the proposed endeavor involves sustainable architecture and trainings.
substantial_merit :-
    proposed_endeavor_sustainable_architecture_and_trainings.

% National importance must be independently supported; the record here contains no such independent evidence.
% We encode that lack of evidence as national_importance_not_established above. Prong 1 passes only if
% substantial merit exists and national importance is not negated.
prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    substantial_merit,
    \+ national_importance_not_established.

% Prong 2: The individual is well-positioned to advance the endeavor.
% The petitioner has credentials and experience, which are necessary but not alone sufficient.
well_positioned_credentials :-
    has_foreign_bachelors_in_architecture,
    has_5_years_progressive_experience.

% Because the record lacks evidence of past successful implementation or concrete U.S. traction,
% the petitioner is not shown to be well-positioned to advance the endeavor in the U.S. context.
well_positioned :-
    well_positioned_credentials,
    \+ lack_of_evidence_of_past_success.

% Prong 3: On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
% This requires credible evidence of prospective benefit (e.g., concrete job creation or measurable public benefit).
% Here, projected job creation is speculative, so the balance does not favor a waiver.
balance_favors_waiver :-
    \+ speculative_job_creation.

% Aggregate Dhanasar eligibility: all three prongs must be satisfied.
dhanasar_prongs_met :-
    prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    balance_favors_waiver.

% Overall eligibility: must qualify for EB-2 and satisfy Dhanasar prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_qualified,
    dhanasar_prongs_met.

% Decision wrapper and entrypoint (as requested)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-

        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
```

text: The petitioner is a native and citizen of Guatemala who was granted U-2 nonimmigrant status from May 2017 until May 2021. She timely filed a Form I-485 (application for adjustment of status) in March 2021 as a derivative U nonimmigrant seeking lawful permanent resident status. The Vermont Service Center director denied her I-485, stating she had not submitted a completed Form I-693 (Report of Immigration Medical Examination and Vaccination Record). On appeal, the petitioner submitted a newly executed medical examination and additional evidence; she is appealing the denial to address the director’s determination that her application lacked a completed medical exam.
statutes: Section 245(m) of the Act contains the eligibility requirements for individuals seeking to adjust status to that of a lawful permanent resident (LPR) based on having been granted U nonimmigrant status. In addition, an applicant for adjustment of status under 245(m) must comply with the general eligibility and documentary requirements to adjust status at 8 C.F.R. § 245 .5, which requires that the applicant"have a medical examination by a designated civil surgeon, whose report setting forth the findings of the mental and physical condition of the applicant, including compliance with section 212(a)(l)(A)(ii) of the Act, shall be incorporated into the record."

text:
The petitioner is a dentist-scientist who filed an immigrant petition seeking EB-2 classification as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree and a national interest waiver of the job-offer requirement. He holds a Doctor of Dental Surgery degree earned in Iran in 2017 and a master’s degree in craniofacial sciences earned in 2023; both foreign degrees were evaluated as the equivalent of advanced degrees from a U.S. university. He states an intent to work in the United States conducting research to identify unknown etiological factors in oral diseases, to develop preventive measures (including work on dental biomaterials with antibacterial properties and studying disease markers in saliva), and to publish original dentistry research in peer‑reviewed journals. He also reports he has been admitted to an international dentist program.

In support of the petition the petitioner submitted letters of recommendation and independent advisory opinions from dental researchers and clinicians (including a vice dean of a faculty of dental medicine and an associate professor in Iran, an associate professor and researcher in periodontology in Germany, a board‑certified orthodontist in Seattle, an adjunct clinical assistant professor, and a professor in Brazil), evidence of his peer‑reviewed publications and citations, and additional scholarly articles citing his work. He also provided background materials concerning oral health from sources such as the World Health Organization and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

The Texas Service Center director issued a request for evidence, and after the petitioner submitted an updated statement and additional publications and citation evidence in response, the director denied the petition on the ground that the petitioner had not established that a waiver of the job‑offer requirement would be in the national interest. The petitioner appealed that denial, asserting that the director “did not consider all of the evidence submitted” with the initial petition and in response to the request for evidence.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," we set forth a framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions in the precedent decision Matter of Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. 884 (AAO 2016). Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion1, grant a national interest waiver of the job offer, and thus the labor certification, to a petitioner classified in the EB-2 category if the petitioner demonstrates that (1) the noncitizen's proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; (2) the 1 See also Poursina v. USCIS, 936 F.3d 868 (9th Cir. 2019) (finding USCIS ' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver to be discretionary in nature).

noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor; and (3) that on balance it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification. The first prong, substantial merit and national importance, focuses on the specific endeavor that the noncitizen proposes to undertake. The endeavor's merit may be demonstrated in a range ofareas such as business, entrepreneurialism, science, technology, culture, health, or education. In determining whether the proposed endeavor has national importance, we consider its potential prospective impact. The second prong shifts the focus from the proposed endeavor to the noncitizen. To determine whether the noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor, we consider factors including but not limited to the individual's education, skills, knowledge, and record of success in related or similar efforts. A model or plan for future activities, progress towards achieving the proposed endeavor, and the interest of potential customers, users, investors, or other relevant entities or individuals are also key considerations. The third prong requires the petitioner to demonstrate that, on balance of applicable factors, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification. USCIS may evaluate factors such as whether, in light of the nature of the noncitizen's qualification or the proposed endeavor, it would be impractical either for the noncitizen to secure a job offer or for the petitioner to obtain a labor certification; whether, in I ight of the nature of the noncitizen's qualification or the proposed endeavor, it would be impractical either for the noncitizen to secure a job offer or for the petitioner to obtain a labor certification; whether, even assuming that other qualified U.S. workers are available, the United States would still benefit from the noncitizen's contributions; and whether the national interest in the noncitizen's contributions is sufficiently urgent to warrant forgoing the labor certification process. Each of the factors considered must, taken together, indicate that on balance it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
    research_disease_markers_in_saliva;

% 3) Prong Two: the noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor.
% Consider education, record of success, publications, citations, recommendations, and advisory opinions.

    proposed_endeavor_public_health_research.

prong3_balance_benefit :-
    noncitizen_offers_unique_contribution,
    national_interest_is_urgent_or_beneficial.

% Overall eligibility: all required conditions (EB-2 + three Dhanasar prongs)
eligibility_met :-
    qualifies_eb2,
    prong1_substantial_and_national_importance,
    prong2_well_positioned,
    prong3_balance_benefit.

% Entrypoint and decision output (must be included exactly as specified)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-

    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',

               [Name, Arity])
```
