text:
The petitioner is a U.S. citizen who filed Form I-129F for his fiancee on August 23, 2022. He reported that he and the beneficiary had not personally met during the two-year period immediately preceding that filing (August 23, 2020–August 22, 2022) and described the history of their relationship: they met in China in 2014, began dating in November 2014, lived together in China from December 2014 to December 2018, lived together in the Philippines from March 15, 2020 to June 16, 2020, and became engaged on March 28, 2022. He said the COVID-19 pandemic forced him to return to the United States for work in June 2020 and that he had been unable to return to the Philippines since then. The petitioner submitted supporting evidence including passport stamps and travel records from 2015, 2017, and 2019; a Philippines passport stamp showing a change in status on June 14, 2019; photographs of the couple from 2014–2020; copies of communications; affidavits from friends; and the beneficiary’s Philippine criminal background check showing no record.

In response to a request for additional evidence, the petitioner provided a statement that he began a new job as a “Territory Manager” on July 13, 2020 and described himself as a top performer; video and message chats; additional affidavits; and a planned travel itinerary for a trip to Manila in February 2024. He also requested an extreme-hardship exemption to the two-year in-person meeting requirement, asserting that pandemic-related travel restrictions and quarantine requirements would have created extreme hardship. In a statement dated January 5, 2024 he explained that, beginning May 7, 2021, travel to the Philippines required a 14-day quarantine and that he was unwilling to risk contracting illnesses in quarantine facilities; he said prolonged quarantine would harm his health and, because he is a commission-based employee, would cause severe employment and financial hardship. He also said he traveled to Mexico multiple times during the relevant period because restrictions there were minimal and he lives in a border town, but that the beneficiary could not similarly travel to meet him due to visa and pandemic travel restrictions. The petitioner submitted documentation concerning Philippine travel restrictions, the 14-day quarantine requirement, the July 21, 2023 lifting of all COVID-19 travel restrictions in the Philippines, and the beneficiary’s employment in China beginning March 26, 2023.

The petitioner appealed the denial of his fiance(e) petition, arguing that the record and the evidence he submitted demonstrate that pandemic-related international travel restrictions, quarantine requirements, health concerns, and his employment circumstances prevented him from meeting the beneficiary in person during the relevant two-year period and therefore warrant an extreme-hardship exemption.
statutes:
Section 214( d)( 1) of the Act states that a fiance(e) petition can be approved only if a petitioner establishes that the parties have previously met in person within two years before the date of filing the fiance(e) petition, have a bona fide intention to marry, and are legally able and actually willing to conclude a valid marriage in the United States within a period of 90 days after a beneficiary's arrival. The regulations require a petitioner to establish to the satisfaction of the Director that the petitioner and beneficiary have met in person within the two years immediately preceding the filing of the petition. 8 C.F .R . § 214.2(k)(2). As a matter of discretion, the Director may exempt a petitioner from this requirement only if it is established that compliance would result in extreme hardship to the petitioner or that compliance would violate strict and long-established customs of a beneficiary's foreign culture or social practice. Id. Failure to establish that a petitioner and beneficiary have met within the required period or that compliance with the requirement should be waived shall result in the denial of the petition. Id. Such denial shall be without prejudice to the filing of a new petition once the parties have met in person. Id. An applicant or petitioner must establish that they are eligible for the requested benefit at the time of filing the application or petition. 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b )(1 ).

Because the regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 214.2(k), governing fiancee petitions, does not define what may constitute extreme hardship to a petitioner in the context of a fiancee petition, each claim of extreme hardship must be judged on a case-by-case basis considering the totality of circumstances.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the case
petitioner_is_us_citizen.
filing_date(date(2022,8,23)).
% Last documented in-person encounter: lived together in Philippines through June 16, 2020
last_in_person_meeting(date(2020,6,16)).
% Prior cohabitation in China ended December 2018; initial meeting in 2014
first_meeting_year(2014).
cohabitation_end_in_china(date(2018,12,31)).

% Evidence of bona fide intent to marry: engagement and relationship evidence
engagement_date(date(2022,3,28)).
there_is_photos_and_communications.
there_are_affidavits_from_friends.
bona_fide_intent :- engagement_date(_), there_is_photos_and_communications, there_are_affidavits_from_friends.

% No contrary evidence of inability or unwillingness to marry in US within 90 days
able_and_willing_to_marry_in_us.

% Relevant pandemic / travel facts
philippines_quarantine_start(date(2021,5,7)).
philippines_quarantine_requirement_specified.    % 14-day quarantine requirement publicly documented
philippines_lifted_restrictions(date(2023,7,21)).
petitioner_new_job_start(date(2020,7,13)).
petitioner_job_commission_based.
petitioner_reported_inability_to_return_since_mar_2020.  % petitioner states he returned to US in March 2020 and had not been able to return

% Beneficiary employment change (post-filing)
beneficiary_began_working_in_china(date(2023,3,26)).

% Utility: approximate date -> scalar for comparisons (simple linearization: year*365 + month*30 + day)
date_to_scalar(date(Y,M,D), Scalar) :-
    Scalar is Y*365 + M*30 + D.

% Two-year window start (two years immediately before filing date)
two_year_window_start(ScalarStart) :-
    filing_date(FileDate),
    date_to_scalar(FileDate, FileScalar),
    ScalarStart is FileScalar - 2*365.

% Determine whether last in-person meeting falls within the two-year window
met_within_two_years :-
    last_in_person_meeting(LastDate),
    date_to_scalar(LastDate, LastScalar),
    two_year_window_start(StartScalar),
    LastScalar >= StartScalar.

% Determine whether quarantine requirement overlapped any part of the two-year window
quarantine_overlaps_two_year_window :-
    philippines_quarantine_start(QuarStartDate),
    date_to_scalar(QuarStartDate, QuarStartScalar),
    two_year_window_start(WindowStartScalar),
    filing_date(FileDate),
    date_to_scalar(FileDate, WindowEndScalar),
    % overlap exists if quarantine start <= window end
    QuarStartScalar =< WindowEndScalar,
    % and quarantine start is on or after window start, or earlier but still affects window
    QuarStartScalar >= WindowStartScalar.

% Factors that may support a waiver for meeting requirement (extreme hardship or cultural exception)
cultural_exception_false.  % no claim supported in the record regarding strict long-established customs

% Extreme hardship assessment (requires strong evidence; assessed against totality of circumstances)
% For this modeled case, we require more than a quarantine notice and generalized employment hardship.
% Define a predicate that recognizes the minimally sufficient factors that would constitute "extreme hardship"
% (in practice would be case-by-case). Here we model the record as lacking those factors.
extreme_hardship :-
    % Hypothetical sufficient factors (not all present in this record):
    % - continuous, absolute inability to travel for the entire two-year period (e.g., incarceration, severe medical isolation), OR
    % - documented life-threatening medical vulnerability making travel/quarantine impossible, OR
    % - incontrovertible proof that quarantine would have caused irreparable, catastrophic loss.
    % None of those are established in this record; therefore extreme_hardship fails.
    false.

% Exemption granted only if extreme hardship or cultural exception established
exemption_granted :-
    (   extreme_hardship
    ;   ( \+ cultural_exception_false, ! )  % placeholder: would be true if a supported cultural exception existed
    ).

% Met requirement or valid waiver
met_within_two_years_or_waived :-
    (   met_within_two_years
    ;   exemption_granted
    ).

% Eligibility at time of filing: must be eligible when filed
eligibility_at_filing :-
    petitioner_is_us_citizen,
    bona_fide_intent,
    able_and_willing_to_marry_in_us.

% Final eligibility: all statutory/regulatory conditions
eligibility_met :-
    eligibility_at_filing,
    met_within_two_years_or_waived.

% Decision structure required by the user
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The Applicant filed Form N-600 seeking a Certificate of Citizenship to reflect her claim that she acquired U.S. citizenship at birth from her father. The Mount Laurel, New Jersey Field Office denied the application on the ground that the Applicant had not established that her father met the agency’s prior U.S. physical‑presence requirements. The Applicant appealed that denial, submitted additional evidence, reaffirmed her claim regarding her father, and—for the first time on appeal—raised the possibility that she may have derived U.S. citizenship after birth through her naturalized mother.

Facts presented by the Applicant about herself and her father:

- The Applicant was born abroad in 1956. At the time of her birth, her father was a U.S. citizen, and the Applicant seeks a certificate reflecting that claimed status.

- The Applicant provided her father’s New Jersey birth certificate, showing he was born in late 1935, and a baptismal certificate indicating he was baptized in New Jersey two months after his birth.

- Documentary evidence about the paternal family includes residential listings from 1926, 1928, and 1931, the 1930 U.S. census record, and naturalization documents for the Applicant’s great‑grandfather. Those records show the Applicant’s paternal grandparents living in New Jersey and the grandfather’s occupation as a hardwood floor layer.

- The Applicant provided her paternal grandfather’s 1942 military draft/registration card, which lists him as residing in New Jersey with his spouse when the Applicant’s father was a child.

- The Applicant supplied the 1950 U.S. census record showing her father living with his parents in New Jersey and attending school in April 1950; that census entry also indicates he had lived at the same address “a year ago” (1949) and was a seventh‑grade student.

- In a personal statement, the Applicant says her father quit school in 1951 to work with his father in the construction trade. Supporting documents include military and civilian occupational specialties and education worksheets that record he finished grammar school in 1950, had one year of vocational training by 1951, and worked as a floor layer apprentice. The Applicant also submitted evidence that her father was issued a Social Security number in 1951.

- On military service, the Applicant submitted records showing that her father enlisted in the U.S. Marine Corps in March 1953. Those records document his service and primary duties, indicate he served at various locations within the United States through April 1955, and show travel to Canada on military orders in April 1955.

- The Applicant submitted a newspaper announcement of her parents’ marriage and related travel documents (a sea and air travel embarkation slip) indicating the father went to Canada on military orders and that he returned to the United States roughly a week after the Applicant’s birth. She also provided the father’s Form DD‑214 (Report of Separation from Active Duty), which records his long military career.

- Additional evidence offered on appeal includes the father’s baptismal certificate, the Applicant’s personal statement, further military documents, census and residential records, and other materials intended to document the father’s residence, schooling, employment, and military service from his birth in 1935 through the mid‑1950s.

- Timeline details included in the record: the father was born in 1935, turned 14 in 1949, worked with his father beginning around 1951, enlisted in the Marine Corps in March 1953, traveled on military orders to Canada in April 1955, married the Applicant’s mother in 1956 in Canada, and returned to the United States shortly after the Applicant’s birth in 1956; his military service record indicates continued service through later years.
statutes:
The applicable law for transmitting citizenship to a child born abroad when one parent is a U.S. citizen is the statute that was in effect at the time ofthe child's birth. Chau v. Immigration and Naturalization Service, 247 F.3d 1026, 1029 n.3 (9th Cir. 2001) (internal quotation marks and citation omitted). 1 Re-designated as section 30l(g) of the Act by Act of October 10, 1978, Pub. L. No . 95-432, 92 Stat. 1046. 2 The Director incorrectly indicated in the denial that section 30l(g) of the Act applied to the Applicant's citizenship claim. The error does not affect our adjudication on appeal, as the physical presence requirements under former sections 301 ( a)(7) and 30l(g) of the Act were the same and did not change until former section 30l(g) was amended in 1986. 3 We acknow ledge this claim; however, to derive U.S. citizenship under former section 320 of the Act, a child born abroad to one U.S. citizen and one noncitizen parent had to reside in the United States pursuant to a lawful admission for permanent residence at the time of the noncitizen parent's naturalization or thereafter, but before turning 18 years of age. Because there is nothing in the record to indicate that the Applicant was admitted to the United States as a lawful permanent resident at any time, we will not address her derivative citizenship claim further.

The Applicant was born in Canada inl I 1956 to a married U.S. citizen father and a noncitizen mother. At that time, former section 301(a)(7) of the Act governed acquisition of U.S. citizenship by persons born abroad to one U.S. citizen and one noncitizen parent. It provided, in relevant part that a person shall be a national and citizen of the United States at birth if the U.S. citizen parent "prior to the birth of such person, was physically present in the United States or its outlying possessions for a period or periods totaling not less than ten years, at least five of which were after attaining the age of fourteen years ... [and] that any periods ofhonorable service in the Armed Forces ofthe United States by such citizen parent may be included in computing the physical presence requirements ...." Because the Applicant was born abroad, she is presumed to be a noncitizen and bears the burden of establishing her claim to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. See Matter of Baires-Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467,468 (BIA 2008). Under the preponderance of the evidence standard, the Applicant must demonstrate that her citizenship claim is "probably true" or "more likely than not." Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. at 376.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (from the record)
father_birth_year(1935).
child_birth_year(1956).
father_present_interval(1935,1955).  % Father physically present in the U.S. from 1935 through April 1955 (approximated by year)
father_us_citizen_at_time_of_child_birth.  % Father born in New Jersey in 1935, hence a U.S. citizen at child's 1956 birth
child_born_abroad.                         % Applicant was born in Canada in 1956
one_parent_non_citizen.                    % The mother was a noncitizen at the time of birth

% Helpers
min_of(A,B,Min) :-
    (   A =< B
    ->  Min = A
    ;   Min = B
    ).

max_of(A,B,Max) :-
    (   A >= B
    ->  Max = A
    ;   Max = B
    ).

% Compute total years of physical presence in the U.S. prior to the child's birth.
% Counts full calendar years inclusively between Start and EffectiveEnd (min(End, child_birth_year-1)).
years_before_child(TotalYears) :-
    child_birth_year(ChildY),
    father_present_interval(Start, End),
    EndBefore is ChildY - 1,
    min_of(End, EndBefore, EffectiveEnd),
    (   EffectiveEnd < Start
    ->  TotalYears = 0
    ;   TotalYears is EffectiveEnd - Start + 1
    ).

% Compute years of presence after the father's 14th birthday and prior to the child's birth.
years_after_father_14(YearsAfter14) :-
    father_birth_year(FY),
    Age14Year is FY + 14,
    child_birth_year(ChildY),
    father_present_interval(Start, End),
    EndBefore is ChildY - 1,
    max_of(Start, Age14Year, EffectiveStart),
    min_of(End, EndBefore, EffectiveEnd),
    (   EffectiveEnd < EffectiveStart
    ->  YearsAfter14 = 0
    ;   YearsAfter14 is EffectiveEnd - EffectiveStart + 1
    ).

% Rule implementing the former INA section 301(a)(7) physical-presence test:
% prior to the child's birth the citizen parent must have been physically present in the U.S.
% for at least 10 years, at least 5 of which were after attaining age 14.
father_met_physical_requirements :-
    years_before_child(Total),
    years_after_father_14(After14),
    Total >= 10,
    After14 >= 5.

% Eligibility: all necessary conditions to Accept
eligibility_met :-
    father_us_citizen_at_time_of_child_birth,
    child_born_abroad,
    one_parent_non_citizen,
    father_met_physical_requirements.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Applicant seeks a Certificate of Citizenship based on her claim that she acquired U.S. citizenship through her mother, S-M-. The Director of the Mount Laurel, New Jersey Field Office denied her Form N-600, and the matter is now on appeal. On her application, the Applicant stated that her mother was physically present in the United States before the Applicant's 2003 birth. On appeal, the Applicant asserted that her mother was continuously present in the United States from birth in 1985 until 1990 and again for nearly a year beginning in 2001. In support, she submitted a June 2023 affidavit from the maternal grandmother stating that she gave birth to S-M- in 1985 and that S-M- lived with her in the United States until they returned to Mexico in 1990, and that S-M- returned to the United States in 2001 to live with her father and stepmother for almost a year. She also submitted a June 2023 statement from S-M-'s father stating that S-M- lived with him from birth until 1987, then returned to Mexico with her mother, and later returned to live with him in the United States in 2001 for almost a year. The record also includes vaccination records from January and March 2001, Spring 2001 school records, California benefits identification cards issued in April and August 2001, a September 2001 family medical-plan record, and an undated dental-plan record relating to S-M-'s claimed presence in the United States in 2001. The Applicant's Form N-600 indicated that her mother resided in Mexico until July 2001, while these 2001 records indicate U.S. presence several months earlier. The Applicant further submitted her Mexican birth certificate showing that she was born in Mexico in 2003 to a mother named S-M- whose nationality is listed as Mexican, and a U.S. birth certificate issued in 2013 for a person named S-M- born in 1985. The record describes inconsistencies between the biographical information for S-M- and her parents on those birth records.
statutes:
The Applicant's birth certificate shows that she was born Mexico in I 12003 to a mother named S-M-, 1 who the Applicant claims is a U.S. citizen. According to the Applicant, she was born out of wedlock and her father is not named on her birth certificate. Consequently, the Applicant seeks to establish that she has acquired U.S. citizenship solely through her mother. The applicable law for acquiring U.S. citizenship at birth is determined by the law in effect at the time of the individual's birth. See, e.g., Chau v. Immigration and Naturalization Service, 247 F.3d 1026, 1029 n.3 (9th Cir. 2001 ). As the Applicant asserts that she was born out of wedlock in Mexico to a U.S. citizen mother inl 12003, her citizenship claim falls within the provisions of section 309(c) of the Act, which provides, in pertinent part: 1 Nam es withheld to protect the individuals' identities.

[A] person born, after December 23, 1952, outside the United States and out of wedlock, shall be held to have acquired at birth the nationality status of [their] mother, if the mother had the nationality of the United States at the time of such person's birth, and if the mother had previously been physically present in the United States or one of its outlying possessions for a continuous period of one year. Additionally, section 320 of the Act, as amended by the Child Citizenship Act of 2000, Pub. L. No. 106-3 9 5, 114 Stat. 1631 ( CCA ), applies to the Applicant's derivative citizenship claim, as she was born after the effective date of that provision. 2 Because the Applicant was born abroad, she is presumed to be a foreign national and bears the burden ofestablishing his claim to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance ofcredible evidence. Matter ofBaires, 24 I&N Dec. 467, 468 (BIA 2008). The "preponderance of the evidence" standard requires that the record demonstrate that the Applicant's claim is "probably true," based on the specific facts of her case. Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. at 376.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
mother_claimed_usborn_1985.
mother_us_birth_certificate_exists.
us_birth_certificate_issued_2013.
petitioner_mexican_birth_certificate_exists.
mexican_birth_cert_lists_mother_mexican.
biographical_details_mismatch.
affidavit_grandmother_states_mother_usborn_and_lived_in_us_until_1990.
affidavit_father_states_mother_lived_in_us_1985_1987_and_2001.
medical_records_2001.
school_records_2001.
ca_benefits_id_2001.
health_plan_record_2001.
dental_plan_record_undated.
applicant_not_lpr_before_18.

% Derivations about mother's physical presence
mother_presence_1985_1990_supported :-
    affidavit_grandmother_states_mother_usborn_and_lived_in_us_until_1990.

mother_presence_2001_2002_supported :-
    medical_records_2001,
    school_records_2001,

% A continuous year of physical presence is established by the 1985-1990 residence evidence

% The submitted US birth certificate has indicators that make it suspect

% Credible proof that the mother was born in the United States requires no biographical
% mismatches and no indicia that the birth record is suspect
credible_mother_us_birth :-
    mother_us_birth_certificate_exists,
    \+ biographical_details_mismatch,
    \+ us_birth_certificate_suspect.

% To be a US citizen at the applicant's birth the mother must have been a US citizen then
% which, for these facts, requires credible proof of US birth
mother_us_citizen_at_time_of_applicant_birth :-
    credible_mother_us_birth.

% Preponderance of the evidence for the applicant's claim requires that the mother was a US citizen
% at the applicant's birth, that the mother had the requisite prior physical presence, and that the
% applicant was not an LPR before turning 18.
preponderance_of_evidence :-
    mother_us_citizen_at_time_of_applicant_birth,
    mother_physical_presence_one_year,
    applicant_not_lpr_before_18.

% Eligibility for grant requires meeting the preponderance standard (all necessary conditions)
eligibility_met :-
    preponderance_of_evidence.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-

        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed Form I-140 seeking classification in the employment-based second preference category as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree (or of exceptional ability) and concurrently requested a national interest waiver of the job offer requirement.

In support of the petition the petitioner submitted a foreign-language degree certificate showing a "degree of food engineer" dated October 23, 1987 (institution name in the record is redacted). The petitioner also provided an educational evaluation described as a "document-by-document" evaluation that concluded the degree was the foreign equivalent of a U.S. Bachelor of Science in Food Science and Technology and that the program was five years in duration; the evaluation itself did not identify the specific documents it reviewed. In response to a request for evidence (RFE) from the service center, the petitioner did not submit an academic transcript that had been requested.

For work experience, the petitioner submitted various work certificates, resignation letters, and proof-of-employment letters from multiple employers, and later supplied one additional employer letter indicating the petitioner served in the position of president. The petitioner also submitted a separate document describing positions held at various employers that was not on letterhead and was undated and unsigned.

The petitioner appealed to the Administrative Appeals Office the Nebraska Service Center’s denial of the Form I-140 petition.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest, but only if a petitioner categorically establishes eligibility in the EB-2 classification.

The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2) defines advanced degree to mean any United States academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a baccalaureate. A United States baccalaureate degree or a foreign equivalent degree followed by at least five years of progressive experience in the specialty shall be considered the equivalent of a master's degree and so permit classification as an EB-2 permanent immigrant. Progressive experience can be demonstrated by the Petitioner by providing letters from current or former employers showing that they have at least five years of progressive post-baccalaureate experience in the specialty. The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.S(g)(l) requires letters from current or former employers include the name, address, and title of the writer, and a specific description of the duties performed. If we conclude that a petitioner has an advanced degree or is of exceptional ability such that they have established their eligibility for classification as an immigrant in the EB-2 classification, we evaluate the national interest in waiving the requirement of a job offer and thus a labor certification. Whilst neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," we set forth a framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions in the precedent decision Matter of Dhanasar, see supra. Dhanasar states that USCIS may as a matter of discretion grant a national interest waiver of the job offer, and thus of the labor certification, to a petitioner classified in the EB-2 category if they demonstrate that (1) the noncitizen's proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance, (2) the noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor, and (3) that on balance it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the Administrative Appeals Office case, encoded as Prolog facts.
% Use arity-0 predicates for yes/no facts as required.

filed_i140.
seeking_eb2_advanced_degree_and_niw.

% Educational evidence submitted
submitted_degree_certificate.
degree_description("degree of food engineer").
degree_date(date(1987,10,23)).

submitted_educational_evaluation.
evaluation_conclusion(foreign_equivalent_bachelors_in_food_science_and_technology).
evaluation_program_length(five_year_undergraduate).

% Evaluator did not identify documents reviewed (undermines reliability)
evaluator_did_not_identify_documents_reviewed.

% Transcript requested in RFE and not provided
received_rfe_for_transcript.
did_not_submit_transcript.

% Employment evidence submitted but has weaknesses
submitted_employment_documents.
submitted_work_certificates.
submitted_resignation_letters.
submitted_proof_of_employment_letters.
submitted_later_employer_letter_stating_president.
submitted_unsigned_undated_document_not_on_letterhead.

% Letters lack required elements as described in the case facts:
% (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(g)(1) requires name, address, title of writer, and specific duties)
employer_letters_lack_name_address_title.
employer_letters_lack_specific_description_of_duties.
some_documents_undated_or_unsigned.
some_documents_not_on_letterhead.

% The service center denied the petition and the petitioner appealed
service_center_denied_petition.
petitioner_appealed_denial.

% Rules and legal framework encoded

% EB-2 classification must be shown first to consider a national interest waiver
eb2_classification_established :-
    advanced_degree_established.
    % Note: could also allow exceptional_ability_established, but no facts here support that.

% Advanced-degree professional can be shown either by:
%  - a foreign degree that is above the baccalaureate (foreign equivalent of a US advanced degree), or
%  - a US baccalaureate or foreign equivalent PLUS five years of progressive post‑baccalaureate experience.
advanced_degree_established :-
    foreign_degree_above_baccalaureate_equivalent.
advanced_degree_established :-
    bachelor_equivalent_plus_five_progressive_years.

% The submitted evaluation concluded the foreign degree is the equivalent of a US Bachelor's.
has_foreign_equivalent_bachelor :-
    submitted_educational_evaluation,
    evaluation_conclusion(foreign_equivalent_bachelors_in_food_science_and_technology).

% A conclusion of equivalence to a US bachelor's is not itself a finding of an advanced degree
% (above baccalaureate). No evidence establishes the degree as above a baccalaureate.
foreign_degree_above_baccalaureate_equivalent :-
    submitted_educational_evaluation,
    % Evaluator would need to have concluded the degree is above baccalaureate; it did not.
    evaluation_conclusion(above_baccalaureate),
    evaluation_reliable.

% The evaluation is considered reliable only if the evaluator identified the documents reviewed
% or the petitioner provided an academic transcript when requested. Here both are absent.
evaluation_reliable :-
    evaluator_identified_documents_reviewed.
evaluation_reliable :-
    academic_transcript_submitted.

% Facts reflecting the absence of reliability
evaluator_identified_documents_reviewed :- false.  % evaluator_did_not_identify_documents_reviewed
academic_transcript_submitted :- false.            % did_not_submit_transcript

% The petitioner could alternatively qualify by a Bachelor's (or foreign equivalent) plus 5 years
bachelor_equivalent_plus_five_progressive_years :-
    has_foreign_equivalent_bachelor,
    evaluation_reliable,
    five_years_progressive_experience_documented.

% Progressive experience must be documented by letters from current/former employers
% that include name, address, title of the writer and a specific description of duties (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(g)(1)).
five_years_progressive_experience_documented :-
    submitted_employment_documents,
    employer_letters_include_name_address_title,
    employer_letters_include_specific_description_of_duties,
    letters_show_at_least_five_years_experience.

% Based on the facts, employer letters lack required elements and some are undated/unsigned/not on letterhead;
% therefore the required elements are not present and letters do not reliably show 5 years of progressive experience.
employer_letters_include_name_address_title :- false.       % employer_letters_lack_name_address_title
employer_letters_include_specific_description_of_duties :- false. % employer_letters_lack_specific_description_of_duties
letters_show_at_least_five_years_experience :- false.        % cannot be established from the defective letters

% Without a reliably proven advanced-degree equivalent or reliable demonstration of five years progressive
% experience by compliant employer letters, EB-2 classification is not established.
% (No facts support exceptional ability either; that path is not available here.)

% National interest waiver (Dhanasar) requires (1) substantial merit and national importance,
% (2) the petitioner is well positioned to advance the endeavor, and (3) on balance waiving the job
% offer requirement would benefit the United States. These are considered only if EB-2 is established.
dhanasar_prongs_met :-
    d_has_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    d_is_well_positioned_to_advance,
    d_benefit_to_waive_requirements.

% No record evidence establishes the Dhanasar prongs in this case (and these prongs are not reached
% because EB-2 classification is not established). Represent them as not proven.
d_has_substantial_merit_and_national_importance :- false.
d_is_well_positioned_to_advance :- false.
d_benefit_to_waive_requirements :- false.

% Eligibility for the national interest waiver requires both EB-2 classification and Dhanasar prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_classification_established,
    dhanasar_prongs_met.

% Entrypoint and decision format requested by the user. If eligibility_met succeeds, the case is Accepted,
% otherwise it is Dismissed.
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed Form N-600 seeking a Certificate of Citizenship to reflect that he acquired U.S. citizenship at birth through his mother. He identifies his mother as V‑G‑ (born in Mexico in 1947) and his maternal grandmother as S‑C‑ (born in 1921). The petitioner states that his claim depends on establishing that S‑C‑ resided in the United States during the years spanning S‑C‑’s birth and his mother’s birth. He was born in 1975.

To support his claim, the petitioner submitted U.S. Census records for 1930 and 1940 that list a female named S‑C‑ in California, although he acknowledges the census entries show an age and date of birth that do not exactly match the biographical data he attributes to his grandmother. He has argued that a language barrier in S‑C‑’s household at the time could explain the discrepancies in those census records. The petitioner also submitted two sworn affidavits from his uncles, J‑G‑C‑ and E‑G‑C‑, which provide detailed street addresses in the United States where they assert S‑C‑ lived; the affidavits do not include specific dates tied to those addresses in the affidavits themselves.

On motion he added genealogical and burial information from online sources and indexes showing that S‑C‑’s parents died and were buried in California in 1927, and a Social Security index entry indicating S‑C‑’s Social Security number was issued in California in 1963 and that her last residence was in California. He has stated that these pieces of evidence, taken together, place S‑C‑ in the United States in 1921, 1927, 1930, 1935, and 1940. The petitioner requested that the motion to reopen be held in abeyance while he awaited records subpoenaed from the Child Welfare, Attendance, and Records School District in California (served in January 2024) and indicated additional subpoenas were being issued; he asserted those forthcoming records will provide direct corroboration of his grandmother’s U.S. residence. In his filings the petitioner argued that the aggregate of the census entries, the affidavits, and the genealogical and Social Security records make it likely that S‑C‑ resided in the United States during the relevant periods and that the uncles’ address-specific affidavits will be confirmed by the subpoenaed documents.
statutes:
A motion to reopen must state new facts and be supported by documentary evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(2). Our review on motion is limited to reviewing our latest decision. 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(l)(ii). We may grant motions that satisfy these requirements and demonstrate eligibility for the requested benefit. See Matter of Coelho, 20 I&N Dec. 464, 473 (BIA 1992) (requiring that new evidence have the potential to change the outcome). A motion to reconsider must establish that our prior decision was based on an incorrect application of law or policy and that the decision was incorrect based on the evidence in the record of proceedings at the time of the decision. 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(3). Our review on motion is limited to reviewing our latest decision. 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(l)(ii). We may grant motions that satisfy these requirements and demonstrate eligibility for the requested benefit. The record reflects that the Applicant was born in Mexico inl 11975. The Applicant claims that his parents were married prior to his birth. The Applicant avers that his mother, although born in 1 Formerly section 30l(a)(7) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 140l(a)(7).

Mexico in 1947, acquired U.S. citizenship at birth from her mother (the Applicant's maternal grandmother), who he claims was a U.S. citizen born in California in 1921. The applicable law for transmitting citizenship to a child born abroad when one parent is a U.S. citizen is the statute that was in effect at the time of the child's birth. See Chau v. Immigration and Naturalization Service, 247 F.3d 1026, 1028 n.3 (9th Cir. 2001) (internal citation omitted). Thus, to evaluate the Applicant's U.S. citizenship claim we must first determine which laws govern acquisition of U.S. citizenship at birth by the Applicant and his mother. A. Law in Effect at the Time of the Applicant's Birth At the time of the Applicant's birth in 1975 former section 301(a)(7) of the Act governed acquisition of U.S. citizenship. It provided in relevant part that a child born abroad to one U.S. citizen and one noncitizen parent would acquire citizenship from the U.S. citizen parent if that parent "prior to the birth of such [ child], was physically present in the United States or its outlying possessions for a period or periods totaling not less than ten years, at least five ofwhich were after attaining the age of fourteen years." B. Law in Effect at the Time of the Mother's Birth When the Applicant's mother was born in 1947, section 201 (g) of the 1940 Act, which was in effect from January 13, 1941, until December 24, 1952, provided for transmission of citizenship to foreign­ born children by their U.S. citizen parent. That section provided, in relevant part, that a person born abroad to parents one of whom was a U.S. citizen, would acquire U.S. citizenship at birth if the U.S. citizen parent "has had [10] years' residence in the United States or one of its outlying possessions, at least five ofwhich were after attaining the age of [16] years" before the person's birth. Section 20l(g) of the 1940 Act further mandated that the person who acquired U.S. citizenship at birth had to comply with certain U.S. residence conditions to retain it. Specifically, the person must have resided in the United States or its outlying possessions for a period or periods totaling five years between the ages of 13 and 21 years. Because both the Applicant and his mother were born abroad, they are presumed to be noncitizens and the Applicant bears the burden of establishing their claims to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. Matter of Baires-Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467, 468 (BIA 2008). Under the preponderance of the evidence standard, the Applicant must demonstrate that his and his mother's U.S. citizenship claims are "probably true," or "more likely than not." Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. at 376.

```prolog
% Facts from the record (non-boolean facts may carry values)
petitioner_born_in_mexico.
petitioner_birth_year(1975).
mother_born_in_mexico.
mother_birth_year(1947).
grandmother_asserted_birth_place_california.
grandmother_asserted_birth_year(1921).
parents_of_grandmother_died_and_buried_in_california_1927.
census_1930_1940_entries_match_name_possible_grandmother.
ssn_issued_in_california_1963.
ssn_death_1997.
uncles_affidavits_exist.
uncles_affidavits_lack_specific_dates.
subpoenas_served_january_2024.
additional_subpoenas_pending.

% Evidentiary truth/false (zero-arity predicates as required)
% The record does not, on the present record, provide the preponderance of
% credible evidence necessary to establish the grandmother's continuous U.S.
% residence sufficient to satisfy the statutory residence requirements.
there_is_preponderance_of_evidence_for_grandmother_us_residence :- fail.

% There is no admissible evidence in the record establishing the grandmother
% met the 10-year (with 5 after age 16) residence requirement before 1947.
grandmother_met_10_years_residence_before_mother_birth :- fail.

% There is no admissible evidence that the mother (if a U.S. citizen at birth)
% resided in the United States for the 5 years between ages 13 and 21 required
% to retain citizenship under the 1940 Act.
mother_retained_residence_5_years_between_13_and_21 :- fail.

% There is no admissible evidence that the mother, as a U.S. citizen parent,
% had physical presence in the United States for 10 years (at least 5 after age 14)
% prior to the birth of the petitioner in 1975.
mother_met_10_years_physical_presence_before_applicant_birth :- fail.

% Derived predicates applying the governing statutes as described
% Grandmother is established as U.S. citizen at birth only if asserted AND
% the preponderance-of-evidence requirement is met (not met here).
grandmother_us_citizen_by_birth :-
    grandmother_asserted_birth_place_california,
    there_is_preponderance_of_evidence_for_grandmother_us_residence.

% Grandmother met the 1940 Act residence condition only if proven (not here).
grandmother_met_1940Act_residence_prior_to_1947 :-
    grandmother_us_citizen_by_birth,
    grandmother_met_10_years_residence_before_mother_birth.

% Mother acquired U.S. citizenship at birth only if grandmother was U.S. citizen
% by birth and met the 1940 Act residence requirement prior to 1947.
mother_acquired_citizenship_at_birth :-
    grandmother_us_citizen_by_birth,
    grandmother_met_1940Act_residence_prior_to_1947.

% Mother retained citizenship only if she satisfied the 5-year residence retention rule.
mother_retained_citizenship :-
    mother_acquired_citizenship_at_birth,
    mother_retained_residence_5_years_between_13_and_21.

% Applicant (petitioner) acquires citizenship at birth only if mother was a U.S. citizen
% and met the physical presence requirement in effect in 1975 (10 years, 5 after age 14).
applicant_acquired_citizenship_at_birth :-
    mother_acquired_citizenship_at_birth,
    mother_retained_citizenship,
    mother_met_10_years_physical_presence_before_applicant_birth.

% Eligibility is satisfied only if the applicant acquired citizenship at birth.
eligibility_met :-
    applicant_acquired_citizenship_at_birth.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-

            writeln(Result)
        ),

    ).

        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner is a citizen of Honduras who filed Form I-485 seeking adjustment of status under Section 13 as the spouse of a principal Section 13 applicant. The National Benefits Center denied his application on the ground that the principal applicant was ineligible. The petitioner appealed that denial, submitted the brief filed with the principal applicant’s appeal, and argues he is eligible to adjust status because compelling reasons prevent his wife from returning to Honduras.
statutes: Section 13 is an adjustment of status category for foreign nationals who can demonstrate, in part: (1) failure to maintain A-1, A-2, G-1, or G-2 nonimmigrant status as of the application's filing date; (2) performance of diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties by the principal on behalf of the accrediting country; and (3) inability, because of compelling reasons, to return to the country that accredited the foreign national. 8 U.S.C. § 1255b(b); 8 C.F.R. § 245.3. 2 Section 13 applicants bear the burden of proving their eligibility to adjust status. Section 291 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1361. 1 Pub. L. No. 85-316, 71 Stat. 642, amended by Pub. L. No. 97-116, 95 Stat. 161 (1981). The A nonimmigrant classification is for diplomats and foreign government officials (principal) as well as their immediate family members. The G nonimmigrant classification is for employees of certain international organizations (principal) and their immediate family members. See https:/ /travel.state.gov. 2 If the first three eligibility requirements are met, applicants must also establish that compelling reasons demonstrate that their adjustment would be in the national interest and would not be contrary to the national welfare, safety, or security of the United States and that they are of good moral character and admissible to the United States. Discussion of these remaining criteria is generally unnecessary in cases where the first three eligibility criteria have not been met.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the case
petitioner_is_citizen_of_honduras.
petitioner_filed_i485_section13.
petitioner_is_spouse_of_principal_section13.
principal_found_ineligible_for_lack_of_compelling_reasons.
petitioner_submitted_principal_brief.

% Burden of proof is on Section 13 applicants (not modeled as a predicate to decide).
burden_on_applicant.

% Evidence-based predicates required by Section 13.
% These are arity-0 (true/false style). They must be defined so the program
% does not trigger the handle_undefined/1 path. A predicate that cannot be
% established is implemented to fail.

% 1) Failure to maintain A-1/A-2/G-1/G-2 status as of filing date.
% No evidence in the record that this requirement was established.
failure_to_maintain_status :- fail.

% 2) Principal performed diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties on behalf of the accrediting country.
% No affirmative evidence in the record that this was established.
principal_performed_duties :- fail.

% 3) Inability, because of compelling reasons, to return to the accrediting country.
% The record explicitly states the principal was found ineligible for failing to
% establish compelling reasons. We model that finding and derive that the
% compelling-reasons requirement is not established.
compelling_reasons_prevent_return :- \+ principal_found_ineligible_for_lack_of_compelling_reasons.

% Other Section 13 downstream criteria (only relevant if the first three are met).
% The record does not address these in a way that is dispositive; they are set
% to true so they do not cause dismissal when the first three are satisfied.
good_moral_character.
admissible_and_national_interest_ok.

% Final eligibility: all required elements must be established to Accept.
% Per instructions, eligibility_met must be the last part of the program.
eligibility_met :-
    petitioner_filed_i485_section13,
    petitioner_is_spouse_of_principal_section13,
    failure_to_maintain_status,
    principal_performed_duties,
    compelling_reasons_prevent_return,
    good_moral_character,
    admissible_and_national_interest_ok.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a mechanical and aerospace engineer with a Ph.D. in Aerospace Engineering who filed an I-140 immigrant petition in the EB-2 classification and sought a national interest waiver of the job offer and labor certification requirement. The petition record and later supplemental submissions describe his professional background and documented work in computational fluid dynamics (CFD), turbomachinery aerodynamics, and related applications in aviation safety, gas turbine technology, and wind energy.

During his graduate studies and professional career he conducted, published, and presented research in CFD and related fields (the record includes five published articles and other materials). He worked on a problem involving a cone-shaped probe used in military aircraft: after aircraft manufacturers’ aerodynamicists were unable to solve a faulty in-flight static pressure reading ("pressure droop"), the probe issue was referred to the petitioner for CFD analysis. He determined the flow reached supersonic speeds and caused a shock wave, and he recommended small surface protrusions ("trip dots") to detach the shock wave. Customers implemented the modifications and the probe design was changed to incorporate that solution.

As a contractor and researcher in turbomachinery aerodynamics between roughly 2013 and 2019, he led experimental testing and computational analysis at an exhaust diffuser test rig and developed numerical techniques to model hot gas exhaust flow paths and optimize exhaust diffuser performance under varying load conditions. Supervisors and project leads submitted letters describing his role in developing improved gas turbine exhaust diffusers and turbine blade tip aerodynamics, and stating that his work contributed to delivery of new BL-class gas turbines with a smaller footprint and about 25% higher power output compared to the previous H-class, which were reported as being commercialized and used by utility companies worldwide. The petitioner’s employers and collaborators provided letters attesting that his simulations and design work were applied in real turbine development efforts; some findings were described as included in confidential exhaust diffuser design and development guidelines that could not be publicly disclosed.

The petitioner also presented evidence of cross-disciplinary applications of his work: a professor in Japan praised his doctoral research on multi-element airfoils for vertical-axis wind turbines and cited the petitioner’s work in multiple publications; industry and academic correspondents noted that he adapted aerospace techniques to wind energy engineering. The petitioner submitted screenshots of industry websites reporting worldwide commercialization of the BL-class turbines, including installations in the United States, United Kingdom, and South Korea, and references to fuel savings and reduced CO2 emissions associated with those turbines.

Additionally, the record contains reference letters and expressions of interest from potential employers and collaborators — including an engineering project manager, an assistant professor considering him for a research engineer role, and a research aerospace engineer — that cite his contributions to aviation safety, his CFD expertise, and his suitability for roles in aerospace and turbomachinery work.

The petitioner appealed the denial of the national interest waiver (the Director had found he qualified for the EB-2 advanced-degree classification but denied the waiver) and later filed motions to reopen and reconsider. In those filings and a supplemental brief he argued that the adjudicators had not adequately considered or analyzed strong examples of his record of success and practical industry impact — including the probe solution, his gas turbine diffuser work, the commercial implementation of turbine designs, and the testimonial letters and publications — and he submitted additional documentary evidence to support that contention.
statutes:
The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(l)(i) limits U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' (USCIS) authority to reopen or reconsider to instances where a petitioner has shown "proper cause" for that action. Thus, to merit reopening or reconsideration, a petitioner must not only meet the formal filing requirements at 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(l)(iii) (such as submission of a properly completed and signed Form I-290B, Notice of Appeal or Motion, with the correct fee), but also show proper cause for granting the motion. Specifically, a motion to reopen must state new facts and be supported by documentary evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(2). A motion to reconsider must establish that our prior decision was based on an incorrect application of law or policy and that the decision was incorrect based on the evidence in the record of proceedings at the time of the decision. § 103.5(a)(3). Our 1 Because the Director found the Petitioner eligible for classification as a member of the professions possessing an advanced degree, the decision did not evaluate the Petitioner's alternative claim that he also qualifies for EB-2 classification as an individual of exceptional ability.

I
review on motion is limited to reviewing our latest decision. 8 C.F.R. § 103.S(a)(l )(ii). We may grant motions that satisfy these requirements and demonstrate eligibility for the requested benefit.

IT. BACKGROUND
Immediately prior to filing the instant petition, the Petitioner was employed as a senior engineer for ___________ and in this role, he performed aerodynamics and computational fluid dynamics (CFD) engineering services as a contractor for ___________ in Florida. He indicates that he intends to continue his work in the field of mechanical and aerospace engineering in the United States with a specific focus in CFD, which he explains is a branch of fluid mechanics that uses numerical analysis and data structures to analyze and solve problems that involve fluid flows. The record indicates that the Petitioner's proposed endeavor as a mechanical and aerospace engineer specializing in CFD is to conduct fundamental studies offlow physics for special applications; develop numerical tools and methods for addressing general and special challenges; conduct root cause analysis to identify and resolve aerothermal problems and fluid structure interactions; and design and analyze devices and components with CFD before and after experiments. He indicates that his work will provide greater information and further an understanding in the field of CFD by promoting multi­ disciplinary interactions and applications ofvarious methods and technologies based in CFD research. The Petitioner explained the manner in which his studies would examine the overlap of various technologies, noting the relationship between aircraft wings and turbine blades for power generation and the manner in which aircraft aerodynamics can lead to the production of aerodynamically fuel­ efficient automobiles. The record demonstrates that the Petitioner earned a Ph.D. and a master's degree in aerospace engineering from the ________________ in 2015 and 2011, respectively. The record also indicates that the Petitioner attained two foreign specialized engineering degrees, involving energetics and combustion, prior to completing his graduate work in the United States. Therefore, our appellate decision determined that the record demonstrates that the Petitioner qualifies as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree. In dismissing the combined motions, we affirmed our appellate determination that the Petitioner met the first prong ofthe analytical framework described in the precedent decision Matter ofDhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884 (AAO 2016), but had not sufficiently established that he is well-positioned to advance the proposed endeavor under the second prong. Dhanasar provides that after a petitioner has established eligibility for EB-2 classification, USCIS may, as matter of discretion,2 grant a national interest waiver ifthe petitioner shows: (1) the proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; (2) the individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and (3) on balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. 3 Because our motion decision concluded that the Petitioner did not meet the second 2 See also Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver is discretionary in nature). 3 See Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. at 888-91, for elaboration on these three prongs. 2

prong, we did not analyze prong three, the remaining eligibility ground, under the Dhanasar analytical framework. 4 While the motion was pending, USCIS updated the USCIS Policy Manual's guidance to provide additional guidance regarding specific evidentiary considerations relating to persons with advanced degrees in science, technology, engineering, or mathematics (STEM). 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.5(D)(2), https://www.uscis.gov/policy-manual/volume-6-part-f-chapter-5. See also USCIS Policy Alert, P A-2022-02, National Interest Waivers for Advanced Degree Professionals or Persons of Exceptional Ability (Jan. 21, 2022), https://www.uscis.gov/sites/default/files/document/policy­ manual-updates/20220121-N ationa!InterestWaivers.pdf. As the Petitioner in this case possesses an advanced degree in a STEM field tied to the proposed endeavor he is impacted by the above policy manual updates, but our motion decision did not address them. As such, in February 2024 we reopened the Petitioner's motion sua sponte, pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 103.S(a)(S)(ii), to consider anew the merits of the claims contained in his motion brief. The Petitioner was permitted a period of 30 days in which to provide a supplemental brief. In the Petitioner's supplemental brief, he asserts that our motion decision was based on an incorrect application of USCIS policy and argues the record establishes the Petitioner has met the remaining two prongs set forth in the Dhanasar analytical framework.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
% Facts parsed from the Administrative Appeals Office case (converted into Prolog predicates).
% Use arity 0 predicates for true/false facts per instructions.

% Education and professional standing
advanced_degree.                 % Petitioner holds a Ph.D. in Aerospace Engineering.
profession_as_engineer.          % Petitioner is a mechanical and aerospace engineer.

% Technical focus and contributions
career_focus_cfd.                % Career work focuses on computational fluid dynamics (CFD).
led_experimental_testing.        % Led experimental testing on exhaust diffuser test rig.
developed_numerical_techniques.  % Developed numerical techniques to model hot gas exhaust flow and optimize diffuser performance.
turbomachinery_aerodynamics.      % Work specifically involved turbomachinery aerodynamics.
diagnosed_inflight_instrumentation_issue. % Diagnosed in-flight instrumentation safety problem with cone probes.
solution_implemented_by_manufacturer.    % His solution ("trip dots") was implemented by probe manufacturer for military aircraft.
doctoral_research_wind_energy.   % Doctoral research applied multi-element airfoils to vertical axis wind turbines.
work_cited_by_academic_collaborator. % An academic collaborator cited and used his doctoral work.
contributed_to_commercialized_turbines.  % Contributions instrumental to gas turbines reported to be commercialized and used by utilities.
some_findings_confidential.      % Some project findings included in confidential proprietary guidelines.

% Evidence and support
publications_at_least_five.      % Petitioner has five published articles.
letters_of_support_from_supervisors_and_peers. % Letters from supervisors, project leads, industry engineers, academic researchers.
correspondence_from_potential_employers.      % Correspondence from potential employers expressing interest.

% Intent and proposed endeavor
proposed_endeavor_is_cfd_research_and_development. % Proposed endeavor: continue CFD research and engineering in US.
proposed_endeavor_focuses_on_aerospace_safety_wind_energy_and_cfd_applications.

% Procedural/motion context and regulatory considerations
director_approved_advanced_degree_classification. % Director approved advanced-degree classification (EB-2 classification).
director_denied_niw.             % Director denied the national interest waiver initially.
petitioner_appealed_and_moved.   % Petitioner appealed and filed motions to reopen/reconsider with additional evidence.
motion_included_new_facts_and_evidence. % Motion to reopen included new facts and documentary evidence.
motion_argued_incorrect_application_of_law. % Motion to reconsider argued prior decision applied law/policy incorrectly.
uscis_reopened_sua_sponte.       % USCIS reopened the petition sua sponte to consider updated policy guidance.
supplemental_brief_submitted.     % Petitioner submitted a supplemental brief when given opportunity.
uscis_policy_manual_updates_considered. % USCIS considered updates to policy manual for STEM advanced-degree applicants.

% Legal standards (Dhanasar framework and motion rules)
dhanasar_prong1_requires_substantial_merit_and_national_importance.
dhanasar_prong2_requires_well_positioned_to_advance.
dhanasar_prong3_requires_on_balance_waiving_would_benefit_us.
motion_to_reopen_requires_new_facts_and_documentary_evidence.
motion_to_reconsider_requires_incorrect_application_of_law_or_policy.
review_on_motion_limited_to_latest_decision.

% Evaluation rules mapping the facts to the Dhanasar prongs and procedural requirements.
% Prong 1: Proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance.
proposed_endeavor_has_substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    dhanasar_prong1_requires_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    proposed_endeavor_is_cfd_research_and_development,
    (   proposed_endeavor_focuses_on_aerospace_safety_wind_energy_and_cfd_applications
    ),  % these domains are matters of national importance (aerospace safety, power generation, renewable energy)
    publications_at_least_five.

% Prong 2: Individual is well-positioned to advance the proposed endeavor.
well_positioned_to_advance :-
    dhanasar_prong2_requires_well_positioned_to_advance,
    advanced_degree,
    profession_as_engineer,
    career_focus_cfd,
    (   led_experimental_testing,
        developed_numerical_techniques,
        turbomachinery_aerodynamics
    ),
    (   contributed_to_commercialized_turbines ; solution_implemented_by_manufacturer ),
    letters_of_support_from_supervisors_and_peers,
    correspondence_from_potential_employers.

% Prong 3: On balance, granting waiver would benefit the United States.
waiving_job_offer_would_benefit_united_states :-
    dhanasar_prong3_requires_on_balance_waiving_would_benefit_us,
    % Benefits include contributions to aerospace safety, energy generation efficiency, commercialized products, and employer interest
    (   contributed_to_commercialized_turbines ;
        solution_implemented_by_manufacturer ;
        doctoral_research_wind_energy
    ),
    correspondence_from_potential_employers.

% Procedural requirements for motions/review under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5
procedural_requirements_met :-
    % USCIS reopened the motion sua sponte and allowed supplemental filing; petitioner submitted brief and evidence.
    uscis_reopened_sua_sponte,
    supplemental_brief_submitted,
    usc is_policy_consideration_done.

% Create a bridging predicate name matching the actual predicate used above (avoid spaces).
uscis_policy_consideration_done :-
    usc is_policy_manual_updates_considered.

% Note: The token "usc is_policy_manual_updates_considered." contains a space which is invalid in Prolog.
% Replace with the correct atom name without spaces.
% (Prolog identifiers cannot contain spaces; redefine the correct atom and tie procedural_requirements_met to it.)

% Redefine correctly:
uscis_policy_manual_updates_considered.  % correct atom for policy consideration

% Re-define procedural_requirements_met using the correct atom name.
procedural_requirements_met :-
    uscis_reopened_sua_sponte,
    supplemental_brief_submitted,
    uscis_policy_manual_updates_considered.

% Eligibility is met only if all three Dhanasar prongs and procedural requirements are satisfied.
eligibility_met :-
    proposed_endeavor_has_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned_to_advance,
    waiving_job_offer_would_benefit_united_states,
    procedural_requirements_met.

% Entrypoint and decision output structure required by the user.
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```
