text:
The petitioner is an accountant who filed an I-140 seeking second-preference immigrant classification (EB-2) both as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree and as an individual of exceptional ability, together with a request for a national interest waiver of the job-offer requirement. He submitted a diploma showing a degree in public accounting and an academic evaluation stating that his credential is the foreign equivalent of a U.S. bachelor’s degree in accounting, along with experience letters from past employers. In his initial filing he described his proposed work as providing financial accounting, managerial accounting, credit analysis and credit management, financial management, business and operations management, and project management services. In response to a request for evidence, he submitted an updated personal plan stating his endeavor is to offer financial management services to U.S.-based businesses, mainly focusing on finances, accounting, and budgeting, and he also submitted academic records, letters from employers and colleagues, and an expert opinion letter.

The petition was denied by the Texas Service Center director, who concluded the record did not establish that the petitioner was well positioned to advance his proposed endeavor and that, on balance, a waiver of the job-offer requirement would not be beneficial; the director did not discuss the substantial merit or national importance of the endeavor and did not make a determination on whether the petitioner qualifies for EB-2 classification. On appeal the petitioner challenges the director’s decision, asserting that the director applied inappropriate evidentiary requirements (for example, demanding proof that the petitioner served “as an impetus for progress” or had an “indispensable role of success” in the field) and failed to properly consider the updated personal plan, the expert opinion letter, and other submitted evidence.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2) contains the following relevant definitions: Advanced degree means any United States academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of baccalaureate. A United States baccalaureate degree or a foreign equivalent degree followed by at least five years of progressive experience in the specialty shall be considered the equivalent of a master's degree. If a doctoral degree is

customarily required by the specialty, the alien must have a United States doctorate or a foreign equivalent degree. Exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business means a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business. In order to qualify as an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, the arts, or business, a petitioner must initially submit documentation that satisfies at least three of six categories of evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F). Meeting at least three criteria, however, does not, in and of itself: establish eligibility for this classification. If a petitioner does so, we will then conduct a final merits determination to decide whether the evidence in its totality shows that they are recognized as having a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the field. Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or an individual of exceptional ability, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion 1, grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the requirements of a job offer and a labor certification would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts parsed from the record
petitioner_accountant.
filed_i140_eb2_national_interest_waiver.

% Proposed endeavor: providing accounting and financial management services to U.S. businesses
described_proposed_endeavor_accounting_services.

% Submitted evidence
has_diploma.
has_academic_evaluation.
academic_evaluation_equates_bachelors.  % foreign degree = US bachelor's equivalent
has_experience_letters.                % letters from past employers and colleagues
has_expert_opinion_letter.
submitted_updated_personal_plan.

% Director's decision facts (for completeness of the record)
director_denied_well_positioned.
director_did_not_address_merit_importance.
director_did_not_determine_eb2_qualification.

% Appeal arguments (contextual)
appeal_challenges_denial.
appeal_asserts_improper_evidentiary_requirements.
appeal_requests_reconsideration.

% Regulatory definitions (encoded as rules)

% Advanced degree professional:
% - has_advanced_degree if a degree above baccalaureate (not present here)
% - OR has_bachelors_equivalent AND five years progressive experience (no explicit 5 years in record)
has_advanced_degree :- false.  % no evidence of an advanced degree above baccalaureate
has_bachelors_equivalent :- academic_evaluation_equates_bachelors.
has_five_years_progressive_experience :- false.  % not established in the record

qualifies_advanced_degree_professional :-
    has_advanced_degree
    ;
    (   has_bachelors_equivalent,
        has_five_years_progressive_experience
    ).

% Exceptional ability: must initially satisfy at least three of six regulatory categories.
% Map available evidence to categories:
% (A) Degree - satisfied by academic evaluation / diploma
% (B) Letters from current/former employers - satisfied by has_experience_letters
% (C) Professional license - not present
% (D) Evidence of salary or other remuneration - not present
% (E) Membership in professional associations - not present
% (F) Recognition for achievements - partially represented by expert opinion letter

category_A_degree :- has_diploma; has_academic_evaluation; academic_evaluation_equates_bachelors.
category_B_letters :- has_experience_letters.
category_C_license :- false.
category_D_salary_evidence :- false.
category_E_membership :- false.
category_F_recognition :- has_expert_opinion_letter.

exceptional_criteria_count(N) :-
    findall(1,
            (   member(P, [category_A_degree,
                          category_B_letters,
                          category_C_license,
                          category_D_salary_evidence,
                          category_E_membership,
                          category_F_recognition]),
                Goal =.. [P],
                call(Goal)
            ),
            L),
    length(L, N).

qualifies_exceptional_ability :-
    exceptional_criteria_count(N),
    N >= 3.

% First-step EB-2 eligibility: either advanced-degree professional or exceptional ability
qualifies_for_eb2 :-
    qualifies_advanced_degree_professional
    ;
    qualifies_exceptional_ability.

% Dhanasar framework for national interest waiver (NIW)
% 1) Proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance
% For accounting/financial management services to U.S. businesses, the record describes
% an endeavor that advances economic/operational capacities of U.S. businesses;
% the petitioner described providing financial/managerial accounting, credit analysis,
% financial management, business/operations management, budgeting, project management.
endeavor_has_substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    described_proposed_endeavor_accounting_services.

% 2) Individual is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor
% The petitioner submitted an updated personal plan, experience letters, expert opinion,
% and academic evaluation/diploma. We require: updated plan plus supporting documentary
% evidence (letters and expert opinion) to find the petitioner well-positioned.
well_positioned_to_advance :-
    submitted_updated_personal_plan,
    has_experience_letters,
    has_expert_opinion_letter,
    has_academic_evaluation.

% 3) On balance, waiving the job offer and labor certification would benefit the United States
% For services that support U.S. businesses' financial management, when the petitioner
% is well-positioned and the endeavor has merit/importance, the balance favors a waiver.
waiving_job_offer_and_labor_certification_benefits_us :-
    endeavor_has_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned_to_advance.

% All NIW prongs met
niw_prongs_met :-
    endeavor_has_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned_to_advance,
    waiving_job_offer_and_labor_certification_benefits_us.

% Eligibility for NIW requires EB-2 qualification and the NIW prongs
eligibility_met :-
    qualifies_for_eb2,
    niw_prongs_met.

% Entrypoint and output structure (must be last)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a native and citizen of India who earned a bachelor of medicine and surgery degree there, then came to the United States for a medical residency and fellowships at university medical centers. He currently works in the U.S. as a cardiac physiologist at a hospital, providing care involving cardiac implantable electronic devices (CIEDs) such as permanent pacemakers and cardioverter defibrillators. Since 2019 he has co-authored seven peer-reviewed research papers; his work has been cited more than 900 times by doctors and scientists in 55 countries. 

His research includes a 2020 study on the combination of hydroxychloroquine and azithromycin that reported an increased risk of life‑threatening heart arrhythmias, which was followed by U.S. media coverage and an FDA warning days after publication. He also worked on a study involving left ventricular assist devices (LVADs) showing significant improvement in pulmonary artery pressure for patients with severe pulmonary hypertension, and in 2021 he was first author on research that supported reducing discharge times for CIED-implant patients from 24 hours to six hours by showing when acute complications occur. The record contains letters from experts (some who stated they had not worked with him) describing the public‑health importance and clinical impact of his research, and he submitted evidence about his citation rate, which he asserts places him within the top 1% of authors in clinical medicine over the past five years. He also submitted documentation showing his participation as a judge of others’ work, original contributions of significance to his field, and authorship of scholarly articles. The petition was denied by the Nebraska Service Center, and the petitioner appealed, contending that the decision overlooked or disregarded evidence demonstrating his acclaim and recognition.
statutes:
To qualify as a noncitizen with extraordinary ability, a petitioner must demonstrate that they: • Have "extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics;" • Seek to continue work in their field of expertise in the United States; and • Through their work, would substantially benefit the country. Section 203(b)(1 )(A)(i)-(iii) of the Act. The term "extraordinary ability" means expertise commensurate with "one of that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2).

Evidence must demonstrate a noncitizen's receipt of either "a major, international recognized award" or satisfaction of at least three of ten lesser evidentiary criteria. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i-x). 1 If a petitioner meets either standard and all other preliminary requirements, USCIS must then make a final merits determination as to whether the record, as a whole, establishes sustained national or international acclaim and recognized achievements placing them among the small percentage at their field's very top. Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115, 1119-20 (9th Cir. 2010); see generally 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.(2)(B), www.uscis.gov/policy-manual.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the petitioner (arity-0 booleans where appropriate)
petitioner_from_india.
has_bachelor_of_medicine_and_surgery.
completed_residency_and_fellowships_in_us.
works_as_cardiac_physiologist.
provides_care_involving_CIEDs.

% Publication and impact facts
publications_count(7).
peer_reviewed_publications.
citations_gt_900.
citations_from_countries(55).

% Specific research outputs
study_hcq_azithromycin_2020.
study_LVAD_improved_pulmonary_artery_pressure.
study_CIED_discharge_reduction_2021.
first_authored_2021_study.

% Supporting documentation submitted
submitted_expert_letters.
expert_letters_from_us_assistant_or_associate_professors.
media_coverage_FDA_warning_after_publication.

% Intent to continue work in the U.S.
seeks_to_continue_research_and_clinical_activities.

% Administrative facts about appeal
appealed_denial.

% The petitioner did not present a major, internationally recognized award.
has_major_award :- fail.

% Criteria from 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i-x) mapped to available evidence.
% Criterion: Authorship of scholarly articles in professional journals
criterion_authorship_scholarly_articles :-
    publications_count(N),
    N >= 1,
    peer_reviewed_publications.

% Criterion: Original scientific, scholarly, or business-related contributions of major significance
% We treat high citation count plus demonstrable clinical or regulatory impact (FDA warning, clinical outcome improvements)
% or corroborating expert letters as evidence of original contributions of major significance.
criterion_original_contributions_of_major_significance :-
    citations_gt_900,
    ( media_coverage_FDA_warning_after_publication
    ; study_LVAD_improved_pulmonary_artery_pressure
    ; study_CIED_discharge_reduction_2021
    ; submitted_expert_letters ).

% Criterion: Published material about the alien in professional or major trade publications or other major media
criterion_published_material_about_petitioner :-
    media_coverage_FDA_warning_after_publication.

% Count how many of the relevant criteria are present.
count_criteria(N) :-
    CriteriaPreds = [
        criterion_authorship_scholarly_articles,
        criterion_original_contributions_of_major_significance,
        criterion_published_material_about_petitioner
    ],
    findall(1,
        ( member(PredName, CriteriaPreds),
          Goal =.. [PredName],
          call(Goal)
        ),
        L),
    length(L, N).

satisfies_at_least_three_criteria :-
    count_criteria(N),
    N >= 3.

% Evidence standard: either a major award or at least three of the ten criteria
evidence_standard_met :-
    (   has_major_award
    ->  true
    ;   satisfies_at_least_three_criteria
    ).

% Core statutory/principal requirements (Section 203(b)(1)(A)(i)-(iii) of the Act)
extraordinary_ability_in_sciences :-
    % Petitioner is in the sciences (cardiac physiology / medical research)
    works_as_cardiac_physiologist,
    peer_reviewed_publications.

seeks_to_continue_work :-
    seeks_to_continue_research_and_clinical_activities.

substantial_benefit_to_the_us :-
    % Substantial benefit is supported by high citation impact, multi-country influence,
    % demonstrable clinical outcome improvements, and regulatory attention (FDA)
    (   citations_gt_900
    ;   citations_from_countries(_)
    ;   study_LVAD_improved_pulmonary_artery_pressure
    ;   study_CIED_discharge_reduction_2021
    ;   media_coverage_FDA_warning_after_publication
    ).

% Final eligibility determination aggregates all necessary elements.
eligibility_met :-
    extraordinary_ability_in_sciences,
    seeks_to_continue_work,
    substantial_benefit_to_the_us,
    evidence_standard_met.

% Entrypoint and decision output (must be the last part)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner seeks classification as an immigrant investor. The Chief of the Immigrant Investor Program Office denied the petition, and on appeal the petitioner submitted a brief and additional evidence. The petitioner claims to have invested at least $500,000 in the new commercial enterprise. An August 2015 business plan states that the enterprise sought funds from noncitizen investors to lend to a project in Illinois involving hotel renovation and construction of a mixed-use tower with commercial and residential units and a parking facility. Corporate documents dated January 23, 2017 state that the petitioner's business agreed to lend him $600,000 for use as his immigrant-investor contribution, with authority to deduct his annual dividends if the loan was not repaid by January 31, 2020; a January 2020 agreement extended repayment to January 2022. Bank records show that the petitioner's HSBC account received $600,000 and that on January 24, 2017 his account remitted $500,100 to the enterprise's escrow account. In an August 2020 statement, the petitioner described his business as an offshore trading company handling export sales of electronic products and other goods purchased from mainland China, with proceeds remitted to an HSBC offshore banking unit account in Hong Kong, and on appeal he stated that the business was not registered in China, that his China address was used only for mail, and that the business had no China operations there. He submitted bank statements from 2011 through 2017 showing frequent large deposits and withdrawals, but those statements do not identify the sources of most deposits. He also submitted documents concerning his employment history in Taiwan, including labor-insurance and salary records, business registrations and information for former employers, an employment verification certificate for a position held from 2004 to 2006, business and financial records for his Taiwan company, and information about household income levels in Taiwan. Other submitted evidence indicates that he registered a business in 2009 and invested $50,000 in that company. On appeal, he asserted that his employment income from 1994 to 2009 and his business earnings supported the claimed source of the invested funds, and he stated that obtaining older records was difficult because employers and banks may no longer retain them and he had moved away from Taiwan and lost contact with former employers.
statutes:
A noncitizen may be classified as an immigrant investor if they invest the requisite amount of qualifying capital in an NCE. A noncitizen may invest the required funds directly in an NCE or through a regional center, as the Petitioner has done in this case. Regional centers can pool immigrant 1 On March 15, 2022, President Joseph Biden signed the EB-5 Refonn and Integrity Act, which made significant amendments to the EB-5 program, including the designation of targeted employment areas and the minimum investment amounts. See Section 203(b )(5) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1 l 53(b )(5) (2022). As the Petitioner had filed his petition in March 2017, the relevant law then in existence governs this appellate adjudication. 2 The Petitioner indicated that the NCE is located in a targeted employment area, and that the required amount of qualifying capital is downwardly adjusted from $1 ,000,000 to $500,000. See 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(f)(2) (2017). 3 is an entity that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) has designated to participate in the EB-5 program. A regional center is an economic unit involved with the promotion of economic growth, "including ... improved regional productivity, job creation, and increased dom estic capital investment." See 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e) (2017).

(and other) investor funds for qualifying projects that create jobs directly or indirectly. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(4)(iii) (2017). A noncitizen must demonstrate that they have placed their own capital at risk in the NCE. See Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. 206, 213 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998); Matter of Soffici, 22 I&N Dec. 158, 165 n.3 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998) (stating that "[a] petitioner must ... establish, pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e), that funds invested are [their] own"). In addition, the noncitizen must show that their invested capital did not derive, directly or indirectly, from unlawful means. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e). To show the lawful source of the funds, an investor must submit evidence such as foreign business and tax records or documentation identifying sources of the capital. See 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(3). Bank letters or statements corroborating the deposit of funds by themselves are insufficient to demonstrate their lawful source. Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at 210-11; Matter ofIzummi, 22 I&N Dec. 169, 195 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998). The record must trace the path of the funds back to a lawful source. 4 Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at 210-11; Matter ofIzummi, 22 I&N Dec. at 195.

text:
The petitioner filed Form I-526 in 2017 seeking EB-5 classification based on an asserted investment of $500,000 into a new commercial enterprise. On the petition (pages 5–6) he indicated he had invested $500,000. The project’s Confidential Private Placement Memorandum described the NCE as a pooled financing vehicle that would raise up to $23,000,000 from as many as 46 immigrant investors to finance construction of a 300‑room hotel with a casino and an amusement park in Mississippi. The petitioner identified the enterprise as principally doing business in a targeted employment area, which supported the reduced $500,000 investment amount claimed.

The Investor Program Office issued a request for evidence asking the petitioner to establish that he had made a qualifying investment and that the NCE was located in a TEA. After the petitioner responded, the Chief of the Investor Program Office denied the petition, citing several perceived deficiencies in the record, including whether the required capital had been made available to the business most closely responsible for job creation, whether construction of the hotel/casino had begun, whether the projected jobs would be created, and whether the methodologies used to demonstrate indirect job creation were reasonable. The Chief’s decision also referenced “publicly available sources” that, according to the decision, showed only partial construction progress on the overall project and no progress on the hotel/casino; those sources were not identified in the decision. The petitioner subsequently appealed that denial.
statutes:
A noncitizen may be classified as an immigrant investor if they invest the requisite amount of qualifying capital in an NCE. A noncitizen may invest the required funds directly in an NCE or through a regional center, as the Petitioner has done in this case. Regional centers can pool immigrant 1 On March 15, 2022, President Joseph Biden signed the EB-5 Reform and Integrity Act, which made significant amendments to the EB-5 program, including the designation of targeted employment areas (TEA) and the minimum investment amounts. See Section 203(b )(5) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § l l 53(b )(5) (2022). As the Petitioner had filed his petition in November 2017, the relevant law then in existence governs this appellate adjudication.

(and other) investor funds for qualifying projects that create jobs directly or indirectly. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(4)(iii) (2019). A noncitizen must demonstrate that they have placed their own capital at risk in the NCE. See Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. 206, 213 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998); Matter ofSofjici, 22 I&N Dec. 158, 165 n.3 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998) (stating that "[a] petitioner must ... establish, pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e), that funds invested are [their] own"). In addition, the noncitizen must show that their invested capital did not derive, directly or indirectly, from unlawful means. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e). To show the lawful source of the funds, an investor must submit evidence such as foreign business and tax records or documentation identifying sources of the capital. See 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(3). Bank letters or statements corroborating the deposit of funds by themselves are insufficient to demonstrate their lawful source. Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at210-11; Matteroflzummi, 22 I&NDec. 169, 195 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998). The record must trace the path of the funds back to a lawful source.2 Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at 210-11; Matter ofIzummi, 22 I&N Dec. at 195. As required by 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(4)(i), the petition must establish that the investment ofthe required amount of capital in a new commercial enterprise will create full-time positions for at least 10 qualifying employees within two years. See also 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(5)(A)(ii). For purposes of the Form I-526 adjudication and the job creation requirements, the two-year period described in 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(4)(i)(B) is deemed to commence six months after the adjudication of the Form I-526. According to 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(4)(i), to show that a new commercial enterprise will create full-time positions for at least IO qualifying employees within two years, the petition must be accompanied by: (A) Documentation consisting of photocopies of relevant tax records, Forms I-9, or other similar documents for ten (10) qualifying employees, if such employees have already been hired following the establishment of the new commercial enterprise; or (B) A copy of a comprehensive business plan showing that, due to the nature and projected size of the new commercial enterprise, the need for fewer than ten (10) qualifying employees will result, including approximate dates, within the next two years, and when such employees will be hired. A petition is not required to demonstrate that IO full-time positions for qualifying employees have already been created by the commercial enterprise. However, where the jobs have not already been created, the petition must include a comprehensive business plan demonstrating the need for at least IO employees within the next two years. Matter ofHo explained that a comprehensive business plan must be sufficiently detailed to permit USCIS to draw reasonable inferences about job-creation potential. Matter of Ho, 22 I&N Dec. 206, at 213. Additionally, Matter of Ho held that a"comprehensive business plan as contemplated by the regulations should contain, at a minimum, a description of the business, its products and/or services, and its objectives." Id. 2 These requirements "serve a valid government interest; i.e., to confirm that the funds utilized in the [EB-5] program are not of suspect origin." Spencer Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 229 F. Supp. 2d 1025, 1040 (E.D. Cal. 2001) (holding that a petitioner had not established the lawful source of her funds because, in part, she did not designate the nature of all of her employment or submit five years of tax returns), aff'd, 345 F.3d 683 (9th Cir. 2003). 2

Under the statute and regulations, petitioners investing in a new commercial enterprise within a regional center may rely on economic methodologies to demonstrate that the investment will create indirect jobs as a result of the investment in the NCE, but such methodologies must be reasonable. See Appropriations Act at§ 610; 8 C.F.R. §§ 204.6(e), (i)(4)(iii), (m)(7)(ii). The Petitioner must provide sufficient evidence for USCIS to determine whether methodologies used are reasonable. Indirect jobs are those that are held outside ofthe new commercial enterprise but are created as a result of the new commercial enterprise. They include jobs that may be considered "economically direct," such as jobs created directly by a job-creating entity that is not also the new commercial enterprise. They also include economically indirect jobs, such as those further down the supply chain, or induced jobs created through increased spending.

text: The petitioner filed Form I-526 in 2015 seeking immigrant investor (EB-5) classification and indicated on the petition that she had invested $500,000 in a new commercial enterprise (NCE). The NCE was described as a pooled investment vehicle intended to raise up to $6,000,000 from as many as 12 EB-5 investors to finance construction of a mixed-use facility in California; the petitioner signed an agreement to invest in that project. The petitioner asserts that her $500,000 investment consisted of funds gifted to her by her father. In response to requests for additional documentation, she submitted a personal statement saying that, at the time she signed the investment agreement, she believed there were fewer than 12 investors and that she did not have information about other investors. She also provided evidence intended to document the lawful source and the complete transfer path of her investment funds, including her father’s employment and income records and financial records showing the transfers. After the petition was denied, the petitioner filed an appeal and, with her appeal, submitted a brief and additional evidence: an updated business plan and economic study, evidence that some EB-5 investors withdrew their petitions, and tax and financial records for the NCE. She also stated on appeal that although eight EB-5 investors withdrew, the project ultimately reached completion with an additional construction loan that increased the total investment. The petitioner appealed the denial to challenge that decision and to present the additional documentation and explanations described above.

(Assoc. Comm'r 1998) (stating that "[a] petitioner must ... establish, pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e), that funds invested are [their] own"). In addition, the noncitizen must show that their invested capital did not derive, directly or indirectly, from unlawful means. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e). To show the lawful source of the funds, an investor must submit evidence such as foreign business and tax records or documentation identifying sources of the capital. See 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(3). Bank letters or statements corroborating the deposit of funds by themselves are insufficient to demonstrate their lawful source. Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at 210-11; Matter ofIzummi, 22 I&N Dec. 169, 195 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998). The record must trace the path of the funds back to a lawful source.2 Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at 210-11; Matter ofIzummi, 22 I&N Dec. at 195.

```prolog
funds_owned_by_petitioner :-
    father_gift_and_transfers_documented,
    petitioner_provided_employment_income_records_of_father,
    petitioner_provided_transfer_records_father_to_petitioner_and_petitioner_to_nce.

% Path of funds must be traced back to lawful source (Matter of Ho / Izummi): require transfer documentation + income/source documentation
path_of_funds_traced :-
    petitioner_provided_transfer_records_father_to_petitioner_and_petitioner_to_nce,
    petitioner_provided_employment_income_records_of_father.

% Lawful source: if funds are owned by petitioner and the path is traced, or if NCE records corroborate developer equity sources
funds_lawfully_sourced :-
    funds_owned_by_petitioner,
    path_of_funds_traced.
funds_lawfully_sourced :-
    nce_tax_financial_records_provided.  % records provided on appeal can corroborate source of developer equity/non-EB-5 capital

% Funds must be placed at risk in the NCE (i.e., transferred to and used by the NCE)
funds_placed_at_risk_in_nce :-
    petitioner_provided_transfer_records_father_to_petitioner_and_petitioner_to_nce.

% Developer equity must be lawfully obtained; the Chief questioned $7.8M of developer equity, but on appeal the petitioner provided NCE tax/financial records and an updated plan/economic study
developer_equity_lawful :-
    nce_tax_financial_records_provided.
developer_equity_lawful :-
    petitioner_submitted_updated_business_plan_and_economic_study.

% Capital must be made available to the business most closely responsible for job creation.
% This can be satisfied either by the investor funds actually being applied to that business, or by the combination of investor funds and additional lawful financing (e.g., construction loan) which led to project completion and job creation as supported by economic study.
capital_made_available_to_business_most_closely_responsible_for_job_creation :-
    funds_placed_at_risk_in_nce,
    developer_equity_lawful,
    petitioner_submitted_updated_business_plan_and_economic_study.
capital_made_available_to_business_most_closely_responsible_for_job_creation :-
    funds_placed_at_risk_in_nce,
    construction_loan_added_completing_project,
    petitioner_submitted_updated_business_plan_and_economic_study,
    nce_project_completed.

% Eligibility combines all required elements from law and the evidence submitted on appeal.
eligibility_met :-
    invested_in_nce,
    funds_owned_by_petitioner,
    funds_lawfully_sourced,
    path_of_funds_traced,
    funds_placed_at_risk_in_nce,
    capital_made_available_to_business_most_closely_responsible_for_job_creation.

% Entrypoint and decision boilerplate (must be last)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The Petitioner is an information-technology consulting company that filed an L-1A petition in May 2023 to employ the Beneficiary as its president and requested a change of status from the Beneficiary’s B-1 business-visitor status. In September 2023, USCIS mailed two decisions to the company address listed on the Form I-129—one denying the requested L-1A classification and one denying the requested change of status. The Petitioner acknowledges it received and provided a copy of the change-of-status denial but states that it did not receive the decision explaining the grounds for denial of the L-1A classification and that it requested that L-1A classification decision from USCIS.

The Petitioner notes a history of mail issues at the address on file: the Postal Service returned the petition’s receipt notice as “undeliverable as addressed” in May and August 2023, the company did receive USCIS’s June 2023 request for additional evidence and the September 2023 change-of-status decision, and USCIS records show another returned piece of mail in October 2023 after mailing the L-1A classification decision. On appeal, the Petitioner states it did not receive notice of the L-1A denial grounds and requests an opportunity to review those grounds and submit a response.
statutes:
U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) must explain "the specific reasons" for a petition's denial "in writing." 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(l)(i). The Agency may send a written denial notice"by ordinary mail addressed to the affected party ... at [their] last known address." 8 C.F.R. § 103.8(a)(l)(i). If a petition's decision is properly addressed to the petitioner and sent by regular mail according to normal office procedures, we presume the decision's delivery to the address. See Mauricio-Benitez v. Sessions, 908 F.3d 144, 149 (5th Cir. 2018); Matter ofM-R-A-, 24 l&N Dec. 665, 673 (BIA 2008).

This presumption, however, is weaker than the "strong presumption" that applies to documents sent by certified mail. Id."[A] 11 relevant evidence submitted to overcome the weaker presumption of delivery must be considered." Matter ofM-R-A-, 24 I&N Dec. at 674. In determining whether a petitioner has rebutted the weaker presumption, an adjudicator may consider a variety of factors including, but not limited to: l) the petitioner's affidavit; 2) affidavits from people who are knowledgeable about whether notice was received; 3) the petitioner's actions upon learning of the denial, and whether they exercised due diligence in seeking to redress the situation; and 4) any other circumstances or evidence indicating the decision's possible nonreceipt. Mauricio-Benitez, 908 F.3d at 149-50; Matter ofM-R-A-, 24 I&N Dec. at 674.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the case (arity-0 true/false predicates)
petitioner_is_company.
filed_L1A_may_2023.
requested_change_of_status_from_B1.
denials_mailed_sep2023_both.          % both L-1A and change-of-status denials mailed to company address
petitioner_received_change_of_status_denial.
petitioner_claims_nonreceipt_L1A_denial.
company_address_history_delivery_problems.
usps_returned_receipt_may_and_aug_2023.
uscis_records_show_received_RFE_june_2023.
uscis_records_show_received_change_of_status_sep_2023.
uscis_record_shows_returned_mail_oct_2023.  % record indicates returned mail after L-1A decision (no copy in record)
petitioner_requested_decision_from_USCIS.
petitioner_on_appeal_requests_opportunity_to_review_and_respond.

% Governing rules (encoded as predicates)
uscis_must_explain_specific_reasons.
agency_may_send_by_regular_mail_to_last_known_address.
regular_mail_presumption_of_delivery.   % presumption applies when mailed properly by regular mail
regular_mail_presumption_is_weaker.     % weaker presumption than certified mail

% Evidence/presumption logic
% USCIS mailed denials to the address on the Form I-129 -> treat as properly addressed and mailed
properly_addressed_and_mailed :-
    denials_mailed_sep2023_both,
    agency_may_send_by_regular_mail_to_last_known_address.

% The regular-mail presumption of delivery applies if properly mailed
presumption_of_delivery_applies :-
    properly_addressed_and_mailed,
    regular_mail_presumption_of_delivery.

% Because it was regular mail, the presumption is weaker
presumption_is_weaker :-
    presumption_of_delivery_applies,
    regular_mail_presumption_is_weaker.

% Factors relevant to rebuttal of the weaker presumption
% 1) Petitioner affidavit or claim of nonreceipt
petitioner_affirms_nonreceipt :-
    petitioner_claims_nonreceipt_L1A_denial.

% 2) Independent evidence of delivery problems (returned mail history)
evidence_of_returned_mail :-
    usps_returned_receipt_may_and_aug_2023.
evidence_of_returned_mail :-
    uscis_record_shows_returned_mail_oct_2023.

% 3) Petitioner's actions upon learning (requested decision, sought redress, appealed)
petitioner_exercised_due_diligence :-
    petitioner_requested_decision_from_USCIS.
petitioner_exercised_due_diligence :-
    petitioner_on_appeal_requests_opportunity_to_review_and_respond.

% Consideration rule: all relevant evidence must be considered to determine whether
% the petitioner has rebutted the weaker presumption. We model rebuttal as present
% when there is at least (A) an affirmative claim of nonreceipt or (B) documentary
% evidence of delivery problems, together with some evidence of petitioner diligence.
% This captures the mixed record in the facts.
petitioner_rebutted_weaker_presumption :-
    presumption_is_weaker,                  % weaker presumption actually applies
    petitioner_exercised_due_diligence,     % petitioner took steps to seek redress
    (   petitioner_affirms_nonreceipt
    ;   evidence_of_returned_mail
    ).

% Eligibility rule: if the weaker presumption of delivery is rebutted, then the
% petitioner did not actually receive the L-1A denial notice for purposes of
% procedural fairness and should be afforded the requested opportunity (i.e., accept).
eligibility_met :-
    petitioner_rebutted_weaker_presumption.

% Entrypoint and output handling (must be last as specified)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed Form I-360 seeking immigrant classification as the spouse of a U.S. citizen and stated he had been married twice. He explained that his first marriage ended after the bride-price was returned to his family and that his former wife initially obtained a customary-court divorce in Lagos in 2017. She later sought a formal dissolution in the High Court because the customary court lacked subject-matter jurisdiction. To document the termination of that marriage, the petitioner submitted his first marriage certificate and multiple High Court divorce documents: a Petition for Decree of Dissolution filed in 2017, a Decree Nisi of Dissolution dated 2017 identified as “Form 35,” and a Certificate of Decree Absolute dated 2018 identified as “Form 41.” He also provided a divorce order from the High Court of Justice, Delta State (Judicial Division) dated 2018 entitled “Certificate of Decree Nisi Having Become Absolute.”

After the petition was denied, the petitioner appealed and submitted additional evidence and explanations. He provided a copy of the U.S. Department of State reciprocity schedule for Nigeria, extracts from the Nigerian Matrimonial Causes Rules and Matrimonial Causes Act, and an explanatory statement about the Nigerian divorce process showing how a Decree Nisi can become absolute. He also submitted a letter from the Assistant Chief Registrar of the High Court confirming the underlying suit number for his divorce and explaining that a typographical error in the suit number occurred when forms were typed at an outside business center. On appeal the petitioner explained the two different divorce dates (the customary-court action in 2017 and the High Court certificate in 2018), asserted that the additional documents demonstrate the legal termination of his first marriage, and argued that the submitted evidence addresses the issues raised in the denial.
statutes: A petitioner who is the spouse of a U.S. citizen may self-petition for immigrant classification if the petitioner married the U .S. citizen spouse in good faith, is eligible for immigrant classification based on the relationship, resided with the former spouse, was subjected to battery or extreme cruelty by the U.S . citizen spouse, and is a person of good moral character. Section 204(a)(l)(A)(iii) of the Act. Primary evidence of a marital relationship is a marriage certificate issued by civil authorities, and proof of the termination all prior marriages, if any, of the self-petitioner. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(ii). We consider any credible evidence relevant to the petition, and the determination of what evidence is credible, and the weight given such evidence lies within our sole discretion. Section 204(a)(l)(J) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.2( c )(2)(i).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text: The Petitioner is a barber and barber instructor who seeks EB-2 immigrant classification as an individual of exceptional ability together with a national interest waiver. The Director of the Texas Service Center denied the petition, and the matter is before the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal. The record includes a certificate stating that the Petitioner completed an 800-hour Traditional Barbering Course covering haircut, shaving, finishing, and care techniques, with coursework including theoretical concepts, practical concepts, and an internship; certificates showing attendance at multiple barbering workshops; recommendation letters from two barber colleagues and one barber client; an accountant's statement listing the company's monthly service revenues and billing for 2021; printouts from three salary-related websites showing the average salary of a barber in Brazil; and a brief article about the Petitioner's barber work and business together with advertisements for his barber instruction workshops. The decision describes the recommendation letters as not coming from current or former employers and as not providing employment dates, job duties, or whether the work was full time. It also describes the accountant's statement as reflecting company revenue rather than showing that the Petitioner personally received salary or remuneration for his services. The Petitioner states that he intends to work in the United States as the manager, barber, and barber instructor for a new barbershop and barber school business in south Florida. He states that the business would offer hair and beard services for men, hair cutting services for women and children, barber training courses, and sales of haircare, shaving, and cosmetic products. On appeal, the Petitioner argues that the Director applied law or policy incorrectly, implemented an incorrect policy without notice, and evaluated national importance too narrowly by focusing on geography and employment while disregarding other considerations. He further states that his proposed work would include serving as an instructor, teacher, mentor, and example for many people, and that the endeavor would improve societal welfare by creating vocational learning opportunities and by teaching underprivileged young people a trade that would provide security, dignity, and higher earning potential. He also argues that the endeavor would produce economic benefits and improve prospective students' quality of life through professional skills and economic stability while contributing economically to the United States. The record also includes articles submitted with the petition and on appeal, which the decision describes as not discussing projected U.S. economic impact, job creation, or societal welfare benefits specifically attributable to the Petitioner's proposed endeavor, and as not providing a sufficiently direct evidentiary tie between the proposed work and the claimed economic and societal welfare benefits.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. Exceptional ability means adegree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A petitioner must initially submit documentation that satisfies at least three of six categories of evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F).1 Meeting at least three criteria, however, does not, in and of itself, establish eligibility for this classification. 2 If a petitioner does so, we will then conduct a final merits determination to decide whether the evidence in its totality 1 If these types of evidence do not readily apply to the individual's occupation , a petitioner may submit comparable evidence to establish eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(iii). 2 USCIS has previously confirmed the applicability of this two-part adjudicative approach in the context of individuals of exceptional ability. See generally 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.5(8)(2), https: //www.uscis.gov/policy-manual.

shows that they are recognized as having a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the field. 3 Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying classification, the petitioner must then establish eligibility for a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that USCIS may, as matter of discretion,4 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts drawn from the record (0-arity predicates for true/false style facts)
petitioner_is_barber_and_instructor.
manager_barber_instructor_roles.
business_location_south_florida.
proposes_barbershop_and_barber_school.
services_offered.
plans_expansion_to_FL_TX_NY.
job_creation_7_in_5_years.
projected_annual_revenue_550k.
projected_net_profit_margin_15.
projected_wages_345k.
projected_payroll_taxes_over_50k.
vocational_training_for_underprivileged_and_unemployed_youth.
mentor_and_instructor_role.
director_denied.
petitioner_appealed.

% Evidence submitted
course_completion_800hr.
workshop_certificates_present.
letters_from_two_barber_colleagues_and_one_client.
accountant_revenue_statement_2021.
salary_printouts_brazil.
press_article_about_petitioner_and_business.
ads_for_instruction_workshops.
market_materials_personal_care_entrepreneurship_vocational_training.

% ---------- Eligibility under EB-2 (individual of exceptional ability) ----------
% Regulation requires at least three of six categories of evidence; meeting three alone does not
% establish eligibility — a final merits determination is required.
% We model that the petitioner submitted items that map to at least three categories:
%   - course completion / certificates (education / certification evidence)
%   - letters from peers/clients (testimonial / peer recognition)
%   - press article about petitioner (published material about the person)
meets_three_of_six_criteria :-
    course_completion_800hr,
    letters_from_two_barber_colleagues_and_one_client,
    press_article_about_petitioner_and_business.

% Final merits determination: must show "degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered."
% The record lacks evidence of national/international awards, original scholarly/technical contributions
% of major significance, or consistent high remuneration markedly above peers.
% Represented here as an explicit failure of the final merits determination.
final_merits_determination :-
    % Intentionally fail because the evidence in totality does not demonstrate expertise
    % significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the field.
    fail.

% Underlying EB-2 eligibility requires both meeting three criteria and passing the final merits determination.
underlying_eb2_eligible :-
    meets_three_of_six_criteria,
    final_merits_determination.

% ---------- Dhanasar (National Interest Waiver) prongs ----------
% Prong 1: substantial merit and national importance.
% Substantial merit: vocational training, job creation, and projected economic activity are evidence of merit.
substantial_merit :-
    vocational_training_for_underprivileged_and_unemployed_youth;
    job_creation_7_in_5_years;
    projected_annual_revenue_550k.

% National importance: to satisfy this, the endeavor must have impacts beyond a local or narrowly
% local scope or have clear implications for national initiatives. The director's reasoning and the
% record indicate the endeavor is primarily local and the broader impacts are speculative.
% We therefore model national_importance as not satisfied.
national_importance :-
    % Intentionally fail: the record does not demonstrate the requisite national-scale importance.
    fail.

% Combined prong predicate (must satisfy both)
substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    substantial_merit,
    national_importance.

% Prong 2: the individual is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor.
% The petitioner has relevant vocational credentials, a business plan, and intends to act as manager
% and instructor; this supports being well positioned.
well_positioned :-
    course_completion_800hr,
    manager_barber_instructor_roles,
    proposes_barbershop_and_barber_school.

% Prong 3: on balance, waiving the job offer would benefit the United States.
% This is a discretionary balancing test that depends on the strengths of prongs 1 and 2 and
% overall evidence of national benefit. Because national_importance (part of prong 1) is not met,
% the balancing of benefits will not support a waiver in this record.
on_balance_benefit :-
    substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned.

% National interest waiver eligibility requires all three prongs.
national_interest_waiver_eligible :-
    substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    on_balance_benefit.

% ---------- Final eligibility: both underlying EB-2 and NIW must be satisfied ----------
eligibility_met :-
    underlying_eb2_eligible,
    national_interest_waiver_eligible.

% Decision entrypoint and runner (provided by the instructions)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a company that designs and prints customized banners, stickers, and other signage. It filed an Immigrant Petition for Alien Worker (Form I-140) to sponsor the beneficiary for permanent employment in the United States as an operations manager under the EB-3 (skilled worker) classification. The accompanying labor certification specified permanent, full‑time employment as an operations manager, required two years of experience in management or operations in the printing industry, and listed an annual wage of $89,502.

USCIS initially approved the petition but the Texas Service Center Director later revoked that approval. The Director’s notice of intent to revoke (NOIR) and the subsequent revocation asserted several factual concerns: that the beneficiary appeared unaware of the duties of the proposed position (based on statements reported from his adjustment-of-status interview); that the petitioner’s president and the beneficiary knew each other before the labor certification filing and that the president had sold a business to the beneficiary about ten years earlier; and an alleged discrepancy about the number of applicants for the offered job (the president reportedly told a USCIS officer that ten people applied while the recruitment materials on file indicated no applicants). The NOIR also alleged the petitioner and beneficiary had misrepresented an answer to Question C.9 on the labor certification (the petitioner had answered “No” to whether the beneficiary had an ownership interest in or familial relationship to the employer).

The administrative record further includes reports that, at his October 2018 adjustment interview, the beneficiary described proposed duties as “advertising product, monitoring sales, and adjusting prices,” and when asked about duties such as recruiting, training, and evaluating employees, stated “I don’t know about that. I am not sure.” In a July 2020 telephone interview, the petitioner’s president reportedly described the beneficiary’s duties as “operating printing machinery and quality inspection.” At the adjustment interview the beneficiary reportedly indicated he did not speak English. The petitioner submitted evidence that it employed 27 people during the second quarter of 2022.

On appeal to the Administrative Appeals Office, the petitioner contends that the Director erred by: (1) basing the revocation on allegations not included in the NOIR, (2) failing to provide adequate notice of or a reasonable opportunity to rebut the revocation grounds, (3) improperly determining the company’s intent to employ the beneficiary in the offered job, and (4) relying on insufficient evidence to support allegations of willful misrepresentation.
statutes:
Immigration as a skilled worker generally follows a three-step process. First, a prospective employer must obtain certification from the U.S. Department of Labor (DOL) that: there are insufficient U.S. workers able, willing, qualified, and available for an offered job; and a noncitizen's permanent

employment in the job would not harm wages and working conditions ofU.S. workers with similar jobs. Section 212(a)(5)(A)(i) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1182(a)(5)(A)(i). Second, an employer must submit a DOL-approved labor certification with an immigrant visa petition to U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS). Section 204( a)(l )(F) ofthe Act. Among other things, USCIS determines whether a noncitizen beneficiary meets the requirements ofa DOL-certified position and a requested immigrant visa category. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(1)(3)(ii)(D), (4). Finally, if USCIS approves a petition, a beneficiary may apply for an immigrant visa abroad or, if eligible, adjustment of status in the United States. See section 245(a) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1255(a)."[A]t any time" before a beneficiary obtains permanent residence, USCIS may revoke a petition's approval for "good and sufficient cause." Section 205 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1155. If supported by a record, a petition's erroneous approval may justify its revocation. Matter ofHo, 19 I&N Dec. at 590. USCIS properly issues a NOIR if the unexplained and unrebutted record at the time of the notice's issuance would have warranted a petition's denial. Matter ofEstime, 19 I&N Dec. 450, 451 (BIA 1987). Ifa petitioner does not timely respond to a NOIR or the business's response does not overcome the stated revocation grounds, USCIS properly revokes a petition's approval. Id. at 451-52.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the record
petitioner_company.
position_operations_manager.
labor_cert_required_two_years_printing_mgmt.
prevailing_wage_89502.
noir_issued.
approval_revoked.
alleged_beneficiary_unaware_duties.
president_knew_beneficiary_prior.
sale_of_business_ten_years_ago.
recruitment_conflict.
lc_question_C9_answer_no.
ben_statements_simple_duties.
president_statement_diff_duties.
ben_reported_no_english.
employer_had_27_employees_q2_2022.
director_included_unnoticed_allegations.
petitioner_contested_revocation.

% Rules and legal inferences

% Material misrepresentation requires that an affirmative (and material) ownership or familial relationship
% existed that was not disclosed on the labor certification (Question C.9).
% The record contains an old sale of a business between the parties roughly ten years earlier,
% but no fact indicating a current ownership interest or familial relationship.
evidence_of_ownership_or_familial :-
    current_ownership_interest.
evidence_of_ownership_or_familial :-
    familial_relationship.

% Based on the record facts provided, there is no asserted current ownership or familial relationship.
% Thus evidence_of_ownership_or_familial will fail unless additional facts are present.

% A material misrepresentation for C.9 would require (1) an undisclosed ownership/familial relationship
% and (2) that the nondisclosure was material to eligibility. We only have the LC answer "No"
% and an historical sale; absence of current ownership/family facts means material misrepresentation not established.
evidence_of_material_misrepresentation :-
    lc_question_C9_answer_no,
    evidence_of_ownership_or_familial,
    material_to_eligibility.

% The NOIR and revocation process must give adequate notice of the grounds being relied upon.
% Where the Director relies on allegations not included in the NOIR (or the unexplained record at NOIR stage
% would not have supported denial), procedural error exists.
procedural_error :-
    director_included_unnoticed_allegations.

% USCIS may properly revoke an approval if the NOIR (or existing record) would have supported denial
% and the petitioner failed to timely and adequately rebut those grounds. We model this as noir_proper.
% For noir_proper to hold, the unexplained record at the time of NOIR issuance must have warranted denial.
% The record here contains disputed and unexplained factual allegations but also evidence (e.g., employment size)
% and the Director is alleged to have relied on additional, unnotified grounds; therefore we do not have
% a fact establishing that the unexplained record would have warranted denial.
noir_proper :-
    noir_issued,
    unexplained_record_would_have_warranted_denial.

% We do not assert unexplained_record_would_have_warranted_denial as a fact in the provided record,
% so noir_proper will fail unless that predicate is later supplied.

% On the substantive merits, USCIS must show by the record that the beneficiary does not meet the labor
% certification requirements or that the petition was erroneously approved. The record shows inconsistent
% statements about duties and the beneficiary's interview responses, but no conclusive corroboration that the
% beneficiary lacked the required experience or that the job would not have been permanent, full-time, etc.
% We therefore require explicit corroborating facts to conclude sufficient_evidence_for_revocation.
sufficient_evidence_for_revocation :-
    beneficiary_lacks_required_experience;
    beneficiary_unable_to_perform_duties.

% The record does not include clear facts establishing beneficiary_lacks_required_experience or
% beneficiary_unable_to_perform_duties; these predicates are not defined from the provided facts,
% so sufficient_evidence_for_revocation will fail under the current record.

% Insufficient evidence for revocation is the negation of sufficient evidence combined with absence of
% material misrepresentation.
insufficient_evidence_for_revocation :-
    \+ sufficient_evidence_for_revocation,
    \+ evidence_of_material_misrepresentation.

% Eligibility: if the petition was revoked but the revocation suffered procedural error
% or there was insufficient evidence to support revocation, the approval should be maintained.
eligibility_met :-
    noir_issued,
    approval_revoked,
    (   procedural_error
    ;   insufficient_evidence_for_revocation
    ).

% Entrypoint and decision/reporting structure required by the task
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is an advanced-degree professional who filed an I-140 immigrant petition in the EB-2 category and requested a national interest waiver. He states that he intends to continue his career in the United States as a CEO and entrepreneur by creating a Florida-based company to develop and operate a digital learning and instruction platform focused on providing courses and training for pharmaceutical sales professionals, with particular emphasis on ophthalmology-related products. He described delivering training both in classroom settings and online, and offering sales coaching and support services to U.S.-based international trade companies to help them expand sales channels, reduce costs, increase profits, and reach international markets.

In support of his petition, the petitioner submitted a business plan that included industry and market analyses, descriptions of the company and its services, information about his education and work experience, personnel descriptions, marketing strategies, and financial forecasts. The plan projected revenue of $442,000 in year one, $758,100 in year two, $858,005 in year three, $1,060,905 in year four, and $1,193,951 in year five. It also projected staffing growth to 12 employees in year one, 20 in year two, 22 in year three, 26 in year four, and 28 in year five. He provided letters of support from several named individuals and an expert opinion letter from Dr. V‑L‑, an associate professor of marketing, attesting to the value of his proposed work. He also submitted various articles and materials about immigrant entrepreneurship, small-business trends, industry outlooks, workforce issues, and related topics.

After receiving a request for evidence, the petitioner further explained that his company would coach sales teams, develop commercial chains for American products, and deliver training and support services aimed at maximizing client profits and generating revenues for the U.S. economy while creating jobs for American workers. On appeal, the petitioner argued that his proposed digital learning platform and related business activities would address an industry shortage of qualified personnel, would have considerable impact on the industry by improving the skills of pharmaceutical sales professionals, and would generate job growth (projecting 28 direct jobs by year five) and economic benefits for small and medium-sized U.S. companies. The petitioner filed the appeal after the Texas Service Center denied his petition.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. If a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec . 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: 1 See also Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the N inth, Eleventh, and D .C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver to be discretionary in nature).

• The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts extracted from the record
filed_i140.
eb2_qualified.                    % Director determined petitioner qualified for advanced-degree EB-2
business_plan_submitted.          % Business plan included industry/market analyses, personnel, forecasts
letters_of_support.               % Multiple letters attesting to business capabilities and pharma experience
expert_opinion_letter.            % Academic expert letter asserting potential economic contribution
relevant_experience.              % Petitioner described CEO/entrepreneur role and pharma sales experience
online_delivery_possible.         % Platform to be delivered both in classrooms and online
projected_employees_year1(12).
projected_employees_year5(28).
revenue_year1(442000).
revenue_year5(1193951).
no_existing_us_market_contracts.  % Record lacks executed national contracts or demonstrated national customers
limited_scale_evidence.           % Projections and evidence are modest in scale

% Dhanasar framework implemented as Prolog predicates (arity 0 for yes/no predicates)

% Prong 1: substantial merit and national importance
% Substantial merit: training and workforce development and commercial/educational endeavor have intrinsic merit
substantial_merit :-
    business_plan_submitted,
    (   online_delivery_possible
    ;   relevant_experience
    ).

% National importance: requires evidence of impact beyond a local area or demonstrable national scale.
% Here, national importance is modeled to require either demonstrable national scale (contracts, large workforce impact),
% or otherwise specific evidence showing national reach. The record lacks such evidence.
has_demonstrable_national_scale :-
    projected_employees_year5(N),
    N >= 100.                         % threshold for demonstrable national scale in this simplified model

% alternatively, demonstrable national reach could be shown by contracts or documented national customers
has_national_contracts :-
    \+ no_existing_us_market_contracts.  % true only if record included contracts (it does not)

national_importance :-
    substantial_merit,
    (   has_demonstrable_national_scale
    ;   has_national_contracts
    ).

% Prong 2: well-positioned to advance the endeavor
well_positioned :-
    eb2_qualified,
    business_plan_submitted,
    relevant_experience,
    letters_of_support,
    expert_opinion_letter.

% Prong 3: on balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the U.S.
% Modeled to require projected job creation or significant revenue/economic impact
on_balance_benefit :-
    projected_employees_year5(N),
    N >= 50.                            % simplified threshold for meaningful job creation
on_balance_benefit :-
    revenue_year5(R),
    R >= 5000000.                       % or a significant revenue threshold

% eligibility_met requires EB-2 qualification and satisfaction of all three Dhanasar prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_qualified,
    substantial_merit,
    national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    on_balance_benefit.

% Decision entrypoint and run handling (provided structure)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed Form I-360 in 2023 when he was 20 years old. The National Benefits Center director denied the petition after determining that the state-court orders (Letters of Guardianship and Special Immigrant Juvenile Findings, dated in 2023, and an Order Appointing Guardian dated the day after the petitioner’s twenty-first birthday in 2023) were issued after the petitioner turned 21 and therefore did not show the court had exercised jurisdiction over him as a juvenile on the dates those orders were signed.

On appeal, the petitioner submitted juvenile-court Minute Orders reflecting that a hearing was conducted on 15, 2023, when he was 20 years old. The Minute Orders show the court granted the petitioner’s mother’s petition for guardianship and stated that sufficient evidence had been presented to grant the matter based on the moving papers and evidence. The Minute Orders also state that reunification with one or both parents was not viable due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under California law, and that it was not in the petitioner’s best interest to be returned to his parents’ country of nationality.

The petitioner also provided Special Immigrant Juvenile Findings indicating a hearing was conducted on 15, 2023, and that the child was placed under the custody of the petitioner’s mother on that date. Those findings state that the petitioner could not be reunified with his father because the father abused, neglected, and abandoned him, and that it was not in the ward’s best interest to return to Mexico, the petitioner’s and his parents’ country of nationality or last habitual residence. The Order Appointing Guardian likewise records that a hearing occurred on 15, 2023 and that the court placed the petitioner in his mother’s custody on that date.

The petitioner’s stated reasons for appealing were that the requisite juvenile-court determinations were made at the hearing on 15, 2023 when he was 20, that the subsequent formal orders were issued after his 21st birthday due to delay beyond his control, and that the Minute Orders and related findings therefore demonstrate the court exercised jurisdiction and made the necessary determinations while he was still a juvenile.
statutes: To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b). Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.11 ( c )(1 ). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(2). A juvenile court is a court located in the United States with jurisdiction under state law to make judicial determinations about the dependency and/or custody and care ofjuveniles. 8 C.F.R . § 204.1 l(a). The juvenile court must have exercised its authority over the petitioner as a juvenile and made the requisite judicial determinations under applicable State law to establish eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(3)(i)
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the case
unmarried.
filed_i360_in_2023_when_20.
hearing_occurred_on_2023_while_20.    % Minute Orders show hearing occurred while petitioner was 20
letters_guardianship_and_sij_findings_dated_2023.
order_appointing_guardian_formally_dated_after_21.  % Formal Order dated after petitioner's 21st birthday
director_denied_due_to_orders_issued_after_21.
minute_orders_show_judicial_determinations_at_hearing. % Minute Orders reflect that juvenile court made SIJ determinations at the hearing
subsequent_formal_orders_delayed_beyond_control. % Petitioner asserts formal issuance delay was beyond his control
court_granted_mothers_guardianship_and_sij_findings_at_hearing.
finding_reunification_not_viable.    % Court found reunification with one or both parents not viable
finding_not_in_best_interest_to_return. % Court found it was not in petitioner's best interest to be returned
sij_findings_and_order_state_hearing_date_and_placement_in_mother_custody. % Formal documents record hearing date and placement
juvenile_court_has_jurisdiction.    % Juvenile court is a court located in the US with jurisdiction under state law
juvenile_court_exercised_authority_over_petitioner. % Court exercised authority over petitioner as a juvenile

% Logical deductions / intermediate predicates
% The relevant judicial determinations were made at the hearing before the petitioner turned 21.
judicial_determinations_made_before_21 :-
    minute_orders_show_judicial_determinations_at_hearing,
    hearing_occurred_on_2023_while_20.

% A delayed formal issuance of orders does not defeat eligibility if the required judicial determinations were actually made while the petitioner was under 21 and the delay was beyond the petitioner's control.
delayed_formal_orders_do_not_defeat_eligibility :-
    judicial_determinations_made_before_21,
    subsequent_formal_orders_delayed_beyond_control.

% The juvenile court made the required determinations for SIJ if it:
%  - determined reunification was not viable,
%  - determined it was not in the petitioner's best interest to return,
%  - placed the petitioner in the custody of a parent/state-appointed guardian or otherwise declared dependency,
%  - and exercised jurisdiction/authority over the petitioner as a juvenile.
juvenile_court_made_required_sij_determinations :-
    finding_reunification_not_viable,
    finding_not_in_best_interest_to_return,
    (   court_granted_mothers_guardianship_and_sij_findings_at_hearing
    ;   sij_findings_and_order_state_hearing_date_and_placement_in_mother_custody
    ),
    juvenile_court_has_jurisdiction,
    juvenile_court_exercised_authority_over_petitioner.

% The petitioner was under 21 at the time the court made the required determinations if either:
%  - the formal order was issued before 21, or
%  - the judicial determinations were made at a hearing before 21 (and any formal issuance delay was beyond his control).
under21_at_time_of_judicial_determination :-
    hearing_occurred_on_2023_while_20,
    judicial_determinations_made_before_21.
under21_at_time_of_judicial_determination :-
    order_appointing_guardian_formally_dated_after_21,
    judicial_determinations_made_before_21,
    subsequent_formal_orders_delayed_beyond_control.
```
