text:
The petitioner is an entrepreneur working in the information technology field who filed Form I-140 seeking EB-2 immigrant classification as an advanced-degree professional and a national interest waiver of the job offer requirement. At filing, he described proposed work in the United States as a technology consultant in electronics and wireless communications. In response to a request for evidence, he described himself as an entrepreneur in information technology, stating he intends to serve as chief executive officer of a U.S. company to be located in Colorado. He described that company as providing services such as software and firmware updates for smartphones, tablets, laptops, and smart home devices, and offering engraving on such devices.

On appeal, the petitioner describes establishing and operating a U.S. software-update company and also indicates work in the field of transportation. He states that his company plans to hire 122 employees over the next five years and asserts that each direct job created in the computer systems design and related services industry will generate additional indirect jobs and increased tax revenues at both federal and state levels. He also asserts that his company will provide information technology services and training to other professionals, addressing shortages of STEM talent, and that its services—such as providing software and firmware updates and introducing new technologies—will expand economic opportunity, improve business efficiency, and contribute to national security, the economy, and public health and safety by strengthening resilience against cyber threats and maintaining IT infrastructure.

The petitioner appealed the denial of his petition and, on appeal, maintains that his proposed endeavor has national importance, that he is well positioned to advance it, and that, on balance, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the job-offer and labor-certification requirements.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act."Advanced degree" means any U.S. academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of baccalaureate. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A U.S. baccalaureate degree or a foreign equivalent degree followed by five years ofprogressive experience in the specialty shall be considered the equivalent of a master's degree. Id.

"Profession" means one of the occupations listed in section 101(a)(32) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § l 10l(a)(32), 1 as well as any occupation for which a U.S. baccalaureate degree or its foreign equivalent is the minimum requirement for entry into the occupation. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or an individual of exceptional ability, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCTS) may, as a matter of discretion,2 grant a national interest waiver ifthe petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id. The first prong, substantial merit and national importance, focuses on the specific endeavor that the individual proposes to undertake. The endeavor's merit may be demonstrated in a range ofareas, such as business, entrepreneurialism, science, technology, culture, health, or education. In determining whether the proposed endeavor has national importance, we consider its potential prospective impact. Id. The second prong shifts the focus from the proposed endeavor to the individual. To determine whether they are well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor, we consider factors including, but not limited to: their education, skills, knowledge, and record of success in related or similar efforts; a model or plan for future activities; any progress towards achieving the proposed endeavor; and the interest of potential customers, users, investors, or other relevant entities or individuals. Id. at 890. The third prong requires a petitioner to demonstrate that, on balance, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer, and thus of a labor certification. In performing this analysis, we may evaluate factors such as: whether, in light of the nature of the individual's qualifications or the proposed endeavor, it would be impractical either for them to secure a job offer or to obtain a labor certification; whether, even assuming that other qualified U.S. workers are available, the United States would still benefit from their contributions; and whether the national interest in their contributions is sufficiently urgent to warrant forgoing the labor certification process. In each case, the factors considered must, taken together, establish that, on balance, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements ofa job offer, and thus ofa labor certification. Id. at 890-91. 1 Profession shall include, but not be limited to, architects, engineers, lawyers, physicians, surgeons, and teachers in elementary or secondary schools, colleges, academics, or seminaries. Section 10l(a)(32) of the Act. 2 See also Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver is discretionary in nature). 2
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
        proposed_hires_122
    )
    ;
    claims_protect_against_cyber_threats.

beneficial_on_balance :-
    benefit_from_job_offer_waiver.

% Final eligibility: must qualify for EB-2 and meet all three Dhanasar prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    eligibility_eb2,
    prong1,
    well_positioned,
    beneficial_on_balance.

% Decision structure (entry point must be last as requested)

    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

        ),

    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',

    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',

    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a Venezuelan national who filed Form I-821 to request Temporary Protected Status (TPS). In his initial submission he provided an affidavit and tax documentation, and a coworker also submitted an affidavit on his behalf. The Texas Service Center issued a request for evidence (RFE); in response the petitioner submitted additional tax documentation and a letter from a tax preparer. The Director denied the petition, concluding the evidence on file was insufficient to establish that the petitioner had the required continuous residence and physical presence.

The petitioner appealed that denial. On appeal he submitted further documentation intended to show his presence and residence in the United States, including an assignment of an individual taxpayer identification number (ITIN), a November 2023 notice from the Internal Revenue Service, copies of cashed checks payable to him from March through July 2023, and a request for direct deposit to his bank. The stated reason for the appeal is to challenge the denial and to demonstrate that he meets the continuous residence and continuous physical presence requirements asserted by the Service as the basis for the denial.
statutes:
To be eligible for TPS, a national of a foreign state designated by the Secretary of Homeland Security must establish, in part that they have been continuously physically present in the United States since the effective date of the most recent designation of that foreign state for TPS and have continuously resided in the United States since a date designated by the Secretary of Homeland Security. 8 C.F.R. § 244.2. Individuals applying for TPS offered to Venezuelans (and persons without nationality who last habitually resided in Venezuela) must demonstrate that they have been continuously residing in the 1 See Extension and Redesignation ofVenezuela for Temporary Protected Status, 88 Fed. Reg . 68130 (Oct. 3, 2023).

United States since July 31, 2023, and have been continuously physically present in the United States since October 3, 2023. Continuously resided means "residing in the United States for the entire period specified in the regulations," but an applicant shall not be considered to have failed to maintain continuous residence"because of a brief, casual and innocent absence ... or due merely to a brief temporary trip abroad required by emergency or extenuating circumstances outside the control of the [applicant]." 8 C.F.R. § 244.1. Continuously physically present means "actual physical presence in the United States for the entire period specified in the regulations," but an applicant shall not be considered to have failed to maintain continuous physical presence because of "brief, casual, and innocent absences." Id. The burden ofproof is on the Applicant to demonstrate eligibility for the requested immigration benefit by a preponderance of the evidence. Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. 369,376 (AAO 2010). The sufficiency of all evidence will be judged according to its relevancy, consistency, credibility, and probative value. 8 C.F.R. § 244.9(b). To meet her burden of proof, the Applicant must provide supporting documentary evidence of eligibility apart from her own statements. Id.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text: The Applicant is a native and citizen of Honduras who held U-1 nonimmigrant status from May 2017 until May 2021. She timely filed Form I-485 in April 2021 to adjust status based on that U classification. The Vermont Service Center issued a request for evidence asking specifically for a Honduran birth certificate in the form of a Copia de Folio del Libro de Nacimientos (Inscripción de Nacimiento). In response, the Applicant submitted a Honduran birth document titled Certificación de acta de Nacimiento. The service center denied the I-485 on the ground that she had not submitted the requested Copia de Folio/Inscripción de Nacimiento and that her Certificación de acta de Nacimiento did not comply with the Department of State Reciprocity Schedule for Honduras. On appeal, the Applicant filed a brief and provided new evidence, including a copy of an Inscripción de Nacimiento with an English translation and numerous other documents related to her identity, continuous physical presence, and discretion. She appealed to challenge the denial based on the asserted insufficiency of her birth-certificate evidence and to have the newly submitted documentation considered.
statutes: U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may adjust the status of a U nonimmigrant to that of an LPR if, among other requirements, they have been physically present in the United States for a continuous period of three years since the date of their admission as a U nonimmigrant, and they establish that their continued presence in the United States is justified on humanitarian grounds, to ensure family unity, or is otherwise in the public interest. Section 245(m) of the Act. The Applicant's burden includes complying with and submitting the documentation described in the U adjustment application's instructions. See 8 C.F.R . § 103.2(a)(l) (stating that every form, benefit request, or other document must be submitted and executed in accordance with form instructions which are incorporated into the regulations requiring its submission). U adjustment application instructions require applicants to submit a photocopy oftheir birth certificate issued by the appropriate civil authority from the country of their birth.

```prolog
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a hockey referee from Belarus who has officiated games at both national and international levels and holds an international referee license. He states that he intends to continue working as a hockey referee in the United States and to establish a hockey school for children.

In support of his petition and appeal, he submitted documentary and testimonial evidence including: membership in the Ice Hockey Federation of the Republic of Belarus (IHFRB); a claim that he is “a member of Belarus national ice hockey team of judges and referees”; an article on belarushockey.com naming him among referees selected to work at youth world championship tournaments; individual game reports listing officiating crews in which he appears; sections of the IHFRB rule book and excerpts from Belarusian sports statutes; a “Letter of Gratitude” for the 2014/2015 championship season; and multiple reference letters from colleagues and supervisors (including letters from individuals identified as V‑R‑, U‑R‑, M‑S‑, and V‑K‑). The record contains two letters from V‑R‑ (an IHFRB official) describing the steps to becoming an internationally licensed referee and stating that such progression requires endorsement by the national federation. One of V‑R.’s letters describes the petitioner’s duties as a referee (rule enforcement, game management, penalty assessment, and submission of post-game reports). A separate letter from V‑R‑ states that the petitioner served as an “inspector (supervisor) of judges on ice” during the 2020/2021 and 2021/2022 seasons and describes duties in that role—observing referees, evaluating their skating skills and physical fitness, assessing knowledge and application of hockey rules, and providing feedback during games. Several reference letters describe the petitioner’s officiating and note that he has trained or mentored new referees. The petitioner also referenced unpublished agency decisions and other written materials in support of his arguments. He did not provide additional documentation about the IHFRB’s membership requirements in response to the request for evidence he received.

The petitioner appealed the underlying adjudication because he contends, based on the submissions described above, that he meets additional evidentiary criteria beyond those noted by the adjudicating office. On appeal he reasserts his IHFRB membership and his claimed membership on a national team of judges/referees; emphasizes his international referee license and selection for international tournaments; describes his work as an inspector/supervisor of other referees and his post‑game reporting and mentoring activities; cites the reference letters and other documents as evidence of original contributions and recognition; and argues that certain types of submitted evidence (including comparable‑evidence arguments and prior agency decisions he cited) support his eligibility.
statutes:
To establish eligibility as an individual of extraordinary ability, a petitioner (or anyone on the petitioner's behalf) must establish that they: • Have extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics; • Seek to enter the United States to continue work in their area of extraordinary ability; and that • Their entry into the United States will prospectively substantially benefit the United States. Extraordinary ability must be demonstrated by evidence of sustained national or international acclaim as well as extensive documentation that their achievements have been recognized in the field. Section 203(b)(l) of the Act.

The implementing regulation further states that the term "extraordinary ability" refers only to those individuals in "that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." It also sets forth a multi-part analysis. A petitioner can demonstrate international recognition of their achievements in the field through a one-time achievement (that is, a major, internationally recognized award). If such evidence is unavailable, then they must alternatively provide evidence that meets at least three ofthe ten listed criteria, which call for evidence about other awards they may have received, published material about them in qualifying media, and their authorship of scholarly articles, among other types of evidence. 8 C.F.R. §§ 204.5(h)(2),(3). Where a petitioner meets these initial evidence requirements, we then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination, assessing whether the record shows that the individual possesses the acclaim and recognition required for this highly exclusive immigrant visa classification. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 20 l0) ( discussing a two-part review where the documentation is first counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination); see also Visinscaia v. Beers, 4 F. Supp. 3d 126, 131-32 (D.D.C. 2013); Rijalv. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the administrative record (each 0-arity predicate denotes that the fact is present)
has_international_referee_license.
officiated_international_events.    % includes youth world championship tournaments and Olympic qualifying tournament
officiated_national_competitions.
served_as_inspector.               % served as "inspector (supervisor) of judges on ice" 2020/2021 & 2021/2022
membership_IHFRB.                  % membership evidence for Ice Hockey Federation of the Republic of Belarus
member_of_belarus_national_referees_team.
multiple_reference_letters.        % letters from colleagues, acquaintances, employers praising work
letter_of_gratitude_2014_2015.     % acknowledgement of a "Letter of Gratitude" for 2014/2015 season
plans_to_continue_refereeing_in_US. % petitioner states intent to continue working as a referee in the US
plans_to_establish_hockey_school_in_US.
appealed_additional_criteria_claims. % petitioner asserted meeting additional criteria on appeal

% Regulatory/methodology facts (expressed when present in record)
% Note: absences of these predicates imply lack of persuasive evidence for the corresponding regulatory requirement.
major_international_award_noted :- fail.  % no one-time major internationally recognized award shown

% The regulation's ten evidentiary criteria are modeled below. We assert only those criteria supported by the record.
% Criterion 1: Lesser nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards
criterion_1_prize_or_award :- false.  % only a "Letter of Gratitude" exists, insufficient to establish this criterion

% Criterion 2: Membership in associations that require outstanding achievement
% Record shows membership, but no persuasive evidence that the association's membership requires outstanding achievement.
criterion_2_membership :- false.

% Criterion 3: Published material about the person in professional or major media
criterion_3_published_material :- false.  % no independent published articles in qualifying media shown

% Criterion 4: Participation as a judge of the work of others in the field
criterion_4_judge_of_others :-
    served_as_inspector.  % inspector duties included observing/evaluating referees (matches criterion)

% Criterion 5: Original scientific, scholarly, artistic, athletic, or business contributions of major significance
criterion_5_original_contributions :- false.  % mentoring/training noted but no evidence of original contributions of major significance

% Criterion 6: Authorship of scholarly articles in professional journals or other major media
criterion_6_authorship :- false.

% Criterion 7: Display of work at artistic exhibitions (not applicable to athletics)
criterion_7_display_work :- false.

% Criterion 8: Leading or critical role for organizations or establishments that have a distinguished reputation
% The record shows roles (inspector, member of national team) but lacks persuasive evidence that these are leading/critical roles for a widely distinguished organization.
criterion_8_leading_role :- false.

% Criterion 9: High salary or other significantly high remuneration in relation to others in the field
criterion_9_high_remuneration :- false.

% Criterion 10: Commercial successes in performing arts (not applicable)
criterion_10_commercial_success :- false.

% Map criterion predicates into a single predicate for counting
criterion_met(N) :-
    N = 4,
    criterion_4_judge_of_others.

% Count how many of the regulatory criteria are met
criteria_count(Count) :-
    findall(N, criterion_met(N), L),
    length(L, Count).

meets_three_of_ten_criteria :-
    criteria_count(Count),
    Count >= 3.

% Additional statutory/regulatory requirements
intends_to_continue_work_in_field :-
    plans_to_continue_refereeing_in_US.

% Prospective substantial benefit to the United States (record must show evidence)
% The record contains asserted plans but no persuasive evidence that entry will prospectively substantially benefit the U.S.
prospective_substantial_benefit :- false.

% Sustained national or international acclaim / final merits determination
% The final merits require evidence the individual is among the very top in the field. The record does not show that level of acclaim.
sustained_national_or_international_acclaim :- false.

% Major award alternative (one-time internationally recognized award)
major_award_present :- false.

% eligibility_met is true only if the petitioner either has a qualifying major award
% or meets at least three of the ten regulatory criteria and satisfies the additional statutory requirements.
eligibility_met :-
    (   major_award_present
    ;
        meets_three_of_ten_criteria,
        intends_to_continue_work_in_field,
        prospective_substantial_benefit,
        sustained_national_or_international_acclaim
    ).

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The Petitioner is a non‑denominational church that filed Form I-360 to classify the Beneficiary as a special immigrant religious worker to serve as a staff minister. The petition was initially denied by the California Service Center for reasons including how the Petitioner intended to compensate the Beneficiary, whether the offered position qualified as a religious occupation or minister, and whether the Beneficiary met ministerial qualifications. The Petitioner appealed that decision and the case was remanded; on remand the Petitioner provided evidence regarding compensation but the Director again denied the petition, concluding the Beneficiary was not working solely as a minister. The Petitioner has appealed that second denial.

On appeal the Petitioner argues that the Beneficiary’s ownership of a coffee shop does not constitute secular employment and therefore should not disqualify him from classification as a minister. The Petitioner does not dispute that the Beneficiary owns the coffee shop in addition to working full time for the church; its position is that the Beneficiary’s role is passive ownership and that he does not provide services or labor to the business because day‑to‑day operations are handled by an Operations Manager. To support this, the Petitioner submitted the Beneficiary’s tax returns listing his occupation as a business owner and showing earnings from the business from 2016 to 2021; an affidavit from the Beneficiary describing that he established the business with personal funds brought from Australia and that he receives passive income from his 100% ownership of a business that operates two café locations and employs about 20 people; screenshots of the café’s group messages intended to show he does not participate in daily operations; a letter from the church’s senior pastor stating the Beneficiary’s interaction with the business is strictly as an owner; a letter from the president of a related organization acknowledging the Beneficiary’s rare physical presence at the shop and that management has been handled by staff and managers; and an organizational chart showing the Beneficiary as sole owner. The Petitioner also cited a regulatory definition of “employment” in support of its contention that passive ownership does not amount to employment. The stated reason for the appeal is to challenge the Director’s finding that the Beneficiary’s ownership and income from the coffee shop constitute secular employment that prevents him from qualifying as a minister.
statutes:
Non-profit religious organizations may petition for foreign nationals to immigrate to the United States to perform full-time, compensated religious work as ministers, in religious vocations, or in other religious occupations. The petitioning organizations must establish that the foreign national beneficiary meets certain eligibility criteria, including membership in a religious denomination and continuous religious work experience for at least the two-year period before the petition filing date.

See generally section 203(b)(4) of the Act (providing classification to qualified special immigrant religious workers as described in section 101(a)(27)(C) of the Act). The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(m)(2) states that to be eligible for classification as a special immigrant religious worker, a noncitizen must be coming to the United States to work in a full time ( average of at least 35 hours per week) compensated position in one of the following occupations: (i) Solely in the vocation of a minister of that religious denomination; (ii) A religious vocation either in a professional or nonprofessional capacity; or (iii) A religious occupation either in a professional or nonprofessional capacity. The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(m)(5) defines a minister as an individual who: (A) Is fully authorized by a religious denomination, and fully trained according to the denomination's standards, to conduct such religious worship and perform other duties usually performed by authorized members of the clergy of that denomination; (B) Is not a lay preacher or a person not authorized to perform duties usually performed by clergy; (C) Performs activities with a rational relationship to the religious calling of the minister; and (D) Works solely as a mm1ster in the United States, which may include administrative duties incidental to the duties of a minister.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the case
petitioner_nonprofit_religious_organization.
filed_I360.
position_staff_minister.

% Administrative history facts
director_initial_denial_due_to_compensation_and_minister_issues.
remand_compensation_established.
director_requested_minister_qualification_and_secular_employment_clarification.
director_concluded_not_solely_minister.

% Beneficiary / business facts and petitioner evidence
beneficiary_owns_coffee_shop.
petitioner_claims_ownership_passive.
evidence_group_messages_provided.
evidence_letter_senior_pastor_provided.
evidence_letter_president_provided.
evidence_org_chart_provided.

% Evidence (or absence) relevant to statutory/regulatory elements:
% The petitioner established intent to compensate on remand, but no evidence
% of full-time hours (>=35) was submitted in the record provided here.
remand_compensation_established.
% No explicit evidence of full-time hours in the supplied facts:
evidence_of_full_time_hours :- fail.

% No explicit record facts supplied here that the beneficiary is fully authorized
% or fully trained under the denomination's standards, or that he satisfies the
% membership and two-year continuous experience requirement. Those predicates
% are defined to fail so they are available for calls but return false.
authorized_by_denomination :- fail.
fully_trained_according_to_denomination :- fail.
not_a_lay_preacher :- fail.
activities_have_rational_relationship_to_ministry :- fail.
membership_and_two_years_experience :- fail.

% The petitioner's proof regarding passive ownership could support a finding that
% the business does not amount to secular employment, but the director explicitly
% concluded the beneficiary was not working solely as a minister. The rule below
% models the petitioner's position (works solely if passive-ownership evidence is
% present and no contrary director conclusion). Because director_concluded_not_solely_minister
% is true in the facts, the clause for works_solely_as_minister will fail.
works_solely_as_minister :-
    petitioner_claims_ownership_passive,
    evidence_group_messages_provided,
    evidence_letter_senior_pastor_provided,
    evidence_letter_president_provided,
    evidence_org_chart_provided,
    \+ director_concluded_not_solely_minister.

% Full-time compensated position requirement: must be compensated and full-time.
% Compensation intent was established on remand, but full-time hours were not shown
% in the supplied facts, so compensated_full_time will fail given the available facts.
compensation_intent_established :-
    remand_compensation_established.

compensated_full_time :-
    compensation_intent_established,
    evidence_of_full_time_hours.

% Minister definition requires multiple subcriteria per 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(m)(5).
meets_minister_definition :-
    authorized_by_denomination,
    fully_trained_according_to_denomination,
    not_a_lay_preacher,
    activities_have_rational_relationship_to_ministry,
    works_solely_as_minister.

% eligibility_met must be true for an Accepted decision. All required elements are
% combined here. Given the facts provided (notably the director's conclusion and
% lack of proof of authorization/training/membership/experience and full-time hours),
% this conjunction will fail and the program will output 'Dismissed'.
eligibility_met :-
    petitioner_nonprofit_religious_organization,
    filed_I360,
    position_staff_minister,
    compensated_full_time,
    meets_minister_definition,
    membership_and_two_years_experience.

decision(Result) :-

    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-

        ),

        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-

        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is an actor who filed Form I-140 seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability. The Nebraska Service Center denied the petition, and the petitioner appealed that denial.

On appeal the petitioner maintained that he met additional evidentiary criteria and sought reversal of the denial. He did not contest his previously claimed eligibility under the judging criterion and instead asserted qualification for five additional criteria. He also submitted new evidence and a supplemental brief on appeal (including a new claim based on commercial work).

Facts and evidence the petitioner submitted or relied on in the appeal:
- Membership: He reported “Partner” membership in the National Association of Interpreters (ANDI) and submitted ANDI’s corporate bylaws as support for that membership level.
- Explanations of terms: He referenced dictionary definitions (Merriam‑Webster) for terms such as “quality” and “royalties” in support of his interpretation of ANDI’s membership requirements.
- Published material: He submitted 16 articles and other online items that he identified as published material about his work, and he pointed to two items (on 27prmedia.com and examiner.com) as containing the required title, date, and author. He also provided website traffic and ranking metrics for those online publications.
- Compensation and remuneration: He submitted documents concerning his earnings, including an agent statement describing his earnings as fees, commissions, and other project-based compensation (not traditional hourly wages), and an “Artistic Performance Agreement” describing project-based hiring and services. He provided multiple “VO Release” documents showing flat project fees (for example, a project listing a $500 session fee and a $1,000 usage fee) and also cited industry rate information (voiceover rates he characterized as ranging from about $62 to $1,000 per hour, with $500 per hour being common) from sources such as ZipRecruiter, PayScale, and BLS.
- Tax and income records: He submitted IRS Forms W-2 showing annual earnings of $2,200 (2017), $7,800 (2018), and $19,600 (2019), and additional documents indicating earnings of $1,275 (2020) and $1,125 (2021).
- Commercial/box‑office evidence: He identified film and video projects in which he participated and provided box‑office and video sales figures for those projects. The record includes gross figures he submitted for several titles (examples cited in his filings include amounts such as $1.49M for one project and other projects with reported grosses and video sales figures in the tens or hundreds of thousands of dollars). He also cited specific film releases in which he had roles (including references to The Angriest Man in Brooklyn (2014) and Hank and Asha (2013) and associated gross figures).
- Prior nonimmigrant classification: He had previously been granted O‑1 nonimmigrant status (a classification for individuals of extraordinary ability) in earlier petitions filed on his behalf.

Reason for the appeal:
- The petitioner appealed to challenge the denial of his Form I-140 and to argue that, based on the memberships, publications, compensation evidence, commercial receipts, project agreements, tax records, and other documentation he submitted, he satisfies additional evidentiary criteria required for the sought classification.
statutes:
Section 203(b)(l)(A) of the Act make s visas available to immigrants with extraordinary ability if: (i) the alien has extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics which has been demonstrated by sustained national or international acclaim and whose achievements have been recognized in the field through extensive documentation, (ii) the alien seeks to enter the United States to continue work in the area of extraordinary ability, and (iii) the alien's entry into the United States will substantially benefit prospectively the United States. The term "extraordinary ability" refers only to those individuals in "that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2). The implementing regulation

at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) sets forth a multi-part analysis. First, a petitioner can demonstrate sustained acclaim and the recognition of achievements in the field through a one-time achievement (that is, a major, internationally recognized award) or qualifying documentation that meets at least three of the ten categories listed at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i) - (x) (including items such as awards, published material in certain media, and scholarly articles). Where a petitioner meets these initial evidence requirements, we then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination and assess whether the record shows sustained national or international acclaim and demonstrates that the individual is among the small percentage at the very top of the field of endeavor. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 2010) ( discussing a two-part review where the documentation is first counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination); see also Visinscaia v. Beers, 4 F. Supp. 3d 126, 131-32 (D.D.C. 2013); Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts extracted from the record (arity-0 boolean-style facts)
occupation_actor.
past_nonimmigrant_o1_status.
filed_i140_eb1.
membership_andi_partner.
andi_bylaws_describe_membership_requirements.
published_items_16.
published_sources_varied_online_and_print.
compensation_flat_fees.
agent_statement_self_employed.
w2_2017_2200.
w2_2018_7800.
w2_2019_19600.
other_earnings_2020_1275.
other_earnings_2021_1125.

% Regulatory-criteria predicates (each defined with arity 0).
% These predicates succeed only if the record meets the corresponding 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) criterion.

% (i) One-time major, internationally recognized award
criterion_award :- fail.

% (ii) Membership in associations requiring outstanding achievements
% Although there is a "Partner" membership and bylaws reference, the record does not show
% that membership is limited to persons with outstanding achievement as required by the regulation.
criterion_membership :- fail.

% (iii) Published material about the alien in major media or professional publications
% The record contains 16 published items, but primarily in varied online sources, blogs, and smaller outlets;
% they do not rise to the level of major trade or mainstream media demonstrating sustained acclaim.
criterion_published_about :- fail.

% (iv) Participation as a judge of the work of others
criterion_judge_of_others :- fail.

% (v) Original contributions of major significance in the field
criterion_original_contributions :- fail.

% (vi) Authorship of scholarly articles in professional publications
criterion_authorship_scholarly :- fail.

% (vii) Display of work in exhibitions or showcases (for arts)
criterion_display_work :- fail.

% (viii) Leading or critical role for organizations or establishments with distinguished reputations
criterion_leading_role :- fail.

% (ix) High salary or other significantly high remuneration in relation to others in the field
% The record shows modest W-2 reported earnings and flat fees (examples: $500 session fee, $1,000 usage fee),
% which do not demonstrate the "significantly high remuneration" required by this criterion.
criterion_high_remuneration :- fail.

% (x) Commercial successes in the performing arts (e.g., box office receipts, record sales, etc.)
criterion_commercial_success :- fail.

% List of all ten criterion predicate names for counting
qualifying_criteria_list([
    criterion_award,
    criterion_membership,
    criterion_published_about,
    criterion_judge_of_others,
    criterion_original_contributions,
    criterion_authorship_scholarly,
    criterion_display_work,
    criterion_leading_role,
    criterion_high_remuneration,
    criterion_commercial_success
]).

% Count how many of the regulatory criteria are satisfied by the record
qualifying_count(N) :-
    qualifying_criteria_list(List),
    findall(C, (member(C, List), call(C)), Satisfied),
    length(Satisfied, N).

% Final merits determination: whether the petitioner is among the small percentage at the very top
% The record's totality (modest earnings, limited-quality publicity, membership not shown to require outstanding achievement,
% and lack of other strong evidence) fails the final merits threshold.
final_merits_met :- fail.

% Eligibility: either a single major award OR at least three qualifying criteria combined with a positive final merits determination
eligibility_met :-
    (   criterion_award
    ;   qualifying_count(N),
        N >= 3,
        final_merits_met
    ).

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a native and citizen of Guatemala who last entered the United States without inspection in 2006. In 2018 he was granted U-2 status as the spouse of a victim of qualifying criminal activity. He timely filed Form I-485, Application for Adjustment of Status, in 2022 seeking to become a lawful permanent resident based on his derivative U status.

The Vermont Service Center denied his I-485. The denial notice noted missing certified court and police records related to his past criminal charges and the absence of birth certificates for his three minor U.S. citizen children. In response, the petitioner filed an appeal and submitted additional evidence with that appeal: certified records related to his arrests, birth certificates for his three children, and letters from his sister and employer. He also submitted a letter from a therapist indicating that he is currently participating in substance abuse treatment. The appeal was filed to present this additional evidence and to challenge the denial.
statutes:
U.S. Citizenship
and Immigration Services (USCIS) may adjust the status of a U nonimmigrant to that of an LPR if they meet all other eligibility requirements and, "in the opinion" of USCIS, their"continued presence in the United States is justified on humanitarian grounds, to ensure family unity, or is otherwise in the public interest." Section 245(m) of the Act. The applicant bears the burden of establishing their eligibility, section 291 of the Act, 8 U .S.C. § 1361, and must do so by a preponderance ofthe evidence. MatterofChawathe, 25 I&NDec. 369,375 (AAO 2010). This burden includes establishing that discretion should be exercised in their favor, and USCIS may take into account all relevant factors in making its discretionary determination. 8 C.F.R. § § 245 .24(b )( 6), (d)(l 1). A favorable exercise of discretion to grant an applicant adjustment of status to that of an LPR is generally warranted in the absence of adverse factors and presence of favorable factors. Matter of Arai, 13 l&N Dec. 494, 496 (BIA 1970). Favorable factors include, but are not limited to, family unity, length of residence in the United States, employment, community involvement, and good moral character. Id.; see also 7 USCIS Policy Manual A.10(B)(2), https://www.uscis.gov/policy-manual (providing guidance regarding adjudicative factors to consider in discretionary adjustment of status

determinations). However, where adverse factors are present, the applicant may submit evidence establishing mitigating equities. See 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(d)(ll) (providing that, "[w]here adverse factors are present, an applicant may offset these by submitting supporting documentation establishing mitigating equities that the applicant wants users to consider when determining whether or not a favorable exercise of discretion is appropriate").
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (case-specific)
native_and_citizen_of_guatemala.

entered_without_inspection(2006).
u2_status_granted(2018).
i485_filed(2022).

director_denied.
director_denial_missing_certified_records.
director_denial_missing_birth_certificates.

% Evidence submitted on appeal (all boolean-style facts use arity 0 per instructions)
appeal_submitted_additional_evidence.
submitted_certified_arrest_records.
submitted_children_birth_certificates.
submitted_support_letters.        % letters from sister and employer
submitted_therapy_participation.   % therapist letter indicating participation in substance-abuse treatment

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% Eligibility rules (simplified discretionary balancing model)
% An applicant who is a U-2 derivative who filed an I-485 meets the
% non-discretionary status preconditions for adjustment handled here.
qualify_status :-
    u2_status_granted(_Year),
    i485_filed(_FileYear).

% The core discretionary inquiry: continued presence must be justified
% on humanitarian grounds, to ensure family unity, or otherwise in the
% public interest. We model this as a balancing test where mitigating
% and favorable equities must outweigh adverse factors.
presence_justified :-
    discretionary_balance(FavPoints, AdvPoints),
    FavPoints > AdvPoints.

% eligibility_met is used by the decision entrypoint
eligibility_met :-
    qualify_status,
    presence_justified.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% Discretionary balancing: compute numeric scores for favorable and adverse factors.
discretionary_balance(Fav, Adv) :-
    compute_favorable(Fav),
    compute_adverse(Adv).

% Favorable factors (points)
% family unity (strong if children's birth certificates provided)
compute_favorable(Points) :-
    family_unity_points(F1),
    length_residence_points(F2),
    employment_points(F3),
    community_involvement_points(F4),
    good_moral_points(F5),
    Points is F1 + F2 + F3 + F4 + F5.

family_unity_points(3) :-
    submitted_children_birth_certificates, !.
family_unity_points(0).

% Length of residence measured from last entry to filing year: >=10 years -> 2 points
length_residence_points(2) :-
    entered_without_inspection(EntryYear),
    i485_filed(FileYear),
    Diff is FileYear - EntryYear,
    Diff >= 10, !.
length_residence_points(0).

% Employment (letters from employer) -> 2 points
employment_points(2) :-
    submitted_support_letters, !.
employment_points(0).

% Community involvement (limited record here: credit 1 point if any support letters showing ties)
community_involvement_points(1) :-
    submitted_support_letters, !.
community_involvement_points(0).

% Good moral character / rehabilitation (participation in treatment, community support letters)
good_moral_points(1) :-
    submitted_therapy_participation, !.
good_moral_points(0).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% Adverse factors (points)
compute_adverse(Points) :-
    unlawful_entry_points(A1),
    arrests_points(A2),
    missing_documents_points(A3),
    substance_abuse_points(A4),
    Points is A1 + A2 + A3 + A4.

% Unlawful entry is an adverse factor (1 point)
unlawful_entry_points(1) :-
    entered_without_inspection(_), !.
unlawful_entry_points(0).

% Arrests / criminal history: certified arrest records indicate arrests exist (3 points)
% (Note: absence of convictions in the record could mitigate but arrests themselves are adverse)
arrests_points(3) :-
    submitted_certified_arrest_records, !.
arrests_points(0).

% Missing documents noted by director are adverse unless they have been supplied on appeal
missing_documents_points(0) :-
    director_denial_missing_certified_records,
    submitted_certified_arrest_records, !.
missing_documents_points(0) :-
    director_denial_missing_birth_certificates,
    submitted_children_birth_certificates, !.
missing_documents_points(2) :-
    ( director_denial_missing_certified_records ; director_denial_missing_birth_certificates ), !.
missing_documents_points(0).

% Substance abuse is adverse, but active participation in treatment mitigates it
substance_abuse_points(0) :-
    submitted_therapy_participation, !.
substance_abuse_points(2).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% Required entrypoint and error handling (do not change)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The Petitioner seeks classification as a special immigrant juvenile. The Director of the National Benefits Center denied the Form I-360 after concluding that the record did not establish the required state-law findings concerning parental mistreatment. The Director stated that the court order then in the record did not provide a sufficient factual basis for why reunification with one or both parents was not viable or why return to El Salvador was not in the Petitioner's best interest, and also stated that the order did not reference state law in making an abandonment finding. The Director further noted that, during a credible fear interview conducted after the Petitioner's attempted entry into the United States, the Petitioner had indicated that he was married. The matter was then brought on appeal.

On appeal, the Petitioner submitted a nunc pro tunc amended special juvenile order from the New York Family Court. According to that order, although it was signed in 2023, the court had taken initial jurisdiction over the Petitioner's case when he was under 21. The amended order states that the Petitioner's parents abandoned him, cites New York legal authorities in support of that finding, and says that reunification with his parents is not viable for that reason. In support, the order states that the Petitioner's biological parents have not had contact with him and have not provided care or support. The order also states that return to El Salvador is not in the Petitioner's best interest because his parents are not a viable resource there and he has no other person there able to provide for his needs.

Regarding marital status, the Petitioner states that he has never been legally married. He states that he had a partner in El Salvador with whom he had a child and that he provided her information during his credible fear interview. He explains that his use of the term "wife" for the mother of his child reflected local usage for cohabiting partners rather than a formal marriage. He also submitted a municipal letter stating that, because there was no amendment on his birth certificate, his civil status had not changed from single to married. The amended order likewise states that the New York Family Court found him unmarried when the initial order was entered and that he remained unmarried until the time of the amendment.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) ofthe Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b). Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court; alternatively, the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(l). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative 1 The Petitioner's case has a lengthy procedural history. The SU petition was initially approved, but the Director issued a notice of intent to revoke and thereafter revoked the approval. Due to a class action settlement agreement addressing the treatment of SU petitions originating in New York, the Petitioner filed a motion to reopen. The Director declined to reopen based on the class action but elected to reopen the case on other grounds. After sending a request for evidence, the Director then denied the reopened petition.

determination that it is not in the juvenile's best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(c)(2). U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) has sole authority to implement the SIJ provisions of the Act and regulation. Homeland Security Act of 2002, Pub. L. No. 107-296, §§ 471(a), 451(b), 462(c), 116 Stat. 2135 (2002). SIJ classification may only be granted upon the consent ofthe Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), through USCIS, when the petitioner meets all other eligibility criteria and establishes that the request for SIJ classification is bona fide, which requires the petitioner to establish that a primary reason the required juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under State law.

text: The record concerns a pilot who seeks EB-2 immigrant classification as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree, together with a waiver of the job-offer requirement. The Director of the Texas Service Center denied the petition, concluding that the Petitioner had not established that waiving the job-offer and labor-certification requirements would be in the national interest, and the matter is now on appeal. The Petitioner states that his proposed endeavor is to work as a pilot in the United States, that he has more than eleven years of experience in aviation, and that he is currently employed as a Boeing 737 captain and instructor. He also mentions that he would train others as part of his proposed endeavor. With the initial filing, he submitted a cover letter, ETA Form 750 Part B, evidence of education and experience, an expert opinion, certificates of achievement, a professional plan, membership evidence, employment verification letters, recommendation letters, and industry reports and articles. In response to a Request for Evidence, the Petitioner again stated that he planned to continue his career in the United States as a pilot, and the Director then determined that he had established that he has an advanced degree. The Director nevertheless determined that the evidence did not show national importance for the proposed endeavor or that waiving the job-offer and labor-certification requirements would be beneficial to the United States. The record also includes evidence of aviation licensure, job references, an expert opinion letter from a professor analyzing the national importance of the proposed endeavor and discussing the importance of aviation to the United States, and industry articles including 2022 reports describing a pilot shortage in the United States. In an updated professional plan and statement, the Petitioner asserted that the American economy is highly dependent on commercial aviation and that aviation is an essential component of the modern global economy. On appeal, the Petitioner argues that the Director imposed improper evidentiary requirements, failed to consider the evidence objectively, and overlooked his position that his proposed endeavor as a pilot would have broad national and international implications and significant benefits for multiple sectors.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. Next, a petitioner must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. at 889, provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and 1 See also Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) Uoining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D .C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS ' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver to be discretionary in nature).

• On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Case parsed into Prolog facts and rules for an EB-2 National Interest Waiver (NIW) appeal.
% Output is binary via decision(Result): 'Accepted' or 'Dismissed'.

% ------------------------
% Facts (arity 0 predicates as true/false flags)
% ------------------------
% Petitioner qualifications and employment
holds_masters_equivalent.            % Equivalent of a U.S. master's degree (awarded 2011).
experience_over_11_years.            % More than eleven years of experience in aviation.
employed_boeing_737_captain_instructor. % Employed as a Boeing 737 captain and instructor at time of filing.

% Proposed endeavor (scope and claimed impacts)
proposed_endeavor_pilot_and_training.    % Continue career in US as pilot, with some training of others.
plans_transport_passengers_and_cargo.    % Plans to generate economic impact by transporting passengers/cargo.
plans_organ_transport_humanitarian.      % Plans to provide organ transport and humanitarian flights.

% Evidence submitted
submitted_initial_evidence.          % Cover letter, ETA Form 750B, education and experience evidence, expert opinion, certificates, plan, memberships, employment verification, recommendation letters, industry reports.
responded_to_rfe.                    % Submitted updated cover letter, updated plan/statement, 3 additional recommendation letters, additional reports/articles.
recommendation_letters_submitted.    % Recommendation letters (initial + additional).
professional_plan_submitted.         % Professional plan describing proposed endeavor.
expert_letter_submitted.             % Expert opinion letter submitted.

% Administrative history
appealed_denial.                     % Petitioner appealed director's denial and submitted appeal brief.

% Flags representing what the record actually establishes (explicitly defined)
national_importance_shown :- fail.   % The record does not establish national importance (asserted as not shown).
benefit_shown :- fail.              % The record does not establish, on balance, that waiving the job offer requirement benefits the U.S. (asserted as not shown).

% ------------------------
% Legal rules encoded
% ------------------------
% EB-2 qualification: must be an advanced degree professional or exceptional ability.
qualification_met :-
    holds_masters_equivalent.       % Petitioner meets the advanced degree requirement.

% Dhanasar prong 1: proposed endeavor must have substantial merit and national importance.
% Substantial merit may be satisfied by activities that contribute to the economy, public health, etc.
substantial_merit :-
    proposed_endeavor_pilot_and_training.
substantial_merit :-
    plans_organ_transport_humanitarian.
% National importance must be shown with evidence that the endeavor has prospective influence beyond a local area.
% Here national_importance_shown is explicitly defined above as fail, so it will not succeed.

prong1_met :-
    substantial_merit,
    national_importance_shown.

% Dhanasar prong 2: applicant is well-positioned to advance the endeavor.
well_positioned :-
    experience_over_11_years,
    employed_boeing_737_captain_instructor,
    recommendation_letters_submitted,
    professional_plan_submitted,
    expert_letter_submitted.

% Dhanasar prong 3: on balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the U.S.
prong3_met :-
    benefit_shown.

% Final eligibility: must satisfy EB-2 qualification and all three Dhanasar prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    qualification_met,
    prong1_met,
    well_positioned,
    prong3_met.

% ------------------------
% Entrypoint and decision output (must be last as instructed)
% ------------------------
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-

    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner, identified by the surname R-, was the subject of New York Family Court proceedings in 2022 that produced a juvenile court order addressing custody and guardianship (the SIJ order). In that order the court listed R- both in the caption as the "petitioner" in the guardianship proceedings and as the "subject child." The court found R- to be within the court’s jurisdiction to determine custody and guardianship under New York law. The court also found that reunification with R-’s father was not viable due to abandonment, stating the father failed to provide emotional or financial support in the United States, did not contact R-, and did not care about R-’s stability, home, education, and welfare. The court determined it was not in R-’s best interest to be returned to India, identified as his country of nationality or last habitual residence, and found that R- had no one in India who could provide sustainable protection or financial assistance and that he would have fewer opportunities there. The SIJ order additionally referred to another individual, named M-K-, as having been “committed to the care, guardianship and physical custody” of a distant relative described in the order (the order’s language did not clearly identify the parties), and it included a reference to a third individual, M-S-, as providing emotional and financial support to R-.

In August 2022 the petitioner filed a Form I-360 (SIJ petition) based on the SIJ order. The National Benefits Center issued a notice of intent to deny, citing perceived inconsistencies in the SIJ order—specifically that the order listed R-, rather than the guardian, as the petitioner in the guardianship proceedings and that the names R-, M-K-, and M-S- appeared to be interchanged. In response to that notice, the petitioner submitted an “Order Appointing Guardian of the Person” issued the same day as the SIJ order, which appointed M-K- as R-’s guardian and identified the petitioner as “R-R-,” using his name for both given and family names. The director denied the SIJ petition on the ground that those submissions did not resolve material conflicts in the record, noting the differing name forms (“R-” versus “R-R-”) and the remaining inconsistencies.

The petitioner appealed the denial and on appeal submitted an amended SIJ order, an amended guardianship order, and a letter. In the letter the petitioner stated that the names R- and M-K- had been presented to the court as child and guardian, that the “incorrect names were typographical errors,” and that the errors had been subsequently corrected by the court. The amended SIJ order replaced the reference to M-S- with M-K- but still contained certain captioning language that identified R- as both the “petitioner” and the “subject child,” and it still included the unclear phrasing about M-K- being committed to the care of “his distant relative M-K-.” The amended guardianship order clarified that the petition for appointment of a guardian was filed for the person “R- aka R-R-” by M-K-, who the court determined to be a competent guardian. The captions of both guardianship orders and the letters of guardianship issued by the court explicitly identify M-K- as the petitioner in the guardianship proceedings and R- as the subject of those juvenile court guardianship proceedings.

The petitioner’s reason for appealing was to challenge the director’s denial that rested on the asserted material inconsistencies in the court orders and to submit amended court documents and a letter asserting that the discrepancies were typographical errors that had been corrected, with the goal of clarifying the record regarding the identities of the child (R- or R-R-) and the guardian (M-K-).
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(b).1 The juvenile court must have made a judicial determination related to the petitioner's custodial placement or dependency by declaring the petitioner dependent upon the juvenile court; or legally committing or placing the petitioner under the custody of an agency or department of a state, or an individual or entity appointed by astate or juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(l)(i). In addition, the record must contain a judicial or administrative determination that 1 The Department of Homeland Security issued a final rule, effective April 7, 2022, amending its regulations governing the requirements and procedures for petitioners who seek SIJ classification. See Special Immigrant Juvenile Petitions, 87 Fed. Reg. 13066 (Mar. 8, 2022) (revising 8 C.F.R. §§ 204, 205, 245).

it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country ofnationality or last habitual residence. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.11(c)(2). The juvenile court must have made the requisite judicial determinations under applicable state law to establish eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.11(c)(3). SIJ classification may only be granted upon the consent of U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) when the petitioner meets all other eligibility criteria and establishes that the request for SIJ classification is bona fide, which requires the petitioner to establish that a primary reason the required juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under State law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i)-(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.11(b)(5). USCIS may also withhold consent if evidence materially conflicts with the eligibility requirements such that the record reflects that the request for SIJ classification was not bona fide. 8 C.F.R. § 204.11(b)(5).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the case
unmarried.
under_21.

family_court_judgement(january_2022).
judicial_order_identifies_petitioner_and_child.

court_found_reunification_not_viable_with_father.
reasons_for_nonreunification([failed_emotional_support, failed_financial_support, no_contact, lack_of_care_for_stability_home_education_welfare]).

court_found_not_in_best_interest_return_to_india.
no_one_in_india_to_provide_protection_or_financial_assistance.
fewer_opportunities_in_india.

order_references_MK_committed_to_care_guardianship_and_physical_custody_of_distant_relative.
order_references_MS_providing_emotional_and_financial_support.

references_relationships_unclear.

SIJ_petition_filed(august_2022).

% Helper: member/2 (avoid relying on host-specific builtins)
member(X, [X|_]).
member(X, [_|T]) :-
    member(X, T).

% Legal-criteria predicates mapped from regulations and case facts

% Existence of a juvenile court order from state juvenile court
juvenile_court_order :-
    family_court_judgement(_When),
    judicial_order_identifies_petitioner_and_child.

% Court made a custodial placement/commitment (committed to care/guardianship/physical custody of an individual)
custodial_commitment :-
    order_references_MK_committed_to_care_guardianship_and_physical_custody_of_distant_relative.

% Court made a determination that reunification with a parent is not viable for reasons similar to abuse/neglect/abandonment
court_made_cannot_reunify :-
    court_found_reunification_not_viable_with_father,
    reasons_for_nonreunification(Reasons),
    (   member(failed_emotional_support, Reasons)
    ;   member(failed_financial_support, Reasons)
    ;   member(no_contact, Reasons)
    ;   member(lack_of_care_for_stability_home_education_welfare, Reasons)
    ).

% Court made best-interest determination
best_interest_determination :-
    court_found_not_in_best_interest_return_to_india,
    no_one_in_india_to_provide_protection_or_financial_assistance,
    fewer_opportunities_in_india.

% Court acted under applicable state law (Family Court is a state juvenile court)
made_under_state_law :-
    family_court_judgement(_).

% Bona fide requirement: primary reason for seeking court determinations was relief from parental abandonment/neglect/abuse
% We infer bona fides from the court's findings about the parent's failure to support and the nature of proceedings.
bona_fide :-
    court_made_cannot_reunify,
    juvenile_court_order.

% Material inconsistency that would cause USCIS to withhold consent
% Here the record contains references that are unclear as to relationship, but there is also an express custodial commitment;
% therefore the ambiguity does not rise to a material conflict for the identified facts.
inconsistencies_material_conflict :-
    references_relationships_unclear,
    % Only a material conflict if the record lacks any custodial commitment or contradicts the non-reunification / best-interest findings.
    \+ custodial_commitment.
    
consent_withheld :-
    inconsistencies_material_conflict.

% eligibility_met: all conditions necessary to Accept the case
eligibility_met :-
    unmarried,
    under_21,
    juvenile_court_order,
    custodial_commitment,
    court_made_cannot_reunify,
    best_interest_determination,
    made_under_state_law,
    bona_fide,
    \+ consent_withheld.

% Decision/reporting scaffolding (must be last)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a software engineer who filed Form I-140 seeking EB-2 immigrant classification and a waiver of the job offer requirement (a national interest waiver). She proposes to work as a software developer for a new public emergency-response mobile platform business.

For education, the record includes a diploma showing she was granted a Bachelor of Information Technology in June 2000. She submitted no official academic transcript in the record excerpt provided.

Regarding work experience and employment documents, the petitioner submitted a letter from a former employer indicating she worked as a software engineer from September 15, 2010 to December 20, 2014. She also submitted multiple other employer-related letters and documents comprising job offers, project assignments, and confirmations that she completed probationary periods; some of those letters reference positions such as research engineer, student researcher, project manager, project leader, senior engineer, and assignments to specific projects. The petitioner states she worked for eight employers and claims more than ten years of experience in her occupation. Payroll evidence in the record includes a June 2006 payroll receipt and an April 2005 pay receipt showing earnings of RM5,250; she also asserts an annual base salary of RM61,200 at one employer and reports earning RM5,500 monthly plus RM859 in management incentives while working for another employer.

The petitioner submitted professional certificates and training records: a Cisco Certified Network Associate certificate, two Microsoft course completion certificates (MS WIN 2000 network and operating system essentials; supporting MS WIN 2000 professional and server), and various other training-course certificates. She also submitted membership or certification documents showing ratings from the Professional Association of Diving Instructors (PADI) — specialty instructor, open water scuba instructor, and divemaster — and an IEEE membership certificate dated 2023. The record also contains an informational document related to an integrated platform that is described as the subject of her projects.

The petitioner appealed the Texas Service Center Director’s denial of her I-140 petition. On appeal she reasserts that she qualifies for the EB-2 classification (either as a professional with an advanced degree or as an individual of exceptional ability) and that she merits a national interest waiver.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. An advanced degree is any U .S. academic or professional degree or aforeign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree.1 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A U.S. bachelor's degree or a foreign equivalent 1 Profession shall include, but not be limited to, architects, engineers, lawyers, physicians, surgeons, and teachers in elementary or secondary schools, colleges, academics, or seminaries. Section 101(a)(32) of the Act.

degree followed by five years ofprogressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of amaster's degree. Id. Exceptional ability means a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A petitioner must initially submit documentation that satisfies at least three of six categories of evidence: (A) An official academic record showing that the alien has a degree, diploma, certificate, or similar award from a college, university, school, or other institution of learning relating to the area of exceptional ability; (B) Evidence in the form of letter(s) from current or former employer(s) showing that the alien has at least ten years of full-time experience in the occupation for which he or she is being sought; (C) A license to practice the profession or certification for a particular profession or occupation; (D) Evidence that the alien has commanded a salary, or other renumeration for services, which demonstrates exceptional ability; (E) Evidence of membership in professional associations; or (F) Evidence of recognition for achievements and significant contributions to the industry or field by peers, governmental entities, or professional or business organizations. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii).2 Where a petitioner meets these initial evidence requirements, we then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination and assess whether the record shows that the petitioner is recognized as having a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the field.3 See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 2010) (discussing a two-part review where the documentation is first counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination): see also Visinscaia v. Beers, 4 F. Supp. 3d 126, 131-32 (D.D.C. 2013); Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011). This two-step analysis is consistent with our holding that the "truth is to be determined not by the quantity of evidence alone but by its quality," as well as the principle that we examine "each piece of evidence for relevance, probative value, and credibility, both individually and within the context of the totality of the evidence, to determine whether the fact to be proven is probably true." Matter of Chawathe, 25 l&N Dec. at 376. 2 If these types of evidence do not readily apply to the individual's occupation, a petitioner may submit comparable evidence to establish eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(iii). 3 USCIS has previously confirmed the applicability of this two-part adjudicative approach in the context of individuals of exceptional ability. See generally 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.5(B)(2), https: //www .uscis.gov/policy-manual. 2

Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or an individual of exceptional ability, they must then establish eligibility for a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. 884 {AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that USCIS may, as matter of discretion,4 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id. at 889.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
% Facts from the record (arity 0 true/false predicates)
bachelor_degree.                % Diploma: Bachelor of Information Technology (June 2000)
cert_ccna.                      % Cisco Certified Network Associate certificate
```
