```prolog
no_clear_evidence_of_exceptional_salary. % salary evidence does not demonstrate exceptional remuneration
no_peer_or_gov_recognition.             % absence of evidence of recognition/awards by peers/government/orgs

% Rules implementing EB-2 and exceptional ability initial evidence counting (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii))
criteria_A_academic_record :-
    bachelor_degree.

criteria_B_10_years_experience :-
    % Requires letters from current/former employers showing at least ten years of full-time experience.
    % The record has employment documents but not a letter establishing 10 years at full-time.
    false.  % experience_not_proven_for_10_years implies criteria B is not satisfied

criteria_C_license_or_certification :-
    % The record includes CCNA and Microsoft course certificates. Treat CCNA as a certification relevant to the occupation,
    % but course completion certificates are of limited strength.
    cert_ccna.

criteria_D_high_salary :-
    % Evidence that the alien has commanded a salary demonstrating exceptional ability.
    % The record contains salary statements and market citations but no convincing evidence of exceptional remuneration.
    false.

criteria_E_professional_membership :-
    ieee_membership.

criteria_F_recognition :-
    % Evidence of recognition for achievements and significant contributions.
    % The record contains no peer/government/organization recognition.
    false.

% Count how many criteria are satisfied
satisfied_criteria_count(N) :-
    findall(P, (member(P, [criteria_A_academic_record, criteria_B_10_years_experience,
                           criteria_C_license_or_certification, criteria_D_high_salary,
                           criteria_E_professional_membership, criteria_F_recognition]),
                Goal =.. [P], call(Goal)), L),
    length(L, N).

initial_evidence_met :-
    satisfied_criteria_count(N),
    N >= 3.

% Advanced-degree member of the professions: master-equivalent by bachelor's + 5 years progressive experience
five_years_progressive_experience_documented :-
    % The record contains fragmentary employment documents but does not credibly document five years of progressive experience after the bachelor's.
    false.

master_equivalent :-
    bachelor_degree,
    five_years_progressive_experience_documented.

eligible_as_advanced_degree :-
    master_equivalent.

eligible_as_exceptional_ability :-
    initial_evidence_met,
    final_merits_show_exceptional_ability.

% Final merits assessment (Kazarian two-step): evaluate quality, probative value, and credibility
final_merits_show_exceptional_ability :-
    % The record must overcome concerns about relevance, probative value, and credibility.
    % The director found, and the record here shows, multiple concerns:
    % - fragmented/insufficient employment evidence for the required experience level,
    % - many certificates are course completions rather than licenses or strong credentials,
    % - professional membership shown only recently,
    % - lack of evidence of recognized achievements or exceptional salary.
    % These concerns mean the initial evidentiary showing does not translate into final merits success.
    false.

% Dhanasar framework for national interest waiver
dhanasar_prong1_substantial_merit :-
    % Proposed endeavor: building a public emergency-response mobile platform for a new business.

    integrated_platform_document.

dhanasar_prong2_well_positioned :-

    % Given those documentary weaknesses, the petitioner is not shown to be well-positioned.
    false.

dhanasar_prong3_balance_favors_waiver :-
    % On balance, waiving the job offer should benefit the U.S.
    % This balancing depends materially on prong 2 (being well-positioned). Because prong 2 is not satisfied, prong 3 fails.
    false.

dhanasar_all_prongs :-
    dhanasar_prong1_substantial_merit,
    dhanasar_prong2_well_positioned,
    dhanasar_prong3_balance_favors_waiver.

% Overall eligibility: must first qualify under EB-2 either as advanced degree professional or individual of exceptional ability,
% and then meet the discretionary national interest waiver prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    ( eligible_as_advanced_degree ; eligible_as_exceptional_ability ),
    dhanasar_all_prongs.

% Entrypoint and decision structure required by the instructions
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-

        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),

    ).

               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is an architect who filed a Form I-140 seeking EB-2 classification based on an advanced degree and a national interest waiver of the job‑offer requirement. The Texas Service Center Director denied that petition. 

The petitioner submitted an appeal on Form I-290B and provided a Form G-28 (dated June 16, 2023) when responding to a request for evidence. The appellate filing and the G-28 included images of the petitioner’s signature. On appeal the petitioner reiterated the same arguments and evidence she had submitted to the Director rather than identifying specific factual or legal errors in the prior decision. The stated purpose of the appeal was to challenge the Director’s denial of the immigrant petition and the denial of the national interest waiver.
statutes:
The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(a)(2) provides that "[u]nless otherwise specified in this chapter, an acceptable signature on a benefit request that is being filed with the USCIS [U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services] is one that is either handwritten or, for benefit requests filed electronically as permitted by the instructions to the form, in electronic format." 1 USCIS policy explains that a valid signature is "any handwritten mark or sign made by a person" and such signature must be made by the person who is the affected party with standing to file an appeal or motion to signify that "[t]he person knows of the content ofthe request and any supporting documents; [t]he person has reviewed and approves of any information contained in such request and any supporting documents; and [t]he person certifies under penalty of perjury that the request and any other supporting documents are true and correct." See generally l USCIS Policy Manual B.2(B) (emphasis added), https://www.uscis.gov/policymanual. A person's signature on an immigration form 1 Because this Form 1-290B was not electronically filed , none of the provisions relating to electronic filings applies in this case. We note the Form 1-290B is not a form that is available for parties to file electronically online. Form s Available to File Online, USCIS (Mar. 21, 2024), https://www .uscis.gov/file-online/forms-available-to-file-online.

establishes a strong presumption that the signer knows its contents and has assented to them, absent evidence of fraud or other wrongful acts by another person. Matter of Valdez, 27 I&N Dec. 496,499 (BIA 2018) (citing Thompson v. Lynch, 788 F.3d 638, 647 (6th Cir. 2015); Bingham v. Holder, 637 F.3d I 040, l 045 (9th Cir. 2011 ). The probative force of a declaration subscribed under penalty of perjury derives from the signature of the declarant. The USCIS Policy Manual provides that in "general, any person requesting an immigration benefit must sign their own immigration benefit request, and any other associated documents, before filing it with USCIS." See generally 1 USCIS Policy Manual, supra, at C.l (citing to 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(a)(2)). Agency policy provides that "[a] signature is valid even if the original signature on the document is photocopied, scanned, faxed, or similarly reproduced. Regardless of how it is transmitted to USCIS, the copy must be of an original document containing an original handwritten signature, unless otherwise specified." See generally I USCIS Policy Manual, supra, at B. Finally, the regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 292.4(a) requires that the Form G-28, Notice ofEntry ofAppearance as Attorney or Accredited Representative "must be properly completed and signed by the petitioner, applicant, or respondent to authorize representation in order for the appearance to be recognized by DHS." If someone acting on behalf of a petitioner-to include someone from their attorney's office­ performs the function of electronically applying a signature to a Form I-290B, that act nullifies the filing because it is not a valid signature and it is not properly signed under the penalty of perjury. Ultimately, even if a filing party presents a photocopy of a Form I-290B to USCIS, that photocopied form must contain a filing party's original signature that is consistent with how the person signing normally signs his or her name because "[a]n applicant or petitioner must sign his or her benefit request." 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(a)(2). Although the "regulations do not require that the person signing submit an 'original' or 'wet ink' signature on a petition, application, or other request to USCIS," we do "not accept signatures created by a typewriter, word processor, stamp, auto-pen, or similar device." See generally I USCIS Policy Manual, supra, at B. Also see generally I USCIS Policy Manual, supra, at A (stating that "[e]xcept as otherwise specifically authorized, a benefit requestor must personally sign his or her own request before filing it with USCIS"). USCIS has implemented these regulations and attendant policies "to maintain the integrity of the immigration benefit system and validate the identity of benefit requestors." See generally I USCIS Policy Manual, supra, at A. In the same way that one person signing a declaration "for" another person carries no evidentiary force, neither will an image of a signature duplicated in using some electronic means or method. Without the Petitioner's actual and personal signature as the declarant, the declaration under the penalty of perjury on the Form I-290B has no evidentiary force. See In re Rivera, 342 B.R. 435, 45859 (D. N.J. 2006); Blumberg v. Gates, No. CV 00-05607, 2003 WL 22002739 (C.D. Cal. Aug. 19, 2003). Moreover, ifwe determine that a benefit request does not contain a valid or a proper signature, we reject, deny, or dismiss it without providing an opportunity to correct or cure a deficient signature. 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(a)(7)(ii)(A); see generally I USCIS Policy Manual, supra, at A. The USCIS Policy Manual further explains that the agency interprets the regulatory term "valid signature" to mean a signature that "is consistent with how the person signing normally signs his or 2

her name." See generally 1 USCJS Policy Manual, supra, at B (explaining that the appearance of the signature on USCIS forms must be preponderantly consistent with that person's normal signature).

text:
The petitioner is a business owner and performer who operates a company that produces events, festivals, theatrical performances, and media content. She filed an I-140 seeking EB-2 immigrant classification (as a professional with an advanced degree or as an individual of exceptional ability) and requested a national interest waiver of the job‑offer requirement. In her filings she described her proposed role as an “Independent Performer of Educational Shows” and included a business plan stating the company’s mission to produce entertaining plays, host family‑friendly festivals and events, and create media content for Jewish communities (English, Hebrew, and Spanish‑speaking) in South Florida and, eventually, nationwide.

The petitioner submitted evidence intended to support her endeavor: a business plan with staffing and budget projections (planning to employ seven people with a $220,000 staffing budget in year one, rising to 15 employees and a $498,038 staffing budget by year five), a Statement of Intent noting that her enterprise would promote the local economy by hiring employees, letters of support from colleagues attesting to her experience and the benefits of theater and extracurricular programming, and articles addressing challenges in the U.S. education system such as teacher shortages, children’s mental health concerns, and pandemic‑related learning gaps. In a letter attached to her appeal she argued that her programs advance children’s social and emotional development and that creating educational programs for children has broader, including global, implications.

The Form I-140 denial by the Texas Service Center is the action she appealed. On her Form I‑290B she indicated she would submit a written brief to the Administrative Appeals Office within 30 days of filing the appeal; the record shows no written brief was filed within that timeframe.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 immigrant classification, as either an advanced degree professional or as an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. An advanced degree is any United States academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. A United States bachelor's degree or foreign equivalent degree followed by five years ofprogressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). Exceptional ability means a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A petitioner must initially submit documentation

that satisfies at least three of six categories of evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F). 1 Meeting at least three criteria, however, does not, in and of itself, establish eligibility for this classification. 2 If a petitioner does so, we will then conduct a final merits determination to decide whether the evidence in its totality shows that they are recognized as having a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the field. If a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 T&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion,3 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.

label: Dismissed

text:
The petitioner is a Brazil-born minor who, in 2022 when she was 17 years old, was the subject of a Massachusetts Probate and Family Court “Judgment of Dependency” and a separate “Special Findings of Fact and Rulings of Law.” The family court’s orders stated that the petitioner was an unmarried child under 21, that she was dependent on the court, that her biological father had neglected her and had no contact with her, that reunification with her father was not viable due to neglect and abandonment, and that it was not in her best interest to return to Brazil. The special findings also indicated the petitioner’s mother had been unable and remained unable to provide for her in Brazil.

The petitioner filed a Form I-360 special immigrant juvenile petition relying on the family court’s dependency judgment and special findings. The National Benefits Center director denied the petition. The petitioner appealed that denial, contesting the director’s analysis of the family court orders. She argued that the record documented her lack of relationship with her father, that her request for protection from the court was bona fide, and that the court had a factual basis for its determinations.

The petitioner further stated that the family court had ordered relief by referring her for counseling, but that this referral was mistakenly omitted from the original dependency judgment submitted with the petition. To support this point, she submitted an amended dependency judgment, backdated to 2022, which includes an additional notation indicating the court referred her to probation services for counseling. The petitioner’s appeal rests on her contention that the family court did, in fact, order remedial support (counseling) and that this supports her challenge to the denial.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b). 1 Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court; alternatively, the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(l). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the juvenile's best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204. ll(c)(2). 1 The Department of Hom eland Security issued a final rule, effective April 7, 2022, am ending its regulations governing the requirem ents and procedures for petitioners who seek SIJ classification. See Special Immigrant Juvenile Petitions, 87 Fed. Reg . 13066 (Mar. 8, 2022) (revising 8 C.F.R. §§ 204, 205, 245).

USCIS has sole authority to implement the SIJ provisions of the Act and regulation. Homeland Security Act of 2002, Pub. L. No. 107-296, §§ 47l(a), 45l(b), 462(c), 116 Stat. 2135 (2002). SIJ classification may only be granted upon the consent of the Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), through USCIS, when the petitioner meets all other eligibility criteria and establishes that the request for SIJ classification is bona fide, which requires the petitioner to establish that a primary reason the required juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under State law.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
initial_order_missing_referral.       % the original copy submitted to USCIS omitted a counseling referral
amended_order_backdated.              % petitioner submitted an amended dependency judgment backdated to 2022
amended_order_includes_referral.      % the amended order indicates referral to probation services for counseling

% Define what counts as court-ordered relief in this record:
% The amended order's referral to probation services for counseling is modeled as court-ordered remedial/protective relief.
referral_to_probation_counseling :- amended_order_includes_referral.
court_ordered_relief :- referral_to_probation_counseling.

% Judicial determination that reunification is not viable (map to statutory requirement)
court_found_reunification_nonviable :- reunification_not_viable_due_to_neglect.

% Bona fide requirement: a primary reason the juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental neglect/abandonment.
% Here the record shows lack of relationship with the father and findings of neglect/abandonment supporting that the primary purpose was relief.
primary_reason_sought_relief :- court_found_father_neglected, father_no_contact.
bona_fide_request :- primary_reason_sought_relief.

% Eligibility for SIJ classification requires all listed conditions (mapped from statute and regulation)
eligibility_met :-
    unmarried,
    age_under_21,
    juvenile_court_dependency,
    court_found_reunification_nonviable,
    court_ordered_relief,
    best_interest_not_return,
    bona_fide_request.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

        OtherArity \= Arity

    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
```

text:
The petitioner is a computer user support specialist who filed Form I-140 seeking classification in the EB-2 category as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or, alternatively, as an individual of exceptional ability, and also requested a national interest waiver of the job offer requirement. At the time of filing he stated that he "firmly meets the requirements of both occupational categories." The record includes evidence that the petitioner holds a degree, diploma, certificate, or similar award from a college, university, school, or other institution of learning and that he has at least ten years of full‑time experience in the occupation. USCIS issued a request for evidence (RFE) asking for details about the specific endeavor he proposes to pursue, and the petitioner submitted a response.

The petitioner described his proposed endeavor as a plan to advance his career as an IT specialist by providing services to small and large businesses in both the private and public sectors in the United States. In his RFE response and again on appeal, he emphasized generalized information about the information technology field and business, and he asserted that this generalized information demonstrates his proposed endeavor’s national significance. On appeal he also argued that the Biden‑Harris Administration’s priorities for economic recovery and the importance of advanced STEM degrees align with the national interest, and he cited generalized information about labor shortages in information technology to support the contention that his work would help fill those gaps. The petitioner appealed the denial of his petition and reiterated these factual descriptions and arguments.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," we set forth a framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions in the precedent decision Matter of

Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884 (AAO 2016). Dhanasar states that, after a petitioner has established eligibility for EB-2 classification, USCIS may, as a matter of discretion, grant a national interest waiver ifthe petitioner demonstrates: (1) that the noncitizen's proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; (2) that the noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor; and (3) that, on balance, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification. See Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver is discretionary in nature). The first prong, substantial merit and national importance, focuses on the specific endeavor that the noncitizen proposes to undertake. The endeavor's merit may be demonstrated in a range of areas such as business, entrepreneurialism, science, technology, culture, health, or education. In determining whether the proposed endeavor has national importance, we consider its potential prospective impact. See Matter ofDhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. at 888-91, for elaboration on these three prongs.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
filed_I140_eb2.
requested_niw.
has_degree.
has_10_years_experience.
claimed_both_categories.

% Facts about the petitioner's proposed endeavor and the evidence submitted
proposed_endeavor_general_it.         % endeavor described as advancing career as IT specialist for businesses
rfe_submissions_generalized.          % RFE response focused on generalized IT/business info
publications_and_general_statements.  % included publications and general statements about IT industry
no_specific_project.                  % no specific project or initiative tied to petitioner
no_measurable_national_effects.       % no measurable national-scale effects attributable to petitioner

% Administrative findings and appeal assertions
director_denied_exceptional_ability.  % director found petitioner did not qualify as exceptional ability
director_denied_niw.                 % director concluded NIW not in the national interest
appeal_reiterated_general_info.      % on appeal petitioner reiterated generalized information
appeal_cited_admin_prioritization.    % appealed citing admin prioritization of economic recovery / STEM
appeal_cited_labor_shortage.         % appealed citing generalized evidence of IT labor shortages

% Explicit negative/limiting facts (used with negation-as-failure to avoid undefined calls)
has_advanced_degree_no.              % record does not establish an advanced degree for EB-2 advanced-degree subcategory
exceptional_ability_evidence_no.     % record does not establish required exceptional ability evidence
has_personal_evidence_no.            % no tailored evidence showing petitioner is well positioned (e.g., no specific letters/projects)
balance_no.                          % the balance-of-benefits test is not satisfied on the record

% EB-2 eligibility: must qualify as either advanced-degree professional or exceptional ability.
% Use negation-as-failure against explicit negative facts to keep predicate calls defined.
qualifies_advanced_degree :-
    \+ has_advanced_degree_no,
    has_degree,
    has_10_years_experience,
    claimed_both_categories.

qualifies_exceptional_ability :-
    \+ exceptional_ability_evidence_no,
    claimed_both_categories.

eb2_eligible :-
    qualifies_advanced_degree
    ;
    qualifies_exceptional_ability.

% Dhanasar prongs for NIW

% Prong 1: substantial merit and national importance.
% Substantial merit can be shown by work in technology/business.
substantial_merit :-
    proposed_endeavor_general_it.

% National importance requires prospective national-scale impact tied to the petitioner's specific endeavor.
national_importance :-
    \+ no_specific_project,
    \+ no_measurable_national_effects.

prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    substantial_merit,
    national_importance.

% Prong 2: well positioned to advance the endeavor.
% Requires tailored evidence (projects, records, letters) showing ability to advance the specific proposed endeavor.
well_positioned :-
    \+ has_personal_evidence_no.

prong2_well_positioned :-
    well_positioned.

% Prong 3: on balance beneficial to waive job offer/labor certification.
% Requires that benefits of waiver outweigh protective purpose of labor certification.
benefit_balance :-
    \+ balance_no.

prong3_balance_beneficial :-
    benefit_balance.

% Eligibility for NIW requires EB-2 eligibility plus all three Dhanasar prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_eligible,
    prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    prong2_well_positioned,
    prong3_balance_beneficial.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner states that he earned an associate’s degree in public relations in 2011 and afterward founded a business development company in Nigeria that provides media production and advertising services. He has served as that company’s chief executive officer and as its creative director. He says the company produced radio and television programs and a record album. He entered the United States as a B-2 visitor in July 2021.

In his immigrant-petition filings, the petitioner identified four evidentiary points he relied on: (1) membership in professional associations that require outstanding achievements, (2) original contributions of major significance to his field, (3) a leading or critical role for distinguished organizations, and (4) high remuneration for his services. On appeal he did not pursue the issue of a leading or critical role, and he maintains that he meets the other three points (membership, original contributions, and high remuneration). He appealed the Nebraska Service Center decision denying his Form I-140 petition and submitted additional materials in support of his appeal.

Regarding professional associations, the petitioner submitted a certificate showing his participation in an Advertising Practitioners Council of Nigeria (APCON) professional development seminar/workshop, but did not submit documentation showing formal APCON membership. He is a member of the Electronic Media Content Owners Association of Nigeria (EMCOAN). In response to a request for evidence, he provided EMCOAN’s Constitution and letters from EMCOAN officials. The Constitution states: membership is open to any natural or legal person who is an owner of programmes intended for broadcast; there are two membership categories (corporate members—registered companies that have paid fees—and associate members—individuals with a verifiable track record of media content production for at least six months); membership applications are submitted to the General Secretary, directed to the executives, and if executives are satisfied the application is presented at the next general meeting for ratification by simple majority. The petitioner’s membership certificate names his company, indicating he joined as a corporate member. He also submitted a letter attributed to the president of EMCOAN that describes an internal vetting process by existing membership.

On compensation, the petitioner stated that, as a creative director, he earns approximately 3,600,000 Nigerian Naira annually and compared that figure to published salary figures for creative directors in Nigeria. He did not submit payroll records or income tax forms. He provided personal and company bank statements for 2017–2022 showing irregular credits from multiple sources (including transfers from his company and from his spouse) and varying annual totals (for example, about N24 million in 2017 and under N800,000 in 2018). He did not provide documentation that clearly identified which deposits, if any, represented regular salary payments.

To support his claim about high remuneration, the petitioner submitted salary-survey material from several websites: initial screen captures from MySalaryScale and Salary Explorer, and later printouts from Salary Explorer (showing an annual range and mean/median), a Payscale graph, and Glassdoor statistics (including employer-specific reported salaries). Those sources produced differing figures for creative-director pay in Nigeria (monthly and annual figures, and employer-specific numbers ranging widely). In response to the Service Center’s request for evidence, the petitioner resubmitted bank records and the surveys described above. On appeal, he also asserted that Nigeria is a developing country and that available wage data may be less sophisticated than data from developed countries, and that the wage-survey data in the record should be sufficient to show his comparative earnings.

The petitioner also submitted other documentary material and letters in support of his petition and appeal, including earlier supporting letters from EMCOAN officials and documentation reflecting his company’s work for certain clients and publicity campaigns. His appeal centers on his assertion that the record establishes his eligibility based on the membership, original-contributions, and high-remuneration points he has presented.
statutes:
Section 203(b)(l)(A) of the Act makes immigrant visas available to individuals with extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics which has been demonstrated by sustained national or international acclaim and whose achievements have been recognized in the field through extensive documentation. These individuals must seek to enter the United States to continue work in the area of extraordinary ability, and their entry into the United States will substantially benefit the United States. The term "extraordinary ability" refers only to those individuals in "that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2). The implementing regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) sets forth a multi-part analysis. First, a petitioner can demonstrate international recognition of their achievements in the field through a one-time achievement in the form of a major, internationally recognized award. Or the petitioner can submit evidence that meets at least three of the ten criteria listed at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x), including items such as awards, published material in certain media, and scholarly articles. If those standards do not readily apply to the individual's occupation, then the regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4) allows the submission of comparable evidence.

Once a petitioner has met the initial evidence requirements, the next step is a final merits determination, in which we assess whether the record shows sustained national or international acclaim and demonstrates that the individual is among the small percentage at the very top of the field of endeavor. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 2010) (discussing a two-part review where the documentation is first counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination); see also Visinscaia v. Beers, 4 F. Supp. 3d 126, 131-32 (D.D.C. 2013); Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011).

```prolog
% Facts from the record (zero-arity predicates)
petitioner_creative_director.

served_as_ceo_and_creative_director.
produced_radio_television_and_record_album.
entered_us_b2_july_2021.

attended_apcon_professional_development_seminar.
not_apcon_member.
emcoan_corporate_member.
emcoan_constitution_describes_membership_categories.
emcoan_constitution_requires_owners_or_6_months_track_record.
emcoan_officials_letters_describe_vetting.

claimed_annual_compensation_3600000_ngn.

appealed_and_maintains_membership_original_contributions_and_high_remuneration_claims.
did_not_challenge_director_conclusion_on_leading_or_critical_role_on_appeal.

% Evaluation rules for the regulatory evidentiary criteria (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3))
% We represent whether each claimed criterion is met based on the record.
% Per instructions, these are zero-arity yes/no predicates.

% 1) Membership in associations that require outstanding achievements:
% Record: EMCOAN constitution shows basic eligibility (owners of programs or 6 months track record),
% APCON only attendance certificate (not membership). Record does not show that membership requires
% outstanding achievements. Therefore criterion is not met.
meets_criterion_membership_assoc :-
    % explicit fail because record does not show required level
    !,
    fail.

% 2) Original contributions of major significance:
% Record: company projects and publicity exist, but no persuasive independent evidence showing
% that petitioner's contributions rose to "major significance" in the field.
meets_criterion_original_contribution :-
    % explicit fail because record evidence is insufficient to show major significance
    !,
    fail.

% 3) Evidence of high remuneration for services:
% Record: claimed salary and bank statements exist, but lack of payroll/tax records and
% inconsistent bank credits and reliance on public salary surveys do not establish high remuneration.
meets_criterion_high_remuneration :-
    % explicit fail because record does not reliably establish high, well-documented remuneration
    !,
    fail.

% (Other criteria could be listed similarly; petitioner did not pursue leading/critical role on appeal.)

% Count how many of the claimed/needed criteria are actually established by the record.
criteria_met_count(N) :-
    findall(1,
            (   meets_criterion_membership_assoc
            ;   meets_criterion_original_contribution
            ;   meets_criterion_high_remuneration
            ),
            L),
    length(L, N).

% Final merits determination (Kazarian): sustained national/international acclaim and being among the
% small percentage at the very top of the field. Record does not demonstrate sustained acclaim or that
% the petitioner is among the small percentage at the very top.
final_merits :-
    % explicit fail because record does not show sustained acclaim/top-of-field status
    !,
    fail.

% Eligibility: must meet at least 3 criteria AND pass the final merits determination.
eligibility_met :-
    criteria_met_count(Count),
    Count >= 3,
    final_merits.

% Decision and runtime entrypoint (provided structure)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The applicant filed Form N-600 seeking a Certificate of Citizenship, asserting that he acquired U.S. citizenship at birth through his father. He did not provide specific dates of his father’s periods of physical presence on the Form N-600, instead relying on documentary evidence and sworn statements to show his father’s presence in the United States.

The applicant submitted a social security earnings statement for his father showing income in the United States between 1970 and 1975, and a school “Cumulative Record Card” showing his father first registered with a school in August 1966, with a notation that he “dropped” in January 1968 and an address entry indicating he was residing with his mother at the time of registration. The applicant also submitted a 1973 baptismal record for his older sister.

The applicant provided multiple affidavits from people who said they knew his father during the relevant period: his mother stated she first met the father in 1960, they began dating in 1963, and she would visit him and he would visit her; a cousin described visiting him at the home of his paternal grandmother and provided addresses and dates; a paternal aunt listed dates and addresses where she remembers him living with their father; another affiant said her father rented an apartment to the applicant’s father between 1962 and 1965; and one affiant stated she met the father in 1970 and that he resided in the United States from that time until his death in 2018. The applicant identified his father by initials A‑P‑ and noted that his father was born in Texas in 1950.

The reason the applicant filed the appeal was that he contended the adjudicator did not properly consider or weigh the evidence in the record. On his Form I-290B Notice of Appeal or Motion, he indicated he would submit a brief and additional evidence within 30 calendar days of filing the appeal; however, no brief or additional evidence was received by the appeals office.
statutes:
The applicable law for transmitting citizenship to a child born abroad when one parent is a U.S. citizen is the statute that was in effect at the time ofthe child's birth. Chau v. Immigration and Naturalization Service, 247 F.3d 1026, 1029 n.3 (9th Cir. 2001) (internal quotation marks and citation omitted). The Applicant was born abroad in 1975 to unmarried parents2, a U.S.-born citizen father and a Mexican citizen mother. At that time, former section 301(a)(7) of the Act governed acquisition of U.S. citizenship by children born abroad to one U.S. citizen and one noncitizen parent. It provided, in relevant part, that a child would acquire citizenship from the U.S . citizen parent if the parent "prior to the birth of such [child], was physically present in the United States or its outlying possessions for a period or periods totaling not less than ten years, at least five of which were after attaining the age of fourteen years." 1 Re-designated as section 301(g) of the Act by Act of October 10, 1978, Pub. L. No. 95-432, 92 Stat. 1046. 2 At the time ofhis birth, the Applicant's parents were not married. They would subsequently marry in Mexico in __ 1975 .

Because the Applicant was born abroad, he is presumed to be a noncitizen and bears the burden of establishing his claim to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. Matter ofBaires­ Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467, 468 (BIA 2008). The "preponderance of the evidence" standard requires the Applicant to demonstrate that his citizenship claim is "probably trne," based on the specific facts of his case. Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. at 376.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts: case background and submitted evidence
father_us_born.
applicant_born_abroad_1975.
parents_unmarried_at_birth.

% Documentary evidence
ss_earnings_1970_1975.                       % Social Security earnings statement shows earnings in US 1970-1975
cumulative_record_card_school_1966_1968_address_mexico.  % School registration Aug 1966 - Jan 1968, address listed in Mexico
baptismal_record_1973_sister.

% Affidavits (content summarized; many lack corroborating detail)
affidavit_mother_met_1960_dating_1963.
affidavit_cousin_and_aunt_provide_addresses_dates_but_few_details.
affidavit_gqs_rented_apartment_1962_1965.   % states her father rented apartment to paternal grandfather 1962-1965
affidavit_mgv_resided_us_from_1970_to_2018.  % states father continually resided in US from 1970 until death in 2018

% Procedural facts
procedural_no_specific_dates_on_form.        % Applicant did not list specific dates of father's physical presence on Form N-600
procedural_no_additional_evidence_on_appeal. % No brief or additional evidence received by AAO within stated period

% Burden of proof
applicant_bears_burden.

% Legal requirements for acquisition under former section 301(a)(7) (as of 1975)
req_total_years_before_birth(10).
req_years_after_14(5).

% Quantified inferences drawn from documentary evidence
% Social security earnings cover 1970 through 1975 inclusive -> 6 calendar years of recorded U.S. earnings
years_from_ss(6).

% Affidavits and other documents were lodged but contain limited detail and do not independently establish additional discrete years
% of continuous physical presence that can be reliably counted toward the 10-year requirement.
% For modeling the AAO conclusion, treat affidavit-supported years as insufficiently corroborated to add quantifiable years.
years_from_affidavits(0).

% Total years of proven physical presence prior to birth (as established by admissible/documentary evidence on record)
total_years_proven(T) :-
    years_from_ss(S),
    years_from_affidavits(A),
    T is S + A.

% Proof that the U.S. citizen parent was physically present for at least the required total years prior to the child's birth
meets_total_requirement :-
    total_years_proven(T),
    req_total_years_before_birth(R),
    T >= R.

% Proof that at least the required number of years were after the parent attained age 14.
% The record does not establish the father's date of birth or other reliable proof that the documented years were all after age 14.
% Therefore, do not assert age_proven_over_14; absence of such a fact means the requirement is not met.
% (Per instruction: boolean/yes-no predicates use arity 0; age_proven_over_14 is intentionally not defined to reflect lack of proof.)
% age_proven_over_14.

meets_after14_requirement :-
    age_proven_over_14,
    req_years_after_14(R),
    total_years_proven(T_after),    % placeholder: would represent years proven after age 14 if age were proven
    T_after >= R.

% Preponderance standard: applicant must show by the preponderance of the evidence that requirements are "probably true."
% Applicant bears the burden; here we model that the preponderance is not met because one or both statutory requirements are not proved.
preponderance_established :-
    applicant_bears_burden,
    meets_total_requirement,
    meets_after14_requirement.

% Eligibility is all necessary conditions to Accept. Because preponderance_established fails given the record as modeled, eligibility_met will fail.
eligibility_met :-
    father_us_born,
    applicant_born_abroad_1975,
    parents_unmarried_at_birth,
    preponderance_established.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Applicant, a native and citizen of Mexico, seeks adjustment to lawful permanent resident status based on derivative U nonimmigrant status. The Vermont Service Center denied his Form I-485. He then filed a combined motion to reopen and reconsider with the Director, which was denied, and the matter is now on appeal. On appeal, he submitted a brief and the documents previously filed with that motion. The Director's denial referenced a 2021 arrest and charge described in the record as "sex with minor (3+ years younger)." In his appeal brief, the Applicant acknowledges a California conviction under section 261.5(c) and states that he provided documents showing the statute of conviction and the nature of the conviction. The record includes a 2022 sentencing minute order from the Superior Court of California. According to that minute order, he was charged with a misdemeanor offense, imposition of sentence was suspended, he was placed on one year of conditional probation, a restraining order was entered to protect the minor through 2025, and he was ordered to serve 30 days in county jail, pay fines, complete community service, and attend mental health sessions. The record description states that he also submitted documentation showing he completed community service and attended sessions with a psychotherapist, but that the record does not include police or court records describing the underlying circumstances of the arrest and conviction, the age of the minor, or other details of the offense. In a personal statement, the Applicant says he came to the United States at age two, attended school and graduated from high school here, is employed, and has parents and siblings living in the United States. On appeal, he argues that his compliance with probation should be treated as a favorable factor. He also argues, citing Esquivel-Quintana, that his offense should not be treated as categorical sexual abuse of a minor, and he asserts that the Director mischaracterized the offense by describing it as a crime against a three-year-old. He further argues that his motion rights gave him only 30 days to respond to the denial, rather than the longer period associated with a request for evidence, which he says limited his ability to submit additional mitigating evidence; the record description of that argument does not identify any specific additional evidence he said he would have submitted or steps he was taking to obtain it.
statutes:
USCIS may adjust the status of a U nonimmigrant to that of an LPR if they meet all other eligibility requirements and, "in the opinion" ofUSers, their "continued presence in the United States is justified on humanitarian grounds, to ensure family unity, or is otherwise in the public interest." Section 245(m) of the Act. The applicant bears the burden of establishing that discretion should be exercised in their favor, and USCIS may take into account all relevant factors in making its discretionary determination. 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(b)(6), (d)(ll) . USCIS may deny a benefit request accepted for adjudication if there is a deficient signature. See generally 1 USCIS Policy Manual B .2(A), https://www.uscis.gov/policy-manual (providing, as guidance, "[i]f users accepts a request for adjudication and later determines that it has a deficient signature, users denies the request.") In order to maintain the integrity of the immigration benefit system and validate the identity of benefit requestors, USCIS requires a valid signature on

applications, petitions, requests, and certain other documents filed with USCIS. Id. USCIS does not accept signatures created by a typewriter, word processor, stamp, auto-pen, or similar device. Id. at B.2(8) (explaining, as guidance, the requirements for a valid signature).

11.

```prolog
% Facts about the petitioner and case (all predicates are 0-arity booleans)
petitioner_from_mexico.
derivative_U_status_oct2017_oct2021.
filed_I485_oct2021.

conviction_pc_261_5_c.          % Conviction under California Penal Code § 261.5(c)
minute_order_misdemeanor.       % The minute order reflects a misdemeanor
sentence_suspended.             % Imposition of sentence was suspended
probation_1yr.                  % One year of conditional probation
restraining_order_through_2025. % Protective restraining order through 2025
jail_30_days.                   % Ordered to serve 30 days in county jail
fines_ordered.                  % Fines ordered
community_service_completed.    % Record shows community service completed
psychotherapy_completed.        % Record shows psychotherapy completed
no_police_court_records_underlying. % No police/court records describing underlying circumstances or age of minor

came_to_US_age_2.
graduated_high_school_US.
employed.
family_in_US.
personal_statement_lacks_details.

motion_reopen_denied.
appealed_i290b.
appeal_brief_submitted.
rfe_did_not_address_arrest.     % Claim that the RFE did not address the arrest record
cites_esquivel_quintana.       % Applicant cites Esquivel-Quintana v. Sessions
asserts_compliance_favorable.

signature_simulated.            % Adjudicator finds the Form I-290B signature appears simulated

% USCIS policy requirement (guidance) that a valid signature is required
uscis_requires_valid_signature.

% Signature handling
signature_deficient :-
    signature_simulated.

has_valid_signature :-
    uscis_requires_valid_signature,
    \+ signature_deficient.

% Criminal conviction as negative discretionary factor:
% When there is a conviction for unlawful sexual intercourse with a minor and the
% record lacks underlying circumstances (including age of the minor), the conviction
% is treated as a significant adverse factor for discretionary adjustment.
criminal_conviction_negative_factor :-
    conviction_pc_261_5_c,
    no_police_court_records_underlying.

% Positive equities
positive_equities :-
    came_to_US_age_2,
    graduated_high_school_US,
    employed,
    family_in_US,
    community_service_completed,
    psychotherapy_completed.

% Discretion: USCIS exercises discretion considering all factors.
% For this simplified model, discretion favors the applicant only if positive equities
% exist and there is no significant adverse criminal factor and the signature is valid.
discretion_favors_applicant :-
    positive_equities,
    \+ criminal_conviction_negative_factor,
    has_valid_signature.

% Eligibility: all necessary conditions for acceptance (per instruction, eligibility_met is the conjunction)
eligibility_met :-
    derivative_U_status_oct2017_oct2021,
    filed_I485_oct2021,
    has_valid_signature,
    discretion_favors_applicant.

% Decision entrypoint (do not modify)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a native and citizen of Mexico who is currently residing in the United States and has applied to adjust status to lawful permanent resident. In July 2000 she attempted to enter the United States and presented a passport containing a fraudulently obtained Form I-551 stamp. On her Form I-485 she answered “no” to questions about arrests, submitting fraudulent or counterfeit documentation to obtain immigration benefits, and concealing or misrepresenting information on immigration applications; on appeal she does not contest the finding of inadmissibility and states she is remorseful and that she did not review the Form I-485 before signing it. The petitioner is married (31 years) to a lawful permanent resident and the couple has six children. She is not employed outside the home and performs primary household and caregiving duties: managing the household, preparing meals, doing laundry, supervising and assisting the children with bathing, dressing, grooming, medical and educational needs, and providing hands-on support for children with developmental and mental-health needs (including helping with mobility, personal care, and communication where needed). The petitioner also would be eligible to work and to receive In-Home Supportive Services payments for providing full‑time care for one child if she is permitted to remain and work in the United States. 

She appealed the denial of her Form I-601 waiver because she disagrees with the decision and asserts that prior counsel failed to convey the full extent of the hardship her spouse and children would suffer in her absence—specifically the emotional and psychological effects on the children and the resulting helplessness her spouse would feel in trying to ease that suffering.
statutes:
Any alien who , by fraud or willfully misrepresenting a material fact, seeks to procure ( or has sought to procure or has procured) a visa, other documentation, or admission into the United States or other benefit provided under the Act, is inadmissible. Section 212(a)(6)(C)(i) of the Act. There is a waiver of this inadmissibility if refusal of admission would result in extreme hardship to the United States citizen or lawful permanent resident spouse or parent of the alien. If the alien demonstrates the existence ofthe required hardship, then he or she must also show that USCIS should favorably exercise its discretion and grant the waiver. Section 212(i) of the Act.

A determination of whether denial of admission will result in extreme hardship depends on the facts and circumstances of each case. Matter of Cervantes-Gonzalez, 22 I&N Dec. 560, 565 (BIA 1999) (citations omitted). We recognize that some degree ofhardship to qualifying relatives is present in most cases; however, to be considered "extreme," the hardship must exceed that which is usual or expected. See Matter of Pilch, 21 I&N Dec. 627, 630-31 (BIA 1996) (finding that factors such as economic detriment, severing family and community ties, loss of current employment, and cultural readjustment were the "common result of deportation" and did not alone constitute extreme hardship). In determining whether extreme hardship exists, individual hardship factors that may not rise to the level of extreme must also be considered in the aggregate. Matter ofIge, 20 I&N Dec. 880, 882 (BIA 1994) ( citations omitted).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
