text:
The Petitioner, a videographer, seeks classification as an individual of extraordinary ability. She states that she is a graduate of a directing program and intends to continue her work in the United States in television and film production. The Director of the Nebraska Service Center denied the petition after concluding that the record did not establish that she had satisfied at least three of the initial evidentiary criteria, and the matter is now on appeal.

On appeal, the Petitioner contends that she meets three of the disputed criteria and that the Director disregarded evidence and made factual errors in reviewing the record. The record includes articles showing that she was interviewed and that her work was discussed in ICG Magazine, including discussion of her transition from China and her work on several productions. The denial stated that the evidence did not establish that ICG Magazine was a professional or major trade publication or other major media and did not otherwise acknowledge that submitted material.

The record also initially included a printed World Journal article and a certified translation, both showing a link to the story on the publication's website, while the Director characterized World Journal as a web portal open to user-created and marketing content. In a request for evidence, the Director stated that the link was not functional and that the article therefore could not be verified. In response, the Petitioner submitted the same article from a different link on the publication's website, described it as an updated link, and also submitted the same translation certificate showing the original translation date and original link.

The Petitioner further asserts that independent film projects she wrote and directed have been exhibited at several film festivals. The record includes festival documentation listing the films, emails informing her of the festivals' intent to screen her films, and letters from industry managers attesting to the inclusion of her work in those festivals. The record also shows that she has worked as a focus puller, overseen camera crews, and written and directed independent projects. The denial stated that her titles of videographer, first assistant camera, and focus puller were not artistic in nature and therefore disqualified her, including the statement that a videographer is not an artist. On appeal, the Petitioner disputes that characterization as a misunderstanding or mistake of fact, and the record includes expert opinion letters and other documentation describing television and film production roles, including that participants may take on multiple roles and that titles may vary by project.
statutes:
An individual is eligible for the extraordinary ability classification if they have extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics which has been demonstrated by sustained national or international acclaim and their achievements have been recognized in the field through extensive documentation; they seek to enter the United States to continue work in the area of extraordinary ability; and their entry into the United States will substantially benefit prospectively the United States. Section 203(b)(l)(A) of the Act. The term "extraordinary ability" refers only to those individuals in "that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2). The implementing regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) sets forth a multi-part analysis. First, a petitioner may demonstrate international recognition of their achievements in the field through a one-time achievement (that is, a major, internationally recognized award). Absent such an achievement, a petitioner must provide sufficient qualifying documentation demonstrating that they meet at least three ofthe ten criteria listed at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x).

Where a petitioner meets these initial evidence requirements, we then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination and assess whether the record shows sustained national or international acclaim and demonstrates that the individual is among the small percentage at the very top of the field of endeavor. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 2010) ( discussing a two-part review where the documentation is fust counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination); see also Visinscaia v. Beers, 4 F. Supp. 3d 126, 131-32 (D.D.C. 2013); Rijal v. USCIS , 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W .D . Wash . 2011).

IL
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Parsed Administrative Appeals Office case into Prolog
% Facts are encoded as zero-arity predicates (true/false style).
% The program outputs either 'Accepted' or 'Dismissed'.

% Evidence facts derived from the record
has_published_material.         % Articles in trade and ethnic media (ICG Magazine, World Journal)
has_artistic_exhibitions.      % Independent film projects selected for multiple film festivals
has_leading_or_critical_role.   % Worked as focus puller, first assistant camera, oversaw camera crews, creative/directing roles
expert_opinion_present.        % Expert-opinion letters describing production roles and significance
prior_o1_approval.             % Previously beneficiary of an approved O-1B nonimmigrant petition
seeks_to_continue_work.        % Seeks to continue working in the US in television and film production
entry_will_benefit_us.         % Prospective substantial benefit to the US (experience on major productions, industry contributions)

% Other possible criteria (not established in the record as facts here):
% major_award.                % No major, internationally recognized one-time award shown in the record

% Count qualifying criteria relevant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)
criteria_count(Count) :-
    ( has_published_material -> C1 = 1 ; C1 = 0 ),
    ( has_artistic_exhibitions -> C2 = 1 ; C2 = 0 ),
    ( has_leading_or_critical_role -> C3 = 1 ; C3 = 0 ),
    % Additional criteria placeholders (not supported by explicit facts here)
    ( expert_opinion_present -> C4 = 0 ; C4 = 0 ),  % expert opinions support merits but are not a listed criterion themselves
    ( prior_o1_approval -> C5 = 0 ; C5 = 0 ),       % prior O-1B approval is supportive evidence, not one of the 10 listed criteria
    Count is C1 + C2 + C3 + C4 + C5.

% Initial evidentiary requirement: either a major award or at least three of the regulatory criteria
meets_initial_evidence_requirement :-
    (   % major_award -> true
        (   current_predicate(major_award/0),
            major_award
        )
    ;   % otherwise at least 3 qualifying criteria
        criteria_count(N),
        N >= 3
    ).

% Final merits determination (Kazarian two-step): assess sustained national/international acclaim
% For this record, final merits are considered satisfied when:
%  - the petitioner meets the initial criteria (>=3), and
%  - the record contains independent expert opinion letters corroborating significance, and
%  - there is additional supporting evidence such as prior O-1B approval or leading/critical roles.
final_merits_satisfied :-
    meets_initial_evidence_requirement,
    expert_opinion_present,
    ( prior_o1_approval ; has_leading_or_critical_role ).

% Has extraordinary ability if either one-time major award or both initial evidence and final merits
has_extraordinary_ability :-
    (   current_predicate(major_award/0),
        major_award
    )
    ;
    (   meets_initial_evidence_requirement,
        final_merits_satisfied
    ).

% Eligibility is met when extraordinary ability is shown and the statutory entry/benefit conditions are satisfied
eligibility_met :-
    has_extraordinary_ability,
    seeks_to_continue_work,
    entry_will_benefit_us.

% Decision structure required by the task
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a consultant who filed Form I-140 seeking classification in the EB-2 category as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree and a national interest waiver of the job offer requirement. She asserts that her foreign education in industrial engineering is equivalent to a U.S. bachelor’s degree and that she also has five years of progressive post‑baccalaureate experience. In support of the petition she submitted a diploma for a degree in industrial engineering, a degree certificate, an honorable mention certificate, an evaluation of her educational credentials, and a letter from a former employer. In response to a request for evidence she provided an employment letter confirming her work at a company; that letter stated she was an Assistant Manager from 2004 to 2021. Her originally submitted curriculum vitae, however, stated she was a Manager at that company from 2002 to 2008, creating an inconsistency in the record. She does not claim extraordinary ability.

The petitioner appealed the Texas Service Center’s denial of her I-140. On appeal she contends that she submitted sufficient evidence to qualify as an advanced degree professional and that the educational evaluation demonstrates her foreign degree is equivalent to a U.S. bachelor’s degree.
statutes:
To qualify for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, a petitioner must establish they are an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b)(2)(A) of the Act. An advanced degree is any U.S. academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. A U.S. bachelor's degree or foreign equivalent degree followed by five years of progressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. 8 C.F.R . § 204.5(k)(2). If a petitioner establishes eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then demonstrate that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship

and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the petition and submitted evidence
filed_i140.
seeks_eb2.
seeks_national_interest_waiver.

submitted_diploma.
submitted_degree_certificate.
submitted_honorable_mention.
submitted_educational_evaluation.
submitted_cv.
submitted_employer_letter.

asserts_foreign_degree_in_industrial_engineering.
asserts_bachelors_equivalence.    % educational credentials evaluation asserts U.S. bachelor's equivalence
claims_five_years_progressive_experience.

% Conflicting employment history in CV vs employer letter -> inconsistency
employer_letter_dates_2004_2021.
cv_dates_manager_2002_2008.
there_is_inconsistency.

% Director determination (record shows the Texas Service Center concluded ineligibility for EB-2)
director_concluded_ineligible_eb2.

% Rules implementing statutory/regulatory framework for EB-2 and NIW

% A foreign degree that is equivalent to a U.S. bachelor's degree
foreign_equivalent_bachelor :-
    asserts_foreign_degree_in_industrial_engineering,
    submitted_educational_evaluation,
    asserts_bachelors_equivalence.

% Five years of progressive post-baccalaureate experience is only established if
% the petitioner claims it, submitted supporting documentation, and there is no inconsistency that undermines the employment history.
five_years_progressive_experience :-
    claims_five_years_progressive_experience,
    submitted_employer_letter,
    submitted_cv,
    \+ there_is_inconsistency.

% An advanced-degree equivalent (the EB-2 "advanced degree" requirement) is met
% when the petitioner holds a foreign equivalent master's/advanced degree OR
% holds a foreign-equivalent bachelor's plus five years progressive experience.
% For this case we model only the paths relevant to the record.
advanced_degree_equivalent :-
    % Path: foreign-equivalent bachelor's + five years progressive experience
    foreign_equivalent_bachelor,
    five_years_progressive_experience.

% Dhanasar (NIW) prongs: define predicates but make them fail when not established.
% They are defined (so evaluation does not cause undefined-predicate errors),
% but they fail unless affirmative supporting facts exist. The record here does not
% establish these prongs, so they are implemented to fail.
substantial_merit_and_national_importance :- fail.
individual_well_positioned :- fail.
waiving_benefit_to_us :- fail.

% NIW prongs all must be satisfied
niw_prongs_met :-
    substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    individual_well_positioned,
    waiving_benefit_to_us.

% eligibility_met requires both the underlying EB-2 advanced-degree classification
% and the discretionary NIW prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    advanced_degree_equivalent,
    niw_prongs_met.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Applicant seeks a Certificate of Citizenship based on her claim that she derived citizenship through her naturalized U.S. citizen father. The Atlanta, Georgia Field Office denied her Form N-600 after concluding that she had not shown that she was residing in her father's legal custody in the United States because the divorce order she submitted did not award custody to him. On appeal, she submitted additional evidence about her residence with her father, his support and care, her mother's illness, and her parents' agreement that she remain in his primary care. The record includes an order from the Customary Court of State of Nigeria granting her parents' divorce. The Applicant argues that, although the order does not expressly grant custody to either parent, it shows that her father was primarily responsible for the children's care because her mother was ordered to pay child support and the order assigned the children's education and health responsibilities to him. She also argues that her father's actual and uncontested custody should be treated as sufficient even without an express custody award. The appeal record and USCIS records indicate that the Applicant has resided with her father since at least 2018. She also submitted her father's 2020 through 2022 tax returns and IRS transcripts showing that he filed as head of household and listed her as a dependent, dentistry and orthodontic bills dated in 2018 and 2021 through 2022, and immunization records from 2017, 2021, and 2023 together with a September 2023 high school transcript identifying her father as her parent and listing a common address for them. In addition, the Applicant submitted a letter from her father and an affidavit from her mother stating that she resides with her father under his primary care. Her mother states that she came to the United States as a visitor, became ill shortly after arrival, remained for medical care, and agreed with the Applicant's father that the Applicant should continue residing with him; she states that legal and physical custody have rested with the father since the Applicant arrived in the United States in 2016. Her father likewise states that he has lived with the Applicant and has been primarily responsible for her care since she came to the United States and her mother became ill. The record also includes a Form I-864A submitted by the father promising to support the Applicant.
statutes:
The record reflects that the Applicant was born in Nigeria in 2005 to married noncitizen parents. Her parents divorced in Nigeria in 2015. The Applicant's father naturalized as a U.S. citizen in January 2020 when the Applicant was 14 years old. There is no evidence that her mother is a U.S. citizen. The Applicant was admitted to the United States as a B-2 visitor in 2016, and adjusted status to lawful permanent residency in April 2022 when she was 16 years old. To determine whether the Applicant derived U .S. citizenship from her father we apply "the law in effect at [the] time the critical events giving rise to eligibility occurred." Minasyan v. Gonzales, 401 F.3d 1069, 1075 (9th Cir. 2005). We apply section 320 of the Act because the qualifying events in this case all occurred after that section went into effect in 2001. Section 320 of the Act provides, in pertinent part, that: (a) A child born outside of the United States automatically becomes a citizen of the United States when all of the following conditions have been fulfilled:

(1) At least one parent of the child is a citizen of the United States, whether by birth or naturalization. (2) The child is under the age of eighteen years. (3) The child is residing in the United States in the legal and physical custody of the citizen parent pursuant to a lawful admission for permanent residence. The term "legal custody" means "responsibility for and authority over a child." 8 C.F.R. § 320.1. If the child's parents are divorced, U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) will find a U.S. citizen parent to have legal custody of a child where there has been an award of primary care, control, and maintenance of a minor child to a parent by a court of law or other appropriate government entity pursuant to the laws of the state or country ofresidence. 8 C.F.R. § 320.1(2). USCIS will also consider a U.S. citizen parent who has been awarded "joint custody," to have legal custody of a child. Id. There may be other factual circumstances under which USCIS may find the U.S. citizen parent to have legal custody for purposes of section 320 of the Act. Id. Because the Applicant was born abroad, she is presumed to be a noncitizen and bears the burden of establishing her claim to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. Matter ofBaires­ Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467,468 (BIA 2008).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (case specifics)
birth_year(2005).
parents_divorced_year(2015).
father_naturalized_year(2020).  % father naturalized January 2020
mother_is_us_citizen(false).
child_admitted_b2_year(2016).
lpr_year(2022).                % adjusted status to lawful permanent resident April 2022

% Boolean evidence facts (use arity 0 for true/false predicates)
mother_affidavit_present.
father_letter_present.
medical_records_document_mother_ill.
tax_returns_father_head_of_household_2020_2022.
dental_and_orthodontic_bills_present.
immunization_records_present.
high_school_transcript_2023_present.
pastor_letter_confirming_father_care.
joint_bank_account_2023_present.
form_i864a_submitted_by_father.
father_signed_i485_and_t693.
g325a_lists_fathers_address.

% Residency fact
child_resided_in_us_since(2016).

% Helper predicates: pieces of residence evidence (arity 1 items to be collected)
residence_piece(immunization) :- immunization_records_present.
residence_piece(school_transcript) :- high_school_transcript_2023_present.
residence_piece(dental_bills) :- dental_and_orthodontic_bills_present.
residence_piece(g325a) :- g325a_lists_fathers_address.
residence_piece(pastor_letter) :- pastor_letter_confirming_father_care.
residence_piece(bank_account) :- joint_bank_account_2023_present.

% Helper predicates: pieces of financial/support evidence
financial_piece(tax_returns) :- tax_returns_father_head_of_household_2020_2022.
financial_piece(i864a) :- form_i864a_submitted_by_father.
financial_piece(father_signed_forms) :- father_signed_i485_and_t693.
financial_piece(bank_account) :- joint_bank_account_2023_present.

% Evaluate whether the father was a U.S. citizen at the time the child became an LPR
father_citizen_at_time_of_lpr :-
    father_naturalized_year(NatY),
    lpr_year(LPRY),
    NatY =< LPRY.

% Child under 18 at time of LPR
child_under_18_at_lpr :-
    birth_year(BY),
    lpr_year(LPRY),
    Age is LPRY - BY,
    Age < 18.

% Lawful admission for permanent residence exists if an LPR year is recorded
lawful_admission_for_permanent_residence :-
    lpr_year(_).

% Child was residing in the United States at time of LPR
residing_in_us_at_lpr :-
    child_resided_in_us_since(Year),
    lpr_year(LPRY),
    Year =< LPRY.

% Determine whether there is sufficient residence evidence: require at least two distinct residence pieces
evidence_of_residence :-
    findall(Piece, residence_piece(Piece), Pieces),
    length(Pieces, N),
    N >= 2.

% Determine whether there is sufficient financial/support evidence: require at least two distinct financial pieces
evidence_of_financial_support :-
    findall(Piece, financial_piece(Piece), Pieces),
    length(Pieces, N),
    N >= 2.

% Determine whether factual circumstances support that the father has legal and physical custody.
% Because the divorce order did not explicitly award custody, we rely on other factual circumstances:
% require (1) mother's affidavit and medical records documenting inability to provide care,
% (2) at least two residence proof items showing the child lived with the father, and
% (3) at least two financial/support items showing father exercised financial responsibility.
legal_and_physical_custody_of_father :-
    mother_affidavit_present,
    medical_records_document_mother_ill,
    evidence_of_residence,
    evidence_of_financial_support.

% Eligibility: all conditions under Section 320 must be satisfied
eligibility_met :-
    father_citizen_at_time_of_lpr,
    child_under_18_at_lpr,
    lawful_admission_for_permanent_residence,
    residing_in_us_at_lpr,
    legal_and_physical_custody_of_father.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The applicant is a citizen of Honduras who was granted derivative U nonimmigrant status on April 2, 2014, and admitted to the United States on September 20, 2014, with that status initially valid through September 30, 2017. He filed Form I-539 to extend his U status and was granted an extension from October 1, 2017, until March 6, 2020. He submitted his Form I-485 (application to adjust status) on April 27, 2021. The Vermont Service Center director denied the Form I-485, finding that the record did not show the applicant continued to hold U nonimmigrant status at the time he filed.

On appeal, the applicant explains that he was unable to file his adjustment application timely because his U status expired during the COVID-19 pandemic. He states that during the pandemic he was largely confined to his home, that it took him a long time to find employment that would allow him to file the application, and that the disruptions of the pandemic caused him to forget the filing deadline. He asks that pandemic-related leniencies extended by government agencies be applied in his case and emphasizes the severe effect the decision will have on him and his mother, who is a victim of a crime.
statutes:
U.S. Citizenship
and Immigration Services (USCIS) may adjust the status of a U nonimmigrant to that of an LPR ifthe applicant establishes, among other requirements, that they were admitted to the United States as a U nonimmigrant. Section 245(m)(l) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(b)(2)(i). The applicant must also demonstrate that they continue to hold such status at the time of application for adjustment of status. 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(b )(2)(ii).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the petitioner and dates (dates as date(Year,Month,Day)).
petitioner_citizen(honduras).

% The petitioner was granted derivative U nonimmigrant status on April 2, 2014,
% and admitted to the United States as a U nonimmigrant on September 20, 2014.
granted_u_date(date(2014,4,2)).
admitted_as_u_date(date(2014,9,20)).

% Periods the petitioner's U status was valid:
% initial validity through September 30, 2017 (starting at admission date)
status_period(date(2014,9,20), date(2017,9,30)).

% Extension granted from October 1, 2017 until March 6, 2020
status_period(date(2017,10,1), date(2020,3,6)).

% The petitioner filed Form I-485 (U adjustment application) on April 27, 2021.
i485_filed(date(2021,4,27)).

% The Vermont Service Center director denied the I-485 for failing to establish
% continued U nonimmigrant status at the time of filing.
director_denied.
appeal_argument_pandemic_excuse.
appeal_argument_hardship_for_petitioner_and_mother.

% For true/false / yes/no predicates use arity 0 as required
% The petitioner was admitted as a U nonimmigrant (true).
admitted_as_u.

% Helper to convert a date/1 term to a numeric value for comparison
date_value(date(Y,M,D), N) :-
    N is Y*10000 + M*100 + D.

% Check whether a given date falls within any status_period (inclusive)
date_within_any_status(Date) :-
    status_period(Start, End),
    date_value(Start, S),
    date_value(End, E),
    date_value(Date, D),
    D >= S,
    D =< E.

% The applicant must demonstrate that they continue to hold U nonimmigrant status
% at the time of filing the adjustment application.
% This predicate is arity 0 as required: it succeeds only if the filing date falls
% within a valid U-status period.
continue_to_hold_status_at_filing :-
    i485_filed(FileDate),
    date_within_any_status(FileDate).

% Eligibility is met only if admitted_as_u and continue_to_hold_status_at_filing
eligibility_met :-
    admitted_as_u,
    continue_to_hold_status_at_filing.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-

    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Petitioner seeks classification as a special immigrant juvenile. In 2023, when he was 17 years old, a Texas district court issued an order in a suit affecting the parent-child relationship that included findings related to his SIJ eligibility, appointed his brother as sole managing conservator, and stated that reunification with his mother was not viable because of abandonment and neglect and was not in his best interest. Based on that order, he filed his SIJ petition in February 2024. In June 2024, the Director issued a request for evidence seeking additional proof that the order included a determination that it was not in the Petitioner's best interest to be returned to his or his parent's country of nationality or last habitual residence. The Petitioner responded with another copy of the order and a letter asserting that the required determination was already included, but the Director denied the petition after concluding that the record did not show that the order contained that return-related best-interest determination. On appeal, the Petitioner again submitted the order and argues that, read as a whole, it includes the necessary finding. He points to language stating that, for the reasons listed under the order's abuse, abandonment, and neglect findings, it was not in his best interest to be reunified with his mother at that time, and he notes that the order indicates that his mother resides in Guatemala. The order, as described in the record, also contains factual statements that he was left without provision and necessary care, was left in a situation exposing him to substantial risk of harm without necessary care being arranged, and that his mother demonstrated an intent not to return.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b), (c)(l). Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Id. The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii) ofthe Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(2). SIJ classification may only be granted upon the consent of the Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) , through U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS), when the petitioner meets all other eligibility criteria. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(iii) of the Act. The petitioner must also establish that the request for SIJ classification is bona fide, which requires showing that a primary reason the required juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse,

neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under State law. Section lO l ( a)(27)(J)(i)-(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(b)(5).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

% Facts derived from the provided case description
unmarried.
age_under_21.

% District court SIJ order and its contents

order_appointed_brother_sole_managing_conservator.
order_found_abandonment_and_neglect.
mother_resides_in_guatemala.
order_references_mother_residence.
order_includes_best_interest_phrase.  % "it is not in the best interest ... to be reunified with [his mother] at this time"

% Administrative history (for context; not required for eligibility rules below)
rfe_issued_june_2024.
rfe_response_submitted.

% Rules reflecting statutory/regulatory eligibility criteria for SIJ classification

% The juvenile court placed the petitioner in the custody of an individual appointed by the court

% The record must contain a judicial determination that it is not in the petitioner's best interest to return.

bona_fide :-
    order_found_abandonment_and_neglect.

% Aggregate eligibility: all required elements for SIJ eligibility (as modeled here) must be present.
% Per the instructions, eligibility_met represents "all the conditions necessary to Accept the case."
eligibility_met :-

text:
The petitioner is a social worker who seeks employment-based immigrant classification in the EB-2 category and a national interest waiver of the job-offer requirement. She intends to work as a social and community service manager by establishing and operating a nonprofit organization in Central Florida focused primarily on mental health services and improving access to benefits for the Hispanic community. The record shows she holds an advanced degree and that the Texas Service Center previously made a determination on her petition.

In support of her petition and appeal, the petitioner submitted a business proposal and documentation of her academic credentials, certificates, and training; expert evaluations of her educational credentials; records of her prior work experience in social work; details of proposed projects for the NGO; and other materials related to the organization, including social media posts and evidence of volunteer activities.

On appeal, the petitioner argues that her proposed endeavor will have a significant impact, asserting that she will replicate successful models used by other organizations, provide training and education to community members, and produce positive outcomes in areas she identifies such as economic growth, social inclusion, and youth mental health support. She also emphasizes a nationwide shortage of social workers and contends that professionals with her expertise are needed. The petitioner appeals the denial of the national interest waiver and asserts that her proposed work and credentials demonstrate the impact and positioning necessary to carry out the endeavor.
statutes:
To qualify for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, a petitioner must establish they are an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b)(2)(A) of the Act. If a petitioner establishes eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then demonstrate that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: 1 See Flores v. Ga rland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D .C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver is discretionary in nature).

• The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id. The first prong, substantial merit and national importance, focuses on the specific endeavor that the individual proposes to undertake. The endeavor's merit may be demonstrated in a range of areas such as business, entrepreneurialism, science, technology, culture, health, or education. In determining whether the proposed endeavor has national importance, we consider its potential prospective impact. Id.. at 889. The second prong shifts the focus from the proposed endeavor to the individual. To determine whether they are well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor, we consider factors including, but not limited to: their education, skills, knowledge and record ofsuccess in related or similar efforts; a model or plan for future activities; any progress towards achieving the proposed endeavor; and the interest of potential customers, users, investors, or other relevant entities or individuals. Id. at 890. The third prong requires a petitioner to demonstrate that, on balance, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification. In performing this analysis, we may evaluate factors such as: whether, in light of the nature of the individual's qualifications or the proposed endeavor, it would be impractical either for them to secure a job offer or to obtain a labor certification; whether, even assuming that other qualified U.S. workers are available, the United States would still benefit from their contributions; and whether the national interest in their contributions is sufficiently urgent to warrant forgoing the labor certification process. In each case, the factor(s) considered must, taken together, establish that on balance, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements ofa job offer and thus of a labor certification. Id. at 890-91.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts extracted from the record
submitted_evidence.
business_proposal.
academic_credentials.
training_certificates.
prior_work_experience.
expert_evaluation.
proposed_projects_described.
social_media_posts.
volunteer_activities.
addresses_nationwide_problem.    % petitioner noted nationwide shortages of social workers and youth mental health crisis
intent_local_community.          % primary focus is Central Florida Hispanic community
replication_of_other_models_claim.
% Negative/absence facts inferred from the record and the denial (matters not established in the file)
no_existing_partnerships.
no_current_funding.
no_record_scaling_projects.
no_demonstrated_outcomes.
no_impracticality_labour_certification_shown.
no_urgent_national_need_shown.

% Underlying EB-2 classification: advanced degree professional or individual of exceptional ability.
% The petitioner submitted academic credentials and an expert evaluation of those credentials.
advanced_degree_or_exceptional_ability :-
    academic_credentials,
    expert_evaluation.

% Dhanasar prong 1: the proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance.
prong1_substantial_merit_national_importance :-
    substantial_merit,
    national_importance.

% Substantial merit: health/mental health work and proposed projects suffice to show merit.
substantial_merit :-
    proposed_projects_described.
substantial_merit :-
    addresses_nationwide_problem.

% National importance requires prospective impact beyond a purely local or regional scope.
% It is satisfied where the record shows a credible plan for national reach or scaling,
% partnerships/funding for broader deployment, or demonstrable prospect of producing
% outcomes with national impact. The record shows only a local focus and replication claims
% without evidence of partnerships or funding, so national_importance is not established.
national_importance :-
    addresses_nationwide_problem,
    credible_plan_for_national_scale.

credible_plan_for_national_scale :-
    replication_of_other_models_claim,
    has_partnerships_or_funding,
    documented_past_scaling_success.

% Auxiliary predicates that would establish national scale, not present here.
has_partnerships_or_funding :-
    \+ no_existing_partnerships,
    \+ no_current_funding.

documented_past_scaling_success :-
    \+ no_record_scaling_projects,
    \+ no_demonstrated_outcomes.

% Dhanasar prong 2: the individual is well-positioned to advance the proposed endeavor.
prong2_well_positioned :-
    has_education_skills_and_experience,
    model_for_future_activities,
    progress_toward_endeavor,
    interest_of_stakeholders.

has_education_skills_and_experience :-
    academic_credentials,
    training_certificates,
    prior_work_experience.

model_for_future_activities :-
    business_proposal,
    proposed_projects_described.

progress_toward_endeavor :-
    volunteer_activities;
    social_media_posts.

% Interest of stakeholders (customers/users/investors/partners) must be shown by documented demand,
% commitments, letters of support indicating adoption, or similar evidence. The record lacks that.
interest_of_stakeholders :-
    \+ no_existing_partnerships.  % true only if partnerships exist

% Dhanasar prong 3: on balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
prong3_balance_beneficial :-
    impracticality_of_labour_certification;
    benefit_even_if_us_workers_available;
    urgent_national_interest.

impracticality_of_labour_certification :-
    \+ no_impracticality_labour_certification_shown.

benefit_even_if_us_workers_available :-
    % would require evidence that petitioner's unique qualifications materially supplement US workforce
    unique_contribution_documented,
    \+ no_demonstrated_outcomes.

unique_contribution_documented :-
    % record would need to show qualifications or methods that are meaningfully distinct;
    % not established here (no_record_scaling_projects and lack of documented outcomes)
    \+ no_record_scaling_projects,
    \+ no_demonstrated_outcomes.

urgent_national_interest :-
    \+ no_urgent_national_need_shown.

% Eligibility: EB-2 classification AND all three Dhanasar prongs must be met.
eligibility_met :-
    advanced_degree_or_exceptional_ability,
    prong1_substantial_merit_national_importance,
    prong2_well_positioned,
    prong3_balance_beneficial.

% Entrypoint and decision output (must be last)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The Petitioner is a company that sells shaligrams and other Hindu religious items and services, primarily through online retail. It filed Form I-129 to extend the Beneficiary’s temporary employment in the United States as its chief executive officer (CEO) under an L-1A classification. On the Form I-129 filed November 29, 2022, the Petitioner indicated it had one employee; the record and the Petitioner acknowledge that the Beneficiary was the company’s sole U.S. employee at the time of filing. The company submitted a September 2021 business plan prepared for a prior new-office petition that identified four “key business personnel” expected to staff the U.S. operation and projected first‑year salaries for those positions.

At filing the Petitioner provided two lists of the Beneficiary’s duties. One list allocated his time across categories (policy making and strategy, department meetings and interaction with managers and executives, management reporting and board/high-level meetings, networking, supervising senior management and executives, and hiring and firing managers and executives). A second list included tasks more operational in nature, such as examining merchandise for pricing and display, supervising employees engaged in sales or inventory-taking, designing store layout, organizing product displays and store openings, and training supporting staff on accounting and administrative functions. The Petitioner also submitted an organizational chart showing the Beneficiary as the direct supervisor of a chief advisor, a financial analyst, an IT specialist, a sales specialist, and an e-commerce specialist, but noted these individuals were “identified as potential candidates” and had not been hired as of the filing date.

The Director issued a request for evidence (RFE) asking for a more detailed description of duties and additional evidence regarding staffing and organizational structure. In response, the Petitioner submitted a revised position description stating that many start‑up duties had been completed during the first year (setting goals, planning budgets, promoting products, coordinating with the foreign entity, handling merchandise, planning recruiting and hiring, and preparing training materials). The Petitioner described the Beneficiary’s proposed duties under the extension as falling into categories labeled “strategic leadership,” “financial management,” “human resources management,” and “marketing and sales.” The Petitioner explained that its initial hiring plan had been delayed by labor shortages, inflation, pandemic-related visa processing delays, and by the Beneficiary being outside the United States for nearly two months in early 2023.

The Petitioner further stated that it had filled the finance manager, sales manager, IT consultant, and e-commerce developer positions as of January 1, 2023—identifying the same individuals who had been named in the September 2021 business plan. In support, the Petitioner submitted a new organizational chart and revised position descriptions for subordinate staff, IRS Form 941 showing five employees for the first quarter of 2023, employee pay statements, IRS Forms 1099‑MISC evidencing payments to a CPA and an attorney in 2022, and an amended Form I-129 page showing the company’s current number of employees as “5” instead of “1.” The record also shows that the four newly hired employees were residents of Nebraska while the Petitioner’s business is in Indiana.

At the time of filing, the record includes evidence that the Beneficiary reported nearly $340,000 in gross receipts on his IRS Form 1040, Schedule C for 2022 and that the company conducted sales activity in 2022 while staffed only by the Beneficiary. On appeal, the Petitioner submitted additional evidence about its products and argued that the Director’s decision placed undue emphasis on the company’s staffing level at the time of filing; the Petitioner asserted the Director did not give sufficient weight to the Beneficiary’s job duties or to the Petitioner’s explanations for why the office remained unstaffed at the end of its first year. These arguments and the additional evidence are the basis for the Petitioner’s appeal.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for the L-lA nonimmigrant visa classification, a qualifying organization must have employed the beneficiary "in a capacity that is managerial, executive, or involves specialized knowledge," for one continuous year within three years preceding the beneficiary's application for admission into the United States. Section 101(a)(15)(L) of the Act. In addition, the beneficiary must seek to enter the United States temporarily to continue rendering his or her services to the same employer or a subsidiary or affiliate thereof in a managerial or executive capacity. Id. 1 The Petitioner previously filed a "new office" petition on the Beneficiary's behalf which was approved for a one-year period beginning on December 22, 2021. A "new office" is an organization that has been doing business in the United States through a parent, branch, affiliate, or subsidiary for less than one year. 8 C.F.R. § 214.2(l)(l)(ii)(F). The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 214.2(1)(3)(v)(C) allows a "new office" operation one year within the date of approval of the petition to support an executive or managerial position.

A petitioner seeking to extend an L-1 A petition that involved a new office must submit a statement of the beneficiary's duties during the previous year and under the extended petition; a statement describing the staffing of the new operation and evidence of the numbers and types of positions held; evidence of its financial status; evidence that it has been doing business for the previous year; and evidence that it maintains a qualifying relationship with the beneficiary's foreign employer. 8 C.F.R. § 214.2(1)(14)(ii).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

text:
The petitioner is a citizen of Honduras. In 2021, when he was 17 years old, the Juvenile Court of [redacted] Tennessee issued an order appointing his aunt as his sole legal guardian. That order stated he was placed in his aunt’s custody, that it was not in his best interests to be returned to Honduras, and that reunification with his parents “is not viable due to the age of the minor child and the violence in Honduras.” In response to a request for additional evidence, the petitioner submitted a second juvenile-court order, issued in 2021 after he turned 18, which found that reunification with his parents “is not viable due to the child’s age.” The underlying petition for guardianship had requested that the court specifically find reunification was not possible “due to [their] abandonment of the minor child,” but the court orders did not include that language.

The petitioner submitted, on appeal to the administrative appeals office, a brief and additional materials including copies of previously submitted documents, copies of state-court SIJ decisions, excerpts from the juvenile-court hearing transcript, a brief filed on appeal of the juvenile court’s first order, his mother’s affidavit, and news articles concerning the juvenile court judge. In the hearing transcript the judge is recorded as saying, “I cannot find abandonment.” The petitioner filed an appeal of the juvenile court’s first order in part because the judge did not find abandonment. On appeal to the administrative appeals office, the petitioner contends that, even though the juvenile court did not use the words “abandonment” or “neglect,” the facts found by the court, the hearing transcript, the second order, and his mother’s affidavit show he was abandoned and neglected by his parents and therefore could not reunify with them. The petitioner also submitted that the second order was issued after he turned 18 and was not issued nunc pro tunc, and he did not indicate how the juvenile court retained jurisdiction over him as a juvenile.
statutes: To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101 (a)(27)(J)(i) ofthe Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.11 (b). Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 10l(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(c)(l). The record must also contain ajudicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 10l(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(c)(2).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

% stated reunification not viable due to petitioner's age and violence in Honduras (no explicit "abandonment" or "neglect").

% Legal rule: to be eligible for SIJ classification the juvenile court must determine reunification with
% one or both parents is not viable due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law.
% We model that requirement as an explicit finding in the juvenile-court orders containing language
% that mentions abandonment/neglect/abuse or an explicit statutory-equivalent phrase.
%
% Detect an explicit statutory-style finding by searching order reason atoms for keywords 'abandon', 'neglect', or 'abuse'.

    (   juvenile_order2_reason(R2),

        )

% Eligibility: all elements required by statute / regulation modeled here must be true.
% - unmarried
% - under_21

% - judicial determination it is not in petitioner's best interest to return
% - juvenile court made the required reunification-not-viable finding due to abuse/neglect/abandonment or similar
eligibility_met :-
    unmarried,
    under_21,
    placed_in_custody_of_aunt,
    best_interest_not_to_return,
    court_found_abandonment_or_neglect_or_abuse.

decision(Result) :-

    ).

text:
The applicant was born in Iraq in 2010 to married foreign-national parents. He was admitted to the United States as a refugee in November 2016 at age six. His mother later became a U.S. citizen through naturalization in April 2022. The applicant’s birth certificate shows his biological relationship to his mother, and he remains under 18 years of age.

The applicant filed Form N-600 seeking a Certificate of Citizenship to reflect that he derived U.S. citizenship from his mother. USCIS records show his Form I-485 (Application to Adjust Status) was denied in November 2018 because his Form I-693 (Report of Medical Examination and Vaccination Record) was incomplete. The applicant confirms that he has not adjusted status to lawful permanent resident. The field office denied his Form N-600, and he appealed that denial. On appeal the applicant does not dispute that he has not held lawful permanent resident status; he states there was a misunderstanding of the I-485 requirements due to a language barrier and submits a new, complete Form I-693 for consideration.
statutes:
The applicable law for derivative citizenship purposes is "the law in effect at the time the critical events giving rise to eligibility occurred." See Minasyan v. Gonzales, 401 F.3d 1069, 1075 (9th Cir. 2005). In the present matter, the Applicant was born in Iraq in I 12010, to married foreign national parents. The Applicant's mother became a U.S. citizen in April 2022 and his father became a U.S. citizen in August 2022. Accordingly, section 320 of the Act, as amended by the Child Citizenship Act of 2000, Pub. L. No. 106-395, 114 Stat. 1631 (Oct. 30, 2000) (CCA) , which became effective on February 27, 2001, applies to his case. The CCA repealed former section 321 of the Act and amended , in part, former section 320 of the Act. The amendments to section 320 of the Act took effect on February 27, 2001, and apply to individuals who satisfy the requirements of section 320 of the Act, as in effect on that date. Matter ofRodriguez­ Tejedor, 23 I&N Dec. 153, 157 (BIA 2001).

Section 320 of the Act, as amended in Febrnary 2001 and currently in effect, provides that: (a) A child born outside of the United States automatically becomes a citizen of the United States when all of the following conditions have been fulfilled: (1) At least one parent of the child is a citizen of the United States, whether by birth or naturalization. (2) The child is under the age of eighteen years. (3) The child is residing in the United States in the legal and physical custody ofthe citizen parent pursuant to a lawful admission for permanent residence. Moreover, applicants must meet the definition of a "child" in section l0l(c)(l) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § ll0l(c)(l), which defines the term "child" in pertinent part to mean "an unmarried person under twenty-one years of age and includes a child legitimated under the law of the child's residence or domicile, or under the law of the father's residence or domicile, whether in the United States or elsewhere[.]" The applicant must have either a biological or legal adoptive relationship with the claimed U.S. citizen parent. See Matter of Guzman-Gomez, 24 I&N Dec. 824, 826 (BIA 2009) ( determining that "child" as defined under section 101 ( c) of the Act encompasses biological or legal adoptive children, but not stepchildren). A child born in wedlock is considered to be a legitimate child. See Matter ofKubicka, 14 I&N Dec. 303, 304 (BIA 1972) ("A 'legitimate' child is, of course, a child 'born in wedlock' ....") (quoting Webster's New Dictionary, 1971). Applicants born abroad are presumed to be foreign nationals and bear the burden of establishing their claim to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. See Matter ofBaires-Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467, 468 (BIA 2008). The "preponderance of the evidence" standard requires that the Applicant establish that their claim is "probably trne," based on the specific facts of their case. See Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. 369,376 (AAO 2010) (citing Matter ofE-M-, 20 I&N Dec. 77, 7980 (Comm'r 1989)).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

text:
The petitioner is a personal finance and entrepreneurship consultant who sought EB-2 immigrant classification with a request for a national interest waiver. She reports more than 18 years of experience in the accounting field, holding roles described as Founder/CEO, Contracts Consultant, Tax Auditor, Tax Analyst, and Accounting Analyst. Her most recent position was as the Founder/CEO of a Brazil-based company specializing in tax services, accounting, auditing, and consulting. The petitioner states that she is a board member of the Brazilian‑American Chamber of Commerce and that she volunteers with the IRS Volunteer Income Tax Assistance (VITA) program.

The petitioner proposed to establish a U.S. company, C-F-A-LLC, of which she would be the 100% owner and serve as Chief Executive Officer. According to her business plan and cover letters, the company would provide accounting, tax, contract management, and financial consulting services to U.S. clients, and would aim to educate individuals, entrepreneurs, and small- and medium-sized enterprises on financial and tax matters to support their financial health, long-term development, and competitiveness. She described plans to analyze financial information and develop financial strategies for clients, to hire and train staff over time, and to contribute to job creation and tax revenue. The petitioner included projected tax payments of $86,910 in year one and $217,999 in year five. She also stated an intention to work in and serve some Opportunity Zones and other underprivileged areas, and to promote trade and investment between Brazil and the United States.

In support of her petition, she submitted documentation including her education and experience records, a personal statement, a business plan, an expert opinion letter, letters of recommendation and support, industry reports, and articles. After receiving a Request for Evidence, she filed a response with a cover letter, an updated resume, supplemental employer letters, educational documentation, and additional support letters. The record also contains an email showing that she applied for an accounting assistant position and that the employer sought to schedule an interview; the petitioner’s filings include both that job‑search evidence and the business‑formation materials.

On appeal, the petitioner argued that the prior adjudicative findings contained “innumerous erroneous conclusion[s] of law or statement[s] of fact.” She asserted that she is an expert in accounting and taxation with comprehensive industry knowledge, strategic skills, and the ability to optimize processes to improve financial health and operational efficiency for client companies. She maintained that her business plan sufficiently described her proposed endeavor, reiterated that her company would provide services and education to clients and SMEs, asserted that her work would create qualified industry professionals and jobs, and contended that her activities would support economic objectives such as tax generation and promotion of U.S.–Brazil trade. She also argued that her volunteering with VITA and plans to serve economically distressed communities were relevant to the merits of her petition.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. Next, a petitioner must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. at 889, provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion,1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well -positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and 1 See also Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) Uoining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS ' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver to be discretionary in nature).

• On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

expert_opinion.                 % Expert opinion letter submitted
letters_of_support.             % Letters of recommendation/support submitted
financial_projections_present.  % Revenue and tax projections included (yr1 -> yr5)
applied_for_employment_conflict. % Email shows application for "Accounting Assistant" position (conflict)

strong_supporting_evidence :- expert_opinion, letters_of_support, financial_projections_present.

qualified_for_eb2 :- has_advanced_degree, has_relevant_experience.

% Dhanasar prong: substantial merit and national importance
meets_substantial_merit_national_importance :-
    endeavor_has_merit,
    endeavor_has_national_importance.

% Dhanasar prong: well-positioned to advance the endeavor
well_positioned :-
    qualified_for_eb2,
    credible_business_plan,
    strong_supporting_evidence.

% Dhanasar prong: on balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States
on_balance_benefit_us :-
    expected_job_creation,
    expected_tax_revenue,
    service_to_underserved,
    potential_trade_promotion.

% Eligibility requires all Dhanasar prongs and underlying EB-2 qualification
eligibility_met :-
    qualified_for_eb2,
    meets_substantial_merit_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    on_balance_benefit_us.

    ).

text:
The applicant is a noncitizen who was convicted in 1995 of attempted Criminal Possession of Stolen Property in the third degree. He was placed in removal proceedings in 2003 and an Immigration Judge ordered his removal in 2004. In 2015, Immigration and Customs Enforcement joined in a joint motion to reopen and terminate those proceedings before the Board of Immigration Appeals, and the Board granted that motion, which allowed the applicant to pursue adjustment of status and to file a Form I-601, Application for Waiver of Grounds of Inadmissibility. The applicant does not dispute that he was found inadmissible based on that conviction.

The applicant filed a Form I-601 seeking a waiver so he could adjust status to lawful permanent resident. The field office director denied the waiver, noting the applicant had not submitted documentation of hardship for the applicant’s spouse, who had been listed as the sole qualifying relative on the Form I-601. In November 2018 the applicant received a Request for Evidence instructing him to provide an affidavit and supporting documentation addressing whether a qualifying relative would suffer hardship if the waiver were denied.

The applicant appealed the director’s denial and submitted additional evidence on appeal. He provided a written brief; medical documentation for his spouse; a psychological report covering himself and his wife; a statement from his son; and evidence that his spouse meets the medical requirements for Social Security Disability benefits. The applicant also submitted documentation from the Social Security agency, provided after the waiver denial, indicating his spouse’s monthly disability payment will be $1,232.80 (an annualized amount of $14,793.60). He also submitted statements from his spouse and son attesting to his good character and the record reflects he has had a clean record since the 1995 conviction and that more than 15 years had passed since the criminal activity.

In his appeal the applicant asks that the new evidence be considered and contends that, taken together, the hardship to his spouse and his two U.S. citizen sons would constitute extreme hardship if the waiver is not granted.
statutes: A noncitizen convicted of (or who admits having committed, or who admits committing acts which constitute the essential elements of) a crime involving moral turpitude (other than a purely political offense) or an attempt or conspiracy to commit such a crime is inadmissible. Section 212(a)(2)(A)(i) of the Act. Individuals found inadmissible under section 212(a)(2)(A)(i) of the Act may seek a discretionary waiver of inadmissibility under section 212(h) of the Act. Where the activities resulting in inadmissibility occurred more than 15 years before the date of the application, a waiver is available if admission to the United States would not be contrary to the national welfare, safety, or security of the United States, and the noncitizen has been rehabilitated. Section 212(h)(l)(A) of the Act. A discretionary waiver is also available if denial of admission would result in extreme hardship to a U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident spouse, parent, son, or daughter of the noncitizen applicant. Section 212(h)(l)(B) of the Act. A determination of whether denial of the waiver would result in extreme hardship depends on the facts and circumstances of each case. Matter of Cervantes-Gonzalez, 22 l&N Dec. 560, 565 (BIA 1999). We recognize that some degree of hardship to qualifying relatives is present in most cases; however, to be considered "extreme," the hardship must exceed that which is usual or expected. See Matter of Pilch, 21 l&N Dec. 627, 630-31 (BIA 1996) (finding that factors such as economic detriment, severing family and community ties, loss of current employment, and cultural readjustment were the "common result of deportation" and did not alone constitute extreme hardship). In determining whether extreme hardship exists, individual hardship factors that may not rise to the level of extreme must also be considered in the aggregate. Matter of lge, 20 l&N Dec. 880, 882 (BIA 1994). An applicant may show extreme hardship in two scenarios: 1) if the qualifying relative remains in the United States separated from the applicant, and 2) if the qualifying relative relocates overseas with the applicant. See generally 9 USCIS Policy Manual B.4(B), https://www.uscis.gov/legal­ resources/policy-memoranda. Demonstrating extreme hardship under both scenarios is not required ifthe applicant's evidence demonstrates that one ofthese scenarios would result from the denial ofthe waiver. See id. The applicant may meet this burden by submitting a statement from the qualifying relative certifying under penalty of perjury that the qualifying relative would relocate with the applicant, or would remain in the United States, if the applicant is denied admission. See id. In the present case, the record is clear that the Applicant's spouse would remain in the United States, where she must care for her 86-year-old mother who is "a heart patient with many medical needs," if the Applicant's waiver application is denied. The Applicant's wife says that [to] separate from [her] husband would be traumatic." Therefore, the Applicant may establish eligibility under section 212(h)(l)(B) of the Act by showing that if he is denied admission, his spouse would experience extreme hardship upon separation. The Applicant's two sons have not indicated whether they would relocate or remain in the United States. In either case, demonstrating rehabilitation or demonstrating extreme hardship, the Applicant must show that the waiver should be granted as a matter of discretion, with favorable factors outweighing the unfavorable factors. Section 212(h) of the Act. 2

text:
The petitioner is a company that provides cable, Internet, and telephone service and that operates a free, ad-supported streaming television (FAST) platform built into several smart television brands and available as an app on others. The petitioner employs the beneficiary as a vice president in charge of that streaming service. The beneficiary earned a Master of Business Administration in Japan in 2008 and previously worked in various positions at a related company from 2000 to 2014. He became chief operating officer of another related entity in 2014 soon after its founding, and when the petitioner acquired the streaming platform in 2020 he began serving as the petitioner’s vice president. The beneficiary is present in the United States on an O-1 nonimmigrant visa for individuals with extraordinary ability.

The record describes the beneficiary’s duties as leading and overseeing business development for the streaming platform and managing all facets of contract development, negotiation, and closeout with over 150 content partners. The petitioner submitted contracts and license agreements that the beneficiary signed on the platform’s behalf, letters from industry executives crediting him as “a key figure” in creating and maintaining content relationships, and statements from the platform’s president and chief financial officer asserting that the beneficiary was essential to the platform’s success and future growth. A former chief executive officer (now a senior vice president) described the beneficiary as one of the leading core executives since the platform’s initial conception and as the driving force behind many partnerships with major smart television brands. The record also includes company plans and forecasts and documentation of the beneficiary’s ongoing efforts to secure content and broaden distribution. The platform and its products are shown in the record to have won national industry awards in 2018 and 2020 and to reach more than 20 million monthly active users.

The petitioner appealed the Nebraska Service Center director’s denial of the Form I-140 petition. The director denied the petition on the basis that the record did not establish that the beneficiary’s proposed work met the director’s standards for national significance and related criteria, and had previously stated in a request for evidence that the petitioner had not described the importance of the beneficiary’s role as the platform’s vice president. On appeal, the petitioner contends the director did not fully consider or explain the evidence submitted and challenges the director’s stated deficiencies in the record.
statutes:
To qualify for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first show the beneficiary's eligibility for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. Once a petitioner demonstrates the beneficiary's EB-2 eligibility, they must then establish that the beneficiary merits a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that USCIS may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: 1 See Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts, and Third in an unpublished decision, in concluding that USCIS' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver is discretionary).

• The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.

text:
The Petitioner is a registered nurse and entrepreneur in aesthetic and cosmetic nursing who filed an immigrant petition seeking EB-2 classification as a professional holding an advanced degree and requesting a national interest waiver of the job offer requirement. She proposed to establish and operate a medical clinic in New York that would provide specialized services including treatment of skin lesions of arterial or venous origin, peeling treatments, tattoo removals, diabetic foot care, wound care and dressing changes, burn treatments, stoma bag changes, home nursing consultations, basic dressing-change courses, and teleconsultations.

At the time of the administrative proceedings, the Director had determined the Petitioner met the EB-2 advanced-degree profession requirement. In support of her request for a national interest waiver, the Petitioner submitted a business plan and an economic impact study projecting first-year revenues of $760,354 to $1,140,532, net profits of $431,181 to $676,132, and economic-output and earnings estimates (the study estimated outputs of $1,816,962 to $5,450,888, earnings of $707,510 to $2,122,530, and projected job creation of about 36.37 jobs in year one, 45.05 jobs in year five, and roughly 203 jobs over five years per $1,000,000 invested). She stated she would initially staff the clinic with one nurse and expand the team as demand grows. The economic impact study also asserted indirect benefits to sectors such as real estate, rental and leasing, finance and insurance, professional and technical services, administrative support, and waste management.

The Petitioner also submitted articles and industry reports on aesthetic nursing, nurse education, healthcare spending, diabetes care, and nursing entrepreneurship; four executive orders relating to prevention, health promotion, and public health; a study from the American Journal of Public Health; and an American Association of Colleges of Nursing report and Department of Labor commentary addressing nursing shortages. She described plans to share her knowledge through lectures and training for patients, students, colleagues, and other U.S. healthcare workers and argued her experience as a nurse entrepreneur in wound care and aesthetics—including a graduation certificate, nursing diploma, certificates in health systems and hospital management, transcripts, a resume, employment verification letters, a Brazilian nursing license, and recommendation letters—position her to contribute to U.S. nursing practice.

The Petitioner appealed the Director’s denial of the national interest waiver. In her appeal she contended that she will work autonomously through her company (making a separate job offer unnecessary), that she submitted documentation demonstrating economic benefits at filing and in response to a request for evidence, and that the Director abused discretion and failed to exercise due diligence by not adequately considering the evidence she provided or recognizing the national significance of her proposed endeavor.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act."Advanced degree" means any U.S. academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of baccalaureate. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A U.S. baccalaureate degree or a foreign equivalent degree followed by five years ofprogressive experience in the specialty shall be considered the equivalent of a master's degree. Id."Profession" means one of the occupations listed in section 101(a)(32) of the Act, 8 U .S.C. § 1101(a)(32), 1 as well as any occupation for which a U.S. baccalaureate degree or its foreign equivalent is the minimum requirement for entry into the occupation. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). 1 Profession shall include, but not be limited to, architects, engineers, lawyers, physicians, surgeons, and teachers in elementary or secondary schools, colleges, academics, or seminaries. Section 101 ( a)(32) of the Act.

Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or an individual of exceptional ability, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion2, grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id. The first prong, substantial merit and national importance, focuses on the specific endeavor that the individual proposes to undertake. The endeavor's merit may be demonstrated in a range ofareas, such as business, entrepreneurialism, science, technology, culture, health, or education. In determining whether the proposed endeavor has national importance, we consider its potential prospective impact. Id. The second prong shifts the focus from the proposed endeavor to the individual. To determine whether they are well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor, we consider factors including, but not limited to: their education, skills, knowledge and record of success in related or similar efforts; a model or plan for future activities; any progress towards achieving the proposed endeavor; and the interest of potential customers, users, investors, or other relevant entities or individuals. Id. at 890. The third prong requires a petitioner to demonstrate that, on balance, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification. In performing this analysis, we may evaluate factors such as: whether, in light of the nature of the individual's qualifications or the proposed endeavor, it would be impractical either for them to secure a job offer or to obtain a labor certification; whether, even assuming that other qualified U.S. workers are available, the United States would still benefit from their contributions; and whether the national interest in their contributions is sufficiently urgent to warrant forgoing the labor certification process. In each case, the factor(s) considered must, taken together, establish that on balance, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements ofa job offer and thus of a labor certification. Id. at 890-91.

label: Dismissed
