```prolog
% Parsed Administrative Appeals Office case into Prolog.
% Program evaluates eligibility for EB-2 National Interest Waiver (NIW).
% The output is binary: 'Accepted' or 'Dismissed'.
% The logic encodes the statutory/precedential framework (Dhanasar)
% and the factual record provided. Where the record lacks confirmatory
% evidence for required legal showings, the corresponding predicates fail,
% resulting in a Dismissed decision.

% ---------- Facts (0-arity predicates) ----------
% Petitioner background and proposed endeavor
petitioner_registered_nurse.
nationality_brazil.
proposes_clinic_new_york.
endeavor_healthcare.          % proposed endeavor is in health (aesthetic/wound care)

% Submitted documentary evidence (existence of documents)
evidence_graduation_certificate.
evidence_nursing_diploma.
evidence_transcripts.
evidence_nursing_license_brazil.
evidence_certificates.
evidence_resume.
evidence_employment_verification.
evidence_recommendation_letters.
evidence_business_plan.
evidence_economic_impact_study.
evidence_articles_reports.
evidence_executive_orders.

% Arguments on appeal
argues_autonomy.
argues_economic_benefits_documented.

% Profession threshold (nursing is a profession where a baccalaureate is a typical entry)
profession_requires_baccalaureate.

% ---------- EB-2 (underlying classification) ----------
% Advanced degree equivalency: either a master's or foreign equivalent, or
% a baccalaureate plus five years progressive experience.
has_master_degree :- fail.  % no documentary evidence of an advanced degree above baccalaureate

has_baccalaureate :-
    evidence_nursing_diploma,
    evidence_transcripts.

% Five years of progressive experience is not established in the record
five_years_progressive_experience :- fail.

advanced_degree_equivalent :-
    has_master_degree.
advanced_degree_equivalent :-
    has_baccalaureate,
    five_years_progressive_experience.

% Exceptional ability in sciences, arts, or business is not established by the record
exceptional_ability :- fail.

% Underlying EB-2 eligibility requires either advanced degree equivalence or exceptional ability
underlying_eb2 :-
    advanced_degree_equivalent.
underlying_eb2 :-
    exceptional_ability.

% ---------- Dhanasar framework (national interest waiver) ----------
% First prong: substantial merit and national importance
% Merit: healthcare/wound care/aesthetic nursing is an area of potential substantial merit
evidence_demonstrates_merit :-
    endeavor_healthcare,
    evidence_articles_reports.    % articles and industry reports support the field's merit

% National importance requires prospective impact beyond a local setting;
% mere local clinic proposals and generalized economic projections are insufficient absent evidence of broader reach.
% The record includes an economic impact study and a business plan, but lacks corroborating evidence of prospective
% nationwide or sector-wide impact (investor commitments, multi-site plans, contracts with regional/national entities, etc.).
has_nationwide_impact_support :- fail.

evidence_demonstrates_national_importance :-
    evidence_economic_impact_study,
    evidence_business_plan,
    has_nationwide_impact_support.

substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    evidence_demonstrates_merit,
    evidence_demonstrates_national_importance.

% Second prong: the individual is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor
% Factors considered: education, skills, record of success, a model or plan, progress toward the endeavor, and interest of customers/users/investors.
evidence_of_education_and_skills :-
    evidence_nursing_license_brazil,
    evidence_certificates,
    evidence_resume.

evidence_of_model_or_plan :-
    evidence_business_plan.

% Progress toward achieving the endeavor (e.g., executed leases, investments, contracts) is not shown
progress_toward_endeavor :- fail.

interest_of_potential_customers_or_investors :- fail.

well_positioned :-
    evidence_of_education_and_skills,
    evidence_of_model_or_plan,
    progress_toward_endeavor,
    interest_of_potential_customers_or_investors.

% Third prong: on balance, waiving the job offer/labor certification requirement would benefit the United States
% Considerations include impracticality of obtaining a job offer/labor certification, added value beyond available US workers, urgency, etc.
% The petitioner argues autonomy and submitted economic projections, but the record does not establish impracticality, uniqueness, or urgency sufficient to outweigh labor certification protections.
evidence_of_impracticality_for_job_offer :- fail.
unique_contribution_over_us_workers :- fail.
urgent_national_interest :- fail.

benefit_on_balance :-
    evidence_of_impracticality_for_job_offer;
    unique_contribution_over_us_workers;
    urgent_national_interest.

% ---------- Eligibility combination ----------
% All required conditions must hold to Accept the petition.
eligibility_met :-
    underlying_eb2,
    substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    benefit_on_balance.

% ---------- Decision and runtime entrypoint (as required) ----------
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner states that he met his wife in May 2018 at a mutual friend’s birthday party in Queens. He says he was attracted to her, they dated—going out to dinner and shopping—and the relationship became serious in October 2018. He describes proposing in late October 2018 and marrying in November 2018 with a friend and the wife’s mother and friend as witnesses. After the wedding, the petitioner says his wife and her son moved in with him. He recalls that the early months of the marriage were good, with weekend outings to the park and restaurants, and that they shared holidays such as a Valentine’s Day dinner and exchanged gifts.

According to the petitioner, the relationship began to deteriorate in April 2019 when his wife started arguing with him, and she ultimately left in August 2019. He explains that he bought a life insurance policy in August 2019 naming his wife as beneficiary and continued to make payments on it because he hoped they would reconcile and wanted to provide for her and her son if anything happened to him. He also says he planned to add his wife to his lease but the landlord would not accept her as a co-renter because she was unemployed. The petitioner reports that it took him months to accept that his wife would not return and that he tried to reconcile after the separation.

As part of his filing, the petitioner submitted a jeweler’s receipt for rings dated November 2018; joint bank account statements addressed to him and his wife for June through September 2019; a life insurance policy purchased in August 2019 naming his wife as beneficiary; copies of the petitioner’s and his wife’s 2018 federal and state income tax returns filed as married filing jointly; photographs of the petitioner, his wife, and her son on various occasions; and, after a request for evidence, additional photographs. He also provided letters from three friends who stated they knew of the marriage and the subsequent marital difficulties: one friend said he had interacted with the wife at lunches or dinner parties and thought the couple seemed lovely; a second friend said he attended the wedding and occasionally observed the couple supporting each other after marriage; and a third friend said he went to the couple’s home for dinner once and observed that they complemented each other. The petitioner additionally submitted his 2020 tax return showing he filed married filing separately and copies of his individual bank statements dated after the separation.

The petitioner appealed the service center’s denial of his petition. He states that he appealed because the director concluded the record lacked probative details showing he entered the marriage in good faith; on appeal he argues that his friends’ statements, his original affidavit, and the additional eleven‑page affidavit he submitted on appeal provide detailed, probative information about how he met his wife, their courtship, his proposal, their married life, and his intentions in marrying her.
statutes: A petitioner may self-petition for immigrant classification as the abused spouse of a U.S. citizen if the petitioner establishes, in part, that they entered the marriage in good faith. Section 204(a)(l)(A)(iii)(l)(aa) of the Act. Evidence of entry into the marriage in good faith may include documents showing that one spouse has been listed as the other's spouse on insurance policies, property leases, income tax forms, or bank accounts; evidence regarding their courtship, wedding ceremony, shared residence, and experiences; birth certificates of any children born during the marriage; police, medical, or court documents providing information about the relationship; affidavits from individuals with personal knowledge of the relationship; and any other credible evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(i), (vii). We consider any credible evidence relevant to the petition, and the determination of what evidence is credible, and the weight given such evidence lies within our sole discretion. Section 204(a)(l)(J) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(i).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
met_in_may_2018.
relationship_serious_by_oct_2018.

celebrated_valentines.

tax_return_2020_filed_married_separately.
individual_bank_statements_after_sep.
eleven_page_affidavit.
planned_to_add_wife_to_lease_but_landlord_refused.

% Legal rule: evidence relevant to establishing entry into marriage in good faith.
% Listed evidentiary categories (paraphrased from statute/regulation).
evidence_type(tax_return_joint) :- tax_returns_2018_joint.
evidence_type(joint_account) :- joint_bank_statements_jun_sep_2019.
evidence_type(insurance_listing_spouse) :- life_insurance_policy_aug_2019_naming_wife.

% Corroboration: presence of at least one documentary evidence item listed by regulation
% constitutes probative support for entry into the marriage in good faith.
documentary_evidence_present :-
    evidence_type(tax_return_joint)
    ;
    evidence_type(joint_account)
    ;
    evidence_type(insurance_listing_spouse)
    ;
    evidence_type(wedding_evidence)
    ;
    evidence_type(shared_residence).

% Additional corroboration: testimonial and photographic evidence strengthen the record.
testimonial_and_photo_evidence_present :-
    evidence_type(affidavits),
    evidence_type(photos).

% A marriage entered in good faith may be established by documentary evidence OR
% by a combination of testimonial/photo evidence together with at least some
% documentary corroboration (consistent with 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)).
good_faith_marriage :-
    documentary_evidence_present.
good_faith_marriage :-
    testimonial_and_photo_evidence_present,
    (   evidence_type(wedding_evidence)
    ;   evidence_type(shared_residence)
    ;   evidence_type(joint_account)
    ;   evidence_type(insurance_listing_spouse)
    ;   evidence_type(tax_return_joint)
    ).

% Presence of separation or later filings alone does not automatically rebut
% a showing of good faith (court/agency discretion). Only explicit evidence of
% immigration fraud would negate good faith; no such evidence is present in the facts.
no_evidence_of_immigration_fraud.

% Eligibility: for the present simplified model, establishing entry into the marriage
% in good faith is a necessary condition to accept the petition.
eligibility_met :-
    good_faith_marriage,
    no_evidence_of_immigration_fraud.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner is a Venezuelan national who filed Form I-821 seeking Temporary Protected Status. On her initial application she (or the preparer) answered that she was a member of the Communist party and in a supplement indicated that this membership was tied to a relative who was a congressman. In response to a request for evidence, the petitioner stated she had never been a member of the Communist party, explained that the person who prepared her initial application made an error by answering “yes,” and said she had been required to join the Partido Socialista Unido de Venezuela (PSUV) in Venezuela. The petitioner also explained that she joined the PSUV to continue providing essentials to neighbors and described that the preparer confused involuntary PSUV membership with membership in a Communist party. The Texas Service Center denied the I-821 on the ground that she was inadmissible as a member of a totalitarian/Communist party. She appealed the denial, arguing that the decision lacked a detailed explanation for finding her RFE response inadequate, that the denial relied on incorrect factual assumptions from the initial preparer’s error, and that she never knowingly or willingly joined the Communist party and therefore would not have applied if she had been a willing member.
statutes:
Generally, a noncitizen is ineligible for TPS if they are inadmissible to the United States as an immigrant. Section 244(c)(l)(A) of the Act. Section 212(a)(3)(D)(i) of the Act states that "[a]ny immigrant who is or has been a member of or affiliated with the Communist or any other totalitarian party ( or subdivision or affiliate thereof), domestic or foreign, is inadmissible." Section 212(a)(3)(D)(ii) of the Act provides an exception to inadmissibility for involuntary membership. It states: Clause (i) shall not apply to an alien because of membership or affiliation if the alien establishes to the satisfaction of the consular officer when applying for a visa ( or to the satisfaction of the Attorney General when applying for admission) that the membership or affiliation is or was involuntary, or is or was solely when under 16

years of age, by operation of law, or for purposes of obtaining employment, food rations, or other essentials of living and whether necessary for such purposes.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
petitioner_venezuelan.
filed_i821.
initial_answer_member_communist.
supplement_explained_relative_congressman.
rfe_response_never_member_communist.
rfe_response_preparer_error.
rfe_response_compelled_join_psuv.
director_denied_inadmissible_totalitarian.

on_appeal_explained_preparer_confusion.
appeal_relative_not_communist_congressman.
appeal_never_member_communist.
appeal_psuv_compelled_to_provide_essentials.
appeal_would_not_have_applied_if_willing_member.
appeal_denial_lacked_detailed_explanation.

% The Service treated the ruling party (PSUV) as a totalitarian party for purposes of inadmissibility
psuv_is_totalitarian_party.

% Determine actual membership in a Communist party.
% The petitioner initially answered "yes" for Communist membership, but later denied membership.
% We treat actual membership in a Communist party as established only if there is no contrary
% denial on the record.
actual_member_of_communist_party :-
    initial_answer_member_communist,
    \+ rfe_response_never_member_communist,
    \+ appeal_never_member_communist.

% Determine actual membership in other totalitarian (non-Communist) party (e.g., PSUV).
% The record shows the petitioner was required to join PSUV while in Venezuela.
actual_member_of_other_totalitarian_party :-
    psuv_is_totalitarian_party,
    (   rfe_response_compelled_join_psuv
    ;   appeal_psuv_compelled_to_provide_essentials
    ).

% A person is a member of a totalitarian party if member of Communist or other totalitarian party.
member_of_totalitarian_party :-
    actual_member_of_communist_party.
member_of_totalitarian_party :-
    actual_member_of_other_totalitarian_party.

% Exception for involuntary membership or membership for purposes of obtaining essentials.
% The statute excepts membership that is involuntary or solely for obtaining employment, food rations,
% or other essentials of living (and whether necessary for such purposes).
involuntary_membership :-
    rfe_response_compelled_join_psuv.
involuntary_membership :-
    appeal_psuv_compelled_to_provide_essentials.

membership_for_essentials :-
    appeal_psuv_compelled_to_provide_essentials.
membership_for_essentials :-
    rfe_response_compelled_join_psuv.

exception_applies :-
    involuntary_membership.
exception_applies :-
    membership_for_essentials.

% An applicant is inadmissible under 212(a)(3)(D) if they are/been a member of a Communist or other
% totalitarian party, unless an exception (involuntary, under 16, by operation of law, or for essentials) applies.
inadmissible_totalitarian_member :-
    member_of_totalitarian_party,
    \+ exception_applies.

% For TPS, generally an applicant is ineligible if inadmissible as an immigrant.
% We therefore consider eligibility met if not inadmissible under the totalitarian-party rule.
eligibility_met :-
    \+ inadmissible_totalitarian_member.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is the applicant’s father, a U.S. citizen by birth, who filed Form N-600K seeking a Certificate of Citizenship on his child’s behalf. The child is under 18 and the father included documentation of the child’s biological relationship to him (a birth certificate). The petitioner listed the same residential address in Ireland for himself and the child on the Form N-600K and submitted evidence of mail and an Irish government document delivered to the child at that address. On appeal the petitioner states the child continues to live with him at that Ireland address and he provided an additional letter from Ireland’s Department of Social Protection dated April 2024 addressed to the child at that same address.

To support his claim of custodial authority, the petitioner submitted a 2013 Irish District Court “Order Appointing a Father to Be a Guardian” pursuant to the Guardianship of Infants Act, 1964, which names him as joint guardian of the child together with the mother. He also submitted an affidavit from S‑J‑, a Peace Commissioner in Ireland, attesting that the 2013 court order grants the petitioner joint guardianship and awarded joint custody that provides him with the day‑to‑day care and home life of the child. 

The petitioner appealed the denial of the Form N-600K, renewing his request for issuance of a Certificate of Citizenship and submitting the additional evidence described above to show that the child resides with him in his legal and physical custody outside the United States.
statutes:
The record reflects that the Applicant was born in Ireland inl 12007, to unmarried parents. The Applicant's father was born in the United States (Illinois) and is a U.S. citizen, and his paternal grandfather became a naturalized U.S. citizen in December 1971 and resided in the United States from 1953 to 1973. There is no evidence to indicate that the Applicant's mother is a U.S. citizen, and he seeks derivative citizenship only through his father and with his paternal grandfather's physical presence. Section 322 of the Act (as amended by the Child Citizenship Act of 2000, Pub. L. No. 106-395, 114 Stat. 1631 (Oct. 30, 2000), applies to children who, like the Applicant were born and reside outside of the United States. It provides, in pertinent part that:

(a) A parent who is a citizen of the United States ... may apply for naturalization on behalf of a child born outside of the United States who has not acquired citizenship automatically under section 320 [of the Act], [8 U.S.C. § 1432] 1 .... [and] the [Secretary of Homeland Security] shall issue a certificate of citizenship to such applicant upon proof ... that the following conditions have been fulfilled: (1) At least one parent ... is a citizen of the United States, whether by birth or naturalization. (2) The United States citizen parent (A) has ... been physically present in the United States or its outlying possessions for a period or periods totaling not less than five years, at least two of which were after attaining the age of fourteen years; or (B) has ... a citizen parent who has been physically present in the United States or its outlying possessions for a period or periods totaling not less than five years, at least two of which were after attaining the age of fourteen years. (3) The child is under the age of eighteen years. (4) The child is residing outside of the United States in the legal and physical custody of the [ citizen parent]. ... (5) The child is temporarily present in the United States pursuant to a lawful admission, and is maintaining such lawful status.... Regulations at 8 C.F.R. § 322.1 provide that, for section 322 of the Act purposes, the term "child" means a person who meets the requirements of section IOI(c) of the Act; 8 U.S.C. § 1IOI(c). Section 101 (c) of the Act defines the term "child," in pertinent part, as "an unmarried person under twenty­ one years of age and includes a child legitimated under the law of the child's residence or domicile, or under the law of the father's residence or domicile, whether in the United States or elsewhere[.]" If the application for Certificate of Citizenship is approved, and after the applicant takes the oath of allegiance (unless the oath is waived), U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) will issue a Certificate of Citizenship. The child is a citizen as of the date of approval and administration of the oath of allegiance. 8 C.F.R. § 322.S(a). 1 Section 320 of the Act provides, in pertinent part, that a child born outside of the United States automatically becomes a citizen of the United States if: (1) at least one parent of the child is a citizen of the United States, whether by bi1ih or naturalization; (2) the child is under the age of eighteen years; and (3) the child is residing in the United States in the legal and physical custody of the citizen parent pursuant to a lawful admission for pe1manent residence. 2

Because the Applicant was born abroad, he is presumed to be a noncitizen, and his father bears the burden of establishing eligibility for a Certificate of Citizenship on the Applicant's behalf by a preponderance of credible evidence. See Matter ofBaires-Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467, 468 (BIA 2008).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text:
The Obligor signed an Immigration Bond (Form I-352) on January 27, 2022, as the sole obligor on a $5,000 delivery bond, agreeing to deliver the bonded individual to ICE and to comply with the bond’s conditions. The bonded individual entered the United States around December 13, 2022, was taken into ICE custody, and was later released on the $5,000 bond. The bonded individual was subsequently ordered removed by the Immigration Court after failing to appear for a hearing. On November 9, 2023, ICE mailed the Obligor a Form I-340, Notice to Obligor to Deliver Alien, requesting delivery of the bonded individual on December 11, 2023; the record includes a green USPS return receipt showing the Obligor signed for that mailing on November 14, 2023. The Obligor did not produce the bonded individual on the requested delivery date, and on December 19, 2023 ICE declared the bond breached and mailed a Form I-323, Notice of Immigration Bond Breached.

In his appeal, the Obligor asserted that he never received the Form I-340 and submitted evidence indicating the bonded individual had entered Canada on January 31, 2023 and been granted protected status by the Immigration and Refugee Board of Canada, valid from January 31, 2023 to January 31, 2027. Based on those facts, the Obligor appealed the bond-breach finding.
statutes: A delivery bond creates a contract between the U .S. Government and an obligor. United States v. Minn. Tr. Co., 59 F.3d 87, 90 (8th Cir. 1995); Matter ofAllied Fid. Ins. Co ., 19 I&N Dec. at 125. An obligor secures its promise to deliver a bonded foreign national by paying a designated amount in cash or its equivalent. 8 C.F.R. § 103.6(d). A breach occurs upon substantial violation of a bond's conditions. 8 C.F.R. § 103.6(e). Conversely, substantial performance of a bond's conditions releases an obligor from liability. 8 C.F.R. § 103.6(c)(3). Several factors inform whether a bond violation is substantial: the extent of the violation; whether it was intentional or accidental; whether it was in good faith; and whether the obligor took steps to comply with the terms of the bond. Matter ofKubacki, 18 I&N Dec. 43, 44 (Reg'l Comm'r 1981) (citing Int'! Fidelity Ins. Co. v. Crosland, 490 F. Supp. 446 (S.D.N.Y. 1980)); see also Aguilar v. United States, 124 Fed. CL 9, 16 (2015).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (arity 0 used for yes/no predicates)
obligor_signed_bond.
bond_amount_5000.
bonded_national_turkish.
bonded_national_ordered_removed_after_failed_appear.
form_i340_mailed_on_2023_12_11.
usps_return_receipt_present.
usps_return_receipt_signature_on_2023_11_14.
obligor_did_not_deliver_on_2023_12_11.
ice_declared_bond_breached_on_2023_12_19.
i323_mailed_to_obligor_on_2023_12_19.
bonded_national_entered_canada_on_2023_01_31.
bonded_national_granted_protected_status_effective_2023_01_31_through_2027_01_31.
obligor_asserts_never_received_i340.

% Evidentiary rule: a USPS return receipt with an original signature is strong evidence of receipt.
received_i340 :-
    usps_return_receipt_present,
    usps_return_receipt_signature_on_2023_11_14.

% Fact about presence/absence on the delivery date:
% The delivery date is 2023-12-11. The bonded national entered Canada on 2023-01-31
% and was granted protected status effective that date through 2027-01-31.
% Therefore the bonded national was not present in the United States on the delivery date.
bonded_person_absent_on_delivery_date :-
    bonded_national_entered_canada_on_2023_01_31,
    bonded_national_granted_protected_status_effective_2023_01_31_through_2027_01_31,
    form_i340_mailed_on_2023_12_11.

% Legal rules (simplified to the factors relevant to this appeal)
% A breach occurs upon substantial violation of the bond's conditions.
% Substantial performance (or impossibility of performance due to absence of the alien)
% releases an obligor from liability.

% If the bonded person was absent from the United States on the required delivery date,
% the obligor could not be required to deliver the bonded individual and the obligor
% is released from liability (eligibility to overturn breach).
eligibility_release_for_absence :-
    obligor_signed_bond,
    bonded_person_absent_on_delivery_date.

% Even if the obligor contends nonreceipt, an official USPS return receipt showing acceptance
% may establish that the obligor received notice. That influences whether the obligor had notice,
% but does not override the impossibility defense if the alien was not in the United States.
% (Both predicates are present so they can be considered; the presence of receipt does not
% prevent release when delivery was impossible.)

% eligibility_met means conditions necessary to Accept the appeal are satisfied.
% We accept (overturn the breach) if the obligor is released due to absence of the bonded person.
eligibility_met :-
    eligibility_release_for_absence.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Petitioner is a computer scientist specializing in medical imaging and computer vision who filed a Form I-140 petition seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability. The Texas Service Center Director denied the petition, and the matter is now on appeal. Before the Director, the agency determined that the Petitioner met criteria relating to judging and authorship of scholarly articles, but not the criterion concerning original contributions of major significance. On appeal, the Petitioner maintains that he satisfies the contributions criterion. The record includes evidence concerning his citation record and multiple support letters, including additional letters submitted in response to a request for evidence. In his appeal brief, the Petitioner presented OpenAlex data comparing his research impact with that of other researchers in computer science and asserted that his citation record placed him among the top 0.11% or 0.14% of researchers in that field. The OpenAlex material in the record was undated, and the Petitioner's graph reflected publications between 2015 and 2023, including his work through an unspecified point in 2023. The submitted material also reflected that he set the field parameter as "computer science," although OpenAlex includes a more specific category, "Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition," and the record did not explain that choice or how the cited percentile figures were calculated. The support letters described the Petitioner's research as credible, relevant, and broadly applicable. One letter stated that his record demonstrated an ability to produce credible and relevant research for the United States and the computer science field, and another stated that his pancreatic-cancer detection methodology was vital to the United States and had applications across medical imaging beyond pancreatic cancer. Additional letters asserted that two projects relied on his published work, that his automatic segmentation algorithms were being integrated into a private company's PACS system, and that his software had been implemented in a hospital radiation treatment planning system to help delineate tumor volumes and organs at risk for treatment planning.
statutes:
To qualify under this immigrant classification, the statute requires the filing party demonstrate: • The foreign national enjoys extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics; • They seek to enter the country to continue working in the area of extraordinary ability; and • The foreign national's entry into the United States will substantially benefit the country in the future. Section 203(b)(1 )(A)(i)-(iii) of the Act. The term "extraordinary ability" refers only to those individuals in "that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2).

The implementing regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) sets forth a multi-part analysis. First, a petitioner can demonstrate international recognition of his or her achievements in the field through a one-time achievement (that is, a major, internationally recognized award). If that petitioner does not submit this evidence, then he or she must provide sufficient qualifying documentation that meets at least three of the ten criteria listed at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x) (including items such as awards, published material in certain media, and scholarly articles). Where a petitioner meets these initial evidence requirements, we then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination and assess whether the record shows sustained national or international acclaim and demonstrates that the individual is among the small percentage at the very top of the field of endeavor. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115, 1121 (9th Cir. 2010) ( discussing a two-part review where the documentation is first counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination); see also Amin v. Mayorkas, 24 F.4th 383, 394 (5th Cir. 2022).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the petitioner (arity-0 predicates for true/false)
petitioner_has_foreign_bachelors.
petitioner_has_masters.
petitioner_has_phd.
petitioner_research_area_machine_and_deep_learning.
petitioner_research_application_medical_imaging_and_treatment_planning.

% Evidence related facts
there_is_major_international_award :- fail.  % no one-time major award in record

% Regulatory criteria: two were accepted by the director; contributions criterion was not accepted.
criterion_judging.
criterion_authorship.
criterion_contributions :- fail.  % director did not accept contributions-of-major-significance

% Petitioner-submitted materials
openalex_metrics_claim.                        % petitioner asserts top-percentile metrics
openalex_data_includes_post_filing_date.       % OpenAlex materials include data through 2023 (after 2022 filing)
letters_support_contributions_partial.         % letters describe originality and some implementations
letters_show_fieldwide_adoption :- fail.       % letters vary; no clear field-wide adoption shown

% Evaluation helpers
% Count how many of the regulatory criteria the record supports
count_criteria(N) :-
    ( criterion_judging -> I1 = 1 ; I1 = 0 ),
    ( criterion_authorship -> I2 = 1 ; I2 = 0 ),
    ( criterion_contributions -> I3 = 1 ; I3 = 0 ),
    N is I1 + I2 + I3.

meets_three_criteria :-
    count_criteria(N),
    N >= 3.

% Initial evidence requirement satisfied if either a major award exists or at least three criteria are met
meets_initial_evidence :-
    ( there_is_major_international_award
    ; meets_three_criteria
    ).

% Final merits determination requires consideration of all evidence.
% The record must show sustained national or international acclaim and that the individual is among the small percentage at the very top of the field.
% Here, because the petitioner relies on OpenAlex metrics that include post-filing data and supporting letters that vary in scope, the record does not establish sustained acclaim or top-of-field status.
sustained_acclaim :- fail.
among_top_small_percentage :- fail.

final_merits :-
    meets_initial_evidence,
    sustained_acclaim,
    among_top_small_percentage.

% Extraordinary ability is established only by the final merits determination.
extraordinary_ability :-
    final_merits.

% The petitioner intends to continue working in the field (stated in the record)
intends_to_continue_work.

% Substantial benefit requires the final merits showing
substantial_benefit :-
    final_merits.

% Eligibility is met only if all three statutory conditions are satisfied
eligibility_met :-
    extraordinary_ability,
    intends_to_continue_work,
    substantial_benefit.

% Entrypoint and decision structure (must be last)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a man who was granted U nonimmigrant status as the qualifying family member of his daughter after she was sexually assaulted when she was a minor. He received an extension of that U status and filed Form I-485 (adjustment of status) before his U status expired. The record includes that he was arrested on a recent assault charge; that criminal case was later dismissed, and the petitioner completed anger-management courses. He has provided an order of dismissal, an incident report, court docket entries, and declarations from himself, his spouse, and his daughter. His spouse (the listed victim) stated she attended court dates and explained the case’s resolution; the petitioner’s former counsel submitted a letter about the voluntary nature of the anger-management courses. The petitioner has also submitted letters of support from family and friends and updated family declarations on appeal.

The petitioner is appealing the denial of his adjustment application and subsequent denials of motions to reopen/reconsider. He contends the decision gave undue weight to adverse factors while ignoring or insufficiently considering his positive equities and family ties; he argues the Director failed to properly review and credit the court documentation showing the assault charge was dismissed (and that no fines or penalties were imposed because the case was dismissed); and he emphasizes the hardship his removal would cause his daughter and granddaughter (the daughter conceived a child as a result of the assault) and the other minor child he currently helps raise. On appeal he submitted additional and updated evidence intended to address the adverse factors and support his claim of family unity and hardship.
statutes:
U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may adjust the status of a U nonimmigrant to that of an LPR if they meet all other eligibility requirements and, "in the opinion" of USCIS , their"continued presence in the United States is justified on humanitarian grounds, to ensure family unity, or is otherwise in the public interest." Section 245(m) of the Act. Applicants must also establish that discretion should be exercised in their favor; USCIS may consider all relevant factors in making its discretionary determination. 8 C.F.R. §§ 245.24(b)(6), (d)(l 1). A favorable exercise of discretion to grant an applicant adjustment of status to that of an LPR is generally warranted in the absence of adverse factors and presence of favorable factors. Matter of Arai, 13 I&N Dec . 494, 496 (BIA 1970). Favorable factors include, but are not limited to, family unity, length ofresidence in the United States, employment, community involvement, and good moral character. Id.; see also 7 USCIS Policy Manual A.10(B)(2), https://www.uscis.gov/policy-manual

(providing guidance regarding adjudicative factors to consider in discretionary adjustment of status determinations). However, where adverse factors are present, an applicant may submit evidence establishing mitigating equities. See 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(d)(ll) (providing that, "[w]here adverse factors are present, an applicant may offset these by submitting supporting documentation establishing mitigating equities that the applicant wants USCIS to consider when determining whether or not a favorable exercise of discretion is appropriate").
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (arity 0 predicates for true/false)
% Petitioner U-status and filing facts
u_status_granted.
u_status_extension_granted.
filed_i485_before_u_expired.

% Director action
director_denied_discretionary.
motions_to_reopen_and_reconsider_denied.

% Positive factors noted by the Director or submitted on appeal
family_unity.                % strong family ties, caregiving roles
financial_support.           % petitioner provides financial support to family
employment_history.          % petitioner has steady employment history
letters_of_support.          % letters from family and friends
caregiving_roles.            % raised granddaughter as his own child; raising another minor US-citizen child
hardship_to_minors.          % separation would cause emotional/psychological harm to daughter and granddaughter

% Negative factors identified by the Director
prior_immigration_history.   % adverse prior immigration history
arrest_for_assault.          % recent arrest for assault (as cited by Director)

% Mitigating evidence submitted on appeal
disposition_provided.        % final disposition (court order of dismissal) submitted on appeal
charges_dismissed.           % the assault charges were dismissed (court order)
anger_management_completed.  % petitioner completed anger management courses
anger_management_voluntary.  % completion was voluntary per former counsel
spouse_explains_no_involvement. % spouse/victim explained she appeared for court dates and case dismissed without her involvement

% Other legal eligibility assumptions (we treat as met for the discretionary analysis)
other_eligibility_requirements_met.

% Weighting scheme for discretionary balancing (numeric scoring)
% Positive factor weights:
pos_weight_family_unity(2).
pos_weight_financial_support(1).
pos_weight_employment_history(1).
pos_weight_letters_of_support(1).
pos_weight_caregiving_roles(2).
pos_weight_hardship_to_minors(1).

% Negative factor weights:
neg_weight_prior_immigration_history(2).
neg_weight_arrest_for_assault(3).
neg_weight_motions_denied(0).  % motions denied noted but de minimis for scoring in this model

% Mitigation maximums (how much mitigation can offset negatives)
mitigate_weight_charges_dismissed(3).     % can fully offset arrest weight
mitigate_weight_anger_management(1).
mitigate_weight_voluntary_course(1).
mitigate_weight_spouse_statement(1).

% Compute positive score by summing weights for present positives
positive_score(Score) :-
    (family_unity -> pos_weight_family_unity(A) ; A = 0),
    (financial_support -> pos_weight_financial_support(B) ; B = 0),
    (employment_history -> pos_weight_employment_history(C) ; C = 0),
    (letters_of_support -> pos_weight_letters_of_support(D) ; D = 0),
    (caregiving_roles -> pos_weight_caregiving_roles(E) ; E = 0),
    (hardship_to_minors -> pos_weight_hardship_to_minors(F) ; F = 0),
    Score is A + B + C + D + E + F.

% Compute base negative score from adverse factors
negative_score_base(Base) :-
    (prior_immigration_history -> neg_weight_prior_immigration_history(A) ; A = 0),
    (arrest_for_assault -> neg_weight_arrest_for_assault(B) ; B = 0),
    (motions_to_reopen_and_reconsider_denied -> neg_weight_motions_denied(C) ; C = 0),
    Base is A + B + C.

% Compute mitigation total based on submitted mitigating evidence
mitigating_total(Total) :-
    (charges_dismissed -> mitigate_weight_charges_dismissed(A) ; A = 0),
    (anger_management_completed -> mitigate_weight_anger_management(B) ; B = 0),
    (anger_management_voluntary -> mitigate_weight_voluntary_course(C) ; C = 0),
    (spouse_explains_no_involvement -> mitigate_weight_spouse_statement(D) ; D = 0),
    Total is A + B + C + D.

% Final negative score after applying mitigation (cannot be negative)
negative_score(Final) :-
    negative_score_base(Base),
    mitigating_total(M),
    Temp is Base - M,
    ( Temp < 0 -> Final = 0 ; Final = Temp ).

% Eligibility logic:
% USCIS may grant adjustment if: petitioner meets other eligibility requirements,
% filed timely while U status valid, and discretionary balancing yields favorable result.
% We model favorable result as positive_score >= negative_score after mitigation.
eligibility_met :-
    u_status_granted,
    filed_i485_before_u_expired,
    other_eligibility_requirements_met,
    positive_score(P),
    negative_score(N),
    P >= N.

% Required output structure (entrypoint)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a citizen of Honduras who entered the United States in 2016 as an A-1 nonimmigrant to work at the Honduran Consulate (posted in Texas). He filed Form I-485 seeking adjustment of status under Section 13 of the 1957 Immigration Act. In a sworn statement and at his application interview, he described witnessing multiple instances of alleged corruption at the consulate: timely monthly allowances being deposited but consular accounts not being paid; being asked by the Consul to participate in clandestine sales of money orders and to sell products outside the consulate; being asked to process a personal bank loan for the Consul and then being reassigned when he refused; employees going unpaid for months and at least one money order being rejected by a bank; the Consul selling consular appointments, stealing money orders and depositing them into his personal account, and selling biosafety supplies at inflated prices. The petitioner says he spoke to a Honduran media outlet about the misconduct, after which the Consul called and threatened that the petitioner and his family “would have problems,” saying the Consul could falsify documents and involve the petitioner in criminal acts. The petitioner also reported the illicit activity to local media “in a protected manner.” In 2020 he was transferred to another Honduran consulate, where he contracted COVID-19.

To support his account, the petitioner submitted five text-message threads with colleagues discussing withdrawals from the consular account, fear about reporting the Consul, a rejected money order, and reference to an ambassador who was removed; a letter from a former colleague describing the Consul’s alleged thefts, pressure to steal funds for personal use, the colleague’s and the petitioner’s resignations, the Consul’s threats of “serious problems” if they reported the misconduct, and their report of the corruption to a supervisor followed by personnel transfers; and several news articles (dated 2005–2020) about corruption, poverty, demonstrations, and emigration in Honduras, including articles about consular corruption that predate the petitioner’s service.

On appeal, the petitioner argued that the adjudicating officer failed to address his evidence, did not adequately specify the reasons for denial, and abused discretion by denying the application without first issuing a Request for Evidence. He also asserted that, because of having witnessed corruption and because of threats, he has effectively been left stateless and faces risk of incarceration or death at the hands of Honduran government members; that he could be erroneously implicated in the Consul’s corrupt acts (the petitioner says the Consul threatened to falsely implicate him); and that he could face harm from the public if he were publicly perceived to have been involved. The petitioner noted that his report to local media was made in a protected manner and did not submit evidence showing he had been publicly identified.
statutes: Section 13 is an adjustment of status category for foreign nationals who can demonstrate, in part: (1) failure to maintain A-1, A-2, G-1, or G-2 nonimmigrant status as of the application's filing date; (2) performance of diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties by the principal on behalf of the accrediting country; and (3) inability, because of compelling reasons, to return to the country that accredited the foreign national. 8 U.S.C. § 1255b(b); 8 C.F.R. § 245.3. 2 Section 13 applicants bear the burden of proving their eligibility to adjust status. Section 291 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1361. 1 Pub. L. No. 85-316, 71 Stat. 642, amended by Pub. L. No. 97-116, 95 Stat. 161 (1981). The A nonimmigrant classification is for diplomats and foreign government officials (principal) as well as their immediate family members. The G nonimmigrant classification is for employees of certain international organizations (principal) and their immediate family members. See https:/ /travel.state.gov. 2 If the first three eligibility requirements are met, applicants must also establish that compelling reasons demonstrate that their adjustment would be in the national interest and would not be contrary to the national welfare, safety, or security of the United States and that they are of good moral character and admissible to the United States. Discussion of these remaining criteria is generally unnecessary in cases where the first three eligibility criteria have not been met.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

text:
The petitioner is a man who has filed multiple VAWA self-petitions over several years. He first filed in 2013 and that petition was denied in 2014. He filed a second self-petition in 2016, which was denied in 2018, and his ensuing appeal was dismissed in 2019. The petition under review is his third, filed in 2020. 

He filed an appeal and submitted a brief. In that brief he reiterated and summarized his prior accounts of his relationship and marriage. He asserts that he entered the marriage in good faith, that he shared a residence with his wife, and that she subjected him to a pattern of violence and abuse—offending, threatening, and humiliating him. He states that seeking mental health care and counseling was necessary for his recovery, and says he has evidence of the counseling and therapy he received.

The petitioner also explains on appeal that documentary proof of a good-faith marriage can be difficult or impossible to provide when abuse is involved and that he submitted all the evidence available to him. He further attributes discrepancies in his prior statements about residence to “human error and memory lapse.” He repeatedly states that he is submitting additional evidence with his appeal—including a supplemental affidavit, a bank account statement, photographs, and records of counseling—but those specific documents were not attached to his appeal. He did not identify particular errors in the previous assessment of the evidence he had earlier submitted.

In sum, the petitioner’s appeal challenges the prior adverse outcome by restating that he married in good faith, lived with his spouse, was subjected to abuse, and by asserting that additional supporting documents (an affidavit, financial statement, photos, and counseling records) accompany his appeal—while also explaining difficulty in producing documentary evidence because of the abuse and attributing inconsistencies to memory lapses.
statutes:
A petitioner who is the spouse of a U.S . citizen may self-petition for immigrant classification if the petitioner married the U.S. citizen spouse in good faith, is eligible for immigrant classification based on that relationship, resided with the spouse, was subjected to battery or extreme cruelty by the spouse, and is a person of good moral character. Section 204(a)(l)(A)(iii) of the Act. A petition will not be approved if the petitioner entered into the marriage for the primary purpose of circumventing the immigration laws. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(l)(ix). A petition will not be denied, however, solely because the petitioner is no longer living with the abuser and the marriage is no longer viable. Id. Evidence of good-faith entry into the marriage may include, but is not limited to, joint insurance policies, property leases, income tax forms, bank accounts, and testimony or other evidence regarding courtship, wedding ceremony, shared residence and experiences. Id. at§ 204.2(c)(2)(vii). Other evidence might include police, medical, or court documents providing information about the relationship, and affidavits of persons with personal knowledge of the relationship. Id.

R EV 3/2024 www.uscis.gov

Residence is the place of general abode, which means a person's principal, actual dwelling place in fact, without regard to intent. Section 101 ( a )(3 3) ofthe Act. Evidence ofshared residence may include employment records, utility receipts, school records, hospital or medical records, deeds, mortgages, rental records, insurance policies, affidavits or any other type of relevant credible evidence of residency. Id. at § 204.2( c )(2)(iii). Battery or extreme cruelty includes being the victim of any act or threatened act of violence, which results or threatens to result in physical or mental injury. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(l)(vi). Psychological or sexual abuse shall be considered acts ofviolence. Id. Other abusive action that are part ofan overall pattern of violence may also qualify. Id. Evidence of battery or extreme cruelty may include reports and affidavits from police, judges and other court officials, medical personnel, school officials, clergy, social workers, and other social service agency personnel. Id. at § 204.2(c)(2)(iv). Proof of non­ qualifying abuse may only be used to establish a pattern of abuse and violence and to support a claim that qualifying abuse also occurred. Id. While we consider any credible evidence relevant to the petition, the determination of what evidence is credible, and the weight given such evidence lies within our sole discretion. Section 204(a)(l)(J) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(i).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
% Good moral character: no adverse facts in the record presented — assume not contested in this appeal

    resided_with_spouse,
    subjected_to_battery_or_extreme_cruelty,

    \+ entered_for_primary_purpose_of_circumventing_immigration.

% Decision and runtime entrypoint (provided structure)

    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

        ),

        handle_undefined(PI)

    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
```

text:
The petitioner was eleven years old in 2023. That year, the Juvenile Court in Nashville, Tennessee issued an Order Appointing Sole Legal Custodian placing the petitioner and her siblings in their mother’s custody and finding it was not in the petitioner’s best interests to be returned to Guatemala, the family’s country of nationality. The court’s order stated that reunification with the children’s respective biological fathers was not viable: it attributed the petitioner’s father’s unavailability to his death and attributed the siblings’ father’s unavailability to his total and complete abandonment of the children.

U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services denied the petitioner’s Special Immigrant Juvenile petition on the ground that the record did not establish a qualifying juvenile-court determination regarding parental reunification; the Director concluded the court’s order did not sufficiently show how the father’s death constituted abandonment under the cited state provision.

On appeal, the petitioner submitted an Amended Order Appointing Sole Legal Custodian, issued in January 2024 nunc pro tunc to the 2023 date of the original order. The amended order explicitly states that, under Tennessee law, reunification with the biological fathers is not viable because the petitioner’s father “abandoned his children by virtue of his death.” The petitioner asserts that the amended order addresses the Director’s concern and provides the necessary factual findings regarding the father’s unavailability and the nonviability of reunification.
statutes: To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b). Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.11 ( c )(1 ). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(2).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
    ).
```
