text:
The petitioner is an auditor who filed Form I-140 seeking employment-based second preference (EB-2) classification as an advanced degree professional and a national interest waiver of the job offer requirement. In support of the petition she submitted multiple items, including articles, studies, and reports about the field of accounting; peer‑reviewed articles; a letter of interest from an employer; documentation that she had reviewed manuscripts for various journals; an expert opinion; and letters of support attesting that she is well‑positioned to advance her proposed work.

The Texas Service Center director denied the petition, concluding the petitioner had not established eligibility for a national interest waiver while noting she qualified as an advanced degree professional and that her proposed endeavor had substantial merit but identifying perceived deficiencies regarding national importance, whether she is well‑positioned to advance the endeavor, and whether waiving the job‑offer requirement would benefit the United States.

The petitioner appealed that denial to the Administrative Appeals Office. In the appeal she argued that the director did not apply factors to her evidence or explain why the evidence was insufficient, and that the director failed to address a large portion of the submitted record—specifically the expert opinion and the letters of support—requesting that all her arguments and evidence be considered.
statutes: If a petitioner establishes eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then demonstrate that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion,1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id. 1 See Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) Uoining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver is discretionary in nature).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
petitioner_is_auditor.
filed_form_i140.
seeking_eb2_advanced_degree.
has_advanced_degree.

submitted_multiple_articles.
submitted_studies_reports.
submitted_peer_reviewed_articles.
submitted_documentation_reviewed_manuscripts.
submitted_expert_opinion.
submitted_letters_of_support.
submitted_letter_of_interest_from_employer.

director_denied.
appealed_reason_inadequate_explanation.
appealed_reason_unaddressed_evidence.

% Evidence predicates derived from submitted materials
evidence_substantial_merit :-
    (   submitted_multiple_articles
    ;   submitted_peer_reviewed_articles
    ;   submitted_studies_reports
    ).

evidence_national_importance :-
    (   submitted_studies_reports
    ;   submitted_peer_reviewed_articles
    ;   submitted_multiple_articles
    ).

well_positioned_individual :-
    submitted_letters_of_support,
    submitted_expert_opinion,
    submitted_documentation_reviewed_manuscripts.

benefit_on_balance :-
    (   submitted_letter_of_interest_from_employer,
        submitted_studies_reports
    )
    ;
    submitted_expert_opinion.

% Dhanasar framework prongs
prong1_met :-
    evidence_substantial_merit,
    evidence_national_importance.

prong2_met :-
    well_positioned_individual.

prong3_met :-
    benefit_on_balance.

% Underlying EB-2 eligibility (petitioner filed as advanced-degree professional)
underlying_eb2_eligibility :-
    has_advanced_degree.

% Final eligibility: all necessary conditions to Accept the case
eligibility_met :-
    underlying_eb2_eligibility,
    prong1_met,
    prong2_met,
    prong3_met.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Applicant filed Form I-821 seeking Temporary Protected Status under the 2023 redesignation of Haiti. The Director of the California Service Center denied the application, and the Applicant appealed and submitted additional evidence. On her Form I-821, the Applicant stated that she last entered the United States on December 9, 2022 and had been residing in the United States since that date. The record includes copies of her Haitian passport, a 2018 B1/B2 visitor visa, and a Customs and Border Protection arrivals-and-departures record showing admission in June 2022, departure on September 12, 2022, and return in December 2022 after 88 days. In a personal statement, she said she traveled to Haiti in September 2022 because her spouse was unexpectedly hospitalized there. During adjudication, the Director issued a request for evidence seeking proof of the Applicant's residence in the United States since November 6, 2022, advising that affidavits from people who knew her could be submitted if other records were unavailable, and requesting evidence that her absence was brief or caused by emergency circumstances outside her control. In response, the Applicant submitted her spouse's medical certificate showing hospitalization from September 2, 2022 through October 28, 2022, a passport page with 2022 exit and entry stamps, and an email confirming her 2022 foreign travel dates. The Director recorded that this material did not show continuous residence since November 6, 2022 and that the spouse's medical certificate appeared inconsistent with the Applicant's travel dates; the Director also found the submitted material inadequate to show continuous physical presence since February 4, 2023. On appeal, the Applicant submitted additional documents about her spouse's hospitalization and argued that the medical certificate reflected her spouse's treatment dates, not the dates of her own stay in Haiti. She maintained that her temporary trip to assist her hospitalized spouse did not interrupt her residence or physical presence in the United States. As described in the record, it does not include documentary proof of U.S. residence such as lease, rent, employment, tax, or similar records, and it does not include affidavits or other information showing that her principal dwelling place was in the United States during the relevant period since November 6, 2022.
statutes:
To be eligible for TPS, a national of a foreign state designated by the Secretary of Homeland Security must establish, in part, that they have been continuously physically present in the United States since the effective date of the most recent designation of that foreign state for TPS and have continuously resided in the United States since a date designated by the Secretary of Homeland Security. 8 C.F.R. § 244.2. Individuals applying for TPS offered to Haitians (and persons without nationality who last habitually resided in Haiti) must demonstrate that they have been continuously residing in the United States since November 6, 2022, and have been continuously physically present in the United States since February 3, 2023 .2 1 The Applicant filed the instant Form 1-821 in April 2023 , indicating that it was her initial (first time) TPS request. 2 See Extension and Redesignation ofHaiti for Tempora ry Protected Status, 88 Fed. Reg . 5022 (Jan. 26, 2023).

Continuously resided means "residing in the United States for the entire period specified in the regulations," but an applicant shall not be considered to have failed to maintain continuous residence"because of a brief, casual and innocent absence ... or due merely to a brief temporary trip abroad required by emergency or extenuating circumstances outside the control of the [applicant]." 8 C.F.R. § 244.1. Continuously physically present means "actual physical presence in the United States for the entire period specified in the regulations," but an applicant shall not be considered to have failed to maintain continuous physical presence because of "brief, casual, and innocent absences." Id. The regulations at 8 C.F.R. § 244.9(2) provide an illustrative list of evidence TPS applicants may submit to establish their continuous residence in the United States during the requisite period, including: employment records, rent receipts, and utility bills; school, hospital, or medical records; attestations by churches, unions, or other organizations of the applicant's residence; bank books with dated transactions and tax receipts; insurance policies, receipts, or letters; money order receipts for money sent in or out of the country; and/or any other relevant documents.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (yes/no predicates use arity 0)
national_haitian.
filed_initial_april_2023.

% Travel facts from the record
last_entry(date(2022,12,9)).        % petitioner stated last entry Dec 9, 2022
departed(date(2022,9,12)).          % departed Sept 12, 2022
returned(date(2022,12,9)).          % returned about 88 days later, Dec 9, 2022
absence_days(88).

% Supporting evidence submitted
provided_medical_certificate.
provided_passport_stamps.
provided_email_confirmation.
provided_cbp_printout.
provided_visa.

% Spouse hospitalization evidence
spouse_hospitalized(start(date(2022,9,2)), end(date(2022,10,28))).
% medical certificate in the record shows treatment dates 9/2/2022 - 10/28/2022

% Regulatory required benchmark dates for TPS (Haiti redesignation)
continuous_residence_required(date(2022,11,6)).   % must have continuously resided since Nov 6, 2022
physical_presence_required(date(2023,2,3)).      % must have been physically present since Feb 3, 2023

% Helper: convert date to an integer allowing easy comparison
date_num(date(Y,M,D), N) :-
    N is Y*10000 + M*100 + D.

date_leq(A, B) :-
    date_num(A, NA),
    date_num(B, NB),
    NA =< NB.

date_geq(A, B) :-
    date_num(A, NA),
    date_num(B, NB),
    NA >= NB.

% Predicate: was the petitioner in the U.S. on a given date?
% For the purposes of the record, petitioner was outside the U.S. between departed(Dep) and returned(Ret).
% She is considered present on Date if Date is on or after the documented return date.
was_in_us_on(Date) :-
    returned(Ret),
    date_geq(Date, Ret).

% Exception: an absence is excused if it was brief, casual, and innocent or due to an emergency.
% For this model we encode "brief" as <= 30 days (conservative threshold).
absence_brief_or_casual_and_innocent :-
    absence_days(D),
    D =< 30.

% Emergency shown if documentary evidence demonstrates hospitalization or other emergency that overlaps
% the required continuous residence date. The medical certificate on record shows spouse treatment 9/2–10/28/2022.
% That overlaps the period of absence but does not extend to Nov 6, 2022 in the record.
emergency_proven_for_date(ReqDate) :-
    spouse_hospitalized(start(S), end(E)),
    date_leq(S, ReqDate),
    date_geq(E, ReqDate).

% Continuous residence check: either present on the required date OR exception applies.
continuous_residence_met :-
    continuous_residence_required(ReqDate),
    (   was_in_us_on(ReqDate)
    ;   absence_brief_or_casual_and_innocent
    ;   emergency_proven_for_date(ReqDate)
    ).

% Continuous physical presence check: must have been present on the required physical-presence date.
continuous_physical_presence_met :-
    physical_presence_required(ReqDate),
    was_in_us_on(ReqDate).

% Eligibility: all conditions necessary to Accept the case
eligibility_met :-
    national_haitian,
    filed_initial_april_2023,
    continuous_residence_met,
    continuous_physical_presence_met.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a Kyrgyzstan native and citizen who works as a Taekwon-Do (martial arts) athlete. He began competing in martial arts events in 2010 at age fifteen. He filed an immigrant petition on December 9, 2022, seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability in athletics (EB-1). Throughout the proceeding the petitioner asserted that he has achieved recognition at national and international levels and identified four pieces of claimed evidence: (i) lesser nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards; (ii) membership in associations requiring outstanding achievement; (iii) published material about him in professional or major media; and (iv) participation in judging the work of others.

As evidence, the record includes a certificate (with English translation) recognizing him “for his help in judging” at an International Taekwon-Do Federation (ITF) championship held in 2018 in a city in Kyrgyzstan; an interview article about him published on the Kyrgyz website barakelde.org (B-); a 2011 article from the Media Policy Institute discussing B-’s growth; a 2020 press release from a larger news agency (A-) describing that agency’s reach; documentation relating to a claimed 2019 appearance as a guest on the NTS TV Network (N-), including a 2023 letter from an N- employee stating he appeared on a live broadcast and three pages from N-’s website; and the petitioner’s responses to a request for evidence, which included an additional copy of the judging certificate and reiteration of the basic event details.

The petitioner appealed the Nebraska Service Center’s denial of his petition. In his appeal he contends that the submitted materials demonstrate the required recognition and that certain media submissions (specifically the B- article and his N- television appearance) should be treated as qualifying published material. He also disputes the denial’s characterization of his submitted evidence and asserts the decision was capricious.
statutes:
An individual is eligible for the extraordinary ability classification if they have extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics as demonstrated by sustained national or international acclaim and their achievements have been recognized in the field through extensive documentation; they seek to enter the United States to continue work in the area of extraordinary ability; and their entry into the United States will substantially benefit prospectively the United States. Section 203(b)(l)(A) of the Act. The term "extraordinary ability" refers only to those individuals in "that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2). The implementing regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) sets forth a multi-part analysis. First, a petitioner may demonstrate international recognition of their achievements in the field through a one-time achievement (that is, a major, internationally recognized award). Absent such an achievement, a petitioner must provide

sufficient qualifying documentation demonstrating that they meet at least three ofthe ten criteria listed at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x). Where a petitioner meets these initial evidence requirements, we then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination and assess whether the record shows sustained national or international acclaim and demonstrates that the individual is among the small percentage at the very top of the field of endeavor. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 2010) ( discussing a two-part review where the documentation is first counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination); see also Visinscaia v. Beers, 4 F. Supp. 3d 126, 131-32 (D.D.C. 2013); Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts about the petitioner and submitted evidence (parsed from the case)
petitioner_nationality(kyrgyzstan).
began_competing(2010).
filed_i140(date(2022,12,9)).

% Evidence items the petitioner submitted
evidence_certificate_judging.        % certificate recognizing him "for his help in judging" at 2018 ITF event
evidence_barakelde_article.         % 2021 interview article on barakelde.org
evidence_nts_letter.                % 2023 letter from NTS traffic manager re: 2019 guest appearance
evidence_nts_website_pages.         % three pages from NTS website related to appearance
evidence_aki_background.            % background materials tying barakelde.org to AKIpress (2011, 2020 background docs)

% Evidence limitations noted in the record (these are present and thus used to defeat criteria)
evidence_certificate_lacks_details. % petitioner did not provide specifics about judging (nature, level, selection, event detail)
evidence_barakelde_not_shown_major. % record does not establish barakelde.org is a "major" media outlet
evidence_nts_broadcast_not_clearly_documented. % NTS materials are not sufficient to establish a major broadcast about achievements
evidence_no_membership_documentation. % no documentation showing membership requires outstanding achievement
evidence_no_award_documentation.     % no documentation of nationally/internationally recognized prizes for excellence

% For boolean/yes-no predicates we define them explicitly as true (fact) or false (by defining the predicate but forcing failure).
% Major one-time internationally recognized award (not present)
has_major_award :- fail.

% Criteria under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x) - relevant ones the petitioner asserted
% (i) Receipt of lesser nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards for excellence
meets_criterion_award :-
    evidence_award_proof.
evidence_award_proof :- fail.  % no award documentation presented -> fails

% (ii) Membership in associations requiring outstanding achievement of their members
meets_criterion_membership :-
    evidence_membership_proof.
evidence_membership_proof :- fail.  % no membership documentation -> fails

% (iii) Published material about the petitioner in professional or major media
% Require that the publication be a professional/major outlet; record does not establish that.
meets_criterion_published_major_media :-
    evidence_barakelde_article,
    evidence_barakelde_is_major.
evidence_barakelde_is_major :- fail.  % record does not establish barakelde.org as major media -> fails

% (iv) Participation as a judge of the work of others in the field
% A certificate exists, but lack of event/selection/level detail in the record renders it insufficient.
meets_criterion_participated_as_judge :-
    evidence_certificate_judging,
    \+ evidence_certificate_lacks_details.  % must not have documented lack of details
% evidence_certificate_lacks_details is present in the record, so above will fail

% Final merits: even if criteria are numerically met, the record must show sustained national or international acclaim
% The record does not establish sustained national/international acclaim or that the petitioner is among the very top of the field.
final_merits_positive :- fail.

% Helper to count how many qualifying criteria are met (from the four asserted/claimed relevant criteria)
criteria_met_count(N) :-
    ( meets_criterion_award -> A = 1 ; A = 0 ),
    ( meets_criterion_membership -> B = 1 ; B = 0 ),
    ( meets_criterion_published_major_media -> C = 1 ; C = 0 ),
    ( meets_criterion_participated_as_judge -> D = 1 ; D = 0 ),
    N is A + B + C + D.

% Eligibility rule following the two-part Kazarian analysis:
% - Success on a one-time major award OR
% - At least 3 of the regulatory criteria AND a positive final merits determination
eligibility_met :-
    (   has_major_award
    ;   criteria_met_count(Count),
        Count >= 3,
        final_merits_positive
    ).

% Decision / run structure (as requested). eligibility_met drives acceptance.
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
In June 2015 the Petitioner filed a Form I-918 (U petition) accompanied by a Supplement B signed by a lieutenant from the local police department. The Supplement B and the accompanying incident report described an event in 2014 in which the Petitioner, while running with a friend and a child in a stroller at a park, encountered a man who emerged from the women’s restroom, pulled down his pants, exposed and masturbated his erect penis, made obscene gestures and noises, and then ran after the Petitioner and her companion. The Petitioner reported the incident to police, gave a detailed description of the suspect, and identified him at the police station. A witness to the incident provided a contemporaneous statement corroborating that she saw a man inside the women’s restroom exposing and touching his genitals and urging someone to “come here.” The suspect was located later at a parolee class, and because he was a registered sex offender subject to GPS monitoring, police verified his presence at the scene via the tracking device. The incident report listed several offenses investigated (including indecent exposure, vagrancy, parole violation, and burglary) and the police narrative reflected the sequence of events set out above.

The Nebraska Service Center Director denied the petition, stating the record did not show the Petitioner was the victim of sexual battery and that there was no indication the suspect had been investigated or charged under the sexual battery statute; the Director also concluded the Petitioner had not shown she suffered substantial physical or mental abuse as a result. The Petitioner then submitted additional evidence in August 2020: two further certified Supplements B — one from a lieutenant in the police department and one from an assistant district attorney — each indicating the Petitioner was a victim of attempted sexual assault and attempted abusive sexual contact and citing specific California Penal Code sections (including sections addressing attempted crimes, sexual battery, and indecent exposure). Those certifying officials described the same facts of exposure, masturbation, and pursuit; stated the suspect was a registered sex offender on parole wearing a GPS device; noted the Petitioner reported the crime, provided a detailed description, identified the suspect, and remained cooperative; and observed that the incident appeared to have caused substantial mental trauma.

After multiple motions to reopen, the Director again denied the petition, referring to the incident report’s listing of indecent exposure, vagrancy, parole violation, and burglary and not addressing why the Supplements B were insufficient. On appeal, the Petitioner contends that the police department, another law enforcement office, and the county district attorney’s office each attested she was the victim of attempted sexual assault and attempted abusive sexual contact; she emphasizes that the suspect was a registered sex offender who exposed himself and chased her and her companion (including a baby in a stroller), that her report and identification of the suspect aided the investigation and arrest, and that, but for intervention by an AT&T worker, the situation could have escalated. Her appeal challenges the Director’s denial and seeks reconsideration based on the law enforcement attestations and the additional certified Supplements B.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for U-1 nonimmigrant classification, petitioners must show that they: have suffered substantial physical or mental abuse as a result of having been the victim of qualifying criminal activity; possess information concerning the qualifying criminal activity; and have been helpful, are being helpful, or are likely to be helpful to law enforcement authorities investigating or prosecuting the qualifying criminal activity. Section 101(a)(15)(U)(i) of the Act. The burden of proof is on a petitioner to demonstrate eligibility by a preponderance of the evidence. Section 291 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1361; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(4); Matter of Chawathe, 25 l&N Dec. 369, 376 (AAO 2010). A "victim of qualifying criminal activity" is defined as an individual who has "suffered direct and proximate harm as a result of the commission of qualifying criminal activity." 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(14). "Qualifying criminal activity" is "that involving one or more of' the 28 types of crimes listed at section 101(a)(15)(U)(iii) of the Act or "any similar activity in violation of Federal, State, or local criminal law" and includes the "attempt, conspiracy, or solicitation to commit any of the above mentioned crimes." Section 101(a)(15)(U)(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(9). The term "'any similar activity' refers to criminal offenses in which the nature and elements of the offenses

are substantially similar to the statutorily enumerated list of criminal activities" at section 101(a)(15)(U)(iii) of the Act. 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(9). As required initial evidence, petitioners must submit a Form 1-918 Supplement B, U Nonimmigrant Status Certification (Supplement B), from a law enforcement official certifying the petitioners' helpfulness in the investigation or prosecution of the qualifying criminal activity perpetrated against them. 1 Section 214(p)(1) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(2)(i). U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) has sole jurisdiction over U petitions. 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(1). Although petitioners may submit any relevant, credible evidence for the agency to consider, USCIS determines, in its sole discretion, the credibility of and weight given to all the evidence, including the Supplement B. Section 214(p)(4) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(4).

11.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the case record
filed_2015_june.
incident_exposed_genitals.
incident_masturbation.
incident_obscene_gestures_noises.
incident_chased_victim.
victim_running_with_child_in_stroller.
petitioner_reported_to_police.
provided_detailed_description.
identified_suspect_at_station.
cooperated_with_investigation.
witness_corroborated.
suspect_registered_sex_offender.
suspect_on_parole.
suspect_wearing_gps_placed_at_scene.
suspect_apprehended_at_station.
initial_supplement_b_certified.
additional_supplement_b_certified_by_police.
additional_supplement_b_certified_by_assistant_district_attorney.
certifiers_described_attempted_sexual_assault_and_abusive_sexual_contact.
certifiers_noted_substantial_mental_trauma.
certifiers_stated_cooperation_led_to_arrest.
police_notes_incomplete_initially_and_further_investigation_possible.
director_denied_for_lack_of_sexual_battery_findings.

% Legal predicates (arity 0 for yes/no predicates)

% The incident qualifies as criminal activity similar to enumerated sexual offenses
qualifying_crime :-
    certifiers_described_attempted_sexual_assault_and_abusive_sexual_contact.
qualifying_crime :-
    % alternative factual pathway: exposure + pursuit + suspect known sex offender at scene
    incident_exposed_genitals,
    incident_masturbation,
    incident_chased_victim,
    suspect_registered_sex_offender,
    suspect_wearing_gps_placed_at_scene.

% Petitioner is a victim who suffered direct and proximate harm: substantial physical or mental abuse
suffered_substantial_abuse :-
    certifiers_noted_substantial_mental_trauma.
suffered_substantial_abuse :-
    % corroborating facts supporting substantial mental trauma (threatening exposure while running with child)
    victim_running_with_child_in_stroller,
    incident_chased_victim,
    incident_exposed_genitals,
    witness_corroborated.

% Petitioner possesses information concerning the qualifying criminal activity
possesses_information :-
    provided_detailed_description,
    identified_suspect_at_station,
    witness_corroborated.

% Petitioner has been helpful (or is likely to be helpful) to law enforcement
helpful_to_law_enforcement :-
    (   initial_supplement_b_certified
    ;   additional_supplement_b_certified_by_police
    ;   additional_supplement_b_certified_by_assistant_district_attorney
    ),
    cooperated_with_investigation,
    (   certifiers_stated_cooperation_led_to_arrest
    ;   suspect_apprehended_at_station
    ).

% Required initial evidence: at least one Supplement B was submitted and certified
supplement_b_submitted :-
    initial_supplement_b_certified.
supplement_b_submitted :-
    additional_supplement_b_certified_by_police.
supplement_b_submitted :-
    additional_supplement_b_certified_by_assistant_district_attorney.

% Burden of proof and credibility are agency matters; here we model that the
% presence of multiple independent corroborating items satisfies preponderance
% for this case:
preponderance_of_evidence :-
    % corroboration by witness and law enforcement documentation plus certified B's
    witness_corroborated,
    (   initial_supplement_b_certified
    ;   additional_supplement_b_certified_by_police
    ;   additional_supplement_b_certified_by_assistant_district_attorney
    ),
    (   suspect_wearing_gps_placed_at_scene
    ;   certifiers_stated_cooperation_led_to_arrest ).

% Eligibility: all statutory elements must be present to Accept
eligibility_met :-
    qualifying_crime,
    suffered_substantial_abuse,
    possesses_information,
    helpful_to_law_enforcement,
    supplement_b_submitted,
    preponderance_of_evidence.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The applicant is a woman who attempted to enter the United States without inspection in 2002 and was placed in removal proceedings. She filed an asylum application during those proceedings; in 2004 an Immigration Judge denied her asylum claim and ordered her removed to China. She appealed to the Board of Immigration Appeals, which affirmed and dismissed the appeal in April 2005, and a later motion to reopen was denied in 2011. She has not departed the United States since the removal order.

Because she will be inadmissible upon any departure on account of the prior removal order (and because she will accrue unlawful presence if she departs), she filed Form I-212 seeking conditional approval to reapply for admission before leaving the United States to apply for an immigrant visa abroad. Her husband, a citizen of China, has been a U.S. lawful permanent resident since August 2013 and filed an I-130 petition for her that was approved. The couple has two U.S. citizen children, born in the United States, who were described in the record as ages 16 and 17. Her mother-in-law is a naturalized U.S. citizen.

In support of the Form I-212, the applicant submitted documentation including her marriage certificate and spouse’s permanent resident card, the approved I-130 notice, birth certificates for her two children, her mother-in-law’s naturalization certificate, affidavits from her spouse, mother-in-law, nephew, and two friends, physician statements and medical records for her spouse and mother-in-law, proof of health insurance coverage, jointly filed income tax returns, mortgage, utility, automobile, and health insurance bills, a business certificate in her husband’s name, her children’s school records, family photographs, and news articles and reports about country conditions in China.

The New York City Field Office Director denied the Form I-212. On appeal, the applicant challenges that denial. She contends the Director failed to consider all relevant evidence and applicable policy, pointing specifically to the submitted medical reports concerning her husband’s conditions and physician statements about her husband and mother-in-law, as well as affidavits and letters from relatives and friends. She further asserts the Director did not properly consider the financial hardship her husband, her two children, and her mother-in-law would face if she were removed or if the family relocated with her to China. Finally, she argues the Director applied an incorrect, higher “extreme hardship” standard in adjudicating her Form I-212 and therefore did not appropriately weigh the submitted evidence.
statutes:
Section 212(a)(9)(A)(i i) of the Act provides, in part, that an alien who has been ordered removed under section 240 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1229a, or any other provision of law, or who departed the United States while an order of removal was outstanding, and who seeks admission within 10 years of the date of such departure or removal, is inadmissible. Aliens found inadmissible under section 212(a)(9)(A) of the Act may seek permission to reapply for admission under section 212(a)(9)(A)(iii) of the Act if, prior to the date of the reembarkation at a place outside the United States or attempt to be admitted from foreign contiguous territory, the Secretary of Homeland Security has consented to the alien's reapplying for admission.

Approval of an application for permission to reapply is discretionary, and any unfavorable factors will be weighed against the favorable factors to determine if approval of the application is warranted as a matter of discretion. Matter of Lee, 17 l&N Dec. 275, 278-79 (Reg'l Comm'r 1978). Factors to be considered in determining whether to grant permission to reapply include the basis for the prior deportation; the recency of deportation; length ofresidence in the United States; the applicant's moral character; the applicant's respect for law and order; evidence of the applicant's reformation and rehabilitation; family responsibilities; any inadmissibility under other sections of law; hardship involved to the applicant or others; and the need for the applicant's services in the United States. Matter of Tin, 14 I&N Dec. 371 (Reg'l Comm'r 1973); see also Matter of Lee, supra, at 278 (finding that a record of immigration violations, standing alone, does not conclusively show lack of good moral character, and "the recency of the deportation can only be considered when there is a finding of poor moral character based on moral turpitude in the conduct and attitude of a person which evinces a callous conscience"). The Applicant currently resides in the United States and is seeking conditional approval of her application under the regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 212.2(j) before departing the United States to apply for an immigrant visa. The approval of the application under these circumstances is conditioned upon the Applicant's departure from the United States and would have no effect ifshe fails to depart.

reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (true/false predicates use arity 0 as required)
prior_removal.
entry_without_inspection.
accrued_unlawful_presence.
remained_in_us_after_removal.
filed_i212.
inadmissible_212a9a.
motion_to_reopen_denied.
director_found_insufficient.

% Favorable-family-and-evidence facts
spouse_lpr.                 % spouse is a lawful permanent resident (LPR)
children_us_citizens.       % two U.S. citizen children
length_of_residence_long.   % long residence in the United States (since 2002)
medical_evidence_present.   % physician statements and some medical records for spouse and mother-in-law
financial_evidence_present. % tax returns, bills, mortgage, insurance, etc.
affidavits_present.         % affidavits and letters from relatives and friends
family_unity_docs_present.  % marriage certificate, birth certificates, photos, school records
no_criminal_record.         % no criminal conduct alleged in the record

% Adverse factor valuation rules: produce numeric weights when factor is present.
adverse_factor_value(5) :- prior_removal.                 % prior order of removal (strong adverse)
adverse_factor_value(3) :- entry_without_inspection.      % entry without inspection (adverse)
adverse_factor_value(2) :- accrued_unlawful_presence.     % accrued unlawful presence (adverse)
adverse_factor_value(1) :- remained_in_us_after_removal.  % remained in U.S. despite removal order

% Favorable factor valuation rules: produce numeric weights when factor is present.
favorable_factor_value(3) :- spouse_lpr.                  % LPR spouse (ties to U.S.)
favorable_factor_value(4) :- children_us_citizens.        % U.S. citizen children (significant favorable)
favorable_factor_value(2) :- length_of_residence_long.    % long residence in U.S.
favorable_factor_value(2) :- medical_evidence_present.    % medical hardship evidence for relatives
favorable_factor_value(1) :- financial_evidence_present.  % financial ties and economic hardship evidence
favorable_factor_value(1) :- affidavits_present.          % affidavits and community support letters
favorable_factor_value(1) :- family_unity_docs_present.   % documents showing family unity
favorable_factor_value(2) :- no_criminal_record.          % absence of criminal record / good moral character

% Sum a list of numbers
sum_list([], 0).
sum_list([H|T], Sum) :-
    sum_list(T, Rest),
    Sum is H + Rest.

% Compute adverse score by collecting all adverse_factor_value outputs
adverse_score(Score) :-
    findall(V, adverse_factor_value(V), L),
    sum_list(L, Score).

% Compute favorable score by collecting all favorable_factor_value outputs
favorable_score(Score) :-
    findall(V, favorable_factor_value(V), L),
    sum_list(L, Score).

% Discretionary balancing rule: favorable must strictly outweigh adverse to warrant approval
discretionary_balance_favors :-
    favorable_score(F),
    adverse_score(A),
    F > A.

% Eligibility: applicant filed I-212, is inadmissible under 212(a)(9)(A), and discretionary balance favors approval.
eligibility_met :-
    filed_i212,
    inadmissible_212a9a,
    discretionary_balance_favors.

% Entrypoint and output structure (must be last)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a Honduran national born in November 2009 who filed Form I-360 seeking classification as a special immigrant juvenile. In 2024 a Texas district court issued an Order declaring the petitioner dependent on the court. That Order found reunification with the petitioner’s father was not viable because he neglected the petitioner—failing to provide food, clothing, or shelter, leaving the family, making no effort to communicate or check on her well‑being, and not providing consistent economic support—and concluded it was not in the petitioner’s best interest to return to Honduras because there was no one there with whom she could live. The Order also stated its purpose was to protect the child from further abuse and neglect and to establish a record that could support further custody proceedings.

The National Benefits Center director denied the petitioner’s I-360, concluding the district court’s Order was a declaratory judgment not subject to enforcement and did not constitute a juvenile court order, and that USCIS’ consent to SIJ classification was not warranted. The petitioner appealed that denial, arguing the district court was acting as a juvenile court under Texas law (citing Tex. Govt. Code § 23.001 and Article V, Section 8 of the Texas Constitution) and that the Order is enforceable as having the force and effect of a final judgment under the Texas Uniform Declaratory Judgments Act (citing § 37.003(b)).
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(b), (c)(l). Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b)(4), (c)(l). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country ofnationality or last habitual residence. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii) ofthe Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204. ll(c)(2). SIJ classification may only be granted upon the consent of the Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), through USCIS, when the petitioner meets all other eligibility criteria and establishes that the request for SIJ classification is bona fide, which requires the petitioner to establish that a primary reason the required juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under State law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i)­ (iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b)(5).

For USCIS to consent, the juvenile court order and any supplemental evidence must include, the factual basis for the requisite judicial determinations, and the relief from parental abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under State law granted or recognized by the juvenile court, which may include the court-ordered dependency on the court for the provision of child welfare services and/or other court-ordered or court-recognized protective or remedial relief. 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(d)(5). USCIS may withhold consent if evidence materially conflicts with the eligibility requirements such that the record reflects that the request for SIJ classification was not bona fide. 8 C.F .R. § 204.11 (b )( 5).

text: The Applicant filed Form N-600 seeking a Certificate of Citizenship based on his claim that he acquired U.S. citizenship at birth through his father. The Boise, Idaho Field Office denied the application. According to the denial, the record did not establish that the Applicant had acquired citizenship at birth and did not sufficiently establish either that his father was a U.S. citizen or that, if he was, he satisfied the required physical-presence conditions. The matter is now on appeal before the Administrative Appeals Office, where the Applicant reasserts his citizenship claim. He states that he believes his father is a U.S. citizen by operation of law, but that he never requested evidence of his father's citizenship. To support the claimed paternal line, the Applicant submitted a civil birth certificate for his father, M.A.; according to the agency's description of that document, it does not identify the Applicant's paternal grandfather, who is the claimed link to U.S. citizenship. He also submitted an affidavit from M.A. stating that J.F. is M.A.'s biological father, excerpted text from E.A.'s memoir referring to an unnamed U.S. service member as M.A.'s probable father, a timeline with notes from a 2011 conversation M.A. had with his mother about his biological father, and Ancestry.com research with genetic comparisons indicating possible familial links to Louisiana. The agency record describes this evidence as insufficient to establish M.A.'s parentage.
statutes:
The applicable law for transmitting citizenship to a child born abroad when one parent is a U.S. citizen is the statute that was in effect at the time ofthe child's birth. Chau v. Immigration and Naturalization Service, 247 F.3d 1026, 1029 n.3 (9th Cir. 2001) (internal quotation marks and citation omitted). Accordingly, as an initial matter we must determine which laws apply to the claimed acquisition of U.S. citizenship by the Applicant and his father. A. Law in Effect at the Time of the Applicant's Father's Birth The Applicant's father was born to unmarried parents in 1946. The Applicant claims that his paternal grandfather was a U.S. service member stationed in the United Kingdom between 1943 and 1945. One of the statutes that governed acquisition of U.S. citizenship by persons born outside of the United States and its outlying possessions at the time of the Applicant's father's birth in 1946 was section 1 Former section 301(a)(7) of the Act was re-designated as section 301(g) by the Act of October 10, 1978, Pub. L. No. 95432, 92 Stat. 1046 (1978). However, the re-designation did not change the substantive provisions of former section 30l(a)(7) of the Act, which remained in effect until 1986.

20 l (g) of the 1940 Act, as in effect since January 13, 1941. That section provided in relevant part that a person born abroad to parents one of whom was a U.S. citizen, would acquire U.S. citizenship at birth if the U.S. citizen parent "has had [10] years' residence in the United States or one of its outlying possessions, at least [5] of which were after attaining the age of[ 16] years" before the person's birth. In 1946, Congress passed an amendment adding section 201 (i) to the 1940 Act. See Act of July 31, 1946, Pub. L. 79-571, 60 Stat. 721. Section 20 l (i) allowed a U.S. citizen parent who honorably served in the U.S. Armed Forces during World War II to transmit citizenship to their child if they "had [10] years' residence in the United States or one ofits outlying possessions, at least [5] of which were after attaining the age of [12] years" before the child's birth. Section 212(i) of the 1940 Act applied retroactively to children who were born after January 14, 1941, but before July 31, 1946, as well as prospectively to children born after that date and until the 1940 Act was repealed in 1952. See Matter ofA-, 2 I&N Dec. 799,801 (BIA 1947). Both section 20l(g) and (i) of the 1940 Act mandated that the person who acquired U.S. citizenship at birth had to comply with certain U.S. residence conditions to retain it. Pursuant to subsequently enacted provisions, the retention requirement may be satisfied in part by 5 years ofcontinuous physical presence in the United States between the ages of 14 and 28 years (ifbegun before October 27, 1972). 2 B. Law in Effect at the Time of the Applicant's Birth The Applicant was born in the United Kingdom in 1981 to married parents. If the Applicant can establish that his father was a U.S. citizen, his citizenship claim will fall under the provision of former section 301(a)(7) of the Act, which required the U.S. citizen parent to be "physically present in the United States or its outlying possessions for a period or periods totaling not less than ten years, at least five of which were after attaining the age of fourteen years" prior to the child's birth to transmit U.S. citizenship to that child. C. Burden of Proof Because the Applicant and his father were born abroad, they are presumed to be noncitizens and the Applicant bears the burden of proving his and his father's U.S. citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. Matter of Baires-Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467, 468 (BIA 2008). Under the preponderance of the evidence standard, the Applicant must demonstrate that his and his father's citizenship claims are "probably true." Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. at 376. Stated another way, the Applicant must establish that there is greater than a fifty percent chance that: (1) his father acquired U.S. citizenship at birth from the Applicant's paternal grandfather; and (2) the Applicant himself acquired U.S. citizenship at birth from his father. 2 See former section 30l(b) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, as originally enacted as originally enacted by the Act of June 27, 1952, Pub. L. 82-414, 66 Stat. 163. Absences of less than one year in the aggregate during the requisite five­ year period do not break continuity of physical presence. See Act of September 11, 1957, Pub. L. No. 85-316, 71 Stat. 639. 2

reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the record
applicant_applied_n600.
field_office_denied.
applicant_resides_abroad.

% Identity claims and documentary record
ma_identified_as_father.
birth_certificate_ma_no_father.        % civil birth certificate for M.A. does not list a father
affidavit_ma_present.                 % affidavit from M.A. describing hearsay about biological father
memoir_excerpt_present.               % excerpts referencing an unnamed U.S. service member as probable father
timeline_notes_mother_conversation_present. % timeline reflecting notes from a 2011 conversation M.A. had with his mother
ancestry_genealogy_present.           % genealogical research from Ancestry.com suggesting possible links
genetic_comparison_present.           % genetic comparisons suggesting possible familial links
ma_claims_paternal_grandfather_service_wwii. % claim paternal grandfather (J.F.) was a U.S. service member 1943-45

% Absence of primary documentation (explicit facts of absence)
% Note: by not asserting primary_doc_jff or maternal_direct_statement_present, those predicates are false.
no_primary_documentation_for_jff.     % the record does not include primary documentary proof that J.F. was a U.S. citizen
mother_direct_statement_not_provided. % applicant did not provide a direct statement from his mother

% Legal-rule style predicates (zero-arity for yes/no per instructions)
% Applicable statutory conditions are modeled as predicates that must be satisfied to prove citizenship transfer.
% 1) For J.F. (the paternal grandfather) to be established as a U.S. citizen at M.A.'s (the father's) birth:
%    either primary documentary proof exists, or strong corroborating evidence that meets the preponderance standard.
primary_doc_jff :- false.  % no primary documentary proof in the record

% Strong corroborating proof would require either:
%  - contemporaneous primary proof (handled above) OR
%  - direct, reliable testimony from an eyewitness/mother and/or official service records
% The record here contains multiple pieces of secondary evidence (affidavit, memoir excerpt, genealogy), but lacks direct maternal statement
% and lacks official service records or other primary evidence. We therefore do not treat the secondary set as sufficient.
service_records_jff :- false.               % no official service records are provided
mother_direct_statement_present :- false.   % no direct statement from the mother

% Evaluate whether the available secondary evidence collectively meets the preponderance threshold.
% For this case file, we require either direct documentary/service evidence or a direct maternal statement plus supporting secondary evidence.
secondary_evidence_basic :-
    affidavit_ma_present,
    memoir_excerpt_present,
    ancestry_genealogy_present,
    genetic_comparison_present.

% But to reach "probably true" for J.F.'s U.S. citizenship, we require either primary/service evidence,
% or a direct maternal statement combined with corroboration. Both are absent here.
jff_citizen_probably_true :-
    primary_doc_jff
    ;
    ( service_records_jff )
    ;
    ( mother_direct_statement_present, secondary_evidence_basic ).

% 2) For M.A. (the applicant's father) to have acquired U.S. citizenship at birth from J.F., J.F. must be a U.S. citizen
%    at the time of M.A.'s birth and must satisfy the relevant residence/service-based rules. We model that as requiring
%    jff_citizen_probably_true and evidence that J.F. satisfied applicable residence/service conditions.
jff_met_residence_requirements :- false.  % no evidence that J.F. satisfied the statutory U.S. residence requirements for transmission

father_acquired_citizenship_at_birth :-
    jff_citizen_probably_true,
    jff_met_residence_requirements.

% 3) For the applicant (born 1981) to have acquired U.S. citizenship from his father, the father must have been a U.S. citizen
%    prior to the applicant's birth and the father must have met the physical presence requirement (10 years total, 5 after age 14).
father_was_us_citizen_prior_to_applicant_birth :-
    father_acquired_citizenship_at_birth.  % father's citizenship condition depends on the previous chain

father_met_physical_presence_requirement :- false. % the record contains no evidence that the father met the required U.S. physical presence

applicant_acquired_citizenship_from_father :-
    father_was_us_citizen_prior_to_applicant_birth,
    father_met_physical_presence_requirement.

% Burden of proof / preponderance standard:
% The applicant bears the burden to show that it is more likely than not that both links in the chain are true.
% We model this by requiring the specific predicates above that represent meeting the statutory conditions with sufficient evidence.

eligibility_met :-
    applicant_acquired_citizenship_from_father.

% Final decision structure required by the problem statement
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The applicant is a citizen of Afghanistan who applied for lawful permanent residence under Section 13. He entered the United States on June 6, 2021 as an A-2 nonimmigrant; at that time he was working within the Afghanistan Ministry of Foreign Affairs and U.S. records show the Afghanistan Ministry requested permission for him to enter to attend a specific program. After his arrival, he attended a short training and, according to his sworn statement, developed proposals for the U.S. government related to sending Afghan workers abroad through official channels. In a sworn declaration he said he was active in high-level meetings organized by the Embassy of Afghanistan, meeting with U.S. and international officials (including the Ambassador of Afghanistan), and that those meetings focused on facilitating funds to enable Afghans to work overseas and addressing the situation of orphaned children in Afghanistan. He submitted photographs showing him in meetings with U.S. and international officials and letters from two individuals (S-M- and A-D-) who stated they observed or participated in those meetings and described him as engaged in diplomatic negotiations rather than clerical tasks.

Shortly after his 2021 arrival, in August 2021 the Taliban seized control of Afghanistan and the applicant ceased to be employed by the former government. U.S. records also show entries and exits involving travel to and from Canada in May 2022; the applicant stated he attempted to travel to Canada but was stopped and disputes a record indicating he reentered the United States on May 5, 2022.

The applicant appealed the denial of his adjustment application. He contends that he did not enter the United States on May 5, 2022 and that he performed diplomatic duties after his June 6, 2021 admission, and he submitted declarations, photographs, and supporting letters to document those activities.
statutes: Section 13 is an adjustment of status category for foreign nationals who can demonstrate, in part: (1) failure to maintain A-1, A-2, G-1, or G-2 nonirnmigrant status as of the application's filing date; (2) performance of diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties by the principal on behalf of the accrediting country; and (3) inability, because of compelling reasons, to return to the country that accredited the foreign national. 8 U.S.C. § 1255b(b); 8 C.F.R. § 245.3. 2 Section 13 applicants bear the burden of proving their eligibility to adjust status. Section 291 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1361. 1 Pub. L. No. 85-316, 71 Stat. 642, amend ed by Pub. L. No. 97-116, 95 Stat. 161 (1981). The A nonimmigrant classification is for diplomats and foreign government officials (principal) as well as their immediate family members. The G nonimmigrant classification is for employees of certain international organizations (principal) and their immediate family members. See https://travel.state.gov. 2 If the first three eligibility requirements are met, applicants must also establish that compelling reasons demonstrate that their adjustment would be in the national interest and would not be contrary to the national welfare, safety, or security of the United States and that they are of good moral character and admissible to the United States. Discussion of these remaining criteria is generally unnecessary in cases where the first three eligibility criteria have not been met.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
