```prolog
% Facts (parsed from the case file)
citizen_afghanistan.
entered_as_a2.
entry_date(2021,6,6).
employed_by_af_ministry_at_entry.
ministry_requested_permission_to_enter.
taliban_seized_august_2021.
ceased_employment_august_2021.

% Applicant's declarations and documentary evidence
sworn_statement_attended_training_after_arrival.
sworn_statement_developed_proposals_for_sending_workers.
sworn_statement_claimed_high_level_meetings.
sworn_statement_met_us_and_international_officials_including_ambassador.
photos_show_meetings_with_officials_and_ambassador.

% Third‑party corroboration
letter_from_s_m_supports_diplomatic_negotiations.
letter_from_a_d_affirms_high_level_meetings.

% USCIS record and petitioner's dispute
uscis_record_depart_return_canada_2022_05_03.
petitioner_disputes_may5_2022_entry.
petitioner_explains_attempted_travel_to_canada_but_was_stopped.
petitioner_asserts_engaged_diplomatic_duties_after_admission.

% Procedural fact inferred from the record (the application was filed after events
% described in the record; this is necessary to evaluate failure to maintain status)
filed_application_after_august_2021.

% Legal rules encoded as Prolog predicates
% Section 13 eligibility requires three primary elements:
% 1) failure to maintain A-1/A-2/G-1/G-2 status as of filing date
% 2) performance of diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties on behalf of the accrediting country
% 3) inability, because of compelling reasons, to return to the accrediting country

% 1) Failure to maintain status
% A-2 status depends on the employment/official relationship with the accrediting government.
% If the petitioner ceased employment with the accrediting government before filing, he
% failed to maintain A-2 status as of the filing date.
failure_to_maintain_status :-
    ceased_employment_august_2021,
    filed_application_after_august_2021.

% 2) Performance of diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties
% We treat the principal as having performed diplomatic or semi-diplomatic duties if:
% - the petitioner made a sworn statement describing participation in high-level meetings
%   organized by the Embassy or in furtherance of the accrediting government, AND
% - there is at least one corroborating piece of evidence (photographs or independent letters).
evidence_of_diplomatic_duties :-
    sworn_statement_claimed_high_level_meetings,
    ( photos_show_meetings_with_officials_and_ambassador
    ; letter_from_s_m_supports_diplomatic_negotiations
    ; letter_from_a_d_affirms_high_level_meetings
    ).

performed_diplomatic_duties :-
    evidence_of_diplomatic_duties.

% Note: conflicting travel records are noted but do not by themselves negate evidence of
% diplomatic duties unless they directly undermine the corroborating proof. We record the
% existence of an inconsistency for completeness.
inconsistency_between_uscis_and_petitioner :-
    uscis_record_depart_return_canada_2022_05_03,
    petitioner_disputes_may5_2022_entry.

% 3) Inability to return to the accrediting country
% We treat the Taliban's seizure of the Afghan government as a compelling reason preventing
% safe return to the prior accrediting government.
compelling_inability_to_return :-
    taliban_seized_august_2021.

% Burden of proof: applicants bear the burden of establishing eligibility.
applicant_bears_burden.

% Eligibility is satisfied only if the three primary elements are established.
eligibility_met :-
    applicant_bears_burden,
    failure_to_maintain_status,
    performed_diplomatic_duties,
    compelling_inability_to_return.

% Decision entrypoint (required structure)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner was born in Mexico in November 1996. His mother is a U.S. citizen by birth in the United States in 1975. The petitioner’s parents were unmarried, and the record contains no indication that his father is a U.S. citizen. On his Form N‑600 the petitioner stated that his mother had been physically present in the United States from her birth in 1975 until February 1996. With the application he submitted copies of two older siblings’ U.S. birth certificates (showing births in 1994 and 1995) and affidavits from family members. The El Paso Field Office denied the application, determining that the affidavits the petitioner provided did not establish his mother’s continuous physical presence in the United States because they lacked exact dates or addresses, and therefore concluded the evidence was insufficient. On appeal the petitioner resubmitted the siblings’ birth certificates and provided new affidavits from his mother, grandmother, and great aunt stating that his mother lived with the great aunt at an apartment in Texas from March 1993 until 1996, giving the exact address that matches the address listed for the mother on the siblings’ birth certificates. The petitioner appealed the Field Office denial to challenge that finding and to submit additional evidence regarding his mother’s physical presence in the United States.
statutes: The applicable law for transmitting citizenship to a child born abroad when one parent is a U.S. citizen is the statute that was in effect at the time ofthe child's birth. Chau v. Immigration and Naturalization Service, 247 F.3d 1026, 1029 n.3 (9th Cir. 2001) (internal quotation marks and citation omitted). As the Applicant indicated that his parents were never married to each other, we consider his citizenship claim under section 309( c) of the Act, which provides in pertinent part that a person born abroad "out of wedlock shall be held to have acquired at birth the nationality status of his mother, if the mother had the nationality of the United States at the time of such person's birth, and if the mother had previously been physically present in the United States or one of its outlying possessions for a continuous period of one year." Because the Applicant was born abroad, he is presumed to be an alien and bears the burden of establishing his claim to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. Matter ofBaires­ Larios, 24 l&N Dec. 467, 468 (BIA 2008). Under the preponderance of the evidence standard, the Applicant must demonstrate that his U.S. citizenship claim is "probably true," or "more likely than not." Matter ofChawathe , 25 I&N Dec. 369, 376 (AAO 2010).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts of the case
petitioner_born_abroad(mexico, 1996).
parents_unmarried.

% Mother facts
mother_us_born(1975).                       % mother born in the U.S. in 1975

% Evidence provided
initial_affidavits_insufficient.            % Field office found initial affidavits insufficient
new_affidavits_provided.                    % New affidavits were supplied on appeal
affidavit_states_residence_march_1993_to_1996.  % affidavits state mother lived at named apt in TX from Mar 1993 to 1996
mother_reported_presence_from_birth_until_feb_1996. % petitioner initially asserted mother present in US from birth (1975) until Feb 1996

% Corroborating documents
siblings_birth_certs_1994_1995.             % birth certs of two older siblings born in US in 1994 and 1995
siblings_birth_certs_address_match_affidavit. % the address on siblings' birth certs matches the address in the affidavits

% Legal determinations (arity 0 for yes/no predicates)

% Mother was a U.S. citizen at the time of the petitioner's birth if she was born in the U.S.
mother_was_us_citizen_at_child_birth :-
    mother_us_born(MotherBirthYear),
    petitioner_born_abroad(_, ChildYear),
    MotherBirthYear < ChildYear.

% Mother had the required continuous physical presence of at least one year prior to the petitioner's birth.
% This can be shown by either the broad report of presence until Feb 1996 or by the new affidavits corroborated by sibling birth certificates.
mother_had_one_year_continuous_presence_prior_to_birth :-
    mother_reported_presence_from_birth_until_feb_1996.

mother_had_one_year_continuous_presence_prior_to_birth :-
    affidavit_states_residence_march_1993_to_1996,
    siblings_birth_certs_address_match_affidavit,
    new_affidavits_provided.

% The applicant bears the burden to prove citizenship by a preponderance of the evidence.
% We consider the evidence sufficient (preponderates) when the continuous presence is established and corroborating documents exist.
evidence_preponderates :-
    mother_had_one_year_continuous_presence_prior_to_birth,
    siblings_birth_certs_1994_1995.

% Eligibility is met when all statutory elements under section 309(c) are satisfied:
% parents unmarried, mother was a U.S. citizen at time of birth, and mother had at least one continuous year of physical presence in the U.S. prior to the birth,
% and the preponderance of evidence supports those facts.
eligibility_met :-
    parents_unmarried,
    mother_was_us_citizen_at_child_birth,
    evidence_preponderates.

% Decision output structure (must be the last part)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner was approved for U-4 nonimmigrant status from April 14, 2017, until November 10, 2020. He filed a Form I-485 (U adjustment) in September 2020. In 2019, while he was in U status, he was arrested for simple assault involving his former spouse. In December 2020 he was placed in a pretrial program under Georgia law, and in August 2021 the criminal charges were nolle prossed.

The petitioner’s account of the incident differs from the arrest report: he states that when his former spouse was dropping off their four‑year‑old daughter he tried to remove the child from the car and “pulled very hard on the door handle,” accidentally breaking the passenger‑side handle. The arrest report describes a verbal argument in which the former spouse was fearful, ran to the car, and the petitioner allegedly kept hitting the car, bent the front hood, attempted to disconnect the battery, and ripped the driver’s door handle off, causing the former spouse to fear imminent violent injury; the report also notes the battery’s positive terminal was off but still making contact, allowing the former spouse to leave the scene.

After the Director denied his Form I-485, the petitioner appealed that denial. On appeal he submitted additional materials, including a brief, a sworn statement, letters of support, and documentation of financial support he provided to his former spouse and children, to contest the denial and support his request for adjustment of status.
statutes: U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may adjust the status of a U nonimmigrant to that of an LPR if they meet all other eligibility requirements and, "in the opinion" of USCIS , their"continued presence in the United States is justified on humanitarian grounds, to ensure family unity, or is otherwise in the public interest." Section 245(m) of the Act. The applicant bears the burden of establishing their eligibility, section 291 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1361, and must do so by a preponderance of the evidence. Matter of Chawathe, 25 l&N Dec. 369, 375 (AAO 2010). This burden includes establishing that discretion should be exercised in their favor, and USCIS may take into account all relevant factors in making its discretionary determination. 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(b)(6), (d)(ll). A favorable exercise of discretion to grant an applicant adjustment of status to that of an LPR is generally warranted in the absence of adverse factors and presence of favorable factors. Matter of Arai, 13 l&N Dec. 494, 496 (BIA 1970). Favorable factors include, but are not limited to, family unity, length of residence in the United States, employment, community involvement, and good moral character. Id.; see also 7 USCIS Policy Manual A.10(8)(2), https://www.uscis.gov/policy-manual (providing guidance regarding adjudicative factors to consider in discretionary adjustment of status determinations). However, where adverse factors are present, the applicant may submit evidence establishing mitigating equities. See 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(d)(ll) (providing that, "[w]here adverse factors are present, an applicant may offset these by submitting supporting documentation establishing mitigating equities that the applicant wants USCIS to consider when determining whether or not a favorable exercise of discretion is appropriate").
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (zero-arity predicates for true/false or yes/no facts)
has_u_status.                         % Petitioner was granted U-4 status (4/14/2017 - 11/10/2020)
filed_i485.                           % Petitioner filed Form I-485 in Sept 2020
arrest_in_2019.                       % Arrest for simple assault involving former spouse in 2019
arrest_report_indicates_fear_and_damage.
                                       % Arrest report describes victim fear, hitting/damaging vehicle,
                                       % attempted battery-disconnect, ripping off door handle
entered_pretrial_program.             % Entered pretrial program under Georgia law (Dec 2020)
charges_nolle_prossed.                % Charges were nolle prossed / dismissed (Aug 2021)
submitted_mitigating_evidence.        % Submitted brief, sworn statement, letters, proof of financial support

% Legal and adjudicative rules (converted to Prolog predicates)
% USCIS may adjust a U nonimmigrant if all eligibility requirements are met
% and in USCIS's opinion continued presence is justified (humanitarian, family unity, or public interest).
% The applicant bears the burden to prove eligibility and that discretion should be exercised in their favor.

% Adverse factors are present when credible evidence shows violent or threatening conduct.
adverse_factors_present :-
    arrest_in_2019,
    arrest_report_indicates_fear_and_damage.

% Mitigating evidence can offset adverse factors, but must sufficiently outweigh them by a preponderance.
% Here, the petitioner has some mitigating evidence (dismissal of charges, pretrial program, support letters),
% but the arrest report describes conduct that creates significant adverse weight. For this case we model that
% the mitigating evidence is insufficient to offset the adverse factors.
mitigating_evidence_present :-
    submitted_mitigating_evidence,
    ( entered_pretrial_program ; charges_nolle_prossed ).

mitigating_offsets_adverse :-
    mitigating_evidence_present,
    % Even though mitigating evidence exists, it does not sufficiently outweigh the adverse factors
    % when the arrest report indicates victim fear and destructive acts.
    \+ ( adverse_factors_present, insufficient_mitigation_to_offset ).
    
% Represent the assessor's conclusion that the mitigation is insufficient in this factual scenario.
insufficient_mitigation_to_offset :-
    adverse_factors_present,
    % Specifics of the arrest report produce strong adverse weight:
    arrest_report_indicates_fear_and_damage.

% Favorable exercise of discretion is true when no adverse factors exist OR adverse factors are present
% but the mitigating evidence sufficiently offsets them.
favorable_discretion :-
    \+ adverse_factors_present
    ;
    ( adverse_factors_present, mitigating_offsets_adverse ).

% Eligibility is met only if petitioner has U status, filed I-485, and discretion is exercised favorably.
eligibility_met :-
    has_u_status,
    filed_i485,
    favorable_discretion.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a native and citizen of Iran who left Iran for Pakistan in 1983 to avoid military conscription. His immediate family had already resettled in the United States. While in Pakistan he enrolled in the United Nations refugee program and applied for refugee status to the United States through the U.S. embassy. Fearing return of some refugees to Iran, he traveled to Canada in 1984 using a Portuguese passport that did not belong to him, with stops in Rome and Milan, and applied for asylum in Canada. Shortly thereafter he attempted to enter the United States, initially gave a false name at the border, then disclosed his real identity and requested asylum. He was placed in exclusion proceedings in 1984 and remained in Canada, where he continued the refugee process and eventually became a Canadian citizen.

In 1993 he entered the United States with a visitor visa and an approved waiver of inadmissibility. He did not depart when his authorized stay expired and began living and working in the United States. His U.S. citizen father filed an immigrant petition for him in 2003 (approved in 2009). His father died in 2013, and the petitioner submitted a request for humanitarian reinstatement of that petition. The petitioner filed for adjustment of status in June 2016.

While his adjustment application was pending, he was arrested for possession of a controlled substance (a later conviction based on that arrest was vacated). He was arrested again in 2017 and, after a jury trial, was convicted of offenses related to possession of access card information with intent to defraud. He was sentenced to three years of probation and 80 hours of community service. He was also charged with possession of drug paraphernalia but was not convicted on that charge. Court records show he missed multiple court dates in 2019–2021 and did not complete the required community service within the three-year period following his conviction.

The petitioner reports that he has long family and community ties in the United States and lived in the country for many years. He says he owned and operated two auto mechanic businesses (the first closed in 2005, the second in 2013). He acknowledges a period of drug addiction after his second business closed, stating he used methamphetamine to cope with depression, sought treatment beginning in 2016, entered residential rehabilitation in 2018, and completed a program in November 2018. He has provided a personal statement, statements from his spouse and children, letters of community support, medical documentation for a heart condition, treatment records from rehabilitation facilities, and school records for his adult children. On his 2016 adjustment application he indicated he was not employed; on appeal he states he currently "works seven days a week" but has not submitted evidence of current employment.

On appeal, the petitioner does not contest the finding of inadmissibility but challenges the decision denying his waiver application. He asserts he merits a favorable exercise of discretion and asks that adjudicators give greater weight to his family and community ties in the United States, his long residence here, his claimed rehabilitation from drug addiction, his health needs, and his history of business ownership. He also maintains his innocence regarding the access-card offenses, stating he found two cards at a gas pump, put them in his wallet, and forgot about them.
statutes:
Any noncitizen convicted of, or who admits having committed, or who admits committing acts which constitute the essential elements of a CIMT ( other than a purely political offense) or an attempt or conspiracy to commit such a crime is inadmissible. Section 212(a)(2)(A)(i) of the Act. Noncitizens who are inadmissible under section 212(a)(2)(A)(i) of the Act may seek a discretionary waiver of inadmissibility under section 212(h) of the Act. Such discretionary waiver is available if denial of admission would result in extreme hardship to the noncitizen's U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident spouse, parent, son, or daughter. Section 212(h)(l)(B) of the Act.

A noncitizen who establishes statutory eligibility for a waiver under sections 2 l 2(h)( 1 )(A) or (B) of the Act must also demonstrate that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) should favorably exercise its discretion and grant the waiver. Section 204(1) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1154(1), provides that an applicant who immediately prior to the death of a qualifying relative was the beneficiary of a pending or approved petition for classification as an immediate relative, who resided in the United States at the time of the death of the qualifying relative, and who continues to reside in the United States shall have their application for adjustment of status based upon a family relationship, and any related applications, adjudicated notwithstanding the death of the qualifying relative, unless a discretionary determination is made that approval would not be in the public interest.

label: Dismissed

```prolog
% Facts about the petitioner (all arity 0 as requested)
native_of_iran.
left_iran_1983_to_avoid_conscription.
immediate_family_resettled_in_us.
enrolled_un_refugee_program_in_pakistan.
applied_for_refugee_resettlement_through_us_embassy_pakistan.
traveled_to_canada_using_portuguese_passport_not_his.
applied_for_asylum_in_canada.
attempted_entry_us_initial_false_name_then_disclosed_and_requested_asylum.
asylum_denied_nov_1984_discretionary.
remained_in_canada_continued_refugee_process.
became_canadian_citizen.
left_canada_1993_entered_us_visitor_visa_with_waiver_of_inadmissibility.
overstayed_authorized_stay_and_lived_worked_in_us.
operated_auto_mechanic_business1_closed_2005.
operated_auto_mechanic_business2_closed_2013.
i130_filed_by_father_june2003.
i130_approved_2009.
father_died_2013.
timely_request_for_humanitarian_reinstatement_204l.
filed_adjustment_of_status_june2016.
arrested_possession_ca_hs_11377a.
pleaded_guilty_conviction_vacated_on_constitutional_grounds.
arrested_2017_convicted_after_jury_trial_california_penal_code.
resided_in_us_at_time_of_father_death.
continues_to_reside_in_us.

% Legal status facts derived/assumed from the narrative.
% The record reflects at least one post-2016 criminal conviction (2017),
% which is treated here as a conviction that may constitute a CIMT.
convicted_of_cimt.

% Rule: inadmissible if convicted of a CIMT under section 212(a)(2)(A)(i).
inadmissible_under_212a2aiai :-
    convicted_of_cimt.

% Statutory availability of a discretionary waiver under 212(h):
% available only if inadmissible due to a CIMT and denial would result in extreme hardship
% to a US citizen or LPR spouse, parent, son, or daughter.
statutory_waiver_available_under_212h :-
    inadmissible_under_212a2aiai,
    extreme_hardship_to_usc_or_lpr_relative.

% The record does not establish extreme hardship to a qualifying US or LPR spouse/parent/son/daughter.
% We define the predicate so it exists (and currently fails).
extreme_hardship_to_usc_or_lpr_relative :-
    fail.

% Even if statutorily eligible, the applicant must also show that USCIS should favorably
% exercise its discretion. Based on the criminal record and other equities described,
% the record does not establish that discretion should be exercised favorably.
favorable_exercise_of_discretion :-
    fail.

% Eligibility under section 204(l): beneficiary of a pending/approved petition immediately
% prior to the death of a qualifying relative, resided in the US at time of death,
% and continues to reside in the US.
eligible_under_204l :-
    i130_approved_2009,
    father_died_2013,
    resided_in_us_at_time_of_father_death,
    continues_to_reside_in_us,
    timely_request_for_humanitarian_reinstatement_204l.

% Not inadmissible predicate (succeeds only if the inadmissibility predicate fails).
not_inadmissible :-
    \+ inadmissible_under_212a2aiai.

% Final eligibility rule:
% If the applicant is eligible under 204(l) and either (not inadmissible)
% or (inadmissible but a statutory waiver is available and discretion should be
% exercised favorably), then eligibility_met.
eligibility_met :-
    eligible_under_204l,
    (   not_inadmissible
    ;   (   inadmissible_under_212a2aiai,
            statutory_waiver_available_under_212h,
            favorable_exercise_of_discretion
        )
    ).

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a civil engineer who filed an I-140 petition seeking second-preference (EB-2) classification as an advanced-degree professional and a national interest waiver so he could pursue his proposed business without a job offer or labor certification. In his Proposed Endeavor and supporting materials he describes plans to establish a company that will specialize in architectural design, construction, and consulting for renovations and new residential and commercial real estate projects. He states the company will be headquartered in [redacted] and operate in southeast Florida, and submitted a business plan explaining that the business will address the U.S. housing shortage by increasing the availability of high-quality residential units. His filing included a personal statement, the Proposed Endeavor document, the business plan, and letters submitted in response to the request for evidence (identified in the record by initials such as W-V-, E-A-, and E-T-).

After the Texas Service Center denied the petition, the petitioner appealed. He argued that the denial contained numerous factual errors and cited evidence not in the record, which prevented him from having a fair opportunity to contest the decision. Specifically, he asserted the adjudicator mischaracterized his professional background and proposed work—describing him as intending to work in the mining/mineral sector—whereas his submitted documents consistently describe a construction/architectural business. He also contended the decision used the wrong company reference and attributed projects and letters to him or to responses to the RFE that, he says, are not present in the record. These misstatements and references to nonexistent evidence are the primary grounds he cites for appealing the denial.
statutes:
To qualify for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, a petitioner must establish they are an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(A) of the Act. If a petitioner establishes eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then demonstrate that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec . 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may , as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: 1 See Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts (and Third

• The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the record
petitioner_is_civil_engineer.
filed_i140_eb2_national_interest_waiver.
proposed_endeavor_establish_construction_company.
company_will_specialize_architectural_design_construction_consulting.
headquartered_in_southeast_florida.
submitted_business_plan.
business_plan_addresses_housing_crisis.
personal_statement_consistent_with_business_plan.
record_contains_response_letters.    % the record included response letters submitted with the RFE
director_mischaracterized_evidence.  % director referenced mining/mineral sector and wrong company name
director_referenced_evidence_not_in_record. % director cited invitation and letters not actually in record

% For EB-2 underlying classification: the petitioner is represented in the record as qualifying
advanced_degree_professional.  % petitioner filed under EB-2 and is represented as an advanced-degree professional

% Supporting inferences drawn from the facts
endeavor_addresses_critical_national_problem :- business_plan_addresses_housing_crisis.
endeavor_has_economic_and_social_benefit :- company_will_specialize_architectural_design_construction_consulting.
endeavor_has_regional_and_potential_broader_impact :- headquartered_in_southeast_florida.

petitioner_has_relevant_qualifications :- petitioner_is_civil_engineer, advanced_degree_professional.
petitioner_submitted_coherent_plan_and_vision :- submitted_business_plan, personal_statement_consistent_with_business_plan.

record_contains_supporting_documents :- record_contains_response_letters.

% Dhanasar prongs mapped to predicates (arity 0)
% 1) The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance.
substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    endeavor_addresses_critical_national_problem,
    endeavor_has_economic_and_social_benefit,
    endeavor_has_regional_and_potential_broader_impact.

% 2) The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor.
well_positioned :-
    petitioner_has_relevant_qualifications,
    petitioner_submitted_coherent_plan_and_vision,
    record_contains_supporting_documents.

% 3) On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
benefit_on_balance :-
    endeavor_has_economic_and_social_benefit,
    endeavor_addresses_critical_national_problem.

% Underlying classification requirement: advanced degree or exceptional ability
underlying_classification_met :-
    advanced_degree_professional.

% Overall eligibility: all conditions necessary to Accept the case
eligibility_met :-
    underlying_classification_met,
    substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    benefit_on_balance.

% Entrypoint and decision output as required
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
```

text:
The Petitioner, a U.S. citizen, filed Form I-129F seeking the Beneficiary's admission to the United States as his fiance(e). The California Service Center denied the petition on the ground that he was subject to a filing limitation and had not shown that he merited a discretionary waiver, and he appealed.

On the Form I-129F, the Petitioner answered "no" to questions asking whether he had a criminal history and whether he needed a multiple-filer waiver. Government records in the file indicate that he had been arrested and charged for domestic violence offenses, had been subject to two restraining orders, and had previously filed two fiance(e) petitions that were approved.

In response to the Director's RFEs, the Petitioner submitted a document titled "Request for Out of Custody Complaint Declaration of Probably Cause" describing the circumstances of his criminal issues and restraining orders. According to that document, the events described were not the parties' first domestic violence incident; it states that police had been called in an earlier incident, that a temporary restraining order was imposed on the Petitioner, and that he was required to attend anger management classes. The Petitioner also submitted a statement saying that he had acted in ways he was not proud of, that he supported his children financially and emotionally, and that he had complied with the legal system. He submitted court documentation showing that he began parenting classes on April 6, 2015, and completed them on July 8, 2015, along with three letters offered to show rehabilitation and an employer letter describing him as a dependable and consistent employee.

On appeal, the Petitioner reasserted his request for an extraordinary-circumstances waiver and submitted additional documentation. He said he sought the waiver because of country conditions in Afghanistan and fears for the Beneficiary's safety, described his fiancee as "a prisoner in her own home," and submitted August 2021 news articles about the Taliban's resumption of control over Afghanistan and restrictions on women's movement and opportunities. The record does not include a statement from the Beneficiary addressing those asserted safety concerns.

The record reflects that the Petitioner disclosed a previous spouse on the Form I-129F. The decision describes the evidence submitted with the petition as showing that the marriage to that spouse ended in divorce in 2017, and the evidence submitted on appeal as showing a 2015 divorce from the same spouse. The record does not include documentation establishing that he divorced the beneficiary of his first fiance(e) petition. In his statement, he said he had brought that prior beneficiary to the United States, that within a year she wanted to return to her family, and that he purchased her return flight.
statutes:
Section 214( d)( 1) of the Act states that a fiance(e) petition can be approved only if a petitioner establishes that the parties have previously met in person within two years before the date of filing the fiance(e) petition, have a bona fide intention to marry, and are legally able and actually willing to conclude a valid marriage in the United States within a period of 90 days after a beneficiary's arrival. Page six of the Form I-129F instructions states: Item Numbers 1. -3.c. Criminal Information. Indicate whether you have ever been the subject of a temporary or permanent protection order or restraining order ( either civil or criminal) related to any of the crimes specified below, or arrested, or convicted of any of the crimes specified below. If you were ever arrested or convicted of any of the specified crimes, you must submit certified copies of all court and police records showing the charges and disposition for every arrest or conviction. You must do so even if your records were sealed, expunged, or otherwise cleared, and regardless

of whether anyone, including a judge, law enforcement officer, or attorney, informed you that you no longer have a criminal record. The International Marriage Broker Regulation Act (IMBRA) and section 214(d)(l) of the Act require fiancee visa petitioners to disclose whether they have been convicted of certain specified crimes. 1 If a petitioner has been convicted by a court or military tribunal of any of the specified crimes, 2 they are required to submit certified copies ofall court and police records showing the charges and dispositions for each of these convictions. See generally USeIS Policy Memorandum HQOPRD 70/6.2.11, International Marriage Broker Regulation Act Implementation Guidance 1-2 (Jul. 21, 2006). These records then become part of the I-129F, Petition for Alien Fiance(e), record, and if the petition is approved, the Department of State will disclose the relevant criminal convictions to the beneficiary during their consular interview. Id. The IMBRA also requires a petitioner to request a multiple filer waiver if they have filed two or more fiance(e) petitions. Id. If a petitioner has ever been convicted of a violent criminal offense against a person or persons, USeIS will not grant a filing limitations waiver unless you submit evidence to demonstrate that extraordinary circumstances exist. See, Form I-129F, Petition for Alien Fiance(e) instrnctions at page 7, https://www.uscis.gov/i-l29f. Adjudicators have the discretion to validate assertions or corroborate evidence and information by consulting users or other governmental files, systems, and databases, or by obtaining publicly available information. 8 U.S.e. § 1357(b).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
filed_i129f('2022-09-30').
answered_no_criminal.
answered_no_multiple_filer_waiver.
government_records_show_arrests.
government_records_show_restraining_orders.
previous_petitions_approved(2).
rfe_issued('2023-01-25').
rfe_issued('2023-06-16').
response_submitted.
declaration_of_probable_cause_submitted.
alleged_domestic_violence.
medical_treatment_sought.
child_reported_abuse.
prior_temporary_restraining_order.
parenting_classes_completed.
character_letters_submitted.
requested_extraordinary_circumstances_waiver.
waiver_based_on_country_conditions_and_safety.

% Required eligibility predicates (set to fail where the record does not establish them)
met_in_person_within_2_years :- fail.
bona_fide_intent :- fail.
legally_able_and_willing_90_days :- fail.

% There is no record here of a conviction; only arrests/charges and restraining orders.
conviction_of_violent_crime :- fail.

% Derived determinations based on the facts and rules
% Criminal history disclosure is correct only if the petitioner did not answer "no"
% while government records show arrests or restraining orders.
disclosed_criminal_history_correctly :-
    \+ ( answered_no_criminal,
         ( government_records_show_arrests
         ; government_records_show_restraining_orders
         )
       ).

filed_two_or_more_petitions :-
    previous_petitions_approved(N),
    N >= 2.

% If the petitioner filed two or more petitions, a multiple-filer waiver must be requested.
multiple_filer_waiver_complied :-
    \+ ( filed_two_or_more_petitions,
         answered_no_multiple_filer_waiver
       ).

% Either there is no violent conviction, or if there is one an extraordinary-circumstances waiver was properly shown.
non_conviction_or_waiver_granted :-
    \+ conviction_of_violent_crime
    ;
    ( conviction_of_violent_crime,
      requested_extraordinary_circumstances_waiver,
      waiver_based_on_country_conditions_and_safety
    ).

% All conditions required for approval per section 214(d)(1) and IMBRA-related filing rules.
eligibility_met :-
    petitioner_us_citizen,
    filed_i129f(_Date),
    met_in_person_within_2_years,
    bona_fide_intent,
    legally_able_and_willing_90_days,
    disclosed_criminal_history_correctly,
    multiple_filer_waiver_complied,
    non_conviction_or_waiver_granted.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a U.S. citizen who filed a Form I-600 in March 2019 on behalf of a child from Nigeria, asserting the child had been abandoned by both birth parents. She initially received approval of the petition in June 2019. As part of routine overseas review, a consular officer in Lagos examined the petitioner's submitted evidence, which included an extract from a Nigeria Police Force crime diary (a police extract). An Assistant Regional Security Officer conducted a site visit to Edo State, where a senior police official told the consular team that the police extract was fraudulent and that the officer named as its author, Sgt. J-1-, had been arrested. Following a Form I-604 investigation by the consulate, the petition was returned for potential revocation and the petitioner received a notice of intent to revoke in November 2021.

In response to that notice, the petitioner submitted a personal statement and supporting documents, including online articles referencing Sgt. J-1-, the police extract, and letters from the Nigeria Police Force related to J-1-'s request for a name change. The consulate reviewed her response. After further action by the National Benefits Center, the petitioner timely filed an appeal and resubmitted her response and a brief reiterating the child’s eligibility.

On appeal, the petitioner’s stated reason for contesting the revocation is that the determination about the police extract’s credibility was incorrect: she argues that Sgt. J-1- did not author the police extract, and therefore his arrest should not affect the validity of the police extract or the child’s claimed abandonment.
statutes:
A child who meets the definition of orphan under section 101 (b)(1 )(F)(i) of the Act is eligible for classification as the immediate relative of a U.S . citizen. 8 C.F.R. § 204.3. An orphan is defined as a child, under the age of 16 at the time a petition is filed on his or her behalf, who is an orphan because ofthe death or disappearance of, abandonment or desertion by, or separation or loss from, both parents, or for whom the sole or surviving parent is incapable of providing the proper care and has in writing irrevocably released the child for emigration and adoption; who has been adopted abroad by a United States citizen, or who is corning to the United States for adoption by a United States citizen; provided, that the Secretary of Homeland Security is satisfied that proper care will be furnished if the child is admitted to the United States. Section lOl(b)(l)(F)(i) of the Act. Regarding the revocation of approved visa petitions, section 205 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1155, states the following: "The Secretary of Homeland Security may , at any time, for what he deems to be good and sufficient cause, revoke the approval of any petition approved by him under section 204. Such revocation shall be effective as of the date of approval of any such petition."

The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.3(k)(l), regarding a consular officer's I-604, Determination on Child for Adoption (I-604), provides, in pertinent part: An I-604 investigation must be completed in every orphan case. The investigation must be completed by a consular officer except when the petition is properly filed at a Service office overseas, in which case it must be completed by a Service officer. . . . In any instance where an I-604 investigation reveals negative information sufficient to sustain a denial or revocation, the investigation report, supporting documentation, and petition shall be forwarded to the appropriate Service office for action. Depending on the circumstances surrounding the case, the I-604 investigation shall include, but shall not necessarily be limited to, document checks, telephonic checks, interview(s) with the natural parent(s), and/or a field investigation." The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.3(b) states, in pertinent part, the following: Abandonment by both parents means that the parents have willfully forsaken all parental rights, obligations, and claims to the child, as well as all control over and possession of the child, without intending to transfer or without transferring these rights to any specific persons.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

text:
The petitioner filed a Form I-360 seeking Special Immigrant Juvenile (SIJ) classification and based the petition on a South Carolina Family Court "Final Order." The underlying court case began in 2019 when the petitioner's mother asked the juvenile court for sole custody of her three children, including the petitioner. The Final Order was entered in 2021, at which time the petitioner was 18 years old. In that order the court stated that it had jurisdiction over the petitioner, described the petitioner as dependent on and brought under the court’s jurisdiction “due to exceptional circumstances,” and awarded sole custody to his mother. The court found that the petitioner had been abandoned by his biological father several years earlier, that no family members in Honduras could protect or care for him, that reunification with his father or other family in Honduras was not possible (in part because his father was incarcerated), and that returning to Honduras would place the petitioner at risk to his safety and security.

U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services denied the petitioner’s I-360, concluding the petitioner could not show the juvenile court had exercised jurisdiction over him as a juvenile because he was over 18 when the Final Order was entered. The petitioner appealed that denial, submitting a brief contesting the Director’s determination and arguing that, because the Family Court cited South Carolina statutes that treat certain persons over 18 as children in specified circumstances, the court had in fact exercised jurisdiction over him as a juvenile. The petitioner also provided court decisions interpreting sections of South Carolina’s family code to support this position.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b). 1 Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court; alternatively, the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 10l(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(l). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the juvenile's best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204. l l(c)(2). 1 The Department of Homeland Security issued a final rule, effective April 7, 2022, amending its regulations governing the requirements and procedures for petitioners who seek SIJ classification. See Special Immigrant Juvenile Petitions, 87 Fed. Reg . 13066 (Mar. 8, 2022) (revising 8 C.F.R. §§ 204, 205, 245).

USCIS has sole authority to implement the SIJ provisions of the Act and regulation. Homeland Security Act of 2002, Pub. L. No. 107-296, §§ 471(a), 451(b), 462(c), 116 Stat. 2135 (2002). SIJ classification may only be granted upon the consent of the Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), through USCIS, when the petitioner meets all other eligibility criteria and establishes that the request for SIJ classification is bona fide, which requires the petitioner to establish that a primary reason the required juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under State law.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text:
The applicant was admitted to the United States in U-3 nonimmigrant status in June 2018. Her U-3 status was valid through June 2021. In April 2021 she filed Form I-539 to extend/change nonimmigrant status, and in May 2021 she filed a Form I-485 seeking adjustment of status based on her U classification. USCIS approved her April 2021 I-539 in September 2021, extending her U-3 status through September 2022. The Vermont Service Center denied her initial I-485 and later denied her combined motion to reopen and reconsider. She filed a second I-539 in March 2023 (denied in September 2023) and submitted a second U-based I-485 in December 2023, which remains pending.

On appeal, the applicant contends USCIS erred by not adjudicating her I-485 in a timely manner. She argues that USCIS should have rejected her May 2021 filing so she could refile while still in U nonimmigrant status and that approval of her Form I-765 created an expectation that her I-485 would be held in abeyance for future adjudication. She requests that USCIS treat the filing of a new U adjustment application nunc pro tunc on humanitarian grounds.
statutes: U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may in its discretion adjust the status of an individual admitted into the United States as a U nonimmigrant to that of a lawful permanent resident if, among other requirements, he or she has been physically present in the United States for a continuous period of at least three years since the date of admission as a U nonimmigrant and establishes that his or her continued presence in the United States is justified on humanitarian grounds, to ensure family unity, or is otherwise in the public interest. Section 245(m)(l) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(b)(3), (6). Implementing regulations require a U adjustment applicant to establish, among other requirements, that she "[ w ]as lawfully admitted to the United States" as a U nonimmigrant, she "[ c ]ontinues to hold such status at the time of application," and she "[h ]as continuous physical presence for 3 years" in the United States. 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(b)(2)(i)-(ii), (b)(3). An applicant bears the burden of establishing eligibility and that discretion should be exercised in her favor. Section 291 of the Act, 8 U.S.C . § 1361; 8 C.F.R. § 245.24(d)(l 1); Matter ofChawathe, 25 l&N Dec. 369,375 (AAO 2010).

text: The Petitioner is a native and citizen of El Salvador who seeks special immigrant juvenile classification. In 2022, when he was 17 years old, a Florida circuit court issued an order on a petition for temporary custody by extended family that authorized his uncle to exercise all the rights and responsibilities of a legal parent in the United States. The Petitioner submitted that custody order and the underlying custody petition in support of his Form I-360. The custody petition included information concerning parental reunification and best interests. After USCIS requested additional evidence, he again submitted the custody order and custody petition and also provided his birth certificate. The Director of the National Benefits Center denied the petition, stating that the custody order lacked the judicial determinations needed to support special immigrant juvenile classification, and the matter was then appealed. On appeal, the Petitioner argues that under Florida custody law the order should be understood as permanent rather than temporary and as containing the necessary findings. He also contends that the state judge reviewed evidence and heard testimony before issuing the order and that, together with the custody petition's discussion of parental reunification and best interests, the order should be read as reflecting findings that reunification with one or both parents was not viable because of parental maltreatment and that returning him to El Salvador would not be in his best interest.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 10l(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(b). Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(c)(l). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Section 10l(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(c)(2). U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) has sole authority to implement the SIJ provisions of the Act and regulation. Homeland Security Act of 2002, Pub . L. No . 107-296, §§ 47l(a), 451(b), 462( c ), 116 Stat. 2135 (2002). SIJ classification may only be granted upon the consent ofthe Secretary

of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), through USCIS, when the petitioner meets all other eligibility criteria and establishes that the request for SIJ classification is bona fide, which requires the petitioner to establish that a primary reason the required juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101 ( a )(27)(J)(i)-(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.11 (b )( 5). USCIS may also withhold consent if evidence materially conflicts with the eligibility requirements such that the record reflects that the request for SIJ classification was not bona fide. 8 C.F.R. § 204. l l(b)(5).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

text:
The petitioner states that she married a former U.S. citizen husband in 2017 in Alabama and lived with him for a short period that year. She says he initially supported her immigration process and filed immigration paperwork on her behalf, but the relationship later deteriorated: she reports that he expelled her from their home in 2017 and subsequently filed for divorce. The petitioner submitted a divorce judgment issued in 2021 shortly before she filed her own petition that year.

In support of her account, the petitioner provided a personal statement describing the start of the relationship, the later difficulties, and her expulsion from the marital home. In response to a Request for Evidence, she submitted a second statement saying they shared a residence in Alabama and a joint bank account, and she explained that she did not have wedding photographs or other typical evidence because she had been thrown out of the home. Documentary evidence she submitted includes a bank account statement dated March 2021 listing her as a co-owner, several monthly bank statements dated after their separation (April–May 2019; February–March 2021; March–April 2021) addressed to her former spouse at an address different from the claimed joint residence, and IRS documentation showing the couple filed a 2016 federal tax return as married filing jointly. She also provided affidavits from her father and a family friend stating that the couple married in 2017 and lived together as husband and wife until their separation that year.

The petitioner appealed the denial of her Form I-360. On appeal she argued that the adjudicator gave little weight to her evidence and improperly requested additional documentation; she asserted that part of her former spouse’s abusive behavior was withdrawing his immigration filing for her and that this left the relevant documents in his possession after he expelled her. She also contended that denial would cause extreme hardship to her and to her son, who has special medical needs she says cannot be met in her country of citizenship, Togo. She submitted medical records and photographs of her son and stated that he was born from another relationship after her separation from the former husband.
statutes:
A petitioner who is the former spouse of a U.S. citizen may self-petition for immigrant classification if the petitioner married the U.S . citizen spouse in good faith, and the petitioner demonstrates a connection between the legal termination of the marriage within the past two years and the U.S. citizen spouse's battery or extreme cruelty perpetrated against the petitioner. Section 204(a)(l)(A)(iii)(II)(aa)(CC)(ccc) of the Act. Petitioners must also establish that they are eligible for immigrant classification based on the relationship, resided with the former spouse, were subjected to battery or extreme cruelty by the former spouse, and are persons of good moral character. Id. at 204( a)( 1 )(A)(iii). Evidence ofentry into the marriage in good faith may include documents showing that one spouse has been listed as the other's spouse on insurance policies, property leases, income tax forms, or bank accounts; evidence regarding their courtship, wedding ceremony, shared residence, and experiences; birth certificates of any children born during the marriage; police, medical, or court documents providing information about the relationship; affidavits from individuals with personal knowledge of the relationship; and any other credible evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(i), (vii).

Evidence ofjoint residence may include employment records, utility receipts, school records, hospital or medical records, birth certificates of children, deeds, mortgages, rental records, insurance policies, affidavits or any other type ofrelevant credible evidence ofresidency. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(iii). We consider any credible evidence relevant to the petition, and the determination of what evidence is credible, and the weight given such evidence lies within our sole discretion. Section 204(a)(l)(J) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(i).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

text:
The petitioner is a Buddhist temple that filed Form I-360 seeking to classify the beneficiary to perform services as a monk and dharma teacher. In connection with that petition, the petitioner responded to a director’s request for evidence by submitting bank statements and, on appeal, submitted a 2024 property tax statement showing taxes and exemptions on the temple property, a warranty deed for the property, utility bills in the abbot’s name, and photographs of the temple showing worship areas and living quarters. The petitioner also filed Form I-129 on March 5, 2024 seeking R-1 nonimmigrant status for the beneficiary; that Form I-129 was approved on May 7, 2024, and indicated the beneficiary was residing at the temple at the time of filing.

The petitioner appealed the director’s denial, which was based on the director’s finding that the record did not establish the beneficiary’s continuous full-time compensated religious employment for the two-year period identified in the decision (July 19, 2022 through July 18, 2024). The director emphasized a lack of verifiable evidence that the petitioner had provided the promised non-salaried compensation (described as food, shelter, clothing, and payment for living expenses) and noted the absence of a lease agreement as evidence of room and board. In its appeal, the petitioner asserted that the bank statements and the property ownership and related documents it submitted demonstrate that it paid the beneficiary’s living expenses and provided living quarters at the temple, and therefore challenged the director’s factual basis for denial.
statutes:
Non-profit religious organizations may petition for aliens, or an alien may petition on their own behalf, to immigrate to the United States to perform full-time, compensated religious work as ministers, in religious vocations, or in religious occupations. The alien must meet certain eligibility criteria, including membership in a religious denomination and continuous religious work experience for at least the two-year period before the petition filing date. See generally section 203(b)(4) of the Act (providing classification to qualified special immigrant religious workers as described in section 101(a)(27)(C) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1101(a)(27)(C). Specifically, the alien must have been working continuously in a full time, compensated status in one of the three types of qualifying positions, either in the United States or abroad, for at least the two­ year period immediately preceding the filing of the petition. If the alien gained this experience in the United States, the evidence of their work experience must show that they received salaried compensation, non-salaried compensation, or provided for their own support and that of any

dependents. Employment outside the United States during the two-year period must be documented by comparable evidence. 8 C.F.R. §§ 204.5(m)(4), (11).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text: The Applicant seeks lawful permanent residence based on U nonimmigrant status. The Director of the Vermont Service Center denied the Applicant's Form I-485 after concluding that the required Form I-693 medical examination had not been submitted. The matter is before the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal. According to the Director, the Applicant did not file a medical examination with the initial U adjustment application, and the Director later issued a request for evidence seeking the medical examination and other evidence. The record described by the agency indicates that the Applicant responded to that request without including a medical examination, but he did submit other requested evidence and documentation relating to his claim that he was unable to provide the medical examination by the deadline. On appeal, the Applicant submitted a medical examination, and the AAO described the appeal filing as including new evidence that the Director had not yet reviewed.
statutes: Section 245(m) of the Act contains the eligibility requirements for individuals seeking to adjust status to that of a lawful permanent resident (LPR) based on having been granted U status. In addition, an applicant for adjustment of status under 245(m) must comply with the general eligibility and documentary requirements to adjust status at 8 C.F.R. § 245.5, which requires that the applicant "have a medical examination by a designated civil surgeon, whose report setting forth the findings of the mental and physical condition of the applicant, including compliance with section 212(a)(l)(A)(ii) of the Act, shall be incorporated into the record."
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
% by a designated civil surgeon to be incorporated into the record.

% Rule: evidence submitted on appeal is treated as incorporated into the record
% for purposes of this decision (assumption based on the facts provided).
evidence_on_appeal_considered.

% A completed Form I-693 is considered "in the record" if it was submitted
% initially or if it was submitted on appeal and such evidence is considered.
completed_form_i693_in_record :-
    i693_submitted_on_appeal,

% The medical requirement is satisfied only if a completed I-693 by a
```

text: The Petitioner, an information technology services company, filed an L-1A petition to employ the Beneficiary as its chairman and chief executive officer. The Director of the California Service Center denied the petition, concluding that the record did not establish qualifying continuous foreign employment for one year in the three years before filing, managerial or executive employment abroad, managerial or executive employment in the United States, or that the employment would be temporary, and the matter came forward on appeal. On Form I-129 and in an accompanying letter, the Petitioner stated that the Beneficiary's U.S. duties would remain the same as his Canadian role. The Petitioner submitted evidence showing that the petitioning U.S. entity had one employee, stated that an affiliate had ten employees in the United States, stated that its Canadian affiliate employed more than 100 full-time employees, and submitted an organizational chart showing 111 positions subordinate to the Beneficiary together with a list of responsibilities in four categories. In response to a request for evidence, the Petitioner submitted a longer job description that repeated the earlier version and added further elements within those categories. According to the adjudicator's description of the record, the organizational chart purported to show the Canadian company's structure, but the Petitioner only partially addressed requests for corroboration and provided only fragmentary information about the duties of a small number of claimed subordinates. The Director's denial also stated that changes to the Beneficiary's list of responsibilities raised credibility questions and that the Petitioner had not provided sufficient detail about subordinate staff. The appeal record also included email messages that, according to the adjudicator's description, indicated the Beneficiary had authority over individuals named in the conversations but did not fully corroborate the Petitioner's claimed Canadian company structure. On appeal, the Petitioner argued that the Director did not give proper weight to the email printouts and that the post-request job description did not change the Beneficiary's duties but only added detail.
statutes: To establish eligibility for the L-lA nonimmigrant visa classification, a qualifying organization must have employed the beneficiary "in a capacity that is managerial, executive, or involves specialized knowledge," for one continuous year within three years preceding the beneficiary's application for admission into the United States. Section 101(a)(15)(L) of the Act. In addition, the beneficiary must seek to enter the United States temporarily to continue rendering his or her services to the same employer or a subsidiary or affiliate thereof in a managerial or executive capacity. Id. The petitioner must also establish that the beneficiary's prior education, training, and employment qualify him or her to perform the intended services in the United States. 8 C.F.R. § 214.2(1)(3).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
emails_show_directives.
unaudited_canadian_financials_ca_salaries(2180000).

% Items the record lacks or that the adjudicator found missing/insufficient
continuous_employment_documentation_missing.
missing_subordinate_job_descriptions.
missing_subordinate_salary_info.
temporary_employment_evidence_missing.
prior_qualifications_not_shown.

% Rules interpreting the evidence in relation to statutory/regulatory requirements.
% To be considered employed one continuous year abroad within the relevant period,
% there must be documentary proof of that continuous employment.
employed_one_year_abroad :-
    % documentary proof must be present; if documentation is missing, this fails
    \+ continuous_employment_documentation_missing,
    beneficiary_owner.  % beneficiary-owner status alone is not sufficient without documentation

% To be considered to have been employed in a managerial or executive capacity abroad,
% there must be evidence that the beneficiary actually performed managerial/executive duties
% supported by subordinate staff and documentation (job descriptions, payroll/salary, etc.).
employed_managerial_abroad :-
    ( has_org_chart ; emails_show_directives ),
    % must have subordinate job descriptions and salary info to show staff actually relieved
    % the beneficiary of day-to-day tasks — absence of those items defeats the showing.
    \+ missing_subordinate_job_descriptions,
    \+ missing_subordinate_salary_info.

% To be employed in the United States in a managerial/executive capacity, the petitioner
% must show that the offered U.S. position is managerial/executive, with supporting evidence
% that subordinates will exist and perform non-managerial tasks (job descriptions, payroll).
will_be_employed_managerial_us :-
    provided_job_description_extended,
    \+ missing_subordinate_job_descriptions,
    \+ missing_subordinate_salary_info.

% To establish temporary intent, the petitioner must show the employment in the U.S. is
% for a temporary period consistent with L-1A rules; lack of such evidence fails this element.
employment_temporary :-
    \+ temporary_employment_evidence_missing.

% Prior education/training/employment must be established to qualify the beneficiary.
prior_qualifications :-
    \+ prior_qualifications_not_shown.

% Eligibility requires all elements to be satisfied.
eligibility_met :-
    employed_one_year_abroad,
    employed_managerial_abroad,
    will_be_employed_managerial_us,
    employment_temporary,
    prior_qualifications.

decision(Result) :-

    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

        ),

        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed Form I-360 seeking classification as a special immigrant juvenile. In October 2023 the City Law Department submitted a petition to the Family Court recommending that the petitioner be committed to the custody of the Department of Human Services pursuant to an Order of Protective Custody. In 2023, when the petitioner was 15 years old, the Family Court issued an order (the SU order) finding the petitioner dependent under 42 Pa.C.S. § 6302 and committing her to the custody of the Department of Human Services. That order also stated that reunification with the petitioner’s parents was not viable due to abandonment under 23 Pa.C.S. § 5402, noting the parents live in Guatemala and were unwilling to allow the petitioner to return to their home, and it found that it would not be in the petitioner’s best interest to return to Guatemala because there was no safe and appropriate home there.

The National Benefits Center director issued a request for evidence asserting the SU order was insufficient because it was unclear whether the order had been signed by an individual authorized to sign such state court orders and asking for evidence that a judge had signed the order. In response, the petitioner submitted a 2024 Amended Order from the Family Court signed by Judge C‑R‑M‑. The amended order states that the petitioner’s 2023 SIJ order is amended to include Judge C‑R‑M‑’s full title under the existing signature line and reiterates the findings contained in the 2023 SU order.

The director denied the petitioner’s SIJ petition in February 2024, concluding the petitioner had not demonstrated eligibility at the time of filing. On appeal, the petitioner argues she submitted the requested document showing the SU order had been signed by a judge, that both the amended order and the SU order were signed by Judge C‑R‑M‑ who is authorized to sign such orders, and that her SIJ order contains the judicial findings referenced above.
statutes: To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, a petitioner must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(b). Petitioner must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section I01 (a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(c)(l). The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioner's best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.1 l(c)(2).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the Administrative Appeals Office case
petitioner_filed_i360.
petitioner_unmarried.
petitioner_under21_at_filing.    % Petitioner was 15 in 2023, so under 21 at filing in Feb 2024

% Juvenile court order facts (2023 order)
juvenile_court_order_2023.
juvenile_court_order_depends_and_committed_to_dhs.  % dependent on court; committed to Dept. of Human Services
juvenile_court_found_reunification_not_viable_due_to_abandonment.  % reunification not viable due to abandonment; parents in Guatemala unwilling
juvenile_court_found_not_in_best_interest_to_return.  % not in petitioner's best interest to return; no safe/appropriate home

% Evidence and procedural history about signature
director_issued_rfe_signature_unclear.  % NBC issued RFE indicating signature unclear
petitioner_submitted_amended_order_2024_signed_by_judge_crm.  % 2024 amended order signed by Judge C-R-M-
amended_order_2024_amended_original_order_include_judge_full_title.  % amendment added judge's full title under existing signature line
amended_order_reiterates_2023_findings.  % amended order reiterated the 2023 findings

% Verification that the juvenile order is signed (derived fact)
juvenile_order_signature_verified :-
    petitioner_submitted_amended_order_2024_signed_by_judge_crm,
    amended_order_2024_amended_original_order_include_judge_full_title,
    amended_order_reiterates_2023_findings.

% SIJ eligibility rules (mapped from statute and regulation)
% To be eligible: unmarried, under 21 at filing, juvenile court order finding inability to reunify due to abuse/neglect/abandonment (or similar),
% petitioner declared dependent or placed in custody, and judicial/administrative determination that return is not in petitioner's best interest.
eligibility_met :-
    petitioner_unmarried,
    petitioner_under21_at_filing,
    juvenile_court_order_2023,
    juvenile_court_order_depends_and_committed_to_dhs,
    juvenile_court_found_reunification_not_viable_due_to_abandonment,
    juvenile_court_found_not_in_best_interest_to_return,
    juvenile_order_signature_verified.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner is a computer software engineer who filed a Form I-140 seeking EB-2 classification as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or, alternatively, an individual of exceptional ability, and he also requested a national interest waiver of the job-offer requirement. The Director of the Texas Service Center denied the petition, and the matter was taken to the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal. At filing, the petitioner described his proposed endeavor as continuing software-development work in technical and managerial roles by seeking employment as a backend developer or full stack developer with unspecified employers. In response to the Director's request for evidence, he stated that after filing he had co-founded a Wyoming-based company with a partner, described the business as a startup marketing software-as-a-service products, and said that establishing a U.S.-based company was progress toward his proposed endeavor. The record therefore reflects a shift between the filing-stage description of the endeavor and the later account centered on a company formed after filing. On appeal, the petitioner reasserted that an opinion letter from a professor of computer science established that the proposed endeavor has national importance, and the appeal brief reproduced excerpts from that letter. As described in the AAO decision, that opinion letter did not acknowledge the petitioner's later-presented company-formation facts, and the AAO characterized that omission as undermining the reliability and sufficiency of the letter and the remaining supporting evidence.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," we set forth a framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions in the precedent decision Matter of

Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884 (AAO 2016). Dhanasar states that, after a petitioner has established eligibility for EB-2 classification, USCIS may, as a matter of discretion, grant a national interest waiver ifthe petitioner demonstrates: (1) that the noncitizen's proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; (2) that the noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor; and (3) that, on balance, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification. See Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver is discretionary in nature). The first prong, substantial merit and national importance, focuses on the specific endeavor that the noncitizen proposes to undertake. The endeavor's merit may be demonstrated in a range of areas such as business, entrepreneurialism, science, technology, culture, health, or education. In determining whether the proposed endeavor has national importance, we consider its potential prospective impact. See Matter ofDhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. at 888-91, for elaboration on these three prongs.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
roles_backend_fullstack.
```
