```prolog
company_saas_startup.                 % Company described as a SaaS startup
director_denied.                      % Director denied the petition
submitted_opinion_letter.             % Opinion letter from a professor submitted on appeal

% EB-2 underlying qualification requirements
% No direct evidence in the record that the petitioner has an advanced degree or qualifies as an exceptional ability individual.
has_advanced_degree :- fail.
exceptional_ability :- fail.

% A petitioner must qualify under EB-2 (advanced degree OR exceptional ability)
eb2_qualified :-
    (   has_advanced_degree
    ;   exceptional_ability
    ).

% Dhanasar prongs
% 1) Substantial merit and national importance
% The proposed endeavor is in RD&I (merit), and an opinion letter exists, but there is no corroborating evidence of prospective national impact in the record.
opinion_letter_supports_national_importance :-
    submitted_opinion_letter.
evidence_of_potential_impact :- fail.

substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    proposed_endeavor_rd_i,
    opinion_letter_supports_national_importance,
    evidence_of_potential_impact.

% 2) Well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor
% The petitioner co-founded a company and is an engineer, but there is no evidence of the specific capabilities, track record, or resources demonstrating he is well positioned.
relevant_experience :- fail.
has_company_resources :- fail.

well_positioned :-
    later_cofounded_wy_company,
    petitioner_software_engineer,
    relevant_experience,
    has_company_resources.

% 3) On balance, beneficial to waive the job offer requirement
% No evidence provided to show that waiver would benefit the U.S. (public benefit, economic impact, or lack of adverse effect on labor market).
evidence_of_public_benefit :- fail.
no_adverse_labor_impact :- fail.

balance_favorable :-
    substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    evidence_of_public_benefit,
    no_adverse_labor_impact.

% Eligibility requires EB-2 qualification AND satisfying Dhanasar prongs
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_qualified,
    substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned,
    balance_favorable.

% Decision and runner (provided structure)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

    ).
```

text:
The petitioner, a digital marketing and website consulting firm, filed an L-1A petition to transfer the beneficiary to the United States to work as vice president of a new office. The California Service Center denied the petition, stating that the record did not establish sufficient physical premises for the new office, qualifying foreign employment for the beneficiary during the relevant period, qualifying managerial or executive employment abroad, and the new office's ability to support a managerial or executive position within one year. The petitioner then filed a motion to reopen; the motion was granted, the petition was denied again on the same grounds, and the matter proceeded on appeal.

The record includes a lease for office space with a term from December 2022 through November 2023 and an attached floor plan showing a basically rectangular room without dimensions. The lease states that the tenant may not permit more than five persons per 1,000 square feet to occupy the premises. After a request for evidence seeking a detailed description of the space and an explanation of how it would house the new business, the petitioner submitted photographs of an irregularly shaped office area with several rows of desks and at least nine workers using laptop computers. The decision describes the photographed room as not appearing to match the rectangular floor plan, and the number of workers shown as greater than the petitioner's stated office size. On appeal, the petitioner also submitted a letter attributed to the landlord of newly leased property stating that the premises could be used for office purposes. A later denial notice stated that the newly submitted lease for that property was not yet in effect when the petition was filed.

As to the beneficiary's employment abroad, the petitioner states that the beneficiary entered the United States in December 2021 in B-2 status, which would place about two years of claimed foreign employment within the three years before filing, and it also asserts that the beneficiary worked for the claimed foreign affiliate from 2016 until December 2021. The record includes an August 1, 2022 letter from an official of the foreign entity stating that the listed details pertain to Assessment Year 2021-2022. The petitioner later submitted what it described as a payroll report for calendar year 2020 produced from copies of original payroll records, but the underlying payroll records and direct evidence of salary payment were not submitted. The record also includes a 2021-2022 Indian income tax return acknowledgement for the owner of the foreign business, but not a similar document for the beneficiary. On appeal, the petitioner stated that the company is not large enough to use an outside payroll service and pointed to bank records; the decision describes the 2021 bank documents as showing various transactions but not identifying salary payments to the beneficiary. The beneficiary was in the United States when the petition was filed in December 2022, the petitioner claimed an arrival date of December 28, 2021 without documentary corroboration, and the record contains evidence, as described in the decision, that the beneficiary was in the United States before December 2021. The petitioner also argues on appeal that the director's conclusion conflicts with USCIS's VIBE program.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for the L-1 A nonimmigrant visa classification in a petition involving a new office, a qualifying organization must have employed the beneficiary in a managerial or executive capacity for one continuous year within three years preceding the beneficiary's application for admission into the United States. 8 C.F.R. § 214.2(1)(3)(v)(B). In addition, the beneficiary must seek to enter the United States temporarily to continue rendering his or her services to the same employer or a subsidiary or affiliate thereof in a managerial or executive capacity. Id.

The petitioner must submit evidence to demonstrate that the new office will be able to support a managerial or executive position within one year. This evidence must establish that the petitioner secured sufficient physical premises to house its operation and disclose the proposed nature and scope of the entity, its organizational structure, its financial goals, and the size of the U.S. investment. See generally, 8 C.F.R. § 214.2(1)(3)(v).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
% Verify continuous one-year employment within three years before application for admission

    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Petitioner seeks employment-based second-preference classification as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree and also seeks a waiver of the job offer requirement based on national interest. The Texas Service Center Director denied the petition, and the matter is on appeal. For the underlying classification, the Petitioner claims that she earned the foreign equivalent of a U.S. baccalaureate degree and has more than five years of progressive experience in her indicated specialty. The appeal brief acknowledges the Director's finding that she had not shown eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, but it does not explain why that finding was incorrect; instead, it generally argues that the Director erred and focuses on the national interest waiver request, including an assertion that she satisfies all of the waiver criteria. The record shows that she has the foreign equivalent of a U.S. bachelor's degree in nursing. The record also contains differing descriptions of her proposed work in the United States. In her initial professional plan and statement, she said that she intended to work as a nurse with a health care facility to provide nursing advice and treatment to patients. In response to a request for evidence, she submitted a new professional plan and statement saying that she intended to work as a nurse practitioner and offer her expertise to clinics and hospitals to improve resource management or patient care in the United States, and on appeal she again described the proposed endeavor as offering her expertise as a nurse practitioner in nursing. The evidence reflects experience as a nurse, an assistant nurse, and a technical manager rather than as a nurse practitioner. The record includes additional letters verifying employment as a nurse and as a nursing technician, but, as described in the decision record, those letters do not state her job duties and therefore do not show from the letters whether the work included nurse-practitioner duties. The record also includes additional letters relating to nursing work performed after the petition was filed, which the decision record describes as evidence of facts that did not exist at the time of filing.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB -2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. An advanced degree is any U.S. academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree.1 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A U.S. bachelor's degree or a foreign equivalent 1 Profession shall include, but not be limited to, architects, engineers, lawyers, physicians, surgeons, and teachers in elementary or secondary schools, colleges, academics, or seminaries. Section 101(a)(32) of the Act.

degree followed by five years ofprogressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. Id. Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or an individual of exceptional ability, they must then establish eligibility for a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement '"in the national interest." Section 203(b)(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. 884 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that USCIS may, as matter of discretion,2 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id. at 889.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
% Facts (case-specific)

% Underlying substantive facts supporting that the proposed endeavor is in healthcare and aimed at improving care/resources
healthcare_field.                   % the endeavor is in the field of nursing/healthcare
improves_patient_care.              % petitioner intends to offer expertise to clinics and hospitals to improve patient care and resource management
addresses_nurse_shortage.           % petition emphasizes addressing a shortage of nurses

% EB-2: advanced degree equivalence
advanced_degree_equivalent :-
    has_foreign_bachelors,
    has_5_years_progressive_experience.

eb2_eligible :-
    advanced_degree_equivalent.

% Dhanasar framework (national interest waiver)
% Prong 1: proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance
prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    healthcare_field,
    improves_patient_care,
    addresses_nurse_shortage,
    intended_job_nurse_practitioner.

% Prong 2: the individual is well-positioned to advance the proposed endeavor
% Requires relevant experience and sufficient contemporaneous documentation at the time of filing
has_relevant_experience :-
    ( employment_history_nurse
    ; employment_history_assistant_nurse
    ; employment_history_technical_manager
    ; employment_history_nursing_technician
    ).

sufficient_documentation_at_filing :-
    updated_plan_clarified_nurse_practitioner,
    \+ initial_evidence_inconsistent,
    \+ some_letters_lack_duties,
    \+ some_employment_after_filing.

prong2_well_positioned :-
    has_relevant_experience,
    sufficient_documentation_at_filing.

% Prong 3: on balance, waiving the job offer would benefit the United States
% This predicate assesses whether the endeavor would benefit the U.S.; it does not override prong2.
prong3_benefit_us :-
    prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    addresses_nurse_shortage.

% Eligibility for NIW requires EB-2 eligibility and all three Dhanasar prongs
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_eligible,
    prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    prong2_well_positioned,
    prong3_benefit_us.

% Entrypoint and decision structure (do not modify)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])

               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed Form I-360 seeking immigrant classification as an abused spouse of a U.S. citizen and indicated on the form that she had been married twice. To show that her prior marriage had been ended before she married her U.S. citizen spouse, she submitted copies of a customary divorce decree from a Customary Court in Lagos State and Decree Nisi and Decree Absolute documents purportedly issued by the High Court of Lagos State (signed by an Assistant Chief Registrar). After the petition was denied, the petitioner provided additional divorce decrees signed by a different Assistant Chief Registrar of the High Court.

In a supplemental declaration and supporting filings on appeal, the petitioner described the chronology and provenance of her divorce documentation: she first obtained a customary divorce in Nigeria, then later obtained Decree Nisi and Decree Absolute from the High Court of Lagos, which she says were recognized in Nigeria when she was granted custody of her children and her former husband remarried. She explained that a Nigerian law firm later obtained confirmation from the High Court that her divorce was authentic, that defects in the initial decree were corrected in subsequent copies she submitted in response to a USCIS request for evidence, and that the corrected decrees were certified by the Federal Ministry of Foreign Affairs. She also obtained certification of the decrees from the Nigerian Embassy in Washington, D.C., and submitted amended Decree Nisi and Certificate of Decree Absolute stamped by the Nigerian Embassy and an affidavit from the Nigerian attorney who obtained the federal certification.

The petitioner appealed the denial and the dismissal of her motion because she asserted her divorce documents are authentic, she provided additional certified and corrected decrees and an attorney affidavit to support their authenticity, and she contended that she was not given sufficient information about the adverse information to rebut it. The appeal seeks consideration of the additional documentary evidence and the petitioner’s explanations regarding the validity and certification of her divorce decrees.
statutes: A petitioner who is the spouse of a U.S. citizen may self-petition for immigrant classification if the petitioner married the U .S. citizen spouse in good faith, is eligible for immigrant classification based on the relationship, resided with their spouse, was subjected to battery or extreme cruelty by the U.S. citizen spouse, and is a person of good moral character. Section 204(a)(l)(A)(iii) of the Act. Primary evidence of a marital relationship is a marriage certificate issued by civil authorities, and proof of the termination all prior marriages, if any, of the self-petitioner. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(ii). We consider any credible evidence relevant to the petition, and the determination of what evidence is credible, and the weight given such evidence lies within our sole discretion. Section 204(a)(l)(J) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.2( c )(2)(i).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
ministry_certified_decrees :- submitted_nigerian_attorney_affidavit_and_federal_ministry_of_foreign_affairs_certification.
law_firm_confirmation :- nigerian_law_firm_confirmed_high_court_confirmation.

% Credibility rule: USCIS may weigh evidence, but official authentication by the issuing country's consular or foreign affairs authorities is strong evidence of authenticity.
% We model that embassy seal + ministry certification + corrected decrees together constitute credible proof of legal termination of prior marriage.
prior_marriage_terminated :-
    embassy_seal_certified_decrees,
    ministry_certified_decrees,
    submitted_corrected_decrees_signed_by_different_assistant_chief_registrar.

% If termination is not proven, then the petitioner lacks a qualifying relationship.
lacks_qualifying_relationship :-
    \+ prior_marriage_terminated.

% Eligibility requires all statutory elements and proof of termination of prior marriages (if any)
eligibility_met :-
    married_to_us_citizen,
    married_in_good_faith,
    resided_with_spouse,
    subjected_to_battery_or_extreme_cruelty,
    good_moral_character,
    \+ lacks_qualifying_relationship.

% Entrypoint and decision output (must be last)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is an aviation professional who sought EB-2 immigrant classification as an individual of exceptional ability and a national interest waiver. He stated that he has worked for the same government-owned company for 19 years, with over 10 of those years in his current airport-related occupation. In his Form I-140 he listed his position and occupation as “airport operations specialist,” while in a supporting cover letter and several employer documents he described his role using different titles, including “airport operations professional” and “airport services professional.” He also submitted an uncertified Form ETA-750 and a prior RFE response in which he described himself as “an Airport Operations Specialist with over 19 years of experience,” creating inconsistent position titles across the record.

As evidence of his background and qualifications, the petitioner provided a private pilot’s license issued by ANAC, tax returns for 2019–2021 showing wages and other payments from his employer(s), an employment verification letter, work evaluation reports, expert opinion and reference letters, a list of courses he completed (41 courses between 2006 and 2019), and a wage assessment notice. He asserted that his pilot’s license and course work contributed to his professional qualifications, referred to the pilot license as a form of membership with ANAC, and cited his employment verification letter and evaluation reports as evidence of his contributions and performance in airport operations.

The petitioner initially claimed he met five of the eligibility criteria and later amended his claim to assert that he satisfied all six criteria. On appeal from the Texas Service Center’s denial of his petition, he challenged the characterization of his job title and the appropriateness of comparing his salary to certain occupational salary ranges, reiterated that his pilot license and other training are relevant to his profession, and maintained that his submitted letters and evaluations document his contributions to the field. His appeal seeks reversal of the denial and recognition that he qualifies for the requested classification and waiver.
statutes: Exceptional ability means a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A petitioner must initially submit documentation that satisfies at least three of six categories of evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F). 3 (A) An official academic record showing the noncitizen's possession of a degree, diploma, certificate, or similar award from a college, university, school, or other institution of learning relating to the area of exceptional ability; (B) Letters from current or former employers showing that the noncitizen has at least 10 years of foll-time experience in the proposed occupation; (C) A license to practice the profession or certification for the profession or occupation; (D) Evidence of the noncitizen' s receipt of a salary or other remuneration demonstrating exceptional ability; (E) Proof of membership in professional associations; or (F) Evidence of recognition for achievements and significant contributions to the industry or field by peers, governmental entities, or professional or business organizations. Meeting at least three criteria, however, does not, in and of itself, establish eligibility for this classification. 4 If a petitioner does so, we will then conduct a final merits determination to decide whether the evidence in its totality shows that they are recognized as having a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the field. If a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion5, grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. 3 If these types of evidence do not readily apply to the individual's occupation, a petitioner may submit comparable evidence to establish their eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(iii). 4 USCTS has previously confirmed the applicability of this two-part adjudicative approach in the context of aliens of exceptional ability. 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.5(B)(2), https://www.uscis.gov/policy-manual/volume-6-palt-f-chapter-5. 5 See also Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver are discretionary in nature). 2
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts extracted from the record (truthy facts are declared; absence means false)
long_time_employee.
employer_infraero.
years_at_company_19.
years_in_current_airport_operations_role_10plus.

has_employment_verification_letter.
has_employer_evaluation_reports.
has_tax_returns_2019_2021.
has_pilot_license.                 % petitioner submitted a private pilot's license (ANAC)
has_courses_2006_2019.
has_reference_and_expert_letters.
has_uncertified_eta750.
has_wage_assessment_notice.

% Adverse/limiting factual findings (expressed as facts so relevant rules can negate applicability)
pilot_license_not_a_license_for_occupation.    % pilot license is not a license to practice "airport operations"
membership_with_anac_not_proved.               % petitioner characterized license as membership, but membership not established
salary_does_not_demonstrate_exceptional_ability. % wages do not show exceptional ability compared to relevant ranges
recognition_by_peers_or_gov_not_established.   % letters/evaluations do not amount to recognition by peers/government/orgs
academic_degree_not_provided.                  % no degree/diploma/certificate from college/university was shown

% Mapping regulatory criteria (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F))
% Use arity-0 predicates for yes/no checks per instructions.

% (A) Official academic record showing possession of a degree/diploma/certificate relating to the field
criteria_A :- has_academic_degree.
has_academic_degree :- \+ academic_degree_not_provided, !.  % true only if not contradicted; here academic_degree_not_provided is present, so this fails

% (B) Letters from current/former employers showing at least 10 years of full-time experience
criteria_B :- has_employment_verification_letter, years_in_current_airport_operations_role_10plus.

% (C) A license to practice the profession or certification for the profession or occupation
criteria_C :- has_license_to_practice.
has_license_to_practice :- has_pilot_license, \+ pilot_license_not_a_license_for_occupation.
% Because pilot_license_not_a_license_for_occupation is asserted, has_license_to_practice will fail

% (D) Evidence of receipt of a salary or other remuneration demonstrating exceptional ability
criteria_D :- salary_demonstrates_exceptional_ability.
salary_demonstrates_exceptional_ability :- \+ salary_does_not_demonstrate_exceptional_ability, !.
% salary_does_not_demonstrate_exceptional_ability is asserted, so criteria_D fails

% (E) Proof of membership in professional associations
criteria_E :- membership_in_professional_association.
membership_in_professional_association :- \+ membership_with_anac_not_proved, !.
% membership_with_anac_not_proved is asserted, so criteria_E fails

% (F) Evidence of recognition for achievements and significant contributions to the industry or field
criteria_F :- recognized_by_peers_or_gov.
recognized_by_peers_or_gov :- \+ recognition_by_peers_or_gov_not_established, !.
% recognition_by_peers_or_gov_not_established is asserted, so criteria_F fails

% Helper to check which of the six criteria are satisfied
criteria(letter_a) :- criteria_A.
criteria(letter_b) :- criteria_B.
criteria(letter_c) :- criteria_C.
criteria(letter_d) :- criteria_D.
criteria(letter_e) :- criteria_E.
criteria(letter_f) :- criteria_F.

criteria_met_count(N) :-
    findall(C, (member(C, [letter_a,letter_b,letter_c,letter_d,letter_e,letter_f]), criteria(C)), L),
    length(L, N).

satisfies_at_least_three_criteria :-
    criteria_met_count(N),
    N >= 3.

% Final merits determination: the totality of evidence must show exceptional ability above that ordinarily encountered.
% The record here contains employer letters, trainings, a pilot license, courses, and reference letters,
% but lacks an academic degree, lacks a license/certification relevant to the occupation, lacks salary evidence of exceptional ability,
% and lacks demonstrated recognition by peers/governmental entities. Given those gaps, the final merits determination is not met.
final_merits :- fail.  % explicitly fail to reflect that the totality of evidence does not show exceptional ability

% Dhanasar national interest waiver prongs:
% 1) Proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance
% 2) Individual is well-positioned to advance the endeavor
% 3) On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the U.S.
% The petitioner emphasized long employment, trainings, pilot license, and employer letters, but the record does not establish
% the required showings for these prongs in a manner that would overcome the deficiencies on exceptional ability.
dhanasar_prongs_met :- fail.  % fail to reflect that the national-interest prongs are not established on the record

% eligibility_met must represent all conditions necessary to accept the case.
% That is: at least three regulatory criteria satisfied, final merits determination satisfied, and Dhanasar prongs satisfied.
eligibility_met :-
    satisfies_at_least_three_criteria,
    final_merits,
    dhanasar_prongs_met.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a native and citizen of China who applied to adjust status to lawful permanent resident and filed Form I-601 seeking a waiver of inadmissibility. He submitted a nonimmigrant visa application in November 2013 in which he reported that he worked for Q‑X‑D‑ Co LTD and that his father was the owner. The petitioner later stated that his father had in fact died in 1999 and that a travel agency prepared and translated the application into English for him. He says he did not review the translated English version, trusted the travel agency, and only learned afterward that the agency “made up a fake background” and had represented his father as alive and the company owner. He says the travel agency told him that such false information was necessary to obtain the visa and that he was desperate to leave China, so he “went along with it.” At a December 2017 USCIS interview he explained he was nervous and did not give all of the information; in an April 2024 statement he said he deeply regretted not withdrawing the visa application once he learned of the falsified information.

When he filed Form I-485 in August 2021, he answered “No” to a question asking whether he had ever lied about, concealed, or misrepresented information on an application for a visa or other immigration benefit. He later explained that he completed that I-485 without legal help, that his English was limited, and that he answered “No” because he was very scared he would not be allowed to stay in the United States if he answered “Yes.” He also filed a second Form I-485 in December 2024; that filing again answered “No” to the misrepresentation question (though other later responses on that form indicate he answered “Yes” to an arrest question).

The petitioner also disclosed that in December 2013 he was arrested in China. He reported that 10–15 police officers entered a home where about 16 people were gathered, handcuffed him, transported him to a police station where two male officers interrogated and beat him, and then moved him to a detention center where he was held for 15 days. He stated that his mother paid a fine and he signed a “guarantee letter” agreeing to report periodically to police beginning December 30, 2013, but that he did not report because he left China for the United States on December 24, 2013. On his August 2021 Form I-485 he answered “No” to questions about ever being arrested, cited, charged, or detained; he later said he misunderstood the question (thinking it referred only to incidents in the United States), apologized for the incorrect response, and said his limited English contributed to the error.

In support of his waiver application and on appeal, the petitioner submitted statements from himself, his U.S. citizen spouse, her parents, and friends; a psychological evaluation and subsequent therapy/treatment records for his spouse; family identification documents; financial records, tax records and insurance documents; and country-conditions information addressing treatment of U.S. citizens in China and the availability of nursing jobs there. On appeal he also submitted a brief, his spouse’s immigration documentation, updated financial records, and additional medical/therapy documentation for his spouse (including records from May–September 2024 showing ongoing treatment, diagnoses, prescribed medications, and therapy appointments).

The petitioner appealed the Field Office’s denial of his Form I-601 waiver. The stated basis for the appeal is disagreement with the denial and the contention that denial of the waiver would cause extreme hardship to his qualifying relative (his U.S. citizen spouse). He argues, and has presented documentary and testimonial evidence to support, that his spouse would suffer financial hardship if his income were lost (including possible loss of their home and a reduced standard of living), psychological and emotional hardship due to diagnosed anxiety and depression and ongoing therapy, and additional emotional hardship tied to her traumatic childhood history, prior asylum claim, fears about safety and employment in China, and the couple’s plans for a family.
statutes:
Any alien who, by fraud or willfully misrepresenting a material fact, seeks to procure ( or has sought to procure or has procured) a visa, other documentation, or admission into the United States or other benefit provided under the Act, is inadmissible. Section 212(a)(6)(C)(i) of the Act. This ground of inadmissibility may be waived as a matter of discretion if refusal of admission would result in extreme hardship to the United States citizen or lawful permanent resident (LPR) spouse or parent of the Applicant. Section 212(i) of the Act. A determination of whether denial of admission will result in extreme hardship depends on the facts and circumstances of each case. Matter of Cervantes-Gonzalez, 22 I&N Dec. 560, 565 (BIA 1999) (citations omitted). We recognize that some degree of hardship to qualifying relatives is present in most cases; however, to be considered "extreme," the hardship must exceed that which is usual or expected. See Matter of Pilch, 21 I&N Dec . 627, 630-31 (BIA 1996) (finding that factors such as economic detriment, severing family and community ties, loss of current employment, and cultural readjustment were the "common result of deportation" and did not alone constitute extreme hardship).

In determining whether extreme hardship exists, individual hardship factors that may not rise to the level of extreme must also be considered in the aggregate. Matter ofIge, 20 I&N Dec . 880, 882 (BIA 1994) ( citations omitted).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (truth-valued predicates use arity 0 as required)
sought_visa.  % petitioner applied for a nonimmigrant visa in November 2013

reported_father_alive_and_owner.  % 2013 application reported father alive and owning Q-X-D Co LTD
father_deceased_1999.            % petitioner later stated father had been deceased since 1999

explanation_travel_agency_translated_and_faked.  % petitioner says travel agency translated and "made up a fake background"
did_not_review_translated_version.               % petitioner did not review translated English version

submitted_waiver_with_documents.  % petitioner submitted waiver and supporting documents initially
on_appeal_submitted_additional_docs.  % petitioner submitted brief and additional evidence on appeal

spouse_is_us_citizen.  % qualifying relative is a U.S. citizen spouse
psychological_evaluation_for_spouse.  % psychological evaluation for spouse exists
spouse_ongoing_therapy.  % spouse is receiving ongoing therapy/treatment
family_and_friends_statements.  % letters from spouse, parents, friends
financial_records.  % financial records and tax records submitted
country_conditions_concerning_treatment_and_nursing_jobs.  % country-conditions information submitted

% Legal-rule encodings
% Material misrepresentation exists where prior application statements conflict on a core identity/family fact.
misrepresentation_material :-
    reported_father_alive_and_owner,
    father_deceased_1999.

% Statutory inadmissibility under 212(a)(6)(C)(i) — fraud or willful misrepresentation to procure a visa.
% We record inadmissibility when a material misrepresentation was made in seeking a visa.
inadmissible_by_misrepresentation :-
    misrepresentation_material,
    sought_visa.

% Extreme hardship: aggregate of factors showing hardship beyond usual. For this case record,
% require presence of serious spouse mental-health evidence, ongoing treatment, financial records,
% and adverse country-conditions information that would affect relocation or separation.
extreme_hardship :-
    spouse_is_us_citizen,
    psychological_evaluation_for_spouse,
    spouse_ongoing_therapy,
    financial_records,
    country_conditions_concerning_treatment_and_nursing_jobs.

% A waiver under section 212(i) is available as a matter of discretion if denial of admission
% would result in extreme hardship to the qualifying relative (here, the U.S. citizen spouse).
waiver_applicable :-
    inadmissible_by_misrepresentation,
    extreme_hardship.

% Eligibility for adjustment (as required for acceptance): waiver is available and thus eligibility met.
eligibility_met :-
    waiver_applicable.

% Entrypoint and decision output (do not include a query in the output)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed Form I-140 seeking classification in the EB-2 category with a national interest waiver. The Texas Service Center denied that petition, and the petitioner’s subsequent appeal to the Administrative Appeals Office (AAO) was dismissed. The petitioner then submitted a motion to reopen, asserting ineffective assistance of prior counsel as the basis for the motion.

In her filings and supporting materials, the petitioner stated that before the petition was filed she told prior counsel that her background and U.S. work would focus on organizational psychology and that she provided the attorney with evidence of that experience (including research publications). The petitioner says prior counsel instead prepared the petition to describe her proposed U.S. work as a “corporate human resources counselor” operating through her own company to help small- and medium-sized businesses improve efficiency and profitability by promoting healthy work environments. She asserts that prior counsel submitted two different Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) summaries for the proposed occupation—one for human resource specialists and one for industrial-organizational psychologists—and that the use of two SOC codes created confusion and “weakened the entire [petition].” The petitioner contends that evidence she had given the lawyer (for example, her publications) was “not presented in a cohesive manner” and that critical evidence was omitted from the filed petition.

The petitioner provided an affidavit and a copy of her retainer agreement with prior counsel and stated that the attorney advertised online as an “expert” in national interest waiver petitions. She also submitted a copy of an email she sent to the prior counsel’s firm after the appellate denial criticizing the petition’s preparation, and she says she tried to contact the firm multiple times without success. The petitioner’s brief on motion states she informed prior counsel of the ineffectiveness allegations (referring to an October 21, 2023 contact) and that prior counsel had been encouraging her to file a motion; she says she sought a new opinion after seeing online reviews suggesting others were in similar situations. The petitioner did not file a disciplinary complaint against prior counsel and did not provide an explanation for that decision in the motion.

The petitioner’s stated reason for seeking reopening was that prior counsel misrepresented the nature of her proposed endeavor and failed to organize and present the supporting evidence coherently, and that but for those alleged errors she would have been approved.
statutes: A motion to reopen must state new facts, supported by documentary evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(2). We review only our latest decision and grant a motion that satisfies these requirements and demonstrates eligibility for the requested benefit. 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(l)(i); see also Matter ofCoelho, 20 I&N Dec. 464, 473 (BIA 1992) (requiring new evidence that potentially changes the decision's outcome).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (use arity 0 for true/false predicates as required)
retained_counsel.
written_retainer.
petitioner_experienced_organizational_psychologist.
petition_signed_by_petitioner.
petition_prepared_by_prior_counsel.
motion_to_reopen_filed.
motion_alleges_ineffective_assistance_of_counsel.
attached_affidavit.
attached_motion_brief.
attached_copy_of_email_to_prior_counsel.
attached_screenshots_of_attorney_review_site.
no_corroborating_online_posting.
prior_counsel_described_proposed_work_as_corporate_hr_counselor.
inconsistent_soc_codes_submitted.
petitioner_informed_prior_counsel_of_dissatisfaction.
petitioner_stated_inform_date_oct_21_2023.
did_not_file_disciplinary_complaint.
no_explanation_for_no_disciplinary_complaint.
petitioner_alleges_but_for_errors_petition_would_have_been_approved.

% Legal rules encoded as Prolog predicates

% A motion to reopen must state new facts supported by documentary evidence.
% We treat "documentary evidence" as presence of at least one documentary item in the record.
has_documentary_evidence_of_issues :-
    ( written_retainer
    ; attached_affidavit
    ; attached_motion_brief
    ; attached_copy_of_email_to_prior_counsel
    ; attached_screenshots_of_attorney_review_site
    ).

% The motion alleges ineffective assistance of counsel
motion_raises_new_facts_supported_by_documentation :-
    motion_to_reopen_filed,
    motion_alleges_ineffective_assistance_of_counsel,
    has_documentary_evidence_of_issues.

% Matter of Coelho: the movant must present new evidence that potentially changes the outcome.
% We model "demonstrates_prejudice" (i.e., new evidence that could change the outcome)
% as requiring (1) identification of specific critical evidence that was omitted or
% mischaracterized and (2) some documentary showing that the omitted/mischaracterized evidence
% would likely have altered the outcome.
%
% In the present record the petitioner asserted omissions and mischaracterizations
% but did not meaningfully identify the specific critical evidence and did not provide
% documentary proof that the omitted items would have resulted in approval.
% We therefore model those negative findings as facts in the record.

% The petitioner alleged omissions/mischaracterizations (we have that allegation)
petitioner_alleged_omissions_and_mischaracterizations :-
    prior_counsel_described_proposed_work_as_corporate_hr_counselor;
    inconsistent_soc_codes_submitted.

% But petitioner did not adequately identify specific critical evidence (no detailed list
% showing what exactly was omitted and how it would alter the adjudication)
petitioner_failed_to_identify_specific_critical_evidence.
% And petitioner did not provide documentary proof that the omitted evidence would likely
% have changed the outcome
no_documentary_proof_of_prejudice.

% demonstrates_prejudice only true if petitioner both identified specific critical evidence
% and provided documentary proof that the omission/mischaracterization would likely change the outcome.
demonstrates_prejudice :-
    \+ petitioner_failed_to_identify_specific_critical_evidence,
    \+ no_documentary_proof_of_prejudice.

% Eligibility requires the motion to raise new facts supported by documentary evidence
% and to demonstrate prejudice (i.e., new evidence that could change the outcome).
eligibility_met :-
    motion_raises_new_facts_supported_by_documentation,
    demonstrates_prejudice.

% Entrypoint and output behavior (must be last as instructed)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner was born in Jamaica in November 2001 to unmarried foreign national parents and is the biological child of the woman who later sought naturalization. He was admitted to the United States as a lawful permanent resident in June 2011, when he was nine years old, and the record indicates he remained under 18 years of age through November 2019. The petitioner acknowledges that his mother was not administered the oath of naturalization until January 2022.

On appeal, the petitioner does not dispute that he was over 18 when his mother was ultimately naturalized. He contends, however, that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) intentionally and deliberately delayed adjudication of his mother’s Form N-400 and the scheduling of her oath ceremony—conduct he characterizes as affirmative misconduct—so that he would be prevented from deriving U.S. citizenship. He asserts this alleged delay was motivated by or related to his public criminal arrest in 2018, and he asks that his claim to derivative citizenship be recognized nunc pro tunc (i.e., retroactively) or otherwise remedied because his mother’s naturalization was not timely completed.

To support his assertions, the petitioner submitted his mother’s affidavit and documentary evidence relating to her naturalization process: her Form N-400 receipt notice and application, her naturalization test results, an oath-of-allegiance signature page, a Notice of Continuance dated November 2021, and internal USCIS records labeled as a Naturalization Quality Procedures worksheet, case flags, and case comments. According to the mother’s affidavit, she was interviewed in 2019 at the Brooklyn Field Office, informed the interviewing officer that her address had changed to Queens (which the officer recorded), was told she had passed the tests and that an oath ceremony would be scheduled, but then received no follow-up in the promised four-to-six weeks. She describes an August 2019 “address adjustment” notice, subsequent visits and weekly phone calls with no substantive updates, a December 2019 notice that her case had been transferred to the Long Island Field Office, a canceled October 2020 interview, and her hiring of an attorney in May 2021. She states that in November 2021 she was asked to visit a USCIS office to sign the oath page again (which she says she had already signed in 2019) and that her oath ceremony was ultimately scheduled in January 2022. The petitioner cites these events and documentary items in support of his appeal and also references court decisions in support of his claim that processing delays should not defeat his derivative citizenship claim.
statutes:
The applicable law for derivative citizenship purposes is "the law in effect at the time the critical events giving rise to eligibility occurred." See Minasyan v. Gonzales, 401 F.3d 1069, 1075 (9th Cir. 2005). In the present matter, the Applicant was born in Jamaica inl 12001 , to unmarried foreign national parents. The Applicant was admitted to the United States as a lawful permanent resident (LPR) in June 2011, and his mother became a U.S. citizen in January 2022. Accordingly, section 320 of the Act, as amended by the Child Citizenship Act of 2000, Pub. L. No. 106-395, 114 Stat. 1631 (Oct. 30, 2000) (CCA) , which became effective on February 27, 2001, applies to his case. The CCA repealed former section 321 of the Act and amended , in part, former section 320 of the Act. The amendments to section 320 of the Act took effect on February 27, 2001 , and apply to individuals who satisfy the requirements of section 320 of the Act, as in effect on that date. Matter ofRodriguez­ Tejedor, 23 I&N Dec. 153, 157 (BIA 2001).

Section 320 of the Act, as amended in Febrnary 2001 and currently in effect, provides that: (a) A child born outside of the United States automatically becomes a citizen of the United States when all of the following conditions have been fulfilled: (1) At least one parent of the child is a citizen of the United States, whether by birth or naturalization. (2) The child is under the age of eighteen years. (3) The child is residing in the United States in the legal and physical custody ofthe citizen parent pursuant to a lawful admission for permanent residence. Moreover, applicants must meet the definition of a "child" in section l0l(c)(l) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § ll0l(c)(l), which defines the term "child" in pertinent part to mean "an unmarried person under twenty-one years of age and includes a child legitimated under the law of the child's residence or domicile, or under the law of the father's residence or domicile, whether in the United States or elsewhere[.]" The applicant must have either a biological or legal adoptive relationship with the claimed U.S. citizen parent. See Matter of Guzman-Gomez, 24 I&N Dec. 824, 826 (BIA 2009) ( determining that "child" as defined under section 101 ( c) of the Act encompasses biological or legal adoptive children, but not stepchildren). A child born in wedlock is considered to be a legitimate child. See Matter ofKubicka, 14 I&N Dec. 303, 304 (BIA 1972) ("A 'legitimate' child is, of course, a child 'born in wedlock' ....") (quoting Webster's New Dictionary, 1971). Generally, to derive U.S. citizenship, a foreign-born child must satisfy certain statutory conditions before turning 18 years of age. A child's acquisition of citizenship on a derivative basis occurs by operation of law and not by adjudication. Matter ofFuentes-Martinez, 21 I&N Dec. 893, 896 (BIA 1997). Thus, a child who satisfies requisite conditions will derive U.S. citizenship automatically, even though the actual determination of derivative citizenship may occur after the fact, in the context of a passport application or a claim to citizenship. Id. Applicants born abroad are presumed to be foreign nationals and bear the burden of establishing their claim to U.S. citizenship by a preponderance of credible evidence. See Matter ofBaires-Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467, 468 (BIA 2008). The "preponderance of the evidence" standard requires that the Applicant establish that their claim is "probably true," based on the specific facts of their case. See Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. 369,376 (AAO 2010) (citing Matter ofE-M-, 20 I&N Dec. 77, 7980 (Comm'r 1989)).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts extracted from the record
child_birth_year(2001).
child_born_abroad.
child_place_of_birth(jamaica).
child_unmarried.
child_biological_of_mother.
child_lpr_admission_year(2011).
child_lpr_admitted_june_2011.

mother_interview_year(2019).
mother_passed_english_and_civics_tests.
mother_signed_oath_signature_page_2019.
mother_oath_ceremony_month_year(january,2022).
mother_naturalization_year(2022).

% Evidence submitted by petitioner (present in the record)
affidavit_from_mother.
n400_receipt_and_application_submitted.
naturalization_test_results_submitted.
oath_signature_page_submitted.
notice_of_continuance_november_2021_submitted.
internal_uscis_documents_submitted.

% Legal predicates derived from statute and record
% The child is under 18 at the time of parent's naturalization if naturalization year minus birth year < 18
child_under_18_at_mother_naturalization :-
    child_birth_year(BY),
    mother_naturalization_year(MY),
    Age is MY - BY,
    Age < 18.

% The child was admitted as a lawful permanent resident before the mother's naturalization year
child_was_lpr_before_mother_naturalization :-
    child_lpr_admission_year(LPRY),
    mother_naturalization_year(MY),
    LPRY =< MY.

% Custody/residence facts (record shows LPR admission and living in the United States)
child_residing_in_us_and_in_custody_of_mother.
% biological relationship already asserted as fact: child_biological_of_mother.

% Burden and evidence rules
% Petitioner bears burden to prove derivative citizenship by a preponderance of evidence.
burden_on_petitioner_to_prove_preponderance.

% Petitioner alleges USCIS intentionally/deliberately delayed mother's adjudication and oath ceremony
petitioner_alleges_uscis_deliberate_delay.

% To accept a claim that the mother's naturalization should be treated as effective before the child's 18th birthday
% the petitioner must prove by a preponderance of the evidence that USCIS intentionally and deliberately delayed adjudication
% such that the mother's naturalization should be deemed effective earlier. We model that as deliberate_delay_proved.
% The record here contains documentary support of processing irregularities (internal documents, notices, affidavit),
% but does not establish deliberate, intentional conduct by USCIS sufficient to satisfy the petitioner's burden.
% Therefore deliberate_delay_proved is not established (no clause = fails).

% deliberate_delay_proved.  % Intentionally left undefined because petitioner did not meet preponderance of evidence

% Determine whether the mother was a U.S. citizen while the child was under 18
mother_citizen_while_child_under_18 :-
    child_under_18_at_mother_naturalization.

% Eligibility for derivative citizenship under section 320 (CCA)
% All statutory conditions must be met:
%  - at least one parent is a U.S. citizen (mother_citizen_while_child_under_18)
%  - child is under 18 at that time (child_under_18_at_mother_naturalization)
%  - child is residing in the U.S. in the legal and physical custody of the citizen parent as a LPR
eligibility_met :-
    mother_citizen_while_child_under_18,
    child_residing_in_us_and_in_custody_of_mother,
    child_was_lpr_before_mother_naturalization,
    child_unmarried,
    child_biological_of_mother.

% Decision entrypoint and runtime harness (must be included as requested)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a geoscientist specializing in volcanology who is pursuing doctoral studies at universities in Brazil and Hawaii and who intends to continue her geoscientific research in the United States. She has submitted evidence showing a record of research, fieldwork, publications, presentations, and institutional roles, including: being featured in the Brazilian news outlet O Globo; presenting at dozens of conferences (including the largest annual global volcanology conference and meetings in San Diego, San Francisco, Argentina, Amsterdam, and New Zealand); collaborating with researchers at prominent universities in the United Kingdom and the United States; participating in research expeditions to explore seafloor volcanoes in Hawaii and American Samoa sponsored by the Ocean Exploration Trust; and being invited by or working with the Hawaiian Volcano Observatory (including an invitation to join research on Mauna Loa). Her undergraduate education was largely funded by a scholarship from Brazil’s National Petroleum Agency, and she received multiple awards during graduate and post‑graduate training, including two substantial awards for project proposals. She has presented in a U.S. Geological Survey seminar series, volunteered with USGS work at Kilauea, and participated in study programs in Greece on Santorini.

The record includes letters of support and expert statements describing specific scientific contributions and roles: she maintained an isotope laboratory and was described as the only person able to perform element separation and to operate an inductively coupled plasma–mass spectrometer using a departed lead researcher’s methodology; she supervised and completed ongoing projects after that researcher’s death; she developed programming code to extract eruption metrics (fountain heights, fissure lengths, erupted volumes) from videos of the 2022 Mauna Loa eruption, a tool said to aid eruption response and monitoring; and she produced research revising the timing of a Brazilian volcano’s last eruption (from an earlier estimate of 250,000 years to about 60,000 years), work characterized as important for understanding mantle plumes and of interest to oil and gas organizations. Representatives of Brazil’s largest petroleum corporation commented on her integrated analysis of porosity patterns and chemical signatures that produced an analogous model for exploiting oil and gas in volcanic‑rock reservoirs. The petitioner also has several publications in major scientific journals (many listing her as first author) with a relatively high citation rate and has performed peer‑review work for notable journals. Multiple letters from senior volcanologists and geoscientists describe her research and contributions as notable and praise her scientific abilities.

The petitioner filed an appeal because she contends the service center director overlooked evidence and did not consider the petition in its entirety when conducting the final merits analysis; she argues that the record — including the letters of support and detailed descriptions of her roles and contributions — was not fully or properly weighed in the director’s decision.
statutes:
An individual is eligible for the extraordinary ability classification if they have extraordinary ability in the sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics which has been demonstrated by sustained national or international acclaim and their achievements have been recognized in the field through extensive documentation; they seek to enter the United States to continue work in the area of extraordinary ability; and their entry into the United States will substantially benefit prospectively the United States. Section 203(b )( 1 )(A) of the Act. The term "extraordinary ability" refers only to those individuals in "that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2). The implementing regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) sets forth a multi-part analysis. First, a petitioner may demonstrate international recognition of their achievements in the field through a one-time achievement (that is, a major, internationally recognized award). Absent such an achievement, a petitioner must provide

sufficient qualifying documentation demonstrating that they meet at least three ofthe ten criteria listed at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x). Where a petitioner meets these initial evidence requirements, we then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination and assess whether the record shows sustained national or international acclaim and demonstrates that the individual is among the small percentage at the very top of the field of endeavor. See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 20 I 0) ( discussing a two-part review where the documentation is first counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination); see also Visinscaia v. Beers, 4 F. Supp. 3d 126, 131-32 (D.D.C. 2013); Rijalv. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the Administrative Appeals Office case
% Use arity-0 predicates for true/false facts, per instructions.

% Evidence relevant to the regulatory criteria (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x)):
criterion_publications.                       % multiple publications, often as first author
criterion_original_contributions.             % revised eruption age, developed code, isotope techniques
criterion_judged_others.                      % peer reviewer for scientific journals (judged work of others)
criterion_invitations_to_present.             % unsolicited invitations and invited seminar participation
criterion_awards.                             % scholarship, graduate and post-graduate awards, project funding
criterion_critical_role.                      % led and supervised projects, maintained isotope laboratory, critical capacity

% Additional evidence used in the final merits analysis:
has_international_presentations.              % dozens of presentations worldwide, major conferences
has_high_citations.                            % relatively high citation rate for her field
has_support_letters_by_peers_and_industry.     % letters from colleagues and Brazil's petroleum corporation
has_collaborations_with_us_agencies.           % collaboration with Hawaiian Volcano Observatory, USGS seminar participation
has_leadership_roles.                          % continued supervision after lead researcher's death; maintained research program

% No major, single, internationally recognized award listed
no_major_international_award.

% Intent and benefit
seeks_to_enter_us_to_continue_work.            % pursuing doctoral degrees and collaborations in Hawaii; intends to continue work
entry_will_benefit_us.                         % contributions to hazard understanding, collaborations with US observatories and agencies

% --- Rules implementing the regulatory framework ---

% A petitioner meets the initial evidence requirement if they present a major internationally recognized award
meets_initial_by_major_award :-
    \+ no_major_international_award.  % true only if a major award were present (negated here because none exists)

% Alternatively, a petitioner meets the initial evidence requirement if they document at least three of the ten criteria.
meets_initial_by_criteria :-
    findall(
        C,
        ( member(C, [ criterion_publications,
                      criterion_original_contributions,
                      criterion_judged_others,
                      criterion_invitations_to_present,
                      criterion_awards,
                      criterion_critical_role ]),
          call(C)
        ),
        Satisfied
    ),
    length(Satisfied, N),
    N >= 3.

meets_initial_requirements :-
    (   meets_initial_by_major_award
    ;   meets_initial_by_criteria
    ).

% Final merits determination: sustained national or international acclaim (Kazarian two-step)
% We operationalize "sustained acclaim" as having multiple, independent indicia of recognition beyond merely meeting the initial criteria.
final_merits_positive :-
    meets_initial_requirements,
    findall(
        E,
        ( member(E, [ has_international_presentations,
                      has_high_citations,
                      has_support_letters_by_peers_and_industry,
                      has_collaborations_with_us_agencies,
                      has_leadership_roles ]),
          call(E)
        ),
        Recognitions
    ),
    length(Recognitions, M),
    M >= 3.  % require at least three independent indicators of sustained acclaim/impact

% Eligibility requires: initial requirements passed (either major award or >=3 criteria),
% positive final merits determination (sustained acclaim), intent to enter US to continue work,
% and prospective benefit to the United States.
eligibility_met :-
    final_merits_positive,
    seeks_to_enter_us_to_continue_work,
    entry_will_benefit_us.

% Entrypoint and output handling (must remain last as specified)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner is a citizen of China who was apprehended upon entry to the United States on September 1, 2009. A U.S. immigration officer interviewed him and ordered him removed on October 19, 2009. An Immigration Judge subsequently reviewed and affirmed that decision and entered an order of removal, but that removal order was never executed because the petitioner never departed the United States. In 2021 the petitioner filed Form I-212 seeking permission to reapply for admission. The New York Field Office director denied the application on the basis that, in the director’s view, the record did not show an Immigration Judge had ordered the petitioner removed. On appeal, the petitioner contends that an Immigration Judge did order his removal, that his departure would cause execution of that removal order and render him inadmissible, and that he is therefore eligible to seek conditional approval/waiver of inadmissibility conditioned upon his departure.
statutes: Any noncitizen who has been ordered removed under section 23 5(b )(1) of the Act who again seeks admission within five years of the date of such removal is inadmissible. Section 212(a)(9)(A)(i) of the Act. Noncitizens found inadmissible under section 212(a)(9)(A) of the Act may seek permission to reapply for admission under section 212(a)(9)(A)(iii) of the Act if, prior to the date of their reembarkation at a place outside the United States or attempt to be admitted from foreign contiguous territory, the Secretary of Homeland Security has consented to the noncitizen's reapplying for admission. Approval of an application for permission to reapply is discretionary, and any unfavorable factors will be weighed against the favorable factors to determine if approval of the application is warranted as a matter of discretion. Matter ofLee, 17 l&N Dec . 275, 278-79 (Reg'l Comm'r 1978).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
% Facts (case-specific)
citizen_of_china.

removal_order_entered(2009).
remained_in_united_states.

field_office_denied_believed_no_IJ_order.
petitioner_seeks_conditional_approval.

% Statutory and precedent encodings (rules)
% Section 212(a)(9)(A)(i) creates a 5-year inadmissibility following an order of removal.
% Represented here as inadmissible_within_5_years when the I-212 application is filed within 5 years
% of the removal order year.
removal_year(Year) :-
    removal_order_entered(Year).

application_year(Year) :-
    filed_I212(Year).

inadmissible_under_212a9a :-
    removal_year(RY),
    application_year(AY),
    Diff is AY - RY,
    Diff =< 5.

% The statutory condition to seek permission to reapply is that the Secretary must consent
% prior to reembarkation; an applicant may file the I-212 while present in the United States
% (i.e., before departure). We model "applied_before_departure" as the applicant having filed
% the I-212 and remaining in the United States.
applied_before_departure :-
    filed_I212(_),
    remained_in_united_states.

% Permission to reapply is available to noncitizens who have been ordered removed.
permission_to_reapply_available :-
    immigration_judge_ordered_removed.

% Approval of an I-212 is discretionary (Matter of Lee). We model that discretion exists,
% but the statutory eligibility to request permission is separate from the exercise of discretion.
discretionary_decision_exists.

% Eligibility: for the purposes of accepting this appeal, we treat statutory eligibility
% to request permission to reapply (i.e., there was a removal order and the applicant
% filed the I-212 before departure and seeks conditional approval) as the necessary
% conditions to Accept.
eligibility_met :-
    permission_to_reapply_available,

% Entrypoint and decision structure (must be last as requested)
```
