text: The petitioner describes himself as an entrepreneur in janitorial services and seeks EB-2 immigrant classification as an individual of exceptional ability, along with a national interest waiver. After the Texas Service Center director denied the Form I-140 petition, the petitioner pursued an appeal to the Administrative Appeals Office. On appeal, he characterizes himself as an "Entrepreneur in the field of Janitorial Services of Exceptional Ability." To support the record, he submitted an Electro-Electronic Technician certificate and academic record; according to the agency's description of the record, neither the appeal brief nor the rest of the evidence explains how that electro-electronic field relates to entrepreneurship in janitorial services. He also submitted letters from partners in businesses he has owned and operated. According to the agency's description of the record, his current janitorial-services venture was incorporated in 2022, roughly two months before the petition was filed, and the record does not show that he operated a janitorial-services company before late 2022, although it reflects earlier ventures involving restaurants, parking lots, and a travel agency. The same record description states that the partner letters do not specify whether his work with each business was full-time or part-time. In response to a request for evidence, he also submitted an International Facility Management Association membership certificate and a page from the organization's website. That webpage describes IFMA as the world's largest and most widely recognized association for facility management professionals, but it does not give membership requirements. According to the agency's description of the record, the petitioner provided no further information about the association, the record does not indicate when he joined it, and the membership documentation was not part of the initial filing. In his appeal brief, he argues that the IFMA membership should count as professional-association membership and that letters from business partners, along with articles about some of his restaurants and clubs and local awards received by his restaurants, show recognition and endorsement.
statutes:
To qualify for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, a petitioner must establish they are an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b)(2)(A) of the Act. Exceptional ability means a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). When assessing exceptional ability, USCIS uses a two-step analysis. See generally 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.5(8)(2), https://www.uscis.gov/policy­ manual. First, a petitioner must submit at least three of the following types of evidence: • An official academic record showing the noncitizen's possession of a degree, diploma, certificate, or similar award from a college, university, school, or other institution of learning relating to the area of exceptional ability; • Letters from current or former employers showing that the noncitizen has at least 10 years of full-time experience in the proposed occupation; • A license to practice the profession or certification for the profession or occupation;

• Evidence of the noncitizen's receipt of a salary or other remuneration demonstrating exceptional ability; • Proof of membership in professional associations; or • Evidence of recognition for achievements and significant contributions to the industry or field by peers, governmental entities, or professional or business organizations. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii). If these types of evidence do not "readily apply" to a beneficiary's occupation, a petitioner may establish eligibility by submitting "comparable evidence." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(iii). Meeting at least three criteria does not, in and of itself: establish eligibility for this classification. 1 If a petitioner does so, we will then conduct a final merits determination. Wherein, USCIS evaluates all evidence, considering the petition in its entirety. See generally 6 USCIS Policy Manual, supra, at F.5(8)(2). The Agency must determine whether a petitioner, by a preponderance of evidence, has demonstrated a beneficiary's possession of a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business. Id. If a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Case facts (arity-0 predicates for yes/no facts)
% Petitioner information
petitioner_occupation(janitorial_services).
filed_i140_eb2_exceptional_and_niw.

% Submitted items
submitted_electro_electronic_technician_certificate.
submitted_ifma_membership_certificate.
submitted_partner_and_associate_letters.
submitted_informational_articles_and_local_awards.

% Evidence quality flags (explicit where the record shows weakness)
% The partner/associate letters did NOT specify full-time vs part-time, so they do not
% establish 10 years of full-time employment.
employer_letters_show_10yrs(false).

% The Electro-Electronic Technician certificate was submitted but is not shown in the
% record to be clearly related to the claimed business/janitorial occupation.
certificate_relevant_to_proposed_occupation(false).

% The record contains no clear evidence of receipt of a salary or other remuneration
% demonstrating exceptional ability in the proposed occupation.
salary_or_remuneration_evidence(false).

% The petitioner argues on appeal that the director applied a stricter standard;
% this is an argument in the record but does not by itself establish eligibility.
argues_stricter_standard.

% Mapping the rules (USCIS evidentiary criteria) to predicates.
% A petitioner must submit at least three of the listed types of evidence.

% 1. Academic record (degree, diploma, certificate from institution relating to the area)
has_academic_record :-
    % the certificate submitted is not shown to be an academic record relevant to the claimed occupation
    (   certificate_relevant_to_proposed_occupation(true)
    ->  submitted_electro_electronic_technician_certificate
    ;   fail
    ).

% 2. Letters from current or former employers showing at least 10 years full-time experience
has_employer_letters_10yrs :-
    % only true if the record affirmatively shows 10 years of full-time experience
    (   employer_letters_show_10yrs(true)
    ->  submitted_partner_and_associate_letters
    ;   fail
    ).

% 3. License to practice or certification for the profession/occupation
has_license_or_certification :-
    % only count if the certification is relevant to the proposed occupation
    submitted_electro_electronic_technician_certificate,
    certificate_relevant_to_proposed_occupation(true).

% 4. Evidence of receipt of salary or other remuneration demonstrating exceptional ability
has_salary_evidence :-
    salary_or_remuneration_evidence(true).

% 5. Proof of membership in professional associations
has_professional_membership :-
    submitted_ifma_membership_certificate.

% 6. Evidence of recognition for achievements and significant contributions
has_recognition_evidence :-
    submitted_informational_articles_and_local_awards.

% Helper to sum how many of the six regulatory criteria are present.
criteria_predicates([
    has_academic_record,
    has_employer_letters_10yrs,
    has_license_or_certification,
    has_salary_evidence,
    has_professional_membership,
    has_recognition_evidence
]).

sum_criteria(Count) :-
    criteria_predicates(List),
    sum_criteria_list(List, 0, Count).

sum_criteria_list([], Acc, Acc).
sum_criteria_list([Pred|T], Acc, Count) :-
    (   call(Pred)
    ->  Acc1 is Acc + 1
    ;   Acc1 is Acc
    ),
    sum_criteria_list(T, Acc1, Count).

% Meets the "at least three" requirement
meets_three_criteria :-
    sum_criteria(Count),
    Count >= 3.

% Final merits determination: USCIS must find by a preponderance of the evidence that
% the petitioner possesses a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered.
% The record here does not show that level of documented achievement.
demonstrates_degree_above_ordinary :-
    % The record lacks clear evidence (final merits assessment weighs all evidence;
    % given the weaknesses documented above, this fails).
    fail.

final_merits_determination :-
    meets_three_criteria,
    demonstrates_degree_above_ordinary.

% National interest waiver (NIW) required additional discretionary showing.
% The petitioner must show both eligibility for EB-2 exceptional ability and that a waiver is in the national interest.
national_interest_waiver_established :-
    % Because the underlying final merits determination is not met, NIW cannot be established here.
    final_merits_determination,
    % additional national interest factors would be evaluated here (not present/satisfied)
    fail.

% Overall eligibility: must satisfy the classification (final merits) and the NIW
eligibility_met :-
    final_merits_determination,
    national_interest_waiver_established.

% Entrypoint and decision output (provided structure)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The Petitioner is a research scientist who filed an immigrant petition seeking employment-based second preference classification as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree and a waiver of the job-offer requirement. The Director of the Texas Service Center denied the petition after concluding that, although the Petitioner qualified for the advanced-degree classification, he had not established that waiving the job offer and labor certification requirements would be in the national interest, and the matter then came before the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal. According to the Petitioner, he intends to continue researching process optimization using artificial intelligence to enable next-generation computing platforms, with applications in fields including semiconductor manufacturing, logistics, advanced manufacturing, supply chain management, cancer research, and software development. The record is described as including evidence that his project was being used actively by multiple software companies in the United States, a chief executive officer's letter stating that the Petitioner's machine-learning algorithms and their process-optimization applications were being used to support that company's logistics network across Egypt, letters from outside academia discussing the broad reach and impact of his work, scholarly articles and other documentation concerning his contributions and the dissemination of his results through scientific journals and conferences, evidence that his published work had been frequently cited by independent researchers, and articles documenting his contribution to an open-source hardware chip layout project.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. Section 203(b) of the Act sets out this sequential framework: (2) Aliens who are members of the professions holding advanced degrees or aliens of exceptional ability. (A) In general. - Visas shall be made available ... to qualified immigrants who are members of the professions holding advanced degrees or their equivalent or who because of their exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business, will

substantially benefit prospectively the national economy, cultural or educational interests, or welfare of the United States, and whose services in the sciences, arts, professions, or business are sought by an employer in the United States. (B) Waiver ofjob offer(i) National interest waiver. ... [T]he Attorney General may, when the Attorney General deems it to be in the national interest, waive the requirements of subparagraph (A) that an alien's services in the sciences, arts, professions, or business be sought by an employer in the United States. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," we set forth a framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions in the precedent decision Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884 (AAO 2016). Dhanasar states that after a petitioner has established eligibility for EB-2 classification, U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion 1, grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates: (1) that the foreign national's proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; (2) that the foreign national is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor; and (3) that, on balance, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements ofa job offer and thus ofa labor certification.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the record
petitioner_research_scientist.
filed_i140_eb2.
holds_masters_in_cs.
completing_phd_in_cs.
research_area_advanced_computing.
research_area_ai_optimization.
proposed_research_continue_ai_process_optimization.
proposed_research_hardware_design_automation_alignment_with_chips_act.
research_implications_semiconductor_manufacturing.
research_implications_logistics.
research_implications_advanced_manufacturing.
research_implications_supply_chain.
research_implications_cancer_research.
research_implications_software_development.
publications_and_presentations.
peer_review_activity.
citations_by_independent_researchers.
dissemination_via_journals_and_conferences.
letters_of_support_from_academic_and_industry.
professor_letter_describes_research.
ceo_letter_reports_commercial_use_in_egypt.
evidence_use_by_multiple_us_software_companies.
denial_by_texas_service_center.  % administrative fact: prior denial

% EB-2 qualification (underlying classification)
% EB-2 requires an advanced degree or equivalent; the petitioner holds a master's degree.
qualifies_for_eb2 :-
    holds_masters_in_cs.

% Dhanasar prong 1: proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance.
% Substantial merit can be shown by involvement in advanced computing or AI research, and by scholarly dissemination.
prong1_substantial_merit :-
    research_area_advanced_computing.
prong1_substantial_merit :-
    research_area_ai_optimization.
prong1_substantial_merit :-
    publications_and_presentations.
prong1_substantial_merit :-
    dissemination_via_journals_and_conferences.

% National importance can be shown by implications for critical sectors, alignment with federal priorities (e.g., CHIPS), or demonstrated use by U.S. industry.
prong1_national_importance :-
    proposed_research_hardware_design_automation_alignment_with_chips_act.
prong1_national_importance :-
    research_implications_semiconductor_manufacturing.
prong1_national_importance :-
    research_implications_logistics.
prong1_national_importance :-
    research_implications_advanced_manufacturing.
prong1_national_importance :-
    research_implications_supply_chain.
prong1_national_importance :-
    research_implications_cancer_research.
prong1_national_importance :-
    research_implications_software_development.
prong1_national_importance :-
    evidence_use_by_multiple_us_software_companies.

prong1 :-
    prong1_substantial_merit,
    prong1_national_importance.

% Dhanasar prong 2: the foreign national is well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor.
% Evidence: advanced degrees (or progress toward), publications, peer review, citations, letters of support, and industry adoption.
prong2_well_positioned :-
    (   holds_masters_in_cs
    ;   completing_phd_in_cs
    ),
    publications_and_presentations,
    peer_review_activity,
    citations_by_independent_researchers,
    letters_of_support_from_academic_and_industry,
    evidence_use_by_multiple_us_software_companies.

prong2 :-
    prong2_well_positioned.

% Dhanasar prong 3: on balance, it would be beneficial to waive the job offer and labor certification requirements.
% Factors supporting waiver: the endeavor's national importance, the petitioner's demonstrated ability to advance it, and existing commercial or broad adoption reducing the need for labor certification protections.
evidence_of_commercial_adoption :-
    ceo_letter_reports_commercial_use_in_egypt.
evidence_of_commercial_adoption :-
    evidence_use_by_multiple_us_software_companies.

prong3_beneficial_to_waive :-
    prong1,
    prong2,
    evidence_of_commercial_adoption.

prong3 :-
    prong3_beneficial_to_waive.

% Eligibility: all elements necessary to accept the national interest waiver petition
eligibility_met :-
    qualifies_for_eb2,
    prong1,
    prong2,
    prong3.

% Entrypoint and decision structure (must be the last part of the program)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed a Form I-918 (U petition) in October 2015 with a Supplement B certified by a lieutenant of a California police department. The certifying official indicated on the Supplement B that the petitioner was the victim of criminal activity described as “Domestic Violence” and “Rape,” listing California Penal Code sections 286 and 273.5. The certifying official described the petitioner’s injuries as having been repeatedly struck on the head with a closed fist and repeatedly sodomized, resulting in head pain and lower body soreness, and stated that the petitioner contacted the police, responded to law enforcement inquiries, and cooperated fully in the investigation.

During adjudication, the director issued three requests for evidence and ultimately denied the petition on the ground that the petitioner’s identity and citizenship could not be verified. The director’s denial noted that the petitioner stated he could not obtain any valid proof of identity.

On appeal, the petitioner reasserts his eligibility and explains his personal background and difficulties obtaining documentary proof. He states he was born in France in 1962 to unknown parents, was left in the care of a nun identified as M‑N‑Y who raised but did not adopt him, was sent to a school in Italy around age 11, and later stowed away on a ship that landed in Central America. He says that by approximately 1986 he was living in Mexico, entered the United States at a port of entry with inspection, and began living in California. He also states that he traveled to Mexico multiple times thereafter via ports of entry and was routinely waved through despite not having a passport or visa, and that he has never had legal status in the United States nor held a passport.

The petitioner acknowledges he did not submit a valid birth certificate or passport but provided other identity evidence and statements. The record contains multiple statements from the petitioner; photo identification such as a California driver’s license dated 1990, disabled transit cards (expirations September 1992 and February 2018), an International Collector Records Association card (date illegible), and a Mexican government visitation card dated November 1986; non-photo identification and numerous educational certificates dated 1989–2014; a Nursing Assistant Certificate (valid November 1995–October 1999) and other documents bearing his name and Social Security number (swap meet seller’s report dated January 1997, physician’s physical exam dated June 1995, pay stub dated July 1994, and a U.S. Treasury tax refund check dated May 1994); and letters from medical professionals identifying him as the victim. 

The petitioner appeals the denial to contest the director’s finding that his identity and citizenship could not be verified and to present his explanatory statements and supporting identity documents in support of his claim.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for U-1 nonimmigrant classification, petitioners must show that they have suffered substantial physical or mental abuse as a result of having been the victim of qualifying criminal activity, possess information concerning the qualifying criminal activity, have been helpful, are being helpful, or are likely to be helpful to law enforcement authorities investigating or prosecuting the qualifying criminal activity, and that the qualifying criminal activity occurred in the United States or its territories or possessions. Section 101 ( a)( l 5)(U)(i) of the Act. A "victim of qualifying criminal activity" is defined as an individual who has "suffered direct and proximate harm as a result of the commission of qualifying criminal activity." 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(14). The "spouse, children under 21 years of age and, ifthe direct victim is under 21 years ofage, parents and unmarried siblings under 18 years ofage," are also considered victims of qualifying criminal activity "where the direct victim is deceased due to murder or manslaughter, or is incompetent or incapacitated, and therefore unable to provide information concerning the criminal activity or be helpful in the investigation or prosecution of the criminal activity." 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(14)(i).

"Qualifying criminal activity" is "that involving one or more of'' the 28 types ofcrimes listed at section 10l(a)(l5)(U)(iii) of the Act or "any similar activity in violation of Federal, State, or local criminal law." Section 101 (a)( 15)(U)(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(9). The term "'any similar activity' refers to criminal offenses in which the nature and elements of the offenses are substantially similar to the statutorily enumerated list of criminal activities" at section 10l(a)(l5)(U)(iii) of the Act. 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(a)(9). As required initial evidence, petitioners must submit a Form 1-918 Supplement B, U Nonimmigrant Status Certification (Supplement B), from a law enforcement official certifying the petitioners' helpfulness in the investigation or prosecution of the qualifying criminal activity perpetrated against them. 1 Section 214(p)(l) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(2)(i). U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCTS) has sole jurisdiction over U petitions. 8 C.F.R. § 214.14( c)(4). Although petitioners may submit any relevant, credible evidence for the agency to consider, USCIS determines, in its sole discretion, the credibility ofand weight given to all the evidence, including the Supplement B. Section 214(p)(4) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 214.14(c)(4).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
petitioner(man).
born_in(france, 1962).
parents_unknown.
raised_by(nun, 'M-N-Y').
never_adopted.

landed_in(central_america).

    driver_license_1990,
    disabled_transit_cards,
    icra_card,
    mexican_visit_card_1986,

    nursing_assistant_certificate_1995,
    swap_meet_report_1997,
    physician_exam_1995,
    pay_stub_1994
]).

% Mapping criminal codes to described conduct (as presented in the certification)
crime_code(286, sodomy).        % California Penal Code 286 - sodomy (sexual assault)
crime_code(273_5, corporal_injury). % California Penal Code 273.5 - corporal injury (domestic violence)

% Predicate: the petitioner is a victim of qualifying criminal activity if the
% certifying official described qualifying crimes and listed relevant penal codes.
victim_of_qualifying_crime :-
    supplement_b_provided,
    certification_describes(Descr),
    % the certifier described rape or domestic violence
    (   member(rape, Descr)
    ;   member(domestic_violence, Descr)
    ),
    california_penal_codes_listed(Codes),

    ).

% Predicate: petitioner suffered substantial physical or mental abuse
suffered_substantial_abuse :-
    description_of_conduct(Desc),
    (   member('repeatedly sodomized', Desc)
    ;   member('repeatedly struck on the head with a closed fist', Desc)
    ;   member('head pain', Desc)
    ;   member('lower-body soreness', Desc)
    ).

% Predicate: petitioner possesses information concerning the qualifying criminal activity.
possess_information :-
    contacted_police_and_cooperated.

% Predicate: petitioner has been or is likely to be helpful to law enforcement.
helpful_to_law_enforcement :-
    supplement_b_provided,
    contacted_police_and_cooperated.

% Predicate: qualifying criminal activity occurred in the United States (California)
crime_occurred_in_us :-
    certifying_official(_, california_police_department),
    residence(california).

% Eligibility is met only if all statutory elements are satisfied.
eligibility_met :-
    victim_of_qualifying_crime,
    suffered_substantial_abuse,
    possess_information,
    helpful_to_law_enforcement,
    crime_occurred_in_us.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed Form I-140 seeking classification as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree and requested a national interest waiver of the job‑offer requirement. He described his proposed endeavor as serving as the senior technical manager of engineering and processes at a newly created consulting company that will provide consulting services to companies operating in the U.S. mining industry and assist American companies in expanding to the Latin American mining market.

In his business plan the petitioner identified target clients in Arizona, Nevada, Colorado, South Carolina, and Alaska, and set forth a hiring timeline: one metallurgical engineer in the first year, one mechanical engineer in the second year, and one hydraulic engineer in the third year, with no additional hires in the fourth or fifth years — a total of four employees including himself. Corporate filings and a commercial contract in the record show the company operates at an address matching the private residence the petitioner listed in Florida. In response to a request for evidence, the petitioner stated he will actively recruit professionals from New Mexico and establish a branch there.

The record submitted by the petitioner included a business plan, a statement in response to the RFE, letters of recommendation and support, and documentary evidence of company contracts. On appeal the petitioner summarized his academic and work history, reiterated information about the mining industry and his proposed business, and asserted that his RFE statement, the business plan, three opinion letters, the contracts, and letters of support together demonstrate that his proposed endeavor has national importance — which is the basis for his appeal of the service center’s decision.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," we set forth a framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions in the precedent decision Matter of Dhanasar , 26 l&N Dec. 884 (AAO 2016). Dhanasar states that, after a petitioner has established eligibility for EB-2 classification, USCIS may, as a matter of discretion, grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates: (1) that the noncitizen's proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; (2) that the noncitizen is well positioned to advance the proposed

endeavor; and (3) that, on balance, it would be beneficial to the United States to waive the requirements of a job offer and thus of a labor certification. See Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts (and Third in an unpublished decision) in concluding that USCIS' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver is discretionary in nature). The first prong, substantial merit and national importance, focuses on the specific endeavor that the noncitizen proposes to undertake. The endeavor's merit may be demonstrated in a range of areas such as business, entrepreneurialism, science, technology, culture, health, or education. In determining whether the proposed endeavor has national importance, we consider its potential prospective impact. See Matter ofDhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. at 888-91, for elaboration on these three prongs.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the record (true/false predicates use arity 0)
eb2_advanced_degree.
evidence_submitted.
business_consulting.                 % proposed endeavor: engineering/process consulting
business_targets_multiple_states.     % plan lists AZ, NV, CO, SC, AK
projects_four_employees.              % projects hiring petitioner + 3 engineers = 4 employees
address_matches_residence.            % corporate address matches petitioner's private residence in FL
rfe_added_new_mexico_but_plan_omitted.% petitioner later stated will recruit from NM though plan did not list NM
unclear_employee_work_locations.      % record does not clarify where employees will work
contracts_present.                    % commercial contract and corporate filings in record
letters_of_support_present.           % letters of recommendation and support submitted
opinion_letters_present.              % expert opinion letters describing endeavor
evidence_of_qualifications.           % academic and work experience evidence submitted
director_denied.
appeal_filed.

% Dhanasar prongs and logical evaluation

% Prong 1: substantial merit and national importance
% Substantial merit: the endeavor is in a field (business/technology/engineering) where merit is recognized
proposed_endeavor_has_substantial_merit :-
    business_consulting,
    evidence_submitted,
    (contracts_present; opinion_letters_present; letters_of_support_present).

% Insufficient clarity or indicia that undercut national reach
insufficient_clarity_for_national_importance :-
    address_matches_residence,
    rfe_added_new_mexico_but_plan_omitted,
    unclear_employee_work_locations.

% National importance requires prospective impact or clear national reach; fail if record shows insufficient clarity
proposed_endeavor_has_national_importance :-
    proposed_endeavor_has_substantial_merit,
    business_targets_multiple_states,
    \+ insufficient_clarity_for_national_importance.

% Prong 2: well positioned to advance the endeavor
well_positioned_to_advance :-
    evidence_of_qualifications,
    (contracts_present; letters_of_support_present; opinion_letters_present).

% Prong 3: on balance, beneficial to waive job offer/labor certification
% Requires prongs 1 and 2 and absence of negative balancing factors (small scale, home-based, lacks clear national reach)
negative_balancing_factors :-
    projects_four_employees,
    address_matches_residence,
    unclear_employee_work_locations.

beneficial_to_waive :-
    proposed_endeavor_has_national_importance,
    well_positioned_to_advance,
    \+ negative_balancing_factors.

% Eligibility: EB-2 classification + all three Dhanasar prongs
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_advanced_degree,
    proposed_endeavor_has_national_importance,
    well_positioned_to_advance,
    beneficial_to_waive.

% Decision entrypoint (must be included as provided)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner seeks employment-based second preference immigrant classification with a request to waive the job-offer requirement, based on a proposed entrepreneurial logistics venture. The record describes the proposed endeavor as using a company he jointly owns with his spouse to provide logistics services to U.S. companies and to export goods from the United States to Brazil. The Texas Service Center Director denied the Form I-140 petition, while concluding that the petitioner qualified for the underlying visa classification. The record included a business plan and an economic impact analysis forecasting revenue, profits, and job creation, as well as an opinion letter supporting the national interest waiver request. As described in the record, the Director found that the business plan did not explain how its projections had been calculated, and the record also describes the opinion letter as focusing mainly on the logistics industry generally rather than on the petitioner's specific proposed endeavor. The record further states that letters of intent were created after the petition was filed and were not factored into the Director's decision. On appeal, the petitioner argued that his organizations would be well staffed and positioned to meet increasing demand for logistics services, described an "Air Freight Activity" process for nationwide deliveries, and asserted that the endeavor would create jobs and other positive economic effects in economically distressed areas. The record describes the economically distressed area arguments and evidence offered on appeal as new. It also states that the business plan claimed a Florida office location with rent of $100 per month, but that the lease was not submitted.
statutes:
To qualify for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, a petitioner must establish they are an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b)(2)(A) of the Act. If a petitioner establishes eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then demonstrate that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that USCIS may, as matter of discretion, grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.

USCIS' decision to grant or deny an NIW is discretionary in nature. See Flores v. Garland, 72 F.4th 85, 88 (5th Cir. 2023) (joining the Ninth, Eleventh, and D.C. Circuit Courts of Appeals, as well as the Third Circuit in an unpublished decision).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (case summary)
% Petitioner: businessperson seeking EB-2 with a National Interest Waiver (NIW)
businessperson_petitioner.
proposed_endeavor_entrepreneur_in_logistics.
proposed_services_logistics.
proposed_exports_to_brazil.
joint_ownership_with_spouse.
business_plan_submitted.
economic_impact_analysis_submitted.
letters_addressing_background_submitted.
opinion_letter_submitted.
letters_of_intent_postfiling.    % letters of intent were created after filing
operational_details_submitted.
air_freight_activity_described.
staffing_model_described.
office_location_in_florida_claimed.
lease_amount_unusual.           % $100/month lease claimed (suspicious)
projected_revenue_and_profits_submitted.
anticipated_employment_creation_projected.
no_actual_employment_documented. % no evidence of hires / operations in record
evidence_of_operational_capacity_insufficient.

% Underlying EB-2 classification requirement:
% To qualify petitioner must be advanced degree professional or have exceptional ability.
% The record does not establish that requirement.
underlying_eb2_established :- fail.

% Dhanasar framework predicates (three-prong test)
% Prong 1: Proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance.
% Logistics services and facilitating exports can have substantial merit and national importance.
dhanasar_prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    proposed_services_logistics,
    proposed_exports_to_brazil,
    business_plan_submitted,
    economic_impact_analysis_submitted.

% Prong 2: Individual is well-positioned to advance the endeavor.
% The record contains some planning documents but critical evidence undermines
% whether the petitioner is well-positioned: letters of intent were created after filing,
% the lease appears unusual, there is no documented operational capacity or hires,
% and the staffing and revenue projections are speculative.
dhanasar_prong2_well_positioned :-
    business_plan_submitted,
    economic_impact_analysis_submitted,
    letters_addressing_background_submitted,
    opinion_letter_submitted,
    operational_details_submitted,
    \+ letters_of_intent_postfiling,        % letters of intent must predate filing to be strongly probative
    \+ lease_amount_unusual,                % valid lease evidence required
    \+ evidence_of_operational_capacity_insufficient,
    \+ no_actual_employment_documented.

% Because several of the above negative conditions are present in the record,
% prong 2 cannot be satisfied in this case (the rule above will fail).

% Prong 3: On balance, benefit to the U.S. from waiving the job offer requirement.
% This is a discretionary balancing test that depends on satisfactory proof of prongs 1 and 2.
dhanasar_prong3_on_balance_benefit :-
    dhanasar_prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    dhanasar_prong2_well_positioned.

% Eligibility is met only if underlying EB-2 and all three Dhanasar prongs are satisfied.
eligibility_met :-
    underlying_eb2_established,
    dhanasar_prong1_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    dhanasar_prong2_well_positioned,
    dhanasar_prong3_on_balance_benefit.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
```

text:
The Petitioner is a U.S. corporation, established in 2020, that wholesales wool and cashmere products. It filed an I-140 petition seeking to permanently employ the Beneficiary as its president under the multinational manager/executive classification. The Beneficiary had worked for the Petitioner's parent company in Mongolia from 2014 through 2021 — as an accountant from 2014 to 2016 and as general manager from 2016 to 2021. In 2021 U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services approved the company’s L-1A petition for the Beneficiary, which allowed her to work in the United States in the offered U.S. position of president. The Beneficiary’s L-1A status expired in June 2022; payroll and tax records show she lacked U.S. employment authorization from July through October 2022, returned to work in October 2022, and served as the Petitioner’s sole employee until January 2023. The Petitioner filed the I-140 in July 2022; contemporaneous quarterly tax returns and payroll records indicate the company employed only one person at the time of filing. The company hired two additional employees after the petition was filed. The Petitioner submitted job descriptions describing the Beneficiary’s proposed duties and, in response to a request for evidence, stated that the petition proposed the Beneficiary would continue to serve the company on a permanent basis in the same executive capacity.

The Petitioner appealed the Nebraska Service Center decision denying the I-140. On appeal, the company argued that the adjudicator should have considered evidence of circumstances arising after the petition’s filing (including the hirings that took place during the petition’s pendency), and that those post-filing hirings demonstrated the Beneficiary had been performing executive duties and was in the process of delegating non-executive tasks. The Petitioner also urged that adjudicators should defer to the prior approval of its L-1A petition as evidence that the Beneficiary performed executive or managerial duties, and it maintained that staffing levels were appropriate given the business’s needs and stage of development.
statutes: A noncitizen qualifies as a multinational executive if - in the three years before their U .S. entry as a nonimmigrant - they worked outside the country for at least one year in a managerial or executive capacity. Section 203(b )(1 )(C) ofthe Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.5G)(3)(i)(B). The noncitizen must also seek to provide managerial or executive services in the United States to the same employer or its subsidiary or affiliate. Id. A petitioner for a multinational manager or executive must state that it would employ a beneficiary in the United States in a managerial or executive capacity and "clearly describe" the offered job's duties. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(i)(5). Also, a petitioner must demonstrate that it has been doing business for at least one year and that it has the ability to pay the beneficiary the offered job's proffered wage. 8 C.F.R. §§ 204.5(g)(2), (i)(3)(D).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

text:
The petitioner filed Form I-140 seeking EB-2 classification and a national interest waiver. In her initial filing she stated she intended to work as a general and operations manager in the United States, advising U.S. companies across a variety of industries on planning, directing, and coordinating operations, with particular emphasis on helping companies doing business or planning to do business internationally. She submitted a personal statement, letters of recommendation, industry articles about the field of general and operations managers and the role of operations and innovation in business growth, and an offer letter from a U.S. consulting company indicating its intent to employ her as director of operations.

After receiving a request for evidence asking for clarification of the proposed endeavor and its national importance, the petitioner responded that she intended to operate her own consulting company (name redacted) to offer international relations, foreign trade, and business development consulting to U.S. companies. She submitted a business plan describing services and plans to create “value chains between Turkey and the United States” by providing business consultancy to Turkish companies planning to establish operations in the U.S. and to U.S. companies establishing operations in Turkey. The business plan indicated the company would be based in Florida, that the petitioner would serve as its chief executive officer and head consultant, and that she planned to organize trade events to connect U.S. and Turkish companies; the plan also included employment and revenue projections.

The record also contains an expert opinion letter and additional recommendation letters addressing the petitioner’s experience and qualifications. On appeal, the petitioner did not contest the finding that her RFE business plan represented a material change to the originally stated endeavor. Instead, in her appellate brief she generally asserted that the director did not apply the correct standard of proof and “imposed novel substantive and evidentiary requirements,” though she did not identify specific examples of those errors. Her brief largely reiterated the arguments and documentation submitted in response to the RFE and asserted that her education and professional experience would benefit U.S. companies needing her specialized operations and management skills.
statutes:
To qualify for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, a petitioner must establish they are an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b)(2)(A) of the Act. An advanced degree is any U.S. academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. A U.S. bachelor's degree or foreign equivalent degree followed by five years of progressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. 8 C.F.R . § 204.5(k)(2). If a petitioner establishes eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then demonstrate that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship

and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 1 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts extracted from the Administrative Appeals Office case
% (All boolean-style facts use arity 0 as required.)

% The petitioner established the underlying EB-2 classification (not contested here).
eb2_eligibility.

% The director treated the petitioner's business-plan-based description of operating her own
% consulting company as a material change and did not consider that plan probative.
director_treated_business_plan_as_material_change.
business_plan_not_considered_probative.

% The petitioner did not contest the director's material-change determination on appeal,
% and her appellate brief did not specifically address the national importance of the proposed endeavor.
petitioner_did_not_challenge_material_change_on_appeal.
appellate_brief_did_not_address_national_importance.

% Because the principal evidence of the proposed endeavor (the business plan) was treated as
% a material change and not probative, and the petitioner did not contest that determination,
% the record cannot establish the national-importance prong.
cannot_establish_national_importance.

% For similar reasons the record does not show the petitioner is well-positioned to advance
% the proposed endeavor (business-plan evidence not probative and left unchallenged).
cannot_show_well_positioned.

% Because the key evidentiary basis was disregarded and not contested, on balance waiving
% the job-offer requirement does not favor the United States in this record.
balancing_not_met.

% Dhanasar framework predicates (these succeed only if the corresponding negative flags are absent).
substantial_merit_and_national_importance :-
    \+ cannot_establish_national_importance.

well_positioned_to_advance :-
    \+ cannot_show_well_positioned.

benefit_of_waiving_job_offer :-
    \+ balancing_not_met.

% Eligibility is met only if EB-2 eligibility is established AND all Dhanasar prongs succeed.
eligibility_met :-
    eb2_eligibility,
    substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned_to_advance,
    benefit_of_waiving_job_offer.

% Decision and entrypoint (must be last as requested).
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
```

text: The Applicant seeks a Certificate of Citizenship based on her claim that she acquired U.S. citizenship at birth through her mother. The record states that she was born in Mexico to married parents and that her mother is a U.S. citizen born in 1952. With the N-600 application, she submitted her birth certificate, copies of the biographic pages of her mother's expired and current U.S. passports, and statements from her mother and two maternal cousins. Her mother's materials referred to a chart of periods of physical presence in the United States, but no chart was submitted. In affidavits filed with the application, the cousins stated that the mother spent every July and August and about one week in December with their families in Eagle Pass, Texas, from 1959 to 1969, and every July and August in Las Vegas, Nevada, from 1970 to 1978. The Applicant also submitted her father's master's degree awarded in August 1980, her father's transcript for 1978 to 1981, her brother's birth certificate showing a 1980 birth in Wisconsin, her mother's Wisconsin probationary license expiring in 1982, and her mother's WIC card valid from September 1979 to May 1980. After a second request for additional evidence, she submitted another affidavit from her mother repeating her account of time spent in the United States and additional cousin affidavits repeating the earlier account. The Houston, Texas Field Office denied the application, stating that the record did not establish enough prior physical presence in the United States by the mother to transmit citizenship at the Applicant's birth. In that decision, the Director calculated five years and 18 days of physical presence, including four years, three months, and six days after the mother's fourteenth birthday. The appellate decision also describes the record as indicating 11 months of physical presence before the mother's fourteenth birthday and four years and three months after that birthday. The matter is before the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal. On appeal, the Applicant argues that the Director wrongly treated the record as lacking required primary evidence and wrongly discounted the affidavits from her mother and cousins, and she argues that the application process does not require any particular document so long as the materials prove the U.S.-citizen parent's residence or physical presence in the United States.
statutes: The applicable law for transmitting citizenship to a child born abroad when one parent is a U.S. citizen is the statute that was in effect at the time of the child's birth. See Chau v. Immigration and Naturalization Service, 247 F.3d 1026, 1029 n.3 (9th Cir. 2001) (internal quotationmarks and citation omitted). The statute in effect at the time of the Applicant's birth in 1984 was former section 301(a)(7) of the Act, which provided in pertinent part that a child born abroad to one U.S. citizen and one noncitizen parent would acquire U.S. citizenship if the child's U.S. citizen parent "prior to the birth of such [ child], was physically present in the United States or its outlying possessions for a period or periods totaling not less than ten years, at least five ofwhich were after attaining the age of fourteen years." 1 The Director erroneously stated that former section 30l(g) of the Act applied to the Petitioner, rather than former section 30l(a)(7) of the Act. However , the Director correctly stated the residency requirements of section 301(a)(7) of the Act.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
wi_corroborated_months(24).

    eaglepass_years(Start,End),

    ).
```

text:
The applicant is a Mexican national who applied for permission to reapply for admission to the United States. He first entered the United States without inspection in February 1997 and remained until 2000. He reentered without inspection in January 2001 and departed in June 2001. He attempted to enter in January 2003 and was voluntarily returned to Mexico, then entered without inspection in February 2003 and remained until August 2003. He again attempted to enter in November 2006 and was voluntarily returned to Mexico, and he has remained outside the United States since that time.

In October 2021 the applicant attended a consular interview at the U.S. Consulate in Ciudad Juarez. A Department of State consular officer determined that he was inadmissible because he had reentered the United States without inspection after accruing more than one year of unlawful presence, and informed him he would need to file Form I-212 to seek permission to reapply for admission and that he became eligible to do so as of November 2016 after ten years outside the United States.

The New Orleans Field Office Director denied the applicant’s Form I-212, stating the applicant was ineligible to apply because he had never been deported or removed. The applicant appealed that denial, disputing the Director’s determination and arguing that the consular officer’s finding of inadmissibility made Form I-212 the proper form to seek permission to reapply. On appeal, the applicant submitted evidence including documents from Mexico’s Secretaría de Hacienda y Crédito Público showing tax filings from 2007 to 2020, a police clearance letter from the Prosecutor’s Office of the State of Querétaro dated October 2021, and letters of support from friends.
statutes:
Section 212(a)(9)(C)(i)(I) of the Act provides that any noncitizen who has been unlawfully present in the United States for an aggregate period of more than 1 year, or has been ordered removed, and who enters or attempts to reenter the United States without being admitted, is inadmissible. Noncitizens found inadmissible under section 212(a)(9)(C) of the Act may seek permission to reapply for admission under section 212(a)(9)(C)(ii), which provides that inadmissibility shall not apply to a foreign national seeking admission more than ten years after the date of last departure from the United States if, prior to the reembarkation at a place outside the United States or attempt to be readmitted from a foreign contiguous territory, the Secretary of Homeland Security has consented to the foreign national's 1 There is no indication in the record that the Applicant was ever deported or ordered removed from the United States. He does not, however, seek permission to reapply under section 212(a)(9)(A)(iii) of the Act for having previously been ordered removed, but under section 212(a)(9)(C)(ii).

reapplying for admission.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text: The petitioner is a for‑profit, Russian‑language school that filed Form I-140 to sponsor a noncitizen it employs as an e‑learning coordinator under the employment‑based, third‑preference (EB‑3) "professional" category. The labor certification lists the proffered wage as $49,795 per year and the petition’s priority date is August 29, 2022. The school has employed the beneficiary since 2018; the record includes the beneficiary’s 2022 IRS Form W‑2 showing wages of $55,193.11 and a 2022 federal income tax return. The petition was filed in December 2023 and the Texas Service Center Director denied the petition. The school appealed the Director’s decision, asserting that the Director misread and disregarded evidence.
statutes:
Immigration as a professional generally follows a three-step process. First, a prospective employer must apply to the U.S. Department of Labor (DOL) for certification that: there are insufficient U.S. workers able, willing, qualified, and available for an offered position; and a noncitizen's employment in the position would not harm wages and working conditions of U.S. workers with similar jobs. Section 212(a)(5)(A)(i) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1182(a)(5)(A)(i). Second, an employer must submit a DOL-approved labor certification with an immigrant visa petition to U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS). Section 204(a)(l)(F) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1154(a)(l)(F); 8 C .F.R. § 204.5(1)(3)(i). Among other things, USCIS determines whether a

noncitizen beneficiary meets the requirements of a DOL-certified position and a requested immigrant visa category. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(1)(3)(ii)(e). Finally, if users approves a petition, a beneficiary may apply for an immigrant visa abroad or, if eligible, "adjustment of status" in the United States. See section 245 of the Act, 8 U.S.e. § 1255.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
