text:
The petitioner was born in the Dominican Republic in 1982 to unmarried, noncitizen parents. In 1989, at age seven, he was admitted to the United States as a lawful permanent resident based on an approved Form I-130 filed by his mother. His mother naturalized as a U.S. citizen in June 1996, when the petitioner was 14 years old. The petitioner filed Form N-600 seeking a Certificate of Citizenship, asserting that he derived citizenship from his naturalized mother as her out-of-wedlock child whose paternity had not been established by legitimation.

The record shows the petitioner’s father registered the petitioner’s birth in 1984, acknowledging paternity. The petitioner’s mother has a 1988 divorce and a 2004 marriage to a person other than the petitioner’s father. The petitioner’s father later executed a 2022 sworn “Non-Marriage Affidavit” in the Dominican Republic stating he never married the petitioner’s mother.

After the Form N-600 was denied, the petitioner appealed and then filed a combined motion to reopen and reconsider. On motion he submitted additional evidence intended to show his residence in the United States during the relevant period after his mother’s 1996 naturalization and before his 18th birthday in 2000, including New York school records showing attendance from September 1996 to November 1997 and Florida school records showing attendance from 1997 through 1998 with a Florida address. The record does not indicate that his lawful permanent resident status was ever terminated, rescinded, or formally abandoned after his 1989 admission.
statutes:
A motion to reopen must state new facts and be supported by documentary evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(2). A motion to reconsider, in tum, must establish that our prior decision was based on an incorrect application of law or policy and that the decision was incorrect based on the evidence in the record of proceedings at the time of the decision. 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(3). We may grant motions that satisfy these requirements and demonstrate eligibility for the requested benefit. We may also reopen a proceeding or reconsider a prior decision on Service motion. 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(5). 1 Having concluded that the Applicant was otherwise ineligible for a Certificate of Citizenship, we reserved the issue and did not address it in our decision on appeal.

As previously discussed, while generally both parents of a foreign-born child must naturalize as U.S. citizens for that child to derive citizenship under former section 32l(a) of the Act, the child may also derive citizenship, in pertinent part, upon: (3) ... the naturalization of the mother if the child was born out of wedlock and the paternity of the child has not been established by legitimation; and if (4) Such naturalization takes place while such child is under the age of eighteen years; and (5) Such child is residing in the United States pursuant to a lawful admission for permanent residence at the time ofthe naturalization ofthe parent ... naturalized under clause ... (3) ofthis subsection, or thereafter begins to reside permanently in the United States while under the age of eighteen years. The standard of proof in citizenship proceedings is a preponderance ofthe evidence. Matter ofBaires­ Larios, 24 I&N Dec. 467, 468 (BIA 2008). Under the preponderance of the evidence standard, applicants must demonstrate that what they claim is "probably true," or "more likely than not." Matter ofChawathe, 25 I&N Dec. 369,376 (AAO 2010).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (case record)
child_birth_year(1982).
born_in_dominican_republic.
born_out_of_wedlock.
parents_noncitizens_at_birth.
father_acknowledged_paternity(1984).
father_never_married_mother.        % 2022 affidavit stating no marriage to child's mother
admitted_lpr(1989).                 % admitted to the U.S. as a lawful permanent resident in 1989
i130_filed_by_mother_approved.
mother_naturalized(1996,6).         % mother naturalized June 1996
mother_divorce(1988).
mother_married(2004).

% Documentary evidence submitted on motion to reopen/reconsider
school_records_indicate_residence(1996,1998).  % attendance and addresses in US from Sept 1996 through 1998
new_facts_and_documentary_evidence.           % motion contains new facts and evidence (school records, affidavit, etc.)

% Legal rules and derived predicates

% A child is under eighteen at the time of an event if the event year minus birth year is less than 18.
child_under_18_at(Year) :-
    child_birth_year(BY),
    Age is Year - BY,
    Age < 18.

% Paternity is not considered established by legitimation where the parents never married
% (in this record, father never married the mother).
paternity_not_established_by_legitimation :-
    born_out_of_wedlock,
    father_never_married_mother.

% The statutory element that the mother naturalized while the child was under 18.
mother_naturalized_while_child_under_18 :-
    mother_naturalized(YN,_),
    child_under_18_at(YN).

% The statutory residence/admission requirement:
% child was residing in the United States pursuant to a lawful admission for permanent residence
% at the time of the mother's naturalization or thereafter while under 18.
residing_in_us_as_lpr_at_or_after_naturalization :-
    mother_naturalized(YN,_),
    admitted_lpr(YLPR),
    YLPR =< YN,                                % admitted before or at naturalization
    child_under_18_at(YN).                     % still under 18 at naturalization

% Alternatively, child could begin to reside permanently in the US after naturalization while under 18.
residing_in_us_by_evidence :-
    mother_naturalized(YN,_),
    child_under_18_at(YN),
    school_records_indicate_residence(Sfrom,Sto),
    Sfrom =< Sto,
    Sfrom =< YN,                               % school attendance begins on/before naturalization year (1996)
    Sto >= YN.                                 % school attendance continues through/after naturalization year

% Preponderance of the evidence: the applicant must show facts are more likely than not.
% For this record, the combination of LPR admission, school records covering 1996-1998,
% and documentary acknowledgements constitute meeting the preponderance standard.
preponderance_of_evidence :-
    admitted_lpr(1989),
    school_records_indicate_residence(1996,1998),
    father_acknowledged_paternity(1984).

% Motion standards (8 C.F.R. §103.5)
motion_to_reopen_has_new_facts :-
    new_facts_and_documentary_evidence.

motion_to_reconsider_shows_error :-
    % In this simplified model, we consider the motion to reconsider adequate if
    % the movant presented new legal argument that prior decision failed to consider
    % the statutory path for an out-of-wedlock child whose paternity was not legitimated.
    mother_naturalized_while_child_under_18,
    paternity_not_established_by_legitimation.

motion_valid :-
    % Combined motion (reopen + reconsider) is valid if new facts were submitted and
    % the reconsideration identifies an arguable legal error with support in the record.
    motion_to_reopen_has_new_facts,
    motion_to_reconsider_shows_error.

% Eligibility under former section 321(a) (parsed requirements):
% - child born out of wedlock and paternity not established by legitimation;
% - mother naturalized while child under 18;
% - child residing in the United States as an LPR at the time of naturalization or thereafter while under 18;
% - applicant meets preponderance of the evidence standard.
eligibility_met :-
    born_out_of_wedlock,
    paternity_not_established_by_legitimation,
    mother_naturalized_while_child_under_18,
    (   residing_in_us_as_lpr_at_or_after_naturalization
    ;   residing_in_us_by_evidence
    ),
    preponderance_of_evidence.

% Entrypoint and decision output (do not modify)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner was naturalized as a U.S. citizen in 1998 and holds a Certificate of Naturalization that lists a date of birth of January 26, 1970. At the time of naturalization the petitioner signed the Certificate Preparation Sheet, the Oath Declaration sheet (each showing the January 26, 1970 date of birth), and the face of the Certificate of Naturalization, certifying the pre-printed personal data on the certificate. The petitioner’s Form N-400 (Application for Naturalization) and his Form I-151 (Alien Registration Receipt Card, showing an entry date in 1965) also show the January 26, 1970 date of birth. Records in the administrative file show that when the petitioner was interviewed in December 1984 by an immigration officer, he had an interpreter and swore under oath that the contents of his Form I-590 (Registration for Classification as a Refugee) — including the January 26, 1970 date of birth — were true. A Registration Acknowledgement included with his naturalization filing shows that when he registered for Selective Service in 1991 he listed his date of birth as January 26, 1970. More recently, a U.S. passport issued in April 2022 and a California driver’s license issued in August 2022 each reflect the January 26, 1970 date of birth.

The petitioner asserts that his true date of birth is January 26, 1967 and that the January 1970 month and year were incorrectly ascribed to him during his refugee intake process in Indonesia in 1985. To support this claim, the petitioner submitted a Vietnamese birth certificate showing a January 26, 1967 date of birth and, on appeal, submitted a statement from his father stating that the date of birth on the petitioner’s Form N-400 was based on the incorrect date assigned during the 1985 refugee intake. The petitioner filed Form N-565 seeking a replacement Certificate of Naturalization that would show the different (1967) date of birth.
statutes:
Department ofHomeland Security regulations on corrections of Certificates ofNaturalization provide, in pertinent part at 8 C.F.R. § 338.5, that: (a) [W]henever a Certificate of Naturalization has been delivered which does not conform to the facts shown on the application for naturalization, or a clerical error was made in preparing the certificate, an application for issuance of a corrected certificate may be filed, without fee, in accordance with the form instructions. (e) [T]he correction will not be deemed to be justified where the naturalized person later alleges that the .. . date of birth which the applicant stated to be his or her

correct ... date of birth at the time of naturalization was not in fact his or her ... date of birth at the time of the naturalization.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts from the record (boolean/style predicates are arity 0 per instructions)
petitioner_claims_birth_1967.
vietnamese_birth_cert_shows_1967.
certificate_shows_1970.
passport_2022_shows_1970.
drivers_license_2022_shows_1970.
naturalized_in_1998.
application_n400_shows_1970.
i151_shows_1970.
signed_cert_prep_sheet_1998_shows_1970.
signed_oath_declaration_1998_shows_1970.
selective_service_1991_shows_1970.
father_statement_refugee_intake_error_1985.
i590_interview_1984_sworn_shows_1970.

% Regulatory framing (8 C.F.R. § 338.5)
% Correction may be available if certificate does not conform to facts shown on the application
certificate_does_not_conform_to_application :-
    certificate_shows_1970,
    \+ application_n400_shows_1967.   % certificate differs from application only if application shows a different date

% A clearer explicit check whether certificate and application differ:
certificate_and_application_differ :-
    certificate_shows_1970,
    \+ application_n400_shows_1970.

% No evidence in the record that the application (N-400) showed 1967:
% (application_n400_shows_1970 is a fact above; application_n400_shows_1967 is therefore false)

% Correction may be available if a clerical error was made in preparing the certificate.
% Here the record shows the petitioner signed the certificate and related forms with the 1970 date,
% so there is no independent evidence of a clerical error in preparation.
clerical_error_made :-
    % no positive evidence of a clerical error in the administrative record
    fail.

% Applicant's own representations at the time of naturalization can bar correction under 8 C.F.R. § 338.5(e).
applicant_stated_1970_at_time_of_naturalization :-
    % the N-400 and other naturalization-era documents show 1970 and were signed or sworn by the petitioner
    application_n400_shows_1970,
    ( signed_cert_prep_sheet_1998_shows_1970 ; signed_oath_declaration_1998_shows_1970 ).

correction_disallowed_by_cfr_3385_e :-
    applicant_stated_1970_at_time_of_naturalization.

% Correction allowed if certificate does not conform to the facts shown on the application
% OR if a clerical error was made in preparing the certificate.
correction_allowed :-
    certificate_and_application_differ
    ; clerical_error_made.

% Eligibility is conditional: correction must be allowed and not barred by § 338.5(e).
eligibility_met :-
    correction_allowed,
    \+ correction_disallowed_by_cfr_3385_e.

% Entrypoint structure required by the instructions
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The Petitioner, described as an entrepreneur and swim instructor, seeks EB-2 immigrant classification based on exceptional ability and a waiver of the job-offer requirement. The Director of the Texas Service Center denied the petition, and the matter was appealed to the Administrative Appeals Office. The petition was originally filed on an advanced-degree basis, but the appeal does not address that basis and instead argues that the Director erred in finding that the Petitioner does not qualify as an individual of exceptional ability.

The record includes an official academic record showing completion of a sports-related certificate in 2016. The Director described the record as showing that the Petitioner had seven years of experience in the field of the proposed endeavor at the time of filing. The Director also stated that the Petitioner had not provided evidence of a license or certification to practice the profession, or evidence or a claim that she had commanded a salary or other remuneration demonstrating exceptional ability. The appeal does not address the experience, license-or-certification, or salary points.

The record also shows that the Petitioner is a member of the International Association for Support and Development of Infant and Baby Swimming. In support of treating that organization as a professional association, she submitted her membership card and the association's governing statute and argues on appeal that the association is a nonprofit organization based on voluntary membership and formed to represent and protect common, including professional, interests. The appeal decision describes the submitted materials as not establishing that the occupation, or membership in that association, requires a U.S. bachelor's degree or foreign equivalent.

Other evidence includes recommendation letters and employer statements. As quoted in the decision, one states that the Petitioner's work for a fitness center helped its swimming department achieve 120 percent of its financial plan, and another states that she independently developed special programs and activities for children with special needs. The record also contains a letter of gratitude from a swimming federation for implementing a methodology, many certificates from swimming competitions in which the Petitioner participated, evidence of a Certificate for Master of Sports in Swimming, and two instructional manuals that she wrote. On appeal, she states that obtaining Master of Sports status is more complex than simply completing a course and that the title is awarded for achieving standards in official sports competitions. She also asserts that she qualifies for a national interest waiver.
statutes:
To qualify for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first show eligibility for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. Exceptional ability means a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). A petitioner must initially submit documentation that satisfies at least three of six categories of evidence. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F). 1 Meeting at least three criteria, however, does not, in and of itself, establish eligibility for this classification. 2 If a petitioner does so, we will then conduct a final merits determination to decide whether the evidence in its totality shows that they are recognized as having a degree of expertise and will substantially 1 If these types of evidence do not readily apply to the individual's occupation, a petitioner may submit comparable evidence to establish their eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(iii). 2 USCIS has previously confirmed the applicability of this two-part adjudicative approach in the context of aliens of exceptional ability. 6 U SCJS Policy Manual F.5(B)(2), https://www .uscis.gov/policy-manual/volume-6-part-f-chapter-5.

benefit the national economy, cultural or educational interests, or welfare ofthe United States. Section 203(b)(2)(A) of the Act. If a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion,3 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the record
has_academic_certificate.
certificate_relates_to_field.
advanced_degree_evaluated_master_unrelated.
years_experience(7).
membership_documented.
recommendation_letters_present.
letter_of_gratitude_federation.
competition_certificates_present.
master_of_sports_certificate.
authored_instructional_manuals.
no_professional_license.
no_salary_evidence.

% Regulatory criteria (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii)(A)-(F))
% A: official academic record (degree, diploma, certificate) relating to the field
meets_criterion_A :- has_academic_certificate, certificate_relates_to_field.

% B: letters documenting at least ten years of full-time experience in the occupation
meets_criterion_B :-
    years_experience(Y),
    Y >= 10.

% C: license to practice the profession or certification required for the occupation
% Record contains no professional license -> criterion not met
meets_criterion_C :- false.

% D: evidence of commanding a salary (commercial remuneration) indicating exceptional ability
% Record contains no such evidence -> criterion not met
meets_criterion_D :- false.

% E: membership in professional associations requiring outstanding achievements of members
% The record shows membership documentation but not that the association requires outstanding achievement,
% so this regulatory criterion is not established on the record
meets_criterion_E :- false.

% F: recognition for achievements and significant contributions to the industry or field
% The record contains multiple recommendation letters describing accomplishments, a federation gratitude
% letter, competition certificates, a "Master of Sports" recognition, and authored instructional manuals.
% These items collectively support this criterion.
meets_criterion_F :-
    (   recommendation_letters_present
    ;   letter_of_gratitude_federation
    ;   competition_certificates_present
    ;   master_of_sports_certificate
    ;   authored_instructional_manuals
    ).

% Count how many regulatory criteria are met
num_criteria_met(N) :-
    (meets_criterion_A -> A = 1 ; A = 0),
    (meets_criterion_B -> B = 1 ; B = 0),
    (meets_criterion_C -> C = 1 ; C = 0),
    (meets_criterion_D -> D = 1 ; D = 0),
    (meets_criterion_E -> E = 1 ; E = 0),
    (meets_criterion_F -> F = 1 ; F = 0),
    N is A + B + C + D + E + F.

minimum_three_criteria :-
    num_criteria_met(N),
    N >= 3.

% Final merits determination: even if regulatory criteria are met, totality must show degree of expertise
% significantly above that ordinarily encountered. The record here is insufficient on balance to
% demonstrate exceptional ability in the totality of the evidence.
totality_shows_exceptional :- false.

% Exceptional ability requires meeting the regulatory minima and the final merits determination
exceptional_ability :-
    minimum_three_criteria,
    totality_shows_exceptional.

% Dhanasar framework for national interest waiver:
% 1) substantial merit and national importance
% 2) individual is well-positioned to advance the endeavor
% 3) on balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the U.S.
% The record, as adjudicated, does not establish these prongs on the totality of the evidence.
substantial_merit_and_national_importance :- false.
well_positioned_to_advance :- false.
benefit_balance_prong :- false.

national_interest_waiver :-
    exceptional_ability,
    substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    well_positioned_to_advance,
    benefit_balance_prong.

% Eligibility for acceptance requires both exceptional ability (EB-2 underlying classification)
% and meeting the Dhanasar national interest waiver prongs.
eligibility_met :-
    exceptional_ability,
    national_interest_waiver.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner is a native and citizen of Mexico who was granted U-1 nonimmigrant status from June 2019 through June 2023. She timely filed Form I-485, Application to Adjust Status, in February 2023. The Nebraska Service Center issued a request for evidence (RFE) asking for proof of continuous physical presence, a statement of continuous physical presence, all passport pages valid during her U-1 status, and explanations for several questions on Part 8 of the I-485 (questions 16, 25–29, and 77) that she had answered “yes.” The petitioner responded with evidence of continuous physical presence, a written statement of continuous physical presence, and passport pages, but did not provide the requested explanations for the yes answers. The Service Center issued a second RFE; the petitioner responded with a document from the Justice Center Court. The Director denied the application, finding the responses did not include the required explanations for the affirmative answers and therefore did not satisfy the documentation requests. On appeal, the petitioner submitted an appeal cover letter, the missing explanations for why she answered “yes” to questions 16, 25–29, and 77 in Part 8, and two letters pertaining to her criminal records. She filed the appeal to contest the denial of her I-485 and to have the newly submitted explanations and documents considered.
statutes: U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may adjust the status of a U nonimmigrant to that of an LPR if, among other requirements, they have been physically present in the United States for a continuous period of three years since the date of their admission as a U nonimmigrant, and they establish that their continued presence in the United States is justified on humanitarian grounds, to ensure family unity, or is otherwise in the public interest. Section 245(m) of the Act.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts (case-specific)
petitioner_native_and_citizen_of_mexico.
u1_status_from_june_2019_to_june_2023.
filed_i485_in_february_2023.

% RFE and responses
rfe1_requested_continuous_physical_presence_statement_passports_and_part8_explanations.
responded_rfe1_with_continuous_physical_presence_evidence.
responded_rfe1_with_statement_of_continuous_presence.
responded_rfe1_with_passport_documentation.
did_not_provide_part8_explanations_initially.
rfe2_issued_by_service_center.
responded_rfe2_with_justice_center_court_document.

% Adjudication and appeal
i485_denied_by_service_center.
appeal_filed_to_aa0.
appeal_included_cover_letter.
appeal_included_written_explanations_for_part8_yes_answers.
appeal_included_two_letters_related_to_criminal_records.

% Legal rule (section 245(m)): adjustment available if U nonimmigrant has been physically present for a continuous period of 3 years since admission
% and establishes that continued presence is justified on humanitarian grounds, to ensure family unity, or is otherwise in the public interest.

% Derived facts / legal predicates (all are zero-arity booleans where appropriate)

% The petitioner was admitted as a U-1 nonimmigrant in June 2019 and remained in that status through at least February 2023,
% and therefore has the requisite continuous physical presence of at least three years at the time of filing.
continuous_physical_presence_three_years :-
    u1_status_from_june_2019_to_june_2023,
    filed_i485_in_february_2023.

% Evidence on the record showing physical presence was submitted in response to the first RFE.
there_is_evidence_of_continuous_physical_presence :-
    responded_rfe1_with_continuous_physical_presence_evidence.

% The initial procedural deficiency (missing Part 8 explanations) was later addressed on appeal by providing written explanations.
there_are_explanatory_answers_for_part8_on_appeal :-
    appeal_included_written_explanations_for_part8_yes_answers.

% The appeal included documentation related to criminal records which is relevant to assessing whether continued presence is justified.
there_are_documents_addressing_criminal_records_on_appeal :-
    appeal_included_two_letters_related_to_criminal_records.

% For purposes of this case file, continued presence is treated as justified when the record contains explanatory answers
% for the Part 8 questions and supporting documents addressing criminal-record issues (as submitted on appeal),
% together with the fact of U-1 status and the evidentiary showing of continuous presence.
continued_presence_justified :-
    u1_status_from_june_2019_to_june_2023,
    there_is_evidence_of_continuous_physical_presence,
    there_are_explanatory_answers_for_part8_on_appeal,
    there_are_documents_addressing_criminal_records_on_appeal.

% Timely filing of the adjustment application (filed while in valid U-1 status)
timely_filed_i485 :-
    filed_i485_in_february_2023,
    u1_status_from_june_2019_to_june_2023.

% Eligibility: all necessary conditions to Accept the case (per instruction)
eligibility_met :-
    continuous_physical_presence_three_years,
    continued_presence_justified,
    timely_filed_i485.

% Decision and runtime entrypoint (do not modify)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner is a noncitizen who filed an I-526 petition seeking EB-5 immigrant investor classification. She states that she invested the equivalent of $500,000 in a new commercial enterprise (an NCE) associated with a luxury boutique condo-hotel project in Texas. Bank records in the record show that on September 2, 2018 she transferred 1,876,750 Saudi Riyal (SAR) from her bank account (account number ending in 0014) to the NCE’s account; she contends that this remittance constituted her EB-5 investment and is equivalent to $500,000. A September 2018 letter from the NCE’s chief financial officer states that the petitioner paid a “$500,000 Aggregate Purchase Payment” that was deposited into the NCE’s escrow account. On the same date, bank records also show a separate payment of SAR 187,675 remitted for administrative fees.

The petitioner explains that the funds she invested originated from distributions she has received as a shareholder of a family-owned pastries and Arabic sweets company. She reports that in 1999 her father gifted her a 5.9% ownership interest in that company and that she has received lawful distributions from that ownership interest since 1999. She has repeatedly stated on the record and in her appellate brief that her lawful source of funds for the EB-5 investment “stems from company distributions.”

The record includes bank and company documents showing multiple distributions to the petitioner from the company. Documented transfers to her account ending in 0014 include: SAR 1,309,565 on August 20, 2014; SAR 825,854 on August 9, 2015; SAR 50,000 and SAR 346,171 on December 17, 2015; and SAR 200,000 on May 20, 2018, totaling SAR 2,731,590 between August 2014 and September 2018. The record also includes earlier distributions of SAR 437,309 on December 19, 2012 and SAR 328,100 on May 25, 2013. The petitioner states that she consistently claimed that distributions totaling SAR 2,332,606 were deposited into her account and that the distributions between 2012 and 2018 could finance her EB-5 investment; she also stated on appeal that she was entitled to SAR 1,459,565 in August 2014 but received SAR 1,309,565 because she gifted SAR 150,000 to her sister.

The petitioner submitted complete bank records for account ending in 0014 covering August 1, 2014 through May 31, 2018. Those records also show the account received other large sums from sources the petitioner has not fully identified on the record, including SAR 1,500,000 on December 18, 2017; SAR 40,000 on February 16, 2016; and a total of SAR 1,290,000 on March 24, 2015. The petitioner acknowledged receipt of these additional amounts but did not specify their sources in the record. She also acknowledged, in her appellate brief, that money is fungible.

On appeal the petitioner submitted a brief and additional evidence and maintained that she has established the lawful source of the funds she invested. Her appellate arguments include that the distributions she received from the family company financed her EB-5 investment and that the presence of other funds in the same account (commingling) does not negate the lawful origin of the invested funds. She also pointed to the company distributions and the NCE CFO’s letter as evidence supporting her claim of investment. Finally, the record contains documents reflecting a discrepancy in how her ownership interest was described: the petitioner asserted her father gifted her the ownership interest, while another company document (the articles of association preamble) indicates she paid her father for the interest; the petitioner’s filings address this point in the record.
statutes:
A noncitizen may be classified as an immigrant investor if they invest the requisite amount of qualifying capital in an NCE. A noncitizen may invest the required funds directly in an NCE or through a regional center, as the Petitioner has done in this case. Regional centers can pool immigrant 1 On March 15, 2022, President Joseph Biden signed the EB-5 Reform and Integrity Act, which made significant amendments to the EB-5 program, including the designation of targeted employment areas and the minimum investment amounts. See Section 203(b)(5) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § l 153(b)(5) (2022). As the Petitioner had filed her petition in September 2018, the relevant law then in existence governs this appellate adjudication. 2 The Petitioner indicates that the NCE is in a targeted employment area, and that the required amount of qualifying capital is downwardly adjusted from $1,000,000 to $500,000. See 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(f)(2) (2018). 3 is an entity that U .S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) has designated to participate in the EB-5 program. A regional center is an economic unit involved with the promotion of economic growth, "including.. . improved regional productivity, job creation, and increased domestic capital investment." See 8 C.F.R . § 204.6(e) (2018).

(and other) investor funds for qualifying projects that create jobs directly or indirectly. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(4)(iii) (2018). A noncitizen must demonstrate that they have placed their own capital at risk in the NCE. See Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. 206, 213 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998); Matter ofSofjici, 22 I&N Dec. 158, 165 n.3 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998) (stating that "[a] petitioner must ... establish, pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e), that funds invested are [their] own"). In addition, the noncitizen must show that their invested capital did not derive, directly or indirectly, from unlawful means. 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(e). To show the lawful source of the funds, an investor must submit evidence such as foreign business and tax records or documentation identifying sources of the capital. See 8 C.F.R. § 204.6(j)(3). Bank letters or statements corroborating the deposit of funds by themselves are insufficient to demonstrate their lawful source. Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at210-11; Matteroflzummi, 22 I&NDec. 169, 195 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998). The record must trace the path of the funds back to a lawful source.4 Matter ofHo, 22 I&N Dec. at 210-11; Matter ofIzummi, 22 I&N Dec. at 195.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Facts derived from the record
% (amounts in Saudi Riyal (SAR) unless otherwise noted)

% Evidence of investment/remittance and corroborating letter
cfo_letter_present.                                 % September 2018 NCE CFO letter stating $500,000 aggregate purchase payment
remittance_present.                                 % remittance from petitioner's account to NCE account recorded
remitted_amount_sar(1876750).                       % SAR 1,876,750 remitted on September 2, 2018
remittance_date(date(2018,9,2)).

% Petitioner's asserted source of funds (minority owner by gift in 1999)
gifted_ownership(1999, 5.9).                        % father gifted 5.9% ownership in 1999

% Documented distributions from the company to petitioner's account (selected totals from the record)
% Sum for Aug 2014–May 2018 as given in the record
distributions_sum_sar(2731590).                     % SAR 2,731,590 (Aug 2014 – Sep 2018 period documented)
% Other older documented distributions referenced
distribution_2012_12_19_sar(437309).
distribution_2013_05_25_sar(328100).

% Bank statements availability for the petitioner's account ending in 0014
bank_statements_start(date(2014,8,1)).
bank_statements_end(date(2018,5,31)).               % bank statements provided only through May 31, 2018
missing_bank_statement_range(date(2018,6,1), date(2018,8,31)).  % no statements for Jun 1–Aug 31, 2018
missing_bank_statement_range(date(2012,12,1), date(2014,7,31)). % no statements for Dec 2012–Jul 2014

% Other large deposits into the account during the relevant period that would require tracing
other_large_deposits_present.                       % there were other large incoming transfers (e.g., SAR 1,500,000 on Dec 18, 2017; SAR 1,290,000 on Mar 24, 2015; etc.)

% Substantive legal-rule-based predicates (all yes/no style predicates are arity 0 per instructions)

% Was the required investment amount evidenced? (CFO letter + remittance)
invested_required_amount :-
    cfo_letter_present,
    remittance_present,
    remitted_amount_sar(_).

% Did funds originate from petitioner's own lawful business distributions?
funds_from_own_sources :-
    gifted_ownership(1999, P),
    P > 0,
    distributions_sum_sar(D),
    D > 0.

% To meet lawful-source tracing, the record must:
%  - show that documented lawful distributions sum is at least the remitted amount, and
%  - provide bank statements that cover the path from the documented source into the account up through the remittance date,
%  - and there must not be intervening undocumented large deposits that could have funded the remittance.
lawful_source_traced :-
    distributions_sum_sar(D),
    remitted_amount_sar(R),
    D >= R,                                     % documented distributions at least equal remittance
    bank_statements_end(EndDate),
    remittance_date(RemDate),
    date_geq(EndDate, RemDate),                 % bank statements cover up through remittance date
    \+ other_large_deposits_present.            % no other large unexplained deposits that require tracing

% Eligibility requires all elements be met
eligibility_met :-
    invested_required_amount,
    funds_from_own_sources,
    lawful_source_traced.

% Date comparison helper: true if Date1 >= Date2
date_geq(date(Y1,M1,D1), date(Y2,M2,D2)) :-
    (Y1 > Y2) ;
    (Y1 =:= Y2, M1 > M2) ;
    (Y1 =:= Y2, M1 =:= M2, D1 >= D2).

% Decision and run structure (must be last)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner is a gas station and convenience store business that filed an I-140 immigrant petition to hire the beneficiary as a bookkeeper. The underlying labor certification for the job set a priority date of April 24, 2020, and specified minimum requirements of 24 months of experience as a bookkeeper or 24 months in accounting, along with proficiency in typing and machines and an ability to work independently and create consistent bookkeeping policies. In support of the petition, the record includes employment verification letters, a statement from the beneficiary, payroll and income tax records, government-issued employment certifications, and the petitioner’s federal tax returns for 2019 through 2021. The Texas Service Center denied the petition on the ground that the petitioner did not establish that the beneficiary possessed the minimum experience required for the offered position, and the petitioner appealed that denial.
statutes: To permanently fill a position in the United States with a foreign worker, a prospective employer must fust obtain certification from the U.S. Department of Labor (DOL). See section 212(a)(5) of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1182(a)(5). DOL approval signifies that insufficient U.S. workers are able, willing, qualified, and available for a position. Id. Labor certification also indicates that the employment of a foreign national will not harm wages and working conditions ofU.S. workers with similar jobs. Id. If DOL approves a position, an employer must next submit the certified labor application with an immigrant visa petition to U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS). See section 204 of the Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1154. Among other things, USCIS considers whether a beneficiary meets the requirements of a certified position and a requested immigrant visa classification. IfUSCIS approves the petition, a foreign national may finally apply for an immigrant visa abroad or, if eligible, adjustment of status in the United States. See section 245 of the Act, 8 U.S.C . § 1255.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text: The petitioner is an entrepreneur who proposes to establish a toy sales and rental business in the United States and to work there as its general and operations manager. He seeks immigrant classification based on his claimed exceptional ability and requests a waiver of the job offer requirement so he can found and run this business. He states that he previously worked in Brazil as an owner, director, partner, or general and operations manager of multiple businesses, including a toy manufacturing/sales/rental business (September 2016–April 2022), an automobile repair and wholesale/retail motor vehicle parts business, a car wash and oil exchange business, and a motor fuel station with a convenience store. To document his background and experience, he submitted corporate organizational documents (articles of organization and an amendment/consolidation), income statements from an accounting firm showing profit distribution and pro labore payments of R$400,000 (2018), R$440,000 (2019), R$400,000 (2020), and R$450,000 (2021), affidavits from an accountant (initially submitted), receipts and payment records for membership in a Brazilian Association of Administration, an “Account Details” document showing an American Management Association account (My AMA ID: 3979048) with a listed job title of “Manager” and membership since October 6, 2022, news and advertising items about a 2005 business location opening, letters of recommendation and appreciation from clients, a lawyer, and a service provider, and two letters of intent from prospective U.S. clients expressing interest in investing in and expanding his proposed U.S. business. In response to a request for evidence, the petitioner indicated he would submit additional affidavits from his accountant describing his duties and full-time work history and included quoted material from those affidavits in his response. On appeal, the petitioner reasserts that he is an individual of exceptional ability, argues that he meets the criteria for membership in professional associations and for recognition of achievements and contributions, and contends that he has established that he qualifies for a national interest waiver; he asks that the earlier decision be reviewed.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB -2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Because this classification requires that the individual's services be sought by a U.S. employer, a separate showing is required to establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement is in the national interest. For the purpose of determining eligibility under section 203(b)(2)(A) of the Act, "exceptional ability" is defined as "a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the sciences, arts, or business." 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). The regulations further provide six criteria, at least three

of which must be satisfied, for an individual to establish exceptional ability: (A) An official academic record showing that the alien has a degree, diploma, certificate, or similar award from a college, university, school, or other institution of learning relating to the area of exceptional ability; (B) Evidence in the form of letter(s) from current or former employer(s) showing that the alien has at least ten years of full-time experience in the occupation for which he or she is being sought; (C) A license to practice the profession or certification for a particular profession or occupation; (D) Evidence that the alien has commanded a salary, or other renumeration for services, which demonstrates exceptional ability; (E) Evidence of membership in professional associations; or (F) Evidence of recognition for achievements and significant contributions to the industry or field by peers, governmental entities, or professional or business organizations. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii).1 Meeting at least three criteria, however, does not, in and of itself, establish eligibility for this classification.2 We then consider the totality of the material provided in a final merits determination and assess whether the record shows that the petitioner is recognized as having a degree of expertise significantly above that ordinarily encountered in the field.3 See Kazarian v. USCIS, 596 F.3d 1115 (9th Cir. 2010) (discussing a two-part review where the documentation is first counted and then, if fulfilling the required number of criteria, considered in the context of a final merits determination). This two-step analysis is consistent with our holding that the "truth is to be determined not by the quantity of evidence alone but by its quality," as well as the principle that we examine "each piece of evidence for relevance, probative value, and credibility, both individually and within the context of the totality of the evidence, to determine whether the fact to be proven is probably true." Matter of Chawathe, 25 l&N Dec. at 376. Once a petitioner demonstrates eligibility as either a member of the professions holding an advanced degree or an individual of exceptional ability, they must then establish eligibility for a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the tenn "national interest," Matter of 1 If these types of evidence do not readily apply to the individual's occupation , a petitioner may submit comparable evidence to establish eligibility. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(iii). 2 See generally 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.5(B)(2), https: //www.uscis.gov/policy-manual. 3 USCIS has previously confirmed the applicability of this two-part adjudicative approach in the context of individuals of exceptional ability. See generally 6 USCIS Policy Manual, supra, at F.5(B)(2). 2

Dhanasar, 26 l&N Dec. 884 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that USCIS may, as matter of discretion,4 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. Id. at 889.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed

```prolog
% Case facts (true/false predicates use arity 0)
petitioner_entrepreneur.                           % petitioner is an entrepreneur
seeks_eb2_national_interest_waiver.                % seeks EB-2 with NIW

% Background employment / documents
worked_as_owner_director_partner_manager_in_brazil. % served in multiple managerial roles
toy_business_2016_2022.                            % operated toy manufacturing/sales/rental Sep 2016–Apr 2022
other_brazilian_businesses.                        % evidence of other businesses (car wash, auto repair, fuel station)
income_statement_present.                          % accounting firm income statement submitted
salary_payments_documented.                        % "pro labore" payments documented (2018–2021)
membership_documents_present.                      % AMA and BAA membership-related documents submitted
news_and_ad_publication_present.                   % news publication & advertisement (2005 business opening) present
letters_recommendation_appreciation_present.       % letters of recommendation/appreciation present
letters_of_intent_present.                         % two letters of intent from prospective clients present
rfe_issued.                                        % USCIS issued an RFE asking for more detail
rfe_response_affidavits_submitted.                 % petitioner submitted additional affidavits in response
appeal_reasserts_membership_and_recognition.       % on appeal, petitioner reaffirmed membership and recognition evidence

% Credibility / quality concerns raised by adjudicator
credibility_issues.                                % RFE concerns about job duties/full-time status and insufficiency of evidence

% Exceptional ability criteria (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(ii))
% A: academic record
criterion_a_academic :- fail.                       % no official academic record evidence in the file

% B: letters from employers showing at least ten years full-time experience
% The record shows managerial roles but not clear ten years of full-time experience.
criterion_b_experience :- fail.

% C: license or certification
criterion_c_license :- fail.

% D: evidence of having commanded a salary or other remuneration demonstrating exceptional ability
% Income statements and pro labore payments were submitted; treated as evidence for this criterion.
criterion_d_salary :-
    salary_payments_documented.

% E: membership in professional associations
criterion_e_membership :-
    membership_documents_present.

% F: recognition for achievements and significant contributions
criterion_f_recognition :-
    letters_recommendation_appreciation_present.

% Helper: collect which criteria are satisfied
satisfied_criteria(Names) :-
    Criteria = [criterion_a_academic, criterion_b_experience, criterion_c_license,
                criterion_d_salary, criterion_e_membership, criterion_f_recognition],
    findall(Name,
            ( member(Name, Criteria), Goal =.. [Name], call(Goal) ),
            Names).

% At least three of the regulatory criteria must be satisfied (count step)
at_least_three_criteria :-
    satisfied_criteria(Names),
    length(Names, N),
    N >= 3.

% Final merits determination (Kazarian two-step): quality/credibility of evidence matters.
% Even if regulatory count is met, credibility or probative value may defeat a final favorable
% merits determination. The record contains credibility concerns raised in the RFE and adjudicator
% noted insufficient detail about job duties/full-time status; therefore final merits determination fails.
final_merits_determination :-
    at_least_three_criteria,
    \+ credibility_issues.    % requires absence of credibility issues to pass final merits

% Exceptional ability eligibility requires passing both the counting step and the final merits test.
exceptional_ability_met :-
    final_merits_determination.

% Dhanasar national interest waiver prongs
% 1) Proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance.
% A toy sales and rental business is primarily a local/commercial retail endeavor; record lacks showing of national importance.
dhanasar_substantial_merit_and_national_importance :- fail.

% 2) Individual is well-positioned to advance the proposed endeavor.
% The record contains some managerial experience and two letters of intent, but credibility concerns and limited evidence of U.S. market traction leave this unproven.
dhanasar_well_positioned :- fail.

% 3) On balance, waiver of the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
% Given the lack of national importance and weak positioning, the balance does not favor a waiver.
dhanasar_balance_of_benefits :- fail.

% National interest waiver met only if all three Dhanasar prongs are satisfied.
niw_met :-
    dhanasar_substantial_merit_and_national_importance,
    dhanasar_well_positioned,
    dhanasar_balance_of_benefits.

% Overall eligibility requires both classification as exceptional ability (or advanced degree) and NIW.
% This program focuses on the claimed exceptional ability route; petitioner did not present an advanced degree case.
eligibility_met :-
    exceptional_ability_met,
    niw_met.

% Entrypoint and result handling (must be last)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: The petitioner seeks classification as a special immigrant juvenile. In 2022, a Florida juvenile court issued an order titled Final Judgment on Petition for Custody and Best Interest Order, granting the petitioner's brother custody of the petitioner until the petitioner reached age 18. In July 2022, the petitioner filed his SIJ petition based on that order. The juvenile court order states that while the petitioner lived with his parents in Guatemala, they forced him to work in construction beginning at age 11, limited his access to education, did not allow him to collect his wages, and physically abused him when he complained that he was tired, wanted a day off, or wanted to continue his education. The order further states that the parents continued to neglect the child and failed to provide food, proper housing, and support, and it found that reunification with the parents was not a viable option because of abandonment and neglect. The order also found that it was in the child's best interest to remain with his brother, where he could be protected from further abuse, abandonment, and neglect. The record as described in the decision also indicates that a Petition for Custody by Extended Family was submitted to the juvenile court, set out the basis for the dependency complaint, and was cited in the SIJ order's factual findings about the petitioner's circumstances in Guatemala. The National Benefits Center denied the Form I-360, stating that the record did not contain a qualifying judicial determination, or a sufficient factual basis for such a determination, that it would not be in the petitioner's best interest to return to his or his parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. The matter is before the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal, and the petitioner argues that he has established eligibility and that the SIJ order is sufficient to support the petition.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for SIJ classification, petitioners must show that they are unmarried, under 21 years old, and have been subject to a state juvenile court order determining that they cannot reunify with one or both parents due to abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under state law. Section 101(a)(27)(J) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.ll(b).1 Petitioners must have been declared dependent upon the juvenile court, or the juvenile court must have placed them in the custody of a state agency or an individual or entity appointed by the state or the juvenile court. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i) of the Act. The record must also contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in the petitioners' best interest to return to their or their parents' country of nationality or last habitual residence. Id. at section 101(a)(27)(J)(ii); 8 C.F.R. § 204.11(c)(2). U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) has sole authority to implement the SIJ provisions of the Act and regulation. Homeland Security Act of 2002, Pub. L. No. 107-296, §§ 471(a), 451(b), 462(c), 116 Stat. 2135 (2002). SIJ classification may only be granted upon the consent of the Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (OHS), through USCIS, when the petitioner meets all other 1 The Department of Homeland Security issued a final rule, effective April 7, 2022, amending its regulations governing the requirements and procedures for petitioners who seek SIJ classification. See Special Immigrant Juvenile Petitions, 87 Fed. Reg. 13066 (Mar. 8, 2022) (revising 8 C.F.R. §§ 204, 205, 245).

eligibility criteria and establishes that the request for SIJ classification is bona fide, which requires the petitioner to establish that a primary reason the required juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse, neglect, abandonment, or a similar basis under State law. Section 101(a)(27)(J)(i)-(iii) of the Act; 8 C.F.R. § 204.11(b)(5).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

```prolog
% Facts derived from the case
petitioner_minor.
petitioner_under_21.
petitioner_unmarried.

lived_in_guatemala.
forced_to_work.
limited_education_access.
not_allowed_collect_wages.
physically_abused_by_parents.

juvenile_court_issued_final_judgment_custody_and_best_interest_2022.
juvenile_court_found_reunification_not_viable_due_to_abandonment_and_neglect.
custody_placed_with_brother_until_18.

filed_i360_july_2022.
director_denied_because_no_best_interest_determination_in_record.

% Petitioner contends the order and filings are sufficient and that primary reason was relief from parental abuse/neglect.
primary_reason_sought_relief_from_parental_abuse.

% Legal-rule encodings (simplified for this case extraction)
% Requirement: petitioner must be unmarried and under 21
require_unmarried :-
    petitioner_unmarried.
require_under_21 :-
    petitioner_under_21.

% Requirement: juvenile court order determining cannot reunify with one/both parents due to abuse/neglect/abandonment or similar
cannot_reunify_due_to_abuse_or_similar :-
    juvenile_court_found_reunification_not_viable_due_to_abandonment_and_neglect.

% Requirement: juvenile court declared dependency or placed petitioner in custody of state agency or individual/entity appointed by the state or court
placed_in_custody_by_state_or_court :-
    custody_placed_with_brother_until_18.

% Requirement: record must contain a judicial or administrative determination that it is not in petitioner's best interest to return to petitioner’s or parents' country
% In this case there is a Final Judgment labeled "Custody and Best Interest" and factual findings showing abuse/neglect/conditions supporting a best-interest finding as to return to country.
has_judicial_best_interest_country_determination :-
    juvenile_court_issued_final_judgment_custody_and_best_interest_2022,
    (   juvenile_court_found_reunification_not_viable_due_to_abandonment_and_neglect
    ;   physically_abused_by_parents
    ;   forced_to_work
    ;   limited_education_access
    ).

% Requirement: bona fide showing that a primary reason the juvenile court determinations were sought was to obtain relief from parental abuse/neglect/abandonment
bona_fide_primary_reason :-
    primary_reason_sought_relief_from_parental_abuse.

% Eligibility is met only if all statutory/regulatory criteria (as modelled) are satisfied.
eligibility_met :-
    require_unmarried,
    require_under_21,
    cannot_reunify_due_to_abuse_or_similar,
    placed_in_custody_by_state_or_court,
    has_judicial_best_interest_country_determination,
    bona_fide_primary_reason.

decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text:
The petitioner filed an I-360 seeking immigrant classification as an abused spouse. The Vermont Service Center director denied the petition and dismissed his subsequent motion, and the petitioner has appealed that decision.

The petitioner’s submissions and the court records show a history of arrests and court actions in Washington state over several decades. In 1998 he was arrested after a fight, was ordered to attend anger management classes and placed on probation, and the case was later closed after he complied. In 2000 he was arrested for driving with a suspended or revoked license and paid a fine to resolve that matter. In 2001 he was arrested on a reckless endangerment charge that was dismissed in 2003. In 2003 he was arrested for malicious mischief (criminal damage to property); that case was dismissed with prejudice later in 2003. In 2014 he was arrested for fourth-degree assault (domestic violence); the court deferred disposition, ordered anger management and parenting classes, and issued a no-contact order; after he complied the charge was dismissed in 2017. While the I-360 was pending, he was arrested in 2022 on an extortion charge. In 2023 a court found him guilty of harassment; he was placed on probation and community supervision for 364 days and sentencing was deferred for 364 days.

In statements submitted with his petition and appeal, the petitioner describes circumstances surrounding several incidents. He says his 2014 arrest involved conflicts with his then-wife, whom he says insulted and physically hurt him and who called the police; he states he attended court-ordered anger management classes and felt he benefited from them. He recounts lending money to friends in Washington in 2016–2017, moving to Arizona, and asking another friend to request repayment; one of those friends reported the matter to police, and although she later promised to retract her allegations the authorities would not allow that withdrawal. The petitioner maintains he is innocent of the harassment offense but says he pled guilty because he was “sick and weary” and wanted to return to normalcy as quickly as possible. He also states he has complied with court orders and understands his case will be closed after some time. He admits to having “made mistakes” and to previously engaging in destructive coping patterns, and says he is working on more positive ways to cope. He describes himself as a loving father, a caring friend and neighbor, and a supportive employer.

Along with his motion and appeal, the petitioner submitted supporting evidence including medical records showing treatment for esophageal cancer in 2023 (discharged to home/self care), federal income tax returns for 2018–2022, evidence of a mortgage, and support letters from family and friends. He also submitted information that he financially supports two U.S. citizen children, owns a restaurant, pays a mortgage, and has filed income tax returns for multiple years.

The petitioner’s stated reasons for appealing are that the director erred in how she characterized the conviction and its legal significance; he asserts harassment under Washington law is “not defined as morally reprehensible,” contends he did not act with criminal intent, and maintains his innocence of the harassment offense (explaining he pled guilty only to end the matter quickly).
statutes:
A petitioner who is the spouse of a U.S. citizen may self-petition for immigrant classification if the petitioner married the U .S. citizen spouse in good faith, is eligible for immigrant classification based on that relationship, resided with the spouse, was subjected to battery or extreme cruelty by the spouse, and is a person of good moral character. Section 204(a)(l)(A)(iii) of the Act. Section I0I(f) of the Act designates certain actions that bar a finding of good moral character, including conviction or admission to commission of a crime involving moral turpitude. This section further provides: "The fact that any person is not within any of the [ specified] classes shall not preclude a finding that for other reasons such person is or was not of good moral character." Section lOl(f) of the Act. A petitioner' s good moral character is evaluated on a case-by-case basis taking into account the provisions of section 101 (f) of the Act and the standards of the average citizen in the community. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(l)(vii). Absent extenuating circumstances, petitioners will be found to lack good moral character if they committed unlawful acts that adversely reflect upon their moral character, although the acts do not require an automatic finding of lack of good moral character. Id.

R EV 3/2024 www.uscis.gov

The Act does not designate a time period during which petitioners must establish good moral character. However, primary evidence of a petitioner's good moral character is a local police clearance or state­ issued criminal background check from each of the petitioner's residences of six months or more during the three-year period preceding the filing of the petition. 8 C.F.R. § 204.2(c)(2)(v). Where there is reason to believe a petitioner may lack good moral character beyond this three-year period, we consider all relevant, credible evidence, including affidavits from responsible persons who knowledgeably attest to the petitioner's character. Id. See Matter ofSanchez-Linn, 20 T&N Dec. 362 (BIA 1991) (finding the greater the gravity of a noncitizen's past misconduct, the longer the period of intervening good conduct must be to establish good moral character). While we consider any credible evidence relevant to the petition, the determination of what evidence is credible, and the weight given such evidence lies within our sole discretion. Section 204( a)(l )(J) ofthe Act; 8 C.F .R. § 204.2( c )(2)(i).
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Dismissed
reference_prolog:
```prolog
% Case facts and legal analysis encoded in Prolog
% Binary decision output: 'Accepted' or 'Dismissed'
% Predicates that are simple yes/no use arity 0 as required.

% Procedural facts
filed_i360.
denied_by_director.
appealed_denial.

% Relationship / VAWA eligibility facts (record supports these elements)
petitioner_spouse_us_citizen.
married_in_good_faith.
eligible_relationship.
resided_with_spouse.
subjected_to_battery_or_extreme_cruelty.

% Criminal/arrest history (encoded as yes/no booleans where they are evaluated as
% factors against good moral character)
arrest_1998_fight.
probation_1998_completed.
arrest_2000_driving_suspended.
fine_paid_2000.
arrest_2001_reckless_endangerment.
dismissed_2003_reckless_endangerment.
arrest_2003_malicious_mischief.
dismissed_with_prejudice_2003.
arrest_2014_domestic_violence_assault.
deferred_disposition_2014.
anger_management_and_parenting_ordered_2014.
complied_with_2014_court_orders.
dismissed_2017_2014_charge.

% More recent and dispositive matters
pending_extortion_2022.            % Arrest/charge for extortion while petition pending
convicted_harassment_2023.        % 2023 conviction for harassment
probation_active_2023.            % Probation/community supervision for 2023 conviction

% Supporting / mitigating evidence submitted by petitioner
attended_anger_management.
states_rehabilitation_and_changed_coping.
medical_treatment_esophageal_cancer.
complied_with_court_orders_generally.

% Adverse pattern: multiple arrests and some assault-related conduct in the history
multiple_arrests_adverse :- 
    arrest_1998_fight,
    arrest_2000_driving_suspended,
    arrest_2001_reckless_endangerment,
    arrest_2003_malicious_mischief,
    arrest_2014_domestic_violence_assault.

% Recent conviction / probation and pending felony are significant adverse factors
recent_conviction :- convicted_harassment_2023.
active_probation :- probation_active_2023.
pending_felony :- pending_extortion_2022.

% Rules for lack of good moral character based on the regulations and record.
% The code models the Agency's discretionary balancing: certain convictions,
% active probation, pending felony charges, and a pattern of unlawful acts
% adversely reflecting on moral character will lead to a finding of lack of
% good moral character absent compelling extenuating evidence.

% Lacks good moral character if there is a recent conviction and active probation.
lacks_good_moral_character :-
    recent_conviction,
    active_probation.

% Lacks good moral character if there is a pending felony extortion charge while
% the petition is pending (serious pending criminal allegation).
lacks_good_moral_character :-
    pending_felony.

% Lacks good moral character if there is a pattern of multiple arrests and
% adverse conduct that reflect poorly on moral character (absent strong
% extenuating circumstances).
lacks_good_moral_character :-
    multiple_arrests_adverse.

% The petitioner may offer mitigating facts (rehabilitation, compliance,
% medical conditions). Those are considered but in this record do not
% conclusively overcome the adverse factors above. Model that by allowing
% extenuating_circumstances to negate some findings only if explicitly present.
% Here, the record contains rehabilitation steps but also a recent conviction
% and pending felony; therefore we do not derive extenuating_circumstances.
extenuating_circumstances :- 
    % would require strong, specific long-term evidence; not present as a
    % conclusive fact in this record
    false.

% Good moral character is the negation of lack of good moral character,
% unless extenuating circumstances are found to overcome the adverse evidence.
good_moral_character :-
    \+ lacks_good_moral_character.
good_moral_character :-
    lacks_good_moral_character,
    extenuating_circumstances.

% Eligibility requires all listed elements including good moral character.
eligibility_met :-
    married_in_good_faith,
    eligible_relationship,
    resided_with_spouse,
    subjected_to_battery_or_extreme_cruelty,
    good_moral_character.

% Decision/export entrypoint (provided structure)
decision(Result) :-
    (   eligibility_met
    ->  Result = 'Accepted'
    ;   Result = 'Dismissed'
    ).

run :-
    catch(
        (   decision(Result),
            writeln(Result)
        ),
        error(existence_error(procedure, PI), _),
        handle_undefined(PI)
    ).

handle_undefined(Name/Arity) :-
    (   current_predicate(Name/OtherArity),
        OtherArity \= Arity
    ->  format('Programming error: called ~w/~w, but only ~w/~w is defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity, Name, OtherArity])
    ;   format('Lack of information: predicate ~w/~w is not defined.~n',
               [Name, Arity])
    ).
```

text: This matter concerns an appeal from a Texas Service Center decision in USCIS case number 25674629. The Petitioner is a physician-researcher in urologic oncology who seeks employment-based second preference immigrant classification and a national interest waiver of the job-offer requirement. The Director denied the petition, and the matter is before the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal. On appeal, the Petitioner submitted additional evidence and a brief asserting eligibility for a national interest waiver. He stated that he will continue providing clinical care to cancer patients, conduct research, lead clinical trials, work to reduce healthcare costs, and develop innovative surgical approaches to improve care for patients with cancer. In support, he submitted materials from the American Cancer Society and the National Cancer Institute concerning urologic cancers and the economic burdens of cancer care, along with expert letters discussing the implications of his research for patient treatment and recovery. The record also includes his curriculum vitae, academic records, two published articles, evidence of peer review activity, articles citing his research findings, and letters of endorsement from experts in senior academic and medical positions describing his research accomplishments, including development of surgical techniques that significantly affect patient recovery and have been used by medical centers in the United States and abroad. The record further contains evidence described as showing that he is a research physician with influential published research and surgical techniques and treatment methods adopted by others, as well as evidence described as showing broader economic and public-health benefits associated with progress in improving treatment methods for cancer and other urologic disorders.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. If a petitioner demonstrates eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, they must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter of Dhanasar, 26 I&N Dec . 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S . Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may , as matter of discretion 1, grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: 1 See also Poursina v. USCIS , 936 F.3d 868 (9th Cir. 2019) (finding USCIS' decision to grant or deny a national interest waiver to be discretionary in nature).

• The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States.
question: Should this case be accepted or dismissed?
label: Accepted

text: The Petitioner is a technology company involved in electrical product and integrated software and hardware development and production. It seeks EB-2 classification for the Beneficiary, an embedded systems architect who is described as a member of the professions holding an advanced degree, and it also seeks a national interest waiver of the job offer requirement. The Director of the Texas Service Center initially denied the petition and later affirmed that decision on motion, concluding that the record did not establish that a waiver of the job offer requirement would be in the national interest. The Petitioner initially requested withdrawal of the appeal, and USCIS later reopened the appeal sua sponte to consider the merits of the Petitioner's appeal brief. The matter is before the Administrative Appeals Office on appeal. In support of the request, the record includes letters of support discussing how the Beneficiary's undertaking contributes to advancements in improved renewable energy technologies. The Petitioner submitted documentation indicating that circuit breaker and EV charging technology the Beneficiary has been essential in developing has been implemented with commercial and industrial clients throughout the United States and disseminated to others in the field through publication and presentation of his work. The Petitioner also represented that it intends to have the Beneficiary continue research and development in motor controls for large industrial and commercial facilities and EV charging, later stating that his work would focus on commercial fleet and multifamily EV charging, residential demand response, and solar energy storage product lines, and that this work would be shared through scientific conferences and meetings. Additional submitted information included an article about U.S. government initiatives to build out a national EV charging network to expand charging accessibility and support the competitiveness of the U.S. EV manufacturing industry. The record also contains material indicating widespread benefits associated with research progress in next-generation circuit breaker and EV charging technology.
statutes:
To establish eligibility for a national interest waiver, a petitioner must first demonstrate the beneficiary's qualification for the underlying EB-2 visa classification, as either an advanced degree professional or an individual of exceptional ability in the sciences, arts, or business. Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. An advanced degree is any United States academic or professional degree or a foreign equivalent degree above that of a bachelor's degree. A United States bachelor's degree or foreign equivalent degree followed by five years of progressive experience in the specialty is the equivalent of a master's degree. 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(2). 1 The Petitioner was permitted a period of 30 days in which to provide a supplemental brief. We did not receive a supplemental submission within that time frame and therefore consider the record to be complete as presently constituted.

If a petitioner demonstrates the beneficiary's eligibility for the underlying EB-2 classification, it must then establish that they merit a discretionary waiver of the job offer requirement "in the national interest." Section 203(b )(2)(B)(i) of the Act. While neither the statute nor the pertinent regulations define the term "national interest," Matter ofDhanasar, 26 I&N Dec. 884, 889 (AAO 2016), provides the framework for adjudicating national interest waiver petitions. Dhanasar states that U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) may, as matter of discretion, 2 grant a national interest waiver if the petitioner demonstrates that: • The proposed endeavor has both substantial merit and national importance; • The individual is well-positioned to advance their proposed endeavor; and • On balance, waiving the job offer requirement would benefit the United States. 3

label: Accepted
